```mermaid
    s1["Initial Access Phishing RDP"]
    s2["Execution Macro PowerShell LOLBins"]
    s3["Persistence Scheduled Task Run Key"]
    s4["Defense Evasion Disable Defender AMSI Bypass"]
    s5["Credential Access Mimikatz LSASS Dump"]
    s6["Lateral Movement SMB RDP Pass the Hash"]
    s7["Exfiltration Data Theft for Double Extortion"]
    s8["Impact Encryption Ransom Note Leak Site"]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define malware. Differentiate between polymorphic and metamorphic viruses with suitable examples.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Malware** is any program, code, or software intentionally designed to cause damage, unauthorized access, or disruption to a computer system, network, or data. The term is a contraction of *"malicious software"* and was coined by **Yisrael Radai in 1990**.

| Feature | Polymorphic Virus | Metamorphic Virus |
|---|---|---|
| Encryption | Uses encrypted body + variable decryptor | No encryption; rewrites own code |
| Signature | Decryptor changes, body encrypted | Whole body changes (re-code) |
| Detection | Heuristic / emulation needed | Code normalization + graph isomorphism |
| Example | Storm Worm, Zeus, Conficker | Simile, Zmist, Evulon |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Table (2 marks)

#### **Q2. List and briefly explain any three major eras in the evolution of malware with representative examples.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Experimental Era (1971–1989):** Marked by **Creeper (1971)**, the first worm that spread on ARPANET displaying the message *"I'm the creeper"*. **Brain (1986)** was the first PC boot sector virus, and **Morris Worm (1988)** caused the first internet-scale infection leading to the formation of **CERT/CC**.

2. **Macro and Scripting Era (1995–2000):** Marked by **Concept (1995)**, the first Word macro virus using VBA. **Melissa (1999)** spread via Outlook contacts, and **ILOVEYOU (2000)** used VBScript with a social-engineering subject line causing **$5.5–8.7 billion** in damages.

3. **Cyber-Weapon and Ransomware Era (2010–2019):** Began with **Stuxnet (2010)**, the first known cyber-physical weapon targeting Iran's nuclear centrifuges via Siemens PLCs. **CryptoLocker (2013)** popularized the modern ransomware model with RSA encryption and Bitcoin ransom demands. **Mirai (2016)** weaponized IoT devices, and **WannaCry (2017)** combined worm propagation with ransomware via the EternalBlue exploit.

**Mark Distribution:** Three points (1 mark each) with example.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat timeline, describe the **evolution of malware from Creeper (1971) to Stuxnet (2010)**, identifying the **key technological shift** that triggered each major phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **architecture of Stuxnet**, focusing on its **multi-stage delivery, four zero-day exploits, stolen digital certificates, and Siemens PLC targeting mechanism**. Why is Stuxnet considered a watershed moment in malware history? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Phase-wise Evolution (Pre-Internet to Cyber-Weapon Era):**

1. **1971 – Creeper:** The first worm; spread on ARPANET using a self-replicating mechanism through the TENEX OS. **Trigger:** ARPANET connectivity.

2. **1975 – Wabbit / Rabbit:** A self-replicating program on IBM 360/370 mainframes. **Trigger:** Multiprogramming OS design.

3. **1986 – Brain (Pakistani Brain):** First PC boot sector virus spread via 5.25" floppy disks; contained contact information of the Alvi brothers. **Trigger:** Rise of IBM PC and removable media.

4. **1987 – Jerusalem (Black Friday):** First destructive DOS file virus that deleted programs on Friday the 13th. **Trigger:** File-based executable architecture (.COM / .EXE).

5. **1988 – Morris Worm:** First Internet worm using `sendmail`, `fingerd`, and weak passwords. Infected ~6,000 hosts. **Trigger:** Open UNIX research network.

6. **1995 – Concept:** First macro virus using MS Word VBA. **Trigger:** Office suite integration of scripting.

7. **1999 – Melissa:** First mass-mailer worm via Outlook MAPI. **Trigger:** Integrated email clients.

8. **2000 – ILOVEYOU:** VBScript worm with double-extension social engineering. **Trigger:** Default Windows hidden extension setting.

9. **2001 – Code Red & Nimda:** IIS buffer overflow worms. **Trigger:** Web server monoculture (Microsoft IIS).

10. **2003 – SQL Slammer:** Fastest worm; saturated Internet in 10 minutes. **Trigger:** Unpatched MS SQL Server 2000 on UDP/1434.

11. **2008 – Conficker:** Massive botnet with peer-to-peer update and MD6 crypto. **Trigger:** Weak password policies + unpatched MS08-067.

12. **2010 – Stuxnet:** First cyber-physical weapon targeting PLCs. **Trigger:** Air-gapped SCADA/ICS environments and supply chain access via USB.

**Mark Distribution:** Timeline (5 marks) + Key shifts (2 marks)

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Stuxnet Architecture – A 7-Layer Explanation:**

1. **Delivery Vector:** USB drives (air-gapped networks). Used a **LNK file vulnerability (CVE-2010-2568)** to auto-execute from USB.

2. **Privilege Escalation:** Used a **keyboard layout file zero-day (CVE-2010-3338)** in Windows XP/2003 to escalate from user to SYSTEM.

3. **Local Privilege Escalation:** Exploited **Task Scheduler zero-day (CVE-2010-3888)** to bypass security tokens.

4. **Network Propagation:** Exploited **RPC vulnerability (CVE-2010-2729)** and the **MS08-067 print spooler** bug for lateral movement within LANs.

5. **Code-Signing Authenticity:** Stole **Realtek Semiconductor (VeriSign)** and **JMicron Technology (VeriSign)** digital certificates to sign its drivers, bypassing kernel-mode signature checks.

6. **PLC Targeting:** Specifically targeted **Siemens S7-300/400 PLCs** (SIMATIC Step 7 software). It modified the centrifuge rotor speeds between **807 Hz and 1410 Hz (normal: 1064 Hz)** for short bursts, causing mechanical destruction over weeks.

7. **Self-Destruct / Stealth:** Stuxnet had a **self-destruct date of June 24, 2012** to prevent analysis, and used **rootkit techniques** to hide modified PLC code from operators.

**Why Stuxnet is a Watershed Moment:**

- **First cyber-physical weapon** that caused physical destruction (≈1,000 centrifuges destroyed at Natanz).
- **Used four zero-days in a single payload** – unprecedented resource investment indicating nation-state actors.
- **Crossed the air gap** via USB, demonstrating that isolation is not security.
- **Triggered the modern era of nation-state APTs** (Duqu, Flame, Gauss, USBCulprit), all attributed to the same Equation Group / NSA.

**Mark Distribution:** Architecture (5 marks) + Significance (2 marks)

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of the **SIR epidemic model**, derive the **basic reproduction number $R_0$** and explain its significance in predicting the spread of network worms. Use **Code Red (2001)** as a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **shift from signature-based to behavior-based detection**. Explain **heuristic analysis, sandboxing, and endpoint detection and response (EDR)** as modern countermeasures against polymorphic and fileless malware. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**SIR Model Derivation:**

Let $S(t)$, $I(t)$, $R(t)$ denote susceptible, infected, and removed hosts. The model assumes:

- Constant population: $N = S + I + R$ (no births/deaths during the outbreak).
- Infection rate proportional to $S \cdot I$.
- Removal at rate $\gamma \cdot I$.

The governing equations are:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot S \cdot I$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot S \cdot I - \gamma \cdot I$$

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I$$

**Finding $R_0$:**

At the start of the outbreak, $S \approx N$ and $I$ grows as:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = (\beta \cdot N - \gamma) \cdot I$$

For an epidemic to occur, $dI/dt > 0$, which requires:

$$\beta \cdot N > \gamma$$

Therefore:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta \cdot N}{\gamma}$$

**Interpretation:**

- If $R_0 < 1$, the worm dies out.
- If $R_0 > 1$, the worm reaches epidemic proportions.
- The **epidemic peak** occurs at $S = N / R_0$.

**Numerical Example – Code Red (2001):**

- Total vulnerable IIS servers: $N \approx 360{,}000$ (Symantec estimate).
- The worm scanned ~**12 hosts/second**.
- Effective infection rate: $\beta \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ (per host per infected).
- Average host patch time: $1 / \gamma \approx 2$ days, so $\gamma \approx 5.79 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}^{-1}$.

$$R_0 = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.6 \times 10^{5}}{5.79 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.648}{5.79 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.12$$

Since $R_0 > 1$, Code Red caused an epidemic, peaking at ~**359,000 infected hosts in 14 hours** and DDoSing the White House.

**Mark Distribution:** Model (3 marks) + $R_0$ derivation (2 marks) + Code Red example (2 marks)

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Signature-Based Detection (1970s–2000s):**

- Maintains a database of **known malware hashes / byte sequences**.
- Uses **MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256** of files for matching.
- **Limitation:** Useless against **zero-day, polymorphic, and fileless** malware because the hash changes per copy or no file is written.

**Behavior-Based Detection (Modern Era):**

1. **Heuristic Analysis:**

   - Examines code **structure and instructions** (e.g., suspicious API calls, packed executables, entropy analysis).
   - Uses **static heuristics** (YARA rules, PE section anomalies) and **dynamic heuristics** (API call tracing).
   - Can detect **new variants** without prior signatures.

2. **Sandboxing:**

   - Executes the suspicious file in an **isolated virtual environment** (VM/container) and monitors:
     - File system changes (registry persistence, dropped files).
     - Network traffic (C2 callbacks to known malicious IPs).
     - Process behavior (privilege escalation, code injection).
   - **Limitation:** Sophisticated malware uses **anti-VM tricks** (sleep timers, CPUID checks, sandbox detection).

3. **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR):**

   - Provides **continuous telemetry** from endpoints (process execution, network connections, file changes).
   - Uses **ML-based anomaly detection** to identify TTPs from **MITRE ATT&CK**.
   - Example tools: **CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne, Carbon Black**.
   - **Forensic value:** EDR logs are crucial for **incident response and IOC extraction**.

**Extended Detection and Response (XDR):** Integrates EDR with network, email, cloud, and identity telemetry for **holistic threat correlation**.

**Mark Distribution:** Three techniques (5 marks) + Comparison (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse polymorphic with metamorphic.** Polymorphic uses a variable decryptor stub; metamorphic rewrites the entire body without a decryptor.
> 2. **Creeper (1971) is technically a worm, not a virus** – it does not attach to host files. Use precise terminology.
> 3. **Morris Worm was a worm, not a virus** – write it as *"Internet Worm"* in the answer script.
> 4. **The Morris Worm case is the first CFAA conviction** – this is a frequently asked 1-mark supplementary question.
> 5. **Stuxnet's target is Siemens S7-300/400 PLCs**, NOT SCADA software directly. Specify the model.
> 6. **Log4Shell is a vulnerability, not malware** – it is exploited by malware like **Kinsing, Mirai, and XMRig**.
> 7. **Mirai did not exploit a zero-day** – it used **default Telnet credentials**. Mention this clearly.
> 8. **In SIR model questions, always define $R_0$ in words** alongside the formula; KTU awards the mark for the verbal explanation.
> 9. **For Ransomware**, mention **double / triple extortion** (encryption + leak + DDoS) for full marks in 14-mark questions.
> 10. **Do NOT write "Kali Linux" or "Metasploit" as malware** – they are security tools.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Creeper (1971)** – First worm, ARPANET, Bob Thomas.
- **Brain (1986)** – First PC boot sector virus, Alvi brothers, Pakistan.
- **Morris Worm (1988)** – First Internet worm, led to CERT/CC, first CFAA case.
- **Concept (1995)** – First macro virus (VBA).
- **Melissa (1999)** – First mass-mailer worm (Outlook).
- **ILOVEYOU (2000)** – VBScript, double-extension trick, $5.5–8.7B damage.
- **Code Red (2001)** – IIS buffer overflow, White House DDoS.
- **SQL Slammer (2003)** – 376-byte UDP worm, 10-minute global saturation, $T_d = 8.5$s.
- **Conficker (2008)** – 9–15M hosts, MD6 crypto, P2P updates.
- **Stuxnet (2010)** – First cyber-physical weapon; 4 zero-days; Siemens S7-300/400 PLCs; US/Israel.
- **CryptoLocker (2013)** – Modern ransomware with RSA-2048 + Bitcoin.
- **Mirai (2016)** – IoT botnet, default Telnet credentials, Dyn DNS DDoS.
- **WannaCry (2017)** – EternalBlue (MS17-010), 230,000+ victims in 1 day.
- **NotPetya (2017)** – Wiper disguised as ransomware, $10B+ damage.
- **SUNBURST (2020)** – Supply-chain compromise of SolarWinds Orion, APT29.
- **Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)** – CVSS 10.0, JNDI injection in Log4j.
- **Polymorphic vs Metamorphic:** Polymorphic = variable decryptor; Metamorphic = full body rewrite (no decryptor).
- **SIR Model:** $R_0 = \beta N / \gamma$ – predicts worm epidemic threshold.
- **Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed):** Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions on Objectives.
- **MITRE ATT&CK:** Industry standard for mapping adversary TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures).
- **Modern Trends:** AI-augmented malware, fileless/LOLBins, double/triple extortion ransomware, supply-chain attacks, deepfake vishing.
- **Defense Evolution:** Signature AV → Heuristic → Sandbox → EDR → XDR → Zero Trust Architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malware Types
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Malware Types

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Malware?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Malware** (short for *Malicious Software*) is any program, code, script, macro, or set of instructions deliberately designed to cause damage, disruption, unauthorized access, data theft, or other harmful effects on a computer system, network, server, or user, without the informed consent of the owner.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, malware forensics begins with classifying threats by their **propagation mechanism**, **payload behavior**, and **persistence strategy**. Every forensic investigation pivots on correctly identifying the *type* of malware first.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital emergency ward. Doctors cannot begin treatment until they diagnose what kind of illness the patient has — bacterial, viral, fungal, or parasitic. Each illness requires a **different medicine** (countermeasure) and a **different lab test** (forensic signature).

**Malware types work exactly the same way.** A *virus* is like a human virus — it needs a host file to attach itself to. A *worm* is like airborne contagion — it travels through the network on its own. A *trojan* is like a gift box that looks innocent but contains something dangerous inside. Until a forensic analyst correctly classifies the malware, the **incident response playbook** cannot be chosen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> The classification of malware is the **first step** of any forensic triage. Wrong classification leads to wrong containment strategy, which can allow lateral spread inside an enterprise network.

## 1.2 The Malware Threat Landscape — Master Classification

The malware universe is categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Propagation Vector** — How does it spread? (File infection, network, social engineering)
2. **Payload Objective** — What does it do once executed? (Espionage, destruction, monetization)
3. **Persistence & Stealth** — How does it survive reboot and avoid detection? (Registry hooks, rootkits, fileless)

For KTU exam purposes, malware is traditionally classified into the following **canonical types**:

| # | Malware Type | Primary Vector | Core Objective |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Virus** | Host file attachment | Code execution & spread |
| 2 | **Worm** | Network propagation | Self-replication across systems |
| 3 | **Trojan Horse** | Social engineering / disguise | Backdoor access |
| 4 | **Ransomware** | Phishing / exploit kits | File encryption for extortion |
| 5 | **Spyware** | Bundled freeware | User activity surveillance |
| 6 | **Adware** | Bundled installers | Forced advertising revenue |
| 7 | **Rootkit** | Dropper / second-stage | Deep system stealth |
| 8 | **Bot / Botnet** | C2 (Command & Control) | Distributed attack platform |
| 9 | **Keylogger** | Trojan / phishing | Credential harvesting |
| 10 | **Fileless Malware** | PowerShell / WMI / memory | Living-off-the-Land attacks |
| 11 | **Logic Bomb** | Insider / time trigger | Conditional sabotage |
| 12 | **Backdoor** | Initial payload | Remote unauthenticated access |
| 13 | **Dropper / Downloader** | Stage-0 loader | Deliver secondary payload |
| 14 | **Polymorphic / Metamorphic** | Self-mutating engine | Evade signature detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Reality:** Real-world malware samples are almost always **multi-modal hybrids**. A single incident may involve a *Dropper* delivering a *Trojan* that installs a *Rootkit* which then deploys a *Keylogger* and enrolls the host into a *Botnet*. KTU exam questions expect you to identify the **dominant behavior**.

---

## 1.3 Detailed Definitions of Each Malware Type

### 1.3.1 Virus
A **computer virus** is malicious code that **attaches itself to a legitimate host program** and propagates only when the host is executed. It requires human interaction (running the infected file) to spread. Viruses often modify or corrupt files.

### 1.3.2 Worm
A **worm** is a self-replicating, self-propagating program that spreads **autonomously across networks** without requiring a host file or user action. It exploits vulnerabilities in network services (SMB, RPC, SSH) to jump from system to system.

### 1.3.3 Trojan Horse
A **Trojan** is malicious code disguised as legitimate or desirable software. Unlike a virus, it does **not self-replicate** through file infection, but relies entirely on **social engineering** to trick the user into installing it. It typically opens a backdoor.

### 1.3.4 Ransomware
**Ransomware** encrypts user or organizational data using strong cryptographic algorithms (AES-256, RSA-2048), then demands payment (usually in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Modern variants use **double extortion** — encrypt data *and* exfiltrate it.

### 1.3.5 Spyware
**Spyware** covertly monitors user behavior — keystrokes, browsing history, credentials, screenshots — and transmits the data to an attacker-controlled server. It is the parent category of keyloggers, screen scrapers, and stalkerware.

### 1.3.6 Adware
**Adware** displays unwanted advertisements, often through browser injection, popup overlays, or DNS hijacking. While less destructive, it is a major vector for secondary infections and privacy violations.

### 1.3.7 Rootkit
A **rootkit** is a stealthy collection of tools designed to maintain **persistent, privileged, and undetectable** access to a system. It operates at the kernel, hypervisor, firmware (UEFI), or bootloader level, manipulating the OS to hide its own presence and that of other malware.

### 1.3.8 Bot & Botnet
A **bot** is an infected endpoint (zombie) under remote control of an attacker (bot herder) via a **Command-and-Control (C2) channel**. A **botnet** is the network of such bots, used for DDoS, credential stuffing, cryptomining, and spam campaigns.

### 1.3.9 Keylogger
A **keylogger** is specialized spyware that records every keystroke typed by the user. **Software keyloggers** hook OS APIs; **hardware keyloggers** are physical inline devices. They are the leading cause of credential theft.

### 1.3.10 Fileless Malware
**Fileless malware** operates entirely in memory, leveraging trusted system tools (PowerShell, WMI, PsExec, rundll32) to execute malicious logic **without dropping any file on disk**. It is the dominant modern APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) technique.

### 1.3.11 Logic Bomb
A **logic bomb** is malicious code that remains dormant until a specific **trigger condition** is met — a date (Friday the 13th virus), a user action, or the deletion of an employee record. It is the classic **insider threat** weapon.

### 1.3.12 Backdoor
A **backdoor** is code that bypasses normal authentication to provide remote, hidden access. Some are intentionally built by developers; most are installed by other malware to ensure **surviving access** even if the primary malware is removed.

### 1.3.13 Dropper / Downloader
A **dropper** is a small stage-0 payload whose only job is to install the real malware onto the system. A **downloader** fetches additional components from the internet at runtime. Both evade initial sandboxing because the payload is fetched *after* execution.

### 1.3.14 Polymorphic & Metamorphic Malware
**Polymorphic malware** encrypts its payload with a variable key, changing its signature each time it replicates, while keeping the decryption engine and code logic identical. **Metamorphic malware** rewrites its own code (instruction substitution, register swapping, dead-code insertion) so that **every instance is structurally unique** — no two samples share a signature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Malware Classification

Every malware sample is analyzed across three forensic dimensions. KTU examiners expect you to articulate these clearly.

### Pillar 1: Propagation Mechanism

| Mechanism | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **File Infection** | Attaches to executable host | CIH (Chernobyl) Virus |
| **Boot Sector** | Infects MBR / VBR | Stuxnet Boot Record Module |
| **Macro Infection** | Lives in Office documents | Melissa, Emotet documents |
| **Network Self-Spread** | Exploits protocols | Blaster, Conficker, WannaCry |
| **Social Engineering** | User deception | Trojanized PDF / ISO files |
| **Supply Chain** | Pre-installed in legit software | SolarWinds SUNBURST |
| **Drive-by Download** | Browser exploit kit | Angler EK, RIG EK |
| **Fileless / LOLBins** | PowerShell / WMI abuse | PowerSploit, Cobalt Strike |

### Pillar 2: Payload Objective

| Objective | Behavior | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality Breach** | Data exfiltration | Agent Tesla, RedLine |
| **Integrity Violation** | File corruption / wiping | Shamoon, NotPetya |
| **Availability Disruption** | DoS / DDoS | Mirai Botnet, Mirai variants |
| **Resource Hijack** | Cryptomining / proxy | XMRig, Glupteba |
| **Monetization** | Ransomware / banking trojan | Ryuk, ZeuS, Dridex |
| **Espionage** | Long-term surveillance | APT1, FIN7 toolkit |

### Pillar 3: Persistence & Defense Evasion

| Technique | Level | Counter-Forensic Difficulty |
|---|---|---|
| **Registry Run keys** | User-space | Low |
| **Scheduled Tasks** | User-space | Low |
| **Services / Drivers** | Kernel | Medium |
| **Bootkit (MBR/VBR)** | Pre-OS | High |
| **UEFI/BIOS Implant** | Firmware | Very High |
| **Hypervisor Rootkit** | Below OS | Extreme |
| **Process Injection** | Memory | High |
| **AMSI / ETW Bypass** | Telemetry bypass | Very High |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While malware forensics is qualitative, KTU examiners frequently test **identification, classification logic, and characteristic matching**. The following table is the cheat-sheet you must memorize.

### Master Comparison Table (EXAM-CRITICAL)

| Feature | Virus | Worm | Trojan | Ransomware | Rootkit | Fileless |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-replicates?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No (mostly) | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Needs host file?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **User action to spread?** | ✅ Required | ❌ Autonomous | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Often |
| **Network propagation?** | ❌ No (mostly) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚠️ Lateral | ⚠️ Via C2 | ⚠️ Lateral |
| **Primary payload** | Corruption | Replication | Backdoor | Encryption | Stealth | In-memory exec |
| **Detection surface** | File signature | Network traffic | Behavior | Encryption API | Hook chains | Memory artifacts |
| **Persistence** | File infect | Service | Registry | Registry / Task | Kernel | WMI subscription |
| **Forensic artifact** | File hash change | SMB scans | Suspicious EXE in temp | Ransom note | Hidden process | PowerShell logs |
| **Memory-only?** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ Optional | ✅ Yes |

### Propagation Speed — Epidemiological Model

The spread of worms is often modeled using the **Epidemic SI (Susceptible-Infected) Model** borrowed from epidemiology:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)$$

Where:
- $I(t)$ = number of infected hosts at time $t$
- $S(t)$ = number of susceptible hosts at time $t$
- $\beta$ = infection rate constant (depends on vulnerability density and scan rate)

In the early outbreak phase where $S(t) \approx N$ (total population), this simplifies to:

$$I(t) \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}$$

This **exponential growth** is why worms like **Code Red** (2001) infected 359,000 hosts in under 14 hours, and **WannaCry** (2017) hit 230,000 systems in 24 hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is used by CERT teams to estimate *time-to-saturation* and prioritize patching windows. It is a favorite 14-mark KTU question on worms.

### Ransomware Encryption Strength Metric

For ransomware, the cryptographic resilience is measured by the key length:

$$E_{\text{security}} \approx 2^{k} \text{ operations}$$

Where $k$ is the symmetric key length in bits. Modern ransomware uses **AES-256** ($k = 256$):

$$E_{\text{AES-256}} \approx 2^{256} \approx 1.15 \times 10^{77} \text{ operations}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is computationally infeasible to brute-force with current classical computers. Recovery without the attacker's key requires either (a) payment, (b) a flawed implementation bug (e.g., CryptoLocker's partial key recovery), or (c) quantum-resistant computing (future).

### Polymorphic Mutation Count

A polymorphic engine producing a unique variant per generation:

$$N_{\text{variants}} = K^n$$

Where $K$ = number of equivalent encryption keys/transformations, and $n$ = number of replications. For $K = 64$ and $n = 20$:

$$N_{\text{variants}} = 64^{20} \approx 1.33 \times 10^{36}$$

This exceeds the number of atoms in the observable universe, demonstrating why **signature-based antivirus is obsolete** against modern malware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Forensic Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Worm Outbreak Growth (Code Red Case Study)

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A network worm exploits a buffer-overflow vulnerability. At time $t = 0$, there are $I_0 = 10$ initially infected hosts out of a total population $N = 100{,}000$ vulnerable systems. The infection rate constant is measured as $\beta = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ per host per second. Compute the infected count $I(t)$ at $t = 6$ hours and determine whether the network will reach saturation before administrator intervention.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing differential equation.**
The epidemiological SI model gives:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the early-outbreak approximation.**
When $I(t) \ll N$, we have $S(t) \approx N$, which simplifies the equation to:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} \approx \beta \cdot N \cdot I(t)$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(t)} \frac{dI}{I} = \int_{0}^{t} \beta N \, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both integrals.**

$$\ln(I(t)) - \ln(I_0) = \beta N t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I(t)}{I_0}\right) = \beta N t$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $I(t)$ by exponentiating.**

$$\frac{I(t)}{I_0} = e^{\beta N t}$$

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the given numerical values.**
- $I_0 = 10$
- $N = 100{,}000$
- $\beta = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ per host per second
- $t = 6 \text{ hours} = 6 \times 3600 = 21{,}600 \text{ seconds}$

Compute the exponent:

$$\beta N t = (1.5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (100{,}000) \times (21{,}600)$$

$$= 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.16 \times 10^{9}$$

$$= 3.24 \times 10^{2} = 324$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the exponential.**

$$e^{324} \approx 1.96 \times 10^{141}$$

**Step 8 — Compute $I(t)$.**

$$I(21600) = 10 \times 1.96 \times 10^{141} = 1.96 \times 10^{142}$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the result.**
This astronomical number ($\approx 10^{142}$) vastly exceeds $N = 10^{5}$, meaning the network would have saturated **long before** $t = 6$ hours. In reality, the SI model's assumption $S(t) \approx N$ breaks down once $I(t)$ approaches $N$, and the logistic model must be used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This derivation shows why **worm containment must occur within the first few hours**. By the time IT staff notice the incident, exponential growth has already caused near-complete compromise. Modern SIEM tools trigger automatic isolation once a single host exceeds a threshold scan rate.

### Refined Logistic Growth Model

For late-stage outbreak analysis, the logistic correction yields:

$$\frac{dI(t)}{dt} = \beta \cdot S(t) \cdot I(t) - \mu \cdot I(t)$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I \cdot (N - I) - \mu I$$

Where $\mu$ = removal rate (patching, isolation). The **carrying capacity** becomes:

$$K = N \left(1 - \frac{\mu}{\beta N}\right) = N - \frac{\mu}{\beta}$$

This is the maximum simultaneous infection level under active defense — a critical planning metric for SOC (Security Operations Center) teams.

## 3.2 Forensic Decision Matrix — Identifying Malware Type from Observed Symptoms

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Content:** Forensic analysts in the field use the following symptom-to-class mapping during incident triage. This is the **most frequently tested** framework.

| Observed Forensic Symptom | Most Probable Malware Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Files modified but host program still functional** | Virus | File-infection behavior |
| **High outbound SMB/RPC traffic from one host** | Worm | Self-propagation via network |
| **Unknown .exe in `%TEMP%` after opening PDF** | Trojan / Dropper | Social engineering delivery |
| **Files renamed to `.locked`, ransom note present** | Ransomware | Encryption payload |
| **Browser homepage hijacked, popup ads** | Adware / Browser Hijacker | Forced monetization |
| **Hidden process in Task Manager (using rootkit revealer)** | Rootkit | Kernel-level hiding |
| **Periodic HTTP requests to strange domain** | Bot / C2 beacon | Command & control callback |
| **PowerShell spawned by Word macro** | Fileless / Macro dropper | LOLBin abuse |
| **Anti-virus disabled, UAC bypassed** | Rootkit + Backdoor | Defense evasion chain |
| **Suspicious traffic only during business hours** | Logic Bomb / Insider | Time/event trigger |
| **Registry `Run` key added unknown value** | Trojan / Backdoor | Persistence via autostart |
| **Each infection sample has different hash** | Polymorphic / Metamorphic | Self-mutating code |
| **CPU 100% but no visible process** | Cryptominer (Rootkit-wrapped) | Stealth resource hijack |
| **Credentials leaked from a single host** | Keylogger / Spyware | Keystroke capture |

### Worked Identification Example

**Scenario (KTU-Style):**
A SOC analyst observes the following on a compromised Windows host:

1. A PowerShell process (`powershell.exe`) is running with `winword.exe` as parent.
2. No new files appear in the file system.
3. WMI subscriptions have been created referencing encoded commands.
4. Network traffic shows HTTPS beacons to a domain registered 3 days ago.

**Identify the malware type and justify your reasoning.**

**Solution:**
The presence of **PowerShell spawned by Word** indicates document-borne execution. The **absence of file artifacts** combined with **WMI subscription persistence** points strongly to **Fileless Malware**. The **HTTPS beaconing to a newly registered domain (NRD)** is a classic C2 callback pattern, suggesting the sample is also a **Trojan** providing remote access.

**Primary Classification:** **Fileless Trojan with WMI Persistence**

**Forensic Response:**
- Capture volatile memory (RAM dump with WinPMEM, FTK Imager).
- Extract PowerShell event logs (Event ID 4104 — Script Block Logging).
- Decode the WMI subscription payload.
- Block the NRD at DNS firewall.
- Hunt for lateral movement using Sysmon Event ID 1 (Process Create).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Malware Type Taxonomy — Hierarchical Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Malware Master Class]:::root --> B1[File-Infecting]
    A0 --> B2[Network-Based]
    A0 --> B3[Deception-Based]
    A0 --> B4[Stealth-Based]
    A0 --> B5[Monetization-Based]
    A0 --> B6[Memory-Based]

    B1 --> C1A[Virus]
    C1A --> D1A[File Virus]
    C1A --> D1B[Boot Sector Virus]
    C1A --> D1C[Macro Virus]
    C1A --> D1D[Multipartite Virus]

    B2 --> C2A[Worm]
    C2A --> D2A[Email Worm]
    C2A --> D2B[Internet Worm]
    C2A --> D2C[IM Worm]

    B3 --> C3A[Trojan Horse]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
**Define malware. List any four major types of malware with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**
Malware (Malicious Software) is any program or code intentionally designed to cause damage, unauthorized access, or disruption to a computer system without user consent.

Four major types:
1. **Virus** — Attaches to a host program and spreads when executed.
2. **Worm** — Self-replicates across networks without user intervention.
3. **Trojan Horse** — Disguised as legitimate software but carries malicious payload.
4. **Ransomware** — Encrypts user data and demands ransom for decryption key.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between a virus and a worm. State any two distinguishing characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| Host File Dependency | Requires a host executable to attach to | Standalone; does not require a host |
| Propagation Vector | Spreads via file sharing, USB | Spreads autonomously via network protocols |
| User Action | Needs user to execute infected file | No user action needed once network is reachable |
| Bandwidth Impact | Low (local execution) | High (network scan storms) |
| Speed of Spread | Slow | Very fast (e.g., Code Red in 14 hours) |

**Two distinguishing characteristics:**
1. A worm is **self-propagating** over the network without user action, while a virus requires user execution.
2. A virus **modifies host files**, while a worm typically does not alter files but consumes **network bandwidth** during scanning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of malware based on **propagation mechanism** and **payload behavior**. Discuss in detail the working of **Ransomware**, **Fileless Malware**, and **Polymorphic Malware** with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A network worm infects a subnet with $N = 50{,}000$ vulnerable hosts. The infection rate constant is $\beta = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ per host per second. Initially, only $I_0 = 5$ hosts are infected. Using the SI epidemiological model, estimate the infected population after $t = 4$ hours. Discuss the implications for incident response. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification & Deep Dive

**Classification Framework:**

Malware is classified along three forensic axes:

1. **By Propagation Mechanism:**
   - File-infecting (Virus)
   - Network-propagating (Worm)
   - Social engineering (Trojan)
   - Fileless / LOLBin-based
   - Supply chain
   - Drive-by downloads

2. **By Payload Behavior:**
   - Confidentiality breach (Spyware)
   - Integrity violation (Virus, Logic Bomb)
   - Availability disruption (Ransomware, DDoS Bot)
   - Resource hijack (Cryptominer)
   - Espionage (APT)

3. **By Persistence & Stealth:**
   - User-mode persistence (Registry Run keys)
   - Kernel-mode persistence (Drivers, Services)
   - Pre-OS persistence (Bootkit, UEFI)

**Ransomware — Detailed Working:**

Ransomware operates through a four-stage lifecycle:

1. **Initial Infection Vector** — Phishing email with malicious attachment, exploit kit landing page, or compromised RDP credentials. Example: Conti ransomware group used TrickBot as the loader.

2. **Execution and Persistence** — Payload establishes persistence via Scheduled Tasks, Registry Run keys, or Windows Services to survive reboot.

3. **Encryption Phase** — The malware enumerates local files, mapped network drives, and cloud sync folders (OneDrive, Dropbox). It uses **AES-256** to encrypt file contents and **RSA-2048** to encrypt the AES key — this is called **hybrid cryptography**. The RSA private key resides only on the attacker's server.

4. **Extortion Phase** — A ransom note (e.g., `README_DECRYPT.txt`) is dropped in every directory. Modern variants use **double extortion**: data is exfiltrated before encryption, and the threat actor threatens public leak on a "name-and-shame" site (e.g., Conti's Tor blog).

**Example:** **WannaCry (2017)** — exploited SMBv1 vulnerability (EternalBlue), infected 230,000 systems in 24 hours across 150 countries, demanded $300-$600 in Bitcoin.

**Fileless Malware — Detailed Working:**

Fileless malware is the dominant modern APT technique because it leaves **no on-disk artifact** for signature-based detection. Its working involves:

1. **Living-off-the-Land Binaries (LOLBins)** — Abuses trusted system tools like `powershell.exe`, `wmic.exe`, `mshta.exe`, `rundll32.exe`, `regsvr32.exe`.

2. **In-Memory Execution** — Malicious code is reflected and executed directly in the RAM using .NET reflection or PowerShell `IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(...)`.

3. **Persistence via WMI Event Subscriptions** — A WMI event consumer is registered that re-executes the payload on system startup or specific triggers.

4. **Defense Evasion** — AMSI (Anti-Malware Scan Interface) bypass, ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) patching, and Windows Defender exclusion abuse.

**Example:** **Cobalt Strike's Beacon** — a commercial adversary simulation framework heavily abused by APT29, FIN6, and others.

**Polymorphic Malware — Detailed Working:**

1. **Encryption Engine** — The malware contains a small decryption stub and an encrypted payload blob.
2. **Variable Key Generation** — Each replication generates a new random encryption key.
3. **Mutation Engine** — Inserts NOP-equivalent instructions, swaps equivalent opcodes, and pads with junk code.
4. **Decryption at Runtime** — When executed, the stub decrypts the payload into memory and runs it.

The signature (hash) of the file changes every generation, defeating traditional antivirus.

**Example:** **Storm Worm (2007)** used email-borne polymorphic attachments.

---

#### Part (b) — Worm Outbreak Quantitative Analysis

**Given:**
- $N = 50{,}000$
- $\beta = 2 \times 10^{-7}$
- $I_0 = 5$
- $t = 4 \text{ hours} = 14{,}400 \text{ seconds}$

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation.**

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent.**

$$\beta N t = (2 \times 10^{-7}) \times (50{,}000) \times (14{,}400)$$

$$= 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 7.2 \times 10^{8}$$

$$= 144$$

**Step 3 — Compute $e^{144}$.**

$$e^{144} \approx 2.13 \times 10^{62}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $I(t)$.**

$$I(14{,}400) = 5 \times 2.13 \times 10^{62} = 1.065 \times 10^{63}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.** This number vastly exceeds $N = 50{,}000$. The network would have **saturated within minutes** under realistic parameters. The early-outbreak approximation breaks down very early; the logistic model would yield near-complete infection in roughly 1-2 hours.

**Implications for Incident Response:**
1. **Manual intervention is futile** — by the time an alert reaches a human analyst, the worm has already compromised most of the subnet.
2. **Automated containment is mandatory** — Network Access Control (NAC), automated host isolation via EDR, and DNS sinkholing must trigger on first-detection.
3. **Patching windows must be hours, not days** — vulnerability disclosure-to-exploit time is shrinking (e.g., BlueKeep CVE-2019-0708 was exploited within weeks).
4. **Network segmentation limits blast radius** — internal firewalls (VLAN separation, Zero Trust) prevent the worm from crossing zones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Many students substitute $N$ and $t$ in the wrong units. Always convert $t$ to **seconds** when $\beta$ is given per-second. Mixing hours with a per-second $\beta$ is the #1 cause of numerical errors in this question type.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a **Botnet**. Discuss its role in launching **DDoS attacks**, **spam campaigns**, and **credential stuffing**. Mention any two real-world botnet case studies. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Polymorphic** and **Metamorphic** malware. Explain why signature-based antivirus is ineffective against them. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Botnet Architecture

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Bot Herder (Attacker)** — The operator controlling the botnet.
2. **Command and Control (C2) Server** — Central server issuing commands.
3. **Bots / Zombies** — Compromised endpoints awaiting instructions.
4. **C2 Channels** — Communication protocols (IRC, HTTP/HTTPS, DNS tunneling, Tor hidden services, decentralized peer-to-peer).

**Bot Lifecycle:**
1. Infection via Trojan / Worm / Exploit Kit.
2. Bot registers with C2 server.
3. Bot awaits commands.
4. Herder issues commands.
5. Bots execute coordinated action.

**Role in DDoS Attacks:**
- **SYN Flood** — Bots send massive TCP SYN packets to exhaust server connection tables.
- **UDP Flood** — Bots flood target with UDP packets.
- **HTTP Flood (L7)** — Bots send legitimate-looking GET/POST requests to exhaust application threads.
- **Amplification (DNS/NTP)** — Bots spoof source IP and request large responses from open resolvers.

**Role in Spam Campaigns:**
- Bots send millions of phishing emails daily using the victim's IP reputation.
- Affiliate programs (e.g., **Cutwail** botnet) rent botnet capacity to spammers.

**Role in Credential Stuffing:**
- Bots automate login attempts across thousands of sites using leaked credential databases (combo lists).
- **Mirai variant Okiru** targeted IoT devices for credential stuffing.

**Two Real-World Botnet Case Studies:**

1. **Mirai Botnet (2016)** — Infected ~600,000 IoT devices (CCTV cameras, routers) using default credentials. Launched a **1.1 Tbps DDoS attack** against Dyn DNS, taking down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub. Source code was leaked, spawning countless variants.

2. **Emotet Botnet (2014-2021)** — Initially a banking Trojan, evolved into a polymorphic loader. Used Office macros and OneNote attachments. Distributed Ryuk and Conti ransomware as secondary payloads. Taken down in January 2021 by Europol, but resurfaced briefly in late 2021.

---

#### Part (b) — Polymorphic vs Metamorphic Malware

| Parameter | Polymorphic | Metamorphic |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Logic** | Identical across variants | Logically equivalent but rewritten |
| **Signature Change** | Yes (different hash per variant) | Yes (different hash and structure) |
| **Mechanism** | Encryption with variable key + decryption stub | Code rewriting (instruction substitution, register swap, dead code) |
| **Size Variation** | Yes (varies with padding) | Often constant (rewriting preserves size) |
| **Detection Difficulty** | High (signatures change) | Extreme (structure changes too) |
| **Examples** | Storm Worm, Virut | ZMist, Simile, Evulgrape |

**Why Signature-Based Antivirus Fails:**

1. **Hash-based signatures are deterministic.** AV vendors compute SHA-256 / MD5 of known malware binaries. If the hash changes (as in polymorphic/metamorphic), the signature misses the new sample.

2. **Heuristic-based signatures detect patterns.** Polymorphic samples with proper encryption and clean decryption stubs produce no recognizable pattern. Metamorphic samples with full code rewriting defeat even opcode-level heuristics.

3. **Sample-count explosion.** A polymorphic engine producing $K^n$ variants exhausts the AV vendor's signature database within days.

**Required Defenses:**
- **Behavioral Analysis (Sandboxing)** — Execute the sample in an isolated VM and observe runtime behavior (file writes, registry edits, network calls).
- **EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response)** — Telemetry-based detection using Sysmon, ETW, and API hooking.
- **Machine Learning Classifiers** — Train on static + dynamic features (PE imports, API call sequences, string entropy).
- **YARA Rules** — Pattern-based hunting on memory and disk artifacts rather than file hashes.
- **Memory Forensics (Volatility)** — Detect code injection, hollow processes, and rootkit hooks regardless of file signature.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Students often describe polymorphic and metamorphic as the same. **The distinction is critical** — polymorphic uses encryption with a fixed decryptor, while metamorphic rewrites its own code entirely. Examiners specifically test this differentiation for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Malware Types**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **Malware** = any code intentionally designed for harm.
- **Virus** = needs host file + user action.
- **Worm** = self-replicates over network, autonomous.
- **Trojan** = social engineering, no self-replication.
- **Ransomware** = hybrid crypto (AES-256 + RSA-2048).
- **Rootkit** = kernel/firmware-level stealth.
- **Fileless** = memory-only via LOLBins.
- **Bot** = zombie under C2 control.
- **Logic Bomb** = triggered by condition/time.
- **Polymorphic** = encrypted with variable key.
- **Metamorphic** = code rewriting each generation.

### 🔑 Must-Know Formulas
- **SI Worm Model:** $I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{\beta N t}$
- **Logistic Variant:** $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta I(N-I) - \mu I$
- **Variant Count:** $N_{\text{variants}} = K^n$
- **AES-256 Brute Force:** $2^{256} \approx 1.15 \times 10^{77}$

### 🔑 Must-Know Case Studies
- **Code Red (2001)** — IIS worm, 359,000 hosts in 14 hours.
- **Stuxnet (2010)** — Multi-module worm targeting Iranian uranium centrifuges; first cyberweapon.
- **WannaCry (2017)** — EternalBlue SMB exploit, 230,000 hosts in 24 hours.
- **Mirai (2016)** — IoT botnet, 1.1 Tbps DDoS on Dyn.
- **NotPetya (2017)** — Wiper disguised as ransomware, $10B+ global damage.
- **SolarWinds SUNBURST (2020)** — Supply chain compromise of Orion platform.

### 🔑 Must-Know Forensics Tools
- **Volatility** — Memory forensics framework.
- **Sysmon** — Windows telemetry enhancement.
- **Wireshark** — Network packet analysis.
- **YARA** — Pattern-based malware hunting.
- **PEiD / DIE** — Packer / compiler detection.
- **FTK Imager / WinPMEM** — Disk and memory acquisition.
- **Ghidra / IDA Pro** — Disassembly and reverse engineering.
- **CAPA** — Automated malware capability analysis.

### 🔑 Must-Know Forensic Artifacts
- **Prefetch** (`C:\Windows\Prefetch`) — program execution history.
- **Registry Run Keys** — persistence indicators.
- **Event Logs** (4624, 4625, 4688, 4104) — authentication and process telemetry.
- **AmCache / ShimCache** — program execution evidence.
- **Scheduled Tasks XML** — persistence mechanism.
- **WMI Repository** — fileless persistence storage.
- **SRUM Database** — network and process resource usage history.

### 🔑 Key Differentiators (Frequently Asked)
| Comparison | Key Distinction |
|---|---|
| Virus vs Worm | Worm is autonomous; virus needs host |
| Polymorphic vs Metamorphic | Polymorphic encrypts; Metamorphic rewrites |
| Rootkit vs Trojan | Rootkit hides; Trojan delivers payload |
| Fileless vs Traditional | Fileless has no on-disk artifact |
| Ransomware Locker vs Crypto | Locker locks screen; Crypto encrypts files |
| Worm vs Botnet | Worm self-spreads; Botnet awaits C2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love the **Comparison Table** format. Memorize the master comparison table from Section 2.2 — it alone can answer 8-10 marks of any module-related question on malware types.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Malware Analysis - Static Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Malware Analysis - Static Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Types of Malware Analysis — Formal Definition

In the context of KTU's **PECCT741 – Malware Forensics** syllabus, *Malware Analysis* is defined as the systematic art and science of dissecting malicious software (malware) to understand its origin, functionality, behavior, persistence mechanisms, and Indicators of Compromise (IOCs). This process equips a forensic investigator with actionable threat intelligence to develop robust defensive countermeasures, signatures, and eradication strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Malware Analysis is the process of determining the purpose, functionality, origin, and potential impact of a given malware sample by examining its code, structure, and runtime behavior in a controlled environment.

The discipline is broadly classified into **four canonical types** based on the depth of inspection and the operational state of the malware sample:

1. **Static Analysis (Code Analysis / Static Property Analysis):**
   Examining the binary file *without executing it*. Investigators inspect raw file properties, metadata, embedded strings, cryptographic hashes, and disassembled code logic to derive threat insights passively.

2. **Dynamic Analysis (Behavioral Analysis):**
   Executing the malware inside an isolated, instrumented sandbox to observe live system calls, registry modifications, network traffic, and file-system tampering.

3. **Hybrid Analysis:**
   A fusion of both static and dynamic approaches, correlating static indicators with live behavioral telemetry to obtain comprehensive coverage.

4. **Memory Forensics (Volatile Analysis):**
   Inspecting the live memory (RAM) dump of an infected system to uncover rootkits, fileless malware, and process injection artifacts that evade on-disk static inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 Focus (KTU 2024):** The first module heavily emphasizes *Basic* and *Advanced Static Analysis* as they form the non-destructive, safe first line of forensic triage.

---

### 1.2 What is Static Analysis? — The Foundational Pillar

**Static Analysis** is the forensic procedure of inspecting, evaluating, and dissecting a malicious executable *without launching or executing the malicious code*. It is conducted by examining the file's structural properties, embedded metadata, ASCII/Unicode strings, cryptographic fingerprints, and assembly-level logic via disassembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Static Analysis):** A non-execution-based malware dissection technique that derives functional understanding by inspecting the binary's structure, signatures, metadata, strings, and code patterns through tools like disassemblers, hex editors, and packer detectors.

Static analysis is further bifurcated into two progressive sub-layers within the KTU syllabus:

- **Basic Static Analysis (Level 1):** Surface-level triage. It involves file fingerprinting, hashing, string extraction, header parsing, and packer detection — essentially answering *"What does the file look like?"*
- **Advanced Static Analysis (Level 2):** Deep code-level reverse engineering using disassemblers (IDA Pro, Ghidra) to reconstruct the malware's programmatic logic flow — answering *"What is the code *trying* to do?"*

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dead Body Autopsy" Approach

Imagine a forensic detective arriving at a crime scene with a sealed, potentially explosive briefcase. The briefcase is locked, ticking, and clearly dangerous.

> **Option A (Dynamic Analysis):** Open the briefcase in a reinforced blast chamber, attach sensors, and observe what springs out. This gives you real-time behavior, but it's *risky* and *resource-intensive*.
>
> **Option B (Static Analysis):** Pick up the briefcase. Photograph its exterior (metadata). Scan it with an X-ray machine (hex editor / PE parser). Read any labels, serial numbers, or printed notes on its surface (strings). Use a CT scan to see the internal gears and wires without opening it (disassembly). Then, based on these observations, deduce its mechanism, origin, and intent — *without ever opening the lock*.

Static analysis is the **"X-ray and CT-scan"** phase of digital forensics. It is **safe**, **repeatable**, **non-destructive**, and **executable on air-gapped systems**, making it the indispensable *first step* in any professional malware triage workflow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Static Analysis First?**
> - **Safety:** Zero risk of accidental execution or infection.
> - **Speed:** Provides rapid initial triage (in seconds) before committing to slow sandbox detonation.
> - **Universality:** Works even on dormant, logic-bomb, or time-triggered malware that may *refuse* to execute in a sandbox.
> - **Signature Generation:** Critical for antivirus (AV) and Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) signature creation.

---

### 1.4 Standardized Metrics & Constants in Static Analysis

Certain standard cryptographic and structural metrics are foundational to static analysis and must be memorized:

| Metric | Description | Standard Algorithm |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| **MD5 Hash** | 128-bit cryptographic fingerprint | **MD5 (Message-Digest Algorithm 5)** |
| **SHA-1 Hash** | 160-bit cryptographic fingerprint | **SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm 1)** |
| **SHA-256 Hash** | 256-bit cryptographic fingerprint | **SHA-256 (part of SHA-2 family)** |
| **Magic Number / File Signature** | First few bytes identifying file type | E.g., `4D 5A` ("MZ") for PE |
| **Entropy Threshold** | Indicates packing/encryption if **> 7.0** | Shannon Entropy (log₂ scale) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always quote file signatures in hexadecimal notation. The `MZ` signature (Mark Zbikowski) is the most frequently asked header detail for Windows PE files.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Static Analysis Workflow

The static analysis methodology is best understood as a progressive, layered funnel — moving from the most superficial indicators (outermost shell) to the deepest code-level logic (innermost core).

#### **Layer 1 — File Fingerprinting (Identification Phase)**

Before touching the binary, the forensic examiner computes deterministic **cryptographic hashes** that uniquely identify the file. Even a single-bit change in the binary produces a completely different hash (the *Avalanche Effect*).

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Acquire the suspect binary in a forensically sound manner (chain of custody).
2. Compute **MD5** and **SHA-256** hashes to uniquely tag the file.
3. Cross-reference the hash with public threat intelligence repositories (VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, AlienVault OTX).
4. If a match is found, prior analyses from the community can be reused — a massive time-saver.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Hashing:** Malware authors often use *polymorphic engines* or *server-side payload generators* to ensure that two victims receive a binary with a different hash. Thus, a single hash match is rare in modern campaigns; analysts rely on fuzzy hashing (ssdeep, sdhash) to find *similar* files.

---

#### **Layer 2 — File Header & Format Inspection**

Every well-formed executable follows a rigid structural grammar. Inspecting this grammar reveals the file's true nature, intended platform, and compiler origin.

For **Windows PE (Portable Executable) Files**, the structure is:
1. **DOS Header** — Starts with the `MZ` magic bytes (`0x4D 0x5A`). Contains a stub for DOS compatibility.
2. **DOS Stub** — A tiny program that prints *"This program cannot be run in DOS mode."*
3. **PE Signature** — The literal string `"PE\0\0"` (`0x50 0x45 0x00 0x00`) marking the start of the PE Header.
4. **COFF Header (File Header)** — Defines the *machine type* (e.g., `0x014C` for i386, `0x8664` for x64), number of sections, and timestamp.
5. **Optional Header** — Critical forensic fields: `AddressOfEntryPoint`, `ImageBase`, `Subsystem`, `DllCharacteristics`, and the **Data Directories** (Import, Export, Resource, TLS, Relocation).
6. **Section Table** — Defines the file's logical segments (`.text`, `.data`, `.rdata`, `.rsrc`).
7. **Actual Section Data** — The raw code, data, resources, and relocation tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Red Flag in Section Table:** If the sum of `SizeOfRawData` for all sections is significantly *smaller* than the total file size, the file is almost certainly **packed** (compressed/encrypted). Packing is the #1 obfuscation technique used by malware authors to evade AV signature matching.

---

#### **Layer 3 — String Analysis (The "Low-Hanging Fruit" Phase)**

Strings are human-readable ASCII or Unicode (UTF-16LE) character sequences embedded in the binary. They are gold mines for the analyst because they often reveal:

- **URLs and IP addresses** → C2 (Command & Control) server locations
- **Registry keys** → Persistence mechanisms
- **File paths** → Drop locations for secondary payloads
- **DLL names and API calls** → Imported functions revealing malware capabilities
- **Error messages and dialog text** → Developer fingerprints
- **Mutex names** → Synchronization markers used for single-instance execution

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Extract strings using tools like `strings` (Sysinternals), `FLOSS` (FireEye Labs Obfuscated String Solver), or `BinText`.
2. Filter for high-value patterns: URLs, IPs, registry paths (`HKLM\`, `HKCU\`), `cmd.exe`, `powershell`, `http://`, `https://`.
3. Cross-reference URLs and IPs with threat intel feeds.
4. Note any unusual or attacker-handle-like strings (developer signatures).

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Analysis Counter:** Modern malware uses *string encryption* (XOR, AES) to hide these artifacts. The **FLOSS** tool automatically extracts XOR-encoded and stack-resident strings that manual `strings` misses.

---

#### **Layer 4 — Packer & Obfuscation Detection (Defeating the Wrapper)**

A **packer** is a tool that compresses or encrypts an executable's code, then prepends a small *unpacker stub* that decompresses the original code in memory at runtime. Common packers include **UPX**, **Themida**, **VMProtect**, **ASPack**, and **MPRESS**.

**Detection Techniques:**
- **Entropy Analysis:** Shannon entropy approaching **8.0** (maximum for 8-bit data) signals encryption/compression.
- **Section Name Anomalies:** Names like `UPX0`, `UPX1`, `.aspack`, `.vmp` directly identify the packer.
- **Import Table Collapse:** A packed binary has *very few* imports (often just `LoadLibrary` and `GetProcAddress`), since the real imports are resolved at runtime.
- **PEiD, Exeinfo PE, Detect It Easy (DIE):** Automated packer-identification tools.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Run the file through `Detect It Easy` or `PEiD` to fingerprint the packer.
2. Check the entropy of each section using a hex editor or `pefile` library.
3. If UPX is detected, attempt a clean unpack: `upx -d sample.exe -o unpacked.exe`.
4. If a custom/unknown packer is used, proceed to **dynamic analysis** to capture the unpacked payload in memory.

---

#### **Layer 5 — Disassembly & Code-Level Reverse Engineering**

This is the **Advanced Static Analysis** tier. The binary is translated from machine code (opcodes) into human-readable **assembly language** using a disassembler. The analyst then reconstructs the program's control flow (functions, loops, conditional branches) and infers its logic.

**Key Tools:**
- **IDA Pro** (Commercial gold-standard) — with the decompiler plugin for pseudo-C output.
- **Ghidra** (Free, NSA-developed) — includes a powerful built-in decompiler.
- **Radare2 / Cutter** (Open-source) — popular in CTF and academic settings.

**What to look for in disassembly:**
- **API Call Patterns:** Calls to `InternetOpenA`, `InternetConnect`, `HttpSendRequest` → network capability.
- **Cryptographic Constants:** The constants `0x67452301` and `0x98BADCFE` are the initial values of **MD5's** state buffer — their presence indicates MD5 usage.
- **Anti-Debug Checks:** Calls to `IsDebuggerPresent`, `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess`.
- **Hardcoded Keys & Configs:** Decryption keys, C2 ports, and encryption modes (e.g., `0x20` for RC4).

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — The "Cheat Sheet"

This table summarizes all critical formulas, structures, and threshold values relevant to KTU Module 1 examination questions on Static Analysis.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Value** | **Unit / Notation** | **Forensic Significance** |
|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| MD5 Hash Size | $128 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ bytes}$ | Hex digest: 32 chars | Fast file identification |
| SHA-1 Hash Size | $160 \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ bytes}$ | Hex digest: 40 chars | Stronger collision resistance |
| SHA-256 Hash Size | $256 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ bytes}$ | Hex digest: 64 chars | Modern standard for IOC tagging |
| Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_{2} p(x_i)$ | Unitless, in **bits/symbol** | $> 7.0$ = packed/encrypted |
| Entropy Max (8-bit) | $H_{max} = 8.0$ | bits/symbol | Perfectly random / encrypted |
| PE Magic | `0x5A4D` ("MZ") | Hex | Identifies Windows PE files |
| PE Signature | `0x00004550` ("PE\0\0") | Hex (little-endian) | Start of PE Header |
| ELF Magic | `0x7F 45 4C 46` ("\x7FELF") | Hex | Identifies Linux ELF files |
| i386 Machine Code | `0x014C` | Hex (PE COFF) | 32-bit Intel x86 |
| x86_64 Machine Code | `0x8664` | Hex (PE COFF) | 64-bit Intel/AMD |
| Imports of Unpacked PE | 50 – 500+ functions | Count | Normal complex binary |
| Imports of Packed PE | 3 – 15 functions | Count | Suspicious; runtime resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule on Pipes in Tables:** All absolute-value and conditional notation in formulas uses `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity. Memorize the **Shannon Entropy** formula and the **7.0 entropy threshold** — these are KTU-favorite exam questions.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Static analysis is not merely an academic exercise — it is the backbone of:

1. **Antivirus (AV) Signature Engineering:** YARA rules, ClamAV signatures, and Microsoft Defender definitions are all born from static analysis of malware samples.
2. **Incident Response (IR) Triage:** First responders in a SOC (Security Operations Center) hash suspected files and cross-reference static indicators within seconds.
3. **Software Supply Chain Security:** Tools like *VirusTotal*, *ReversingLabs*, and *Intezer Analyze* use static indicators to attribute malware to specific threat-actor groups (APT labeling).
4. **Digital Forensics & e-Discovery:** Court-admissible forensic reports rely heavily on hash values to prove evidence integrity (chain of custody).
5. **Malware Family Classification:** The PE header, import table, and string patterns are used by ML classifiers to categorize malware into families (e.g., Emotet, TrickBot, Conti).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shannon Entropy for Packer Detection

The cornerstone metric for identifying packed or encrypted executables is **Shannon Entropy**, introduced by Claude Shannon in his foundational 1948 paper *"A Mathematical Theory of Communication."* It quantifies the *information density* or *randomness* of a data sequence.

**Step 1 — Define the Probability Mass Function:**

For a byte stream $B$ of length $N$, count the frequency of every possible byte value $b \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$.

The probability of occurrence of byte value $b$ is:

$$
p(b) = \frac{f(b)}{N}
$$

where $f(b)$ is the absolute frequency of byte $b$, and $N$ is the total byte count. The sum of all probabilities must equal **1**:

$$
\sum_{b=0}^{255} p(b) = 1
$$

**Step 2 — State the Shannon Entropy Formula:**

$$
H(B) = - \sum_{b=0}^{255} p(b) \cdot \log_{2} p(b)
$$

The negative sign ensures that entropy is non-negative (since $0 < p(b) \leq 1$ implies $\log_2 p(b) \leq 0$).

**Step 3 — Determine the Maximum Possible Entropy:**

Maximum entropy occurs when all byte values are **equally likely** — i.e., $p(b) = \frac{1}{256}$ for every $b$.

$$
H_{max} = - \sum_{b=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \cdot \log_{2} \left( \frac{1}{256} \right)
$$

$$
H_{max} = - \sum_{b=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \cdot (-8)
$$

$$
H_{max} = 8 \cdot \sum_{b=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} = 8 \cdot 1 = 8.0 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 4 — Establish the Forensic Decision Threshold:**

Based on empirical analysis of thousands of malware samples, the community has established the following rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(B) < 5.0 & \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Unpacked, low obfuscation (plain code or data)} \\
5.0 \leq H(B) < 6.5 & \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lightly packed or partially obfuscated} \\
6.5 \leq H(B) \leq 7.0 & \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Strong packing or compression} \\
H(B) > 7.0 & \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Encrypted, custom packed, or highly randomized}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example:** Consider a binary section of $N = 1024$ bytes. The byte distribution is:
> - Byte `0x00` appears **400 times** → $p(0x00) = 400/1024 = 0.3906$
> - Byte `0x41` ('A') appears **200 times** → $p(0x41) = 200/1024 = 0.1953$
> - All other 254 byte values appear **2 times each** → $p = 2/1024 = 0.001953$ each
>
> Entropy calculation:
> $$
> H = - \left[ 0.3906 \log_2(0.3906) + 0.1953 \log_2(0.1953) + 254 \times 0.001953 \log_2(0.001953) \right]
> $$
> $$
> H = - [0.3906 \times (-1.357) + 0.1953 \times (-2.357) + 254 \times 0.001953 \times (-9.000)]
> $$
> $$
> H = - [-0.530 + (-0.460) + (-4.460)] = 5.45 \text{ bits/symbol}
> $$
> **Conclusion:** Entropy of **5.45** suggests *light packing* — a typical signature for a UPX-compressed binary.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Static Analysis Procedure

The following exhaustive matrix walks through the canonical KTU board-exam answer for "Explain the procedure of Basic Static Analysis of malware."

| **Step** | **Action** | **Tool(s) Used** | **Expected Output / Decision** |
|----------|------------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Acquire the sample in a forensically sound manner; document the chain of custody. | Forensic write-blocker, hash verification | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 digests |
| 2 | Compute cryptographic hashes to uniquely identify the file. | `md5sum`, `sha256sum`, `hashcalc` | 32-char MD5, 64-char SHA-256 strings |
| 3 | Cross-reference hashes against threat intelligence. | VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, ThreatFox | Match → reuse community analysis; No match → proceed manually |
| 4 | Inspect file type using magic-byte signature. | `file` command, hex editor, TrID | Confirms "PE32 executable", "ELF 64-bit", "PDF", etc. |
| 5 | Parse the PE/ELF header for structural anomalies. | `pefile` (Python), `CFF Explorer`, `PE-bear` | Machine type, entry point, subsystem, timestamp |
| 6 | Compute entropy of every section. | `pefile`, `Detect It Easy` | Sections with $H > 7.0$ flagged as packed |
| 7 | Identify the packer (if any). | DIE, PEiD, Exeinfo PE | "UPX 3.96", "Themida 2.x", etc. |
| 8 | Attempt unpacking (if packer is known/standard). | `upx -d`, manual unpacking | Restores the original PE for deeper inspection |
| 9 | Extract embedded strings. | `strings`, `FLOSS`, `BinText` | URLs, IPs, registry keys, file paths, API names |
| 10 | Analyze the Import Address Table (IAT). | `pefile`, `CFF Explorer`, `Dependency Walker` | Reveals capabilities: networking, crypto, process injection |
| 11 | Look for embedded resources. | `Resource Hacker`, CFF Explorer | Icons, version info, hidden payloads, config blobs |
| 12 | Inspect overlay data (bytes after the last PE section). | Hex editor, `pefile` | Frequently contains second-stage payloads, encrypted configs |
| 13 | Check for digital signature anomalies. | `sigcheck`, `osslsigncode` | Unsigned or invalid signatures are highly suspicious |
| 14 | Generate a comprehensive IOC list. | Manual compilation, MISP, OpenCTI | Hashes, IPs, domains, registry keys, mutexes |
| 15 | Document findings in a forensic report. | Editor, case-management system | Court-admissible report with timeline and IOCs |

---

### 3.3 Conceptual Derivation: Why Hashing is Deterministic

A cryptographic hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ must satisfy three properties to be useful in static analysis:

1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given $h$, finding $m$ such that $H(m) = h$ is computationally infeasible.
2. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given $m_1$, finding $m_2 \neq m_1$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is infeasible.
3. **Collision Resistance:** Finding *any* $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is infeasible.

The **Avalanche Effect** states that flipping a single bit in the input should change approximately **50%** of the output bits:

$$
\forall i \in \{1, \dots, n\},\;\; P\bigl[ H(m')_i \neq H(m)_i \bigr] \approx 0.5
$$

where $m'$ is $m$ with exactly one bit flipped. This is why even a one-byte change in malware produces a brand-new hash, making precise identification possible while frustrating signature-based AV (which is why polymorphic malware exists).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MD5 is Broken for Collisions:** In 2004, researchers demonstrated practical MD5 collisions in under an hour. Malware authors can craft two binaries with *different malicious behavior* but the *same MD5 hash*. KTU exams may ask why SHA-256 is preferred over MD5 in modern forensic workflows.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Static Analysis. List any four tools used for performing basic static analysis of a malware sample.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Static Analysis** is the technique of examining a malicious binary *without executing it* in order to determine its functionality, origin, and potential impact. It involves inspecting the file's metadata, structural properties, embedded strings, and assembly-level code through tools like disassemblers and hex editors.
>
> **Four Basic Static Analysis Tools (1/2 mark each):**
> 1. **`strings`** — Extracts ASCII and Unicode character sequences from the binary.
> 2. **`md5sum` / `sha256sum`** — Generates cryptographic hash digests for file identification.
> 3. **`PEiD` / `Detect It Easy (DIE)`** — Detects packers, compilers, and cryptors used on the binary.
> 4. **`CFF Explorer` / `PE-bear`** — Parses and visualizes the PE (Portable Executable) file structure.
> 5. *(Any other valid: `BinText`, `Resource Hacker`, `Exeinfo PE`)*

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Correct definition: **1 Mark**
- Four valid tools (1/2 each): **2 Marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a cryptographic hash? Why is SHA-256 preferred over MD5 in modern malware forensics?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **cryptographic hash** is a one-way mathematical function that converts an input of arbitrary length into a fixed-size, unique output called a *digest* or *fingerprint*. It is deterministic, meaning the same input always produces the same output.
>
> **SHA-256 is preferred over MD5 because:**
> 1. **MD5 produces only 128-bit digests**, which are vulnerable to practical collision attacks (demonstrated by Wang et al. in 2004). Malware authors can craft two different binaries with the same MD5 hash, defeating integrity verification.
> 2. **SHA-256 produces 256-bit digests** with vastly stronger collision and pre-image resistance, making it computationally infeasible to forge duplicates.
> 3. Modern threat intelligence platforms (VirusTotal, MISP) and government standards (NIST) mandate SHA-256 for IOC sharing.
> 4. SHA-256 follows a more secure Merkle–Damgård construction with stronger avalanche properties.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of cryptographic hash: **1 Mark**
- Two technical reasons for SHA-256 preference: **2 Marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **PE (Portable Executable) file format** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the role of the **DOS Header**, **PE Header**, **Optional Header**, and **Section Table**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **different types of malware analysis**. Compare and contrast **static and dynamic analysis** with at least four distinguishing parameters. (7 Marks)

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — PE File Format (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The **Portable Executable (PE)** format is the standard file format for executables, DLLs, object code, and FON font files on 32-bit and 64-bit Windows operating systems. It is a data structure that encapsulates all information required by the Windows loader to manage the wrapped executable code.

**1. DOS Header (1.5 Marks):**
- The DOS Header is the first structure in the file, occupying the first **64 bytes**.
- It begins with the **magic number `MZ` (0x5A4D)**, referring to Mark Zbikowski, one of the original architects of MS-DOS.
- The most important field is `e_lfanew` at offset **0x3C**, which is a 4-byte pointer to the beginning of the **PE Signature**.
- Its purpose in modern Windows is largely historical — it provides backward compatibility so that the file is recognized as a valid PE even if accidentally executed in DOS.

**2. DOS Stub (0.5 Mark):**
- A small DOS program that follows the DOS Header.
- By default, it prints the message: *"This program cannot be run in DOS mode."* and then terminates.

**3. PE Signature (0.5 Mark):**
- Located at the offset pointed to by `e_lfanew`.
- Contains the 4-byte signature `PE\0\0` (hex `0x50 0x45 0x00 0x00`).
- Immediately marks the start of the **COFF File Header**.

**4. COFF (Common Object File Format) Header (1 Mark):**
- 20-byte structure containing metadata:
  - `Machine` (e.g., `0x014C` = i386, `0x8664` = x86-64)
  - `NumberOfSections` — total sections in the file
  - `TimeDateStamp` — Unix epoch compilation time (a key forensic artifact)
  - `PointerToSymbolTable` and `NumberOfSymbols` — debug info
  - `SizeOfOptionalHeader` — size of the next structure

**5. Optional Header (2 Marks):**
- Despite the name, this is a *required* structure for executable images (not for object files).
- Begins with the `Magic` field: `0x10B` (PE32 / 32-bit) or `0x20B` (PE32+ / 64-bit).
- **Key fields for static analysis:**
  - `AddressOfEntryPoint` (RVA) — first instruction executed; a redirect here often signals packers.
  - `ImageBase` — preferred load address.
  - `Subsystem` (e.g., `2` = GUI, `3` = Console).
  - `DllCharacteristics` — security flags (e.g., ASLR, DEP, CFG).
  - `DataDirectory[16]` — array pointing to the **Import Table**, **Export Table**, **Resource Directory**, **TLS Directory**, and **Relocation Table**.

**6. Section Table + Section Data (1.5 Marks):**
- The Section Table immediately follows the Optional Header and contains one 40-byte `IMAGE_SECTION_HEADER` per section.
- Common section names:
  - `.text` — executable code
  - `.data` — initialized global variables
  - `.rdata` — read-only data and import/export tables
  - `.rsrc` — embedded resources (icons, dialogs, version info)
  - `.reloc` — relocation information
  - `.bss` — uninitialized data (no file footprint)
- Forensic anomaly: If the sum of `SizeOfRawData` is *less* than the total file size, the difference is the **overlay**, which often harbors a second-stage payload.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Malware Analysis Types & Static vs Dynamic (7 Marks)

**Types of Malware Analysis (3 Marks):**

1. **Static Analysis (Code Analysis):** Examines the binary without execution. Includes file fingerprinting, string extraction, header parsing, and disassembly.
2. **Dynamic Analysis (Behavioral Analysis):** Executes the malware in a sandbox to observe live behavior — file modifications, registry writes, network traffic.
3. **Memory Forensics:** Analyzes RAM dumps to detect fileless malware, rootkits, and process injection artifacts.
4. **Hybrid Analysis:** Combines static and dynamic techniques for comprehensive coverage.

**Comparison Table — Static vs Dynamic Analysis (4 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Static Analysis** | **Dynamic Analysis** |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Execution Required?** | No — binary is never run | Yes — binary is detonated in sandbox |
| **Safety** | Completely safe; no infection risk | Risk of escape; requires robust isolation |
| **Speed** | Fast — seconds to minutes for basic level | Slow — minutes to hours for full behavioral capture |
| **Packer Resistance** | Limited — packed/encrypted code is opaque | Strong — unpacked code is observed in memory |
| **Anti-Debug Bypass** | Difficult — cannot observe anti-debug triggers | Possible — but malware may detect the sandbox |
| **Depth of Insight** | High (advanced static gives full code logic) | Medium (only observed behaviors are captured) |
| **Artifacts Produced** | Hashes, strings, IOCs, control flow graphs | PCAPs, memory dumps, file/registry diffs |

**Conclusion:** Both approaches are complementary. Static analysis is the safe, fast, mandatory first step; dynamic analysis is the necessary follow-up for heavily obfuscated or logic-bombed samples.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** What is **packing** in malware? Explain the **role of entropy** in detecting packed malware. Compute the entropy for a hypothetical section with the byte distribution given below and interpret the result. (7 Marks)

| Byte | Frequency |
|------|-----------|
| 0x00 | 512 |
| 0x90 | 256 |
| 0xCC | 256 |
| **Total** | **1024** |

**(b)** Describe the **importance of string analysis** in static malware analysis. With examples, discuss the categories of useful strings an analyst should look for, and explain how obfuscated strings can be recovered. (7 Marks)

---
