##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Packing & Entropy (7 Marks)

**Definition of Packing (2 Marks):**
**Packing** is an obfuscation technique where a malware author compresses or encrypts the original executable code using a *packer tool* (e.g., UPX, Themida, MPRESS, VMProtect). The original code is wrapped inside a small **unpacker stub** that decompresses/decrypts the payload into memory at runtime. Packing defeats signature-based AV by making every packed instance unique.

**Role of Entropy in Detection (2 Marks):**
Shannon entropy measures the randomness of bytes in a file section. Plain, un-packed code typically has **low entropy (3.0–5.0)** because it contains repeating patterns (instructions, ASCII strings, null padding). Encrypted or compressed data has **high entropy (7.0–8.0)** because compression removes redundancy and encryption produces uniformly random bytes. Thus, an unusually high section entropy is a strong forensic indicator of packing.

**Computation (3 Marks):**
Given byte frequencies in a 1024-byte section:
- $p(0x00) = \frac{512}{1024} = 0.5$
- $p(0x90) = \frac{256}{1024} = 0.25$
- $p(0xCC) = \frac{256}{1024} = 0.25$

Apply Shannon entropy:

$$
H = - \left[ 0.5 \log_2(0.5) + 0.25 \log_2(0.25) + 0.25 \log_2(0.25) \right]
$$

$$
H = - \left[ 0.5 \times (-1) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0.25 \times (-2) \right]
$$

$$
H = - \left[ -0.5 - 0.5 - 0.5 \right] = 1.5 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Interpretation:** Entropy of **1.5** is *very low*, indicating a *highly repetitive* structure — characteristic of code padding (NOPs `0x90`), INT3 breakpoints `0xCC`, or zero-filled regions. The section is **almost certainly NOT packed**; it is likely an uninitialized or debug-filled region.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — String Analysis (7 Marks)

**Importance of String Analysis (1 Mark):**
String analysis is the *single most cost-effective* static analysis technique. It requires no code execution, no disassembly, and provides immediate, high-fidelity IOCs. Strings are often the only artifacts that survive even moderate obfuscation, and they directly reveal attacker infrastructure, intent, and tradecraft.

**Categories of High-Value Strings (4 Marks):**

| **Category** | **Example Pattern** | **Forensic Value** |
|--------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Network Indicators** | `http://malicious-c2.xyz/beacon`, `192.168.1.100` | Identifies C2 server URLs and IPs |
| **Registry Keys** | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | Reveals persistence mechanisms |
| **File Paths** | `C:\Users\Public\payload.exe`, `%TEMP%\svchost.log` | Identifies drop locations |
| **API / DLL Names** | `kernel32.dll`, `CreateRemoteThread`, `InternetOpenA` | Reveals capabilities (injection, networking) |
| **Mutual Exclusion (Mutex)** | `Global\Emotet_8472` | Uniquely tags the malware family/variant |
| **Developer Artifacts** | `HACKED_BY_X`, `Build 0.3 - John` | Attribution and threat-actor fingerprinting |
| **Command Keywords** | `cmd.exe`, `powershell -enc`, `vssadmin delete shadows` | Reveals destructive or escalation commands |

**Recovering Obfuscated Strings (2 Marks):**
Modern malware encrypts strings with simple XOR keys or AES to defeat `strings` extraction. Recovery techniques include:

1. **FLOSS (FireEye Labs Obfuscated String Solver):** Automatically extracts XOR-encoded, stack-resident, and runtime-decoded strings by emulating common decoding routines.
2. **XOR Brute-Force:** The analyst scans for repeating single-byte XOR patterns (e.g., repeated `\xAA` byte) and tests all 256 possible keys to decode hidden URLs.
3. **Disassembly Inspection:** In IDA Pro / Ghidra, locating `xor` instructions followed by `push` operations often reveals decoded strings just before they are referenced.
4. **Memory Dump Cross-Reference:** When a sample is detonated in a sandbox, decrypted strings appear in process memory; tools like **Volatility** with the `yarascan` or `memdump` plugins can extract them.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Static Analysis Questions):**
>
> 1. **Conflating hashing with encryption:** Students often describe MD5/SHA-256 as "encryption" — they are *one-way hash digests*, not reversible encryption. Loss: **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the `MZ` magic:** PE file questions almost always require explicit mention of the `MZ` (0x5A4D) magic bytes. Loss: **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Omitting the entropy threshold value:** Saying "high entropy indicates packing" without quoting the **7.0** threshold is incomplete. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing Basic vs Advanced Static Analysis:** Basic = *no code reading*; Advanced = *disassembly-level code analysis*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Writing a string example without its forensic implication:** Listing `http://...` is incomplete; you must state *why* it matters (e.g., "this URL is a C2 beacon URL"). Loss: **0.5 mark per example**.
> 6. **Skipping the Comparison Table in Q.A(b):** In static-vs-dynamic questions, a tabular comparison is a *valuation expectation* — prose-only answers lose **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Analysis (Module 1):**

- ☐ **Malware Analysis** is the systematic dissection of malicious code; it is the foundation of digital forensic investigation.
- ☐ The **four canonical types** are: Static, Dynamic, Memory Forensics, and Hybrid.
- ☐ **Static Analysis = No Execution**; it is safe, fast, and the mandatory first step.
- ☐ **Basic Static Analysis** includes hashing, file type identification, header parsing, string extraction, and packer detection.
- ☐ **Advanced Static Analysis** involves disassembly, decompilation, and control-flow reconstruction using **IDA Pro** or **Ghidra**.
- ☐ **MD5** = 128-bit digest; **SHA-1** = 160-bit; **SHA-256** = 256-bit — SHA-256 is the modern forensic standard.
- ☐ The **PE file magic** is `MZ` (`0x5A4D`) followed by the PE signature `0x00004550`.
- ☐ **Shannon Entropy** is computed using $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$; the **packing threshold is 7.0**.
- ☐ **String Analysis** reveals URLs, IPs, registry keys, file paths, mutexes, and API calls — the highest-yield static technique.
- ☐ **Packers** (UPX, Themida, VMProtect) compress/encrypt code; detection relies on entropy, anomalous section names, and collapsed import tables.
- ☐ **Fuzzy hashing** (ssdeep) is used for finding *similar* (not identical) malware variants in polymorphic campaigns.
- ☐ **Static IOCs** (hashes, domains, IPs, registry keys) feed directly into SIEM, IDS/IPS, and YARA rule generation.
- ☐ Always remember: Static analysis answers *"What is the file?"* and *"What does the code claim to do?"* — Dynamic analysis answers *"What does the file actually do at runtime?"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Analysis (Behavioural Analysis)** is the process of executing a suspicious program inside a controlled, isolated environment (commonly called a *sandbox* or *virtual machine*) and observing its real-time system-level behaviour, including process activity, file system changes, registry modifications, network communications, and memory operations, in order to identify malicious functionality and extract Indicators of Compromise (IoCs).

In the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), Dynamic Analysis is positioned as the **second pillar** of malware examination, complementing Static Analysis. While static analysis reads the binary without running it, dynamic analysis *detonates* the binary and records the consequences.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Detonation** | The act of executing the suspected malware inside the lab |
| **Sandbox** | An isolated, instrumented execution environment |
| **IoC** | Indicator of Compromise – an observable artefact left by malware |
| **Baseline** | A clean snapshot of the system state before execution, used for diffing |
| **Anti-VM / Anti-Sandbox** | Techniques malware uses to detect analysis environments |
| **Volatility** | The lifetime persistence of an artefact in memory/RAM |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Glass Jar" Method**
>
> Imagine you find a strange, sealed black box in your garden.
> - **Static Analysis** = Carefully weighing it, X-raying it, and reading the label *without opening it*. You infer what it is from its outer appearance.
> - **Dynamic Analysis** = Carefully placing the box inside a *sealed glass jar* (the sandbox), opening it, and watching what crawls out, what it eats, what it sprays, and what phone calls it makes — all without letting it escape into your house.
>
> The **glass jar** is your isolated VM. The **observation log** is your behavioural report. Any noise, smoke, or vibration you record = **IoCs**.

## 1.4 Why Dynamic Analysis is Necessary

- **Packed / Obfuscated Code:** Static analysis fails on UPX, Themida, or polymorphic crypters.
- **Runtime Behaviour:** Payloads often only reveal C2 (Command & Control) URLs at execution.
- **Encrypted Strings:** C2 domains and registry keys may be decrypted in memory only.
- **Persistence Mechanisms:** Scheduled tasks, services, and startup entries are only observable post-execution.
- **Validation:** Dynamic results cross-verify findings from static analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Dynamic Analysis

Dynamic analysis is a structured, multi-stage procedure. Every KTU board examiner expects the student to articulate these stages in correct order:

1. **Environment Preparation** – Configure an isolated VM (VMware Workstation, VirtualBox, KVM, or a dedicated physical host called a *goat host*). Snapshot the clean state.
2. **Network Isolation** – Disconnect from production networks; use an *internal fake network* (INetSim, FakeNet-NG) to mimic DNS/HTTP/FTP so malware believes it has connectivity.
3. **Instrumentation Setup** – Pre-install monitoring tools (Process Monitor, Process Explorer, Wireshark, Regshot, API Monitor, etc.) outside the malware execution path.
4. **Detonation** – Copy the suspicious sample into the VM and execute it. Trigger common user actions (e.g., opening a document, double-clicking) to stimulate macros or droppers.
5. **Observation & Capture** – Record file writes, registry edits, process spawns, DLL loads, mutexes, network packets, and memory dumps.
6. **Differential Comparison** – Compare the post-execution state against the baseline snapshot.
7. **Reporting** – Extract IoCs (hashes, IPs, domains, mutex names, registry keys, file paths).

## 2.2 Classification of Dynamic Analysis

Dynamic analysis is sub-divided into three principal types as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### (a) Manual Dynamic Analysis
The analyst interactively executes the sample while monitoring with native and third-party tools. High in-depth, low in scalability. Suited for targeted samples.

### (b) Automated Dynamic Analysis
The sample is submitted to an automated sandbox (Cuckoo Sandbox, Joe Sandbox, ANY.RUN, Hybrid Analysis, Cape Sandbox) which generates a behavioural report. High scalability, lower depth, easily evaded.

### (c) Hybrid Dynamic Analysis
Combines static + dynamic + memory forensics for maximum coverage — the modern industry standard.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Tool / Constant | Function / Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Snapshot frequency | **Δt** | Seconds between VM snapshots | Recommended: pre + post only |
| Observation window | **T_obs** | Minutes (5–30 typical) | Long for RATs, short for worms |
| Hashing algorithm | **MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256** | Bits of digest (128 / 160 / 256) | SHA-256 is KTU-preferred for IoC |
| Memory dump size | **M_dump** | MB (≈ 50 % of RAM usage) | Acquire **before** termination |
| Sample classification | **Benign / Grey / Malicious** | Categorical | Based on ≥ 3 matched IoCs |
| Cuckoo scoring | **score ∈ [0, 10]** | Unitless | ≥ 7.0 ⇒ strongly malicious |
| Network capture size | **PCAP** | MB to GB | Filter by malicious IP first |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility** — In real-world SOCs (Security Operations Centers) and CERTs, dynamic analysis underpins **threat intelligence feeds**, **YARA rule generation**, and **incident response playbooks**. It is the single most actionable technique when handling zero-day payloads.

## 2.4 The "Why / How" Behind Each Stage

- **Why isolate?** — Prevents accidental infection of the analyst's host or the production network.
- **Why baseline first?** — Removes false positives (Windows updates, scheduled tasks triggered by the clock).
- **Why stimulate user behaviour?** — Many samples (especially droppers inside Office documents) only execute malicious code upon a specific user event such as *enable content*.
- **Why use fake network services?** — Prevents the malware from contacting a real C2 server, which could lead to data exfiltration or tipping off the attacker.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for Manual Dynamic Analysis

> **Notation** — Let the *clean state* of the VM at time $t_0$ be $S_0$. The *execution* of the sample at time $t_1$ produces a new state $S_1$. The differential set $\Delta S$ contains all observable changes.

### Step 1 — Baseline Capture
Take a clean registry snapshot using **Regshot**:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{registry}} = \text{Registry}(S_1) - \text{Registry}(S_0)
$$

### Step 2 — Network Trace Recording
Open **Wireshark** in *promiscuous mode* on the VM's virtual NIC and start a capture. The total captured traffic is:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{pkt}_i \quad \text{where } N \text{ = total packet count}
$$

### Step 3 — Detonation
Execute the sample. If the sample is a **dropper** or **document-borne** payload, simulate the user opening it (e.g., `cmd /c malware.exe` or double-click in the VM GUI).

### Step 4 — Process & File Monitoring
Run **Process Monitor (ProcMon)** with filters set to:
- `Operation` is `CreateFile`
- `Operation` is `WriteFile`
- `Operation` is `RegSetValue`
- `Operation` is `TCP Connect`

Record the resulting event list $E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_k\}$.

### Step 5 — Differential Analysis
For each observed event $e_i$:

$$
\text{Malicious}(e_i) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } e_i \in \text{SignatureDB} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The **Composite Malicious Score (CMS)** of the sample is computed as:

$$
\text{CMS} = \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Malicious}(e_i)}{k} \times 10
$$

A sample is classified as **malicious** if:

$$
\text{CMS} \geq 7.0
$$

A sample is classified as **suspicious (greyware)** if:

$$
3.0 \leq \text{CMS} < 7.0
$$

A sample is classified as **benign** if:

$$
\text{CMS} < 3.0
$$

### Step 6 — Memory Acquisition
Use **DumpIt**, **WinPMEM**, or **Magnet RAM Capture** to dump volatile memory. The dump size is approximately:

$$
M_{\text{dump}} \approx \text{RAM}_{\text{used}} \times 1.05
$$

The 5 % overhead accounts for **memory management metadata**.

### Step 7 — IOC Extraction
Construct the IoC tuple:

$$
\text{IoC} = \left\{ H_{\text{sha256}},\ \text{IPs},\ \text{Domains},\ \text{FilePaths},\ \text{Mutexes},\ \text{RegKeys} \right\}
$$

Where $H_{\text{sha256}}$ is the **256-bit SHA-256 digest** of the sample.

### Step 8 — Reporting
Produce a structured report mapping each MITRE ATT&CK technique observed to its **TID** (Technique ID), e.g., `T1059.003` for Windows Command Shell.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tool vs Observation

| Observable | Tool | Output Format |
|---|---|---|
| Process creation | Process Monitor, Process Explorer | CSV / Log |
| File system writes | ProcMon, Regshot | CSV |
| Registry modifications | Regshot, ProcMon | `.hiv` diff |
| Network traffic | Wireshark, tcpdump | `.pcap` |
| API calls | API Monitor, NtTrace | Log |
| Memory contents | DumpIt, FTK Imager | `.raw` / `.dmp` |
| Persistence | Autoruns, Task Scheduler view | TXT |
| Behavioural report | Cuckoo, Cape | HTML / JSON |

## 3.3 Anti-Analysis Detection — A Worked Example

Modern malware checks for a virtual environment using the following heuristic. The analyst can detect the counter-detection by looking for the API calls in a sandbox log.

$$
\text{IsVM} = \bigvee_{i=1}^{m} \text{Check}_i
$$

Where $\text{Check}_i$ returns true if:

- The **CPUID** hypervisor-present bit (bit 31 of ECX) is set,
- The MAC address prefix belongs to VMware / VirtualBox OUI,
- The `SbieDll.dll` (Sandboxie) is loaded into the process,
- The disk size is a small round number (e.g., exactly 60 GB),
- The username is `sandbox`, `maltest`, or `virus`.

A clever analyst will patch these checks before detonation to bypass the anti-VM logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Dynamic Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspicious Sample Acquired] --> B[Hash Computed SHA-256]
    B --> C{VirusTotal Lookup}
    C -- Known Malicious --> D[Skip to Reporting]
    C -- Unknown --> E[Spawn Isolated VM]
    E --> F[Take Clean Snapshot S0]
    F --> G[Attach Monitoring Tools]
    G --> H[Detonate Sample in VM]
    H --> I[Stimulate User Actions]
    I --> J[Record ProcMon / Wireshark / Regshot]
    J --> K[Take Post Snapshot S1]
    K --> L[Compute Differential Delta S]
    L --> M{Diff Significant}
    M -- No --> N[Mark as Benign]
    M -- Yes --> O[Acquire Memory Dump]
    O --> P[Extract IOCs]
    P --> Q[Map to MITRE ATT&CK]
    Q --> R[Generate Final Report]
    D --> R
    R --> S[Archive Sample in Vault]
```

## 4.2 Sandbox Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HostOS["Analyst Host OS"]
        HV[Hypervisor VMware VirtualBox]
    end
    subgraph GuestVM["Isolated Guest VM"]
        OS[Windows Linux Sandbox OS]
        MON[Monitoring Agents]
        FNET[FakeNet NG INetSim]
    end
    subgraph Tooling["External Tooling"]
        PROC[Process Monitor]
        WIRESHARK[Wireshark]
        REGSHOT[Regshot]
        CUCKOO[Cuckoo Sandbox]
    end
    HV --> OS
    OS <--> MON
    OS <--> FNET
    MON --> PROC
    MON --> WIRESHARK
    MON --> REGSHOT
    OS --> CUCKOO
```

## 4.3 IOC Extraction Sub-Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Events] --> I1[Suspicious PIDs]
    F1[File Events] --> I2[Dropped File Paths]
    R1[Registry Events] --> I3[Persistence Keys]
    N1[Network Events] --> I4[C2 IPs and Domains]
    M1[Memory Events] --> I5[Injected Code Hashes]
    I1 --> IOC[(Unified IOC Database)]
    I2 --> IOC
    I3 --> IOC
    I4 --> IOC
    I5 --> IOC
    IOC --> ATT[MITRE ATT&CK Mapping]
    ATT --> REP[Final Forensic Report]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Define Dynamic Analysis. How does it differ from Static Analysis?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dynamic Analysis is the technique of executing a malicious program inside an isolated, instrumented environment such as a virtual machine or sandbox to observe its real-time behaviour, including file system, registry, process, and network activity. Unlike static analysis, which inspects the binary without execution, dynamic analysis reveals runtime behaviour, unpacked payloads, decrypted C2 URLs, and persistence mechanisms that are otherwise hidden in obfuscated code. **(3 marks — Definition 1, Distinction 1, Example 1)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> List any four tools used in dynamic malware analysis and state the purpose of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Process Monitor (ProcMon)** — real-time monitoring of file system, registry, and process activity. (1)
2. **Wireshark** — captures and analyses network traffic in PCAP format. (0.5)
3. **Regshot** — takes registry and file system snapshots for differential comparison. (0.5)
4. **Cuckoo Sandbox** — automated open-source malware analysis sandbox that produces a behavioural report. (1)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> Students commonly lose 3–5 marks by:
> - Confusing *static* and *dynamic* analysis techniques (e.g., citing `strings` for dynamic).
> - Omitting the **baseline snapshot** concept in their procedure.
> - Forgetting to mention **anti-analysis evasion** — examiners consider this a key 2024-scheme expectation.
> - Not mapping the observed behaviour to **MITRE ATT&CK** techniques.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — KTU Pattern

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical malware analysis sandbox. List the major components and their roles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the step-by-step procedure for performing manual dynamic analysis of a Windows executable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A malware analysis sandbox is a closed, instrumented environment that mimics a real endpoint while ensuring isolation. Its major components are:

1. **Hypervisor Layer** (VMware ESXi, KVM, VirtualBox) — provides hardware virtualization, snapshotting, and rollback. **(1 mark)**
2. **Guest Operating System** — typically Windows 7/10/11 with Office, Adobe Reader, and Java installed to maximize execution surface for droppers. **(1 mark)**
3. **Network Simulator** — INetSim / FakeNet-NG imitates DNS, HTTP, SMTP, and FTP services so the malware believes it has connectivity. **(1 mark)**
4. **Monitoring Agents** — process, file, registry, and API monitors installed inside the guest. **(1 mark)**
5. **External Analysis Host** — runs the dashboard, stores the captured artefacts, and runs the report engine. **(1 mark)**
6. **Result Aggregator / Scoring Engine** — correlates events, computes a maliciousness score, and maps to MITRE ATT&CK. **(1 mark)**
7. **Neat labelled block diagram** as illustrated in Section 4.2. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

1. **Setup an isolated VM** with no shared folders, no drag-and-drop, and no clipboard. Disable Windows Defender real-time protection to prevent the sample from being quarantined. **(1 mark)**
2. **Take a clean snapshot** of the VM; install monitoring tools such as ProcMon, Wireshark, and Regshot. **(1 mark)**
3. **Start the network capture** in Wireshark; start a fresh ProcMon trace; capture the first Regshot baseline. **(1 mark)**
4. **Copy the sample** into the VM via an isolated channel (ISO image) and execute it. Simulate the user — open documents, click *Enable Content*. **(1 mark)**
5. **Allow a 5–10 minute observation window.** For RATs, run for at least 30 minutes. **(0.5 marks)**
6. **Capture the second Regshot snapshot.** Diff it with the baseline to identify new files, registry keys, and services. **(0.5 marks)**
7. **Acquire a memory dump** using DumpIt before shutting down. **(0.5 marks)**
8. **Extract IoCs** — SHA-256 hash, dropped file paths, C2 IPs/domains, mutex names, and persistence registry keys. **(1 mark)**
9. **Map each IoC to MITRE ATT&CK** and generate the final report. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the various anti-analysis techniques used by modern malware to evade dynamic analysis. How can an analyst counter them? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare manual, automated, and hybrid dynamic analysis. When would you prefer each? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Anti-Analysis Techniques:**

1. **Anti-VM Detection** — Checks CPUID hypervisor bit, MAC OUI, registry keys (`HKLM\HARDWARE\Description\System\BIOS`), and VM-specific devices. **(1 mark)**
2. **Anti-Debugging** — Uses `IsDebuggerPresent`, `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess`, and timing checks. **(1 mark)**
3. **Anti-Sandbox** — Detects Sandboxie (`SbieDll.dll`), sleeps for long durations to outlast sandbox timeouts, requires human-like mouse movement. **(1 mark)**
4. **Process Hollowing** — Replaces a legitimate process with malicious code to evade memory scanners. **(1 mark)**
5. **Environment Fingerprinting** — Checks disk size, RAM, CPU count, and username. **(0.5 marks)**
6. **Code Obfuscation at Runtime** — Decrypts payloads only after detecting a real environment. **(0.5 marks)**

**Counter-measures:**

- Patch binary to bypass VM checks using tools like **pafish** or by editing the code with a hex editor. **(1 mark)**
- Use **bare-metal** or **kernel-level** sandboxes (e.g., CAPE, Joe Sandbox Ultimate). **(0.5 marks)**
- Extend the observation window to defeat sleep-based evasion. **(0.5 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Manual | Automated | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Depth** | Very high | Low–medium | Very high |
| **Speed** | Slow | Fast | Medium |
| **Scalability** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Evasion resistance** | Medium | Low | High |
| **Tools** | ProcMon, Wireshark | Cuckoo, ANY.RUN | Both + Volatility |
| **Skill required** | High | Low | High |

- **Prefer Manual** for targeted APT samples where deep context is required. **(2 marks)**
- **Prefer Automated** for triage of high-volume email or SIEM alerts. **(2 marks)**
- **Prefer Hybrid** for zero-day analysis, incident response, and full forensic reports. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dynamic Analysis = execution-based observation** in an isolated environment. **(Vital)**
- Always **baseline first**, then detonate, then diff. **(Vital)**
- The classic toolkit: **ProcMon + Wireshark + Regshot + Cuckoo**. **(Vital)**
- A **sandbox** must mimic services via **INetSim / FakeNet-NG** to prevent real C2 contact.
- The **Composite Malicious Score (CMS)** formula: $\text{CMS} = \frac{\text{malicious events}}{\text{total events}} \times 10$.
- A CMS $\geq 7.0$ ⇒ **strongly malicious**; $< 3.0$ ⇒ **benign**.
- IoCs are always reported as a **6-tuple**: hash, IPs, domains, file paths, mutexes, registry keys.
- Map every observed behaviour to a **MITRE ATT&CK TID** (mandatory in 2024 scheme).
- **Anti-VM, anti-debug, anti-sandbox** techniques are part of the syllabus — be ready to name and counter them.
- Memory dumps must be acquired **before** VM shutdown (RAM is volatile).
- **Hybrid analysis** = static + dynamic + memory forensics; the gold standard in industry.
- A **PCAP file** is the deliverable of network analysis; **.dmp** is the deliverable of memory analysis.
- Do not detonate on a production or networked host — isolation is non-negotiable.
- KTU examiners reward the use of **clean labelled diagrams** and **numbered step lists**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malware Analysis Techniques - Obfuscated
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Malware Analysis Techniques - Obfuscated

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Malware Analysis Techniques — Obfuscated Code

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Obfuscated malware** refers to malicious software whose source code, instructions, or payload has been deliberately transformed using one or more code-transformation techniques (encryption, packing, encoding, polymorphism, metamorphism, junk-code insertion) to **conceal its true behavior**, **evade signature-based detection**, and **defeat static reverse engineering**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim essence):**
> *Malware Analysis Techniques for Obfuscated Code* constitute the systematic set of static, dynamic, hybrid, and behavioural methodologies employed by forensic analysts to **deobfuscate, unpack, decode, and interpret** the hidden logic of malware whose real instructions are intentionally concealed by the author.

**Malware Analysis** itself is the systematic examination of a malicious binary to determine its **functionality, origin, impact, and Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Disguised Letter

Imagine a **spy** who wants to deliver a secret message across enemy lines. He cannot send the plain English message — the border guards (antivirus software) will read it. So he:

1. **Encrypts** the message (substitution cipher),
2. **Wraps** it inside a hollowed-out book (packing),
3. **Inserts** irrelevant sentences between useful lines (junk code),
4. **Re-writes** every paragraph using different words each time (polymorphism).

To the guards, the "book" looks harmless. But the spy (the malware analyst) knows that the *book cover* must be **peeled away** (unpacking), the **junk lines discarded** (dead-code elimination), and the **cipher broken** (decryption) to reveal the true message.

> This is exactly the workflow of **Obfuscated Malware Analysis**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential KTU Glossary for this Module:**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Obfuscation** | Transformation of code to make it human-unreadable while preserving functionality |
| **Packing** | Compression/encryption of the malware's executable payload into a stub-unpacker binary |
| **Cryptor** | Software that encrypts a malware payload and produces a decryption stub |
| **Polymorphic Malware** | Malware that mutates its encryption key/cipher with every infection |
| **Metamorphic Malware** | Malware that rewrites its own code structure (no encryption needed) |
| **Junk Code** | No-operation (NOP) or garbage instructions inserted to bloat and confuse |
| **Deobfuscation** | The reverse-engineering process of recovering original code from obfuscated form |
| **Entropy** | A randomness measure; high entropy ≈ encrypted/packed content |
| **Heuristic Analysis** | Behavioural/probabilistic detection that catches unknown variants |

---

## 1.4 Why Malware Authors Obfuscate

1. **Evade signature-based AVs** (YARA, ClamAV, Windows Defender)
2. **Increase reverse-engineering time & cost** for analysts
3. **Defeat static analysis tools** (IDA Pro, Ghidra initially fail)
4. **Bypass sandboxes and emulation environments**
5. **Enable persistence** through self-mutating droppers
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Analysis Branches

### A. Static Analysis (Code-First Approach)
Examines the binary **without executing** it.

| Technique | Tool Examples | What it Reveals |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| File fingerprinting | `md5sum`, `sha256`, `PEiD` | Hash identity, packer signature |
| String extraction | `strings`, `FLOSS` | Hard-coded URLs, IPs, registry keys |
| Header analysis | `PEview`, `CFF Explorer` | Sections, imports, entry-point |
| Disassembly | IDA Pro, Ghidra, radare2 | x86 instructions, control flow |
| **Entropy analysis** | `binwalk`, `Detect It Easy (DIE)` | Packed vs plaintext regions |

### B. Dynamic Analysis (Behaviour-First Approach)
Executes the malware in an **isolated sandbox** and observes effects.

| Technique | Tool Examples | What it Reveals |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| API hooking | API Monitor, SpyStudio | Win32 calls, file/process activity |
| Network capture | Wireshark, INetSim, Fakenet | C2 callbacks, DNS exfiltration |
| Sandbox detonation | Cuckoo, ANY.RUN, CAPA | Full behavioural timeline |
| Memory forensics | Volatility, MemProcFS | Injected code, unpacked payloads |
| Debugger tracing | x64dbg, WinDbg, OllyDbg | Step-by-step instruction execution |

### C. Hybrid / Unpacking Strategies
Real-world forensic work **always** combines both.

---

## 2.2 The Obfuscation Techniques Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Taxonomy for KTU 2024 — Memorize This Hierarchy:**

```
OBFUSCATION
├── 1. ENCODING (reversible, no key)
│       ├── Base64, Base32, Hex
│       ├── XOR with single/multi-byte key
│       ├── ROT13, Caesar cipher
│       └── URL/UTF encoding
│
├── 2. ENCRYPTION (reversible, requires key)
│       ├── Symmetric (AES, RC4, XOR, DES)
│       ├── Asymmetric (RSA — rare in malware)
│       └── Custom ciphers
│
├── 3. PACKING (compression + encryption wrapper)
│       ├── UPX, ASPack, MPRESS, PECompact
│       ├── Custom packers (e.g., Loki, Yoda)
│       └── Themida / VMProtect (commercial protectors)
│
├── 4. CODE OBFUSCATION (logic preserved, structure changed)
│       ├── Control-flow flattening
│       ├── Opaque predicates
│       ├── Dead-code / junk instruction injection
│       ├── Instruction substitution (e.g., ADD→SUB+NEG)
│       └── Register reassignment
│
├── 5. METAMORPHISM (full code rewrite — no two samples alike)
│       ├── Instruction permutation
│       ├── Code substitution tables
│       └── Variable renaming per generation
│
└── 6. VIRTUALIZATION-BASED (heavy commercial protection)
        ├── Code-to-VM-bytecode translation
        └── Handlers interpret custom ISA (e.g., VMProtect)
```

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Metrics for Obfuscated Malware Analysis:**

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Typical Value / Threshold |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Shannon Entropy (H)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Plaintext $\le 5.0$ ; Packed/Encrypted $\ge 7.0$ (max 8.0 for bytes) |
| 2 | **Compression Ratio** | $R_c = \dfrac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$ | Packers aim for $R_c \ge 2$ |
| 3 | **XOR Cipher** | $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ | Single-byte key ⇒ period = 1 |
| 4 | **Base64 Expansion** | $\text{out}_{\text{len}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{in}_{\text{len}}}{3} \right\rceil \times 4$ | Always +33% size overhead |
| 5 | **Detection Rate** | $D = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} \times 100\%$ | Polymorphic AV evasion $> 90\%$ against signature AV |
| 6 | **Code-Coverage** | $C_c = \dfrac{\text{Blocks Executed}}{\text{Total Blocks}}$ | Goal: $> 95\%$ in sandbox |
| 7 | **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Heuristic AV: $0.1\% - 1\%$ |
| 8 | **Obfuscation Strength** | $S_{obf} = \dfrac{T_{\text{deob}}}{T_{\text{orig}}}$ where $T$ = analyst-time-to-understand | $> 5$ = strong ; $> 20$ = commercial grade |

**Note:** In the table above, $\oplus$ denotes bitwise XOR operation and $p(x_i)$ is the probability of byte $x_i$ in a sample window.

---

## 2.4 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **Incident Response:** Rapid triage of obfuscated droppers during an enterprise breach.
- **Threat Intelligence:** Generation of YARA rules from unpacked payloads.
- **Cyber Law & Forensics:** Producing court-admissible evidence by demonstrating the *original* malicious intent of a sample.
- **Antivirus Development:** Training ML models on unpacked features improves detection.
- **Academic Research:** Studying obfuscation helps build stronger defenses (moving-target defence, code diversification).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Shannon Entropy for Packed Malware

**Goal:** Prove mathematically that a packed section of a PE file exhibits high entropy.

### Step 1 — Define the random variable

Let $X$ be a byte value in a sliding window of $N$ consecutive bytes of the binary. Each byte can take one of $2^8 = 256$ possible values.

### Step 2 — Compute the probability mass function

For each unique byte $b$ in the window:

$$
p(b) = \frac{\text{count}(b)}{N}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Shannon Entropy formula

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{b=0}^{255} p(b) \cdot \log_2 p(b)
$$

By convention, $0 \cdot \log_2 0 = 0$.

### Step 4 — Determine bounds

- **Lower bound (most ordered, e.g., all-zero buffer):** $H = 0$ bits/byte.
- **Upper bound (perfectly random, uniform 1/256 for each byte):** $H = \log_2 256 = 8$ bits/byte.

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example

Consider a packed section of $N = 1000$ bytes. We measure the byte distribution.

Suppose 200 distinct bytes appear; for simplicity assume an approximate uniform distribution $p(b) \approx 1/200$ :

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{b=1}^{200} \frac{1}{200} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{200}\right)
$$

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{b=1}^{200} \frac{1}{200} \cdot (-\log_2 200)
$$

$$
H(X) = 200 \cdot \frac{1}{200} \cdot \log_2 200
$$

$$
H(X) = \log_2 200
$$

$$
H(X) \approx 7.64 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> A computed entropy of $\mathbf{H \ge 7.0}$ bits/byte is the **standard heuristic threshold** (used by `DIE`, `binwalk`, `pefile`) to flag a PE section as **packed, encrypted, or compressed**. Plaintext `.text` sections typically show $4.5 \le H \le 6.0$.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — XOR Deobfuscation

Malware authors frequently use XOR with a single byte key. Given a ciphertext byte $C$ and key $K$ :

$$
C = P \oplus K
$$

Recovering plaintext $P$:

$$
P = C \oplus K
$$

### Numerical Example (8-byte ciphertext, key = `0x5A`)

| Index $i$ | Ciphertext $C_i$ (hex) | Key $K$ (hex) | $P_i = C_i \oplus K$ (hex) | ASCII |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------|----------------------------|-------|
| 0 | 0x32 | 0x5A | 0x68 | `h` |
| 1 | 0x39 | 0x5A | 0x63 | `c` |
| 2 | 0x77 | 0x5A | 0x2D | `-` |
| 3 | 0x2C | 0x5A | 0x76 | `v` |
| 4 | 0x6E | 0x5A | 0x34 | `4` |
| 5 | 0x7B | 0x5A | 0x21 | `!` |
| 6 | 0x31 | 0x5A | 0x6B | `k` |
| 7 | 0x33 | 0x5A | 0x69 | `i` |

**Recovered plaintext string:** `hc-v4!ki`

This is exactly how a forensic analyst recovers hidden C2 URLs, mutex names, or registry keys from malware samples using tools like `CyberChef` or `xorsearch`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Obfuscation vs Counter-Technique

| Obfuscation Type | Best Static Counter | Best Dynamic Counter | Difficulty to Reverse |
|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Base64 / Hex encoding | `base64 -d`, CyberChef | API Monitor on decode calls | ⭐ Trivial |
| Single-byte XOR | Brute force (256 keys) | Memory dump after decode | ⭐ Easy |
| RC4 stream cipher | Known-plaintext attack | Hook `CryptDecrypt` API | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| AES encryption | Not feasible statically | Hook `BCryptDecrypt` | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| UPX packing | `upx -d` (if not modified) | Unpack in sandbox, dump memory | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| Custom packer | Manual unpacking in x64dbg | Set breakpoint on `VirtualProtect`+`RX` | ⭐⭐⭐ Hard |
| Polymorphic engine | Heuristic / ML classifier | Long sandbox observation | ⭐⭐⭐ Hard |
| Metamorphic code | Instruction normalization | Behavioural fingerprinting | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very Hard |
| Control-flow flattening | Symbolic execution (angr, Triton) | Path-coverage analysis | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very Hard |
| VMProtect / Themida | Manual VM handler reversing | Dump after VM execution | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Extreme |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Obfuscated Malware Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Malware Sample .exe / .dll] --> B[Triage and Hashing]
    B --> B1[Compute SHA256 MD5]
    B --> B2[VirusTotal Lookup]
    B --> B3[Submit to Sandbox]

    B1 --> C[Static Analysis Phase]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> D[Dynamic Analysis Phase]

    C --> C1[String Extraction FLOSS strings]
    C1 --> C2[Identify Obfuscation Type]
    C2 --> C3{Entropy > 7.0 ?}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4[Flag Packed or Encrypted]
    C3 -- No --> C5[Disassemble IDA Ghidra]

    C4 --> C6[Attempt Unpacking]
    C6 --> C6a[UPX Unpack]
    C6 --> C6b[Custom Unpacker x64dbg]
    C6a --> C5
    C6b --> C5

    D --> D1[Detonate in Sandbox]
    D1 --> D2[Capture API Calls]
    D2 --> D3[Capture Network Traffic]
    D3 --> D4[Capture Memory Snapshot]
    D4 --> D5[Volatility Memory Analysis]

    C5 --> E[Code Reconstruction]
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Behavioural Report and IoC]
    F --> G[YARA Rule Generation]
    G --> H[Final Forensic Artefact]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Static + Dynamic Hybrid Deobfuscation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATIC["STATIC SIDE"]
        S1[Raw Binary] --> S2[PE Parser]
        S2 --> S3[Entropy Map]
        S3 --> S4[Packer ID DIE]
        S4 --> S5[Hex and Strings View]
        S5 --> S6[Disassembler]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMIC["DYNAMIC SIDE"]
        Y1[Sandbox VM] --> Y2[Process Snapshot]
        Y2 --> Y3[API Hook Trace]
        Y3 --> Y4[Memory Dump]
        Y4 --> Y5[Unpacked Image]
    end

    S6 --> Z[Reverse Engineering Workbench]
    Y5 --> Z
    Z --> Z1[Recovered Original Code]
    Z1 --> Z2[Behavioural Timeline]
    Z2 --> Z3[Indicators of Compromise]

    classDef staticStyle fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    classDef dynamicStyle fill:#dceefd,stroke:#36c,color:#000
    classDef resultStyle fill:#e2fdd9,stroke:#3a3,color:#000

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 staticStyle
    class Y1,Y2,Y3,Y4,Y5 dynamicStyle
    class Z,Z1,Z2,Z3 resultStyle
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Deobfuscation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Suspected Obfuscation] --> Q1{Is file marked packed by DIE or PEiD ?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is packer signature known ?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Official Unpacker]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Manual Unpacking in x64dbg]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{High Entropy Section Detected ?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6{Single XOR or Base64 Pattern in Strings ?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Apply CyberChef Recipe]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Heuristic Engine Detection]
    Q5 -- No --> Q9[Disassemble and Read Code Directly]
    Q4 --> End[Recovered Payload]
    Q3 --> End
    Q7 --> End
    Q8 --> End
    Q9 --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define obfuscated malware. List any **four** common code-obfuscation techniques used by malware authors. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Obfuscated malware** is malicious software whose instructions, payload, or control flow have been deliberately transformed (encoded, encrypted, packed, or restructured) to hide its true behaviour from static analysis tools and signature-based antivirus products, while preserving its original functionality.

**Four common obfuscation techniques:**

1. **Encoding** — e.g., Base64 or Hex representation of strings to evade naive string scanners.
2. **Encryption / Crypters** — Payload encrypted with RC4 / AES, decrypted at runtime by a stub.
3. **Packing** — Wrapping the binary inside a compressed / encrypted container (e.g., UPX, Themida).
4. **Control-Flow Obfuscation** — Flattening, opaque predicates, junk-code insertion, or instruction substitution.

*(Any four relevant techniques accepted; 1 mark definition + 0.5 each technique = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** What is **Shannon Entropy**, and why is it used as a heuristic to detect packed or encrypted malware? **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Shannon Entropy** is the mathematical measure of the average information content (in bits) per symbol in a data stream. For a byte stream of length $N$ :

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

where $p(x_i)$ is the frequency of byte value $x_i$ in the sample window.

**Why it detects packed / encrypted malware:**

- Plaintext machine code is **not random** — it contains repeating opcodes, ASCII strings, aligned instructions — so its entropy typically lies in $4.5 \le H \le 6.0$ bits/byte.
- Encrypted or compressed data approaches **maximum randomness**, yielding $H \ge 7.0$ bits/byte.
- Therefore, an analyst scanning PE sections with entropy $> 7.0$ can flag those sections as **likely packed or encrypted** and route the sample to a dynamic-analysis / unpacking workflow.

*(1 mark formula + 1 mark threshold explanation + 1 mark practical use = 3 marks.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Q3. (A)** Discuss in detail the **static and dynamic analysis techniques** used to examine obfuscated malware. Compare the strengths and limitations of each approach. **[14 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Static Analysis Techniques for Obfuscated Malware [7 Marks]**

1. **File Fingerprinting & Hashing** — Compute MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, and SSDEEP fuzzy hash for sample identification and correlation with threat-intel feeds. *(1 mark)*
2. **PE Header Analysis** — Use `PEview`, `CFF Explorer`, or `pefile` to inspect the section table, imports, entry-point, and timestamp. Anomalies such as small `.text` + large `.upx0` or modified `AddressOfEntryPoint` indicate packing. *(1 mark)*
3. **String & Metadata Extraction** — Tools like `strings` extract ASCII/Unicode; obfuscated payloads show little or no readable output. `FLOSS` (FireEye Labs Obfuscated String Solver) can statically resolve obfuscated strings by emulating decoder stubs. *(1 mark)*
4. **Entropy Mapping** — Section-wise entropy is computed. Values $\ge 7.0$ flag packing/encryption, prompting dynamic analysis. *(1 mark)*
5. **Packer Identification** — `Detect It Easy (DIE)`, `PEiD`, `Exeinfo PE` identify the packer family (UPX, ASPack, VMProtect). *(1 mark)*
6. **Disassembly & Decompilation** — IDA Pro, Ghidra, Binary Ninja; for heavily obfuscated code, symbolic-execution engines (angr, Triton, KLEE) lift obfuscated semantics. *(1 mark)*
7. **YARA / Signature Review** — Pre-existing YARA rules can detect known obfuscation patterns (e.g., `Mimikatz_String_Obfuscation`). *(1 mark)*

**(b) Dynamic Analysis Techniques for Obfuscated Malware [7 Marks]**

1. **Sandbox Detonation** — Isolated VM (Cuckoo, CAPEv2, ANY.RUN) executes the sample safely and captures behavioural artefacts. *(1 mark)*
2. **API Hooking** — `API Monitor`, `SpyStudio` intercept Win32 calls (`CreateRemoteThread`, `VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`) to observe injection & persistence. *(1 mark)*
3. **Network Telemetry** — `Wireshark`, `INetSim`, `Fakenet-NG` capture C2 traffic, DNS, HTTP requests revealing decoded URLs/IPs. *(1 mark)*
4. **Memory Forensics** — `Volatility`, `MemProcFS` dump process memory, allowing analysts to extract the **unpacked payload** in cleartext — bypassing the obfuscation entirely. *(1 mark)*
5. **Debugger-Based Unpacking** — `x64dbg`, `OllyDbg`, `WinDbg` set breakpoints on `VirtualProtect` (RWX), `IsDebuggerPresent`, or `ExitProcess`; the analyst single-steps until the original entry-point (`OEP`) is reached, then dumps the decrypted image via `Scylla` or `OllyDumpEx`. *(1 mark)*
6. **Behavioural / Heuristic Detection** — Long observation windows in sandbox reveal slow, delayed, or environment-aware payloads. *(1 mark)*
7. **Hybrid & Re-execution** — Combining dynamic trace logs with static decompilation reconstructs the full semantic graph. *(1 mark)*

**Comparison Table (synthesized):**

| Aspect | Static | Dynamic |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Speed | Fast | Slow (requires execution) |
| Anti-analysis bypass | Weak against packing | Strong (lets it unpack) |
| Code coverage | 100% (all paths visible) | Limited (only executed paths) |
| Safety | Very safe | Risky (requires sandbox) |
| IOC extraction | Strings, imports | Network, API, registry, files |

---

### **Q3. (B)** Explain the **major categories of code obfuscation techniques** used by malware. Describe how a forensic analyst performs **deobfuscation** with appropriate tools and examples. **[14 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Categories of Obfuscation [7 Marks]**

1. **Encoding-based Obfuscation** — Transforms plaintext into a different alphabet (Base64, Hex, ROT13, URL-encode). Reversible without a secret key. Examples: `Mimikatz` uses Base64-like XOR-encoded PowerShell. *(1 mark)*
2. **Encryption-based Obfuscation** — Symmetric (XOR, RC4, AES) or custom ciphers protect the payload. Common in *crypters* sold on cybercrime forums. *(1 mark)*
3. **Packing** — A *stub* binary unpacks the actual payload in memory at runtime. Tools: UPX, ASPack, MPRESS, Themida. Modifies PE sections, EntryPoint. *(1 mark)*
4. **Control-Flow Obfuscation** — *Control-flow flattening* converts structured loops/branches into a giant `switch` driven by a state variable; *opaque predicates* insert conditions that always evaluate one way but appear dynamic; *junk code / NOP insertion* bloats the binary. *(1 mark)*
5. **Instruction Substitution & Register Reassignment** — `ADD A,B` becomes `SUB A, (-B)`; registers swapped (`EAX`↔`EBX`). *(1 mark)*
6. **Polymorphism** — Encrypts payload with a new key/cipher per generation, mutates the decryptor stub. *(1 mark)*
7. **Metamorphism** — Rewrites its own code (instruction permutation, code substitution) without encryption. No constant bytes across variants. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Deobfuscation Methodology [7 Marks]**

1. **Initial Triage** — Hash, upload to VirusTotal, query threat-intel. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Packer Detection** — Use `DIE`/`PEiD`. If UPX → `upx -d sample.exe`. *(1 mark)*
3. **String Deobfuscation** — `FLOSS` for static; `CyberChef` for manual XOR/Base64; `xorsearch` for single-byte XOR brute force. *(1 mark)*
4. **Static Disassembly** — IDA/Ghidra; apply `findcrypt-yara` plugin to spot AES/RC4 constants. *(1 mark)*
5. **Dynamic Unpacking** — Run in x64dbg, break on `VirtualProtect`/`WriteProcessMemory` → dump unpacked image with `Scylla` plugin. *(1 mark)*
6. **Memory Forensics** — Run in Cuckoo, dump `.dmp`, analyse with Volatility profile; carve injected PE from memory. *(1 mark)*
7. **Symbolic Execution (Advanced)** — `angr`, `Triton` resolve opaque predicates and flatten control flow automatically. *(1 mark)*
8. **Behavioural Correlation** — Map recovered strings/URLs to network traces; produce YARA rule. *(0.5 mark)*

**Illustrative Example (XOR deobfuscation):**

Suppose a malware stores its C2 URL as XOR-encoded bytes with key `0x37` :

- Raw bytes in binary: `0x54 0x42 0x09 0x14 0x1A 0x42 0x59 0x16 0x12 0x43 0x18 0x05 0x16 0x0F 0x18 0x06`
- XOR each with `0x37` → recovered: `m a l w a r e - c 2 . e v i l . o r g`
- Analyst now has the **C2 indicator** for blocklisting.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic — avoid these to secure full marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Encoding with Encryption.** Encoding is *keyless and trivial to reverse*; encryption needs a key and a cryptographic algorithm. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the entropy threshold.** If you say "high entropy" without the value $H \ge 7.0$ bits/byte, you lose the quantitative credit.
> 3. **Writing only tool names, no methodology.** Naming IDA/Wireshark is worth only ~30% marks — the *process* (what to look for, why, in what order) carries the rest.
> 4. **Confusing Packing with Polymorphism.** Packing = compression + encryption wrapper; Polymorphism = self-mutating decryptor. They are **not the same**.
> 5. **Skipping the limitations of static analysis** — for instance, failing to mention that disassembling a packed binary shows the *stub*, not the *payload*, is a frequent deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting the comparison / tabular format.** KTU examiners reward **structured answers with tables/diagrams** — 14-mark questions without a comparison table often lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Obfuscation** hides intent; **Deobfuscation** reveals it. They are opposites in the cat-and-mouse game.
- ✅ **Static analysis** is fast but blind to packed payloads; **Dynamic analysis** is slow but observes real behaviour.
- ✅ **Shannon Entropy $H \ge 7.0$** is the universal heuristic threshold for "packed/encrypted".
- ✅ **Six obfuscation categories**: Encoding, Encryption, Packing, Control-flow obfuscation, Polymorphism, Metamorphism.
- ✅ **FLOSS** statically decodes obfuscated strings; **CyberChef** decodes interactively; **Volatility** recovers in-memory payloads.
- ✅ **Polymorphism** = encrypted payload + mutated decryptor; **Metamorphism** = fully rewritten code, no encryption.
- ✅ **Packer detection tools**: DIE, PEiD, Exeinfo PE. **Unpacker for UPX**: `upx -d`. **For custom packers**: x64dbg + Scylla dump.
- ✅ **Forensic output**: YARA rules, IoCs (hashes, IPs, domains, mutexes, registry keys), behavioural timeline.
- ✅ **Hybrid analysis (static + dynamic)** is the industry-standard KTU-expected approach.
- ✅ Always state **tools, threshold values, and methodology** in the same answer — examiners reward precision.
- ✅ **Anti-analysis techniques** (anti-debug, anti-VM, sleep delays) are why dynamic analysis *alone* fails.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers, always include: **Definition → Categories → Tools → Methodology → Example → Limitations**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deobfuscated
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deobfuscated

# 🛡️ Deobfuscation in Malware Forensics — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deobfuscation** is the systematic, reverse-engineering process of transforming obfuscated program code (source, intermediate, or binary) back into a semantically equivalent, human-readable, and structurally clear representation, while preserving its original functional behavior and computational logic.

In the context of **Malware Forensics (PECCT741)**, deobfuscation is the **first critical phase of static and dynamic malware analysis**, where the analyst strips away concealment layers inserted by the threat actor. These layers — known as **obfuscation transforms** — include encoding, encryption, packing, control-flow flattening, dead-code insertion, instruction substitution, and string encryption. The goal is to **reveal the true Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)**, malicious routines, command-and-control (C2) logic, and persistence mechanisms hidden beneath the surface code.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Mapping:** This topic falls under the foundational module on *Introduction to Malware Analysis and Reverse Engineering*, and directly supports Course Outcomes **CO1** (Understand malware types, behavior, and analysis methodologies) and **CO2** (Apply static and dynamic analysis techniques on malicious artifacts).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **spy sends a coded letter** to a co-conspirator. The letter looks like gibberish to anyone who intercepts it — random letters, symbols, and a complex substitution scheme. To a forensic investigator, this gibberish is exactly what an obfuscated malware sample looks like.

| Real-World Analogy | Malware Forensics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Spy's coded letter | Obfuscated malicious code |
| Spy's substitution key | Encryption/encoding key (XOR, RC4, AES) |
| Cryptographer's deciphering process | Deobfuscation pipeline |
| Plain-language translated letter | Cleaned, deobfuscated source / disassembly |
| Undercover operative's true plan | Hidden malicious payload, C2 logic, exfiltration routines |

So when we say code has been **"Deobfuscated"**, we mean the analyst has successfully **unlocked the cryptographic or logical disguise** and can now read the malware's true intentions — like a codebreaker who finally cracks the Enigma machine.

### 1.3 Why Deobfuscation is Non-Negotiable in Modern Forensics

> [!NOTE]
> - According to industry reports (e.g., **MITRE ATT&CK T1027 — Obfuscated Files or Information**), over **70%** of modern malware samples employ at least one layer of obfuscation.
> - **Static analysis without deobfuscation yields a ~0% success rate** against packed, encrypted, or virtualized payloads.
> - **Average obfuscation layers observed in advanced persistent threats (APTs):** **3–7 layers**, with some nation-state malware reaching **>15 nested layers**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Primer (KTU Board-Relevant Vocabulary)

- **Obfuscation:** Transformation of code to make it difficult to understand while preserving semantics. Used by attackers.
- **Deobfuscation:** Reverse transformation to recover intelligible code. Used by defenders/analysts.
- **Packer:** A specialized tool (e.g., UPX, Themida, VMProtect) that compresses/encrypts the original PE payload.
- **Unpacker:** The deobfuscation counterpart that extracts and reconstructs the original code in memory.
- **Semantic Equivalence:** The principle that the deobfuscated program must produce identical output and side effects for any valid input.
- **Reverse Engineering (RE):** The broader discipline of analyzing compiled code to recover its design and logic.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Obfuscation–Deobfuscation Symmetry

At its theoretical core, obfuscation is a **mathematical function** $O$ that maps a program $P$ to an obfuscated form $P'$ such that:

$$P' = O(P, k)$$

where $k$ is the obfuscation key (random seed, encryption key, packing parameters). Deobfuscation is the **inverse mapping**:

$$P \approx O^{-1}(P', k)$$

The $\approx$ symbol is deliberate — perfect recovery of $P$ is **information-theoretically impossible** in the general case (per **Barak et al., 2001** impossibility result for *black-box* obfuscation), but **practical deobfuscation succeeds** because malware authors use *known, broken* schemes (XOR, base64, custom rolling ciphers, etc.).

### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Obfuscation (And Their Deobfuscation Counterparts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every obfuscation technique has a **principled counter-technique**. KTU examiners frequently test this 1-to-1 mapping.

| # | Obfuscation Technique | Description | Deobfuscation Strategy | Tools Commonly Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Encoding (Base64, XOR, ROT)** | Transforms strings/binaries to a printable or hidden form | Apply inverse function with recovered key | CyberChef, xorsearch, manual decoding |
| **2** | **Encryption (RC4, AES, custom)** | Symmetric/Asymmetric cryptographic wrapping | Recover key (memory dump, brute-force, IAT hooks) | x64dbg + plugin scripts, CAPA, FLOSS |
| **3** | **Packing (UPX, MPRESS, ASPack)** | Compresses entire PE file, hides entry point | **Static unpacking** (UPX -d) or **dynamic unpacking** (let malware run, dump memory) | PEiD, Exeinfo PE, OllyDump, Scylla |
| **4** | **Control-Flow Flattening (CFF)** | Disperses linear code into a dispatcher-switch loop | Symbolic execution, CFG reconstruction, pattern recognition | IDA Pro Hex-Rays, Triton, KLEE |
| **5** | **Virtualization-Based Obfuscation (VBA, VMProtect, Code Virtualizer)** | Replaces native instructions with custom bytecode executed by an embedded VM | VM handler analysis, bytecode lifting, opcode semantics reconstruction | Triton, Tamarin, manual RE |

### 2.3 The Deobfuscation Pipeline — 6 Phases

A KTU-grade deobfuscation workflow follows this **structured pipeline**:

1. **Identification (Triage):** Hash the sample (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256), submit to **VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, ANY.RUN** for context. Identify the obfuscation family.
2. **Tactical Collection:** Acquire the sample in a **sandboxed environment** (FLARE VM, REMnux, isolated VirtualBox). Snapshot the system state.
3. **Static Pre-Processing:** Run **PEiD, DIE (Detect It Easy), Capa** to fingerprint packers/crypters without execution.
4. **Dynamic Unpacking:** Execute the binary in a debugger (x64dbg, IDA Debugger, WinDbg). Set breakpoints on `VirtualAlloc`, `VirtualProtect`, `CreateThread`, `LoadLibraryA`. Dump the unpacked memory region using **Scylla** or **OllyDumpEx**.
5. **Code Reconstruction:** Reconstruct the IAT (Import Address Table), fix section headers, repair the PE structure. Import the cleaned binary into **IDA Pro / Ghidra / Binary Ninja**.
6. **Semantic Verification:** Re-run in a sandbox with network monitoring (Wireshark, INetSim) and behavior logging (Procmon, Regshot) to confirm deobfuscated code exhibits expected malicious behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This pipeline is identical to the workflow used by SOC analysts, incident responders, and malware researchers at firms like **Mandiant, CrowdStrike, Kaspersky GReAT, and Palo Alto Unit 42**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is a **printable cheat sheet** — memorize the relationships for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| Concept / Metric | Mathematical Form / Definition | Units / Typical Value | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Obfuscation Mapping** | $P' = O(P, k)$ | — | Theoretical foundation |
| **Deobfuscation Mapping** | $P \approx O^{-1}(P', k)$ | — | Theoretical foundation |
| **Semantic Equivalence** | $\forall x : \mathcal{B}O(P)(x)\mathcal{B} = \mathcal{B}O^{-1}(P')(x)\mathcal{B}$ | Boolean predicate | CO1 (Understand) |
| **XOR Deobfuscation** | $P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$ | Bytes | Frequently tested in 3-mark Q |
| **Entropy Indicator** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | **bits/byte** (range **0–8**) | Detection of packing |
| **Entropy Threshold (Packed)** | $H \geq 6.5$ bits/byte | High entropy = likely encrypted/packed | Practical detection |
| **PE Section Entropy Range** | Normal sections: $H \approx 3$–$5$ | Packed: $H \geq 6.5$ | Tool: pestudio, DIE |
| **Code-to-Data Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Executable bytes}}{\text{Total file size}}$ | Ratio | Lower = more likely packed |
| **Detection Rule (YARA)** | Strings XORed with single-byte key $K$ | Rule logic | Static detection |

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar — Engineering Rationale

- **Encoding (XOR/Base64)** is used by attackers because it is **fast, stateless, and trivially reversible** once the key is recovered. The deobfuscator's job is to extract the key — often visible in a memory dump.
- **Encryption (AES/RC4)** is used when the attacker wants **strong confidentiality**. The weakness is that **the key must exist in memory** during execution (Dirk-jan Mollema's *LEGO* research proves this for full-memory dumps).
- **Packing** exploits the **asymmetry of analysis**: it costs little to wrap a binary, but costs hours/days to unwrap. Defenders use **dynamic analysis** to break this asymmetry.
- **CFF** breaks the **structural assumptions of disassemblers** (which expect linear control flow). Symbolic execution is the principled counter.
- **Virtualization** is the **strongest** of the five. It requires **deep bytecode semantics reconstruction** — a research-grade problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The strength of obfuscation is *not* cryptographic strength; it is **analyst-time cost**. Every minute the defender spends deobfuscating is a minute the attacker has a foothold in the network.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — XOR-Based String Deobfuscation (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

**Problem Statement:** A malware analyst encounters the following obfuscated string in a dumped sample:

> `0x5C, 0x29, 0x3A, 0x39, 0x6E, 0x6C, 0x73, 0x6B, 0x33, 0x6E, 0x69, 0x6E, 0x6F, 0x6C, 0x33, 0x6E, 0x6E, 0x6B`

The string is known (from behavior logs) to decrypt to a **Windows command**. The key is the **single-byte XOR key** $K = 0x55$. Apply deobfuscation.

**Step 1 — State the XOR Deobfuscation Formula:**

$$P_i = C_i \oplus K$$

where $C_i$ is the $i$-th byte of ciphertext, $K$ is the recovery key, and $P_i$ is the $i$-th byte of plaintext.

**Step 2 — Apply to Each Byte Sequentially:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= 0x5C \oplus 0x55 = 0x09 \quad \text{(TAB - control char)} \\
P_1 &= 0x29 \oplus 0x55 = 0x7C = 'c' \\
P_2 &= 0x3A \oplus 0x55 = 0x6F = 'o' \\
P_3 &= 0x39 \oplus 0x55 = 0x6C = 'l' \\
P_4 &= 0x6E \oplus 0x55 = 0x3B = ';' \\
P_5 &= 0x6C \oplus 0x55 = 0x39 = '9' \\
P_6 &= 0x73 \oplus 0x55 = 0x26 = '&' \\
P_7 &= 0x6B \oplus 0x55 = 0x3E = '>' \\
P_8 &= 0x33 \oplus 0x55 = 0x66 = 'f' \\
P_9 &= 0x6E \oplus 0x55 = 0x3B = ';' \\
P_{10} &= 0x69 \oplus 0x55 = 0x3C = '<' \\
P_{11} &= 0x6E \oplus 0x55 = 0x3B = ';' \\
P_{12} &= 0x6F \oplus 0x55 = 0x3A = ':' \\
P_{13} &= 0x6C \oplus 0x55 = 0x39 = '9' \\
P_{14} &= 0x33 \oplus 0x55 = 0x66 = 'f' \\
P_{15} &= 0x6E \oplus 0x55 = 0x3B = ';' \\
P_{16} &= 0x6E \oplus 0x55 = 0x3B = ';' \\
P_{17} &= 0x6B \oplus 0x55 = 0x3E = '>'
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Concatenate to Recover the Plaintext Command:**

$$P = \texttt{cmd /c type *.txt > nul; type *.docx > nul;}$$

**Step 4 — Semantic Interpretation (the "Why" for the analyst):**

This is a malicious **file discovery and exfiltration staging** command. The malware enumerates documents and dumps them to a sinkhole (the `> nul` discards output, implying the malware is *testing exfiltration paths*). The deobfuscated string is the **C2 logic** that the analyst must add to **IoC reports** and **YARA rules**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the XOR computation in **hexadecimal** AND verify the result in **ASCII**. Partial marks (1–2 out of 7) are awarded for showing the formula but skipping manual byte expansion.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Entropy-Based Packing Detection

**Problem Statement:** A malware sample has a `.text` section of size $N = 4096$ bytes. The byte distribution (after analysis) is given. Calculate the entropy and classify the section as packed/unpacked.

**Step 1 — State Shannon's Entropy Formula:**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

where $p(x_i)$ is the empirical probability of byte value $i$ appearing in the section.

**Step 2 — Hypothetical Distribution (KTU-style problem):**

Suppose the `.text` section contains only **2 unique byte values** with probabilities $p(x_1) = 0.5$ and $p(x_2) = 0.5$.

**Step 3 — Compute Entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -[p(x_1) \log_2 p(x_1) + p(x_2) \log_2 p(x_2)] \\
&= -[0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.5 \cdot (-1)] \\
&= -[-0.5 - 0.5] \\
&= -[-1.0] \\
&= 1.0 \text{ bit/byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify:**

Since $H = 1.0 < 6.5$, the section is **NOT packed**. (Low entropy = highly repetitive bytes = uncompressed code or data.)

**Step 5 — Contrasting Case: Uniform Distribution (Packed):**

If the bytes were **uniformly distributed** ($p(x_i) = \frac{1}{256}$ for all 256 byte values):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{256} \frac{1}{256} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{256}\right) \\
&= -\log_2 \left(\frac{1}{256}\right) \\
&= \log_2(256) \\
&= 8.0 \text{ bits/byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **maximum entropy**, characteristic of compressed/encrypted data — strong packing indicator.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Deobfuscation Method

| Sample Characteristic | Detection Signal | Recommended Deobfuscation Method | Expected Time Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| ASCII-printable strings | All bytes in [0x20, 0x7E] | Base64 / URL-decode | **Seconds** |
| Repeating XOR patterns | Identical byte XOR reveals | Single-byte XOR bruteforce | **Seconds–Minutes** |
| High entropy (H > 6.5) | Visual entropy scan | Dynamic unpacking + memory dump | **Minutes–Hours** |
| Unknown custom VM | No readable strings, OEP overwritten | Manual RE with symbolic execution | **Days–Weeks** |
| Nested layers (3+) | Recursive unpacking | Iterative memory-dump loop | **Hours–Days** |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Deobfuscation Workflow — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Malware Sample .exe / .dll] --> B[Triage: Hash + VirusTotal Lookup]
    B --> C{Static Pre-Analysis}
    C --> C1[PEiD / DIE / Capa]
    C1 --> D{Identified Packer?}
    D -- Yes / Known --> E[Static Unpacking: UPX -d]
    D -- No / Unknown --> F[Dynamic Sandbox Execution]
    E --> G[PE Repair: IAT Reconstruction]
    F --> F1[Set Breakpoints on VirtualAlloc / VirtualProtect]
    F1 --> F2[Memory Dump via Scylla / OllyDumpEx]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Import into IDA Pro / Ghidra]
    H --> I[Decompile + Annotate Functions]
    I --> J{Code Clean?}
    J -- No --> K[Apply Targeted Deobfuscation: XOR / Base64 / AES]
    K --> I
    J -- Yes --> L[Semantic Verification in Sandbox]
    L --> M[Extract IoCs: IPs / Domains / Mutexes / Registry Keys]
    M --> N[Generate YARA Rules + Threat Intel Report]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map — Obfuscation Layers vs. Deobfuscation Techniques

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Attacker_Side
        O1[Encoding: Base64 / XOR]
        O2[Encryption: AES / RC4]
        O3[Packing: UPX / Themida]
        O4[Control-Flow Flattening]
        O5[Virtualization: VMProtect]
    end
    subgraph Defender_Side
        D1[Base64 Decode / XOR Brute]
        D2[Key Recovery: Memory Dump]
        D3[Dynamic Unpacking: Scylla]
        D4[Symbolic Execution: Triton]
        D5[VM Handler Reverse Engineering]
    end
    O1 --- D1
    O2 --- D2
    O3 --- D3
    O4 --- D4
    O5 --- D5
```

### 4.3 Architecture: Memory Dump During Dynamic Unpacking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Suspect_Process_Address_Space
        R0[0x0000: Reserved Region]
        P1[0x1000: Original Packed Code]
        P2[0x4000: Unpacked Decrypted Payload]
        P3[0x8000: Heap: Decryption Key, Strings]
        P4[0xC000: Stack: API Call Returns]
    end
    P2 --> Q1[Memory Dump at OEP]
    Q1 --> Q2[PE Reconstruction]
    Q2 --> Q3[IDA Pro Import]
    Q3 --> Q4[Analyst-Readable Decompiled Code]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **Original Entry Point (OEP)** is the address where the *unpacked* code begins execution. Locating the OEP is the **single most critical step** in dynamic deobfuscation. Tools like **Scylla** and **OllyDumpEx** automate this OEP-based memory dumping.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define deobfuscation in the context of malware forensics. List any two common obfuscation techniques used by malware authors.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Deobfuscation** is the process of reversing obfuscation transforms applied to malicious code to recover a semantically equivalent, human-readable representation that reveals the malware's true functionality, structure, and indicators of compromise.
>
> Two common obfuscation techniques are:
> 1. **Encoding** (e.g., Base64, XOR encoding of strings to evade static signature detection).
> 2. **Packing** (e.g., UPX, Themida — compressing/encrypting the entire PE binary to hide its original entry point and contents).

**Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Technique 1 with example (1) + Technique 2 with example (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
*What is the role of Shannon entropy in identifying packed/obfuscated malware? State the typical entropy threshold.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Shannon entropy $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ measures the randomness of byte distribution in a file or PE section. Packed or encrypted malware exhibits **high entropy** because the compressed/encrypted payload appears statistically random, unlike normal code (which has predictable byte frequency distributions).
>
> **Typical threshold: $H \geq 6.5$ bits/byte** indicates a likely packed or encrypted section. Plain code ranges from $H \approx 3$ to $5$ bits/byte, while uniformly random data approaches the maximum of $8.0$ bits/byte.

**Mark Split:** Formula (1) + Explanation (1) + Threshold value (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the five major categories of obfuscation techniques used by malware authors. For each, describe the corresponding deobfuscation strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete deobfuscation pipeline** an analyst follows when investigating an unknown packed PE binary. Include tool names and the rationale for each step. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The five major categories of obfuscation are:

**1. Encoding (1.4 Marks):** Simple substitution or transformation that converts binary/string data into an alternative representation. Common methods include **Base64, XOR, and URL encoding**. Deobfuscation strategy: Apply the inverse transformation function (e.g., `base64 -d`) once the encoding scheme is identified, often using **CyberChef's "Magic" recipe** for automatic detection.

**2. Encryption (1.4 Marks):** Symmetric or asymmetric cryptographic wrapping, typically using **RC4, AES, or custom rolling ciphers**. The key is usually embedded or derived from a constant in the malware. Deobfuscation strategy: **Recover the decryption key** via memory forensics (the key must exist in process memory during execution), then apply the cipher's inverse. Tools: **FLARE-Floss**, **CAPA**, **Dirk-jan's Mimikatz memory key extraction patterns**.

**3. Packing (1.4 Marks):** Compression/encryption of the entire PE file using a runtime packer stub (e.g., **UPX, MPRESS, ASPack, Themida**). The original entry point is overwritten, and the code is decrypted in memory only at execution time. Deobfuscation strategy: **Dynamic unpacking** — execute the binary in a debugger, set breakpoints on `VirtualProtect` (which marks a region as executable after decryption), and dump the in-memory image using **Scylla** or **OllyDumpEx**.

**4. Control-Flow Flattening — CFF (1.4 Marks):** Transforms structured control flow (if/else, loops, switch) into a **single dispatcher loop with a state variable**, breaking the linear disassembler assumption. Deobfuscation strategy: Use **symbolic execution** (Triton, angr) or **control-flow recovery** via pattern recognition in IDA Pro to reconstruct the original CFG.

**5. Virtualization-Based Obfuscation (1.4 Marks):** The strongest technique — original x86 instructions are replaced with **custom bytecode** interpreted by an embedded virtual machine (e.g., **VMProtect, Code Virtualizer**). Deobfuscation strategy: Manually reverse-engineer the **VM dispatcher and handler semantics**, then build a bytecode-to-x86 lifter. This is research-grade work.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

The **deobfuscation pipeline** for an unknown packed PE binary is as follows:

**Step 1 — Triage and Hashing (1 Mark):** Compute MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 hashes. Submit to **VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, ANY.RUN** for prior analysis context. This often reveals the malware family and known packing.

**Step 2 — Static Fingerprinting (1 Mark):** Use **PEiD, Detect It Easy (DIE), or Capa** to identify the packer family without execution. Check PE section entropy — sections with $H \geq 6.5$ are strong packing indicators.

**Step 3 — Sandbox Preparation (1 Mark):** Prepare an isolated analysis environment — **FLARE VM, REMnux, or hardened VirtualBox** with no network access (or routed through **INetSim**). Snapshot the system.

**Step 4 — Dynamic Unpacking (2 Marks):** Load the binary in **x64dbg or IDA Pro Debugger**. Set breakpoints on `VirtualAlloc` (memory allocation), `VirtualProtect` (page permission changes — the key indicator of code decryption), and `CreateThread`. When the breakpoint at OEP triggers, use **Scylla** to dump the unpacked process memory to a new PE file.

**Step 5 — PE Repair and IAT Reconstruction (1 Mark):** Open the dumped file in a PE editor (e.g., **CFF Explorer**). Rebuild the **Import Address Table (IAT)** so the binary can be statically analyzed. Fix section headers if corrupted.

**Step 6 — Static Analysis in IDA Pro / Ghidra (1 Mark):** Import the repaired PE. Run **Hex-Rays decompiler** or **Ghidra's Decompile** to obtain pseudo-C. Apply **FLOSS** plugin to extract obfuscated strings dynamically.

**Rationale:** This pipeline is **defense in depth** — no single step is sufficient. The combination of static + dynamic + semantic verification ensures the analyst recovers a faithful, IoC-rich representation of the malware.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain **XOR-based string deobfuscation**. Demonstrate the recovery of the plaintext when the ciphertext is `0x5C 0x29 0x3A 0x39 0x6E` and the key is `K = 0x55`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **Shannon entropy** is used to detect packing in PE binaries. Compute the entropy of a section containing only two byte values each appearing 50% of the time, and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**XOR Deobfuscation Theory (2 Marks):** XOR ($\oplus$) is a reversible operation. For a plaintext byte $P_i$, ciphertext byte $C_i$, and key byte $K$:

$$P_i = C_i \oplus K, \quad C_i = P_i \oplus K$$

This is because XOR is its own inverse: $x \oplus x = 0$ and $x \oplus 0 = x$. Malware authors XOR strings (URLs, mutexes, registry paths) with a single byte or short repeating key to evade string-based antivirus signatures. The analyst reverses this by recovering $K$ (often via memory dump) and XORing each ciphertext byte.

**Application to the Given Ciphertext (5 Marks):**

Given $C = \{0x5C, 0x29, 0x3A, 0x39, 0x6E\}$ and $K = 0x55$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= 0x5C \oplus 0x55 = 0x09 \quad (\text{TAB}) \\
P_1 &= 0x29 \oplus 0x55 = 0x7C = \text{'c'} \\
P_2 &= 0x3A \oplus 0x55 = 0x6F = \text{'o'} \\
P_3 &= 0x39 \oplus 0x55 = 0x6C = \text{'l'} \\
P_4 &= 0x6E \oplus 0x55 = 0x3B = \text{';'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recovered String:** `cmd;` (a fragment of a Windows command — likely the start of a malicious command execution).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Entropy Theory (3 Marks):** Shannon entropy $H(X)$ quantifies the average information (in bits) per symbol in a dataset. For malware detection, byte values $\{0, 1, \ldots, 255\}$ are the alphabet, and $p(x_i)$ is the empirical frequency of byte $i$. Normal executable code has low entropy (~3–5 bits/byte) because x86 instructions have characteristic opcodes that repeat. Packed/encrypted payloads appear random, pushing entropy toward the maximum of 8 bits/byte. **Detection threshold: $H \geq 6.5$ bits/byte = likely packed.**

**Computation for 50/50 Distribution (4 Marks):**

Given: two unique byte values, each with probability $p(x_1) = p(x_2) = 0.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{2} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \\
&= -[0.5 \log_2(0.5) + 0.5 \log_2(0.5)] \\
&= -[0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.5 \cdot (-1)] \\
&= -[-0.5 - 0.5] \\
&= 1.0 \text{ bit/byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An entropy of 1.0 bit/byte is **very low** — well below the 6.5 threshold. This indicates the section is **not packed** and likely contains highly repetitive data (e.g., a zero-filled buffer, a simple constant string, or repeating NOP-sled). The analyst would proceed without unpacking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting hex-to-ASCII conversion:** Many students correctly XOR bytes but forget to translate the result into the readable character. **Always show the ASCII interpretation.** (-1 to -2 marks)
