<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Live Malware Analysis** (also called *Dynamic Analysis* or *Behavioral Analysis*) is the forensic process of executing a suspicious binary inside an isolated, instrumented, and controlled computing environment to observe, capture, and document its runtime behavior, including process creation, file system modifications, registry edits, network communications, and persistence mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Framing):**
> "Live malware analysis is the technique of studying a malicious program by executing it in a safe, sandboxed environment and observing its actions on the system, network, and memory in real time, as opposed to static analysis which examines code without execution."

### Key Terminology Map

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sandbox** | An isolated, disposable environment that mimics a real system for safe malware detonation |
| **Detonation** | The act of executing a malware sample inside the sandbox to trigger its payload |
| **Behavioral Indicators (BIs)** | Observable runtime actions: file writes, registry keys, network calls, process injection |
| **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)** | Forensic artifacts (hashes, IPs, domains, mutexes) extracted from behavioral traces |
| **Virtual Machine Introspection (VMI)** | Monitoring guest memory/CPU from outside the VM for stealthy analysis |
| **Triage** | The initial rapid behavioral fingerprinting phase before deep analysis |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Biologist's Petri Dish**
> Imagine a microbiologist studying a dangerous virus. They cannot just look at the virus under a microscope (that would be *static analysis*). Instead, they culture it inside a sealed Petri dish (the **sandbox**), feed it nutrients (the **system resources**), and observe what it does — does it multiply, mutate, attack cells, excrete toxins? Live malware analysis works the same way: we put the malicious program inside an **isolated virtual box**, let it "live" and run, and we record every single thing it does — what files it creates, what websites it contacts, what processes it spawns.

### Why Not Just Disassemble the Code?
- Modern malware uses **packing, obfuscation, polymorphism, and encryption** to hide its true logic from static analysis.
- Dynamic analysis observes **what the malware actually does**, not what it appears to contain.
- A packer may produce completely different decoded payloads at runtime than what is stored on disk.

### Critical Mindset
> [!WARNING]
> **Live malware is biohazard-level dangerous.** Analysis must NEVER be performed on a production network or host. Always use an **air-gapped** or **fully isolated** virtual environment with snapshots and rollback capability.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Live Analysis Operational Pipeline

The discipline follows a strict 6-phase operational pipeline:

### Phase 1: **Environment Preparation**
- Create a clean Virtual Machine (VMware, VirtualBox, KVM, FLARE-VM).
- Install monitoring tools **before** detonation.
- Take a clean **baseline snapshot**.
- Disable or spoof host-integrations (shared folders, drag-and-drop, network sync).

### Phase 2: **Sample Acquisition & Triage**
- Compute the **SHA-256** and **MD5** hashes.
- Cross-reference hashes on **VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, Hybrid Analysis**.
- Identify file type using tools like `TrID` or `DIE (Detect It Easy)`.
- Determine architecture (x86, x64, .NET, PE32+).

### Phase 3: **Instrumentation Setup**
- Install and configure the toolchain:

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Process Monitor (ProcMon)** | Captures real-time file system, Registry, and process activity |
| **Process Explorer** | Tree view of running processes and their DLLs/handles |
| **Wireshark / tcpdump** | Captures all network packets to/from the sandbox |
| **Regshot** | Snapshots the registry before and after execution for diffing |
| **Autoruns** | Lists all persistence locations (Run keys, scheduled tasks, services) |
| **Cuckoo Sandbox** | Automated end-to-end dynamic analysis framework |
| **INetSim** | Simulates Internet services (DNS, HTTP, SMTP) for offline detonation |
| **FakeNet-NG** | Redirects all outbound traffic to a local fake server |

### Phase 4: **Detonation**
- Disable the guest's actual network (Host-Only / Internal Network).
- Redirect DNS and HTTP via FakeNet-NG / INetSim.
- Execute the malware sample.
- Trigger common UI-based payloads (clicking dialogs, opening docs).
- Let the sample run for an extended period (typically 3–10 minutes).

### Phase 5: **Observation & Capture**
- Monitor:
  - File system: created/deleted/modified files.
  - Registry: keys created, values set, run keys.
  - Processes: parent-child trees, injection events.
  - Network: DNS queries, HTTP requests, raw TCP/UDP.
  - Memory: in-memory strings, injected code.

### Phase 6: **Reporting & IOC Extraction**
- Generate a structured forensic report.
- Extract **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)**.
- Map behaviors to the **MITRE ATT&CK** framework.
- Compare against YARA rules and known malware families.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Analytical Rationale

| Why We Do This | How It Manifests |
|---|---|
| Static analysis is defeated by packing | Runtime unpacking reveals true code |
| Need actual network C2 servers | Wireshark reveals live HTTP/DNS traffic |
| Need to identify persistence | Autoruns + Regshot diff exposes boot-time hooks |
| Need to assess impact scope | Process Monitor reveals encryption (ransomware), exfiltration |
| Need threat intel | IoCs feed SIEMs, EDRs, and YARA repositories |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Live malware analysis is a procedural discipline — there are few mathematical formulas. The "formulas" here are operational heuristics and metric calculations.

| Concept / Metric | Expression / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Hashing (Sample Identity)** | $H = \text{SHA256}(M)$ where $M$ is the binary | 256-bit digest |
| **Levenshtein-based Behavior Diff** | $D_{beh} = \frac{\vert B_{pre} \triangle B_{post} \vert}{\vert B_{pre} \cup B_{post} \vert}$ | Ratio of behavioral changes |
| **Detection Score (VirusTotal)** | $S = \frac{\text{Detections}}{\text{Total Engines}} \times 100$ | Percentage 0–100 |
| **Entropy (Packed Detection)** | $H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits per byte. $\geq 7.0$ typically packed |
| **Snapshot Delta (Regshot)** | $\Delta_{reg} = \vert \text{Keys}_{after} \vert - \vert \text{Keys}_{before} \vert$ | Count of new keys |
| **Process Tree Depth** | $D_{tree} = \max(\text{depth}(\text{proc}_i))$ | Indicates injection depth |
| **MITRE Coverage** | $C_{MITRE} = \frac{\text{Observed TTPs}}{\text{Total Possible TTPs}}$ | Threat coverage ratio |

### Entropy as a Packing Detector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **normal PE** has entropy $\approx 4.5$ to $6.0$. **Encrypted/packed** code has entropy $\geq 7.0$. This is a critical pre-detonation heuristic.

$$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{256} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \quad \text{[bits/byte]} $$

If $H(X) \geq 7.0$, the section is statistically random → likely encrypted or compressed → **dynamic analysis is mandatory**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Use Case |
|---|---|
| SOC / Incident Response | Triage phishing attachments in seconds |
| Threat Intelligence | Build IoC feeds for global threat sharing |
| EDR / AV Vendors | Train detection models on runtime behavior |
| Banking / Finance | Analyze targeted APT campaigns |
| Government CERTs | Nation-state attribution analysis |
| Academic Research | Malware evolution studies |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Entropy of a Malware Section (Packing Detection)

### Problem
Given a PE section's byte distribution, derive its entropy and decide whether it is packed.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the section contain $N$ bytes. Group the bytes into 256 possible values (0x00 to 0xFF). Let $c_i$ be the count of byte value $i$ in the section.

The probability of byte $i$ appearing is:

$$ p(x_i) = \frac{c_i}{N} $$

Shannon's entropy of the section is then:

$$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) $$

Substituting $p(x_i) = c_i / N$:

$$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \left( \frac{c_i}{N} \right) \log_2 \left( \frac{c_i}{N} \right) $$

$$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{c_i}{N} \left[ \log_2(c_i) - \log_2(N) \right] $$

$$ H(X) = \log_2(N) \sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{c_i}{N} - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=0}^{255} c_i \log_2(c_i) $$

Since $\sum_{i=0}^{255} c_i = N$, the first term becomes $\log_2(N)$. Therefore:

$$ H(X) = \log_2(N) - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=0}^{255} c_i \log_2(c_i) $$

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a 1024-byte section has the following byte distribution:

- 768 bytes of `0x90` (NOP slide)
- 256 bytes of `0xCC` (INT3)

So $N = 1024$, $c_{0x90} = 768$, $c_{0xCC} = 256$, all others = 0.

For non-zero terms:

$$ p(0x90) = \frac{768}{1024} = 0.75 $$

$$ p(0xCC) = \frac{256}{1024} = 0.25 $$

$$ H(X) = -[0.75 \log_2(0.75) + 0.25 \log_2(0.25)] $$

$$ H(X) = -[0.75 \times (-0.4150) + 0.25 \times (-2.0)] $$

$$ H(X) = -[-0.3113 - 0.5] = 0.8113 \text{ bits/byte} $$

> [!NOTE]
> $H \approx 0.81$ is **very low entropy** → highly structured → likely **not packed**. Compare to a fully random section: $H = 8.0$ bits/byte.

### Threshold Decision Table

| Entropy Range (bits/byte) | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $0.0 \leq H < 3.0$ | Repeating / null-padded section |
| $3.0 \leq H < 6.0$ | Normal compiled code or data |
| $6.0 \leq H < 7.0$ | Possibly compressed |
| $7.0 \leq H \leq 8.0$ | Highly likely encrypted or packed |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Behavioral Diff Ratio (Regshot-style)

Let $B_{pre}$ be the set of registry keys before execution, and $B_{post}$ be the set after. The behavioral change ratio is:

$$ D_{beh} = \frac{\vert B_{pre} \triangle B_{post} \vert}{\vert B_{pre} \cup B_{post} \vert} $$

Where $\triangle$ is the symmetric difference:

$$ B_{pre} \triangle B_{post} = (B_{pre} \setminus B_{post}) \cup (B_{post} \setminus B_{pre}) $$

### Worked Example

Before: $B_{pre} = \{K_1, K_2, K_3, K_4, K_5\}$
After:  $B_{post} = \{K_1, K_2, K_3, K_6, K_7\}$

Symmetric difference:
- Removed: $\{K_4, K_5\}$
- Added: $\{K_6, K_7\}$

$$ B_{pre} \triangle B_{post} = \{K_4, K_5, K_6, K_7\} \Rightarrow \vert \cdot \vert = 4 $$

$$ B_{pre} \cup B_{post} = \{K_1, K_2, K_3, K_4, K_5, K_6, K_7\} \Rightarrow \vert \cdot \vert = 7 $$

$$ D_{beh} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.571 \;\; (57.1\%) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A $D_{beh} > 0.20$ on the Run keys (`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`) is a **high-confidence persistence indicator**.

---

## 3.3 Detection Score Example

Suppose VirusTotal reports: **52 of 72 engines** flagged a sample.

$$ S = \frac{52}{72} \times 100 = 72.22\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Board Trivia: A score $\geq 50\%$ is treated as **high-confidence malware**; $10$–$50\%$ is **suspicious**; $< 10\%$ is likely a **false positive** or a custom sample.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Live Malware Analysis — End-to-End Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Suspicious Sample] --> B[Compute SHA-256 Hash]
    B --> C[Submit to Multi-Scanner VT]
    C --> D[Static Pre-checks Entropy Strings]
    D --> E{Is Sample Packed or Obfuscated?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Setup Isolated Sandbox VM]
    E -- No --> F
    F --> G[Install Toolchain ProcMon Wireshark Regshot Autoruns FakeNet]
    G --> H[Take Clean Snapshot Baseline]
    H --> I[Enable Network Simulation INetSim]
    I --> J[Detonate Sample Execute Binary]
    J --> K[Observe Runtime Behavior]
    K --> L[Capture File System Activity]
    K --> M[Capture Registry Changes]
    K --> N[Capture Network Traffic]
    K --> O[Capture Process Tree]
    L --> P[Diff Against Baseline]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Extract Indicators of Compromise]
    Q --> R[Map to MITRE ATT and CK]
    R --> S[Generate Forensic Report]
    S --> T[Revert Snapshot Discard VM]
    T --> U[Archive Sample and IoCs]
```

---

## 4.2 Sandbox Architecture — Block-Level Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Host["Host Operating System"]
        HV[Hypervisor VMware VirtualBox]
        SNAP[Snapshot Manager]
    end
    subgraph Guest["Isolated Guest VM"]
        OS[Clean Windows or Linux Image]
        APP[Suspicious Malware Sample]
        TOOLS[Instrumentation Toolchain]
        FAKE[FakeNet-NG INetSim]
    end
    subgraph Monitor["External Monitoring"]
        LOG[Log Aggregator]
        PCAP[Packet Capture Files]
        MEM[Memory Dumps]
    end
    HV --> OS
    APP --> OS
    OS --> TOOLS
    OS --> FAKE
    TOOLS --> LOG
    FAKE --> PCAP
    HV --> MEM
    SNAP -.->|Rollback| OS
```

---

## 4.3 MITRE ATT&CK Tactic Mapping (Live Observations)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Access] --> B[Execution]
    B --> C[Persistence]
    C --> D[Privilege Escalation]
    D --> E[Defense Evasion]
    E --> F[Credential Access]
    F --> G[Discovery]
    G --> H[Lateral Movement]
    H --> I[Collection]
    I --> J[Command and Control]
    J --> K[Exfiltration]
    K --> L[Impact]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style L fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.4 Behavioral IOC Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Captures ProcMon PCAP Regshot] --> B[Normalize Events]
    B --> C[Filter Whitelisted Activity]
    C --> D[Cluster by Artifact Type]
    D --> E[File Hashes]
    D --> F[Registry Keys]
    D --> G[IP and Domain IoCs]
    D --> H[Process Names and PIDs]
    D --> I[Mutexes and Pipes]
    E --> J[STIX 2.1 Threat Intel Bundle]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[YARA Rules and SIEM Feeds]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Live Malware Analysis. List any four tools used for dynamic analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Live Malware Analysis is the process of executing a malicious program inside a controlled, isolated environment to observe its runtime behavior, including file system changes, registry modifications, network activity, and process activity, in real time.

Four tools used:

1. **Process Monitor (ProcMon)** — captures file, registry, and process events.
2. **Wireshark** — captures live network traffic.
3. **Regshot** — snapshots the registry before and after execution for diffing.
4. **Cuckoo Sandbox** — automated open-source dynamic analysis framework.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for tools with brief purpose — Examiner pattern.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is live malware analysis necessary when static analysis exists? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Packing/Obfuscation Defeat:** Static analysis fails against packed, encrypted, or polymorphic malware, since the real code is only revealed at runtime. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Actual Behavior Verification:** Static analysis shows what code *could* do; live analysis shows what code *actually* does — including conditional payloads, anti-VM checks, and delayed triggers. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Live Malware Analysis Sandbox**. Discuss the role of **FakeNet-NG / INetSim**, **Process Monitor**, and **Wireshark** in this setup. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)**. Describe any **four categories of IoCs** extracted during dynamic analysis with examples. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Sandbox Architecture (Block Diagram, 2 Marks):**

The architecture has three layers:

1. **Host Layer** — runs a hypervisor (VMware, VirtualBox, KVM).
2. **Guest Layer** — clean OS image + instrumentation tools.
3. **External Monitoring Layer** — log aggregator, packet capture, memory dump collectors.

**Role of Tools (5 Marks):**

| Tool | Role | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **FakeNet-NG / INetSim** | Simulates Internet services (DNS, HTTP, SMTP, FTP) | Captures all C2 callback attempts; lets malware "think" it has Internet |
| **Process Monitor** | Hooks file, registry, and process APIs | Reveals dropper locations, persistence keys, DLL injection |
| **Wireshark** | Captures raw network packets on the host-only interface | Exposes C2 IPs, protocols, beacon intervals, encrypted payloads |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **air-gapping** or **disabling the host's bridge network**. Without this, malware can escape the sandbox and infect the host. *(Examiner deducts 1 mark for missing isolation mention.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):** IoCs are forensic artifacts that, when observed on a system, indicate a security breach or compromise with high confidence. They are extracted from runtime behavior of malware.

**Four Categories (5 Marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **File-Based IoCs**
   - Examples: dropped executable hashes (e.g., `C:\Users\victim\AppData\Roaming\update.exe`, SHA-256: `a3f5...`).

2. **Registry-Based IoCs**
   - Examples: `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\Updater = "C:\...\update.exe"`.

3. **Network-Based IoCs**
   - Examples: outbound C2 domain `evil-c2.xyz`, IP `198.51.100.42`, port 443 over HTTPS.

4. **Process/Memory-Based IoCs**
   - Examples: suspicious child of `explorer.exe` named `svch0st.exe` (note the zero), mutex `Global\MyMalwareMutex123`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain **Process Monitor (ProcMon)** in detail. Discuss its three core capture categories and how each contributes to malware analysis. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** What is **entropy**? Derive the entropy formula and explain how it is used to identify **packed malware** before live analysis. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**ProcMon Overview (2 Marks):** Process Monitor is a Windows sysinternals tool that combines the features of **FileMon, RegMon, and Process Tracking** into a single real-time stream. It hooks kernel-level APIs via minifilter drivers.

**Three Core Capture Categories (5 Marks):**

1. **File System Activity (1.5 Marks)**
   - Captures: `CreateFile`, `WriteFile`, `DeleteFile`, `SetRenameInformationFile`.
   - Forensic Value: Identifies dropped payloads, locked files, and ransom-note creation.

2. **Registry Activity (1.5 Marks)**
   - Captures: `RegCreateKey`, `RegSetValue`, `RegDeleteKey`.
   - Forensic Value: Reveals Run-key persistence, service installations, COM hijacks.

3. **Process/Thread Activity (2 Marks)**
   - Captures: `CreateProcess`, `CreateThread` (remote), `LoadImage` (DLL).
   - Forensic Value: Detects process injection, hollowing, and DLL search-order hijacking.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Entropy Definition (1 Mark):** Entropy is a measure of randomness or disorder in a data sample, expressed in bits per byte.

**Formula Derivation (3 Marks):**

Let $N$ = total bytes, $c_i$ = count of byte $i$.

$$ p(x_i) = \frac{c_i}{N} $$

$$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \;\;\text{bits/byte} $$

For a uniformly random byte stream, $p(x_i) = 1/256$ for all $i$, giving $H = 8.0$.

**Packed Malware Identification (3 Marks):**

- Normal compiled code: $H \in [4.5, 6.0]$.
- Compressed/Encrypted code: $H \in [7.0, 8.0]$.
- High entropy in a PE section is a strong indicator that the section contains encrypted or compressed data, typical of packers like UPX, Themida, or custom crypters.
- Therefore, **high-entropy sections are scheduled for live analysis**, where the unpacking stub will execute and reveal the true code in memory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the entropy formula but **forget to mention the 7.0 threshold**. Always pair the formula with the **interpretive threshold table** to get full marks. *(1 mark deduction in past KTU papers.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Live Malware Analysis**

- **Definition:** Executing malware in an isolated environment to observe behavior.
- **Sandbox:** Isolated VM with snapshots, fake network, and instrumentation.
- **Mandatory Tools:** ProcMon, Wireshark, Regshot, Autoruns, FakeNet-NG/INetSim, Cuckoo.
- **Phases:** Acquire → Triage → Setup → Detonate → Observe → Report.
- **Key Heuristic:** Entropy $\geq 7.0$ bits/byte = likely packed.
- **IoC Categories:** File, Registry, Network, Process/Memory, Mutex.
- **Why Dynamic Beats Static:** Packing defeat + actual behavior + C2 verification.
- **MITRE ATT&CK:** Map all observed behaviors to tactics (TA0001–TA0040).
- **Safety:** Air-gap, snapshot rollback, never detonate on production.
- **KTU Buzzwords:** Sandboxing, Detonation, Behavioral Indicators, VMI, IoCs, YARA, STIX 2.1.
- **Output:** Forensic report + IoC bundle + ATT&CK heatmap.
- **Anti-Analysis Tricks to Watch For:** VM detection, sleep timers, sandbox fingerprinting, environment checks.
- **Memory Forensics Link:** RAM dumps analyzed in **Volatility** complement live analysis.
- **Examiner Tip:** Always mention **isolation** in any sandbox-related answer.
- **Threshold Rule:** $H \geq 7.0$ → packed; $D_{beh} > 0.20$ on Run keys → persistence.
- **Common Tools by KTU Module 2:** Cuckoo, FLARE-VM, REMnux, ANY.RUN, Hybrid Analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dead Malware Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dead Malware Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dead Malware Analysis** (also referred to as **Static Malware Analysis** or **Code Analysis**) is the forensic examination of a malicious software binary, script, or document *without* executing it in a live environment. In this methodology, the malware specimen is treated as an *inert artifact* — a "dead" piece of code — and is dissected through manual inspection, automated tooling, reverse engineering, and signature-based evaluation to extract Indicators of Compromise (IOCs), understand functional intent, and classify the threat family.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT741 - Module 2):**
> *Dead malware analysis* is the process of analyzing a malicious binary in an isolated, non-executing state to determine its origin, functionality, payloads, and Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) using static techniques such as hashing, fingerprinting, string extraction, metadata inspection, and disassembly. It is the **first-line triage** step in any malware forensics investigation pipeline.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework, this topic falls under the broader umbrella of **Malware Triage and Reverse Engineering**, and it is foundational to higher-order topics like unpacking, behavioral correlation, and YARA rule authoring.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Forensic Autopsy"

Imagine a forensic pathologist at a crime scene. The victim (the malware binary) is no longer alive — it cannot execute, propagate, or harm. The pathologist's job is to:

1. **Identify the body** → File fingerprinting (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256)
2. **Examine the wounds** → Disassembly, control-flow analysis
3. **Determine the cause of death / motive** → Payload analysis, C2 URL extraction
4. **Identify the suspect's signature** → YARA rules, family attribution

Just as a pathologist uses an X-ray (dissasembler) and a microscope (decompiler) instead of a heart-lung machine, a malware analyst uses **static tools** like Ghidra, IDA Pro, PE-studio, and `strings` instead of letting the binary run on a live host. The binary is "dead" — the analyst is performing a **post-mortem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Dead Analysis** = Binary is NOT executed → Tools: `strings`, `objdump`, Ghidra, PE-bear, DIE (Detect It Easy)
> - **Live Analysis** = Binary IS executed in a sandbox → Tools: Cuckoo Sandbox, Process Monitor, Wireshark
>
> This dichotomy is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU ESE papers.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Expected Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Static Analysis** | Inspection of binary without execution | Synonym for "Dead Analysis" |
| **Fingerprinting** | Generating unique cryptographic hashes of a file | First step of triage |
| **Packer** | Compression/encryption wrapper that obfuscates the original payload | Anti-static-analysis technique |
| **Disassembly** | Conversion of machine code (binary) into assembly mnemonics | Core reverse-engineering activity |
| **PE File** | Portable Executable format (Windows `.exe` / `.dll`) | Most common malware target |
| **String** | A sequence of printable ASCII/Unicode characters embedded in a binary | Reveals C2 URLs, registry keys, error messages |
| **YARA Rule** | Pattern-matching signature for malware family identification | Modern threat-intel standard |
| **Entropy** | Measure of randomness in a byte stream | Detector for packed/encrypted sections |
| **IOC** | Indicator of Compromise — observable artifact of an intrusion | Final output of static analysis |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Dead Analysis

- **Cryptographic Hash Lengths:** **MD5 = 128 bits (32 hex)**, **SHA-1 = 160 bits (40 hex)**, **SHA-256 = 256 bits (64 hex)**.
- **Shannon Entropy Range:** **0.0 bits/byte** (perfectly ordered, e.g., all `\x00`) to **8.0 bits/byte** (perfectly random, e.g., encrypted or compressed data). A value **> 7.0** is a strong indicator of packing or encryption.
- **Suspicious Import Threshold:** A binary importing **> 50 unique Win32 API functions** from sensitive categories (`VirtualAlloc`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `InternetOpen`, `CreateRemoteThread`) is statistically anomalous.
- **Section Count in PE:** Standard PE files have **3–5 sections** (`.text`, `.data`, `.rdata`, `.rsrc`, `.reloc`). Anomalies include **> 8 sections** or sections with **non-standard names** (e.g., `.upx0`, `.vmp0`).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "list indicators that a binary is packed," always cite: high entropy (>7.0), unusual section names (`.upx0`, `.aspack`), very small `.text` section, and a large overlay region.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dead Malware Analysis Pipeline (The "Static Triage Chain")

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to articulate the **sequential workflow** of dead analysis. The standard pipeline consists of **six logical phases**, executed in the following order:

### Phase 1 — Acquisition & Preservation
- The malware sample is received (via email attachment, sandbox export, or honeypot).
- It is placed in an **isolated, read-only forensic environment** (e.g., a Linux forensic workstation with no Windows emulation).
- The original sample is **bit-stream imaged** (using `dd` or FTK Imager) and the working copy is hashed.

### Phase 2 — File Fingerprinting
- Compute cryptographic hashes: **MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256**.
- Query online reputation databases: **VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, ThreatCrowd, Hybrid Analysis**.
- Compute the **file size**, **file type** (via magic-byte detection using the `file` command or `TrID`), and **compile timestamp** (from PE header).

### Phase 3 — Metadata & Header Inspection
- For PE files: parse the **DOS Header**, **PE Signature**, **COFF File Header**, and **Optional Header**.
- Extract: **TimeDateStamp**, **Subsystem**, **Characteristics**, **AddressOfEntryPoint**, **ImageBase**, **SectionAlignment**.
- For other formats: parse PDF (`%PDF-` header), Office docs (OLE/OOXML), or script (shebang line).

### Phase 4 — String Extraction
- Run the `strings` utility (default encoding ASCII, minimum length 4) and `strings -el` for Unicode (UTF-16LE).
- Manually filter and classify strings into:
  - **C2 Indicators** (URLs, IP addresses, domains)
  - **File path indicators** (`C:\Windows\System32\drivers\...`)
  - **Registry keys** (`HKLM\Software\Microsoft\...\Run`)
  - **Error messages** (often hardcoded by malware authors for debugging)
  - **Cryptographic constants** (RSA `0x10001`, AES S-box markers)

### Phase 5 — Disassembly & Decompilation
- Open the binary in **Ghidra** (free, NSA-developed) or **IDA Pro** (commercial, industry standard).
- Identify the **entry point function** and trace the **control flow graph (CFG)**.
- Recognize **cryptographic primitives** (constants like `0x67452301` = MD5 initialization vector).
- Map **imported APIs** to functionality (e.g., `CreateRemoteThread` + `VirtualAllocEx` ⇒ process injection).

### Phase 6 — IOC Generation & YARA Rule Authoring
- Compile findings into a **YARA rule** (Yet Another Recursive Acronym) for future detection.
- Export IOCs (hashes, IPs, domains, mutexes) in **STIX/TAXII** format for threat-intel sharing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters in KTU Answers:** Examiners award 1–2 marks for explaining the *rationale* of each step. For example: *"String extraction is performed in Phase 4 because it is a non-invasive technique that quickly reveals hardcoded network indicators without triggering anti-analysis traps."*

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Examiner's Mental Model)

| Step | Tool Example | What it Reveals | Why It's Critical |
|------|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Hashing | `md5sum`, `sha256sum` | Unique file identity | Cross-referencing against threat-intel DBs |
| File type | `file`, `DIE` | True format (strips extension tricks) | Identifies polyglot / disguised files |
| Strings | `strings`, `FLOSS` | Hardcoded C2s, paths, errors | Quick win for IOCs |
| PE Parsing | `PE-bear`, `CFF Explorer` | Section anomalies, timestamp | Detects packers & timestomping |
| Disassembly | Ghidra, IDA | Program logic, algorithm | Understands payload behavior |
| Entropy | `Detect-It-Easy`, `binwalk` | Section-by-section randomness | Triages packers before deep RE |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. Memorize the entropy formula — it is a **favourite 14-mark derivation question**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Shannon Entropy (per byte)** | $H = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | bits/byte | Range: $0.0 \le H \le 8.0$ |
| 2 | **MD5 Output Length** | $L_{MD5} = 128 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ hex chars}$ | bits | Deprecated for collision resistance |
| 3 | **SHA-1 Output Length** | $L_{SHA1} = 160 \text{ bits} = 40 \text{ hex chars}$ | bits | Deprecated since 2017 (SHAttered) |
| 4 | **SHA-256 Output Length** | $L_{SHA256} = 256 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ hex chars}$ | bits | Current KTU-recommended standard |
| 5 | **File Hash Identity** | $H_{file} = \text{Hash}(B_0 \Vert B_1 \Vert B_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert B_{N-1})$ | — | $B_i$ = i-th byte of file |
| 6 | **Probability of Byte $x_i$** | $p(x_i) = \dfrac{f_i}{N}$ | dimensionless | $f_i$ = frequency of byte value $i$, $N$ = total bytes |
| 7 | **PE Entry Point Offset** | $EP_{offset} = AddressOfEntryPoint - ImageBase$ | bytes | Used to jump to code section in a debugger |
| 8 | **File Size in PE Optional Header** | $SizeOfImage = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Max}(\text{VirtualSize}, \text{VirtualSize aligned})$ | bytes | Reveals loaded memory footprint |
| 9 | **N-gram Frequency (for code similarity)** | $F_{ngram} = \dfrac{\text{count}(g)}{\text{total grams}}$ | ratio | Used in malware family clustering |
| 10 | **Averaged Hash (aHash) for binary similarity** | $aHash = \dfrac{1}{64} \sum_{i=0}^{63} p_i \ge T$ | 64-bit | $T$ = mean pixel threshold; $p_i$ = bit value |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Threat Intelligence Platforms (TIPs)** consume IOCs extracted via dead analysis (e.g., MISP, Anomali).
- **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)** engines use YARA rules derived from static analysis to block malware at scale (e.g., CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender).
- **Incident Response Retainers** (Mandiant, Kaspersky GERT) bill hundreds of dollars per hour for static analysis reports.
- **Academic Malware Research** (e.g., the EMBER dataset by Endgame) uses static PE features (imports, byte histograms, entropy) to train ML classifiers for malware detection.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Connection:** Static analysis is the cornerstone of **DevSecOps pipelines**. Tools like **Clang Static Analyzer**, **Coverity**, and **Semgrep** perform source-level static analysis on code *before* it ships — the same fundamental philosophy as binary-level static analysis on malware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon Entropy Calculation for a Malware Section

> **Problem Statement:** A malware binary's `.text` section contains the following byte frequency distribution (out of 4096 total bytes):
> - Byte `\x90` (NOP) appears **2048** times.
> - Byte `\xCC` (INT3) appears **1024** times.
> - Byte `\x00` (NULL) appears **1024** times.
> - All other 253 byte values appear **0** times.
>
> Calculate the Shannon Entropy of this section and interpret whether it indicates a **packed** or **unpacked** section.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the Shannon Entropy Formula.**

$$
H = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

where $p(x_i)$ is the probability (relative frequency) of byte value $x_i$ in the section.

**Step 2: Compute the probability of each non-zero byte value.**

For byte `\x90` (NOP):
$$
p(\text{NOP}) = \frac{2048}{4096} = 0.5
$$

For byte `\xCC` (INT3):
$$
p(\text{INT3}) = \frac{1024}{4096} = 0.25
$$

For byte `\x00` (NULL):
$$
p(\text{NULL}) = \frac{1024}{4096} = 0.25
$$

For all other 253 byte values:
$$
p(x_i) = \frac{0}{4096} = 0 \quad \text{(contribute } 0 \text{ to the sum, as } 0 \cdot \log_2 0 \to 0\text{)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute each term of the sum.**

Term for `\x90`:
$$
- p(\text{NOP}) \cdot \log_2 p(\text{NOP}) = -0.5 \cdot \log_2 0.5
$$

Using the identity $\log_2 0.5 = -1$:
$$
- 0.5 \cdot (-1) = 0.5
$$

Term for `\xCC`:
$$
- p(\text{INT3}) \cdot \log_2 p(\text{INT3}) = -0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25
$$

Using the identity $\log_2 0.25 = -2$:
$$
- 0.25 \cdot (-2) = 0.5
$$

Term for `\x00`:
$$
- p(\text{NULL}) \cdot \log_2 p(\text{NULL}) = -0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25 = 0.5
$$

**Step 4: Sum all terms.**

$$
H = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation against the KTU Threshold Table.**

| Entropy Range | Interpretation |
|---------------|----------------|
| $0.0 \le H < 1.0$ | Highly repetitive (e.g., all zeros, all NOPs) |
| $1.0 \le H \le 6.0$ | Normal compiled code, ASCII text, or typical data |
| $6.0 < H \le 7.0$ | Compressed (e.g., gzip, ZIP) |
| $7.0 < H \le 8.0$ | **Encrypted or cryptographically random — likely packed** |

**Conclusion:** $H = 1.5$ bits/byte is **very low**, indicating the section is **NOT packed**. The pattern of `NOP-INT3-NULL` repetition suggests this might be a **NOP-sled** or a **sandbox-detection stalling routine** (common in evasive malware that wastes analyst time in debuggers).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PE File Entry Point Address Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A PE binary has the following header values extracted from `OptionalHeader`:
> - `AddressOfEntryPoint` = `0x00012340`
> - `ImageBase` = `0x00400000`
>
> Calculate the **file offset** of the entry point, given that the `.text` section has:
> - `VirtualAddress` = `0x00001000`
> - `PointerToRawData` = `0x00000400`
> - `VirtualSize` = `0x0000A000`
>
> The file alignment is `0x200` and Section Alignment is `0x1000`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the Relative Virtual Address (RVA) of the entry point.**

$$
RVA_{EP} = AddressOfEntryPoint - ImageBase
$$

$$
RVA_{EP} = 0x00012340 - 0x00400000 = 0x00012340 \quad \text{(since EP < ImageBase in low-alignment binaries)}
$$

Wait — re-evaluating. In standard PE binaries, the `AddressOfEntryPoint` is *already* an RVA (relative to `ImageBase`), not a VA. The correct interpretation:

$$
RVA_{EP} = AddressOfEntryPoint = 0x00012340
$$

**Step 2: Compute the offset within the `.text` section.**

$$
\text{Section Offset} = RVA_{EP} - \text{VirtualAddress}_{\text{text}}
$$

$$
\text{Section Offset} = 0x00012340 - 0x00001000 = 0x00011340
$$

**Step 3: Convert to File Offset by adding `PointerToRawData`.**

$$
\text{File Offset}_{EP} = \text{Section Offset} + \text{PointerToRawData}_{\text{text}}
$$

$$
\text{File Offset}_{EP} = 0x00011340 + 0x00000400 = 0x00011740
$$

**Step 4: Convert to Decimal (Optional, for KTU).**

$$
0x00011740 = 1 \times 16^4 + 1 \times 16^3 + 7 \times 16^2 + 4 \times 16^1 + 0 \times 16^0
$$

$$
= 65536 + 4096 + 1792 + 64 + 0 = 71{,}488 \text{ bytes from file start}
$$

**Step 5: Verification with VirtualSize Check.**

We must ensure $RVA_{EP}$ lies within the section's virtual range $[\text{VirtualAddress}, \text{VirtualAddress} + \text{VirtualSize}]$:
- Lower bound: $0x00001000$ ✓
- Upper bound: $0x00001000 + 0x0000A000 = 0x0000B000$ ✗

Since $0x00012340 > 0x0000B000$, the entry point is **OUTSIDE** the `.text` section — a **major red flag** indicating possible **section overlapping, hollow process injection, or a non-standard packer stub**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Students often forget that `AddressOfEntryPoint` is an RVA, not a VA, and incorrectly subtract `ImageBase`. KTU examiners specifically test this conceptual nuance for 1–2 marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hash-Based File Identity Verification

> **Problem Statement:** A forensic analyst receives two malware samples, `malware_A.bin` (1024 bytes) and `malware_B.bin` (1024 bytes). The MD5 of `malware_A` is `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592`. The MD5 of `malware_B` is `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592`. The first 4 bytes of `malware_A` are `hello`. The first 4 bytes of `malware_B` are `hellp`. Determine whether these are the same malware variant.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the Avalanche Property of Cryptographic Hash Functions.**

A secure hash function exhibits the **strict avalanche criterion (SAC)**: changing *any single bit* of the input should change *approximately 50%* of the output bits. For MD5, the 128-bit output should be entirely different.

**Step 2: Hash Computation in Theory.**

For a 1024-byte file $M = (B_0, B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_{1023})$:
$$
H(M) = \text{MD5}(B_0 \Vert B_1 \Vert \ldots \Vert B_{1023})
$$

**Step 3: Compare the two hashes.**

$$
H_A = \text{5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592}
$$

$$
H_B = \text{5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592}
$$

$$
H_A = H_B \implies \text{byte-for-byte identical files}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

Despite the first 4 bytes of the file *appearing* different (`hello` vs `hellp`), the **hashes are identical**. This is **cryptographically impossible under MD5** unless the files are **bit-for-bit identical**. The discrepancy in the displayed prefix is either:
- A **typo in the problem statement** (most likely — `hello` and `hellp` differ in the 5th character),
- Or an indication that the analyst misread the hex output (the first 4 bytes of the hex hash are the same prefix).

**KTU Takeaway:** Hash equality ⇒ **bitwise file equality** ⇒ **same malware variant**. This is the basis of **malware family clustering** and **threat-intel deduplication**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection for Dead Analysis

| Analysis Goal | Best Tool (Open Source) | Best Tool (Commercial) | Skill Level | Time to Master |
|---------------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------|----------------|
| File hashing | `md5sum`, `sha256sum` | HashCalc, FTK Imager | Beginner | 1 hour |
| String extraction | `strings`, `FLOSS` | BinText | Beginner | 2 hours |
| File type detection | `file`, `TrID`, `DIE` | — | Beginner | 2 hours |
| PE header parsing | `pefile` (Python), `PE-bear` | CFF Explorer, PEview | Intermediate | 1 day |
| Disassembly | Ghidra, `objdump` | IDA Pro, Binary Ninja | Advanced | 1 month |
| Decompilation | Ghidra, RetDec | IDA Pro (Hex-Rays) | Expert | 3 months |
| Packer detection | `DIE`, `Exeinfo PE` | — | Intermediate | 1 day |
| YARA rule creation | YARA CLI | CrowdStrike Falcon | Intermediate | 1 week |
| Family attribution | MalwareBazaar, VT | Mandiant Advantage | Beginner | 2 hours |
| Code similarity | `ssdeep`, `TLSH` | — | Intermediate | 3 days |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Dead Malware Analysis Workflow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Malware Sample] --> B[Compute Cryptographic Hashes]
    B --> C{Malware Sample}
    C --> D[File Type Detection]
    D --> E[String Extraction]
    E --> F[PE Header Parsing]
    F --> G[Entropy Analysis]
    G --> H{Is Packed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Identify Packer Signature]
    H -- No --> J[Direct Disassembly]
    I --> K[Static Unpacking or Dynamic Unpacking]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Control Flow Analysis]
    L --> M[Import API Mapping]
    M --> N[IOC Extraction]
    N --> O[YARA Rule Authoring]
    O --> P[Threat Intel Submission]
    P --> Q[Final Forensic Report]
```

**Figure Explanation:** This flowchart depicts the canonical six-stage KTU pipeline for dead malware analysis. Stage 1 (Acquisition) leads into Stage 2 (Fingerprinting via hashing). The binary is then triaged through header parsing and string extraction. The pivotal decision point is the **packing check** (Stage 3 entropy analysis): if entropy > 7.0, the binary is packed and must be unpacked before disassembly. Stage 4 (Disassembly) and Stage 5 (API Mapping) culminate in Stage 6 (IOC Extraction and YARA rule writing), which feeds into a threat-intel database and the final forensic report.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — PE File Structure (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DOS[DOS Header MZ Signature] --> DOSSTUB[DOS Stub Code]
    DOSSTUB --> PESIG[PE Signature PE00]
    PESIG --> COFF[COFF File Header]
    COFF --> OPTHD[Optional Header]
    OPTHD --> SECTHD1[Section Header .text]
    OPTHD --> SECTHD2[Section Header .data]
    OPTHD --> SECTHD3[Section Header .rdata]
    OPTHD --> SECTHD4[Section Header .rsrc]
    OPTHD --> SECTHD5[Section Header .reloc]
    SECTHD1 --> TEXT[.text Section - Code]
    SECTHD2 --> DATA[.data Section - Initialized Data]
    SECTHD3 --> RDATA[.rdata Section - Read-only Data]
    SECTHD4 --> RSRC[.rsrc Section - Resources]
    SECTHD5 --> RELOC[.reloc Section - Relocations]
    OPTHD --> DDIR[Data Directories]
    DDIR --> IAT[Import Address Table IAT]
    DDIR --> EDT[Export Directory]
    DDIR --> RDIR[Resource Directory]
    DDIR --> TLSDIR[TLS Directory]
    DDIR --> IDD[Import Descriptor]
    IAT --> IMP1[Import DLL1 kernel32.dll]
    IAT --> IMP2[Import DLL2 user32.dll]
    IAT --> IMP3[Import DLL3 wininet.dll]
```

**Figure Explanation:** This block-level functional architecture maps the anatomy of a Windows Portable Executable (PE) file — the most common target of dead analysis. The **DOS Header** (magic bytes `MZ`) and **DOS Stub** are legacy remnants from MS-DOS, preserved for backward compatibility. The **PE Signature** (`PE\0\0`) marks the start of the modern header. The **COFF File Header** contains machine type, number of sections, and timestamp. The **Optional Header** (mandatory in modern PE files) houses the entry point, image base, and 16 **Data Directories** including the critical **Import Address Table (IAT)** — the analyst's goldmine for understanding which Windows APIs the malware calls. The **Section Headers** describe the layout of `.text` (code), `.data` (variables), `.rdata` (constants), `.rsrc` (icons, version info), and `.reloc` (relocation info).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Logic: Packed vs. Unpacked Binary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Analyze PE File] --> ENT[Compute Entropy of .text Section]
    ENT --> Q1{Entropy > 7.0?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> PACKED[SUSPICIOUS - Likely Packed or Encrypted]
    PACKED --> ID[Check for Known Packer Signatures]
    ID --> Q2{Is Packer Identified?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> UNPACK1[Apply Static Unpacking Tool]
    Q2 -- No --> UNPACK2[Use Dynamic Analysis in Sandbox]
    UNPACK1 --> RE1[Resume Dead Analysis on Unpacked Binary]
    UNPACK2 --> RE1
    Q1 -- No --> CHECK2[Check Section Anomalies]
    CHECK2 --> Q3{Non-standard Section Names?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ANOM[Anomaly Detected - Manual Investigation]
    Q3 -- No --> CLEAN[Binary Appears Normal]
    CLEAN --> RE2[Proceed to Full Disassembly]
    ANOM --> RE2
    RE1 --> RE2
    RE2 --> END[End: Generate Forensic Report]
```

**Figure Explanation:** This decision tree illustrates the **triage decision logic** that KTU examiners expect students to articulate. The first branch tests **Shannon Entropy** against the **7.0 bits/byte threshold** — values above this indicate cryptographic randomness consistent with packing. The second branch examines **section name anomalies** — strings like `.upx0`, `.vmp0` (VMProtect), or `.aspack` are direct signatures of known packers. Both branches converge at the full disassembly stage, where the analyst can proceed to control-flow analysis with confidence that the code being viewed is the malware author's actual logic (not a stub).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECCT741]**

> *Define Dead Malware Analysis. List any four tools used for performing static analysis on a Windows PE binary.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Dead Malware Analysis, also known as static malware analysis, is the forensic process of examining a malicious binary *without* executing it. The malware sample is treated as an inert artifact and is dissected through file fingerprinting, string extraction, header parsing, and disassembly to extract Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) and understand its functional intent.

**Tools (1 Mark per tool, any 4):**
1. **Ghidra** — open-source disassembler and decompiler developed by the NSA.
2. **IDA Pro** — industry-standard commercial disassembler with the Hex-Rays decompiler plugin.
3. **PE-bear / CFF Explorer** — PE file header parsers.
4. **DIE (Detect It Easy)** — file type and packer detection tool.
5. **strings / FLOSS** — extract printable strings (FLOSS also extracts obfuscated strings).
6. **YARA** — pattern-matching engine for malware classification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT list sandbox tools (Cuckoo, Process Monitor) — those are *dynamic* (live) analysis tools. The question specifically asks for **static** tools. Students commonly lose 1 mark for this confusion.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — Comparative Reasoning
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECCT741]**

> *Differentiate between Dead (static) and Live (dynamic) malware analysis. State two advantages and two disadvantages of dead analysis.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Differentiation Table (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Dead (Static) Analysis | Live (Dynamic) Analysis |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Execution | Binary is **not** executed | Binary **is** executed in a sandbox |
| Environment | Isolated, read-only forensic workstation | Controlled sandbox/VM with instrumentation |
| Risk | **Zero** risk of infection | Risk of sandbox escape (low but non-zero) |
| Speed | Fast (minutes to hours) | Slow (requires malware runtime, often > 5 min) |
| Tooling | `strings`, Ghidra, PE-bear | Process Monitor, Wireshark, Cuckoo |

**Advantages of Dead Analysis (1 Mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Safety** — no risk of accidental infection since the binary never executes.
2. **Speed & Repeatability** — analysis can be paused, resumed, and shared without time pressure.

**Disadvantages of Dead Analysis (1 Mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Cannot observe runtime behavior** — anti-analysis tricks (conditional payloads, time-bombs) remain hidden.
2. **Obfuscation defeats inspection** — packed, encrypted, or virtualized binaries require unpacking before meaningful static analysis is possible.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Module Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Entropy, Packing & PE Analysis
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECCT741 — Modified]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete workflow of dead malware analysis** from sample acquisition to IOC generation. List at least **four phases** with their objectives and tool examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **concept of Shannon Entropy** in malware forensics. A 2048-byte section of a PE binary contains only two unique byte values: `\x4D` (ASCII `M`) appearing **1024** times and `\x5A` (ASCII `Z`) appearing **1024** times. Calculate the entropy and **interpret** whether this section is packed, normal code, or a stub. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
The student is expected to draw a **multi-stage pipeline** with at least 4 boxes. A correct representation is:

```
[Acquire] → [Hash] → [String Extract] → [PE Parse] → [Disassemble] → [IOC / YARA]
```

**Phase Descriptions (4 Marks — 1 per phase):**

1. **Acquisition & Hashing (1 Mark):** The binary is acquired in an isolated environment, and cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-256) are computed to uniquely identify the sample. Tools: `md5sum`, `sha256sum`, FTK Imager. The hash is then queried against VirusTotal/MalwareBazaar to identify the malware family.

2. **String Extraction (1 Mark):** ASCII and Unicode strings are extracted using `strings` or `FLOSS`. This reveals hardcoded C2 URLs, file paths, registry keys, and error messages — the "low-hanging fruit" of static analysis.

3. **PE Header Parsing (1 Mark):** Tools like `PE-bear`, `CFF Explorer`, or the Python `pefile` library parse the PE headers to extract the entry point, sections, imports, and timestamp. Section anomalies (`.upx0`, `.vmp0`) and high-entropy sections indicate packing.

4. **Disassembly & Decompilation (1 Mark):** The binary is opened in Ghidra or IDA Pro. The analyst traces the control-flow graph from the entry point, maps imported APIs (`VirtualAlloc`, `CreateRemoteThread`) to functionality (process injection), and identifies the malware's payload logic.

5. **(Bonus) IOC Generation (Optional, 1 Mark):** IOCs (hashes, IPs, mutexes, registry keys) are exported and a YARA rule is authored for future detection.

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:

**Concept of Shannon Entropy (2 Marks):**
Shannon Entropy, in the context of malware forensics, is a quantitative measure of the **randomness or unpredictability** of byte values within a binary section. It is computed per-byte using the formula:

$$
H = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

where $p(x_i)$ is the relative frequency of byte value $x_i$. Entropy is used as a **packer detector**: legitimate compiled code typically has entropy between 4.0 and 6.0, while packed or encrypted sections exhibit entropy > 7.0 (approaching 8.0, the theoretical maximum for perfectly random data).

**Step-by-Step Calculation (3 Marks):**

Total bytes: $N = 2048$.
Frequencies: $f(\text{M}) = 1024$, $f(\text{Z}) = 1024$.

Probabilities:
$$
p(\text{M}) = \frac{1024}{2048} = 0.5
$$

$$
p(\text{Z}) = \frac{1024}{2048} = 0.5
$$

Entropy:
$$
H = -[p(\text{M}) \log_2 p(\text{M}) + p(\text{Z}) \log_2 p(\text{Z})]
$$

$$
H = -[0.5 \cdot \log_2 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \log_2 0.5]
$$

$$
H = -[0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.5 \cdot (-1)]
$$

$$
H = -[-0.5 - 0.5] = 1.0 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

**Interpretation (2 Marks):**
The entropy $H = 1.0$ bit/byte is **very low**, indicating the section is **NOT packed and NOT encrypted**. The pattern `\x4D\x5A` corresponds to the ASCII letters `M` and `Z` — the **DOS Header Magic Bytes** (`MZ`). This is the classic signature of a Windows PE file's DOS stub, which is a known constant region with no variability. The analyst can safely skip this section in deeper analysis and proceed to higher-entropy sections like `.text`.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### Question B (14 Marks) — Hashing, Signatures & YARA
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECCT741 — Modified]**

> **(a)** Explain the role of **cryptographic hashing** in dead malware analysis. Compute and compare the **MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256** output lengths in bits, and state which algorithm is **recommended for malware sample identification** in 2024 along with the cryptographic reason. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **structure of a YARA rule** for malware detection. Author a YARA rule that detects a hypothetical malware family called `KeralaBanker` which creates a mutex named `Global\KeralaBankerMutexV2` and contains the string `cmd.exe /c vssadmin delete shadows /all`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:

**Role of Cryptographic Hashing (3 Marks):**
Cryptographic hashing plays a **four-fold role** in dead malware analysis:

1. **Unique File Identification (1 Mark):** A cryptographic hash produces a fixed-length, deterministic "fingerprint" of a binary. Any single-bit change in the input produces a completely different hash (the *avalanche effect*). This allows analysts to refer to a specific malware variant unambiguously across reports, threat-intel feeds, and sandbox logs.

2. **Reputation Lookup (1 Mark):** Hashes are submitted to online databases (VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, Hybrid Analysis) to check whether the sample has been previously analyzed and classified.

3. **Sample Integrity (1 Mark):** During forensic acquisition, hashes are computed to ensure the working copy is bit-for-bit identical to the original evidence, preserving the chain of custody.

**Algorithm Comparison Table (3 Marks — 1 Mark per row):**

| Algorithm | Output Length (bits) | Output Length (hex) | Status (2024) |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **MD5** | 128 bits | 32 hex chars | **Broken** (collision attacks feasible) |
| **SHA-1** | 160 bits | 40 hex chars | **Deprecated** (SHAttered attack, 2017) |
| **SHA-256** | 256 bits | 64 hex chars | **Recommended** — part of SHA-2 family, no known practical collisions |

**Recommendation (1 Mark):**
**SHA-256 is recommended** for malware sample identification in 2024. It is part of the SHA-2 family standardized by NIST (FIPS 180-4), has no known practical collision attacks, and provides a sufficient 128-bit security level against birthday attacks. MD5 and SHA-1 are **forbidden** in modern forensic practice due to demonstrated collision vulnerabilities (MD5: Flame malware, 2012; SHA-1: SHAttered PoC, Google/CWI, 2017).

#### Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:

**YARA Rule Structure (3 Marks):**
A YARA rule consists of three sections:
1. **Meta Section (1 Mark):** Contains descriptive metadata (author, date, description, reference hash).
2. **Strings Section (1 Mark):** Defines patterns (text strings, hex bytes, regex) to match.
3. **Condition Section (1 Mark):** Boolean logic specifying when the rule fires (e.g., `all of them`, `$a and $b`).

**Authored YARA Rule (4 Marks):**

```yara
rule KeralaBanker_Malware_Family
{
    meta:
        author = "KTU Malware Forensics Student"
        description = "Detects KeralaBanker ransomware family"
        date = "2024-12-01"
        reference = "PECCT741 Module 2 Case Study"
        hash_known_sample = "a1b2c3d4e5f6..."

    strings:
        $mutex = "Global\\KeralaBankerMutexV2" ascii wide
        $vsscmd = "cmd.exe /c vssadmin delete shadows /all" ascii
        $ransom_note = "Your files have been encrypted" ascii

    condition:
        $mutex and $vsscmd and $ransom_note
}
```

**Explanation of the Rule (Bonus, up to 1 Mark):**
- The `ascii wide` modifier on `$mutex` ensures the rule matches both ASCII and UTF-16LE encodings (Windows APIs internally use wide strings).
- The `condition` requires **all three strings** to be present — this combination is highly specific and minimizes false positives.
- The `$vsscmd` string is a strong IOC because deleting Volume Shadow Copies is a known anti-recovery tactic of ransomware families.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to escape backslashes in the mutex name (`Global\\KeralaBankerMutexV2`, not `Global\KeralaBankerMutexV2`). This single-character error renders the rule syntactically invalid and costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)

- [ ] **Dead Analysis = No Execution.** Tools: `strings`, Ghidra, PE-bear, DIE. NOT Cuckoo, NOT Process Monitor.
- [ ] **Hashing Standards (2024):** Use **SHA-256** (256 bits, 64 hex). MD5/SHA-1 are deprecated.
- [ ] **Shannon Entropy Threshold:** $> 7.0$ bits/byte ⇒ **packed or encrypted**. Normal code: 4.0–6.0.
- [ ] **PE File Structure Hierarchy:** DOS Header → DOS Stub → PE Signature (`PE\0\0`) → COFF Header → Optional Header → Section Headers → Sections.
- [ ] **AddressOfEntryPoint is an RVA**, not a VA. Do NOT subtract `ImageBase` from it.
- [ ] **Top Static IOCs to Extract:** Cryptographic hashes, C2 URLs/IPs, mutex names, registry keys, file paths, embedded PDB paths.
- [ ] **YARA Rule = meta + strings + condition.** Always escape backslashes (`\\`).
- [ ] **Packer Signatures:** High entropy + non-standard section names (`.upx0`, `.vmp0`, `.aspack`) + small `.text` + large overlay.
- [ ] **First step in any forensic pipeline:** Compute SHA-256, query VirusTotal, then proceed.
- [ ] **Anti-Analysis Detection (Static Clues):** `IsDebuggerPresent` API import, `rdtsc` instruction, suspicious TLS callbacks, `PIMAGE_NT_HEADERS` checksums.

### 📚 Key Terms to Memorize for KTU Viva / ESE

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|------|--------------------|
| IOC | Indicator of Compromise — observable artifact proving intrusion |
| YARA | Pattern-matching tool for malware classification |
| PDB | Program Database — debugging symbols revealing original file paths |
| PE | Portable Executable — Windows `.exe` / `.dll` binary format |
| IAT | Import Address Table — list of functions imported from DLLs |
| RVA | Relative Virtual Address — offset from `ImageBase` |
| Entropy | Randomness measure (0–8 bits/byte) for packer detection |
| Sandbox | Isolated environment for safely executing malware |
| Packer | Compressor/encryptor that hides malware code from static analysis |
| Magic Bytes | File signature bytes identifying true file type (e.g., `MZ` for PE) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "explain dead malware analysis," always structure your answer as: **(1)** Definition, **(2)** Sequential pipeline (acquire → hash → strings → parse → disassemble → IOCs), **(3)** Tool examples, **(4)** Advantages, **(5)** Limitations, and **(6)** A worked example (entropy calculation or hash comparison). This 6-part structure guarantees full marks under KTU's structured marking scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analyzing Traces of Malware
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analyzing Traces of Malware

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Malware Trace Analysis** is the systematic forensic discipline of identifying, collecting, examining, and interpreting the residual artifacts (digital traces) left behind by malicious software on a host system, network infrastructure, or volatile memory. These traces constitute the *forensic footprint* of the malware and are categorized as **persistent artifacts** (survive reboot, stored on disk) or **volatile artifacts** (exist in RAM, network state, or running processes).

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, trace analysis maps directly to:
- **CO2:** Apply forensic methodologies to identify and analyze malware artifacts.
- **CO3:** Evaluate the operational behavior of malicious code through residual evidence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Module 2 of PECCT741 specifically requires students to demonstrate competency in recognizing **file system traces, registry artifacts, network logs, prefetch files, event logs, and timeline reconstruction** — all of which fall under the umbrella of "traces of malware."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a burglar breaks into a house. Even if the burglar escapes, they leave behind:
- **Fingerprints** on doorknobs (analogous to *registry keys touched*),
- **Footprints in mud** (analogous to *file system modifications*),
- **Security camera footage** (analogous to *event logs*),
- **A discarded glove** (analogous to *dropped malware binaries in temp folders*),
- **A getaway car's license plate caught on traffic cameras** (analogous to *network traffic captures*).

A malware forensic analyst is essentially the **digital detective** who reconstructs the burglar's entire movements using only these residual clues. The "crime scene" is the compromised system, and the "evidence" is the trace data.

## 1.3 Classification of Traces — High-Level Overview

| Trace Type | Persistence | Storage Location | Example |
|------------|-------------|------------------|---------|
| **File System Traces** | Persistent | NTFS/FAT partitions | Created/modified files, alternate data streams |
| **Registry Artifacts** | Persistent | Windows Registry hives | `Run` keys, `Image File Execution Options` |
| **Execution Evidence** | Persistent | `%SystemRoot%\Prefetch` | `.pf` prefetch files (Windows) |
| **Event Log Traces** | Persistent | `%SystemRoot%\System32\winevt\Logs` | Security/System/Application logs |
| **Network Traces** | Semi-persistent | PCAP files, firewall logs | DNS queries, C2 beaconing |
| **Memory Traces** | Volatile | RAM | Injected threads, hollow processes |
| **Volatile System State** | Volatile | RAM, swap | Network connections, process list |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** The **order of volatility (OOV)** dictates forensic priority. Per RFC 3227, CPU registers and RAM are acquired first, then disk artifacts. KTU examiners frequently test this hierarchy.

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standards

- **MACB Times:** Modified, Accessed, Changed (metadata), Born (created). These four timestamps form the cornerstone of **timeline forensics**.
- **MFT Entry Size (NTFS):** Typically **1024 bytes** per record.
- **Prefetch File Hash (Windows 7+):** A **SHA-1 hash** of the executable path (up to 30 characters, truncated).
- **Default Prefetch Retention:** Windows keeps the **last 1024 executed programs** in the prefetch folder.
- **Event Log Default Size:** **20 MB** per log (Security log: **20 MB**, Application/System: configurable up to **4 GB**).
- **Standard Reference Frameworks:** **NIST SP 800-86**, **RFC 3227**, **ISO/IEC 27037**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of Trace Categories

### 2.1.1 File System Traces

When malware executes, it interacts with the file system in four canonical ways:

1. **File Creation** — Dropping payloads (e.g., in `%TEMP%`, `%APPDATA%`, `C:\ProgramData`).
2. **File Modification** — Patching legitimate binaries (e.g., DLL search-order hijacking).
3. **File Deletion** — Anti-forensic cleanup (often leaving MFT-resident entries).
4. **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** — NTFS-specific hiding mechanism, e.g., `malware.exe:hidden_zone`.

**Why it matters:** Even deleted files persist in the **Master File Table (MFT)** until the record is reused. The `$MFT` itself is a meta-file and can be parsed using tools like `analyzeMFT`.

### 2.1.2 Windows Registry Traces

The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database (`hives`) that stores low-level OS settings. Malware abuses specific keys for **persistence**:

$$
\text{Persistence Keys} = \{HKLM\backslash SOFTWAREMicrosoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run, \ldots\}
$$

High-yield registry locations to memorize for KTU exams:

- `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` — Auto-start on user logon.
- `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` — Executes once then deletes.
- `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders` — Startup folder redirection.
- `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services` — Malicious service registration.
- `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options` — Debugger hijacking (e.g., "SilentProcessExit").
- `HKCR\exefile\shell\open\command` — EXE association hijack.
- `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Browser Helper Objects` — IE BHO injection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Registry Hive Files (on disk):** `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`, `SAM`, `DEFAULT`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`. Knowing the on-disk hive filename is essential for offline forensic analysis.

### 2.1.3 Prefetch Files

Windows Prefetch is a **performance optimization feature** introduced in Windows XP. It pre-loads commonly used applications into memory. Crucially for forensics, it also creates a record of program execution.

- **Location:** `C:\Windows\Prefetch`
- **Naming Convention:** `EXECUTABLENAME-XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX.pf` (e.g., `CMD.EXE-4A01B4D2.pf`)
- **Hash Function:** SHA-1 truncated to **8 hex characters** (Windows XP) or **SHA-1 of full Unicode path** with **17 hex chars** (modern versions).
- **Retention:** Last **1024** executions (configurable via `EnablePrefetcher`).

**Last Execution Time (LET)** can be extracted from the prefetch file header — an invaluable timeline artifact.

### 2.1.4 Event Log Traces

Windows Event Logs are XML-based (`.evtx` since Vista) records of system, security, and application events. Forensic value:

| Event ID | Source | Significance |
|----------|--------|--------------|
| **4624** | Security | Successful logon |
| **4625** | Security | Failed logon (brute-force indicator) |
| **4688** | Security | New process created (with command line if audit enabled) |
| **4720** | Security | User account created |
| **7045** | System | Service installed (ransomware/persistence indicator) |
| **1001** | Application | Windows Error Reporting (crash dumps) |
| **1102** | Security | Audit log cleared (anti-forensic!) |

> [!WARNING]
> Event log clearing generates **Event ID 1102**. Smart attackers wipe this first, so its absence is itself an indicator of compromise (IOC).

### 2.1.5 Network Traces

- **DNS logs:** Unusual TXT record queries, high-entropy subdomains (DGA - Domain Generation Algorithm detection).
- **NetFlow / PCAP:** Beaconing patterns (regular interval C2 traffic).
- **Firewall logs:** Outbound connections on non-standard ports.
- **Proxy logs:** User-Agent anomalies, TLS fingerprinting.

### 2.1.6 Memory Traces

Volatile artifacts requiring tools like **Volatility**:
- Process injection evidence (VAD — Virtual Address Descriptor anomalies).
- Injected DLLs (mismatched in-memory vs. on-disk).
- Unlinked/hollowed processes (legitimate name, malicious memory).
- Network connections mapped to PIDs.
- Command-line history (`cmdscan`, `consoles` plugins).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Trace Persistence

| Mechanism | Underlying Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Registry persistence** | Ensures execution on every reboot. |
| **Scheduled tasks** | Trigger at specific time/event. |
| **Services** | Run with `SYSTEM` privileges pre-user-logon. |
| **WMI event subscriptions** | Fileless persistence — no on-disk binary. |
| **Bootkits** | Survive OS reinstall via MBR/VBR. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

| Reference | Value / Location | Use Case |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| **Prefetch hash algorithm** | `SHA-1(ExecutablePath)[0:8]` (XP) or `[0:17]` (Win7+) | Correlate execution to binary |
| **MFT record size** | **1024 bytes** (default NTFS) | File system forensics |
| **Cluster slack** | `SectorSize − DataBytes` | Hidden data detection |
| **RAM acquisition priority** | **0** (highest, per RFC 3227) | Order of volatility |
| **Order of Volatility** | Registers → Cache → RAM → Disk → Off-site | Forensic acquisition order |
| **Event log default size** | **20 MB** (Security) | Log retention check |
| **Prefetch retention** | **1024 files** | Determine if older traces overwritten |
| **MACB time source** | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (file) vs. `$FILE_NAME` (MFT) | Timeline anti-tampering |
| **Common log directory** | `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs` | `.evtx` files location |
| **Registry transaction log** | `SOFTWARE.LOG`, `SYSTEM.LOG` etc. | Recover deleted keys |
| **Volume Shadow Copy** | `\\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyN` | Access historical artifacts |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response (IR):** Trace analysis is the foundation of every IR playbook (NIST SP 800-61).
- **Threat Intelligence:** IOCs extracted from traces feed SIEM rules and YARA signatures.
- **Legal Forensics:** Traces are admissible as digital evidence under Section 65B of the Indian IT Act, 2000.
- **Malware Attribution:** Toolmarks, PDB paths, and timestamps help attribute campaigns to APT groups.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Procedural Derivations

## 3.1 Master Forensic Workflow — Step-by-Step

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes process-oriented learning. Below is the exhaustive 8-step trace analysis pipeline:

### Step 1: Evidence Identification & Volatility Triage
$$
\text{Priority} = f(\text{Decay Rate}) \Rightarrow \text{Highest Decay} \Rightarrow \text{Acquire First}
$$

In practice: live RAM → swap/pagefile → network state → running processes → disk images → log files.

### Step 2: Evidence Preservation
Generate cryptographic hash to ensure integrity:

$$
H_{\text{evidence}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{evidence}_i)
$$

Verify with:
$$
\text{Verify}(H_{\text{computed}}, H_{\text{stored}}) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{TRUE} & \text{if intact} \\
\text{FALSE} & \text{if tampered}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Extraction of File System Traces
Parse the MFT to extract `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME` attributes:

$$
T_{\text{MACB}} = \{M, A, C, B\} \in \mathbb{Z}_{\text{Unix-Epoch}}
$$

Compare `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` vs `$FILE_NAME` MACB times. If they differ significantly, this strongly indicates **timestamp tampering (timestomping)** — a hallmark of sophisticated malware.

### Step 4: Registry Hive Analysis
Mount acquired hives using `regload` or `RegistryExplorer`. For each suspected key:

$$
\text{Suspicion Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot I_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of indicator $i$ and $I_i \in \{0,1\}$ is its presence. For example, `Run` key with non-Microsoft binary and unsigned path → high suspicion.

### Step 5: Prefetch Examination
For each `.pf` file, decode the binary header to extract:

$$
\text{LET}_{\text{prefetch}} = \text{FileHeader}_{\text{offset 0x80:0x88}} \text{ (Win7+)}
$$

Cross-reference `LET` with malware infection window to confirm execution.

### Step 6: Event Log Correlation
Build an event super-timeline:

$$
E_{\text{super}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \text{Events}_i \quad \text{sorted by } t_{\text{event}}
$$

Identify the kill chain using Lockheed Martin's **Cyber Kill Chain** phases mapped to event IDs.

### Step 7: Network Trace Forensics
For PCAP analysis, compute inter-arrival times of C2 beacons:

$$
\Delta t_k = t_{k+1} - t_k \quad \forall k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N-1\}
$$

If $\text{Var}(\Delta t) < \epsilon$ (low variance), this suggests **automated beaconing** (a classic RAT indicator).

### Step 8: Timeline Reconstruction & Reporting
Merge all timelines using a common temporal axis:

$$
T_{\text{final}} = \text{merge}(T_{\text{FS}}, T_{\text{Registry}}, T_{\text{Prefetch}}, T_{\text{EventLog}}, T_{\text{Network}})
$$

This super-timeline is the **deliverable artifact** of trace analysis.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trace Correlation

| Trace Source | Indicates | Common Tool | Typical Artifact |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| `$MFT` | File create/modify/delete | `analyzeMFT`, `MFTECmd` | `bodyfile` output |
| Registry `Run` keys | Persistence | `RegistryExplorer`, `RegRipper` | `.reg` exports |
| `.pf` files | Program execution | `PECmd`, `WindowsPrefetch` | LET + run count |
| `.evtx` files | Logon, process, service | `evtx_dump`, `Chainsaw`, `Hayabusa` | CSV/JSON events |
| Prefetch + AmCache | Execution + hash | `AmcacheParser` | SHA-1 of executed binary |
| Shimcache (AppCompatCache) | Program execution (kernel mode) | `AppCompatCacheParser` | Execution entry |
| UserAssist | GUI program execution (ROT-13 encoded) | `UserAssistView`, `RegRipper` | Decoded paths |
| BAM (Background Activity Moderator) | Executed full paths | `bam_parser.py` | Path + execution time |
| SRUM (System Resource Usage Monitor) | Resource usage history | `srum-dump`, `SRUMExtractor2` | Process network bytes, CPU time |
| Scheduled Tasks XML | Task-based persistence | `tasks_parser.py` | `<Exec>` tags |
| Shimcache | Compatibility fixes/persistence | `AppCompatCacheParser` | Execution record |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Timestomp Detection Logic

A forensic investigator suspects a malware binary `payload.exe` has been timestomped. The analyst has the following data:

$$
T_{SI} = \{M: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01, A: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01, C: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01, B: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01\}
$$

$$
T_{FN} = \{M: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01, A: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01, C: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01, B: 2024-03-15\ 10:22:01\}
$$

$$
T_{\text{Prefetch-LET}} = 2024\text{-}03\text{-}17\ 14{:}55{:}22
$$

The logic proceeds:

$$
T_{SI} = T_{FN} \quad \text{(identical — suspicious on its own)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{Prefetch-LET}} - T_{SI.B} = (2024\text{-}03\text{-}17) - (2024\text{-}03\text{-}15) = 2\ \text{days, } 4\ \text{h, } 33\ \text{min}
$$

Since the file cannot have been *executed* before it was *created* (in this analyst's reconstructed timeline), and prefetch is generally reliable:

$$
\therefore\ T_{SI} \text{ and } T_{FN} \text{ have been falsified.}
$$

**Conclusion:** Timestomp confirmed. The attacker used tools like `timestomp.exe` (from Metasploit) or PowerShell `Set-ItemProperty` to backdate the file to 2 days prior to actual first execution.
