# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define malware trace analysis. List any four Windows registry keys commonly abused for malware persistence.**

**Model Answer:**
Malware trace analysis is the forensic process of identifying, collecting, and interpreting residual artifacts left behind by malicious software on a host or network to reconstruct its activities and impact. It encompasses file system, registry, event log, prefetch, and memory artifacts.

Four registry keys commonly abused for persistence:
1. `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
2. `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce`
3. `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services`
4. `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options`

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Explain the concept of MACB times in NTFS. Why is comparing `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` with `$FILE_NAME` timestamps important in forensic investigations?**

**Model Answer:**
MACB refers to **M**odified, **A**ccessed, **C**hanged (metadata), and **B**orn (created) timestamps stored as file attributes in NTFS. The `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` attribute is mutable and can be altered by user-mode APIs, while `$FILE_NAME` is modified only by the file system itself (kernel mode).

**Importance in forensics:**
- When an attacker uses timestomping tools, `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` is altered but `$FILE_NAME` retains the original time.
- Comparing both attributes reveals tampering.
- It helps reconstruct accurate file activity timelines and detect anti-forensic techniques.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Windows Registry architecture** and describe how malware exploits registry keys for **persistence and execution**. List at least **six** specific registry locations used by malware along with their forensic significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **NTFS file system traces** left by malware. Explain MFT parsing, Alternate Data Streams (ADS), and the concept of **timestamp tampering (timestomping)** with appropriate examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Windows Registry Architecture & Malware Exploitation:**

The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database organized as a tree of **keys** (folders) and **values** (data entries). The architecture has five root keys:
- `HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT (HKCR)`
- `HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU)`
- `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM)`
- `HKEY_USERS (HKU)`
- `HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG (HKCC)`

On disk, these map to hive files: `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`, `SAM`, `DEFAULT`, `NTUSER.DAT`, and `UsrClass.dat`.

**Six registry locations used by malware:**

| Registry Path | Purpose | Forensic Significance |
|---------------|---------|----------------------|
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | Auto-start on logon | Indicates persistence mechanism |
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` | Single execution, then delete | Evidence of one-time payload delivery |
| `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<name>` | Service registration | Persistence at kernel level |
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\<exe>` | Debugger hijack | Triggers payload when legitimate app runs |
| `HKCR\exefile\shell\open\command` | EXE association hijack | All executable launches trigger malware |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders` | Startup folder redirect | Persistence via dropped LNK file |

**(b) NTFS File System Traces:**

NTFS organizes data using the **Master File Table (MFT)**, where every file/directory has at least one **MFT entry** (typically 1024 bytes). Key forensic elements:

1. **MFT Parsing:** Each MFT entry contains attributes such as `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`, `$FILE_NAME`, `$DATA`, and `$LOGGED_UTILITY_STREAM`. Forensic tools like `MFTECmd` parse the MFT and produce a `bodyfile` for timeline analysis.

2. **Alternate Data Streams (ADS):** NTFS supports multiple data streams per file. Malware hides payloads in streams like `innocent.txt:malware.exe`. Detection uses:
$$
\text{Command: dir /R} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Get-Item -Path *.txt -Stream *}
$$

3. **Timestomping:** A technique where malware alters the `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` attribute using APIs like `SetFileTime()` to disguise its true creation time. Forensic detection:
$$
\text{If } T_{SI} \neq T_{FN} \text{ OR } T_{FN.B} > T_{\text{actual-event-log-entry}} \Rightarrow \text{Timestomp suspected}
$$

**Example:** The Metasploit `timestomp.exe` utility allows setting all four MACB times to a specified value, commonly backdating files to blend with system binaries.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **Windows Prefetch mechanism**. How does it serve as a forensic trace for malware execution? Discuss the structure of a `.pf` file and the algorithm used to generate its filename. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Windows Event Log forensics** in the context of malware investigation. List at least **eight critical Event IDs** with their forensic significance and explain how log clearing by an attacker is itself detectable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Windows Prefetch Mechanism:**

Prefetch is a Windows performance feature that pre-loads frequently used applications into memory during boot or application launch to reduce startup time. As a side effect, it records execution metadata useful for forensics.

**Location:** `C:\Windows\Prefetch`

**Filename Algorithm:**
$$
H_{\text{prefetch}} = \text{Truncate}(\text{SHA-1}(\text{UnicodePathOfExecutable}), n)
$$

where $n = 8$ for Windows XP (8 hex chars) and $n = 17$ for Windows 7 and later.

**Example:**
- Executable: `C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe`
- SHA-1 hash: `4A01B4D289C6E2A1...`
- Prefetch file: `CMD.EXE-4A01B4D2.pf`

**Structure of a `.pf` file:**
The file begins with a header containing:
- **Version** (Win 8.1: v30, Win 10: v31)
- **File Info block** containing embedded binary of the executable
- **Metrics Array** — run count, last run time(s) (up to 8 in Win 8+)
- **File Metrics** — list of files loaded by the executable

**Forensic utility:**
- The **Last Execution Time (LET)** is extracted from offset `0x80` (Win 7) or `0x80:0x128` (Win 10 super-fetch records).
- Default retention: **1024** files — older entries are recycled.
- Tools: `PECmd`, `Windows-Prefetch-Parser`.

**(b) Windows Event Log Forensics:**

Windows Event Logs (`.evtx` files) are XML-based records of significant system, security, and application events stored at `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs`.

**Eight Critical Event IDs:**

| Event ID | Log | Description | Forensic Use |
|----------|-----|-------------|--------------|
| **4624** | Security | Successful logon | Tracks user sessions, RDP/console logons |
| **4625** | Security | Failed logon | Detects brute-force attacks |
| **4688** | Security | New process created | Captures command-line if audit enabled |
| **4697** | Security | Service installed | Detects malicious service creation |
| **4720** | Security | User account created | Detects rogue account creation |
| **7045** | System | Service installed | Alternative to 4697 |
| **1102** | Security | Audit log cleared | Anti-forensic indicator |
| **1001** | Application | Windows Error Reporting | Captures crash dumps, useful for exploit analysis |

**Detection of log clearing:**
- Event ID **1102** is generated when the security log is cleared.
- If the log is empty but historical artifacts (file system, registry) suggest activity, this is a strong indicator of anti-forensics.
- Attackers may also clear the `Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational` log to hide malicious script execution.
- Mitigation: Enable **Event Log Subscription** or **Windows Event Forwarding (WEF)** to a write-once collector.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** Event ID 4624 (logon) with 4634 (logoff). Examiners deduct 1 mark for each mix-up.
> 2. **Prefetch filenames** are *not* based on MD5 — they are SHA-1. Writing MD5 in your answer costs 2 marks.
> 3. **MACB ordering** — "C" stands for "Changed (MFT entry metadata)", NOT "Created." Created is "B" (Born).
> 4. **Registry hive location** — do not write "registry is in the registry." Mention the on-disk hive filenames (`SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `NTUSER.DAT`) to score full marks.
> 5. **Event ID 1102** is generated only for the *Security* log. Clearing the System or Application log does not generate it — a common student error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Trace analysis** = identifying residual artifacts of malware on host/network.
- ✅ **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** CPU → RAM → Disk → Off-site media.
- ✅ **MACB times:** Modified, Accessed, Changed, Born. Source: `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME`.
- ✅ **MFT record size:** 1024 bytes; resides in `$MFT` meta-file.
- ✅ **Registry persistence keys:** `Run`, `RunOnce`, `Services`, `IFEO`, `Shell\open\command`, `Browser Helper Objects`.
- ✅ **Registry on-disk hives:** `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `DEFAULT`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`.
- ✅ **Prefetch:** Located in `C:\Windows\Prefetch`; filename = `<EXECNAME>-<SHA1_TRUNC>.pf`; 1024 file retention.
- ✅ **Event ID 1102** = log cleared (anti-forensic IOC).
- ✅ **Event ID 4624/4625** = successful/failed logon.
- ✅ **Event ID 4688** = process creation (requires command-line auditing).
- ✅ **Event ID 7045** = service installation.
- ✅ **ADS (Alternate Data Streams):** NTFS-specific; viewed via `dir /R` or PowerShell `Get-Item -Stream`.
- ✅ **Timestomping** = alteration of `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`; detect by comparing with `$FILE_NAME` and prefetch LET.
- ✅ **Memory forensics tool:** Volatility 3; plugins — `pslist`, `pstree`, `malfind`, `netscan`, `cmdscan`.
- ✅ **AmCache** stores SHA-1 of executed binaries — gold mine for malware hash correlation.
- ✅ **Shimcache (AppCompatCache)** records program execution at kernel level.
- ✅ **BAM (Background Activity Moderator)** records full executable paths of recently run programs (Win 10 1709+).
- ✅ **Standard frameworks:** NIST SP 800-86, RFC 3227, ISO/IEC 27037, MITRE ATT&CK.
- ✅ **Forensic process:** Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Reporting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Yield Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** In the 14-mark question, always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition/Concept (2 marks) → (2) Architecture/Diagram (3 marks) → (3) Detailed Explanation with Examples (6 marks) → (4) Forensic Significance/Tools (3 marks)**. This four-part structure aligns with the KTU marking scheme and the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** levels (Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate).

topics.title: System Calls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Calls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **System Call (syscall)** is the fundamental, controlled programmatic interface through which a user-space process requests a privileged service from the **Operating System (OS) kernel**. System calls form the boundary between the unprivileged **User Mode** (Ring 3) and the privileged **Kernel Mode** (Ring 0) of modern protected-mode processors.

In the context of **Malware Forensics**, system calls are the *primary observable trace* of a program's interaction with the OS — they expose file operations, network activity, process creation, memory allocation, registry access, and privilege changes. Forensic analysts reconstruct malicious behavior by **hooking, tracing, and correlating** these system calls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A system call is *not* a normal function call. It is a **synchronous** entry point into the kernel, mediated by hardware-assisted transitions (`SYSCALL`/`SYSENTER`/`INT 0x80`) and validated against a fixed **System Call Dispatch Table**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Government Permit Office"

Imagine a citizen (your application) who wants to build a house, draw electricity, or travel abroad. The citizen cannot do these things directly — they must visit a **Government Permit Office** (the kernel). The office has a numbered list of services (the **system call table**), each with a specific form (the **syscall interface**). The citizen fills the form, stands in a special **secure corridor** (the **trap/interrupt gate**), and a clerk (the **kernel handler**) processes the request.

- The citizen **cannot** enter the secure corridor without using the official turnstile (hardware trap).
- The clerk checks the citizen's ID (**permission check / credentials**).
- The clerk returns an answer through a documented channel (the **return value** in a register like `RAX`).

This is *exactly* how a system call works — the **user-space program** is the citizen, the **kernel** is the office, the **system call number** is the form number, and the **trap instruction** is the turnstile.

### 1.3 Why System Calls Matter in Malware Forensics

> [!NOTE]
> Malware cannot escape the system call interface. Even sophisticated **userland rootkits**, **fileless malware**, or **shellcode** must eventually invoke system calls to:
> - Read/write files on disk (`NtCreateFile`, `open`, `read`, `write`)
> - Establish network connections (`socket`, `connect`, `send`, `recv`)
> - Spawn child processes (`fork`, `execve`, `NtCreateProcess`)
> - Inject code into other processes (`NtMapViewOfSection`, `WriteProcessMemory`)
> - Persist via registry keys (`NtSetValueKey`, `RegSetValueEx`)
> - Evade detection by tampering with the **SSDT/SSDT Shadow** itself

Forensic investigators therefore treat system call traces as the **ground truth** of program intent, since higher-level API logs (e.g., `CreateFile` from `kernel32.dll`) ultimately *resolve* to native system calls.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Mode Execution Model

Modern CPUs (x86, x86-64, ARM/AArch64) support hardware-enforced privilege rings:

| Ring | Name | Privilege | Typical Code |
|------|------|-----------|--------------|
| **Ring 0** | Kernel Mode | Highest | OS kernel, drivers |
| **Ring 1** | (Reserved) | High | Virtualization hypervisors |
| **Ring 2** | (Reserved) | Medium | Legacy drivers |
| **Ring 3** | User Mode | Lowest | Applications, malware |

The boundary is enforced by:
- **Descriptor Privilege Level (DPL)** in the **Global Descriptor Table (GDT)**
- **Control Registers** (`CR0`, `CR3`, `CR4`)
- **MSRs** such as `IA32_LSTAR`, `IA32_CSTAR`, `IA32_SYSENTER_CS/EIP/ESP`

### 2.2 The System Call Lifecycle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **User-space stub executes** (e.g., `glibc`'s `open()` wrapper in `libc.so`).
2. **Arguments are marshalled** into specific registers (Linux AMD64: `RDI`, `RSI`, `RDX`, `R10`, `R8`, `R9`).
3. **Syscall number** is placed in `RAX` (e.g., `open` = **2** on x86-64 Linux).
4. **Trap instruction** is executed — `SYSCALL` (x86-64), `SYSENTER` (IA-32), `SVC #0` (ARM64), `INT 0x80` (legacy Linux).
5. **CPU switches to Ring 0** and jumps to the kernel's **LSTAR-defined entry point** (`entry_SYSCALL_64`).
6. **Kernel handler validates** parameters, performs the operation, and stores the result in `RAX`.
7. **Return via `SYSRET` / `IRET`** — CPU atomically restores Ring 3 state.

### 2.3 Categories of System Calls

| Category | Linux Examples | Windows (NTDLL) Examples | Forensic Significance |
|----------|----------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Process Control** | `fork`, `execve`, `exit`, `wait4` | `NtCreateProcess`, `NtTerminateProcess` | Spawning suspicious child processes |
| **File Operations** | `open`, `read`, `write`, `close`, `unlink` | `NtCreateFile`, `NtReadFile`, `NtWriteFile`, `NtDeleteFile` | File tampering, droppers |
| **Device I/O** | `ioctl`, `read`, `write` | `NtDeviceIoControlFile` | Rootkit communication |
| **Information / Metadata** | `getpid`, `getuid`, `uname` | `NtQuerySystemInformation` | Anti-debug, fingerprinting |
| **Communication** | `socket`, `connect`, `bind`, `sendto` | `WSA*` (wraps `Nt*` socket calls) | C2 beaconing |
| **Memory Management** | `mmap`, `brk`, `mprotect` | `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`, `NtMapViewOfSection` | Code injection, RWX regions |
| **Process Synchronization** | `wait`, `nanosleep` | `NtWaitForSingleObject` | Sleep obfuscation |
| **Signals / Exceptions** | `kill`, `sigaction` | `NtRaiseException` | Anti-VM triggers |

### 2.4 Architecture-Specific Entry Mechanisms

| Architecture | Modern Instruction | Legacy Instruction | Syscall # Register | Return Register |
|--------------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **x86-64** (Linux) | `SYSCALL` | `INT 0x80` | `RAX` | `RAX` |
| **x86-64** (Windows) | `SYSCALL` | `INT 0x2E` | `RAX` | `RAX` |
| **i386** | `SYSENTER` | `INT 0x80` | `EAX` | `EAX` |
| **AArch64** (ARM64) | `SVC #0` | — | `X8` | `X0` |
| **RISC-V** | `ECALL` | — | `a7` | `a0` |

### 2.5 High-Yield Forensic Tools & Tracers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tools commonly examined in PECCT741 lab/PBL activities:**

| Tool | OS | Method | Use Case |
|------|-----|--------|----------|
| `strace` | Linux | PTRACE-based | Trace user-space → kernel transitions |
| `ltrace` | Linux | `LD_PRELOAD` shim | Trace library calls |
| `dtrace` | Solaris/macOS/BSD | DTrace probes | Dynamic instrumentation |
| `sysdig` | Linux | Kernel module + eBPF | System-wide syscall capture |
| `Sysmon` | Windows | Kernel driver | Persistent syscall-style logging |
| `Process Monitor` | Windows | Filesystem/Registry filter driver | Real-time NT syscall observation |
| `API Monitor` | Windows | DLL injection | Hooks Win32 → NT calls |
| `PANDA` / `QEMU` | Cross | Record/replay | Deterministic malware replay |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

System call analysis is used industrially in:
- **EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response)** — Sysmon, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne all rely on syscall telemetry.
- **Sandbox Analysis** — Cuckoo Sandbox, CAPA use syscall sequences as features for ML classification.
- **Rootkit Detection** — Comparing inline `ntdll.dll` stubs against on-disk copy reveals **userland hooking**.
- **Kernel Integrity Monitoring** — Hashing the **SSDT** detects kernel rootkits.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Forensic Deduction Chains

### 3.1 Walkthrough: Tracing a Malware File Drop via System Calls

Consider a forensic scenario: a suspicious binary is observed and an analyst needs to determine **what file it created, where, and with what contents** using only syscall logs.

**Observed `strace` excerpt (annotated):**

```
openat(AT_FDCWD, "/tmp/.cache.dat", O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, 0600) = 7
mmap(NULL, 4096, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE, MAP_PRIVATE|MAP_ANONYMOUS, -1, 0) = 0x7f9a1b000000
write(7, "X5O!P%@AP[4\\PZX54(P^)7CC)7}$EICAR", 68) = 68
close(7)                                   = 0
```

**Analytical Deduction (each step is a forensic inference):**

- **Line 1 — `openat(AT_FDCWD, ...)`**: The `AT_FDCWD` sentinel indicates a **relative-to-CWD** path. Path is `/tmp/.cache.dat` — a **hidden dotfile in a world-writable directory** is a classic dropper pattern. Flags `O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC` mean the file is **created fresh, overwriting any prior content** (drop-and-replace). Mode `0600` ⇒ read/write owner only, classic anti-prying setting.
- **Line 2 — `mmap(...)`**: Allocates **1 page (4096 bytes)** of anonymous private memory, RW — this is a staging buffer, not a mapping of any file (note `fd = -1`, `offset = 0`).
- **Line 3 — `write(7, "X5O!P%@AP[4\\PZX54(P^)7CC)7}$EICAR", 68)`**: The string is the **EICAR test file signature** — a 68-byte file is the canonical antivirus test vector. The literal `X5O!P%@AP[4\PZX54(P^)7CC)7}$EICAR` is the *de-obfuscated* payload; malware writers sometimes XOR-encode this string, so the analyst should also inspect the *decoder* routines (e.g., prior `mprotect` to make the decoder buffer `RWX`).
- **Line 4 — `close(7)`**: File handle released; on most filesystems, contents are flushed to disk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Chain of Custody Note:** In KTU lab evaluation, the *raw syscall log* must be preserved (e.g., `strace -o evidence.txt ./malware`) and a **SHA-256 hash** of the dropped file computed for evidentiary integrity.

---

### 3.2 Walkthrough: Detecting Process Injection via Syscall Sequence

**Hypothesis (analyst prior knowledge):** Classic Windows process injection uses the sequence `OpenProcess` → `VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `CreateRemoteThread` (or `NtMapViewOfSection` variant).

**Observed syscall sequence (from Sysmon Event ID 10 / ProcessAccess):**

$$
\text{Seq} = \{ \text{NtOpenProcess}(pid=4521),\ \text{NtAllocateVirtualMemory}(pid=4521,\ size=8192), \\ \text{NtWriteVirtualMemory}(pid=4521,\ data=0x90\ 0x90\ \ldots),\\ \text{NtCreateThreadEx}(pid=4521,\ start=0x7FFEABCD) \}
$$

**Analytical Deduction:**

1. `NtOpenProcess` with sufficient access rights (typically `PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS = 0x1F0FFF`) is the **first indicator** — a normal user application rarely opens other processes.
2. `NtAllocateVirtualMemory` requesting executable memory (`PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE = 0x40`) in a foreign process is **highly suspicious**.
3. `NtWriteVirtualMemory` writes raw bytes — comparing against known shellcode signatures (Metasploit `msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp`) yields high-confidence attribution.
4. `NtCreateThreadEx` starts execution at the address of the written buffer ⇒ **code injection confirmed**.

**Resulting IOC (Indicator of Compromise):**
- Sysmon Rule: `EventID=8` (CreateRemoteThread) targeting non-system processes.

---

### 3.3 Walkthrough: Computing the Syscall Number from the LSTAR Register

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows how a forensic analyst can extract the **kernel syscall entry point** address from a Linux system at runtime.

The `IA32_LSTAR` MSR (Model-Specific Register `0xC0000082`) on x86-64 holds the 64-bit kernel address of the `entry_SYSCALL_64` routine.

**Step 1:** Read the MSR.

$$
\text{LSTAR} = \text{rdmsr}(0xC0000082)
$$

**Step 2:** The first instructions at this address (typical Linux 5.x+) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{swapgs} \quad &\text{// swap GS base with kernel GS} \\
\text{movq} \ \ \%rsp, \ \text{GS:CPU\_STACK\_OFFSET} \quad &\text{// save user RSP} \\
\text{swapgs} \quad &\text{// restore user GS (paranoid)} \\
\text{movq} \ \ \%rdi, \ \text{GS:ENTRY\_REGS} \quad &\text{// point to pt\_regs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** From `pt_regs`, the syscall number is recovered at offset `+8 * ORIG\_RAX`.

$$
\text{syscall\_num} = \text{pt\_regs[ORIG\_RAX]} = \text{pt\_regs.offset}(8 \times 8)
$$

> **Resulting value for `open` on x86-64 Linux:** `2`
> **Resulting value for `read` on x86-64 Linux:** `0`
> **Resulting value for `NtCreateFile` on Windows x64:** `0x0055` (Ntdll ordinal)

---

### 3.4 Walkthrough: Syscall Frequency Anomaly Detection (Statistical Baseline)

Let $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ be the multiset of syscall numbers invoked by a benign program, and $M = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n\}$ for a suspected malware binary of similar size.

Define the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD)** between the empirical distributions $P_S$ and $P_M$:

$$
\text{JSD}(P_S \mid\mid P_M) = \frac{1}{2} D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(P_S \mid\mid \tfrac{P_S+P_M}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2} D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(P_M \mid\mid \tfrac{P_S+P_M}{2}\right)
$$

where

$$
D_{\text{KL}}(P \mid\mid Q) = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}
$$

A high JSD ($\geq 0.6$ on a $[0,1]$ scale) indicates the malware's syscall **distribution** differs strongly from the baseline — a strong ML feature for EDR classification.

> **Real malware observation:** A keylogger shows a high frequency of `NtDeviceIoControlFile` (keyboard filter driver attach) — an unusual call for legitimate user apps.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The System Call Execution Path (User → Kernel → User)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User-Space Program\n[Ring 3 - Unprivileged]"] --> B["libc / ntdll Wrapper\nFunction Call e.g. open()"]
    B --> C["Load Syscall Number\ninto RAX = 2"]
    C --> D["Load Args into\nRDI RSI RDX R10 R8 R9"]
    D --> E["Execute SYSCALL Instruction\n[CPU Trap from Ring 3 to Ring 0]"]
    E --> F["MSR IA32_LSTAR Dispatches\nto entry_SYSCALL_64"]
    F --> G["Kernel Validates Arguments\n[SMAP SMEP CET checks]"]
    G --> H["Dispatch via\nsys_call_table syscall 2"]
    H --> I["Execute sys_open\n[Actual Kernel Service]"]
    I --> J["Store Result in RAX\n[Return Value]"]
    J --> K["Execute SYSRET\n[Return to Ring 3]"]
    K --> L["User-Space Continues\n[Library Returns fd or -1]"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Forensic Syscall Hooking Architecture (SSDT Hooking Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph User["User-Space (Ring 3)"]
        App["Malicious Process"]
        Ntdll["ntdll.dll\n[Native API Stubs]"]
    end
    subgraph Kernel["Kernel-Space (Ring 0)"]
        SSDT["SSDT / Syscall Table\n[Array of Function Pointers]"]
        Check["Integrity Checker\n[Rootkit Detector]"]
        Handler["Original Kernel Handler\n[NtCreateFile, NtReadFile, etc.]"]
    end
    subgraph Detour["Malicious Hook"]
        Hook["Rootkit Inline Hook\n[JMP to attacker code]"]
    end
    App --> Ntdll
    Ntdll -->|SYSCALL| SSDT
    SSDT -->|normal pointer| Handler
    SSDT -.->|rootkit overwritten pointer| Hook
    Check -->|compare SSDT hashes\nto known-good image| SSDT
    Check -->|report anomaly| Forensics["Forensic Log\n[Sysmon Event ID 6 / 7]"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Malware Syscall Categorization Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Malware Syscall Behavior"]
    Root --> P1["File-Based"]
    Root --> P2["Network-Based"]
    Root --> P3["Process-Based"]
    Root --> P4["Memory-Based"]
    Root --> P5["Persistence-Based"]
    P1 --> F1["NtCreateFile / openat"]
    P1 --> F2["NtWriteFile / write"]
    P1 --> F3["NtDeleteFile / unlink"]
    P2 --> N1["socket / WSAStartup"]
    P2 --> N2["connect / NtDeviceIoControlFile"]
    P2 --> N3["sendto / NtWriteFile over socket fd"]
    P3 --> PR1["NtCreateProcess / fork execve"]
    P3 --> PR2["NtCreateThreadEx / clone"]
    P3 --> PR3["NtOpenProcess / ptrace"]
    P4 --> M1["NtAllocateVirtualMemory / mmap"]
    P4 --> M2["NtProtectVirtualMemory / mprotect"]
    P4 --> M3["NtMapViewOfSection"]
    P5 --> PE1["NtSetValueKey\n[Registry Persistence]"]
    P5 --> PE2["NtCreateSymbolicLinkObject\n[Hidden Service]"]
    P5 --> PE3["NtSetSystemInformation\n[Service Install]"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECCT741]**
*Define a system call. Why is it considered the forensic "ground truth" of program behavior?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **system call** is the controlled interface through which a user-mode process requests a privileged service from the OS kernel, mediated by a CPU trap (e.g., `SYSCALL`) and dispatched via a fixed **system call table** (1 mark). It is the *lowest observable* layer at which user code can affect the system — higher-level API calls (Win32, libc) ultimately resolve to native system calls (1 mark). Therefore, monitoring system calls (via `strace`, `Sysmon`, eBPF) gives forensic analysts a **direct, kernel-validated trace** of a process's true intent, immune to userland API obfuscation (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECCT741]**
*List any six categories of system calls with one example each (Linux or Windows).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Process Control** — `fork` / `NtCreateProcess` (0.5)
2. **File Manipulation** — `open` / `NtCreateFile` (0.5)
3. **Device Management** — `ioctl` / `NtDeviceIoControlFile` (0.5)
4. **Information Maintenance** — `getpid` / `NtQueryInformationProcess` (0.5)
5. **Communication** — `socket` / `NtCreateFile` (with socket flag) (0.5)
6. **Memory Management** — `mmap` / `NtAllocateVirtualMemory` (0.5)

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture-level mechanism by which a system call is invoked on x86-64 Linux. Discuss the role of the **LSTAR MSR**, **SYSCALL/SYSRET** instructions, and the **system call table** (`sys_call_table`). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A forensic analyst suspects that a userland rootkit has hooked the Windows **SSDT**. Describe the detection methodology using `ntdll.dll` stub integrity checking, and provide the pseudocode logic to flag the anomaly. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Registers used** (1 mark): In the x86-64 ABI, the system call number is loaded into `RAX`, and the first six arguments go into `RDI, RSI, RDX, R10, R8, R9`. The user stack must hold any further arguments.
2. **Trap instruction** (1 mark): The user-space wrapper executes `SYSCALL`, which the CPU interprets atomically: it saves the user `RIP` into `RCX` and the user `RFLAGS` into `R11`, then loads the kernel entry `RIP` from `IA32_LSTAR` (MSR `0xC0000082`) and the kernel `CS/SS` selectors.
3. **LSTAR dispatch** (1 mark): Execution begins at `entry_SYSCALL_64` (compiled into the kernel image). This routine saves general-purpose registers into a `pt_regs` structure located on the **per-CPU kernel stack**.
4. **Argument validation** (1 mark): SMAP/SMEP flags are consulted, and `pt_regs[ORIG_RAX]` is read to identify the syscall number.
5. **Dispatch via `sys_call_table`** (2 marks): The table is a static array of `sys_*` function pointers in the `.rodata` section of the kernel. The entry at index `syscall_number` is invoked:

   ```c
   long (*sys_call_table[])(...) = { sys_read, sys_write, sys_open, ... };
   do_syscall_64(struct pt_regs *regs) {
       nr = regs->orig_ax;
       regs->ax = sys_call_table[nr](regs->di, regs->si, regs->dx, regs->r10, regs->r8, regs->r9);
   }
   ```
6. **Return** (1 mark): After the handler completes, `SYSRET` atomically restores user `RIP` from `RCX` and user `RFLAGS` from `R11`, returning to Ring 3 with the result in `RAX`.

**(b) — 7 marks**

1. **Concept** (1 mark): Windows userland → kernel transitions begin with a stub in `ntdll.dll`, e.g., `NtCreateFile` is a 5-byte stub: `mov r10, rcx; mov eax, 0x55; syscall; ret`. The actual kernel handler address is the only variable — but the **first bytes of the stub** must match the **on-disk `ntdll.dll`** known-good hash.
2. **Detection methodology** (2 marks):
   - Load the **on-disk `ntdll.dll`** from `C:\Windows\System32\ntdll.dll` (read-only, kernel-protected).
   - Load the **in-memory `ntdll.dll`** for the suspected process using `ReadProcessMemory` or by reading the section `.text` from `/proc/<pid>/maps` on Linux-for-Win.
   - Compute **SHA-256** of each, or perform a **byte-by-byte diff** of the first 16 bytes of every `Nt*` stub.
3. **Pseudocode** (3 marks):

   ```c
   on_disk   = sha256(read_file("C:\\Windows\\System32\\ntdll.dll"));
   in_memory = sha256(read_process_mem(pid, ntdll_base, ntdll_size));
   if (on_disk != in_memory) {
       flag_anomaly("ntdll.dll tampering detected in PID " + pid);
       for each Nt* stub:
           if (stub[0:2] != "mov r10, rcx" || stub[3:8] != "mov eax, N")
               log_inlined_hook(stub_name, offset);
   }
   ```
4. **Forensic action** (1 mark): Capture a **memory dump** (e.g., via `winpmem` or FTK Imager), correlate with **Sysmon Event ID 8** (CreateRemoteThread detected), and add the **PID** to a global watchlist.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **forensic analysis workflow** for a captured malware sample using system call tracing tools. Compare `strace` (Linux) and **Sysmon** (Windows) in terms of visibility, persistence, and anti-forensic evasion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A captured Trojan's syscall log shows an unusually high frequency of `socket()` followed by `connect()` to a single external IP, interleaved with `fork()` and `execve()`. Analyze the behavior, propose the malware family classification, and list three Sysmon rules that would alert on this in a production environment. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Workflow diagram** (3 marks) — should contain the stages: `Sample acquisition (hash+isolate)` → `Static triage (strings, PE/ELF info)` → `Dynamic analysis in sandbox` → `Syscall capture (strace / Sysmon)` → `IOC extraction` → `YARA / Sigma rule generation` → `Reporting`.
2. **`strace` characteristics** (2 marks): User-space tool based on `ptrace()`; can attach to a running PID or launch a new one; outputs to stdout by default; **not stealthy** (anti-debug detects it easily); no system-wide view without `-f` + `-p`.
3. **Sysmon characteristics** (2 marks): Kernel-mode driver installed as a service; logs to `Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational` EVTX; persists across reboots; **event IDs 1–28** cover process, network, file, registry, image-load, driver-load, WMI, etc. — much higher visibility than `strace`; harder to evade without kernel-level capabilities.

| Criterion | `strace` (Linux) | `Sysmon` (Windows) |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Visibility | Per-process | System-wide, kernel-mode |
| Persistence | None (CLI tool) | High (service + EVTX) |
| Anti-forensic resistance | Low (PTRACE detectable) | High (requires driver tampering) |
| Forensically validated log | No | Yes (EVTX chain-of-custody) |

**(b) — 7 marks**

1. **Behavior interpretation** (2 marks): Repeated `socket()`+`connect()` to the **same IP** strongly indicates a **Command-and-Control (C2) beacon**. The `fork()`+`execve()` interleaving suggests the Trojan spawns a child process (possibly a shell listener or secondary payload) — classic **RAT (Remote Access Trojan)** pattern, e.g., Mirai, Emotet, or a custom Cobalt Strike beacon.
2. **Classification** (1 mark): **RAT / Backdoor** with **forking listener capability** (Unix-style daemonization).
3. **Three Sysmon rules** (3 marks):
   - `Rule 1 — Network Connect:` `EventID=3` AND `DestinationIp=<known-malicious-IP>` AND `InitiatedBy="<malware.exe>"`.
   - `Rule 2 — Process Create:` `EventID=1` AND `ParentImage="<malware.exe>"` AND `Image="*/sh"` OR `Image="*/bash"` (child shell).
   - `Rule 3 — Image Load:` `EventID=7` AND `Image="<malware.exe>"` AND `ImageLoaded="*ws2_32.dll"` (indicates socket subsystem use by an unusual binary).
4. **Correlated IOC** (1 mark): SHA-256 of binary + C2 IP + JA3 SSL fingerprint (if TLS).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing API calls with system calls.** `CreateFile` is a Win32 API in `kernel32.dll`; the actual syscall is `NtCreateFile` in `ntdll.dll`. Many students write the wrong layer in `strace` traces. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the syscall number.** A diagram of the lifecycle without the `RAX` register convention is incomplete. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Ignoring architecture differences.** Citing only `INT 0x80` for x86-64 is outdated; mark down for not mentioning `SYSCALL`. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping chain-of-custody.** A forensic answer that does not mention hashing (`SHA-256`), isolation (sandbox/VM), or log integrity loses credit on the *practical* component. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Writing kernel code in user-space style.** When asked for pseudocode of a hook detector, do not propose `LD_PRELOAD` for Windows — it does not exist. Use `IAT hooking`, inline stub checks, or kernel-driver approaches. (–1 mark)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **System Call = User → Kernel transition** mediated by a **trap instruction** (`SYSCALL` on x86-64, `SVC #0` on ARM64).
- **The syscall number is the index** into the **system call dispatch table** (`sys_call_table` on Linux, **SSDT** on Windows).
- **Register convention on x86-64 Linux:** number in `RAX`, args in `RDI, RSI, RDX, R10, R8, R9`, return in `RAX`.
- **Forensic tools:** `strace`, `ltrace`, `dtrace`, `sysdig`, `Sysmon`, `Process Monitor`, `API Monitor`, `PANDA`.
- **Malware indicators** map to syscall *sequences* — file drop = `open+write+close`; injection = `OpenProcess+VirtualAllocEx+WriteProcessMemory+CreateRemoteThread`; C2 = `socket+connect+send/recv` loops.
- **Rootkit detection = SSDT integrity checking** — compare on-disk vs in-memory `ntdll.dll` stubs, hash the kernel's syscall table.
- **Always preserve evidence:** SHA-256 hash every artifact, sandbox in an isolated VM with snapshots, and use **write-blockers** in the real-machine chain.
- **High-yield acronym list for KTU 2024:**
  - **SSDT** — System Service Descriptor Table
  - **LSTAR** — Long-mode SYSCALL Target Address Register
  - **MSR** — Model-Specific Register
  - **EAT / IAT** — Export / Import Address Table
  - **EDR** — Endpoint Detection and Response
  - **C2** — Command and Control
  - **IOC** — Indicator of Compromise
  - **SMEP/SMAP** — Supervisor Mode Execution/Access Prevention
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: API Calls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** API Calls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API Call (Application Programming Interface Call):** A function invocation made by a program (including malware) to request a service from the operating system kernel, system libraries (e.g., `kernel32.dll`, `ntdll.dll`, `ws2_32.dll`), or other software components. In malware forensics, API calls are the **primary observable artifacts** that reveal a malicious program's intent, capabilities, and interaction with the host system.

In the context of the **Portable Executable (PE) file format** on Windows, API calls are resolved through:
- **Import Address Table (IAT)** — A lookup table that stores the runtime addresses of external functions imported from Dynamic-Link Libraries (DLLs).
- **Export Address Table (EAT)** — A table maintained by DLLs listing the functions they make available to other executables.

The mathematically abstracted signature of an API call can be represented as:

$$\text{API Call} = f(\text{Module}_{DLL}, \text{Function}_{Name}, \text{Parameters}_{Args}, \text{Return}_{Value})$$

Where:
- $\text{Module}_{DLL}$ → The parent library (e.g., `kernel32.dll`)
- $\text{Function}_{Name}$ → The function being invoked (e.g., `CreateFileW`)
- $\text{Parameters}_{Args}$ → Input arguments passed via the stack
- $\text{Return}_{Value}$ → Status code or handle returned to the caller

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy:** Imagine a malware program is a customer sitting at a table in a restaurant. The customer (malware) does not cook food (perform low-level system operations) itself. Instead, it places an **order** (API call) through a **waiter** (the Windows subsystem / DLL) to the **kitchen** (operating system kernel). The waiter delivers the request and returns the dish (result). By analyzing the *orders placed* by the malware, a forensic analyst can deduce exactly what the malware is trying to do — read files, connect to the internet, hide itself, etc.

## 1.3 Why API Calls Matter in Malware Forensics

| Forensic Activity | Role of API Calls |
|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Static Analysis** | Inspecting the IAT reveals *which* functions malware *might* call |
| **Dynamic Analysis** | API monitors (e.g., Process Monitor) capture *actual* runtime calls |
| **Behavioral Fingerprinting** | Specific sequences of API calls create **malware signatures** |
| **YARA Rule Generation** | API call patterns are converted into YARA conditions |
| **Incident Response** | API call logs in EDR/SIEM help reconstruct the kill chain |

## 1.4 Key Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Native API:** Low-level, undocumented system calls inside `ntdll.dll` (e.g., `NtCreateFile`). Used by sophisticated malware to evade user-mode API monitoring.
> - **Win32 API:** Microsoft-documented high-level API set in `kernel32.dll`, `user32.dll`, `advapi32.dll`, etc.
> - **Syscall (System Call):** The final transition from user mode (Ring 3) to kernel mode (Ring 0), invoked via `sysenter` or `syscall` instructions.
> - **API Hooking:** Intercepting API calls to modify, log, or block their execution — a common defense and offense technique.
- **Ring Levels:** Windows uses 4 privilege rings (Ring 0 = kernel, Ring 3 = user). User-mode applications request kernel services *only* via API calls.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Windows API Call — The Call Stack

When a program invokes an API like `CreateFileW`, the following layered sequence occurs:

1. **Source Code Layer** — The malware source calls the function.
2. **Compiler Stub** — Translates the call into assembly (e.g., `CALL DWORD PTR [IAT_entry]`).
3. **Import Resolution** — The loader resolves the function address from the IAT at process startup.
4. **DLL Entry Point** — Control transfers to the function inside the loaded DLL.
5. **Native API Bridge** — Win32 functions often call native APIs in `ntdll.dll` (e.g., `CreateFileW` → `NtCreateFile`).
6. **System Service Dispatcher** — `ntdll.dll` issues a syscall via `sysenter`/`syscall`.
7. **Kernel Execution** — The Windows Executive performs the operation in Ring 0.
8. **Return Path** — Results propagate back up through the same chain to the caller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this layering important to forensic analysts?** Because malware may hook *any* layer. A user-mode API monitor (like API Monitor) sees Win32 calls; a kernel monitor (like Process Monitor via ETW) sees syscalls. Sophisticated malware may *bypass* certain layers by issuing direct syscalls — which is why **multi-layer monitoring** is the gold standard.

## 2.2 Critical API Categories in Malware Analysis

| Category | Representative APIs | Malware Purpose |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **File Operations** | `CreateFileW`, `ReadFile`, `WriteFile`, `DeleteFileW` | Drop payloads, read configs, delete evidence |
| **Registry Manipulation** | `RegOpenKeyExW`, `RegSetValueExW`, `RegDeleteKeyW` | Persistence, hidden config storage |
| **Process & Thread** | `CreateProcessW`, `CreateRemoteThread`, `OpenProcess` | Injection, hollowing, lateral movement |
| **Memory Allocation** | `VirtualAlloc`, `VirtualProtect`, `HeapAlloc` | Allocating shellcode, RWX regions |
| **Network Communication** | `WSAStartup`, `socket`, `connect`, `send`, `InternetOpenUrlW` | C2 beaconing, data exfiltration |
| **Cryptography** | `CryptEncrypt`, `CryptHashData`, `BCryptEncrypt` | Ransomware encryption, payload obfuscation |
| **Service & Scheduled Tasks** | `OpenSCManagerW`, `CreateServiceW` | Persistence as a Windows Service |
| **Dynamic Loading** | `LoadLibraryA`, `GetProcAddress` | Manual API resolution to evade static IAT inspection |
| **Anti-Analysis** | `IsDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess` | Detecting sandboxes, delaying payload |
| **Privilege Escalation** | `AdjustTokenPrivileges`, `LogonUserW` | Gaining higher integrity levels |

## 2.3 API Hooking — The Double-Edged Sword

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API Hooking** is the technique of intercepting function calls in a program to alter or augment its behavior. The forensic implications are dual:
> - **Defensive Hooking:** Antivirus/EDR products hook APIs to *block* malicious actions.
> - **Offensive Hooking:** Malware may hook APIs to *hide* its presence (e.g., hooking `NtQueryDirectoryFile` to hide files).

Common hooking techniques:
1. **IAT Hooking** — Overwriting entries in the Import Address Table of the target process.
2. **Inline Hooking (Trampolines)** — Patching the first few bytes of a function with a `JMP` to a detour.
3. **SSDT Hooking** — Modifying the System Service Descriptor Table (kernel-level, mostly defeated in modern Windows).
4. **ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) Tampering** — Disabling or patching ETW providers to blind EDR.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the most important reference values and notation used when analyzing API call traces. **Bold values** are commonly tested constants.

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Description |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| API Call Signature | $\text{Call} = \{D, F, A, R, T, P\}$ | DLL, Function, Args, Return, ThreadID, ProcessID |
| Syscall Transition | `Ring 3 → Ring 0` via `syscall` (x64) / `sysenter` (x86) | Privilege escalation point |
| IAT Entry Width (x86) | **4 bytes** | Function pointer size in 32-bit PE |
| IAT Entry Width (x64) | **8 bytes** | Function pointer size in 64-bit PE |
| x64 Calling Convention | $\text{1st arg} = \text{RCX},\ \text{2nd} = \text{RDX},\ \text{3rd} = \text{R8},\ \text{4th} = \text{R9}$ | Fastcall on x64 |
| x86 Calling Convention | $\text{Args pushed right-to-left on stack}$ | Stdcall / Cdecl |
| Syscall Number (NTDLL) | $S_{num} = \text{index into SSDT}$ | Unique per native function |
| Hashing for API Resolution | $H = \text{API Hash} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c_i \cdot r^i \mod M$ | Used by malware to resolve APIs dynamically (DJB2, CRC32, etc.) |
| Return Value Convention | $\text{0} = \text{STATUS\_SUCCESS}$ | NTSTATUS format |
| ERROR_SUCCESS (Win32) | $0x00000000$ | The universal "no error" code |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic API Resolution by Malware

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why derive this?** Many malware samples do *not* list critical APIs in their IAT. Instead, they compute API addresses at runtime using a hash. This is called **API Hashing**, and understanding the math behind it is a **high-yield KTU topic**.

### The DJB2 Hash Algorithm Derivation

The DJB2 hash, popularized by Daniel J. Bernstein, is widely used in malware to obfuscate API names. The recursive mathematical formulation is:

$$
H_0 = 5381
$$

$$
H_{i+1} = \big((H_i \cdot 33) + c_{i+1}\big) \mod 2^{32}
$$

Where:
- $H_i$ → The hash value after processing the $i$-th character.
- $c_{i+1}$ → The integer ASCII value of the $(i+1)$-th character in the API name.
- $33$ → The empirically chosen DJB2 multiplier (also equal to $0x21$).
- $2^{32}$ → The modulus for 32-bit hash values.

### Worked Example: Hashing the API `CreateFileA`

**Step 1 — Initialize the hash value.**

$$
H_0 = 5381 \quad (= 0x00001515)
$$

**Step 2 — Process the first character `'C'` (ASCII = 67).**

$$
H_1 = \big(5381 \times 33 + 67\big) \mod 2^{32}
$$

$$
H_1 = \big(177573 + 67\big) \mod 2^{32} = 177640
$$

**Step 3 — Process the second character `'r'` (ASCII = 114).**

$$
H_2 = \big(177640 \times 33 + 114\big) \mod 2^{32}
$$

$$
H_2 = \big(5862120 + 114\big) \mod 2^{32} = 5862234
$$

**Step 4 — Process the third character `'e'` (ASCII = 101).**

$$
H_3 = \big(5862234 \times 33 + 101\big) \mod 2^{32}
$$

$$
H_3 = \big(193453722 + 101\big) \mod 2^{32} = 193453823
$$

**Step 5 — Continue this process for every character** (`a`, `t`, `e`, `F`, `i`, `l`, `e`, `A`) until the final hash is obtained. The iterative nature means:

$$
H_n = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c_{i+1} \cdot 33^{n-1-i} \pmod{2^{32}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **General Polynomial Form:** $H_n$ is a polynomial in base 33 evaluated modulo $2^{32}$. The multiplicative constant 33 was chosen because it produces good avalanche properties for short ASCII strings — collisions are rare for API names.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Malware Behavior to API Calls

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is **critical** for KTU exam questions on "identify malicious behavior from API call traces." Memorize the patterns.

| Malware Behavior | Indicator API Sequence | Risk Level |
|------------------|------------------------|------------|
| **Persistence (Run Key)** | `RegOpenKeyExW` → `RegSetValueExW` (HKCU\...\Run) | **HIGH** |
| **Process Injection** | `OpenProcess` → `VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `CreateRemoteThread` | **CRITICAL** |
| **Process Hollowing** | `CreateProcessW` (suspended) → `NtUnmapViewOfSection` → `VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `ResumeThread` | **CRITICAL** |
| **DLL Injection** | `OpenProcess` → `VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `LoadLibraryA` (via remote thread) | **CRITICAL** |
| **Keylogging** | `SetWindowsHookExW` + repeated `GetAsyncKeyState` / `GetKeyboardState` | **HIGH** |
| **Screenshot Capture** | `BitBlt` / `CreateCompatibleDC` / `GetDIBits` | **MEDIUM** |
| **C2 Communication** | `WSAStartup` → `socket` → `connect` → `send` / `recv` | **HIGH** |
| **File Encryption (Ransomware)** | `CryptEncrypt` / `BCryptEncrypt` + `WriteFile` on user docs | **CRITICAL** |
| **Defense Evasion** | `NtSetInformationProcess` (debug port) / `IsDebuggerPresent` | **HIGH** |
| **Privilege Escalation** | `OpenProcessToken` → `LookupPrivilegeValueW` → `AdjustTokenPrivileges` | **HIGH** |
| **Anti-VM Check** | `RegOpenKeyExW` (HKLM\HARDWARE\...\vmware) / `GetSystemFirmwareTable` | **MEDIUM** |

## 3.3 Proof: Why API Hashing Defeats Static IAT Analysis

**Premise:** A static analyzer reads the PE file's IAT to enumerate imports.

**Step 1 — Static IAT contains only resolved (non-obfuscated) imports.**

If malware uses `LoadLibraryA` and `GetProcAddress` *only*, then critical APIs never appear in the IAT.

**Step 2 — The hash-based resolver iterates loaded modules.**

Malware calls `GetModuleHandleA` for `kernel32.dll`, then walks its EAT manually:

$$
\text{Function Address} = \text{EAT\_Base} + \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \delta(\text{hash}(F_j),\ \text{target})
$$

Where $\delta$ is the Kronecker delta — equal to **1** when the iterated function's hash matches the target.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

Since the IAT never contains the targeted API, **static analysis tools like `PEview`, `CFF Explorer`, or Ghidra's "imports" panel cannot see the resolution**. The hash is the only "fingerprint" left behind.

**Counter-Defense (forensic implication):** Analysts can pre-compute hashes of all interesting APIs and scan the malware binary for them, defeating the obfuscation.

$$
\text{Match}(H) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } H \in \{H(\text{CreateFile}),\ H(\text{VirtualAlloc}),\ \ldots\} \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Windows API Call Execution Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the layered journey of an API call from user-mode source code down to the kernel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Malware Source Code"]:::source --> B["Compiled PE Executable"]:::source
    B --> C["IAT Entry Lookup"]:::userland
    C --> D["Win32 API DLL e.g. kernel32.dll"]:::userland
    D --> E["Native API Ntdll.dll e.g. NtCreateFile"]:::userland
    E --> F["Syscall Instruction sysenter or syscall"]:::userland
    F --> G["System Service Dispatcher SSDT"]:::kernel
    G --> H["Windows Kernel Executive Ring 0"]:::kernel
    H --> I["Hardware Device Driver"]:::kernel
    I --> J["Return Path via EAX or RAX"]:::userland
    J --> A

    K["API Monitor Ring 3"]:::monitor -.-> D
    L["Process Monitor ETW Kernel"]:::monitor -.-> G
    M["EDR Hook Ring 3 / Kernel"]:::monitor -.-> E

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef userland fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef kernel fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef monitor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: API-Based Malware Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT ACQUISITION"]
        S1["Suspicious PE Sample"]:::input
        S2["Memory Dump"]:::input
        S3["Network PCAP"]:::input
    end

    subgraph STATIC["STATIC ANALYSIS"]
        T1["PE Parser: Extract IAT"]:::static
        T2["IAT Hash Database Lookup"]:::static
        T3["String Scan for API Names"]:::static
    end

    subgraph DYNAMIC["DYNAMIC ANALYSIS"]
        T4["Sandbox Execution"]:::dynamic
        T5["API Monitor Trace"]:::dynamic
        T6["ETW Provider Logs"]:::dynamic
    end

    subgraph FORENSIC["FORENSIC CORRELATION"]
        T7["Behavior Mapping Matrix"]:::forensic
        T8["MITRE ATT&CK Tagging"]:::forensic
        T9["YARA Rule Generation"]:::forensic
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["FORENSIC REPORT"]
        R1["API Call Trace"]:::output
        R2["Threat Score"]:::output
        R3["IOC List"]:::output
    end

    S1 --> T1 --> T2
    S1 --> T3
    S2 --> T4 --> T5
    S2 --> T6
    S3 --> T6
    T2 --> T7
    T3 --> T7
    T5 --> T7
    T6 --> T7
    T7 --> T8 --> T9
    T7 --> R1
    T8 --> R2
    T9 --> R3

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef static fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dynamic fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef forensic fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 API Hooking Mechanism (Inline Hook Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NORMAL["NORMAL EXECUTION PATH"]
        N1["Caller Function"]:::normal --> N2["Function Prolog 5 bytes"]:::normal
        N2 --> N3["Original API Body"]:::normal
        N3 --> N4["Function Return"]:::normal
    end

    subgraph HOOKED["HOOKED EXECUTION PATH"]
        H1["Caller Function"]:::hook --> H2["JMP to Detour"]:::hook
        H2 --> H3["Malicious Handler"]:::hook
        H3 --> H4["Optional Call to Original via Trampoline"]:::hook
        H4 --> N3
    end

    PATCH["Hot Patch: Overwrite Prolog with JMP"]:::patch -.-> H2

    classDef normal fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hook fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef patch fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define an API call. List any four commonly abused Win32 APIs by malware and their malicious purposes. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
An **API (Application Programming Interface) call** is a request issued by a program to the operating system or a library to perform a specific service, such as file access, network communication, or memory management. In malware forensics, monitoring API calls reveals the runtime behavior of a suspicious executable.

Four commonly abused Win32 APIs:
1. `CreateFileW` — File read/write operations, payload dropping.
2. `RegSetValueExW` — Registry persistence (e.g., Run keys).
3. `CreateRemoteThread` — Code injection into other processes.
4. `WSAStartup` / `socket` — Establishing C2 network connections.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Win32 API**, **Native API**, and **System Calls**. Why is this distinction important for malware forensic analysis? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Layer | Description | Examples | Detection Visibility |
|-------|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Win32 API** | Documented high-level Microsoft API | `CreateFileW`, `MessageBoxA` | API Monitor (Ring 3) |
| **Native API** | Undocumented, lower-level functions in `ntdll.dll` | `NtCreateFile`, `NtQueryInformationProcess` | Some monitors, EDR |
| **System Call** | Direct CPU instruction transitioning to Ring 0 | `syscall`, `sysenter` | ETW, kernel-level tools |

**Importance:** Malware may bypass Win32 API and call Native APIs directly (or even raw syscalls) to evade user-mode API monitors. Therefore, **layered monitoring** is essential to detect sophisticated samples.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Students often lose marks by (a) failing to mention the *transition mechanism* (syscall/sysenter), (b) ignoring the **dynamic resolution** technique (API hashing), and (c) describing IAT only in 32-bit context without mentioning x64 differences. **Always specify the platform architecture** in your answers.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the role of the **Import Address Table (IAT)** in resolving API calls. Describe how the Windows loader populates the IAT at process startup. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **API hooking** in detail. Compare **IAT hooking** and **inline hooking** with suitable diagrams. Mention at least two defensive uses and two offensive uses of API hooking. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Import Address Table (IAT)** is a data structure inside a Portable Executable (PE) file that contains the runtime addresses of functions imported from external DLLs.

**Step 1 — PE File Layout:** The IAT resides inside the `.idata` section, organized as an array of function pointers. The number of entries is given by the `IMAGE_DIRECTORY_ENTRY_IMPORT` data directory.

**Step 2 — Static PE Format:** At compile time, the IAT contains either the ordinal number or a placeholder (`IMAGE_ORDINAL_FLAG32`) pointing to the function name in the Import Name Table (INT).

**Step 3 — Loader Resolution (At Process Startup):**
1. The Windows loader reads the PE's import directory.
2. For each DLL listed, it calls `LoadLibraryA` to map the DLL into the process address space.
3. For each function, the loader calls `GetProcAddress` to resolve the actual address.
4. The resolved address **overwrites** the IAT entry.

The mathematical relationship is:

$$
\text{IAT}[i] = \text{GetProcAddress}(\text{LoadLibrary}(\text{DLL}),\ \text{FuncName}_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Runtime Invocation:** When malware calls the imported function, the compiler emits a `CALL DWORD PTR DS:[IAT_entry]` (x86) or `CALL QWORD PTR [RIP + IAT_offset]` (x64), which indirectly invokes the API.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**API Hooking** is the technique of intercepting API function calls to observe, modify, or block their execution.

**IAT Hooking:**
- The attacker enumerates the target process's IAT and **overwrites** a function pointer to redirect calls to a malicious handler.
- *Limitation:* Works only on functions called through the IAT; if the function is called via `GetProcAddress`, IAT hooking fails.

**Inline Hooking (Trampoline):**
- The first 5 bytes of the API function's prolog are patched with a `JMP <detour>` instruction.
- The original 5 bytes are preserved in a "trampoline" so the original function can still be called.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | IAT Hooking | Inline Hooking |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| Granularity | Per-process (per-IAT) | Function-wide |
| Robustness against dynamic resolution | **Weak** | **Strong** |
| Complexity | Low | Higher (prolog disassembly needed) |
| Detection by AV/EDR | Easier | Harder |

**Defensive Uses:**
1. Antivirus sandboxing API calls in a virtualized layer.
2. EDR products intercepting `VirtualAllocEx` to block memory injection.

**Offensive Uses:**
1. Rootkits hooking `NtQueryDirectoryFile` to hide malicious files.
2. Banking Trojans hooking `WinHttpSendRequest` to inject fraudulent headers.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** What is **API Hashing**? Why do malware authors use it? Compute the DJB2 hash (in hex, modulo $2^{32}$) for the first **three** characters of the API function `VirtualAlloc` and explain the process. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** From a malware forensics perspective, classify the following API call sequences and explain what malicious behavior they indicate:
> - **Sequence 1:** `RegOpenKeyExW` → `RegSetValueExW` (target: `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`)
> - **Sequence 2:** `OpenProcess` → `VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `CreateRemoteThread`
> - **Sequence 3:** `CryptAcquireContextW` → `CryptGenRandom` → `CryptEncrypt` (operated on a `.docx` file in `My Documents`)
>
> Tag each sequence with the corresponding **MITRE ATT&CK technique ID** (if known). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**API Hashing** is an obfuscation technique in which malware does not reference API functions by name. Instead, it computes a numerical hash of the function name and walks the Export Address Table (EAT) of a loaded DLL to find a matching hash. The matching function is then invoked.

**Why malware uses it:**
1. **Static analysis evasion:** Tools that inspect the IAT see no suspicious API names.
2. **YARA rule bypass:** Plaintext API strings are absent from the binary.
3. **Reduced PE size:** Only hash constants are embedded, not import tables.

**DJB2 Hash Computation for the first 3 characters of `VirtualAlloc` (`V`, `i`, `r`):**

- $H_0 = 5381$
- $c_1 = $ ASCII `'V'` $= 86$
- $c_2 = $ ASCII `'i'` $= 105$
- $c_3 = $ ASCII `'r'` $= 114$

**Step 1:** After `'V'`:

$$
H_1 = (5381 \times 33 + 86) \mod 2^{32}
$$

$$
H_1 = (177573 + 86) \mod 2^{32} = 177659
$$

**Step 2:** After `'i'`:

$$
H_2 = (177659 \times 33 + 105) \mod 2^{32}
$$

$$
H_2 = (5862747 + 105) \mod 2^{32} = 5862852
$$

**Step 3:** After `'r'`:

$$
H_3 = (5862852 \times 33 + 114) \mod 2^{32}
$$

$$
H_3 = (193474116 + 114) \mod 2^{32} = 193474230
$$

$$
H_3 = 0x0B89 \text{C776}
$$

The analyst would then continue this iteration for the remaining characters (`t`, `u`, `a`, `l`, `A`, `l`, `l`, `o`, `c`) to obtain the full hash for the API name `VirtualAlloc`.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Sequence | Identified Behavior | MITRE ATT&CK Technique | Severity |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|----------|
| **Sequence 1** | **Registry Run-Key Persistence** — Malware is adding itself to the auto-start location to survive reboot. | **T1547.001** — Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys | **HIGH** |
| **Sequence 2** | **Process Injection** — The classic 4-step "classic injection" pattern. The malware opens a target process, allocates memory inside it, writes shellcode, and executes it via a remote thread. | **T1055** — Process Injection | **CRITICAL** |
| **Sequence 3** | **Ransomware-style Data Encryption** — Acquiring a crypto context, generating randomness, and encrypting user documents is a textbook ransomware pattern. | **T1486** — Data Encrypted for Impact | **CRITICAL** |

**Forensic Implications:**
- **Sequence 1** should trigger an immediate registry investigation: enumerate all `Run` keys and compare against a known-good baseline.
- **Sequence 2** requires memory forensics (e.g., using Volatility's `malfind` plugin) to locate the injected code region.
- **Sequence 3** demands rapid isolation of the host to prevent encryption spread; the random key may be recoverable from memory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "API Calls" for KTU 2024 Module 2**

- ⭐ **Definition:** An API call is a function invocation that requests OS services. The **IAT** stores the resolved addresses.
- ⭐ **Three Layers:** Win32 API → Native API (`ntdll.dll`) → System Call (`syscall/sysenter`). Each layer can be hooked.
- ⭐ **Ring Levels:** User applications run in **Ring 3**; kernel operations occur in **Ring 0**. API calls are the only legitimate way to cross.
- ⭐ **High-Risk API Categories to memorize:** File, Registry, Process/Thread, Memory, Network, Crypto, Service.
- ⭐ **API Hashing (DJB2):** $H_{i+1} = (H_i \times 33 + c_{i+1}) \mod 2^{32}$, starting with $H_0 = 5381$. Used to evade static IAT analysis.
- ⭐ **API Hooking types:** IAT hooking (per-process, weak vs. dynamic resolution), Inline hooking (function-wide, robust).
- ⭐ **MITRE ATT&CK Mapping:**
  - `RegSetValueExW` on Run key → **T1547.001**
  - `OpenProcess` + `VirtualAllocEx` + `WriteProcessMemory` + `CreateRemoteThread` → **T1055**
  - `CryptEncrypt` on user docs → **T1486**
- ⭐ **x64 vs. x86 IAT width:** **8 bytes** vs. **4 bytes** respectively — common exam pitfall.
- ⭐ **Syscall Number:** Each Native API has a unique index in the SSDT/SST.
- ⭐ **NTSTATUS Convention:** Return value `0` = `STATUS_SUCCESS` (i.e., the operation succeeded).
- ⭐ **Forensic Tools:** API Monitor, Process Monitor (ETW), Wireshark (for network APIs), Ghidra/CFF Explorer (for IAT).
- ⭐ **Anti-API-Hashing Countermeasure:** Pre-compute hashes of suspicious APIs and scan the malware binary for matching constants.
- ⭐ **Always specify architecture (x86/x64)** and the **ring level** in exam answers to gain full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Registries ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Windows Registry** is a hierarchical, centralized database used by Microsoft Windows operating systems to store low-level configuration settings, hardware information, software installation parameters, user preferences, and—critically for malware forensics—**persistent evidence of program execution, user activity, and system modification**. From a forensic standpoint, the Registry is a **volatile-on-arrival, but file-system-resident** artifact, composed of multiple binary files known as **hives** (e.g., `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`). In malware forensics, the Registry is the **single most valuable non-file artifact** because nearly every action performed on a Windows host—program installation, driver loading, USB insertion, document opening, network connection, persistence establishment—writes at least one entry into the Registry. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT741):** > The Windows Registry is a *tree-structured repository of system and user configuration data*, organized into **Root Keys → Subkeys → Key Values**, which serves as the primary persistence mechanism for Windows malware and a key forensic artifact for incident reconstruction. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building's Blueprint Room" Imagine a large corporate office building. Inside the building is a **locked blueprint room** that contains: - **The master floor plan** (where every office, room, and corridor is defined). - **Records of who has keys** to which rooms. - **A logbook** of every time a door was opened, by whom, and when. - **Maintenance instructions** for every piece of equipment installed. - **Vendor contact sheets** listing every software "supplier" and what they installed. If a thief breaks in and installs a hidden camera, the blueprint room will record: 1. **WHERE** it was installed (which room). 2. **HOW** it was activated (which switch, which schedule). 3. **WHEN** it was added (timestamp of entry). 4. **WHO** added it (which user account made the change). The Windows Registry is exactly this blueprint room for your computer. When malware infects a system, it leaves traces in this room like a thief leaving fingerprints. The forensic investigator's job is to enter this room with the right tools and read every logbook entry. ### 1.3 Why the Registry Matters in Malware Forensics | Forensic Question | Registry Answer | |---|---| | *Did malware persist after reboot?* | Autorun keys (Run, RunOnce, Services) | | *When was the system infected?* | Key LastWrite timestamps | | *Which user account was compromised?* | NTUSER.DAT of that profile | | *Was a USB device used as the infection vector?* | SYSTEM\MountedDevices, USBSTOR | | *What programs were run by the attacker?* | UserAssist, Shimcache, Amcache | | *What network shares were accessed?* | HKCU\Network, RecentServer list | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask "List the forensic artifacts stored in the Windows Registry." A 14-mark answer that only lists locations without explaining *what each artifact proves* will lose 4–5 marks. Always pair the **registry path** with the **forensic inference**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following metrics are standardized across Windows versions (NT 5.0 onwards, i.e., Windows 2000 → Windows 11): - **Maximum registry key name length:** **255 characters** (Unicode UTF-16). - **Maximum value name length:** **16,383 characters**. - **Maximum value data size:** **1 MB** per registry value (practical limit due to memory management). - **Registry hive page size:** **4 KB (4096 bytes)** per "bin" allocation unit. - **Maximum total hive size (theoretical):** **~4 GB**, but practical sizes are < 500 MB. - **Five (5) predefined root keys** on a live system. - **Standard LastWrite timestamp resolution:** **100-nanosecond intervals** (Windows FILETIME / Win32 epoch). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architectural Hierarchy — The Five-Tier Tree The Registry is not a flat file; it is a **strictly hierarchical, tree-indexed database** with five logical tiers. Each tier has a precise forensic role. **Tier 1 — Root Keys (5 fixed logical handles):** 1. `HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT` (`HKCR`) — File extension and COM object associations. 2. `HKEY_CURRENT_USER` (`HKCU`) — Profile of the currently logged-in user. 3. `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE` (`HKLM`) — Computer-wide configuration. 4. `HKEY_USERS` (`HKU`) — All user profiles on the system (`.DEFAULT` + SIDs). 5. `HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG` (`HKCC`) — Current hardware profile (live view of `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Hardware Profiles\Current`). > [!NOTE] > Only `HKLM` and `HKU` correspond to **physical hive files** on disk. The other three are **runtime merged views** created by the Configuration Manager (`CmpRootHive`). **Tier 2 — Hives (the physical files):** | Hive File | On-Disk Path | Backs Root Key | Forensic Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `SYSTEM` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SYSTEM` | `HKLM\SYSTEM` | Services, drivers, hardware, USB | | `SOFTWARE` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SOFTWARE` | `HKLM\SOFTWARE` | Installed software, autoruns | | `SAM` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM` | `HKLM\SAM` | User accounts, password hashes | | `SECURITY` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SECURITY` | `HKLM\SECURITY` | Security policies, audit settings | | `DEFAULT` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\DEFAULT` | `HKU\.DEFAULT` | Default user profile template | | `NTUSER.DAT` | `C:\Users\\NTUSER.DAT` | `HKCU` | User-specific activity (per profile) | | `UsrClass.dat` | `C:\Users\\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\UsrClass.dat` | `HKCU\Software\Classes` | User shell extensions, file associations | **Tier 3 — Keys (folders/subdirectories):** Named containers holding other keys and values. Example: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`. **Tier 4 — Values (named data entries):** Each key holds multiple values. Format: `(Value Name, Data Type, Data)`. **Tier 5 — Data (the actual binary content):** Strings, integers, binary blobs, multi-strings, expandable strings. ### 2.2 The Eight Registered Data Types | Type ID (Hex) | Symbolic Name | Meaning | Forensic Example | |---|---|---|---| | `0x0000` | `REG_NONE` | Undefined | Rare; corrupt hive | | `0x0001` | `REG_SZ` | Fixed Unicode string | `"C:\malware.exe"` | | `0x0002` | `REG_EXPAND_SZ` | String with `%VAR%` placeholders | `"%AppData%\update.exe"` | | `0x0003` | `REG_BINARY` | Raw binary data | Service binary path blob | | `0x0004` | `REG_DWORD` | 32-bit integer | Service Start type (`2` = AUTO_START) | | `0x0005` | `REG_DWORD_BIG_ENDIAN` | Big-endian 32-bit | Obsolete | | `0x0007` | `REG_MULTI_SZ` | Array of null-terminated strings | Multiple ServiceDll paths | | `0x000B` | `REG_QWORD` | 64-bit integer | 64-bit timestamps | ### 2.3 Forensic "Hot Zones" — High-Yield Registry Paths These are the **paths examiners must memorize** for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. **A. Persistence (Autorun) Keys:** $$\text{Persistence Path} = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} \text{HK}\{LM,CU\} \backslash \text{Software} \backslash \text{Microsoft} \backslash \text{Windows} \backslash \text{CurrentVersion} \backslash \{\text{Run} \mid \text{RunOnce} \mid \text{RunOnceEx}\}$$ **B. Service Registration:** $$\text{Service Key} = \text{HKLM} \backslash \text{SYSTEM} \backslash \text{CurrentControlSet} \backslash \text{Services} \backslash \langle \text{ServiceName} \rangle$$ **C. USB / Removable Device History:** $$\text{USB Evidence} = \text{HKLM} \backslash \text{SYSTEM} \backslash \text{CurrentControlSet} \backslash \text{Enum} \backslash \text{USBSTOR} \cup \text{HKLM} \backslash \text{SYSTEM} \backslash \text{MountedDevices}$$ **D. User Execution Evidence:** $$\text{User Execution} = \text{HKCU} \backslash \text{Software} \backslash \text{Microsoft} \backslash \text{Windows} \backslash \text{CurrentVersion} \backslash \text{Explorer} \backslash \text{UserAssist}$$ **E. File / Network History:** $$\text{OpenSave History} = \text{HKCU} \backslash \text{Software} \backslash \text{Microsoft} \backslash \text{Windows} \backslash \text{CurrentVersion} \backslash \text{Explorer} \backslash \text{ComDlg32}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Registry Analysis | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | LastWrite → UTC conversion | $T_{utc} = (T_{filetime} - 116444736000000000) \times 10^{-7}$ | Convert Windows FILETIME to human-readable date | | 2 | UserAssist decoding | $P_{run} = \text{ROT13}(\text{GUID}) \oplus \text{ROL7}$ | Decode obfuscated executable names | | 3 | Shimcache entry size | $N_{entries} = \lfloor L_{blob} / 0x30 \rfloor$ | Count programs executed (Win7–Win8.1) | | 4 | Amcache hash lookup | $H_{sha1} = \text{Amcache} \rightarrow \text{SHA-1}$ | Identify known malware by hash | | 5 | Service Start type | $S \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ | 0=Boot, 1=Sys, 2=Auto, 3=Manual, 4=Disabled | | 6 | Registry hive page check | $\text{Valid} \Leftrightarrow \text{Signature} = \text{0x72656766}$ (`regf`) | Confirm hive integrity | | 7 | USB serial length | $L_{serial} \leq 128$ chars | Validate USBSTOR entries | | 8 | Number of root keys | $N_{root} = 5$ | Standard answer for "list root keys" | | 9 | $N_{values\_per\_key}$ | $\text{Theoretical max} = 16{,}384$ | Memory allocation ceiling | | 10 | FILETIME epoch | $E_{win} = 1601\text{-}01\text{-}01\text{-}00\text{-}00\text{-}00$ | Reference point for all timestamps | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formula 1 (timestamp conversion)** is a KTU-favorite 3-mark question. The constant **116,444,736,000,000,000** is the number of 100-nanosecond intervals between **1601-01-01** (Win32 epoch) and **1970-01-01** (Unix epoch). ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In industry, Registry forensics is integrated into **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** tools such as CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, and SentinelOne. Every alert ("Suspicious service installed", "Autorun modified") traces back to a Registry WriteEvent. Incident responders using **Velociraptor**, **KAPE**, or **Autopsy** rely on Registry triage as the **first 10-minute step** of any live or post-mortem analysis. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Converting a Registry LastWrite Timestamp to a Readable Date This is the **single most-tested calculation** in KTU Module 2. We will derive it exhaustively. **Step 1 — Understand the inputs.** A Registry key's LastWrite timestamp is stored as a **Windows FILETIME** value, which is a 64-bit integer counting the number of **100-nanosecond intervals** since the Win32 epoch. $$\text{Win32 Epoch} = 1601\text{-}01\text{-}01 \text{ T } 00\text{:}00\text{:}00.0000000 \text{ UTC}$$ **Step 2 — Establish the relationship to Unix time.** The Unix epoch is **1970-01-01 T 00:00:00 UTC**. We must compute the offset between the two epochs. There are **369 years** between 1601 and 1970, consisting of: - 89 leap years (1604 → 1968, every 4 years, minus century exceptions). Number of days in 369 years: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{total} &= (369 \times 365) + 89 \\ &= 134{,}685 + 89 \\ &= 134{,}774 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert days to 100-nanosecond intervals.** Each day has $24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86{,}400$ seconds. Each second has $10^7$ hundred-nanoseconds. $$ \begin{aligned} N_{intervals} &= 134{,}774 \text{ days} \times 86{,}400 \text{ sec/day} \times 10^{7} \text{ intervals/sec} \\ &= 134{,}774 \times 8{,}640{,}000 \times 10^{7} \\ &= 1{,}164{,}448{,}736 \times 10^{8} \\ &= 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ Thus, the **conversion formula** is: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{unix\_seconds} &= \frac{T_{filetime} - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10^{7}} \\ T_{utc} &= T_{unix\_seconds} \text{ formatted as YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Worked numerical example.** Suppose a forensic tool reports a LastWrite timestamp of: $$T_{filetime} = 132{,}923{,}504{,}500{,}000{,}000$$ Apply the formula: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{filetime} - \text{Offset} &= 132{,}923{,}504{,}500{,}000{,}000 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000 \\ &= 16{,}478{,}768{,}500{,}000{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ Divide by $10^7$ to get Unix seconds: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{unix\_sec} &= \frac{16{,}478{,}768{,}500{,}000{,}000}{10^{7}} \\ &= 1{,}647{,}876{,}850 \end{aligned} $$ Convert Unix seconds to a human-readable date. We can split into days and remainder: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{elapsed} &= \left\lfloor \frac{1{,}647{,}876{,}850}{86{,}400} \right\rfloor = 19{,}072 \text{ days} \\ R_{sec} &= 1{,}647{,}876{,}850 - (19{,}072 \times 86{,}400) = 1{,}647{,}876{,}850 - 1{,}647{,}820{,}800 = 56{,}050 \text{ sec} \end{aligned} $$ Convert remainder to HH:MM:SS: $$ \begin{aligned} H &= \left\lfloor \frac{56{,}050}{3600} \right\rfloor = 15 \\ M_{rem} &= 56{,}050 - (15 \times 3600) = 56{,}050 - 54{,}000 = 2{,}050 \\ M &= \left\lfloor \frac{2{,}050}{60} \right\rfloor = 34 \\ S &= 2{,}050 - (34 \times 60) = 2{,}050 - 2{,}040 = 10 \end{aligned} $$ Compute the year by adding 19,072 days to 1970-01-01: $$ \begin{aligned} 1970 + (19{,}072 \div 365.25) &\approx 1970 + 52.21 \approx 2022 \end{aligned} $$ Exact computation gives **2022-03-10**. **Final answer:** The LastWrite timestamp corresponds to **2022-03-10 15:34:10 UTC**. ### 3.2 Derivation: UserAssist Decoding Logic The `UserAssist` key stores ROT13-obfuscated and 7-bit-ROL-rotated executable paths.
