We derive the decoding: **Step 1 — Apply ROT13 to alphabetic characters only.** $$ \text{ROT13}(c) = \begin{cases} ((c - 65 + 13) \bmod 26) + 65 & \text{if } c \in [A,Z] \\ ((c - 97 + 13) \bmod 26) + 97 & \text{if } c \in [a,z] \\ c & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 — Apply 7-bit left rotation to each character.** $$ \text{ROL7}(b) = ((b \ll 7) \oplus (b \gg 1)) \text{ \& } \text{0xFF} $$ **Step 3 — Decode the focus time.** The `Focus Time` and `Count` are 16-bit values; Count is `count / 5` (so multiply by 5 to get actual execution count). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Forensic Indicators by Registry Path | Registry Path | Indicator Type | Decoding Required | Malware Class Detected | |---|---|---|---| | `HKLM\…\Run` | Autorun persistence | None | Trojans, downloaders | | `HKLM\…\RunOnce` | One-shot autorun | None | Droppers, stage-2 loaders | | `HKLM\SYSTEM\…\Services\` | Service persistence | Start type | Rootkits, ransomware | | `HKLM\…\Image File Execution Options\\Debugger` | IFEO hijack | None | Process killers, debuggers | | `HKLM\…\Winlogon\Shell` | Shell replacement | None | Backdoors | | `HKCU\…\Explorer\Shell Folders` | Startup folder redirect | None | Ransomware | | `HKCU\…\Explorer\UserAssist` | Program execution | ROT13 + ROL7 | Any user-run malware | | `HKLM\…\AppCompat\…\Shimcache` | Program execution | None (binary) | Fileless malware | | `HKLM\…\AppCompat\…\Amcache` | Program execution + hash | Parse AMCACHE hive | Known malware (YARA) | | `HKLM\SYSTEM\Enum\USBSTOR` | USB device history | Decode serial | USB-borne attacks | | `HKCU\…\Explorer\TypedPaths` | Manual file navigation | None | Reconnaissance | | `HKLM\SAM\Domains\Account\Users` | Account enumeration | RID decoding | Lateral movement | | `HKLM\…\Tcpip\Parameters\Interfaces` | Network configuration | Parse IP blobs | C2 communication | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's tip:** Always state **both** the path **and** the data type you would look at. For example, do not just write "check Services" — write "check `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services` for `ImagePath` (REG_EXPAND_SZ) and `Start` (REG_DWORD, value 0/2)." --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Registry Hive Architecture (Block-Level Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DISK["PHYSICAL HIVE FILES ON DISK"] H1["SYSTEM hive
System32 config SYSTEM"] H2["SOFTWARE hive
System32 config SOFTWARE"] H3["SAM hive
System32 config SAM"] H4["SECURITY hive
System32 config SECURITY"] H5["DEFAULT hive
System32 config DEFAULT"] H6["NTUSER DAT
Users user NTUSER DAT"] H7["UsrClass dat
AppData Local UsrClass dat"] end subgraph LIVE["FIVE LIVE ROOT KEYS"] R1["HKEY CLASSES ROOT HKCR"] R2["HKEY CURRENT USER HKCU"] R3["HKEY LOCAL MACHINE HKLM"] R4["HKEY USERS HKU"] R5["HKEY CURRENT CONFIG HKCC"] end subgraph FOR["FORENSIC HOT ZONES"] F1["Persistence
Run RunOnce Services"] F2["Execution Evidence
UserAssist Shimcache Amcache"] F3["USB History
USBSTOR MountedDevices"] F4["User Activity
TypedPaths RecentDocs"] F5["Network Artifacts
Network MappedDrives"] end H1 --> R3 H2 --> R3 H3 --> R3 H4 --> R3 H5 --> R4 H6 --> R2 H7 --> R1 R3 --> F1 R2 --> F2 R3 --> F3 R2 --> F4 R2 --> F5 ``` ### 4.2 Registry Forensics Workflow (Step-by-Step) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["ACQUIRE HIVE FILES
copy SYSTEM SOFTWARE SAM
NTUSER DAT UsrClass dat"] --> A2["VERIFY INTEGRITY
check regf signature
verify hash"] A2 --> A3["LOAD INTO TOOL
Registry Viewer RegRipper
Autopsy FTK"] A3 --> A4["ENUMERATE PERSISTENCE
Run RunOnce Services
Image File Execution Options"] A4 --> A5["ANALYZE EXECUTION
UserAssist Shimcache
Amcache Prefetch correlation"] A5 --> A6["TIMELINE CONSTRUCTION
sort all LastWrite times
build super timeline"] A6 --> A7["IOC EXTRACTION
hash IP path
export YARA rules"] A7 --> A8["REPORT AND PRESERVE
document chain of custody
archive hives"] ``` ### 4.3 Registry Key Internal Structure (hbin Allocation) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["REGHEADER regf
signature 72656766"] --> P2["HBIN BLOCK 1
4 KB allocation unit"] P2 --> P3["VK Records
value entries"] P2 --> P4["SK Records
subkey list"] P2 --> P5["LF LH RI Records
index structures"] P4 --> P6["Child Key Names
security descriptors"] P3 --> P7["Data Blobs
strings integers binary"] ``` ### 4.4 Forensic Indicator Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1["SUSPICIOUS MALWARE INDICATOR"] --> Q2{"Persistence
mechanism?"} Q2 -->|Run key| R1["HKLM SOFTWARE Run
HKCU SOFTWARE Run"] Q2 -->|Service| R2["HKLM SYSTEM Services"] Q2 -->|Scheduled task| R3["NTUSER DAT SOFTWARE
Microsoft Windows
CurrentVersion Explorer
Taskband"] Q2 -->|IFEO hijack| R4["Image File Execution Options
Debugger value"] R1 --> S1["Decode binary data
extract executable path"] R2 --> S2["Verify ImagePath
check Start value"] R3 --> S3["Parse binary task cache"] R4 --> S4["Read Debugger string
identify hijacked binary"] S1 --> OUT["INDICATOR OF COMPROMISE"] S2 --> OUT S3 --> OUT S4 --> OUT ``` ### 4.5 USB Device Forensic Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR U1["USB DEVICE INSERTED"] --> U2["HKLM SYSTEM Enum USBSTOR"] U2 --> U3["Vendor Product Serial
Device instance ID"] U3 --> U4["HKLM SYSTEM MountedDevices"] U4 --> U5["Drive letter assignment
binary volume signature"] U5 --> U6["HKCU SYSTEM MountedDevices"] U6 --> U7["User-specific mount
last user attached"] U7 --> U8["HKLM SYSTEM CurrentControlSet Enum USB
setupapi log correlation"] U8 --> U9["TIMELINE
first insertion to last use"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define the Windows Registry. List any three of its root keys and state the physical hive file backing each of the listed root keys.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database used by Microsoft Windows operating systems to store configuration settings and system information used by the OS, hardware, and installed software. **(1 mark)** Three root keys and their backing hive files: **(2 marks)** 1. `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE` — backed by `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SAM`, `SECURITY` hives located in `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\`. 2. `HKEY_USERS` — backed by `DEFAULT` hive and each user's `NTUSER.DAT`. 3. `HKEY_CURRENT_USER` — backed by the `NTUSER.DAT` file of the currently logged-in user. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *What is a Registry "hive"? Mention the standard signature bytes used to validate a registry hive file.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Registry hive** is the physical binary file on disk that stores the contents of a logical section (root key) of the Windows Registry. Each hive is a transactionally-logged file composed of 4 KB allocation units called `hbin` blocks. **(2 marks)** The standard signature bytes used to validate a registry hive file are the four ASCII characters **`regf`** (hexadecimal `72 65 67 66`) appearing at offset 0 of the hive header. Any deviation indicates a corrupt or non-Registry file. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 2 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Persistence & Autoruns** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the Windows Registry persistence mechanisms commonly abused by malware. Discuss at least **four** distinct autorun/persistence registry paths, the data types expected, and the forensic value of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the internal structure of a Windows Registry hive file. Explain how the `regf` signature and `hbin` allocation units contribute to hive integrity verification. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a)** Persistence mechanisms abused by malware in the Windows Registry: 1. **`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`** *(REG_SZ)*: Contains the path of an executable that runs at every user logon. Forensic value: identifies malware that survives reboot; check for unusual executable paths or unsigned binaries. **(2 marks)** 2. **`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\`** *(ImagePath: REG_EXPAND_SZ, Start: REG_DWORD)*: A service with Start=2 (AUTO_START) loads the malicious driver/service at boot. Forensic value: identifies kernel-mode rootkits and persistent services; check for services pointing to `Temp`, `AppData`, or unknown paths. **(2 marks)** 3. **`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\\Debugger`** *(REG_SZ)*: When a target process is launched, the OS executes the Debugger string instead. Forensic value: identifies "IFEO hijacking" used to silently launch malware whenever a legitimate program (e.g., `explorer.exe`) runs. **(1.5 marks)** 4. **`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders`** *(Startup folder redirect)*: Malware redirects the Startup folder to a directory containing its executable. Forensic value: identifies low-privilege persistence that bypasses UAC. **(1.5 marks)** **(b)** Internal structure of a Registry hive file: A Windows Registry hive file is a binary, transactionally-logged file composed of three logical sections: 1. **Header (`regf` signature, 4096 bytes):** Contains the file signature `0x72656766` ("regf"), primary sequence number, last-written timestamp, and root cell offset. If the signature is missing, the file is corrupt. **(2 marks)** 2. **Hive Bins (`hbin` allocation units):** Each `hbin` block is exactly **4 KB (4096 bytes)** and begins with the magic `hbin` (`0x6E696268`). The hive is a sequence of `hbin` blocks holding the actual key, value, security descriptor, and index records. **(2 marks)** 3. **Records (cells):** Within each `hbin`, data is stored as variable-length cells with 4-byte size headers. Cell types include `nk` (named key), `vk` (value key), `sk` (security key), `lf`/`lh` (leaf index), `ri` (red-black tree index), and `db` (data block). **(2 marks)** 4. **Transactional logging:** Each hive is paired with `.LOG` (intent log) and `.LOG1`/`.LOG2` files (transaction logs). On system failure, the kernel replays logs to reconstruct the hive, ensuring transactional integrity. **(1 mark)** **Diagram (text representation):** | Offset 0 | `regf` header (4096 bytes) | Magic, timestamp, root cell offset | | Offset 4096 | `hbin` block 1 | 4 KB, contains `nk`, `vk`, `lf`, `db` cells | | Offset 8192 | `hbin` block 2 | 4 KB, more cells | | ... | ... | ... | | End | Trailing `hbin` block | Last 4 KB aligned block | **Forensic verification process:** - Validate `regf` at offset 0 (file signature). If invalid → file is not a hive. - Validate `hbin` magic at every 4 KB boundary. - Verify the root cell offset from the header points to a valid `nk` record. - Confirm the SHA-256 hash of the hive matches the hash logged in the case-management system. **(0 mark — process explained, mark already allocated above)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — User Execution Evidence** **(a)** Explain the `UserAssist`, `Shimcache`, and `Amcache` registry artifacts. For each, specify the registry path, the data stored, and how a forensic investigator would decode it to identify malware execution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An investigator recovers a Registry hive file with the following properties: - File: `NTUSER.DAT` from user profile `jdoe` - LastWrite timestamp (raw FILETIME): `133,025,491,200,000,000` - A `UserAssist` entry contains: `ROT13-decoded path = C:\Users\jdoe\AppData\Roaming\update.exe`, with a Count field value of `25` and a LastRun timestamp `2 days ago`. Calculate: (i) The actual Unix timestamp and the UTC human-readable date. **(3 marks)** (ii) The actual number of program executions recorded in the UserAssist Count. **(1 mark)** (iii) The estimated last execution time given the present time. **(3 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a)** Comparison of three execution-evidence artifacts: | Feature | UserAssist | Shimcache | Amcache | |---|---|---|---| | **Path** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist\{GUID}\Count` | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\AppCompatCache` (Win7/8) | `C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\Amcache.hve` (Win8+) | | **Data stored** | Executable path, run count, last execution time | Executable path, file size, last modified time of binary | Executable path, SHA-1 hash, PE metadata, first execution time | | **Encoding** | ROT13 + 7-bit ROL7 on the path string | Plain binary, `0x30`-byte entries | Parsed key-value pairs | | **Forensic value** | Identifies *user-initiated* execution with count and time | Identifies execution even after the file is deleted | Provides *hash* for YARA/malware family identification | | **Limitation** | Disabled when Group Policy disables UserAssist history | Limited to ~1024 entries (Win7); disabled in Win10 | Only first execution is recorded; rotated entries | **(2.5 marks for the comparison + 1 mark for decoding explanation each ≈ 4.5 marks rounded to 7)** Detailed decoding: - **UserAssist:** Apply ROT13, then ROL7 to recover path; divide Count by 5 to get actual executions; convert LastRun FILETIME to UTC. **(1 mark)** - **Shimcache:** Walk the binary blob in `0x30`-byte increments until end marker `0x00` for the full path field. **(1 mark)** - **Amcache:** Load the `Amcache.hve` hive into a registry parser (AmcacheParser) and export the `File` key subkeys; the SHA-1 hash field can be cross-referenced with VirusTotal. **(0.5 mark)** **(b) Numerical solution:** **(i) Unix timestamp and UTC date:** Apply the formula: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{unix\_sec} &= \frac{133{,}025{,}491{,}200{,}000{,}000 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10^{7}} \\ &= \frac{16{,}580{,}755{,}200{,}000{,}000}{10^{7}} \\ &= 1{,}658{,}075{,}520 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ Convert to days: $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{1{,}658{,}075{,}520}{86{,}400} \right\rfloor = 19{,}190 \text{ days} $$ Convert days to date. 19,190 days after **1970-01-01** is **2022-07-15**. Compute remainder seconds: $$ R = 1{,}658{,}075{,}520 - (19{,}190 \times 86{,}400) = 1{,}658{,}075{,}520 - 1{,}658{,}016{,}000 = 59{,}520 $$ Convert remainder to HH:MM:SS: $$ \begin{aligned} H &= 59{,}520 \div 3{,}600 = 16 \text{ hours} \\ M_{rem} &= 59{,}520 - (16 \times 3{,}600) = 1{,}920 \\ M &= 1{,}920 \div 60 = 32 \text{ minutes} \\ S &= 1{,}920 - (32 \times 60) = 0 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Result: 2022-07-15 16:32:00 UTC** **(3 marks)** **(ii) Actual number of executions:** UserAssist stores `Count = 5 × N`. Therefore: $$ N = \frac{25}{5} = 5 \text{ executions} $$ **Result: 5 actual executions** **(1 mark)** **(iii) Estimated last execution time:** The problem states the LastRun was "2 days ago". If the present investigation time is, say, **2022-07-17 10:00:00**, then: $$ T_{last\_exec} = T_{now} - 2 \text{ days} = 2022\text{-}07\text{-}15 \text{ } 10\text{:}00\text{:}00 $$ Cross-check with (i): the file's LastWrite was **2022-07-15 16:32:00** — close to the estimated last execution, indicating the malware likely created/modified the key in close temporal proximity to its final run. **(3 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing root keys with hive files** — Students often list 5 root keys but forget to map them to actual on-disk hive files (costs 2 marks). > 2. **Wrong epoch constant** — Using $10^9$ (Unix nanoseconds) instead of $10^7$ (Windows 100-nanoseconds) is a 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Forgetting ROT13 in UserAssist** — Examiner will not award full marks if the decoding step is omitted. > 4. **Listing paths without data types** — Always specify `REG_SZ`, `REG_DWORD`, etc. > 5. **Ignoring the SAM hive** — Many students omit SAM, which contains user account forensics. Lose 1 mark. > 6. **Not stating the forensic inference** — "Run key contains malware" is incomplete; write "Run key contains `C:\Temp\svchost.exe` which is an unsigned binary located in Temp — a strong malware indicator." > 7. **Forgetting the `regf` magic** — Signature `0x72656766` is mandatory knowledge. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Registries** - **Registry Definition:** Hierarchical, tree-structured database of Windows configuration; **5 root keys** (HKCR, HKCU, HKLM, HKU, HKCC); **physical hives** on disk. - **Hive files:** `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `DEFAULT`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`. - **Internal structure:** `regf` header → `hbin` blocks (4 KB each) → cells (`nk`, `vk`, `sk`, `lf`, `lh`, `ri`, `db`). - **Hive signature:** `0x72656766` ("regf"); `hbin` magic `0x6862696E`. - **Persistence keys to memorize:** `Run`, `RunOnce`, `RunOnceEx`, `Services`, `Image File Execution Options\Debugger`, `Winlogon\Shell`, `AppInit_DLLs`. - **Execution evidence:** `UserAssist` (ROT13 + ROL7), `Shimcache` (`AppCompatCache`), `Amcache.hve` (SHA-1). - **USB evidence:** `USBSTOR` (device serial), `MountedDevices` (drive letter assignment). - **Account evidence:** `SAM\Domains\Account\Users` (RID 500 = Administrator, 501 = Guest). - **Timestamp formula:** $T_{unix} = (T_{filetime} - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000) / 10^7$. - **Service Start codes:** 0=Boot, 1=System, 2=Auto, 3=Manual, 4=Disabled. - **Tools:** RegRipper, Registry Viewer, Autopsy Registry Plug-in, FTK Registry Viewer, AmcacheParser, RegistryExplorer (Eric Zimmerman). - **Best practice:** Always preserve original hives (write-block + SHA-256 hash), work on copies. - **Forensic principle:** The Registry is a **persistent, system-wide event log** — every action that leaves a trace is recorded somewhere here.

topics.title: Network Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Network Activities ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Network Activities** in the context of Malware Forensics refers to the systematic observation, capture, recording, and analysis of network-based communications, behaviors, and artifacts produced by malicious software (malware) as it interacts with local networks, the internet, and remote command-and-control (C2) infrastructures. It encompasses the identification of suspicious protocols, port usage, packet payloads, DNS resolutions, HTTP/HTTPS requests, SMTP/IRC traffic, beaconing patterns, lateral movement attempts, data exfiltration flows, and covert channel communications. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Network Activities* fall under the **live-system and network-based forensic artifact** category, where investigators reconstruct the network footprint of malware to identify infection vectors, scope of compromise, communication endpoints, and persistence mechanisms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Network Activities is a high-weightage topic in Module 2 because it directly supports the course outcome *CO2: Analyze network-based artifacts and live system behaviors produced by malware* and maps to Bloom's cognitive level **Analyze (L4)** and **Evaluate (L5)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a thief who breaks into a house. Even if you never see the thief directly, you can piece together a profile of their activities by examining the evidence they leave behind: - **Footprints in the mud** → Network packets traversing a switch - **Fingerprints on a glass** → Unique malware signatures in packet payloads - **Phone calls to an accomplice** → C2 (Command-and-Control) beaconing - **Bag full of stolen goods carried out the back door** → Data exfiltration traffic - **A hidden spare key under the mat** → Backdoors and persistence mechanisms A malware forensic investigator essentially performs this reconstruction — but on a digital scale, where the "house" is a corporate network and the "evidence" consists of packets, logs, and protocol metadata. ### 1.3 Key Sub-Domains of Network Activities | Sub-Domain | What It Captures | |---|---| | **Packet Capture (PCAP)** | Raw network traffic at the bit/frame level | | **Flow Analysis (NetFlow)** | Aggregated session metadata (src/dst IP, ports, bytes) | | **DNS Forensics** | Domain resolutions, tunneling, DGA patterns | | **HTTP/HTTPS Forensics** | URLs, User-Agents, POST bodies, TLS metadata | | **C2 / Beaconing Analysis** | Periodic outbound callbacks to attacker servers | | **Lateral Movement Tracking** | SMB, RDP, WMI, PsExec traffic across hosts | | **Data Exfiltration Detection** | Unusual outbound volume, FTP, DNS tunneling, steganography | | **Covert Channels** | ICMP tunneling, hidden fields in protocol headers | > [!NOTE] > A **Network Activity Indicator (NAI)** is any observable network event that can serve as evidence of malicious behavior. The strength of a forensic investigation depends on correlating multiple NAIs into a coherent **attack timeline**. ### 1.4 Why Network Activities Matter in Forensics Network traffic is one of the **most volatile** evidence types — it disappears the moment a packet leaves the wire. Unlike a hard drive image (which can be preserved bit-for-bit), network captures must be performed in real time or via pre-deployed sensors. This makes network forensics both critical and challenging. ### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used - **Bandwidth (bps)** — Bits per second, used to detect data exfiltration anomalies - **PPS (Packets Per Second)** — Indicator of DDoS, port scans, or flooding - **CPS (Connections Per Second)** — Beaconing frequency - **Jitter (ms)** — Variation in packet inter-arrival time - **RTT (Round-Trip Time)** — Latency to C2 servers - **TTL (Time-To-Live)** — Used for OS fingerprinting and hop-counting - **Entropy (bits/byte)** — Indicates encrypted or packed payloads > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **full packet capture (FPC)** and **flow data (NetFlow/sFlow)** — memorize their trade-offs: FPC gives payload visibility but is storage-intensive; flow data is lightweight but payload-blind. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Network Forensics Investigation Lifecycle The KTU 2024 syllabus structures network activity analysis into a **four-phase operational model**: 1. **Detection & Capture Phase** — Identifying anomalous traffic and preserving it 2. **Identification & Extraction Phase** — Isolating malicious sessions from benign traffic 3. **Analysis & Correlation Phase** — Reconstructing the attack timeline 4. **Response & Documentation Phase** — Producing admissible forensic reports ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic #### Phase 1: Detection & Capture - Deploy **sensors** at strategic network choke points (SPAN ports, TAPs, inline IDS) - Capture live traffic using tools such as **Wireshark**, **tcpdump**, **NetworkMiner**, **Suricata** - Preserve original PCAP files with **SHA-256 hashes** for chain-of-custody - Capture timing metadata (timestamps in UTC) for accurate correlation #### Phase 2: Identification & Extraction - Filter traffic by IP, port, protocol, or signature - Extract **HTTP objects** (files downloaded by malware) - Reassemble **TCP streams** to view full session payloads - Decode protocols (DNS, HTTP, SMB, FTP) using protocol dissectors #### Phase 3: Analysis & Correlation - Match extracted artifacts against threat intelligence feeds (YARA, Snort rules) - Identify **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)**: IPs, domains, file hashes, JA3 fingerprints - Correlate with host-based logs (Sysmon, Event Logs) to confirm infection - Reconstruct **kill-chain** mapping to MITRE ATT&CK #### Phase 4: Response & Documentation - Block malicious IPs/domains at the firewall - Document all findings in a forensic report with chain-of-custody - Provide remediation steps and detection signatures ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the key formulas, thresholds, and metrics frequently tested in KTU examinations for Network Activities. | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Forensic Utility | |---|---|---|---| | Beaconing Interval | $\Delta t_{beacon} = t_{n+1} - t_{n}$ | seconds (s) | Detects periodic C2 callbacks | | Beacon Jitter | $J = \sigma(\Delta t_{beacon}) / \mu(\Delta t_{beacon})$ | dimensionless | Lower jitter → automated malware beacon | | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)$ | bits/byte | Detects encrypted/packed payloads | | Bandwidth Anomaly Ratio | $R_{bw} = \frac{BW_{observed}}{BW_{baseline}}$ | dimensionless | Flags exfiltration (R > 3 typical threshold) | | Packet Inter-Arrival Time | $IAT = t_{i+1} - t_{i}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Distinguishes human vs. automated traffic | | Connection Success Rate | $CSR = \frac{N_{successful}}{N_{total}} \cdot 100\%$ | percent (%) | C2 servers often have 100% CSR per source | | DNS Query Entropy | $H_{DNS} = -\sum p(d) \log_2 p(d)$ | bits/domain | High entropy → DGA-based malware | | TCP RTT Estimation | $RTT \approx t_{SYN-ACK} - t_{SYN}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Geolocation hint for C2 server | | JA3 Hash | $JA3 = MD5(cipher\_exts \vert exts \vert curves)$ | hash string | TLS client fingerprinting | | TTL Hop Count | $Hops = TTL_{initial} - TTL_{observed}$ | hops | OS and topology inference | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The Shannon Entropy formula is the most-tested equation in KTU Malware Forensics papers. Always show the **full summation expansion** when calculating it — partial credit is heavily rewarded. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Network activity analysis is foundational in: - **Incident Response (IR)** — Rapid triage of active breaches - **Threat Intelligence** — Mining IoCs from observed attacks - **SOC Operations** — Feeding SIEM rules from forensic findings - **Legal Proceedings** — Admissible evidence under Section 65B (India IT Act) - **Threat Hunting** — Proactive search for dormant malware C2 --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Detecting Beaconing via Jitter Analysis A malware sample is observed contacting an external server. The following 5 inter-arrival times (in seconds) are recorded between successive beacons: $$\Delta t = \{60, 62, 59, 61, 60\}$$ We need to determine whether this is **automated malware beaconing** (low jitter) or **human-driven traffic** (high jitter). #### Step 1: Compute the Mean Inter-Arrival Time $$ \begin{aligned} \mu(\Delta t) &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta t_i \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{5} (60 + 62 + 59 + 61 + 60) \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{5} \cdot 302 \\[4pt] &= 60.4 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 2: Compute the Standard Deviation First, compute the squared deviations: $$ \begin{aligned} (\Delta t_1 - \mu)^2 &= (60 - 60.4)^2 = (-0.4)^2 = 0.16 \\ (\Delta t_2 - \mu)^2 &= (62 - 60.4)^2 = (1.6)^2 = 2.56 \\ (\Delta t_3 - \mu)^2 &= (59 - 60.4)^2 = (-1.4)^2 = 1.96 \\ (\Delta t_4 - \mu)^2 &= (61 - 60.4)^2 = (0.6)^2 = 0.36 \\ (\Delta t_5 - \mu)^2 &= (60 - 60.4)^2 = (-0.4)^2 = 0.16 \end{aligned} $$ Sum of squared deviations: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{5} (\Delta t_i - \mu)^2 &= 0.16 + 2.56 + 1.96 + 0.36 + 0.16 \\ &= 5.20 \end{aligned} $$ Standard deviation: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma(\Delta t) &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\Delta t_i - \mu)^2} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{\frac{5.20}{5}} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{1.04} \\[4pt] &\approx 1.0198 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3: Compute the Jitter (Coefficient of Variation) $$ \begin{aligned} J &= \frac{\sigma(\Delta t)}{\mu(\Delta t)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1.0198}{60.4} \\[4pt] &\approx 0.0169 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4: Forensic Interpretation > [!IMPORTANT] > A jitter value of **J ≈ 0.017 (1.69%)** is extremely low. Empirical thresholds in industry: > - **J < 0.05** → Strong indicator of automated malware beaconing > - **0.05 ≤ J ≤ 0.20** → Suspicious, warrants deeper inspection > - **J > 0.20** → Likely human-driven or random behavior > > **Conclusion:** The traffic exhibits classic malware beaconing behavior with a fixed 60-second callback interval. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Shannon Entropy of a DNS Exfiltration Payload A malware uses DNS TXT records to exfiltrate data. The following payload string is captured: $$\text{Encoded data} = \text{``a8f3b2c1d4e5f6''}$$ We need to determine the entropy of the payload. #### Step 1: Count Character Frequencies The string contains: `a, 8, f, 3, b, 2, c, 1, d, 4, e, 5, f, 6` - `a` → 1 - `8` → 1 - `f` → 2 - `3` → 1 - `b` → 1 - `2` → 1 - `c` → 1 - `1` → 1 - `d` → 1 - `4` → 1 - `e` → 1 - `5` → 1 - `6` → 1 Total characters: **N = 14** #### Step 2: Compute Probabilities For each unique character: $$ p_i = \frac{\text{count of character } i}{\text{total characters}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} p(a) = p(8) = p(3) = p(b) = p(2) &= p(c) = p(1) = p(d) \\ &= p(4) = p(e) = p(5) = p(6) = \frac{1}{14} \end{aligned} $$ $$ p(f) = \frac{2}{14} = \frac{1}{7} $$ #### Step 3: Apply the Shannon Entropy Formula $$ \begin{aligned} H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i) \\[4pt] &= -\left[ 12 \cdot \frac{1}{14} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{14}\right) + \frac{2}{14} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{14}\right) \right] \\[4pt] &= -\left[ \frac{12}{14} \cdot (-\log_2 14) + \frac{2}{14} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{7}\right) \right] \\[4pt] &= \frac{12}{14} \cdot \log_2 14 + \frac{2}{14} \cdot \log_2 7 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4: Numerical Evaluation $$ \begin{aligned} \log_2 14 &= \log_2(2 \cdot 7) = 1 + \log_2 7 \\ &\approx 1 + 2.8074 = 3.8074 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \log_2 7 &\approx 2.8074 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} H(X) &\approx \frac{12}{14} \cdot 3.8074 + \frac{2}{14} \cdot 2.8074 \\[4pt] &\approx 0.8571 \cdot 3.8074 + 0.1429 \cdot 2.8074 \\[4pt] &\approx 3.2635 + 0.4011 \\[4pt] &\approx 3.6646 \text{ bits/character} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 5: Forensic Interpretation > [!NOTE] > **Entropy Thresholds for DNS payloads:** > - **H < 2.5** → Likely human-readable or low-randomness (legitimate) > - **2.5 ≤ H ≤ 3.5** → Suspicious (could be Base32/Base64 encoding) > - **H > 3.5** → Strong indicator of encrypted or random exfiltration > > **Conclusion:** With H ≈ 3.66 bits/character, the DNS payload exhibits high entropy consistent with encoded exfiltration traffic. ### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Bandwidth Anomaly Ratio for Exfiltration A workstation's baseline outbound bandwidth is **500 KB/hour**. During a suspected incident, the observed outbound bandwidth is **2.5 MB/hour**. #### Step 1: Apply the Bandwidth Anomaly Ratio Formula $$ \begin{aligned} R_{bw} &= \frac{BW_{observed}}{BW_{baseline}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2.5 \text{ MB/h}}{500 \text{ KB/h}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2500 \text{ KB/h}}{500 \text{ KB/h}} \\[4pt] &= 5.0 \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 2: Forensic Interpretation > [!IMPORTANT] > An anomaly ratio of **R_bw = 5.0** is 10× higher than the typical threshold of 3.0. This strongly suggests data exfiltration. The investigator should: > 1. Identify the destination IP/port of the bulk traffic > 2. Reassemble the TCP stream to extract the exfiltrated content > 3. Cross-reference with DLP (Data Loss Prevention) logs ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Malware Network Behavior Classification | Malware Family | Protocol Used | Port | Beaconing Interval | Encryption | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Emotet** | HTTP/HTTPS | 80, 443 | ~60 s | TLS | | **TrickBot** | HTTPS | 443 | ~30 s | TLS | | **Zeus** | HTTP | 80 | ~90 s | XOR + Base64 | | **Mirai** | Telnet/SSH | 23, 22 | N/A (DDoS) | None | | **DNS Exfil** | DNS | 53 | Per query | Base32/Base64 | | **ICMP Tunnel** | ICMP | N/A | Variable | Custom payload | | **Cobalt Strike** | HTTPS/Malleable C2 | 443 | Configurable (jitter) | TLS | | **Conti** | SMB / HTTP | 445, 80 | ~45 s | AES + HTTPS | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Malware Network Communication Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB victimHost[Infected Host] c2Server[Command and Control Server] dnsResolver[DNS Resolver] proxyServer[Proxy or VPN] lateralTarget[Lateral Movement Target] exfilDest[Exfiltration Destination] socSensor[SOC Sensor or IDS] analyst[Forensic Analyst] victimHost -->|Beacon 60s| c2Server victimHost -->|DNS Query| dnsResolver dnsResolver -->|Resolution| c2Server c2Server -->|Command Payload| victimHost victimHost -->|Lateral SMB| lateralTarget victimHost -->|HTTP POST Encrypted| exfilDest socSensor -.->|Mirrored Traffic Capture| victimHost socSensor -->|Alert Generation| analyst victimHost -->|Tunneled Traffic| proxyServer proxyServer -->|Filtered Output| c2Server analyst -->|Report and Block| socSensor ``` ### 4.2 Network Forensics Investigation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Phase 1: Detection] --> B[Phase 2: Identification] B --> C[Phase 3: Analysis] C --> D[Phase 4: Response] A1[Deploy TAP or SPAN] --> A2[Capture PCAP] A2 --> A3[Hash and Preserve] A3 --> B1[Filter by IoC] B1 --> B2[Extract Sessions] B2 --> B3[Decode Protocols] B3 --> C1[Correlate with Threat Intel] C1 --> C2[Reconstruct Kill Chain] C2 --> C3[Map to MITRE ATT and CK] C3 --> D1[Block IoCs] D1 --> D2[Generate Forensic Report] D2 --> D3[Hand Over to Legal or IR] ``` ### 4.3 Network Activity Analysis Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB L7[Layer 7: Application Protocols] L6[Layer 6: TLS and SSL Analysis] L5[Layer 5: Session Reconstruction] L4[Layer 4: TCP and UDP Analysis] L3[Layer 3: IP and Routing Forensics] L2[Layer 2: MAC and ARP Inspection] L1[Layer 1: Physical Capture via TAP] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> L7 L7 --> OUT[Forensic Output: IoCs, Timeline, Evidence] ``` ### 4.4 Beaconing Pattern Detection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Capture Network Traffic] --> check{HTTP or HTTPS
Outbound} check -->|No| ignore[Discard as Benign] check -->|Yes| count[Count Connections to Same IP] count --> threshold{Connection Count > 10
and Consistent Interval} threshold -->|No| ignore threshold -->|Yes| jitter{Jitter Less than 0.05} jitter -->|No| suspicious[Mark as Suspicious] jitter -->|Yes| c2[Flag as Probable C2 Beacon] c2 --> correlate[Correlate with Threat Intel Feed] correlate --> block[Block IP at Firewall] block --> report[Generate IoC Report] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the term "Network Activity Indicator (NAI)" in the context of malware forensics. List any four common types of NAIs with one example each.* **Model Answer:** A **Network Activity Indicator (NAI)** is any observable network event, artifact, or behavioral pattern that provides forensic evidence of malicious software activity on a network. Four common types of NAIs are: 1. **IP/Domain Indicators** — Example: Outbound connection to a known malicious IP 2. **Protocol/Port Indicators** — Example: Unusual use of port 4444 (Metasploit default) 3. **Packet Payload Indicators** — Example: HTTP POST containing Base64-encoded data 4. **Behavioral/Timing Indicators** — Example: Periodic 60-second beaconing to an external server *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four types with examples)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between full packet capture (FPC) and NetFlow-based analysis in network forensics. State one advantage and one limitation of each.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Full Packet Capture (FPC) | NetFlow Analysis | |---|---|---| | **Data Captured** | Full packet headers + payload | Metadata (src/dst IP, ports, bytes, duration) | | **Storage** | Very high (GB to TB per day) | Low (KB per flow record) | | **Advantage** | Payload-level deep inspection possible | Scalable across large networks | | **Limitation** | Not scalable; privacy concerns | No payload visibility; cannot detect content-based malware | *(3 marks: 1 for the differentiation, 1 each for advantage and limitation)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **four-phase network forensics investigation lifecycle** in detail. For each phase, list the tools commonly used and the forensic artifacts produced. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A malware sample communicates with a C2 server. The inter-arrival times (in seconds) of 6 consecutive beacons are: **{45, 47, 44, 46, 45, 46}**. Compute the **mean inter-arrival time**, **standard deviation**, and **jitter coefficient**. Based on the jitter value, determine whether this is automated malware beaconing or human-driven traffic. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (a):** **Phase 1 — Detection & Capture** - **Tools:** Wireshark, tcpdump, Suricata, Zeek (formerly Bro) - **Activities:** Deploy network TAPs/SPAN ports, capture live traffic - **Artifacts:** PCAP files, IDS alerts, raw packet logs **Phase 2 — Identification & Extraction** - **Tools:** NetworkMiner, tshark, Wireshark filters - **Activities:** Filter traffic by IoC, extract HTTP objects, reassemble TCP streams - **Artifacts:** Extracted files, decoded sessions, HTTP objects **Phase 3 — Analysis & Correlation** - **Tools:** Splunk, ELK Stack, Maltego, MITRE ATT&CK Navigator - **Activities:** Correlate with threat intel, reconstruct kill chain - **Artifacts:** Attack timeline, IoC list, ATT&CK mapping **Phase 4 — Response & Documentation** - **Tools:** Firewall rule managers, ticketing systems, report generators - **Activities:** Block IoCs, document chain-of-custody, submit evidence - **Artifacts:** Forensic report, updated detection rules, legal evidence *(7 marks: ~1.5 per phase, distributing marks across tools, activities, and artifacts)* **Model Answer (b):** **Step 1: Mean Inter-Arrival Time** $$ \begin{aligned} \mu &= \frac{1}{6}(45 + 47 + 44 + 46 + 45 + 46) \\ &= \frac{1}{6} \cdot 273 \\ &= 45.5 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Standard Deviation** Squared deviations: $$ \begin{aligned} (45 - 45.5)^2 &= 0.25 \\ (47 - 45.5)^2 &= 2.25 \\ (44 - 45.5)^2 &= 2.25 \\ (46 - 45.5)^2 &= 0.25 \\ (45 - 45.5)^2 &= 0.25 \\ (46 - 45.5)^2 &= 0.25 \end{aligned} $$ Sum = 5.50 $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{5.50}{6}} \\ &= \sqrt{0.9167} \\ &\approx 0.9574 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Jitter Coefficient** $$ \begin{aligned} J &= \frac{0.9574}{45.5} \\ &\approx 0.0210 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Forensic Interpretation** J ≈ 0.021, which is **well below 0.05** → **Strong indicator of automated malware beaconing** with a 45-second callback interval. *(7 marks: 2 for mean, 2 for standard deviation, 1 for jitter, 2 for interpretation)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a typical malware C2 communication flow**. Label all components and describe the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A workstation's baseline outbound DNS traffic is **20 queries/hour**. During an incident, the workstation is observed making **500 queries/hour** to a single suspicious domain. The DNS payload strings show high randomness. Calculate the **bandwidth anomaly ratio** and explain the **Shannon entropy** concept. Using entropy thresholds, determine the likely nature of the traffic. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (a):** The architecture of a typical malware C2 communication flow consists of the following components: **1. Infected Host (Bot/Agent)** - Runs the malware binary - Initiates outbound beacon to C2 server - Executes commands received from C2 **2. C2 Server (Command-and-Control)** - Receives beacons from infected hosts - Sends commands (download, execute, exfiltrate) - Often uses domain generation algorithms (DGA) **3. DNS Resolver** - Translates C2 domain names to IP addresses - Often abused for DNS tunneling/exfiltration **4. Network Infrastructure** - Proxies, VPNs, Tor nodes used by malware to hide C2 traffic - Compromised legitimate websites used as dead-drop resolvers **5. Exfiltration Destination** - FTP server, cloud storage, paste site, or another C2 channel - Receives stolen data from infected hosts **Block Diagram Representation:** ``` [Infected Host] --(beacon/HTTPS)--> [Proxy/VPN] --> [C2 Server] | | |---(DNS exfil via TXT)----> [DNS Resolver] -----------| | | v v [Exfiltration Destination] <--(stolen data)---- [C2 Server] ``` *(7 marks: 4 for architecture explanation, 3 for diagram and labels)* **Model Answer (b):** **Step 1: Bandwidth Anomaly Ratio** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{bw} &= \frac{500 \text{ queries/h}}{20 \text{ queries/h}} \\ &= 25.0 \end{aligned} $$ This is a **25× spike** — extremely abnormal. Likely DNS tunneling/exfiltration. **Step 2: Shannon Entropy Concept** Shannon Entropy measures the **average information content (randomness)** per symbol in a message: $$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i) $$ where $p(x_i)$ is the probability of symbol $x_i$ in the message. - **H = 0** → All symbols identical (no information) - **H = log₂(N)** → Maximum randomness (uniform distribution) **Step 3: Application to DNS Payload** Typical DNS entropy thresholds: | Entropy Range (bits/char) | Interpretation | |---|---| | H < 2.5 | Legitimate (human-readable) | | 2.5 ≤ H ≤ 3.5 | Suspicious (Base32/Base64 encoded) | | H > 3.5 | Encrypted or random exfiltration | Since the DNS payloads show **high randomness**, entropy would likely exceed **3.5 bits/character**, indicating **encrypted or encoded exfiltration traffic** — consistent with DNS tunneling malware. **Conclusion:** The combination of a 25× query volume spike and high-entropy payloads strongly indicates **DNS-based data exfiltration**. *(7 marks: 2 for ratio, 2 for entropy concept, 1 for thresholds, 2 for conclusion)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Network Activities Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to state units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks when units (seconds, bits/byte, hops) are omitted in numerical answers. > 2. **Skipping the log base in entropy** — Always explicitly write "log₂" in the Shannon entropy formula. Writing "log" alone is considered ambiguous and may cost a mark. > 3. **Confusing jitter with standard deviation** — Jitter is the **ratio** σ/μ, not σ alone. This is the single most common conceptual error. > 4. **Not correlating NAIs** — Examiners expect students to *correlate* multiple indicators (e.g., high DNS volume + high entropy + low jitter) rather than presenting them in isolation. > 5. **Ignoring chain-of-custody** — Any forensic answer must mention **hash preservation (SHA-256)** and **timestamp recording (UTC)** for full marks. > 6. **Mixing up FPC vs. NetFlow** — A frequent 1-mark loss comes from incorrectly stating that NetFlow captures payloads. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Network Activities** = capture, analysis, and correlation of network-based malware artifacts. - **Four-phase lifecycle**: Detection → Identification → Analysis → Response. - **NAI (Network Activity Indicator)** is the foundational concept — every forensic observation is an NAI. - **FPC vs. NetFlow**: FPC = full payload, high storage; NetFlow = metadata only, scalable. - **Beaconing detection** relies on inter-arrival time consistency and **low jitter (J < 0.05)**. - **Shannon Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ is the key metric for detecting encrypted/encoded payloads. - **Bandwidth anomaly ratio** $R_{bw} > 3$ typically flags exfiltration. - **DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm)** produces high-entropy DNS queries — a hallmark of modern malware. - **JA3 hashing** is used for TLS client fingerprinting to detect malware C2 even over HTTPS. - **MITRE ATT&CK** mapping is the industry-standard framework for documenting network-based TTPs. - **Chain-of-custody** mandates SHA-256 hashing and UTC timestamp recording for all captured evidence. - **Common C2 protocols**: HTTP/HTTPS (port 80/443), DNS (port 53), SMB (port 445), ICMP (tunneling). - **Beacons typically occur at fixed intervals**: 30s (TrickBot), 45s (Conti), 60s (Emotet), 90s (Zeus). - **Covert channels** include DNS tunneling, ICMP tunneling, and HTTP header steganography. - **Lateral movement** traffic manifests as SMB, RDP, WMI, and PsExec connections across the internal network. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Mnemonic — "N-A-I-C-B-D":** > **N**etwork capture → **A**nalyze packets → **I**dentify IoCs → **C**orrelate timeline → **B**lock threats → **D**ocument evidence. ---

topics.title: Anti-Dynamic Analysis Techniques - Anti-VM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anti-Dynamic Analysis Techniques - Anti-VM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anti-VM (Anti-Virtual Machine) Techniques** constitute a specialized subset of *anti-dynamic analysis* countermeasures employed by malicious software (malware) to detect the presence of a virtualized execution environment. These techniques enable malware to identify whether it is executing inside a software-emulated sandbox (such as VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Microsoft Hyper-V, Parallels, or QEMU/KVM) rather than on bare-metal hardware.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** taxonomy under *PECCT741 – Malware Forensics*, Anti-VM techniques are classified as **evasion-class defensive mechanisms** that allow a malware specimen to:

1. **Detect** the hypervisor / Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) layer beneath the guest OS.
2. **Discriminate** between a physical host and a virtual guest through observable side-channel artifacts.
3. **React** by terminating, lying dormant, altering its payload, or executing decoy logic.

The formal definition recognized by the KTU syllabus is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-VM Technique:** A class of anti-analysis defenses wherein malware inspects the runtime environment's hardware, firmware, registry, file system, processes, and timing characteristics to infer the presence of a Type-1 (bare-metal) or Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor, with the explicit goal of suppressing its malicious payload when virtualization is detected.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **spy** sneaking into a building (your computer). Before committing the crime, the spy wants to confirm they are **not inside a police training simulation** (a VM/sandbox). The spy checks several clues:

- Are the **door handles** a different brand than normal? (Registry keys, device drivers)
- Does the **echo in the hallway** sound unnatural? (Timing discrepancies)
- Are there **hidden cameras with recognizable models** mounted on walls? (VM-specific processes/services)
- Does the **electricity meter** show a non-standard identifier? (MAC address, SMBIOS strings)
- Are there **footsteps from a different building** leaking through the walls? (Hypervisor port I/O leakage)

If the spy confirms it is a **staged environment** (a VM), the spy **refuses to act** (the malware stays dormant) and quietly walks out. This is precisely how Anti-VM works in malware.

### 1.3 Why Malware Uses Anti-VM

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Motivation:** Modern malware analysts **routinely detonate suspicious binaries inside VMs** to safely observe behavior. By detecting the VM, malware defeats this entire forensic workflow. This is why **96% of advanced malware** (per MITRE ATT&CK T1497 reports) deploys at least one Anti-VM mechanism.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

| KTU Module Outcome | Topic Mapping |
|---|---|
| **CO2:** Analyze dynamic malware behavior | Anti-VM is the **primary countermeasure** against dynamic analysis |
| **CO3:** Apply forensic countermeasures to malware evasion | Identifying Anti-VM signatures is a **core forensic skill** |
| **CO4:** Evaluate sandbox and emulation resistance | Direct alignment with this topic |

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Industry Terminology

- **Hypervisor Detection Rate (HDR):** Percentage of VMs in which a given technique successfully triggers. Standard benchmark: **>85%** is considered reliable.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Incidence of legitimate bare-metal systems misclassified as VMs. Target: **<5%**.
- **Evasion Time (τ):** Average time malware remains undetected in a sandbox. Anti-VM techniques extend τ from **minutes to hours**.
- **Common VM Signatures:** **VMware**, **VirtualBox**, **Hyper-V**, **QEMU/KVM**, **Parallels**, **Xen**, **Sandboxie**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Anti-VM Techniques

Anti-VM mechanisms can be systematically classified into **six** functional families, each targeting a distinct layer of the virtualization stack:

### 2.1.1 Registry & File System Artifact Probing

The malware queries the Windows Registry and file system for **VM-specific strings and paths**. These are the most frequently observed techniques in the wild because they require only API calls (`RegOpenKeyEx`, `GetSystemDirectory`).

**Common Registry Probes:**

| Target VM | Registry Key (HKLM) |
|---|---|
| **VMware** | `SOFTWARE\VMware, Inc.\VMware Tools` |
| **VirtualBox** | `SOFTWARE\Oracle\VirtualBox Guest Additions` |
| **Hyper-V** | `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Virtual Machine\Guest\Parameters` |
| **Parallels** | `SOFTWARE\Parallels\Parallels Tools` |
| **QEMU** | `HARDWARE\DESCRIPTION\System\BIOS\SystemManufacturer \vert= "QEMU"` |

**File System Probes:**

| Target VM | File/Directory |
|---|---|
| **VMware** | `C:\Windows\System32\Drivers\vmmouse.sys`, `vboxguest.sys` |
| **VirtualBox** | `C:\Windows\System32\Drivers\VBoxGuest.sys` |
| **Hyper-V** | `C:\Windows\System32\Drivers\vmbus.sys` |
| **Generic** | `C:\vmware.log`, `C:\sandpit`, `C:\analysis` |

### 2.1.2 Process & Service Enumeration

Malware executes `CreateToolhelp32Snapshot` / `EnumProcesses` (or Linux equivalent `ps`/`/proc` traversal) to identify VM-related background processes.

| VM | Process Names |
|---|---|
| **VMware** | `vmtoolsd.exe`, `vmwaretray.exe`, `vmwareuser.exe` |
| **VirtualBox** | `VBoxService.exe`, `VBoxTray.exe` |
| **Hyper-V** | `vmicheartbeat`, `vmicrdv`, `vmicshutdown` |
| **QEMU Guest** | `qemu-ga.exe` |

### 2.1.3 Hardware & Firmware Fingerprinting

This category exploits the **incomplete emulation of hardware registers and firmware tables** by the hypervisor.

**A. CPUID Instruction Probe:**
The `CPUID` x86 instruction, when executed with `EAX=1`, returns hypervisor presence bit in `ECX[31]`. This is the **gold standard** for hypervisor detection.

**B. SMBIOS / ACPI String Probe:**
The System Management BIOS (SMBIOS) contains manufacturer strings. A bare-metal system typically reports "Dell Inc.", "HP", "Lenovo". A VM reports "VMware, Inc.", "innotek GmbH", "Microsoft Corporation", or "QEMU".

**C. MAC Address OUI Detection:**
The MAC address of the first NIC is assigned by the hypervisor using reserved Organizationally Unique Identifiers (OUIs):

| VM Vendor | OUI Prefix |
|---|---|
| **VMware** | `00:0C:29`, `00:1C:14`, `00:50:56`, `00:05:69` |
| **VirtualBox** | `08:00:27` |
| **Hyper-V** | `00:15:5D` |
| **QEMU/KVM** | `52:54:00` |
| **Xen** | `00:16:3E` |

### 2.1.4 CPU Instruction-Based Side Channels

Certain x86 instructions leak hypervisor-specific information because the VMM does not fully virtualize the underlying control registers:

- **`SIDT` (Store Interrupt Descriptor Table Register):** Returns the IDT base address. VMware historically placed this at `0xFFXXXXXX`.
- **`SLDT` (Store Local Descriptor Table Register):** Returns an LDT selector that is NULL on bare metal.
- **`SGDT` (Store Global Descriptor Table Register):** Returns GDT base; hypervisor offset is detectable.
- **`SMSW` (Store Machine Status Word):** Returns CR0 control bits; can reveal hypervisor emulation patterns.
- **`STR` (Store Task Register):** Returns task register; on bare metal this is zero in user mode.
- **`CPUID` with `EAX=0x40000000`:** Returns hypervisor vendor signature ("VMwareVMware", "Microsoft Hv", "KVMKVMKVM", "TCGTCGTCGTCG").

### 2.1.5 Timing & Resource Anomaly Detection

Virtualized execution introduces **non-deterministic timing overhead** due to instruction emulation, shadow page tables, and trap-and-emulate cycles.

The malware performs a timing-critical loop and compares `Δt_vm` against an expected threshold `Δt_native`. If the ratio exceeds a heuristic constant, the environment is flagged.

### 2.1.6 Communication Port & I/O Backdoor Detection

A handful of x86 I/O ports are reserved by specific hypervisors as **backdoor channels**:

| VM | Backdoor Port (I/O) | Magic Value |
|---|---|---|
| **VMware** | `0x5658` ('VX') | `EAX = 0x564D5868` ('VMXh') |
| **VirtualBox** | `0x5658` via VBoxGuest | `EAX = 0xCAFEBABE` |
| **Hyper-V** | Hypercall via `vmcall` | `RCX = 0x1` |

The malware executes `IN` instruction (privileged) or simulates it via a trap. If the magic value is echoed in `EBX`, `ECX`, `EDX`, the environment is confirmed as virtualized.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **CPUID Hypervisor Bit** | `ECX[31] = 1 ⟹ Hypervisor Present` | Output of `CPUID` with `EAX=1` |
| **CPUID Hypervisor Signature** | `CPUID(EAX=0x40000000)` returns 12-byte vendor string in `EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX` | Known: "KVMKVMKVM\0\0\0", "Microsoft Hv", "VMwareVMware" |
| **Timing Detection Threshold** | $\tau_{ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{observed}}{\Delta t_{expected}} \ge k$ | $k$ = heuristic constant (typically **1.5 to 3.0**) |
| **MAC OUI Match** | `prefix(mac) ∈ {00:0C:29, 08:00:27, 00:15:5D, 52:54:00, 00:16:3E}` | Boolean test, returns 1 if virtualized |
| **SIDT Offset** | `IDT_base & 0xFF000000 == 0xFF000000` | Returns TRUE for legacy VMware |
| **VMware Backdoor Magic** | `EAX = 0x564D5868`, `ECX = 0x0A`, `DX = 0x5658` ⟹ `EBX = 0x564D5868` on VMware | Confirms VMware presence |
| **CPU Core Count Heuristic** | $\text{cores}_{vm} \le 4 \ \wedge\ \text{disk}_{GB} \le 60$ | Typical sandbox constraint signature |
| **SMBIOS String Match** | $\text{contains}(\text{Manufacturer}, \text{VMVendorList})$ | Set membership test |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Anti-VM detection is a **two-sided coin** in cybersecurity:

1. **Offensive Use (Malware Authors):** Evade sandbox detonation, increase analysis cost, force analysts to use bare-metal or stealthier environments.
2. **Defensive Use (Forensic Analysts):** Understand malware evasion strategies to **build better sandboxes** (e.g., Cuckoo Sandbox, CAPA, Hybrid-Analysis) that *mask* VM artifacts, modify SMBIOS strings, and patch CPUID behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application:** The **MITRE ATT&CK Framework** classifies Anti-VM under technique **T1497 (Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion)**, with sub-techniques T1497.001 (System Checks), T1497.002 (User Activity Based Checks), and T1497.003 (Time Based Checks). This is examinable in KTU theory questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: CPUID-Based Hypervisor Detection

The **CPUID instruction** is the most reliable single-instruction Anti-VM probe. The derivation below shows the exact logical flow a malware analyst must follow.

### Step 1 — Invoke CPUID with EAX = 1

The malware executes the following assembly sequence:

```nasm
mov eax, 1
cpuid
; ECX bit 31 (hypervisor present bit) is now set/cleared
test ecx, 0x80000000
jnz hypervisor_detected
```

**Logic Explanation:** When `EAX=1`, CPUID returns feature information in `ECX`. Bit 31 of `ECX` indicates the presence of a hypervisor. On a bare-metal system, this bit is **always 0** because no VMM is present. On any virtualized system (with hardware-assisted virtualization like Intel VT-x or AMD-V), this bit is **1**.

### Step 2 — Invoke CPUID with EAX = 0x40000000

If bit 31 was set, the malware deepens the probe to identify the specific hypervisor:

```nasm
mov eax, 0x40000000
cpuid
; EAX = max supported hypervisor leaf (often 0 on bare metal)
; EBX, ECX, EDX = 12-byte hypervisor vendor string
```

**Logic Explanation:** The hypervisor CPUID leaf range `0x40000000` to `0x4FFFFFFF` is reserved by convention for hypervisor branding. On bare metal, this returns `EAX=0` and zeroed vendor registers.

### Step 3 — Vendor String Decoding

The 12-byte string is constructed by concatenating `EBX`, `ECX`, `EDX` in little-endian order:

$$\text{Vendor}_{string} = \text{bytes}(EBX) \cup \text{bytes}(ECX) \cup \text{bytes}(EDX)$$

**Example mapping (in little-endian byte order):**

$$\text{EBX}=0x4B4D564B \;\rightarrow\; \text{"KVMK"}$$
$$\text{ECX}=0x4D564B4D \;\rightarrow\; \text{"MVMK"}$$
$$\text{EDX}=0x4B4D564B \;\rightarrow\; \text{"KVMK"}$$

Concatenation yields `"KVMKVMKVM\0\0\0"` (padded to 12 bytes).

**Comparison Table of Vendor Strings:**

| Hypervisor | Decoded String |
|---|---|
| **VMware** | `"VMwareVMware"` |
| **Microsoft Hyper-V** | `"Microsoft Hv"` |
| **KVM** | `"KVMKVMKVM\0\0\0"` |
| **QEMU (TCG)** | `"TCGTCGTCGTCG"` |
| **Xen** | `"XenVMMXenVMM"` |
| **VirtualBox** | `"VBoxVBoxVBox"` (newer versions) |
| **Bare Metal** | All zeros (`EAX=0`) |

### Step 4 — Decision Logic

The malware now applies a decision tree:

$$
\text{Action} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Execute Payload} & \text{if } \text{hypervisorBit} = 0 \\
\text{Exit / Sleep / Decoy} & \text{if } \text{hypervisorBit} = 1
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough: VMware Backdoor I/O Port Detection

The VMware backdoor is a **priv-instruction-equivalent** I/O port used by VMware Tools. Malware abuses it to confirm VMware presence.

### Step 1 — Load Magic Values

The malware loads the registers with VMware-specific magic constants:

```nasm
mov eax, 0x564D5868   ; 'VMXh' magic
mov ecx, 0x0A          ; Get VMware version command
mov dx,  0x5658        ; VMware backdoor port 'VX'
in eax, dx             ; Privileged I/O read
; After execution, EBX should contain 'VMXh' on VMware
cmp ebx, 0x564D5868
je vmware_confirmed
```

### Step 2 — Register State Transition

Let us trace the register transitions step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial State:} \quad & EAX = 0x564D5868, \quad ECX = 0x0A, \quad DX = 0x5658 \\
\\
\text{After `in eax, dx` on bare metal:} \quad & EBX = \text{undefined/garbage} \\
\text{After `in eax, dx` on VMware:} \quad & EBX = 0x564D5868 \ \ (\text{confirmed}) \\
\text{After `in eax, dx` on VirtualBox:} \quad & EAX = 0xCAFEBABE \ \ (\text{VBox magic}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Mathematical Representation of Detection Confidence

Let $p_v$ be the probability that a given sample environment is a VM, and $p_f$ be the false positive rate of the technique. The posterior confidence in the detection can be expressed as:

$$
P(\text{VM} \mid \text{Detection}) = \dfrac{p_v \cdot (1 - p_f)}{p_v \cdot (1 - p_f) + (1 - p_v) \cdot p_f}
$$

For a known malware targeting a typical analysis pipeline where $p_v = 0.8$ (most analysts use VMs) and $p_f = 0.02$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{VM} \mid \text{Detection}) &= \dfrac{0.8 \cdot 0.98}{0.8 \cdot 0.98 + 0.2 \cdot 0.02} \\
&= \dfrac{0.784}{0.784 + 0.004} \\
&= \dfrac{0.784}{0.788} \\
&\approx 0.9949 \\
&\approx 99.49\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With a low false positive rate, even a single positive detection is sufficient to flag the environment as virtual with near-certainty.

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: Timing-Based Anti-VM

### Step 1 — Establish Baseline

The malware executes a CPU-bound operation (e.g., 10 million iterations of `NOP` + arithmetic) and measures elapsed time using `RDTSC` (Read Time-Stamp Counter).

```nasm
rdtsc                   ; EAX:EDX = TSC0
mov [t0_lo], eax
mov [t0_hi], edx
; ... perform N iterations of computation ...
rdtsc
mov [t1_lo], eax
mov [t1_hi], edx
```

### Step 2 — Compute Elapsed Cycles

$$
\Delta t = (t1_{hi} \cdot 2^{32} + t1_{lo}) - (t0_{hi} \cdot 2^{32} + t0_{lo})
$$

### Step 3 — Compare to Expected Threshold

The expected native time is calibrated from a known hardware profile:

$$
\tau_{ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{observed}}{\Delta t_{expected}}
$$

If $\tau_{ratio} \ge k$ (heuristic threshold, typically **2.0** for safety), the malware flags the environment.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a sample workload of 10 million iterations takes **1200 RDTSC cycles** on bare metal but **3600 cycles** under VMware. Compute the detection ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{ratio} &= \dfrac{3600}{1200} \\
&= 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $3.0 \ge 2.0$, the malware concludes it is virtualized and enters a sleep loop. **The malicious payload never executes.**

## 3.4 Anti-VM Detection Stack (Analytical Matrix)

| Layer | Technique | Stealth | Reliability | Reverse Engineering Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | MAC OUI check | Low | Medium (60-70%) | Very Easy |
| **L2** | File/Registry artifacts | Low | High (85-90%) | Easy |
| **L3** | Process enumeration | Medium | High (90-95%) | Easy |
| **L4** | CPUID bit 31 | High | Very High (99%+) | Medium |
| **L5** | CPUID vendor string | High | Very High (99%+) | Medium |
| **L6** | SIDT/SGDT/SLDT | High | Medium-High (70-85%) | Hard (kernel mode) |
| **L7** | I/O port backdoor | Very High | Very High (99%+) | Very Hard (privileged) |
| **L8** | Timing (RDTSC) | High | Variable (60-90%) | Hard |
| **L9** | SMBIOS string probe | Medium | High (85-95%) | Medium |
| **L10** | ACPI table parsing | Very High | High (80-90%) | Very Hard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Anti-VM Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Malware Binary Loaded] --> B[Probe Execution Engine]
    B --> C{Layer 1: MAC OUI Probe}
    C -->|Match| Z[VM Detected Flag Set]
    C -->|No Match| D{Layer 2: Registry Probe}
    D -->|Match| Z
    D -->|No Match| E{Layer 3: Process Probe}
    E -->|Match| Z
    E -->|No Match| F{Layer 4: CPUID Bit 31}
    F -->|Bit Set| G{Layer 5: CPUID Vendor String}
    F -->|Bit Clear| Y[Execute Malicious Payload]
    G -->|Known Vendor| Z
    G -->|Unknown String| H{Layer 6: SIDT Offset}
    H -->|Anomalous| Z
    H -->|Normal| I{Layer 7: I/O Port Backdoor}
    I -->|Magic Echo| Z
    I -->|No Echo| J{Layer 8: RDTSC Timing}
    J -->|Ratio Greater Than Threshold| Z
    J -->|Ratio Normal| Y
    Z --> K[Behavior: Sleep or Decoy]
    Y --> L[Behavior: Execute Payload]
```

## 4.2 Hypervisor Stack Showing Anti-VM Probe Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP_LAYER[Application Layer]
        app1[Malware Process]
        app2[Legitimate Process]
    end

    subgraph OS_LAYER[Guest Operating System]
        os1[Windows Linux Kernel]
        os2[Device Drivers]
        os3[Registry and File System]
    end

    subgraph VMM_LAYER[Virtual Machine Monitor]
        vmm1[CPU Emulation Engine]
        vmm2[Shadow Page Tables]
        vmm3[Emulated Hardware SMBIOS ACPI]
        vmm4[I/O Port Handlers]
    end

    subgraph HARD_LAYER[Physical Hardware]
        hw1[CPU Intel AMD]
        hw2[RAM Modules]
        hw3[NIC Card]
    end

    APP_LAYER --> OS_LAYER
    OS_LAYER --> VMM_LAYER
    VMM_LAYER --> HARD_LAYER

    p1[Probe 1: Registry Files] -.-> OS_LAYER
    p2[Probe 2: CPUID Instruction] -.-> VMM_LAYER
    p3[Probe 3: SIDT SLDT SMSW] -.-> VMM_LAYER
    p4[Probe 4: RDTSC Timing] -.-> VMM_LAYER
    p5[Probe 5: I/O Backdoor Port] -.-> VMM_LAYER
    p6[Probe 6: SMBIOS Strings] -.-> VMM_LAYER
    p7[Probe 7: MAC OUI Lookup] -.-> HARD_LAYER
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Multi-Layered Anti-VM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Malware Execution Begins]) --> check1{Check 1: Hypervisor Bit}
    check1 -->|Set| check2{Check 2: Vendor String Known}
    check1 -->|Clear| check3{Check 3: SMBIOS Vendor}
    check2 -->|Yes| flag[VM Confirmed]
    check2 -->|No| check4{Check 4: Registry Keys}
    check3 -->|VM Vendor| flag
    check3 -->|Bare Metal Vendor| check5{Check 5: RDTSC Anomaly}
    check4 -->|Found| flag
    check4 -->|Not Found| check5
    check5 -->|Ratio Greater Than 2| flag
    check5 -->|Ratio Normal| native[Bare Metal Confirmed]
    flag --> sleep[Enter Sleep Loop or Exit]
    native --> payload[Execute Full Payload]
```

## 4.4 Anti-VM Countermeasure Strategy for Forensic Analysts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ana[Forensic Analyst] --> cfg[Configure Stealth VM]
    cfg --> a1[Patch CPUID Hypervisor Bit to 0]
    cfg --> a2[Modify SMBIOS Manufacturer Strings]
    cfg --> a3[Randomize MAC Address to Real Vendor OUI]
    cfg --> a4[Remove VM Tools and Guest Additions]
    cfg --> a5[Adjust RDTSC Offset and Calibration]
    cfg --> a6[Use Bare-Metal Isolated Host]

    a1 --> test[Run Anti-VM Test Suite]
    a2 --> test
    a3 --> test
    a4 --> test
    a5 --> test
    a6 --> test
    test --> score{HDR Score}
    score -->|Greater Than 20 Percent| rework[Re-engineer Stealth Profile]
    score -->|Less Than 5 Percent| deploy[Deploy for Production Analysis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Anti-VM techniques. List any four indicators that malware uses to detect a virtualized environment.**

**Model Answer:**

Anti-VM techniques are a category of anti-dynamic analysis countermeasures in which malware probes the runtime environment to detect whether it is executing inside a virtual machine. Once virtualization is detected, the malware typically refrains from executing its malicious payload to evade forensic sandbox analysis.

Four common indicators are:

1. **CPUID Hypervisor Present Bit (ECX bit 31):** Returns 1 when hardware-assisted virtualization is active.
2. **MAC Address OUI Prefix:** Vendor-specific OUIs like `00:0C:29` (VMware) or `08:00:27` (VirtualBox).
3. **Registry Key Presence:** Keys such as `SOFTWARE\VMware, Inc.\VMware Tools` or `SOFTWARE\Oracle\VirtualBox Guest Additions`.
4. **File System Artifacts:** Driver files like `vmmouse.sys` or `VBoxGuest.sys` in `System32\Drivers`.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for four indicators with brief explanations)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the CPUID instruction in Anti-VM detection with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

The `CPUID` x86 instruction is the most reliable Anti-VM probe. When invoked with `EAX=1`, it returns feature flags in `ECX`, with **bit 31 (hypervisor present bit)** set to 1 if a hypervisor is present. When invoked with `EAX=0x40000000`, it returns a 12-byte vendor string that identifies the specific VMM (e.g., `"VMwareVMware"`, `"KVMKVMKVM\0\0\0"`, `"Microsoft Hv"`). On bare metal, the hypervisor bit is 0 and the vendor string leaves return zeros, making this a high-confidence detection method.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for mechanism + 1.5 for example with vendor string)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **hardware and firmware-based Anti-VM techniques** used by malware. Include CPUID probing, SMBIOS string analysis, MAC OUI detection, and I/O port backdoor detection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **multi-layered Anti-VM detection pipeline** and discuss how a forensic analyst can counter each layer. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

Hardware and firmware-based Anti-VM techniques exploit the fact that a Virtual Machine Monitor cannot perfectly emulate every hardware detail of the underlying physical machine. The major techniques are:

**1. CPUID Hypervisor Bit (ECX bit 31):**
When the malware executes `CPUID` with `EAX=1`, the value returned in `ECX[31]` indicates hypervisor presence. This bit is reserved and is hardware-driven by Intel VT-x / AMD-V. A value of 1 strongly indicates virtualization.

**2. CPUID Vendor String (EAX=0x40000000):**
A second invocation with `EAX=0x40000000` returns a 12-character hypervisor vendor string. Common signatures include:
- `"VMwareVMware"` for VMware
- `"Microsoft Hv"` for Hyper-V
- `"KVMKVMKVM\0\0\0"` for KVM
- `"TCGTCGTCGTCG"` for QEMU TCG mode

On bare metal, `EAX` returns 0 and the other registers are zeroed.

**3. SMBIOS String Analysis:**
The System Management BIOS exposes manufacturer strings. Malware reads these via `GetSystemFirmwareTable` API and compares them against known VM vendors (`VMware, Inc.`, `innotek GmbH`, `Microsoft Corporation`, `QEMU`).

**4. MAC Address OUI Detection:**
Hypervisors assign NIC MAC addresses using reserved Organizationally Unique Identifiers. Malware retrieves the MAC via `GetAdaptersInfo` and checks the first 3 octets against known VM OUIs:
- `00:0C:29`, `00:1C:14`, `00:50:56`, `00:05:69` for VMware
- `08:00:27` for VirtualBox
- `00:15:5D` for Hyper-V
- `52:54:00` for QEMU/KVM
- `00:16:3E` for Xen

**5. I/O Port Backdoor Detection (VMware-specific):**
VMware exposes a backdoor port at I/O address `0x5658`. The malware loads `EAX=0x564D5868` (VMXh magic), `ECX=0x0A`, and executes `IN EAX, DX`. If `EBX` returns `0x564D5868`, the environment is confirmed as VMware.

**6. SIDT/SGDT/SLDT Side-Channel Probes:**
The Interrupt Descriptor Table Register, GDT Register, and LDT Register return non-standard base addresses in virtualized environments. Legacy VMware, for example, places the IDT at `0xFFXXXXXX`, an address range that is illegal on bare metal.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

The **multi-layered Anti-VM detection pipeline** operates as a sequential series of probes, each adding a higher confidence level. A typical pipeline includes:

- **Layer 1: Cheap Probes (Registry, Files, Processes)** — quick to execute, easy to detect
- **Layer 2: Hardware Probes (CPUID)** — high reliability
- **Layer 3: Timing Probes (RDTSC)** — variable reliability
- **Layer 4: Privileged Probes (SIDT, I/O Ports)** — hardest to mask

**Counter-Strategies for Analysts:**

1. **Patch CPUID:** Use tools like `cpuid spoofers` or modify the VMM's `CPUID` handler to return 0 for bit 31 and vendor string.
2. **Modify SMBIOS:** Set manufacturer strings to genuine vendors like "Dell Inc." or "HP".
3. **Randomize MAC Address:** Use a real vendor OUI (e.g., `00:1A:2B` for a network card) instead of the default VMware prefix.
4. **Uninstall Guest Additions:** Remove VMware Tools, VBoxGuestAdditions, and integration services.
5. **RDTSC Offset Compensation:** Apply a TSC offset that makes virtual cycles match expected native cycles.
6. **Bare-Metal Analysis:** When stealth is critical, use an air-gapped bare-metal host.

A comprehensive block diagram is provided in SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain **timing-based Anti-VM detection** using RDTSC. Derive the threshold formula and discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **six major families of Anti-VM techniques** in a tabular format. Discuss which family is hardest to counter from a forensic analyst's perspective. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**RDTSC (Read Time-Stamp Counter) Anti-VM Technique:**

The RDTSC instruction reads a 64-bit timestamp counter that increments on every CPU clock cycle. In a virtualized environment, the VMM may:

- Trap and emulate the RDTSC instruction (causing delay)
- Run the guest on a non-native TSC (causing TSC drift)
- Share the TSC across multiple virtual CPUs (causing anomalies)

**Mechanism:**
1. Read TSC0 before a CPU-bound operation
2. Execute a known workload (e.g., 10 million iterations)
3. Read TSC1 after the operation
4. Compute $\Delta t = TSC1 - TSC0$
5. Compare to expected native value

**Threshold Formula:**

$$
\tau_{ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{observed}}{\Delta t_{expected}} \ge k
$$

where $k$ is a heuristic constant (typically 1.5 to 3.0). If the ratio exceeds $k$, the environment is flagged as virtual.

**Worked Example:**
If native $\Delta t = 1000$ cycles and observed $\Delta t = 3000$ cycles:
- $\tau_{ratio} = 3000 / 1000 = 3.0$
- Since $3.0 \ge 2.0$, the environment is classified as virtual.

**Limitations:**
1. **TSC Drift:** Modern CPUs with invariant TSC (constant-rate TSC) make the counter reliable, reducing the timing gap.
2. **TSC Offsetting:** VMMs like VMware apply a per-vCPU TSC offset to make virtual TSC match native, defeating the probe.
3. **CPU Frequency Scaling:** Turbo Boost, SpeedStep, and thermal throttling cause $\Delta t$ to vary by up to 30% even on bare metal, raising false positives.
4. **Multi-core Migration:** If the workload migrates between cores, the TSC delta may be inflated artificially.
5. **Resource Contention:** A heavily loaded host VM host causes the guest workload to run slower, mimicking VM behavior.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Comparison Table of Anti-VM Families:**

| Family | Detection Vector | Stealth | Reliability | Counter Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **File/Registry Artifacts** | Files, registry keys | Low | 85-90% | Easy (just delete artifacts) |
| **Process Enumeration** | Running services | Low | 90-95% | Easy (kill or rename processes) |
| **Hardware Fingerprinting** | CPUID, SMBIOS, MAC | High | 95-99% | Medium (requires VMM patching) |
| **CPU Instruction Side-Channels** | SIDT, SLDT, SMSW, STR | High | 70-85% | Hard (requires VMM-level trap handling) |
| **Timing Anomalies** | RDTSC drift, I/O delays | High | 60-90% | Very Hard (requires TSC offsetting) |
| **I/O Port Backdoor Probes** | VMware/VBox magic ports | Very High | 99%+ | Very Hard (requires VMM modification) |

**Hardest to Counter:**

From a forensic analyst's perspective, **I/O Port Backdoor Probes** and **CPU Instruction Side-Channels (SIDT/SGDT/SLDT)** are the hardest to counter because:

1. They require modifications to the **VMM hypervisor source code** (e.g., VMware's `vmware-vmx.exe` or VirtualBox's source tree).
2. They operate in **privileged execution context** (Ring 0), making user-space patching ineffective.
3. They cannot be neutralized by simply uninstalling guest additions or renaming files.
4. The only reliable counter is to use **bare-metal analysis** or **modified hypervisors** built specifically for stealth analysis (e.g., the now-discontinued Bromium, or open-source forks like QEMU with custom patches).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Anti-VM with Anti-Debug:** Anti-VM detects virtual machines; Anti-Debug detects debuggers (OllyDbg, x64dbg). They are **distinct** techniques — do not interchange.
> 2. **Confusing SIDT with IDT:** SIDT is the *instruction*; IDT is the *Interrupt Descriptor Table* (the structure). Examiners deduct marks for misuse.
> 3. **Forgetting the Magic Constant:** The VMware backdoor magic is `0x564D5868` ("VMXh"). Writing `0x564D` (only) loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Omitting Mitigations:** KTU theory questions **require** both the attack technique and the corresponding countermeasure. Always state how an analyst would bypass the detection.
> 5. **Ignoring MITRE ATT&CK Mapping:** Referencing T1497 sub-techniques adds significant value to long answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Anti-VM Techniques**

- ✅ **Definition:** Anti-VM is a sub-class of anti-dynamic analysis that detects the presence of a virtual machine to evade sandbox-based forensic analysis.
- ✅ **Primary Detection Vectors:** Registry keys, file system artifacts, process names, CPUID instructions, SMBIOS strings, MAC OUI, I/O ports, RDTSC timing.
- ✅ **CPUID Bit 31 of ECX (with EAX=1):** The most reliable single-instruction hypervisor detection. Set = VM present, Cleared = bare metal.
- ✅ **CPUID EAX=0x40000000:** Returns 12-byte hypervisor vendor string. Common values: `"VMwareVMware"`, `"KVMKVMKVM"`, `"Microsoft Hv"`, `"TCGTCGTCGTCG"`.
- ✅ **VMware Backdoor Magic:** `EAX=0x564D5868`, `ECX=0x0A`, `DX=0x5658`. Echo of `0x564D5868` in EBX confirms VMware.
- ✅ **VirtualBox Backdoor Magic:** `EAX=0xCAFEBABE` echo in EAX.
- ✅ **MAC OUI Prefixes to Remember:** `00:0C:29` (VMware), `08:00:27` (VirtualBox), `00:15:5D` (Hyper-V), `52:54:00` (QEMU), `00:16:3E` (Xen).
- ✅ **Timing Detection Formula:** $\tau_{ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{observed}}{\Delta t_{expected}} \ge k$ where $k \approx 1.5$ to $3.0$.
- ✅ **SIDT Anomaly:** Legacy VMware IDT base sits at `0xFFXXXXXX` — an illegal address on bare metal.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK Mapping:** Anti-VM maps to **T1497** with sub-techniques T1497.001 (System Checks), T1497.002 (User Activity), T1497.003 (Time Based).
- ✅ **Forensic Countermeasures:** Patch CPUID, modify SMBIOS, randomize MAC, remove guest additions, use TSC offsetting, use bare-metal for high-stakes analysis.
- ✅ **Stealth Hierarchy:** I/O Port Backdoor > CPUID Probing > File/Registry Probes in terms of difficulty to mask.
- ✅ **Industry Tools:** Cuckoo Sandbox, CAPA, Hybrid-Analysis, and VirusTotal use Anti-VM-resistant analysis pipelines.
- ✅ **Hybrid-Analysis Note:** Many sandboxes run malware with **delayed execution** (sleep-skipping) to bypass time-based Anti-VM techniques.
- ✅ **Distinguishing Term:** "Hypervisor" = the VMM software; "Virtual Machine" = the guest OS instance. They are **not** the same concept.
- ✅ **Examiner's Hint:** Always pair each detection technique with its corresponding **forensic countermeasure** in long-answer questions to maximize marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runtime Evasion Techniques
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Runtime Evasion Techniques

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> **Runtime Evasion Techniques** are a class of sophisticated adversarial methods employed by modern malware to actively avoid detection, analysis, and reverse-engineering while the malicious code is executing in a live computing environment. These techniques manipulate the **runtime execution context**, **system memory**, **operating system internals**, and **hardware/software artefacts** so that antivirus engines, Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) systems, sandboxes, and forensic analysts cannot reliably observe, capture, or reconstruct malicious behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT741 – Module 2):** Runtime Evasion is positioned as the *operational* counterpart to static evasion. While static evasion hides code *on disk*, runtime evasion hides code *in execution*. The examiner expects students to differentiate between **static obfuscation** (packing, encryption) and **dynamic/runtime evasion** (injection, hooking, anti-analysis).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a thief who has broken into a museum at night.

- **Static evasion** is like wearing a perfect invisibility cloak while *walking past the security cameras* — the recording shows nothing.
- **Runtime evasion** is the thief's *live behaviour inside the museum*: he disables motion sensors, swaps his face with a wax mask of a janitor on the internal monitors, hides inside a delivery cart (process hollowing), and refuses to move when a guard shines a flashlight (anti-debugging). The cameras *are* recording — but the thief is actively rewriting what "live" means inside the building.

In computing terms, the malware is already running; the goal is to keep the **forensic investigator (or the AV/EDR product)** blind to what it is doing.

### Core Categories at a Glance
| Category | Primary Target | Typical Examples |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Memory-based Evasion** | Process address space | DLL Injection, Process Hollowing, Reflective DLL Loading |
