| **Execution-flow Hijack** | API call resolution | IAT Hooking, Inline Hooking, SSDT Hooking |
| **Anti-Analysis** | Debuggers, VMs, sandboxes | `IsDebuggerPresent`, `RDTSC` timing, CPUID checks |
| **Persistence + Stealth** | Kernel / boot | Kernel-mode rootkits, bootkits |
| **Environmental Evasion** | Sandbox artefacts | Mouse movement checks, recent-file-count checks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric in Forensics — Entropy (H):** A key analytical tool used to *quantify* the "randomness" of a memory region that has been packed/injected. Values are reported in **bits per byte** on a scale of **0 – 8**. Run-time injections of encrypted/encoded payloads typically exhibit entropy **≥ 7.2**, while a benign `.text` section sits around **5.0 – 6.0**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Runtime Evasion Pipeline (Operational Logic)
Modern evasive malware follows a deterministic, layered execution pipeline. The forensic investigator must understand *each gate* to identify the technique used.

1. **Initial Trigger Gate** — A dropper or loader executes. The malware first performs **environmental reconnaissance** to ensure it is not inside a sandbox/VM/debugger.
2. **Persistence Gate** — If the environment is "clean," the malware establishes a foothold (registry run-key, scheduled task, service).
3. **Injection Gate** — The malicious payload is moved into another legitimate process's address space to hide under its identity.
4. **Hooking Gate** — Critical OS APIs are intercepted so that when the OS or an AV queries them, the response is forged.
5. **Anti-Analysis Gate** — Continuous runtime checks (timing, integrity, debugger artefacts) are polled to detect the analyst.
6. **Command & Control (C2) Gate** — Outbound communication begins, often tunneled or delayed to defeat sandbox timeouts.

### 2.2 Memory Injection — The Three Pillars

#### 2.2.1 Classic DLL Injection
- The malware uses `OpenProcess` → `VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `CreateRemoteThread` to force a target process to load a malicious DLL.
- **Forensic indicator:** The remote thread's start address points to `LoadLibraryA` rather than legitimate code, and the source DLL is on disk (visible to static scanners).

#### 2.2.2 Process Hollowing (a.k.a. RunPE)
- The malware spawns a *legitimate* process in the **suspended** state (`CREATE_SUSPENDED`).
- It uses `NtUnmapViewOfSection` to unmap the legitimate image, then `VirtualAllocEx` + `WriteProcessMemory` to plant its own PE image in the hollowed cavity.
- The thread is resumed with `ResumeThread`, and the process now *is* a legitimate name (e.g., `svchost.exe`) but contains attacker code.
- **Forensic indicator:** Mismatch between the **on-disk** PE image and the **in-memory** PE image (different entry-point, different section hashes).

#### 2.2.3 Reflective DLL Injection
- The malicious DLL is **never written to disk**. It is loaded manually by walking the PE structure inside memory and calling its own `ReflectiveLoader` function.
- **Forensic indicator:** A `LOAD_DLL_DEBUG_EVENT` is *absent*, and the loaded module does not appear in the standard PEB (Process Environment Block) `InMemoryOrderModuleList`.

### 2.3 API Hooking Variants (Why and How)

| Hooking Type | What Is Patched | Detection Difficulty | Stealth Level |
|--------------|-----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **IAT Hooking** | Import Address Table entries of a loaded module | Low — table is at a fixed, known offset | Low |
| **Inline Hooking** | First bytes (a `JMP` trampoline) of the API function itself | Medium — requires disassembly of ntdll/kernel32 | High |
| **SSDT Hooking** | System Service Descriptor Table in kernel | High — needs kernel driver or EPT | Very High (rootkit-level) |
| **IRP Hooking** | Driver I/O Request Packets | Very High | Very High |
| **Syscall Hooking (direct)** | Replaces the `syscall` instruction in ntdll stubs | Extremely High (user-mode AV cannot see) | Extreme |

### 2.4 Anti-Debugging / Anti-VM (Anti-Analysis)
The malware continuously probes the runtime for signs of an analyst. Common Windows APIs and their meaning:

- `IsDebuggerPresent` — checks the `BeingDebugged` flag in the PEB.
- `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent` — checks if *another* process is debugging.
- `NtQueryInformationProcess` with class 7 (`ProcessDebugPort`) — returns the debug port if attached.
- `RDTSC` — reads the Time Stamp Counter; if delta is unexpectedly large, a debugger has likely single-stepped.
- `CPUID` with `EAX=1` — returns bit 31 of `ECX` (`hypervisor present` bit) when running under a hypervisor.
- `GetTickCount`, `QueryPerformanceCounter` — sandbox time-bomb evasion: malware sleeps > sandbox-timeout.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> **Table Note:** Absolute value and conditional checks use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` operators to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Purpose in Forensics |
|---|-------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Shannon Entropy** of a memory region | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_{2} p(x_i)$ | Quantifies randomness of injected payloads (encrypted blobs $\to$ $H \geq 7.2$) |
| 2 | Entropy Bounds (per byte) | $0 \leq H \leq 8$ | 0 = perfectly uniform, 8 = perfectly random (compressed/encrypted) |
| 3 | RDTSC Delta Threshold | $\Delta t = \vert \text{RDTSC}_{t_{1}} - \text{RDTSC}_{t_{0}} \vert$ | If $\Delta t > \tau_{\text{debug}}$ ⇒ debugger single-step detected |
| 4 | Suspicious Region Indicator | $S = \begin{cases} 1, & H \geq H_{\text{thresh}} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Flag injected/encrypted regions in memory forensics |
| 5 | PEB `BeingDebugged` offset | `PEB+0x02` on 32-bit / `PEB+0x02` on 64-bit | Direct read of debug flag without API call |
| 6 | Hooked-Byte Trampoline Size | $L_{\text{jmp}} = \begin{cases} 5 \text{ bytes} & \text{(32-bit)} \\ 12 \text{ to } 14 \text{ bytes} & \text{(64-bit)} \end{cases}$ | Length overwritten on first instruction of an API to redirect flow |
| 7 | Virtual-to-Physical Translation | $\text{Phys} = \text{PFN} \times 2^{12} + \text{Offset}$ | Used in kernel forensics to trace injection through page tables |
| 8 | Process Hollowing Hash Mismatch | $\text{Match} = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } H_{\text{disk}} = H_{\text{mem}} \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Mismatch $\Rightarrow$ hollowed process |

> **Real-World Utility:** In a live incident response engagement, entropy ($H$) of an unknown `VirtualAllocEx` region is the **fastest first-pass indicator** of an injected payload, and the **RDTSC threshold check** is mirrored by modern EDR products (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne) as a *userland hook telemetry* signature.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Shannon Entropy of an Injected Region
Forensic examiners compute entropy over a memory window (commonly **256 bytes** or **4096 bytes**) to determine whether the bytes look like code, ASCII, or encrypted/compressed injection.

**Step 1 — Define the random variable.**
Let $X$ be the byte value at any position. There are $n = 256$ possible byte values (0x00 to 0xFF).

**Step 2 — Compute the empirical probability.**
For each byte value $i$, count the number of occurrences $c_i$ in the window of size $N$. The empirical probability is:

$$p(x_i) = \frac{c_i}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Shannon formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \cdot \log_{2} p(x_i) \\
     &= -\sum_{i=0}^{255} \left(\frac{c_i}{N}\right) \cdot \log_{2}\left(\frac{c_i}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical worked example (8-byte sample, N = 8).**
Suppose the window contains: `0x90 0x90 0x90 0x90 0xCC 0xCC 0xEB 0xFE`
Counts: $c_{0x90}=4$, $c_{0xCC}=2$, $c_{0xEB}=1$, $c_{0xFE}=1$, all others $=0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\left[\frac{4}{8}\log_2\frac{4}{8} + \frac{2}{8}\log_2\frac{2}{8} + \frac{1}{8}\log_2\frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{8}\log_2\frac{1}{8}\right] \\
     &= -\left[0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.25 \cdot (-2) + 0.125 \cdot (-3) + 0.125 \cdot (-3)\right] \\
     &= -\left[-0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375\right] \\
     &= 1.75 \text{ bits/byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Forensic interpretation:** A value of $1.75$ is *low* — this looks like legitimate x86 code (NOP sled + INT3 + JMP), **not** an encrypted injection. An encrypted injection (e.g., AES-CBC output) typically yields $H \approx 7.99$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How to *Identify* Each Technique During Live Forensics

| Technique | Live Memory Artefact | Static / Disk Artefact | Volatility Tool That Detects It |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| DLL Injection | `CreateRemoteThread` start address = `LoadLibraryA` | Malicious DLL on disk | `volatility` → `malfind`, `dlllist` |
| Process Hollowing | Section mappings differ from on-disk PE | Legitimate binary on disk (clean) | `volatility` → `pslist` vs `psext` (PID reuse, hollowed name) |
| Reflective DLL Injection | Module not in PEB list | **Nothing on disk** | `malfind` (RWX private pages with PE headers) |
| IAT Hook | Entry points point outside the module's `.text` | Clean on disk | `volatility` → `apihooks` |
| Inline Hook | First 5/14 bytes of ntdll = `JMP <addr>` | Clean on disk | `volatility` → `apihooks`, manual disassembly |
| SSDT Hook | Service index points outside `ntoskrnl.exe` | Driver on disk | `volatility` → `ssdt` plugin |
| Anti-Debug (RDTSC) | High TSC deltas | Conditional jump to clean exit | Dynamic instrumentation (Frida, x64dbg) |
| Sandbox Evasion (Sleep) | `Sleep(300000)` in disassembly | Lazy dropper | Run in bare-metal sandbox, or patch sleep |

### 3.3 Worked Walkthrough — Detecting Process Hollowing in Memory

**Step 1 — Acquire the memory image** (e.g., using `winpmem`, Magnet RAM Capture).

**Step 2 — Identify target process.** Suppose PID `4128` is named `svchost.exe`.

**Step 3 — Run `pslist`** to enumerate the EPROCESS list and record the `ActiveProcessLinks` of PID 4128. Run `psext` (or `psxview`) to cross-validate. If the process is missing from `psscan` (pool-tag scan) or its module list is empty ⇒ **hollow candidate**.

**Step 4 — Dump memory and compute hashes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{disk}} &= \text{SHA256}(\text{C}\colon\text{Windows\System32\svchost.exe}) \\
H_{\text{mem}}  &= \text{SHA256}(\text{memory dump of PID 4128 image region}) \\
\text{Match}   &= (H_{\text{disk}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{mem}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
If $H_{\text{disk}} \neq H_{\text{mem}}$, the process is **hollowed**, and the difference is the injected malicious PE.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Runtime Evasion — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Layer 1 - Environmental Recon] --> B[Layer 2 - Persistence]
    B --> C[Layer 3 - Memory Injection]
    C --> D[Layer 4 - API Hooking]
    D --> E[Layer 5 - Anti Analysis Loop]
    E --> F[Layer 6 - Command and Control]

    A1[IsDebuggerPresent] --> A
    A2[RDTSC timing] --> A
    A3[CPUID hypervisor bit] --> A
    A4[Recent file count] --> A

    B1[Registry run key] --> B
    B2[Scheduled task] --> B
    B3[Service install] --> B

    C1[DLL Injection] --> C
    C2[Process Hollowing] --> C
    C3[Reflective DLL] --> C
    C4[APC Injection] --> C

    D1[IAT Hook] --> D
    D2[Inline Hook] --> D
    D3[SSDT Hook] --> D
    D4[Syscall Hook] --> D

    E1[Debug port check] --> E
    E2[Integrity hash check] --> E
    E3[Parent process check] --> E

    F --> F1[Domain fronting]
    F --> F2[Tor hidden service]
    F --> F3[DNS tunneling]
```

### 4.2 Process Hollowing — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 - CreateProcess in SUSPENDED state] --> S2[Step 2 - Read malicious PE from disk or memory]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 - NtUnmapViewOfSection to hollow the target]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 - VirtualAllocEx new RWX region in target]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 - WriteProcessMemory copies malicious PE sections]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 - SetThreadContext updates entry point to malicious AddressOfEntryPoint]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 - ResumeThread executes malicious code under legitimate process name]
```

### 4.3 IAT vs Inline Hooking — Side-by-Side Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEGIT[Legitimate API Call Path]
        L1[Caller] --> L2[IAT Entry Points to real KERNEL32]
        L2 --> L3[Real VirtualAlloc in kernel32.dll text section]
    end

    subgraph IH[Inline Hook Variant]
        I1[Caller] --> I2[Real VirtualAlloc function head]
        I2 --> I3[First 5 bytes overwritten with JMP trampoline]
        I3 --> I4[Malicious hook handler]
        I4 --> I5[Optional call to real VirtualAlloc]
    end

    subgraph IAT[IAT Hook Variant]
        IA1[Caller] --> IA2[IAT Entry now points to attacker address]
        IA2 --> IA3[Malicious handler injected in remote module]
        IA3 --> IA4[Optional call to real VirtualAlloc]
    end
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Distinguish between *static obfuscation* and *runtime evasion* in malware. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Static obfuscation** modifies the *on-disk representation* of the malware so that signature-based AV cannot recognise it. Example: a packer (e.g., UPX) compresses the payload, or a polymorphic engine mutates the byte sequence at rest.
- **Runtime evasion** modifies the *behaviour during execution* so that even when the AV is looking at running code, the malware hides. Example: Process Hollowing, where a legitimate process is created in suspended state, hollowed, and filled with malicious code that runs under the legitimate process name. The on-disk file is *clean* — only the in-memory image is malicious.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** What is the *forensic value* of computing Shannon Entropy over a suspicious memory region? Mention the typical entropy band that indicates an encrypted injection.

**Model Answer:**
Shannon Entropy $H(X)$ quantifies the randomness of bytes in a memory window. Benign code/ASCII strings cluster in the range $H \approx 3$–$6$ bits/byte. Encrypted or compressed payloads (typical of injected shellcode or reflective DLLs) approach the theoretical maximum of $H \approx 7.99$ bits/byte, signalling a high-entropy injected region. In forensic practice, $H \geq 7.2$ is the standard threshold for flagging a region as a likely encrypted/injected payload during live triage or memory dump analysis.

---

### Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Part B Format (KTU 2024):** Answer **any one** full question. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Memory Injection Suite**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain **Process Hollowing** as a runtime evasion technique. List the sequence of Windows APIs invoked and explain why it defeats basic AV scanning. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
Process Hollowing is a stealth technique in which the malware spawns a legitimate, digitally signed process in the *suspended* state, then surgically replaces its image in memory with a malicious PE, and finally resumes the thread. The on-disk binary remains clean and is a known good file (e.g., `svchost.exe`), so signature-based AV sees nothing suspicious on disk.

**API sequence (chronological):**

1. `CreateProcessA("svchost.exe", ..., CREATE_SUSPENDED, ...)` — launches the legitimate process in suspended state.
2. `NtUnmapViewOfSection(hProcess, pImageBase)` — un-maps the original PE image from the target's address space, leaving a "hollow."
3. `VirtualAllocEx(hProcess, maliciousBase, imageSize, MEM_COMMIT|MEM_RESERVE, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE)` — carves a fresh RWX cavity.
4. `WriteProcessMemory(hProcess, maliciousBase, &maliciousPE, sizeof(sections), NULL)` — copies each malicious PE section (`.text`, `.data`, `.reloc`) into the cavity.
5. `SetThreadContext(hThread, &ctx)` with `ctx.Eax/ctx.Rcx = AddressOfEntryPoint` — redirects the thread's first instruction to the malicious entry point.
6. `ResumeThread(hThread)` — execution begins under the legitimate process name.

**Why it defeats AV:** The on-disk artefact is the *real* signed binary. Static AV cannot find malicious bytes. Memory-based AV must perform a **PE image hash mismatch** check between disk and memory — many legacy AVs do not.

**Diagram (text representation):**
```
Legit svchost.exe  →  CreateProcess(SUSPENDED)  →  NtUnmapViewOfSection
                                                      ↓
                        WriteProcessMemory( malicious PE )
                                                      ↓
                        SetThreadContext → ResumeThread
                                                      ↓
                  Process is now named "svchost.exe" but runs malicious code
```

**(b)** Explain the difference between **classic DLL Injection** and **Reflective DLL Injection**. Why is reflective injection significantly harder to detect? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Classic DLL Injection | Reflective DLL Injection |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| On-disk artefact? | **Yes** — the malicious DLL is dropped to disk | **No** — DLL lives only in memory |
| Loading mechanism | `LoadLibraryA` invoked in remote process | Custom `ReflectiveLoader` walks PE manually |
| PEB appearance | Appears in `InMemoryOrderModuleList` | **Absent** from PEB module list |
| `LOAD_DLL_DEBUG_EVENT` | Fired by the OS loader | Not fired — manual load |
| Forensic detection | Easy — `dlllist` plugin finds it | Hard — requires scanning all RWX private memory pages for PE headers (`malfind`) |

**Why reflective injection is stealthier:** The OS loader is never asked to map the file; the malware implements its own PE loader in user-mode that parses the in-memory PE, resolves imports, fixes relocations, and jumps to `DllMain`. Because no `LoadLibrary` is called, no loader lock is taken, no debug event is fired, and the module never enters the standard enumeration lists. Detection requires memory scanning for **PE signatures (`MZ` followed by valid `PE\0\0` header)** inside anonymous, RWX memory regions — a much more expensive and heuristic search.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hooking & Anti-Analysis**

**(a)** Compare **IAT Hooking** and **Inline Hooking** in terms of *what is modified*, *stealth level*, and *forensic detection method*. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**IAT (Import Address Table) Hooking:**
- *What is modified:* The IAT entry of an imported function inside a specific module. The actual API code in `kernel32.dll`/`ntdll.dll` is untouched.
- *Stealth level:* Low. The IAT lives at a predictable offset, and a parser can read it and verify that each entry points inside the expected module's address range.
- *Detection:* `volatility apihooks` plugin, or static analysis of the IAT — entries that point outside the bounds of the module's `.text`/`.data` sections are flagged.

**Inline Hooking:**
- *What is modified:* The first few bytes of the *actual* function prologue in `ntdll.dll` (or other core DLL) in memory. Typically a 5-byte (x86) or 12-byte (x64) `JMP <hook>` trampoline is written, redirecting every caller — including those that resolved the address at runtime via `GetProcAddress`.
- *Stealth level:* High. To detect, the forensic tool must disassemble the prologue of every imported function and check if the first instruction is the expected `MOV EDI, EDI` / `MOV R10, RCX` (the documented ntdll stubs). Bypassing this is what tools like **Dumpert** and **SyscallWhispers** do.
- *Detection:* `apihooks` plugin (Volatility) plus manual disassembly; behavioural detection via EDR that uses kernel-level callbacks.

**Summary:**
- IAT hooks are easier to perform *and* easier to detect (table-based).
- Inline hooks require a write into read-only memory (achieved via `VirtualProtect`) and are stealthier, but the byte-level tampering leaves an artefact detectable by disassembly.

**(b)** Describe **three runtime anti-debugging checks** used by malware. For each, state *what the check probes* and *how a forensic analyst can defeat it*. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **`IsDebuggerPresent` (PEB.BeingDebugged)**
   - *Probes:* The `BeingDebugged` byte at `PEB+0x02`, set by the OS loader to 1 when a debugger attaches.
   - *Defeat:* Patch the byte in memory *before* malware checks it; or use a plugin (e.g., ScyllaHide in x64dbg) that auto-zeroes the flag.

2. **`NtQueryInformationProcess` with `ProcessDebugPort` (class 7)**
   - *Probes:* Queries the kernel for the value of the debug port. A non-zero value means a debugger is attached.
   - *Defeat:* Hook the `NtQueryInformationProcess` syscall to return 0 for class 7; or run inside a hypervisor that suppresses the debug port.

3. **`RDTSC` Timing Check**
   - *Probes:* Reads the Time Stamp Counter before and after a piece of code. If the delta $\Delta t = \vert t_1 - t_0 \vert$ exceeds an empirical threshold $\tau_{\text{debug}}$ (typically a few thousand cycles for a tight loop), the malware concludes a debugger has single-stepped through it and exits.
   - *Defeat:* Patch the `RDTSC` instruction to return a constant value, or use a hardware emulator (QEMU/unicorn) that lets the analyst control TSC emulation, or hook the instruction at the hypervisor level.

> A *fourth* commonly tested check is the **`CPUID` hypervisor-present bit** — bit 31 of `ECX` when `EAX=1` is set by most hypervisors. The defeat is to use bare-metal execution or a hypervisor that explicitly clears this bit (e.g., KVM with custom CPUID masks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing packing with runtime injection.** Packing is *static*; process hollowing / reflective DLL are *runtime*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the student uses "packing" as a runtime example.
> 2. **Forgetting the `ntdll` syscall layer.** Inline hooks against user-mode ntdll are now routinely bypassed by malware that directly invokes `syscall` via its own stub — this is a frequent short-note question in KTU 2024.
> 3. **Not mentioning `malfind` / `apihooks` (Volatility plugins).** Any memory-injection question that lacks a forensic-detection answer mentioning these tools is marked incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the entropy value band.** When asked how to *identify* an encrypted injection, students often say "check if it's encrypted" without quoting the **7.2 – 7.99** threshold band. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Writing the syscall number when asked for the API.** KTU expects *Windows API names* (`OpenProcess`, `VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `CreateRemoteThread`) and their **order of invocation**, not Linux syscall numbers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Runtime evasion ≠ static obfuscation.** Static hides *on disk*; runtime hides *in execution*. Always specify *which* in your answer.
- **Three canonical injection techniques:** Classic DLL Injection, Process Hollowing, Reflective DLL Injection — know the *API sequence* for each.
- **Three canonical hooking techniques:** IAT, Inline, SSDT — know *what is patched* and *how to detect* each.
- **Anti-debugging toolkit:** `IsDebuggerPresent`, `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess` (ProcessDebugPort), `RDTSC`, `CPUID` hypervisor bit.
- **Forensic first-pass metric:** Compute **Shannon Entropy** $H(X)$ over suspicious memory windows. **Threshold $H \geq 7.2$** ⇒ likely encrypted/injected payload. **Max = 8** (perfectly random).
- **Process Hollowing tells:** Hash mismatch between on-disk PE and in-memory PE; legitimate process name with anomalous module list (`psxview` mismatch in Volatility).
- **Reflective DLL tells:** PE headers inside **anonymous, RWX, non-mapped** memory pages (caught by `malfind`); module *absent* from PEB `InMemoryOrderModuleList`.
- **IAT Hook tells:** IAT entries point outside the parent module's address range.
- **Inline Hook tells:** First 5/12/14 bytes of an API prologue are a `JMP`/`MOV` trampoline — detected by `apihooks` or disassembly.
- **SSDT Hook tells:** Service index in kernel resolves to an address outside `ntoskrnl.exe` — detected by `ssdt` plugin (Volatility).
- **Sandbox evasion tells:** Long `Sleep()` calls, recent-file-count checks, mouse-movement probes, RDTSC deltas.
- **Volume Shadow / kernel artefact:** A userland EDR *cannot* see a direct-syscall injection that bypasses `ntdll` — only a **kernel callback driver** (ETW, minifilter, kernel notify routine) can.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Every runtime evasion answer should pair the **technique → API/artefact → detection method → Volatility plugin**. A complete answer is *always* 4-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Malware Sandbox
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Malware Sandbox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Malware Sandbox** is an isolated, tightly controlled virtual execution environment that mimics a real end-user operating system and its associated network resources, designed specifically to safely detonate, observe, and analyze the runtime behaviour of suspicious binaries, documents, scripts, URLs, and other potentially malicious artefacts without exposing the host system, the production network, or the analyst to compromise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A sandbox is a *behavioural detonation chamber* — a confined compute substrate where malicious code is intentionally executed so that its **API calls, file-system mutations, registry edits, network traffic, persistence mechanisms, and process injections** can be recorded and reverse-engineered without any lateral consequence to genuine assets.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the sandbox belongs to the **Dynamic Malware Analysis** family of techniques and is treated as the *gold standard* for generating high-fidelity Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) such as mutex names, C2 (Command and Control) domains/IPs, dropped file hashes, registry Run keys, scheduled task names, and process hollowing signatures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bio-Safety Level 4 (BSL-4) Laboratory**
> Imagine you are a virologist who has just received a sealed test-tube containing an unknown, potentially lethal pathogen. You **cannot** open it in your regular office because a single droplet could infect the city. Instead, you place it inside a **hermetically sealed, negative-pressure containment chamber** with thick glass walls, robotic manipulators, airlocks, and an array of sensors. The pathogen is *allowed to do whatever it wants* inside the chamber — replicate, attack cells, release toxins — but it physically *cannot* escape. Sensors record everything: temperature changes, chemical signatures, cell-death patterns. Later, you analyse the recordings to build an antidote.

A **Malware Sandbox is the cybersecurity equivalent of that BSL-4 lab:**

| BSL-4 Lab Element | Malware Sandbox Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sealed containment chamber | Hypervisor-isolated VM / container |
| Robotic manipulators (gloved box) | API hooking & syscall interception |
| Sensors & cameras | Behaviour monitors (file, registry, network, process) |
| Airlocks / decontamination shower | Snapshot revert / network black-hole |
| Pathogen report (forensic dossier) | Automated IOC report & MITRE ATT&CK mapping |

## 1.3 Why Sandboxes Are Indispensable — The Core Engineering Problem

Modern malware is **heavily obfuscated** through packing, polymorphism, metamorphism, and encrypted payloads. Pure static analysis (disassembly, string extraction, signature matching) frequently fails because the malicious payload is never visible without first executing it. The sandbox solves this by **forcing execution under surveillance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics Used in Sandbox Evaluation (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Detection Rate (DR):** Percentage of malicious samples correctly flagged as malicious. Typical industry target: **> 95%**.
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR):** Percentage of benign samples wrongly flagged. Target: **< 1%**.
> - **Evasion Resistance:** Ability to detect sandbox-aware malware that uses VM-detection, sleep timers, or human-interaction checks.
> - **Time-to-Verdict (TtV):** Average analysis duration. Typical: **60 s – 10 min**.

## 1.4 Classification of Sandboxes (KTU Syllabus-Aligned)

Sandboxes are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Implementation Substrate**
   - **Full Virtualization (Type-I/Type-II Hypervisor):** VMware Workstation, VirtualBox, KVM — strongest isolation.
   - **OS-level Virtualization / Containers:** Cuckoo Sandbox's `cuckoo` Linux guest, Docker-based sandboxes — lighter weight.
   - **Emulation-based:** QEMU, Bochs, TEMU — instrumentation at the CPU-emulation layer.
   - **Bare-metal / Barebox:** Detux, Limon, sandox-on-Windows — runs directly on hardware to defeat VM-aware malware.

2. **By Deployment Topology**
   - **On-premise / Standalone:** Air-gapped analyst workstation.
   - **Cloud-hosted / SaaS:** ANY.RUN, Joe Sandbox Cloud, Hybrid Analysis, CrowdStrike Falcon Sandbox, Microsoft Defender, Tria.ge.
   - **Hybrid:** Local pre-filter + cloud deep-analysis.

3. **By Interactivity Model**
   - **Non-interactive (headless):** Fully automated, no human input — fast but trivially defeated by user-input checks.
   - **Interactive:** Human analyst clicks menus, types into dialogs to mimic real user behaviour.
   - **Replay-based / Recorded-Interaction:** Real user session is recorded and replayed inside the sandbox.

4. **By Target Artefact Type**
   - **Document sandboxes:** Specialised in PDF, Office (macro/VBA), RTF, LNK.
   - **URL sandboxes:** Browser-driven (e.g., URLscan.io, PhishTank-style).
   - **Mobile sandboxes:** Android emulators (e.g., DroidBox, MobSF).
   - **ICS/IoT sandboxes:** Custom firmware emulation (e.g., ICSREF, Avatar).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Blueprint of a Modern Malware Sandbox

A production-grade sandbox is a **multi-layered instrumentation pipeline**. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames it as having five logical tiers:

### Tier 1 — Sample Ingestion & Triage Layer
- Accepts uploads via REST API, email gateway, EDR forwarding, or SIEM webhook.
- Performs **pre-filtering**: hash lookup against threat-intel feeds (VirusTotal, MISP, AlienVault OTX).
- Performs **file-type magic-byte verification** (e.g., a `.txt` extension with `MZ` header is flagged).

### Tier 2 — Execution Environment (The Guest)
- A pristine VM/Container with a *real* OS image (Windows 10/11, Ubuntu 22.04, Android 13, etc.).
- Includes *intentional* victim artefacts: PDF reader, Office suite, Java runtime, browser with Flash/Java plugins, dummy documents on Desktop, browser history, cookies, recent files.
- **Snapshot-and-Restore:** VM is reverted to a clean golden image after each run to guarantee determinism.

### Tier 3 — Instrumentation & Hooking Layer (The "Sensors")
This is the *heart* of the sandbox. Multiple sensors are wired into the guest:

- **API Hooking (User-mode):** Intercepts Win32 API calls (`CreateFile`, `RegSetValueEx`, `InternetOpenUrlA`, `CreateProcess`, `VirtualAllocEx`).
- **Syscall Tracing (Kernel-mode):** Uses ETW (Event Tracing for Windows), minifilters, or kernel callbacks to capture ring-0 activity.
- **File System Monitor:** Watches file creation, modification, deletion in `C:\Users\`, `C:\Windows\Temp`, `%APPDATA%`.
- **Registry Monitor:** Watches `HKLM\...\Run`, `HKCU\...\Run`, services, scheduled tasks.
- **Process Monitor:** Tracks parent-child process trees, DLL injection, handle operations.
- **Network Monitor:** Captures PCAP of all outbound traffic, DNS queries, HTTP/HTTPS requests, IRC pings.

### Tier 4 — Scoring & Detection Engine
- Translates raw behavioural events into **MITRE ATT&CK® technique IDs** (e.g., T1055 = Process Injection).
- Applies heuristic rules, ML classifiers, or signature-based IOC matching.
- Generates a **maliciousness score** between 0.0 (benign) and 1.0 (definitely malicious).

### Tier 5 — Reporting & IOC Export Layer
- Produces JSON/STIX 2.1 reports, annotated screenshots, MITRE heat-maps.
- Pushes IoCs to SIEM, EDR, SOAR, YARA repositories.

## 2.2 The Core Operational Principle — *Why* It Works

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Theorem of Sandboxing:** *Any meaningful malware must, at some point during its lifecycle, perform observable side-effects on the host or the network.* The sandbox simply makes these side-effects *visible* by placing the execution inside an instrumented panopticon.

This theorem has an inverse — the **Sandbox Evasion Principle** — which states: *Malware that can detect the sandbox before performing side-effects will remain dormant and thus evade detection*. This cat-and-mouse dynamic is the central research theme of modern sandbox engineering.

## 2.3 Detailed Anti-Sandbox Techniques (KTU-Favourite Theory Topic)

Modern malware authors deploy an arsenal of evasion tactics:

| Evasion Category | Specific Technique | Detection Counter |
|---|---|---|
| **VM Detection** | Checks `HKLM\SOFTWARE\VMware`, `vmwaretools.exe`, MAC OUI `00:0C:29` | Bare-metal sandbox (Detux), MAC spoofing |
| **Sleep / Stall** | `Sleep(300000)` (5 min) to outlast the analysis window | Time acceleration (`timewarp`), hook `Sleep`/`NtDelayExecution` |
| **User Interaction Check** | Waits for double-click, mouse movement, keypress | Synthetic input replay (cuckoomon + Replay) |
| **Sandbox Process Check** | Looks for `vmtoolsd.exe`, `vboxservice.exe`, `cuckoomon.dll` | Custom guest images with renamed processes |
| **CPU/RAM Fingerprinting** | Checks low CPU cores (1-2) or low RAM (1-2 GB) | Allocate realistic resources (4+ cores, 8+ GB) |
| **Registry/Filesystem Probes** | Looks for `C:\agent`, recent Office docs, browser history | Pre-seed the sandbox with realistic artefacts |
| **Network C2 Check** | Only phones home if real internet is reachable | Use a transparent proxy + INetSim |
| **Code Integrity Check** | Detects API hooks via `IsDebuggerPresent`, `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent` | Hypervisor-level introspection (VMI), CPU emulation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all** the key sandbox metrics, formulas, and engineering constants you must memorise for the KTU 2024 ESE.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Rate (DR)** | $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless, **0% – 100%** |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | dimensionless, ideally **< 1%** |
| **True Negative Rate (TNR)** | $TNR = 1 - FPR = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | dimensionless, **0% – 100%** |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | dimensionless |
| **Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | dimensionless |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Recall}{PPV + Recall}$ | dimensionless, **0 – 1** |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | dimensionless |
| **Matthews Correlation Coeff.** | $MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | **−1 to +1** |
| **True Positive (TP)** | Malicious sample correctly flagged | count |
| **True Negative (TN)** | Benign sample correctly cleared | count |
| **False Positive (FP)** | Benign sample wrongly flagged | count |
| **False Negative (FN)** | Malicious sample missed | count |
| **Sample Throughput** | $\lambda = \frac{N_{samples}}{T_{window}}$ | samples / hour |
| **Mean Time to Verdict** | $\overline{TtV} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i$ | seconds, typical **60 – 600 s** |
| **Evasion Resistance Score** | $ERS = 1 - \frac{N_{evaded}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless, target **> 0.9** |
| **Hook Coverage** | $HC = \frac{\vert H_{instrumented} \vert}{\vert H_{total} \vert}$ | dimensionless |
| **Snapshot Size** | $S_{snap} = S_{disk} + S_{mem}$ | GB, typical **20 – 40 GB** |
| **Golden Image Reset Time** | $t_{reset}$ | seconds, target **< 10 s** |

> **Notation Guard:** All absolute-value delimiters are rendered as `\vert` to protect markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sandboxes are deployed in **five high-value production roles**:

1. **Incident Response Triage:** SOC analysts upload suspect attachments from phishing emails for a 60-second verdict before opening them on production machines.
2. **Threat Intelligence Generation:** Automated pipelines (e.g., the Cuckoo-based CAPE sandbox) detonate millions of samples per month to harvest new IoCs.
3. **Security Product Validation:** Vendors use sandboxes to test anti-virus efficacy, red-team tooling, and EDR behavioural rules.
4. **Software Supply-Chain Auditing:** Third-party binaries are detonated before deployment to detect hidden backdoors.
5. **Academic & Forensic Research:** Sandbox traces feed datasets used to train next-generation ML-based malware classifiers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Sandbox Analysis Workflow — Exhaustive Stepwise Trace

The complete deterministic workflow from sample ingestion to IOC export is described below. Every transition is enumerated for KTU ESE 14-mark answers.

### Step 1 — Sample Submission
The analyst (or an upstream EDR/SIEM system) submits the suspicious artefact. The sandbox front-end accepts the binary via a secure upload channel (HTTPS POST, multipart form-data). The artefact is stored in a **quarantine repository** with its original file-system metadata, MIME type, and submission context (email sender, URL source, process tree origin).

### Step 2 — Pre-Execution Static Triage
Before detonation, the sandbox performs cheap, fast checks:
- Hash computation: **MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256** of the file.
- Magic-byte identification using `libmagic` or `TrID`.
- String extraction via `strings` for suspicious markers (URLs, IP literals, API names).
- Packer detection via entropy analysis: $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i$.
- Hash lookup against threat-intel databases.

> An entropy value **> 7.2** on a 4 KB sliding window is a strong indicator of packing or encryption.

### Step 3 — Environment Preparation (Snapshot Restoration)
The sandbox engine loads the **golden image** for the appropriate guest OS. The image is pre-configured with realistic artefacts: a populated browser history, recent Office documents, dummy user credentials, common productivity software. The image is restored from a snapshot to guarantee a *known-good* baseline state — no residual state from previous detonations.

### Step 4 — Artefact Delivery & Triggering
The sample is delivered into the guest via a *realistic* channel — for example, a phishing email simulation, a browser download, or a USB autorun event. The sandbox then issues a *trigger* action: a double-click, an open command, or `cmd.exe /c <sample>`.

### Step 5 — Behavioural Capture (The Core Loop)
For a configured observation window $T_{obs}$ (typically 60 – 600 seconds), the instrumentation layer records every observable event:

$$
E = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots, e_n\}, \quad \text{where } e_i = \langle t_i, \, \text{type}_i, \, \text{actor}_i, \, \text{target}_i, \, \text{metadata}_i \rangle
$$

Each event $e_i$ is a tuple containing the timestamp $t_i$, the event category (file, registry, process, network), the source process, the target object, and rich metadata (full path, registry value, payload bytes).

### Step 6 — Periodic Screenshots & Memory Dumps
At configurable intervals (e.g., every 5 seconds), the sandbox captures:
- A full-screen bitmap of the guest desktop.
- A memory snapshot of key processes (used for memory-only malware, e.g., fileless/PowerShell).

### Step 7 — Cleanup & Snapshot Revert
After the observation window expires, all network connections are forcibly terminated, the guest is powered off, and the virtual disk is reverted to the pristine golden image. Any analysis artefacts (PCAPs, memory dumps, screenshots, JSON logs) are exported to the reporting subsystem.

### Step 8 — Scoring & IOC Extraction
The captured events $E$ are fed into the scoring engine, which:

1. Matches each event against a library of **YARA rules** (signature-based).
2. Applies heuristic rules (e.g., "Process A writes to `HKLM\...\Run` AND spawns `cmd.exe` ⇒ T1547.001 Boot/Logon Autostart Execution").
3. Optionally applies an **ML classifier** (Random Forest, XGBoost, or Deep Neural Network) trained on millions of historical traces.

The maliciousness score is computed as:

$$
S_{mal} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot m_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}, \quad w_i \in [0,1], \quad m_i \in \{0,1\}
$$

where $w_i$ is the analyst-assigned weight of rule $i$ and $m_i$ is the binary match flag.

### Step 9 — Report Generation
A human-readable report is produced containing:
- Sample metadata (hashes, file type, size).
- Verdict: **Malicious / Suspicious / Clean**.
- Maliciousness score $S_{mal}$.
- MITRE ATT&CK heatmap.
- Full event timeline.
- Network IoCs (domains, IPs, URLs).
- Host IoCs (file paths, registry keys, mutexes).
- Screenshots and memory dump references.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sandbox Comparison (KTU ESE Favourite)

| Feature | **Cuckoo Sandbox** | **Joe Sandbox** | **CAPE** | **Detux** | **Hybrid Analysis** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open Source | ✅ Yes | ❌ Commercial | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Free tier |
| Architecture | Host-Controller + Guest Agent | Host + Guest | Cuckoo fork | Bare-metal Linux | Cloud SaaS |
| Anti-Evasion | Moderate | High (Anti-VM tricks) | High | N/A (Linux) | High |
| Report Format | JSON / HTML | JSON / PDF | JSON / HTML | JSON | JSON / Web |
| MITRE Mapping | Plugin | Native | Native | Manual | Native |
| Internet Simulation | INetSim | Built-in | INetSim | None | Real (with care) |
| Sample Throughput | Medium | High | Medium | Low | High |
| OS Support | Win/Linux/Mac/Android | Win/Linux/Android | Win | Linux only | Win/Android |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Detection Rate Calculation)

> **Problem:** A sandbox was tested on a corpus of **1,000** samples: **800** malicious and **200** benign. After analysis, the engine flagged **760** malicious samples as malicious and **180** benign samples as benign, but also wrongly flagged **20** benign samples as malicious while missing **40** malicious samples.

> **Step 1 — Build the Confusion Matrix:**

$$
TP = 760, \quad FN = 800 - 760 = 40, \quad TN = 180, \quad FP = 20
$$

> **Step 2 — Compute Detection Rate (Recall):**

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{760}{760 + 40} = \frac{760}{800} = 0.95
$$

> **Step 3 — Compute False Positive Rate:**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 180} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10
$$

> **Step 4 — Compute Precision:**

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{760}{760 + 20} = \frac{760}{780} \approx 0.974
$$

> **Step 5 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Recall}{PPV + Recall} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.974 \times 0.95}{0.974 + 0.95} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9253}{1.924} \approx 0.962
$$

> **Step 6 — Compute Accuracy:**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{760 + 180}{1000} = \frac{940}{1000} = 0.94
$$

> **Step 7 — Interpret:** The sandbox has a strong detection rate of **95%**, but a high FPR of **10%**, indicating the heuristic rules are too aggressive. In KTU valuation, this trade-off discussion typically earns full marks for the 7-mark sub-question on detection metrics.

## 3.4 Evasion Probability Model (Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Advanced Theory (KTU Higher-Order Thinking Skill — HOTS):** Consider a sandbox with observation window $T_{obs}$ and a malware sample that uses a sleep-based evasion of duration $T_{sleep}$. The probability of evasion is:

$$
P_{evade} = \begin{cases} 1, & T_{sleep} \ge T_{obs} \\ 0, & T_{sleep} < T_{obs} \wedge \text{hook-invalidated} \\ \frac{T_{sleep}}{T_{obs}}, & \text{otherwise (unhooked)} \end{cases}
$$

When the sandbox **hooks** the `Sleep` API to artificially reduce the wait to $t_{s}' = \min(t_{sleep}, \tau)$ where $\tau$ is a small constant (e.g., 1 s), the evasion probability collapses to approximately **0** because the malware wakes up immediately and performs its payload within the observation window. This is why **time-warp hooks are a critical defensive feature**.

## 3.5 The Threat-Coverage Bound Theorem

For a sandbox with hook coverage $HC \in [0,1]$, the **maximum achievable detection rate** is bounded by:

$$
DR_{max} \le HC \cdot \alpha + (1 - HC) \cdot \beta
$$

where $\alpha$ is the probability of detection when the relevant API is hooked, and $\beta$ is the probability when it is unhooked. Setting $HC = 1$ yields the *ideal* upper bound $DR_{max} = \alpha$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Sandbox Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Submission Layer<br/>REST API Email SIEM EDR] --> B[Triage and Static Filter<br/>Hash Lookup Magic Bytes Strings]

    C -- No --> D[Environment Provisioner<br/>Load Golden Snapshot]
    D --> E[Artefact Delivery<br/>Email Browser USB Autorun]

    G --> H[Instrumentation Sensors<br/>API Hooks Syscall Trace FS Mon Reg Mon Proc Mon Net Mon]
    H --> I[Event Stream Buffer<br/>Time Ordered Log]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *"What is a malware sandbox? List any three behavioural indicators a sandbox typically captures."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A malware sandbox is an isolated, instrumented virtual environment used to safely execute and observe suspicious files/URLs without risking the host system or production network. The captured behavioural indicators include:

1. **File-system mutations** — files created, modified, or deleted in user-writable directories (e.g., `%APPDATA%`, `C:\Windows\Temp`).
2. **Registry persistence modifications** — Run keys, services, scheduled tasks (e.g., `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`).
3. **Outbound network traffic** — DNS queries, HTTP POST to C2 servers, IRC joins, Tor circuit establishment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for the definition and 1 mark each for the three distinct indicators.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *"Differentiate between static analysis and sandbox-based dynamic analysis."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Analysis | Sandbox-Based Dynamic Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Execution required | **No** — code is dissected without running it | **Yes** — sample is detonated inside a VM |
| Packer resistance | **Weak** — packed/encrypted payloads are opaque | **Strong** — payload decrypts itself at runtime |
| Speed | **Very fast** (milliseconds) | **Slower** (60 – 600 seconds per sample) |
| Evasion risk | **Low** | **High** — VM-aware malware can evade |
| Output | Strings, opcodes, control-flow graph | Behavioural timeline, PCAP, memory dump |

> Award 1 mark per correct row (any 3 rows earn 3 marks).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice means the student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B *in full*. Both choices are presented below for examiner reference.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Workflow

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
> *(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical malware sandbox.* **(7 Marks)**
> *(b) Describe the step-by-step workflow of detonating a sample inside the sandbox, from ingestion to report generation.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Architecture

A modern malware sandbox consists of **five cooperating tiers**:

1. **Ingestion Tier** — accepts samples via REST API, SMTP forwarding, EDR integration.
2. **Triage Tier** — performs hash computation, magic-byte identification, entropy analysis, threat-intel lookup.
3. **Execution Tier** — provisions an isolated guest VM (Windows/Linux/Android) from a pristine golden image with realistic victim artefacts (browser history, recent documents).
4. **Instrumentation Tier** — installs hooks on critical APIs (`CreateFile`, `RegSetValueEx`, `InternetOpenUrlA`, `CreateProcess`, `VirtualAllocEx`); kernel-mode sensors capture syscalls, file-system events, registry mutations, process trees, network traffic.
5. **Scoring & Reporting Tier** — applies YARA rules, heuristic scoring, ML classifiers; maps events to MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs; exports IoCs in STIX 2.1 and JSON.

(Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** for visual support — include this diagram in your answer sheet.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Workflow

The end-to-end workflow executes the nine-step pipeline detailed in **Section 3.1** above. Briefly recap:

1. Sample submission → quarantine.
2. Pre-execution static triage (hash, strings, entropy, packer detection).
3. Snapshot restoration of guest VM.
4. Artefact delivery via realistic channel (email/browser/USB).
5. Trigger execution (double-click / ShellExecute).
6. Behavioural capture for window $T_{obs}$.
7. Periodic screenshot + memory dumps.
8. Cleanup, power-off, snapshot revert.
9. Scoring, IOC extraction, MITRE mapping, JSON/PDF report.

Award 1 mark per correctly identified step, with 2 marks reserved for the verdict logic and IOC export integration.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Anti-Sandbox Evasion & Counter-Measures

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *(a) Enumerate and briefly explain any four anti-sandbox evasion techniques used by modern malware.* **(7 Marks)**
> *(b) Discuss the counter-measures the sandbox engineer must deploy to defeat each of these techniques.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Evasion Techniques

1. **VM Detection** — Malware enumerates registry keys (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\VMware`), MAC OUIs (`00:0C:29` for VMware), and running processes (`vmtoolsd.exe`, `vboxservice.exe`). On detection, it aborts its payload.
2. **Sleep / Stall Evasion** — Calls `Sleep(600000)` (10 minutes) to outlast the sandbox's observation window. Since most cloud sandboxes time-out at 60 – 300 s, the malware stays dormant.
3. **User-Interaction Check** — Polls the message queue (`GetMessage`, `GetAsyncKeyState`) for genuine mouse/keyboard events before activating. A headless sandbox produces no such events.
4. **Process and File Probes** — Checks for the presence of `cuckoomon.dll`, `agent.py`, `sbmonitor.log`, or `C:\agent` directories — all tell-tale signs of a Cuckoo-style sandbox.

*(Award 1.75 marks per technique: 1 mark for naming and 0.75 mark for explanation.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Counter-Measures

1. **Bare-metal / QEMU-evaded analysis** — Use Detux, Limon, or Avatar to run samples directly on hardware or via full-system emulation that masks QEMU fingerprints.
2. **Time-Warp Hooks** — Hook `Sleep`, `NtDelayExecution`, `WaitForSingleObject` to translate $T_{sleep}$ to a small constant $\tau$ (e.g., 1 s). The malware wakes up instantly and triggers its payload.
3. **Synthetic Input Replay** — Cuckoo's `replay.py` replays recorded mouse clicks, keystrokes, and dialog interactions to satisfy user-interaction checks.
4. **Realistic Environment Seeding** — Pre-populate the golden image with browser history, recent Office docs, dummy credentials, and rename sandbox processes (e.g., rename `cuckoomon.dll` to `wbemprox.dll`).

*(Award 1.75 marks per counter-measure.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on Sandbox questions:**
> 1. **Conflating sandbox with antivirus** — a sandbox is *not* a signature-based AV; it is a *behavioural observation engine*. Lose 1 mark if you claim otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting snapshot revert** — the deterministic *reset-to-golden-image* property is a hallmark of proper sandbox design; omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the "why" of API hooks** — merely listing `CreateFile` without explaining *why* it matters (file drop / persistence) is incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing hook coverage with detection rate** — they are mathematically distinct; see the Threat-Coverage Bound Theorem in Section 3.5.
> 5. **Omitting anti-evasion techniques** — KTU 2024 explicitly tests "anti-sandbox awareness"; students who mention only the basic architecture lose 2 – 3 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring units in numerical answers** — always state units (samples/hour, seconds, dimensionless) where applicable.
> 7. **No diagram** — for 7-mark architecture questions, a labelled block diagram is **mandatory**. Without it, expect a 1 – 2 mark penalty.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Sandbox = isolated behavioural detonation chamber with API hooks, snapshot-restore, and IOC export.
- ✅ **Primary purpose:** Defeat packing/obfuscation by forcing the malware to decrypt and execute.
- ✅ **Five tiers:** Ingestion → Triage → Execution → Instrumentation → Scoring/Reporting.
- ✅ **Key instruments:** API hooks, syscall tracers, FS/Registry/Process/Network monitors.
- ✅ **Snapshot revert:** Mandatory for determinism.
- ✅ **Four orthogonal classifications:** implementation substrate, deployment topology, interactivity model, target artefact.
- ✅ **Anti-evasion techniques:** VM detection, sleep stalls, user-interaction probes, sandbox process checks, network probing, entropy/timing checks.
- ✅ **Counter-measures:** Bare-metal sandbox, time-warp hooks, synthetic input replay, realistic seeding, hypervisor-level introspection, INetSim.
- ✅ **Detection Rate formula:** $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.
- ✅ **FPR formula:** $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$.
- ✅ **F1-Score formula:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Recall}{PPV + Recall}$.
- ✅ **Hook Coverage formula:** $HC = \frac{\vert H_{instrumented} \vert}{\vert H_{total} \vert}$.
- ✅ **Evasion probability under sleep:** $P_{evade} = \frac{T_{sleep}}{T_{obs}}$ when unhooked; $\approx 0$ when time-warp applied.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK mapping** is now a *required* output of modern sandboxes.
- ✅ **Industry sandboxes:** Cuckoo, CAPE, Joe Sandbox, Hybrid Analysis, ANY.RUN, Detux.
- ✅ **Time-to-Verdict** typically **60 – 600 s**; longer windows increase evasion resistance but reduce throughput.
- ✅ **Cloud sandboxes** (SaaS) offer scale; **on-premise** offers data sovereignty.
- ✅ **Evasion Resistance Score:** $ERS = 1 - \frac{N_{evaded}}{N_{total}}$, target **> 0.9**.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:** *Architecture → Instrumentation → Anti-Evasion → Metrics → Reporting. Master these five pillars and no KTU 2024 sandbox question can surprise you.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monitoring with Process Monitor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monitoring with Process Monitor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Monitor (ProcMon)** is an advanced, real-time monitoring utility for the Microsoft Windows operating system, originally developed by **Mark Russinovich** and **Bryce Cogswell** as part of the **Sysinternals Suite** (now owned by Microsoft). It is a unified investigative tool that combines the capabilities of two legacy utilities — **FileMon** (file system activity monitor) and **RegMon** (Windows Registry activity monitor) — while extending real-time surveillance to **Process/Thread activity**, **Network (TCP/UDP) activity**, and **Profiler events**.

In the context of **Malware Forensics**, Process Monitor is the de-facto standard tool for **dynamic behavioural analysis**. It captures every low-level kernel event generated by running processes and presents them in a chronologically ordered, filterable, and exportable log — enabling an investigator to reconstruct the precise runtime footprint (file drops, registry persistence keys, mutex creation, network C2 callbacks, DLL injection, process hollowing, etc.) of a suspected malicious binary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT741 / Module 2):**
> *Process Monitor is a Windows Sysinternals tool that provides real-time monitoring of file system, Registry, process, thread, and network activity. It is used in malware forensics to observe the live behavioural footprint of suspect code without relying on signature-based detection.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **highly secured bank vault** that has four separate observation rooms:

1. **Vault Door Watcher** — logs every time a locker is opened or closed (File System).
2. **Guest Register Watcher** — logs every entry written in the visitor's register (Registry).
3. **Guard Patrol Watcher** — logs every time a guard enters, exits, takes a break, or is replaced (Process/Thread).
4. **Telephone Exchange Watcher** — logs every internal and external phone call placed from the vault (Network).

**Process Monitor is the "control room console"** that aggregates the feeds from all four watchers into a single, time-stamped, colour-coded logbook. A forensic investigator does not have to be in four places at once — ProcMon does the watching and records the data for later, repeatable analysis.

For a malware sample, this means: instead of guessing *what the malware does*, you **let the malware run in a sandboxed lab machine and watch everything it touches**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** ProcMon works at the **kernel level** by registering with the **I/O Manager** and other Windows kernel components. It therefore sees every system call, not just user-mode API calls — making it far more powerful than user-mode API hooks used by many AV products.

## 1.3 Salient Features at a Glance

- **Capture of 4 event classes:** File System, Registry, Process/Thread, Network.
- **Non-destructive capture** — events are intercepted, not modified.
- **Powerful filter engine** — Boolean filter expressions on PID, path, operation, result, etc.
- **Boot Logging** — captures activity from the moment Windows boots.
- **Dropped-events indicator** — warns when the kernel buffer is overrun.
- **Export to PML, CSV, XML, EVTX** for archival and offline analysis.
- **Process tree visualisation** — shows parent-child relationships.
- **Stack tracing** — captures the call stack for any single event (with symbols).
- **Bookmarks and highlighting** — for collaborative forensic casework.

---

## 1.4 Position in the Malware Forensics Workflow

```
[Suspect Binary]
      │
      ▼
[Isolated Lab VM / Sandbox]
      │
      ▼
[ProcMon + ProcExp + Regshot + Wireshark + etc.]
      │
      ▼
[Captured Event Log (PML)]
      │
      ▼
[IOC Extraction & Timeline Construction]
      │
      ▼
[Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) for Detection Engineering]
```

ProcMon is a **first-line dynamic analysis tool** that complements static analysis (PE parsing, disassembly) and memory forensics (Volatility).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Metric:** A typical Windows 10 boot in default ProcMon capture mode generates between **3,000,000 and 8,000,000 events** in roughly 90 seconds. Forensic analysts therefore *never* capture without a filter — they design filters *first*, then run the sample.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of Process Monitor

Process Monitor has a **two-tier architecture** — a user-mode GUI front-end and a kernel-mode driver:

| Component | Layer | Function |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| **procmon.exe** | User-mode (GUI) | Renders event stream, applies filters, manages capture session |
| **PROCMON24.sys** | Kernel-mode driver | Hooks the Windows I/O Manager and other kernel callbacks to intercept system calls |
| **Event Buffer (Kernel)** | Kernel ring buffer | Circular buffer where intercepted events are queued |
| **Filter Engine** | User-mode | Evaluates Boolean filter expressions against incoming events |
| **Symbol Engine (DBGHELP.DLL)** | User-mode | Resolves raw addresses to function names via PDB symbols |
| **Persistence Files** | User-mode | `PML`, `PMC`, `PMS` format log files |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the kernel driver is mandatory:** Many malware techniques (e.g., direct kernel object manipulation, raw disk writes) operate *below* the user-mode API layer. A pure user-mode tool would be blind to them. ProcMon's kernel driver intercepts events at the **I/O Request Packet (IRP)** level, making such low-level activity visible.

## 2.2 The Four Event Classes (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 File System Events
- **Operations captured:** `CreateFile`, `ReadFile`, `WriteFile`, `DeleteFile`, `RenameFile`, `SetRenameInformationFile`, `QueryInformation`, `FSCTL`, `SetEndOfFile`, `CloseFile`.
- **What it tells the investigator:** Where the malware drops payloads, modifies host files, accesses autorun locations (`%APPDATA%`, `%TEMP%`, `Startup` folder), or stages data for exfiltration.

### 2.2.2 Registry Events
- **Operations captured:** `RegCreateKey`, `RegOpenKey`, `RegSetValue`, `RegQueryValue`, `RegDeleteValue`, `RegCloseKey`.
- **What it tells the investigator:** Persistence mechanisms — `Run`, `RunOnce`, `Image File Execution Options`, `Winlogon\Shell`, `AppInit_DLLs`, `LSA Security Packages`, `Services`.

### 2.2.3 Process and Thread Events
- **Operations captured:** `Process Start`, `Process End`, `Thread Create`, `Thread Exit`, `Load Image` (DLL load), `Unload Image`.
- **What it tells the investigator:** Process injection (DLL load into a foreign process), process hollowing (image load of the legitimate target), child process spawning (`cmd.exe`, `powershell.exe`, `wmic.exe`, `rundll32.exe`).

### 2.2.4 Network Events
- **Operations captured:** `TCP Connect`, `TCP Disconnect`, `TCP Send`, `TCP Receive`, `UDP Send`, `UDP Receive`.
- **What it tells the investigator:** C2 server contact, beaconing interval, exfiltration destinations, port and protocol used. **Note:** does not capture packet contents — Wireshark is used for payload analysis.

## 2.3 The Filter Subsystem — The Most Critical Forensic Skill

Filters are the **single most important feature** for forensic use. A capture without filters is unusable noise.

**Filter expression structure:**

```
<Field> <Relation> <Value> [Boolean <Field> <Relation> <Value>] ...
```

**Supported fields include:** `Process Name`, `PID`, `Operation`, `Path`, `Result`, `Detail`, `Category`, `Time of Day`, `User`, `Image Path`, `Company`, `Description`, `Command Line`, `Relative Time`, `Duration`, `Event Class`.

**Supported relations include:** `is`, `is not`, `contains`, `excludes`, `begins with`, `ends with`, `less than`, `more than`, `equals`.

> [!TIP]
> **Gold-Standard Forensics Filter Set (memorise this for KTU exams):**
>
> 1. `Process Name is suspect.exe` — to isolate only the suspect process.
> 2. `Operation is CreateFile` — to see all file creations.
> 3. `Operation is RegSetValue` — to see all registry value writes.
> 4. `Operation is TCP Connect` — to see network callbacks.
> 5. `Path contains \AppData\` — to look for `%APPDATA%` writes (a classic malware staging location).
> 6. `Path contains \CurrentVersion\Run` — to detect Run-key persistence.

## 2.4 Boot Logging — Capturing Pre-User Activity

Some advanced malware (rootkits, bootkits) load **before** the user logs on or before GUI tools start. ProcMon's **Boot Logging** feature loads the kernel driver at boot and begins capturing immediately. The resulting log is opened in the GUI *after* Windows finishes booting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Enabling Boot Logging and then opening ProcMon *before* the boot log file is fully flushed. The correct procedure is: enable boot log → reboot → Windows loads → *do not open ProcMon yet* → wait for full boot → *then* open ProcMon to convert/load the boot log.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Format | Unit / Type | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Event class categories | $\mathcal{C} = \{F, R, P, N\}$ | Set (F=File, R=Reg, P=Process, N=Network) | Identify which class a captured event belongs to |
| Filter complexity (informal) | $F = (f_1 \land f_2) \lor \lnot f_3$ | Boolean expression | Construct and reason about filters |
| Capture duration | $T_{\text{cap}}$ | seconds | Time span of capture |
| Event throughput | $\lambda = \frac{N}{T_{\text{cap}}}$ | events per second | Estimate log size: $S \approx \lambda \cdot T_{\text{cap}} \cdot \bar{s}$ |
| Mean event size | $\bar{s}$ | bytes/event | Used to size the kernel buffer |
| Estimated log size | $S \approx \lambda \cdot T_{\text{cap}} \cdot \bar{s}$ | bytes | Decide whether to pre-filter or export |
| Dropped-event count | $D$ | integer | Forensic completeness indicator |
| Capture completeness ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{captured}}}{N_{\text{captured}} + D}$ | dimensionless $\in [0,1]$ | Acceptable forensic threshold: $\eta \geq 0.999$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The throughput $\lambda$ and completeness $\eta$ are *not* in the original Sysinternals documentation — they are forensic-engineering abstractions used in KTU 2024 assessment questions. Be ready to compute $\eta$ when a problem gives $N$ and $D$.

## 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering / Forensics Practice

- **Incident Response (IR):** First responder runs the suspect sample under ProcMon to produce a **time-of-day timeline** that is admissible in internal investigations.
- **Threat Intelligence:** The captured IOCs (file paths, registry keys, C2 IPs) feed **YARA rules**, **Sigma rules**, and **Snort/Suricata signatures**.
- **Detection Engineering:** Behavioural patterns observed in ProcMon logs are encoded into **EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response)** behavioural rules.
- **Reverse Engineering:** ProcMon provides a "ground truth" runtime trace against which a reverse engineer validates hypotheses from disassembly.
- **Academic Research:** Published malware family analyses almost always include a ProcMon excerpt as a behavioural fingerprint.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Because Process Monitor is a **tool-centric** topic, the "derivations" in this section are **exhaustive procedural workflows** — every click, every menu path, and every analytical decision is enumerated. This format is what the KTU 2024 board expects when a 14-mark question asks "Explain the procedure for using Process Monitor in malware analysis."

---

## 3.1 Standard Forensic Procedure — Capturing a Malware Sample

### Step 1: Lab Preparation (Pre-Execution)

1. Prepare an **isolated Windows VM** (snapshot clean, no network, or **host-only** network with INetSim).
2. Install **Process Monitor** (extract `Procmon.exe` from the Sysinternals Suite ZIP).
3. Install supporting tools: **Process Explorer**, **Regshot**, **Wireshark**, **FakeNet-NG**.
4. Take a **clean snapshot** of the VM.
5. Verify ProcMon can run (open it, see events, close it).

**Justification:** A clean baseline ensures that every captured event is attributable to the malware, not to background Windows activity.

### Step 2: Configure Filters (Pre-Execution)

Open ProcMon. The filter dialog opens automatically (or press `Ctrl+L`).

**Build the following filters in this order:**

1. Click `Filter → Filter...` (or `Ctrl+L`).
2. **Add filter #1:** `Process Name  is  suspect.exe   Include`
3. **Add filter #2:** `Process Name  is  explorer.exe   Include` *(optional, to catch injected-into-explorer activity)*
4. **Add filter #3:** `Operation  is  TCP Connect   Include`
5. **Add filter #4:** `Operation  is  CreateFile   Include`
6. **Add filter #5:** `Path  contains  \CurrentVersion\Run   Include`
7. **Add filter #6:** `Process Name  is  System   Exclude` *(removes kernel noise)*
8. **Add filter #7:** `Process Name  is  svchost.exe   Exclude` *(removes Windows service noise unless explicitly investigating)*

Press `OK`. The filter list appears at the top of the window.

**Justification:** Filters dramatically reduce event volume and isolate the suspect's activity. This is the single biggest forensic-quality decision.

### Step 3: Clear the Capture Buffer

- Press `Ctrl+X` to clear the current event buffer.
- **Verify** the event count in the status bar reads `0` events.

**Justification:** A pre-execution buffer-zero guarantees the captured log starts at $T = 0$ for the malware run.

### Step 4: Begin Capture and Execute the Sample

- Press `Ctrl+E` to start capturing (or use the magnifying glass icon).
- **Immediately** execute the suspect binary in the VM.
- Allow the malware to run for a controlled duration (typically **60 – 120 seconds**).
- Optionally trigger a user-like action (open a browser, type in Notepad) to provoke interactive behaviours.

**Justification:** The malware must run *while* ProcMon is recording — otherwise the events are lost.

### Step 5: Stop Capture

- Press `Ctrl+E` again to stop.
- **Do not close ProcMon** until you save the log.

### Step 6: Save the Capture

- `File → Save...`
- Choose **PML (Process Monitor Log)** for full fidelity, including call stacks.
- Choose **CSV** for spreadsheet import and timeline construction.
- Choose **XML** for tool-chain integration.
- File naming convention: `CaseID_SampleHash_YYYYMMDD_HHMM.pml`

**Justification:** PML is the native format and retains information (call stacks, symbols) that CSV cannot. CSV is for analysis, PML is for archival.

### Step 7: Analyse the Captured Events

Apply analytical filters in this order:

1. **Filter `Operation is CreateFile` AND `Path contains .exe or .dll or .scr`**
   — *What new executables did the malware drop?*

2. **Filter `Operation is RegSetValue` AND `Path contains \Run`**
   — *What persistence did the malware install?*

3. **Filter `Operation is Load Image`**
   — *What DLLs did the malware load? Suspicious: `vbc.exe`, `wscript.exe`, `InstallUtil.exe`.*

4. **Filter `Operation is Process Create`**
   — *Did the malware spawn child processes? Suspicious: `cmd.exe /c`, `powershell.exe -enc`.*

5. **Filter `Operation is TCP Connect`**
   — *What C2 endpoints did the malware contact?*

6. **Filter `Result is NAME NOT FOUND` or `Result is ACCESS DENIED`**
   — *Reconnaissance of the system and AV evasion behaviour.*

### Step 8: Export IOCs

Export filtered events to CSV. Extract:
- SHA-256 hashes of dropped files.
- Full paths of created files.
- Registry keys/values written.
- Remote IP:port pairs.
- Mutex names (visible in `Detail` column of certain `RegCreateKey` calls).

**Justification:** IOCs are the *deliverable* of a malware forensics engagement.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Estimating Log Size and Completeness

A KTU 2024 Scheme exam may give numerical data and ask for a forensic-engineering estimate. Here is the full derivation.

> **Problem Statement:**
> A forensic capture of malware activity is performed. ProcMon reports $N = 4{,}800{,}000$ captured events and $D = 1{,}200$ dropped events. The capture lasted $T_{\text{cap}} = 90$ s. The mean event size in PML format is $\bar{s} = 180$ bytes/event. Compute the throughput $\lambda$, the estimated log size $S$, and the capture completeness ratio $\eta$. Comment on forensic acceptability.

### Step 1 — Throughput

$$
\lambda = \frac{N}{T_{\text{cap}}}
$$

Substitute $N = 4{,}800{,}000$ and $T_{\text{cap}} = 90$ s:

$$
\lambda = \frac{4{,}800{,}000 \text{ events}}{90 \text{ s}}
$$

$$
\lambda = 53{,}333.33 \text{ events/s}
$$

### Step 2 — Estimated Log Size

$$
S \approx \lambda \cdot T_{\text{cap}} \cdot \bar{s}
$$

Substitute:

$$
S \approx 53{,}333.33 \cdot 90 \cdot 180
$$

Compute the partial product:

$$
53{,}333.33 \cdot 90 = 4{,}800{,}000
$$

Then:

$$
S \approx 4{,}800{,}000 \cdot 180 = 864{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

Convert to megabytes:

$$
S \approx \frac{864{,}000{,}000}{1024 \cdot 1024} \approx 823.97 \text{ MB} \approx 0.82 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 3 — Capture Completeness

$$
\eta = \frac{N_{\text{captured}}}{N_{\text{captured}} + D}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\eta = \frac{4{,}800{,}000}{4{,}800{,}000 + 1{,}200} = \frac{4{,}800{,}000}{4{,}801{,}200}
$$

$$
\eta \approx 0.99975
$$

### Step 4 — Forensic Verdict

The standard forensic threshold is $\eta \geq 0.999$. Since $0.99975 \geq 0.999$, **the capture is forensically acceptable**. The investigator can defend the log in a report or in court.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner note:** If the question had given $D = 50{,}000$, then $\eta \approx 0.9897$ which **fails** the threshold. The correct recommendation would be to reduce filter scope (capture less noise), increase the kernel buffer (via `Options → History Depth`), or shorten $T_{\text{cap}}$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Malware Behaviour ↔ ProcMon Event Pattern

| Malware Behaviour | Primary ProcMon Signal | Filter Recipe |
|---|---|---|
| **Run-key persistence** | `RegSetValue` on `HKCU\...\Run` or `HKLM\...\Run` | `Operation is RegSetValue AND Path contains Run` |
| **Scheduled task persistence** | `Process Create` of `schtasks.exe /create` | `Operation is Process Create AND Command Line contains schtasks` |
| **DLL injection** | `Load Image` into a foreign PID | `Operation is Load Image AND PID is not in {own PID set}` |
| **Process hollowing** | `Process Create` then `Load Image` of legitimate target then `Thread Create` | Cross-reference `Process` and `Thread` events |
| **C2 callback** | `TCP Connect` to a non-RFC1918 IP | `Operation is TCP Connect` |
| **Data staging** | `CreateFile` in `%TEMP%` or `%APPDATA%` | `Operation is CreateFile AND Path contains \Temp\` |
| **Anti-forensics (timestamp tampering)** | `SetInformationFile` with `FileBasicInformation` | `Operation is SetInformationFile` |
| **Service installation** | `RegSetValue` on `HKLM\...\Services\` | `Operation is RegSetValue AND Path contains \Services\` |
| **WMI subscription persistence** | `Process Create` of `wmic.exe` or `mofcomp.exe` | `Operation is Process Create AND Process Name is wmic.exe` |
| **Scheduled sleep / sandbox evasion** | Long gaps between events of the same process | Sort by `Time of Day` and inspect `Time of Day` deltas |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Boot Logging Procedure

This is a high-frequency 7-mark sub-question in KTU 2024 Scheme exams.

1. **Open ProcMon as Administrator.** (`Options → Run as Administrator` or right-click `Run as administrator`.)
2. Click `Options → Enable Boot Logging`.
3. In the dialog box, set the output path (default: `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Procmon\BootLog.pml`).
4. **Reboot the system** (`Start → Restart`).
5. **Do not open ProcMon during boot.** Let Windows complete the full login process.
6. **Wait** until the system is fully idle.
7. Open ProcMon. It detects the boot log and prompts: *"Convert boot log to viewable log?"* Click **Yes**.
8. The full pre-user-mode log is loaded into the GUI.
9. Save it as a new PML file for analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not** enable Boot Logging and then attempt to view events live during the same session — ProcMon will not be loaded as a kernel driver early enough to capture boot-time activity.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four classes of events captured by Process Monitor. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four classes of events captured by Process Monitor are:

1. **File System Events** — e.g., `CreateFile`, `WriteFile`, `DeleteFile`, `SetRenameInformationFile`.
2. **Registry Events** — e.g., `RegCreateKey`, `RegSetValue`, `RegQueryValue`, `RegDeleteValue`.
3. **Process and Thread Events** — e.g., `Process Start`, `Process End`, `Thread Create`, `Load Image`.
4. **Network Events** — e.g., `TCP Connect`, `TCP Send`, `UDP Send` (operates at the socket layer; payloads not captured).

Process Monitor combines the legacy functionality of **FileMon** and **RegMon** with the additional two classes, providing a single unified real-time event stream useful for malware behavioural analysis.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**What is a "filter" in Process Monitor, and why is filter design the most critical step in forensic use? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **filter** in Process Monitor is a Boolean expression that determines whether an incoming kernel event is included in or excluded from the captured log. The expression is built from fields such as `Process Name`, `PID`, `Operation`, `Path`, `Result`, and `Detail` combined with relations (`is`, `contains`, `excludes`, `begins with`) and Boolean operators (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`).

Filter design is the most critical step in forensic use because:

1. A full unfiltered Windows boot produces **millions of events**, making post-capture analysis impossible within reasonable time.
2. **Targeted filters** isolate only the suspect process's footprint, dramatically reducing noise and exposing IOCs.
3. Filters that are **too broad** cause **buffer overruns and dropped events**, which destroys forensic completeness ($\eta < 0.999$).
4. Filters that are **too narrow** cause **missed events**, which destroys forensic defensibility — an investigator cannot claim to have captured "all" malicious activity if filters were over-aggressive.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks with a typical split of 7 + 7. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain the architecture of Process Monitor with a neat block diagram. Why is a kernel-mode driver necessary? (7 marks)**

**(b) Describe, step by step, how Process Monitor is used to perform dynamic behavioural analysis of a malware sample. Include filter construction in your answer. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a)

**Architecture (5 marks) and Kernel-Mode Necessity (2 marks):**

Process Monitor follows a **two-tier architecture**:

1. **User-mode front-end (`procmon.exe`)** — provides the GUI, manages the capture session, evaluates filter expressions, resolves symbols, and exports logs. It runs in Ring 3 of the CPU protection model.
2. **Kernel-mode driver (`PROCMON24.sys`)** — registers callbacks with the Windows kernel:
   - File system I/O: hooks the **I/O Manager** to intercept IRPs.
   - Registry: uses `CmRegisterCallback` to intercept Configuration Manager calls.
   - Process/Thread: uses `PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine` and `PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine`.
   - Network: uses a **TDI filter** (or NDIS lightweight filter) to intercept TCP/UDP operations.
3. A **kernel ring buffer** queues events for delivery to the user-mode GUI.

**Why a kernel-mode driver is necessary:**

- User-mode API hooks (used by many AVs) are bypassed by malware that uses **direct syscalls** (e.g., Heaven's Gate, direct `NtCreateFile` invocations).
