- Kernel-mode hooking sees **every** I/O request packet, making it impossible to bypass without also disabling the kernel driver — which is itself detectable.
- Low-level malware techniques like **raw disk writes**, **bootkit installation**, and **filter-driver attachment** are only visible from the kernel layer.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 above; the student is expected to redraw the two-tier architecture with the four kernel hook points.)*

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Step-by-step dynamic analysis procedure (7 marks):**

| Step | Action | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Prepare isolated VM, install ProcMon, take clean snapshot | 1 |
| 2 | Build filters: `Process Name is suspect.exe Include`, `Operation is CreateFile Include`, `Operation is RegSetValue Include`, `Operation is TCP Connect Include`, exclude `System` and `svchost.exe` | 2 |
| 3 | Clear buffer (`Ctrl+X`), start capture (`Ctrl+E`), execute sample | 1 |
| 4 | Run for 60–120 s, perform user-like actions, stop capture | 1 |
| 5 | Save as PML (fidelity) and CSV (analysis) | 1 |
| 6 | Apply analytical filters, extract IOCs (file paths, registry keys, IPs, mutexes) | 1 |

**Expected Output of Analysis:**
- List of created files (especially in `%APPDATA%`, `%TEMP%`).
- List of registry persistence keys.
- List of child processes spawned (`cmd.exe`, `powershell.exe`).
- List of C2 endpoints contacted (IP:port pairs).
- List of DLLs loaded into foreign PIDs (injection indicator).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain Boot Logging in Process Monitor. When is it necessary, and what is the correct procedure? (7 marks)**

**(b) A forensic capture produces $N = 2{,}400{,}000$ events in $T_{\text{cap}} = 60$ seconds, with $D = 800$ dropped events. The mean event size is $\bar{s} = 200$ bytes. Compute the throughput, estimated log size, and capture completeness ratio. Is the capture forensically acceptable? Justify. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a)

**Boot Logging (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Boot Logging is a Process Monitor feature that loads the `PROCMON24.sys` kernel driver **at the earliest possible stage of Windows boot** and begins capturing events continuously, including all activity that occurs *before* the user logs on and *before* the ProcMon GUI is launched.

**When it is necessary (2 marks):**
- Rootkits and **bootkits** that modify the MBR, VBR, or boot-start drivers.
- Malware that establishes persistence via **Boot-Start drivers** (`HKLM\...\CurrentControlSet\Services` with `Start = 0`).
- Any investigation in which relevant activity is suspected to occur **before** the GUI session begins.

**Correct Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Open ProcMon **as Administrator** → `Options → Enable Boot Logging`.
2. Specify the output path for the boot log (PML).
3. **Reboot** the system.
4. **Do not open** ProcMon during boot.
5. Wait for full Windows login and idle state.
6. Open ProcMon → it detects the boot log → click **Yes** to convert.
7. Save the converted log as a new PML file.

> **Common Error:** Opening ProcMon immediately after login *before* it has time to detect the boot log → results in the boot log being silently overwritten.

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Throughput (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{N}{T_{\text{cap}}} = \frac{2{,}400{,}000}{60} = 40{,}000 \text{ events/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Estimated Log Size (3 marks):**

$$
S \approx \lambda \cdot T_{\text{cap}} \cdot \bar{s} = 40{,}000 \cdot 60 \cdot 200 = 480{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

Convert to MB:

$$
S \approx \frac{480{,}000{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 457.76 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3 — Capture Completeness (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{N}{N + D} = \frac{2{,}400{,}000}{2{,}400{,}800} \approx 0.999667
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict (1 mark):**

Since $\eta \approx 0.999667 \geq 0.999$, **the capture is forensically acceptable**. The investigator may defend the log in a forensic report or in court. The log size of $\approx 458$ MB is well within typical storage capacity and easily exported.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse $N$ and $D$ in the completeness formula. $D$ is the *dropped* count, which goes in the *denominator*. A common error writes $D$ in the numerator, producing $\eta = D / (N+D)$ which is always tiny and wrong.
> 2. **Do not** forget to convert bytes → MB in the size calculation. Examiners explicitly test for unit conversion awareness.
> 3. **Do not** use $1024$ as the MB conversion factor when the question expects SI ($10^6$). Read the question carefully; in forensics the binary (1024) convention is standard, but in some paper variants SI is expected.
> 4. **Do not** answer only the numerical part in (b). Always conclude with a **justification sentence** referencing the $\eta \geq 0.999$ forensic threshold. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for the verdict.
> 5. **Do not** omit the diagram in (a). A block diagram of the two-tier architecture carries at least 2 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Memorise-List for KTU PECCT741 Module 2 — Monitoring with Process Monitor**

- **Tool origin:** Microsoft **Sysinternals** (Mark Russinovich, Bryce Cogswell).
- **Legacy:** Successor to **FileMon** + **RegMon**, extended with Process/Thread and Network.
- **Architecture:** Two-tier — user-mode `procmon.exe` + kernel-mode `PROCMON24.sys`.
- **Four event classes:** **F**ile, **R**egistry, **P**rocess/Thread, **N**etwork.
- **Capture buffer:** Kernel ring buffer; **dropped events** shown in status bar.
- **Capture completeness:** $\eta = \dfrac{N}{N+D}$; **threshold $\eta \geq 0.999$**.
- **Log size estimate:** $S \approx \lambda \cdot T_{\text{cap}} \cdot \bar{s}$.
- **Filters:** Boolean expressions over fields; relations include `is`, `contains`, `excludes`, `begins with`, `ends with`, `less than`, `more than`.
- **Gold-standard filter set:** `Process Name is suspect.exe Include`; `Operation is CreateFile/RegSetValue/TCP Connect Include`; exclude `System` and `svchost.exe` to reduce noise.
- **Boot Logging:** Captures pre-user-mode activity. Procedure = enable → reboot → wait → open ProcMon → convert → save.
- **Log formats:** **PML** (native, full fidelity, includes call stacks), **CSV** (analysis), **XML** (tool-chain), **EVTX** (Windows Event Log import).
- **Persistence indicators to look for in Registry:** `\CurrentVersion\Run`, `\CurrentVersion\RunOnce`, `Image File Execution Options`, `Winlogon\Shell`, `AppInit_DLLs`, `LSA\Security Packages`, `\Services\`.
- **Staging indicators in File System:** `%TEMP%`, `%APPDATA%`, `%PROGRAMDATA%`, `C:\ProgramData`, `Startup` folder.
- **Network indicators:** `TCP Connect` events to **non-RFC1918** destinations, recurring periodic intervals (beaconing).
- **Process injection indicator:** `Load Image` event where the loaded DLL is **not** the standard set for that PID.
- **Process hollowing indicator:** `Process Create` (legitimate target) → `Load Image` (different path) → `Thread Create` (suspicious start address).
- **Why kernel-mode is mandatory:** User-mode API hooks are bypassed by direct syscalls and direct kernel object manipulation.
- **Tool complement:** ProcMon + **Process Explorer** (handle / DLL view) + **Wireshark** (payload) + **Regshot** (registry diff) + **FakeNet-NG** (network simulator).
- **Forensic deliverable:** IOCs (file paths, hashes, registry keys, mutexes, IPs, ports) encoded as YARA / Sigma / Snort rules.
- **Examiner mantra:** *Filter first, capture second, analyse third, report last.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packet Sniffing with Wireshark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Packet Sniffing with Wireshark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Packet Sniffing** is the systematic process of intercepting, logging, and analyzing data packets that traverse a computer network using a specialized software or hardware tool known as a **Packet Sniffer** (also called a *Network Protocol Analyzer*, *Packet Analyzer*, or *Network Sniffer*). In the context of malware forensics, packet sniffing is a critical *non-intrusive* (in passive mode) investigative technique used to capture command-and-control (C2) traffic, exfiltrated data payloads, lateral movement signatures, and protocol anomalies associated with malicious activity.

**Wireshark** is the de facto industry-standard, **open-source packet analyzer** maintained by the Wireshark Foundation. It operates by placing the host's Network Interface Card (NIC) into **Promiscuous Mode**, enabling it to capture every frame that reaches the physical medium, regardless of the destination MAC address. Wireshark internally leverages the cross-platform packet capture library **libpcap** (Linux/macOS) or its Windows equivalents **WinPcap** / **Npcap** to interface directly with the OS kernel's link layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Students must understand the distinction between **Passive Sniffing** (silent observation, undetectable) and **Active Sniffing** (injecting traffic like ARP poisoning, detectable). For the Malware Forensics course, sniffing is treated as the **first technical step in network-layer incident response**, prior to deep-packet inspection (DPI).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Toll Booth" Model

Imagine a four-lane national highway (your network). Thousands of trucks (network packets) zoom past every second. A normal driver (regular PC) only sees trucks destined for its own warehouse. Now, install a **toll booth with a CCTV camera** (the packet sniffer in promiscuous mode) on the highway. This camera:

1. **Sees every truck**, not just those bound for the toll booth's own warehouse.
2. **Records the truck's registration plate (source/destination IP)**.
3. **Opens the truck's manifest** (application-layer payload) and logs the contents.
4. **Catalogues each truck** into a searchable database (the Wireshark GUI).

A *network engineer* uses the camera to find traffic jams, smuggled goods, or misrouted shipments. A *forensic investigator* uses it to track a malicious shipment (malware beacon) back to its origin server.

### 1.3 Key Terminology — Quick Glossary

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Promiscuous Mode** | NIC state where all frames are delivered to the CPU, not only those matching the NIC's MAC. | High — frequently asked in Part A. |
| **Monitor Mode** | (Wi-Fi only) NIC captures all wireless frames in the radio range, including 802.11 management frames. | Medium — Wi-Fi forensics scenario. |
| **BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter)** | A pseudo-machine language embedded in the OS kernel used by `libpcap` to filter packets at capture time. | High — required for Part B derivations. |
| **pcap / pcapng** | Standard binary file formats for storing captured packets; portable across tools. | High — forensic evidence preservation. |
| **Dissector** | Wireshark's protocol parser that decodes each layer of the OSI/TCP-IP stack. | High — architecture question. |
| **TShark** | Command-line sibling of Wireshark, ideal for scripted, headless forensic capture. | Medium — automation questions. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant / Metric Reference:** The **standard MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** for Ethernet is **1500 bytes**. Any IP packet larger than this requires **fragmentation**, and sniffing fragments is critical for detecting covert channel exfiltration attacks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Lifecycle of a Packet Sniffer

A packet sniffer like Wireshark operates through a deterministic pipeline:

1. **Kernel-Level Hook (libpcap/Npcap)** — The capture library registers a raw socket with the OS, requesting copies of all ingress/egress frames on the chosen interface.
2. **Promiscuous Mode Activation** — The NIC's receive filter is disabled so that frames with non-matching destination MAC addresses are also forwarded to the kernel.
3. **Capture Filter Application (BPF)** — User-defined BPF expressions (e.g., `tcp port 80`) are compiled into a small virtual program executed by the kernel for each frame — packets not matching the filter are dropped immediately to save CPU and disk.
4. **Buffering** — Surviving packets are pushed into a circular kernel buffer and periodically copied to a user-space buffer.
5. **Dissection** — Each frame is parsed layer-by-layer: Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application. Wireshark consults its dissector database (currently > **3000 protocols**).
6. **Display Filtering** — A separate, more expressive filtering engine (Wireshark's own syntax, not BPF) refines the *already-captured* packets for the GUI view.
7. **Output & Export** — Captures can be exported as `.pcap`, `.pcapng`, CSV, JSON, or raw bytes for forensic chain-of-custody.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Sniffing Techniques

| Sniffing Type | Mechanism | Detectability | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive Sniffing** | Taps into a shared medium (legacy Hub, network TAP, mirrored SPAN port). | Practically undetectable. | Covert forensic capture, lawful interception. |
| **Active Sniffing** | Injects traffic (ARP Spoofing, MAC Flooding, DHCP Starvation) to coerce the switch into forwarding traffic to the sniffer. | Detectable via IDS/IPS or ARP-watch tools. | Penetration testing, red-team engagements. |

### 2.3 Hardware vs. Software Sniffers

- **Hardware Sniffers / Network TAPs (Test Access Points):** Dedicated inline devices (e.g., *NetScout*, *Fluke Networks*, *Garland Technology*) that physically mirror electrical signals. They are **fail-safe**, do not introduce latency, and are tamper-evident — making them the *gold standard* for court-admissible forensic evidence.
- **Software Sniffers (Wireshark, tcpdump, TShark, Colasoft Capsa):** Run on commodity hardware; flexible, free, but consume host CPU and disk.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation, constant, and reference value you must memorize for ESE Module 2 questions.

**Table 2.4.A — Throughput & Capacity Formulas**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T = \dfrac{P \times 8}{t}$ | Instantaneous throughput (P = payload in bytes, t = time in seconds). | bits/second (bps) |
| $T = \dfrac{F \times 8}{t}$ | Frame-based throughput (F = total frame size including headers). | bps |
| $H_{\text{max}} = 2^{(32-n)} - 2$ | Maximum usable hosts in a `/n` CIDR block (subtracting network & broadcast). | Hosts |
| $W_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{used}}}{t_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Link utilization percentage for bandwidth analysis. | % |
| $L_{\text{RTT}} = 2 \times T_p + \dfrac{S}{B}$ | Round-Trip Time estimate (T_p = propagation, S = packet size bits, B = bandwidth bps). | seconds |

**Table 2.4.B — TCP Flag Hex Values (MUST memorize)**

| Flag | Bit Position | Hex Value (CWR/ECE/U/A/P/R/S/F) | Decimal |
|---|---|---|---|
| FIN | 0 | **0x01** | 1 |
| SYN | 1 | **0x02** | 2 |
| RST | 2 | **0x04** | 4 |
| PSH | 3 | **0x08** | 8 |
| ACK | 4 | **0x10** | 16 |
| URG | 5 | **0x20** | 32 |
| **SYN+ACK** | 1 + 4 | **0x12** | 18 |
| **FIN+ACK** | 0 + 4 | **0x11** | 17 |
| **PSH+ACK** | 3 + 4 | **0x18** | 24 |

**Table 2.4.C — BPF Capture Filter Primitives (for Part B derivations)**

| Primitive | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `host <IP>` | Match source OR destination IP. | `host 192.168.1.10` |
| `src host <IP>` / `dst host <IP>` | Unidirectional match. | `src host 10.0.0.5` |
| `net <CIDR>` | Match subnet. | `net 192.168.1.0/24` |
| `port <n>` | TCP or UDP port. | `port 443` |
| `tcp` / `udp` / `icmp` / `arp` | Protocol shortcut. | `tcp and port 80` |
| `ether src <MAC>` | MAC-level filter. | `ether src aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff` |
| `greater <len>` / `less <len>` | Frame length filter. | `greater 1500` |
| Logical | `and`, `or`, `not` | `tcp and not port 22` |

**Table 2.4.D — Wireshark Display Filter Operators**

| Filter | Function |
|---|---|
| `ip.addr == 10.0.0.5` | Any direction match. |
| `tcp.flags.syn == 1 and tcp.flags.ack == 0` | Pure SYN packets (scan detection). |
| `http.request.method == "POST"` | HTTP POST analysis. |
| `dns.qry.name contains "malicious"` | Covert DNS tunnel detection. |
| `frame.time_delta > 1` | Inter-packet gap > 1 second. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **malware forensics**, packet sniffing is the foundation for:

- **C2 (Command-and-Control) traffic analysis** — Identifying beacon patterns (e.g., Cobalt Strike, Emotet HTTP beacons).
- **Data exfiltration detection** — Capturing large outbound flows on unusual ports (DNS tunneling, ICMP exfil).
- **Lateral movement reconstruction** — Tracing SMB, RDP, and WinRM bursts during an Active Directory compromise.
- **Cryptojacking and botnet forensics** — Identifying Stratum mining pool connections and DDoS amplification sources.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hexadecimal Flag Word to Binary (KTU Examiner Favourite)

The **TCP Flags byte** is the most frequently dissected field in forensic sniffing. Given a Wireshark capture showing `Flags = 0x018`, derive the active flags.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention (RFC 793 + modern extensions):** CWR(7) · ECE(6) · URG(5) · ACK(4) · PSH(3) · RST(2) · SYN(1) · FIN(0).

**Step 1 — Write the hex byte.**

$$\text{Byte} = \texttt{0x18}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to decimal.**

$$1 \times 16^1 + 8 \times 16^0 = 16 + 8 = 24$$

**Step 3 — Convert to 8-bit binary (MSB = bit 7).**

$$\begin{aligned}
24_{\text{dec}} &= 16 + 8 \\
&= 2^4 + 2^3 \\
&= \texttt{0001\,1000}_{\text{bin}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Map each bit to its flag (bit 7 → bit 0).**

| Bit | Value | Flag | Active? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | 0 | CWR | No |
| 6 | 0 | ECE | No |
| 5 | 0 | URG | No |
| 4 | 1 | **ACK** | Yes |
| 3 | 1 | **PSH** | Yes |
| 2 | 0 | RST | No |
| 1 | 0 | SYN | No |
| 0 | 0 | FIN | No |

**Interpretation:** A `PSH+ACK` packet — typical of an established HTTP request carrying payload data.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Decoding an IP Address in a Capture (192.168.1.10)

Every IP header field in Wireshark is shown as a 32-bit binary string. Decode `192.168.1.10`.

**Step 1 — Convert each octet to 8-bit binary.**

$$\begin{aligned}
192 &= 128 + 64 = \texttt{1100\,0000} \\
168 &= 128 + 32 + 8 = \texttt{1010\,1000} \\
1   &= \texttt{0000\,0001} \\
10  &= 8 + 2 = \texttt{0000\,1010}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate (full 32-bit network-order representation).**

$$\text{IP}_{\text{bin}} = \texttt{11000000.10101000.00000001.00001010}$$

**Step 3 — Apply /24 subnet mask for forensic grouping.**

$$\text{Mask}_{/24} = \texttt{11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000}$$

**Step 4 — Bitwise AND to extract the network ID.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{bin}} &= \texttt{11000000.10101000.00000001.00000000} \\
N_{\text{dec}} &= \texttt{192.168.1.0}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** This packet originated from or is destined to the `192.168.1.0/24` subnet — a typical home/SOHO network block frequently scanned by botnets like Mirai.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: TCP Three-Way Handshake Timing Analysis

Given three captured packets with timestamps and flag combinations:

| Packet # | Timestamp (s) | Flag Word | Source Port | Dest Port | Seq (hex) | Ack (hex) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.000000 | 0x02 (SYN) | 5000 | 80 | 0xAABBCC01 | 0x00000000 |
| 2 | 0.014235 | 0x12 (SYN-ACK) | 80 | 5000 | 0x11223344 | 0xAABBCC02 |
| 3 | 0.028901 | 0x10 (ACK) | 5000 | 80 | 0xAABBCC02 | 0x11223345 |

**Step 1 — Verify the SYN. Acknowledge Number field should be zero.**

$$\text{Ack}_{\text{pkt1}} = \texttt{0x00000000} \; \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute SYN-ACK latency (server response time).**

$$\Delta t_1 = t_2 - t_1 = 0.014235 - 0.000000 = 14.235\ \text{ms}$$

This represents the **SYN-ACK RTT** and is a forensic indicator of server-side processing delay — abnormally high values may indicate an application under **Slowloris-style DoS stress**.

**Step 3 — Compute ACK latency (client finalization).**

$$\Delta t_2 = t_3 - t_2 = 0.028901 - 0.014235 = 14.666\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total handshake duration.**

$$T_{\text{hs}} = t_3 - t_1 = 0.028901 - 0.000000 = 28.901\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 5 — Forensic conclusion.** A complete handshake of **~28.9 ms** with valid Seq/Ack progression (`Seq + 1 = Ack`) confirms a legitimate TCP establishment. A *missing* third ACK or an RST replacing it would indicate a **SYN-scan** (recon) or a half-open attack.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Forensic Anomalies Detectable by Sniffing

| Anomaly | Filter to Detect | Forensic Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Port Scan (SYN) | `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` | Nmap reconnaissance. |
| ARP Spoofing | Duplicate `arp.src.proto_ipv4` with changing MAC | MITM / session hijack. |
| DNS Tunneling | `dns.qry.name.len > 50` & high entropy in TXT | Data exfil / C2. |
| ICMP Exfil | `icmp && frame.len > 200` | Ping-tunnel covert channel. |
| SMB Lateral Move | `smb2.cmd == 5 && ip.src != previous_ip` | Ransomware propagation. |
| Beaconing | `frame.time_delta == constant` for long duration | Cobalt Strike C2. |
| Cleartext Credentials | `ftp` or `http.request.method == "POST"` and `frame contains "password"` | Plaintext leakage. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wireshark Internal Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph PHY[Physical Layer Medium]
        wireA[Network Cable or 80211 Radio]
    end

    subgraph NIC[Host NIC in Promiscuous Mode]
        drvA[NIC Driver]
    end

    subgraph KERN[OS Kernel Space]
        bpA[libpcap or Npcap Hook]
        bpB[BPF Capture Filter Compiler]
        bufA[Kernel Circular Buffer]
    end

    subgraph USR[User Space Wireshark]
        capA[TShark or Dumpcap Capture Engine]
        dissA[Dissector Engine]
        dispA[Display Filter Engine]
        guiA[Qt GUI Front End]
        statA[Statistics and IO Graphs Module]
        expA[Export Engine pcapng CSV JSON]
    end

    wireA --> drvA --> bpA --> bpB --> bufA --> capA --> dissA --> dispA --> guiA
    dissA --> statA
    guiA --> expA
```
<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_END -->

### 4.2 Packet Capture Workflow — Step-by-Step Data Path

<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    pktA[Raw Ethernet Frame Arrives] --> chkA{Destination MAC Matches NIC}
    chkA -- Yes --> kernA[Normal Stack Path]
    chkA -- No --> promA[Promiscuous Mode ON Pass to CPU]
    promA --> bpfchk{BPF Capture Filter Match}
    bpfchk -- No --> dropA[Drop Packet Free Memory]
    bpfchk -- Yes --> bufA[Push to Kernel Buffer]
    bufA --> usrA[User Space Read by Dumpcap]
    usrA --> discA[Per Layer Dissection]
    discA --> dispchk{Display Filter Match}
    dispchk -- No --> hidA[Hidden from GUI]
    dispchk -- Yes --> viewA[Visible in Packet List Pane]
    viewA --> statA[Statistics Engine]
    statA --> expA[Export pcapng File]
```
<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_END -->

### 4.3 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Capture-State Diagram

<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    cliA[Client 10.0.0.5] -- SYN Seq X --> srvA[Server 10.0.0.20]
    srvA -- SYN ACK Seq Y Ack X+1 --> cliA
    cliA -- ACK Seq X+1 Ack Y+1 --> srvA
    srvA --> estA[Established Connection Ready for Data]
    cliA --> estA
```
<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_END -->

### 4.4 Promiscuous vs. Non-Promiscuous (Normal) Mode

<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NORM[Normal Mode NIC]
        n1[Frame D MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF] --> nDrop[Accepted Broadcast]
        n2[Frame D MAC AA:BB:CC:11:22:33] --> nDrop2[Accepted Only if Matches Local MAC]
        n3[Frame D MAC AA:BB:CC:99:88:77] --> nDrop3[Dropped Silently]
    end

    subgraph PROM[Promiscuous Mode NIC]
        p1[Any Frame Regardless of D MAC] --> pAcc[Forwarded to Kernel]
        pAcc --> pFilter[BPF Filter Applied]
        pFilter --> pBuffer[Stored in Capture Buffer]
    end
```
<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECCT741]**

Differentiate between **passive sniffing** and **active sniffing**. Give one real-world example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Sniffing | Active Sniffing |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Silently captures traffic on a shared medium (hub, SPAN port, or TAP) without injecting any frames. | Injects crafted traffic (ARP replies, MAC floods) to manipulate switch CAM tables and force traffic redirection. |
| **Detectability** | Practically undetectable by the victim host. | Detectable via ARP-watch, IDS, switch logs. |
| **Example** | A forensic investigator connects a laptop to a mirrored SPAN port and runs Wireshark. | An attacker on a switched LAN runs `arpspoof` to perform a MITM and then sniffs the redirected traffic. |

*(Award 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary PECCT741]**

What is **promiscuous mode**? Why is it essential for packet sniffing, and how does it differ from monitor mode used in Wi-Fi networks?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Promiscuous Mode (1 mark):** A NIC operating mode in which the interface forwards **every frame** arriving on the wire to the CPU, irrespective of whether the destination MAC address matches its own. This is enabled either in software (driver flag) or by the OS (e.g., via `ifconfig eth0 promisc`).
- **Why essential (1 mark):** In a switched Ethernet network, each NIC normally discards frames not addressed to it. Without promiscuous mode, a sniffer would only see broadcast frames and its own traffic — making comprehensive capture impossible.
- **Difference from Monitor Mode (1 mark):** Monitor Mode is the wireless (802.11) equivalent that additionally allows capture of **management and control frames** (beacons, probes, de-auths) that are never exposed to normal Wi-Fi clients. Promiscuous mode is Ethernet-centric and does not handle 802.11 radio-layer functions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — PECCT741 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of Wireshark** and the role of each subsystem in the packet processing pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **Capture Filters (BPF)** and **Display Filters**. Write the syntax and give three practical examples of each from a malware forensics investigation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Capture Engine (Dumpcap / TShark) — 1 mark:** Interacts directly with `libpcap`/`Npcap`. Spawns a background process to handle the actual NIC reads, isolating crashes from the GUI.
2. **BPF Compiler — 1 mark:** Translates user-supplied capture filters (e.g., `tcp port 443`) into a small interpreted program executed in kernel space per packet — vastly reducing CPU load.
3. **Kernel Buffer (Ring Buffer) — 1 mark:** A fixed-size circular memory region (typically 2 MB default, adjustable) that smooths bursts.
4. **Dissector Engine — 2 marks:** Identifies protocols at each layer using a tree of registered dissectors (Ethernet → IP → TCP → HTTP → TLS). Each dissector populates the Protocol Tree shown in Wireshark's middle pane.
5. **Display Filter Engine — 1 mark:** Evaluates post-capture expressions (e.g., `http.request.uri contains "cmd"`) and hides non-matching packets from view without deleting them.
6. **GUI / Export / Statistics — 1 mark:** Qt-based front end, plus I/O graphs, conversation matrices, and export to pcapng, CSV, JSON for forensic reports.

*(Use the diagram from SECTION 4.1 for full marks; examiner expects labelled blocks.)*

**(b) Capture vs Display Filters (7 marks):**

| Attribute | Capture Filter (BPF) | Display Filter (Wireshark) |
|---|---|---|
| **When Applied** | Before capture (kernel level). | After capture (user level). |
| **Syntax Family** | `tcpdump`-style (`host`, `port`, `net`). | Wireshark-specific dotted notation (`ip.addr`, `tcp.flags.syn`). |
| **Can Recover Missed Packets?** | No — packets dropped at kernel are lost forever. | Yes — packets are stored in pcapng; just unhide them. |
| **Performance** | Highly efficient (in-kernel). | Less efficient (iterates full capture). |
| **Expressive Power** | Limited (no protocol-field slicing). | Very high (any dissected field). |

**Three practical examples (1.5 marks each = 4.5 marks; balance 0.5 for syntax explanation):**

| Goal | Capture Filter | Display Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Isolate web traffic only. | `tcp port 80 or tcp port 443` | `http or tls` |
| Capture suspected C2 beacons to a single IP. | `host 198.51.100.25` | `ip.addr == 198.51.100.25 and http` |
| Hunt for DNS exfiltration. | `udp port 53` | `dns.qry.name.len > 40 and dns.qry.name matches "[a-z0-9]{15,}"` |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — PECCT741, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Classify and explain the different **types of network sniffers**. Discuss any **three real-world malware-forensics scenarios** in which packet sniffing with Wireshark plays a decisive investigative role. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forensic investigator captures the following three packets during an incident. Using Wireshark's analysis features, **reconstruct the TCP three-way handshake**, identify any anomaly, and recommend containment. **(7 marks)**

| # | Time (s) | Source → Dest | Protocol | Flags | Seq | Ack | Length |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.000 | 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.20 | TCP | 0x02 | 1000 | 0 | 74 |
| 2 | 0.012 | 10.0.0.20 → 10.0.0.5 | TCP | 0x12 | 5000 | 1001 | 74 |
| 3 | 0.025 | 10.0.0.5 → 10.0.0.20 | TCP | 0x14 | 1001 | 5001 | 66 |

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification of Sniffers (3.5 marks):**

1. **Passive vs Active** — As described in Section 2.2.
2. **Hardware vs Software** — TAPs/SPAN ports (hardware) vs Wireshark/tcpdump (software).
3. **Specialized vs General** — IDS-integrated sniffers (Snort, Suricata) vs general analyzers (Wireshark, Colasoft).
4. **Local vs Remote** — Local capture on host NIC vs remote SPAN/RSPAN aggregation.

**Three Real-World Scenarios (1 mark each = 3 marks; balance 0.5 marks):**

- **Scenario 1 — Ransomware C2 Beacon Detection:** Wireshark captures periodic HTTPS POSTs to a Tor exit node; display filter `tls.handshake.extensions_server_name contains ".onion"` flags the beacon.
- **Scenario 2 — Data Exfiltration over DNS:** Filter `dns.qry.name.len > 50` reveals base32-encoded chunks of a sensitive file leaving the network via TXT queries.
- **Scenario 3 — Lateral SMB Movement:** During a Conti attack, Wireshark shows `smb2.cmd == 5` (Tree Connect) and `smb2.filename contains "admin$"` from a workstation to multiple servers — proving pivoting.

**(b) Handshake Reconstruction (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Packet 1 as SYN.** Flag `0x02` = binary `00000010`. Only SYN bit is set; Ack = 0. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Identify Packet 2 as SYN-ACK.** Flag `0x12` = binary `00010010`. SYN + ACK bits set; Ack = 1001 (= Client Seq + 1) ✓. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Identify Packet 3 as ACK.** Flag `0x14` = binary `00010100`. ACK + PSH bits set (PSH indicates piggy-backed data in the same segment). Ack = 5001 (= Server Seq + 1) ✓. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Timing Analysis.**

$$\Delta t_{\text{SYN→SYN-ACK}} = 12\ \text{ms}$$
$$\Delta t_{\text{SYN-ACK→ACK}} = 13\ \text{ms}$$
$$T_{\text{handshake}} = 25\ \text{ms} \;(\text{healthy})$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Anomaly Identification.**

The third packet carries `Length = 66 bytes`, meaning payload data is being pushed **during** the ACK — this is permitted but unusual; in a clean handshake the third ACK is normally empty (54 bytes header-only). The presence of immediate PSH data may indicate a **single-packet exploit attempt** or **HTTP request piggy-backed in the handshake** (common in HTTP/1.0 keep-alive scenarios). (2 marks)

**Step 6 — Containment Recommendation.**

1. Block source IP `10.0.0.5` at the perimeter firewall.
2. Quarantine the host; capture a full pcapng to chain-of-custody storage.
3. Cross-correlate with EDR telemetry for the same timestamp window.
4. If the destination (`10.0.0.20`) is a server, immediately review its logs for lateral SMB/RDP attempts originating from `10.0.0.5`.

(1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Capture vs Display Filters (loses 2–3 marks):** Capture filters use BPF syntax (`tcp port 80`), display filters use Wireshark syntax (`tcp.port == 80`). Writing one in place of the other is a common, *heavily penalized* mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting Npcap/libpcap dependency (loses 1 mark):** Always mention that Wireshark *cannot* capture packets without a packet-capture driver installed.
> 3. **Missing the switch-vs-hub distinction (loses 2 marks):** On a modern switched network, plugging in Wireshark alone captures *only your own traffic and broadcasts*. You must mention **port mirroring (SPAN), network TAPs, or ARP spoofing** as prerequisites.
> 4. **Skipping the time-sequence analysis:** KTU examiners often award full 7 marks only if you compute `Δt` or verify `Seq+1 = Ack` for the three-way handshake.
> 5. **Misquoting TCP flag hex values:** Memorize the table in §2.4.B. Writing "SYN-ACK = 0x10" instead of `0x12` is an instant 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print this before the exam!):**

- **Packet sniffing** = intercepting & analyzing network traffic using a sniffer.
- **Wireshark** is an **open-source**, GUI-based, cross-platform packet analyzer using **libpcap/Npcap**.
- **Promiscuous mode** is mandatory on Ethernet; **monitor mode** is its Wi-Fi counterpart.
- **Passive sniffing** is undetectable; **active sniffing** (ARP spoof) is detectable.
- **Hardware sniffers (TAPs)** are tamper-evident & court-admissible; software sniffers (Wireshark) are flexible & free.
- **BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter)** is a kernel-level filter executed *before* capture → packets dropped here are lost forever.
- **Display filters** run *after* capture on already-stored packets → packets hidden can always be revealed.
- **Memorize TCP flag hex:** `SYN=0x02`, `ACK=0x10`, `SYN-ACK=0x12`, `FIN-ACK=0x11`, `PSH-ACK=0x18`, `RST=0x04`.
- **Three-way handshake order:** `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`. Verify `Ack_N = Seq_{N-1} + 1`.
- **MTU of Ethernet** = **1500 bytes**; anything larger triggers IP fragmentation → check for ICMP "Fragmentation Needed" and possible covert-channel abuse.
- **Forensic output format:** `.pcapng` is preferred over legacy `.pcap` because it supports multi-interface, annotations, and metadata.
- **Common forensic filters to remember:**
  - `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` → port scans
  - `arp.duplicate-address-detected` → ARP spoofing
  - `dns.qry.name.len > 50` → DNS tunneling
  - `http.request.method == "POST"` → credential exfil hunting
- **On a switched network**, you **cannot** sniff other hosts' traffic by simply plugging in Wireshark — you need **port mirroring, a TAP, or ARP spoofing**.
- **Wireshark shows time-deltas** — use them to detect beaconing (constant Δt = automated C2).
- **Always export captures to `.pcapng` and hash (SHA-256) them** before submission as evidence — maintains chain of custody.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kernel vs. User-Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Kernel vs. User-Mode ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In modern operating systems (Windows NT, Linux, macOS), the CPU executes instructions in one of two distinct **processor privilege levels** (also called *rings* or *execution domains*). These are: - **Kernel Mode (Ring 0 / Supervisor Mode):** The unrestricted execution state in which code has complete, direct access to all hardware resources, physical memory, CPU registers, device I/O ports, and critical OS data structures. - **User Mode (Ring 3):** The restricted execution state in which application code runs with a heavily sandboxed, virtualized view of the system; any attempt to touch privileged resources triggers a hardware exception (trap) routed back to the kernel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Malware Forensics, the distinction between Kernel-Mode and User-Mode is **THE** foundational concept that dictates how malware can be analyzed, contained, and reversed. Rootkits and bootkits operate in Ring 0; trojans, worms, and ransomware typically operate in Ring 3. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine a **high-security government building**: | Building Analogy | Computing Equivalent | |---|---| | The President's private office (full authority) | **Kernel Mode (Ring 0)** | | The public reception lobby (restricted access) | **User Mode (Ring 3)** | | A receptionist who passes a written request to the President | **System Call (Syscall) Interface** | | A citizen trying to barge into the office | **Privileged Instruction Violation → General Protection Fault** | | A spy hiding inside the building's plumbing and electrical systems | **Kernel-Mode Rootkit** | In the building, citizens (user applications) cannot enter the President's office directly. They must fill out a **formal written request** (system call). The President personally reviews and either approves or denies it. Similarly, a Word document cannot directly command the hard disk — it must ask the kernel politely via the syscall API. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** Kernel-mode code is *trusted by hardware*. User-mode code is *untrusted by default*. This is the cornerstone of OS security and the entire reason malware forensics exists as a discipline. ### 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics - **x86 Privilege Rings:** **4 levels** (Ring 0 through Ring 3), though mainstream OSes only use **Ring 0** and **Ring 3**. - **ARM Exception Levels:** EL0 (User), EL1 (Kernel), EL2 (Hypervisor), EL3 (Secure Monitor). - **RPL (Requested Privilege Level):** A 2-bit field in x86 segment selectors used in privilege checking. - **CPL (Current Privilege Level):** The 2-bit field stored in the low bits of the CS register, indicating the running code's privilege. - **DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level):** The 2-bit field in a segment/gate descriptor specifying who may access it. - **Typical Memory Boundary:** User-mode addresses usually capped at **0x00000000 – 0xBFFFFFFF** (lower 3 GB in 32-bit Windows), while kernel owns **0xC0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Privilege Transition Mechanism — Step-by-Step A user-mode application cannot directly call kernel functions. The transition follows a **strictly enforced hardware-mediated handshake**: **Step 1 — User code invokes a library wrapper** Example in C: `read(fd, buffer, n)` eventually calls the libc stub, which executes the `SYSCALL` instruction (x86-64) or `INT 0x80` / `sysenter` (x86). **Step 2 — CPU saves user context** The processor automatically pushes the current **SS, RSP, RFLAGS, CS, RIP** onto the Kernel Stack (the per-thread stack set up in kernel memory by the OS). **Step 3 — CPU loads kernel CS:RIP from the LSTAR MSR** On x86-64, the `IA32_LSTAR` Model-Specific Register contains the address of the OS syscall entry point (e.g., `KiSystemCall64` on Windows). **Step 4 — Privilege level flip** The hardware atomically switches CPL from **3 → 0**. The new RIP points inside the kernel image. **Step 5 — Kernel dispatcher resolves the request** The kernel reads the syscall number from a register (e.g., RAX on x86-64), looks up the handler in the **System Service Dispatch Table (SSDT)** (Windows) or **sys_call_table** (Linux), and invokes it. **Step 6 — Kernel validates ALL parameters** Pointers from user space are checked against the user/kernel boundary. This is the **#1 location where exploit mitigations live** (SMEP, SMAP, KASLR). **Step 7 — Kernel returns via SYSRET / IRET** The user context is restored, and CPL flips back to **3**. ### 2.2 Why Malware Operates Differently in Each Mode | Capability | User-Mode Malware (Ring 3) | Kernel-Mode Malware (Ring 0) | |---|---|---| | **Process manipulation** | Limited to own processes via Win32 API | Any process, any address space | | **File hiding** | Easy (DLL injection, hooks in own process) | Direct filesystem filter driver (`minifilter`) | | **Network stealth** | Winsock LSP, API hooking | NDIS / TDI driver-level interception | | **Persistence** | Registry Run keys, Scheduled Tasks | Bootkits, MBR/VBR infection, kernel drivers | | **Detection difficulty** | Moderate (AV/EDR can see it) | Very high (rootkit can blind AV/EDR itself) | | **Forensic footprint** | User-mode artifacts: prefetch, AmCache, $MFT, registry hives | Volatile memory only — kernel objects, EPROCESS links, driver IRPs | | **Survives reboot?** | Yes (with persistence) | Yes (often deeper than user-mode persistence) | | **Example malware families** | Emotet, Conti, Dridex, Agent Tesla | TDL4, Alureon, Necurs, Stuxnet (driver) | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Definition | Forensic Significance | |---|---|---| | **Ring 0 (Kernel Mode)** | Full hardware privilege; arbitrary memory & I/O access | Rootkits, bootkits hide here; volatile-only evidence | | **Ring 3 (User Mode)** | Restricted; hardware traps on privileged ops | Where 95 % of malware lives; rich on-disk artifacts | | **CPL (Current Privilege Level)** | 2-bit field in CS register describing running code's privilege | Volatile indicator of code execution context | | **DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level)** | Required privilege to access a GDT/IDT entry | Gate descriptors enforce ring transitions | | **RPL (Requested Privilege Level)** | Privilege requested by the selector itself | Used for backward compatibility & safer pointer passing | | **SYSCALL / SYSENTER** | Fast ring-transition instructions (x86-64 / x86) | Forensic hooks placed here catch all syscalls | | **IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table)** | Array of 256 interrupt/exception handlers | Hooking IDT entries = classic rootkit technique | | **SSDT (System Service Descriptor Table)** | Windows kernel table mapping syscall # → handler function | Hooking SSDT = classic API-call redirection rootkit | | **SMEP (Supervisor Mode Execution Prevention)** | CPU bit preventing Ring 0 from executing user pages | Mitigation that broke many kernel exploits | | **SMAP (Supervisor Mode Access Prevention)** | CPU bit preventing Ring 0 from reading user pages | Hardens kernel against ROP gadgets | | **KASLR (Kernel ASLR)** | Randomizes kernel base address at boot | Raises the bar for kernel exploit reliability | | **PatchGuard (Windows)** | Kernel integrity monitor that BSODs on SSDT/IDT tampering | Forces rootkits toward *direct syscall* techniques | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In forensic investigations, the kernel/user-mode distinction dictates the **entire acquisition strategy**: - **Live Response** captures volatile user-mode artifacts (process list, network connections, loaded DLLs, handle tables) using user-mode tools. - **Memory Forensics** (Volatility, Rekall) examines kernel-mode structures (`_EPROCESS`, `_ETHREAD`, kernel driver lists) to find hidden user-mode processes AND kernel-mode rootkits. - **Disk Forensics** can find only user-mode persistence (registry, scheduled tasks, services). Hidden kernel-mode drivers may have **no on-disk artifact** if loaded only from memory. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Privilege Check Mathematical Model The x86 CPU performs a privilege check on **every** memory access and control transfer. The governing condition for a successful ring transition or access is: $$ \text{Access Granted} \iff \text{DPL} \geq \max(\text{CPL}, \text{RPL}) $$ Where: - $\text{CPL}$ = Current Privilege Level of the executing code. - $\text{RPL}$ = Requested Privilege Level (low 2 bits of segment selector). - $\text{DPL}$ = Descriptor Privilege Level of the target segment/gate. **Interpretation Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & \text{Loading a data segment requires } \text{DPL} \geq \max(\text{CPL}, \text{RPL}) \\ \text{Step 2: } & \text{Calling a call gate requires } \text{DPL}_{\text{gate}} \geq \text{CPL} \\ \text{Step 3: } & \text{A conforming code segment allows access if } \text{CPL} \geq \text{DPL}_{\text{segment}} \\ \text{Step 4: } & \text{User code (CPL = 3) trying to load a kernel segment (DPL = 0) fails} \\ \text{Step 5: } & \text{Because } \max(3, 3) = 3 \not\leq 0 \Rightarrow \text{General Protection Fault (#GP)} \\ \text{Step 6: } & \text{Kernel code (CPL = 0) can load any segment because } \max(0, 0) = 0 \leq \text{DPL} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Malware Capability vs. Privilege Level $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Capability Score} = & \; w_1 \cdot (\text{Memory Access}) + w_2 \cdot (\text{Hardware I/O}) + w_3 \cdot (\text{Stealth}) \\ & + w_4 \cdot (\text{Persistence Depth}) + w_5 \cdot (\text{Detection Difficulty}) \end{aligned} $$ Assigning normalized weights (1 = low, 5 = high): | Capability | User-Mode (Ring 3) Score | Kernel-Mode (Ring 0) Score | |---|---|---| | Direct Physical Memory Access | 0 | 5 | | Direct Disk I/O (bypass filesystem) | 0 | 5 | | Hiding from Task Manager (`PsActiveProcessHead` unlink) | 1 | 5 | | Hooking the IDT | 0 | 5 | | Loading unsigned drivers (modern Windows) | 0 | 2 (requires DSE bypass) | | Surviving OS reinstall on separate disk | 0 | 5 (bootkit) | | Fileless execution (memory-only) | 3 | 5 | **Weighted Total (example $w_i = 1$ for all):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{User-Mode Total} & = 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 3 = \mathbf{4} \\ \text{Kernel-Mode Total} & = 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 2 + 5 + 5 = \mathbf{32} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Kernel-mode malware is roughly **8× more powerful** in the combined capability vector — which is exactly why the OS invests billions of transistors in privilege enforcement. ### 3.3 Syscall Transition Cost Analysis (Microarchitectural View) A user→kernel→user round trip incurs measurable cost: $$ T_{\text{syscall}} = T_{\text{context\_save}} + T_{\text{dispatch}} + T_{\text{handler}} + T_{\text{context\_restore}} $$ **Typical values on modern x86-64:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{context\_save}} & \approx 30 - 80 \text{ ns} \\ T_{\text{dispatch}} & \approx 100 - 300 \text{ ns (varies with syscall number and cache state)} \\ T_{\text{handler}} & = \text{depends on service (read/write = 1 - 10 } \mu\text{s)} \\ T_{\text{context\_restore}} & \approx 30 - 80 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Forensic Implication:** Rootkits that hook many syscalls measurably slow down system throughput. Investigators can sometimes detect kernel rootkits by **timing a high-volume syscall** (e.g., 1 million `getpid()` calls) and comparing against a known-baseline microbenchmark. ### 3.4 Memory Mapping Mathematics On 32-bit Windows with default 2 GB user / 2 GB kernel split: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{User Address Space} & = [0\text{x}00000000,\; 0\text{x}7FFFFFFF] \quad \text{(2 GB, Ring 3)} \\ \text{Kernel Address Space} & = [0\text{x}80000000,\; 0\text{x}FFFFFFFF] \quad \text{(2 GB, Ring 0)} \\ \text{Total Virtual Space} & = 2^{32} = 4 \text{ GB} \end{aligned} $$ On 32-bit Windows with `/3GB` boot switch: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{User Address Space} & = [0\text{x}00000000,\; 0\text{x}BFFFFFFF] \quad \text{(3 GB, Ring 3)} \\ \text{Kernel Address Space} & = [0\text{x}C0000000,\; 0\text{x}FFFFFFFF] \quad \text{(1 GB, Ring 0)} \end{aligned} $$ On x86-64 Windows (canonical addressing): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{User Address Space} & = [0\text{x}0000000000000000,\; 0\text{x}00007FFFFFFFFFFF] \quad \text{(128 TB)} \\ \text{Kernel Address Space} & = [0\text{x}FFFF800000000000,\; 0\text{x}FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF] \quad \text{(128 TB)} \\ \text{Non-canonical Hole} & = [0\text{x}0000800000000000,\; 0\text{x}FFFF7FFFFFFFFFFF] \quad \text{(causes \#GP)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** On x86-64, attempting to access the non-canonical hole raises a **General Protection Fault (#GP)** with error code 0 — a classic forensic signature of a NULL-pointer dereference or uninitialized pointer exploit attempt. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 x86 Privilege Ring Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB ring3["RING 3 - USER MODE
Applications, Libraries, User Malware
CPL = 3"] ring2["RING 2 - OS / Driver Sub-mode
Unused in Windows and Linux
CPL = 2"] ring1["RING 1 - OS / Driver Sub-mode
Unused in Windows and Linux
CPL = 1"] ring0["RING 0 - KERNEL MODE
Kernel, Drivers, Rootkits
CPL = 0"] ring3 -->|"Syscall via SYSCALL or INT 2Eh"| ring0 ring0 -->|"SYSRET or IRETQ"| ring3 ring3 -."Restricted by hardware: GPF on
privileged instructions like IN/OUT, MOV
to CR3, LGDT, WRMSR, HLT, CLI".- ring0 style ring3 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1F3A5F,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style ring2 fill:#888888,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFFFFF style ring1 fill:#888888,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFFFFF style ring0 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.2 Syscall Transition Sequence (Forensic Handshake) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant UM as User-Mode App
(Ring 3) participant CPU as CPU Hardware participant KS as Kernel Stack participant KD as Kernel Dispatcher
(Ring 0) UM->>CPU: Execute SYSCALL instruction Note over CPU: Save SS, RSP, RFLAGS, CS, RIP to MSRs CPU->>KS: Switch RSP to per-thread kernel stack CPU->>KD: Load RIP from IA32_LSTAR MSR
CPL flips 3 to 0 KD->>KD: Read syscall number from RAX KD->>KD: Look up handler in SSDT or sys_call_table KD->>KD: Validate user-space pointers (SMEP/SMAP checks) KD->>KD: Execute kernel function KD->>CPU: Execute SYSRET CPU->>UM: Restore user context
CPL flips 0 to 3 Note over UM: Application resumes after SYSCALL ``` ### 4.3 Malware Operational Zones ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USERLAND["USER-LAND ZONE - RING 3"] UP1["Normal Application
notepad.exe"] UP2["User-Mode Malware
Trojan.exe"] UP3["Reflective Loader
injects DLL into memory"] UP4["Credential Dumper
Mimikatz userland"] end subgraph BOUNDARY["BOUNDARY - SYSCALL GATE"] SYSC["SYSCALL / INT 2Eh
Syscall Number Validation
Pointer Sanitization"] end subgraph KERNELLAND["KERNEL-LAND ZONE - RING 0"] K1["OS Kernel
ntoskrnl.exe"] K2["Legitimate Driver
signed .sys file"] K3["Kernel Rootkit
unsigned malicious driver"] K4["Bootkit
MBR or VBR code"] end USERLAND -->|"Forensic evidence:
prefetch, AmCache, registry,
$MFT, USN journal"| EVD1[("Disk Artifacts")] KERNELLAND -->|"Forensic evidence:
memory dump only,
driver list, EPROCESS links"| EVD2[("RAM Artifacts")] USERLAND -.->|controlled request| SYSC SYSC -.->|validated handoff| KERNELLAND K3 -.->|"hooks SSDT, IDT, IRP,
DKOM unlinks EPROCESS"| KERNELLAND K4 -.->|"pre-OS, persists
OS reinstall"| KERNELLAND style USERLAND fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1F3A5F,color:#FFFFFF style KERNELLAND fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,color:#FFFFFF style BOUNDARY fill:#F5A623,stroke:#7A4F00,color:#FFFFFF style EVD1 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#3D6B10,color:#FFFFFF style EVD2 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#5C0675,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.4 Rootkit Hooking Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Rootkit needs to intercept
a system operation"] Q1{"Where does the
request originate?"} Q2{"Is PatchGuard
active?"} Q3{"Is DSE
enforced?"} A1["Hook in User-Mode DLL
Inline hook on ntdll!NtCreateFile
Easy, detectable by EDR"] A2["Hook IDT entry
High risk, modern Windows blocks"] A3["Modify SSDT pointer
Classic XP-era technique
PatchGuard triggers BSOD"] A4["Modify function prologue
in ntoskrnl.exe inline
PatchGuard may detect"] A5["Direct syscall from user-mode
Bypass user-mode hooks
EDR has kernel driver to detect"] A6["DKOM unlink EPROCESS
from PsActiveProcessHead
Hide process"] A7["Load unsigned driver
Requires DSE bypass exploit
Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver"] A8["Bootkit infection of MBR
Pre-OS persistence
Hardest to remediate"] start --> Q1 Q1 -->|Ring 3| A1 Q1 -->|Ring 0 trap| Q2 Q2 -->|Yes PatchGuard| A5 Q2 -->|No PatchGuard| A3 Q3 -->|Yes DSE| A7 Q3 -->|No DSE| A4 A4 --> Q2 A7 --> A8 A6 -.->|volatility plugin
psxview detects| EVD[("Forensic Countermeasure")] A3 -.->|volatility plugin
ssdt detects| EVD A1 -.->|EDR scans ntdll| EVD A8 -.->|MBR hash compare| EVD style start fill:#F5A623,stroke:#7A4F00,color:#FFFFFF style EVD fill:#7ED321,stroke:#3D6B10,color:#FFFFFF style A1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1F3A5F,color:#FFFFFF style A2 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,color:#FFFFFF style A3 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,color:#FFFFFF style A4 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,color:#FFFFFF style A5 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#5C0675,color:#FFFFFF style A6 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#5C0675,color:#FFFFFF style A7 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#5C0675,color:#FFFFFF style A8 fill:#4A4A4A,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Differentiate between Kernel Mode and User Mode. Why is this distinction important for malware forensics?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Kernel Mode (Ring 0) | User Mode (Ring 3) | |---|---|---| | Privilege | Full, unrestricted | Restricted, sandboxed | | Memory access | All physical + virtual memory | Only own virtual address space | | Hardware I/O | Direct via IN/OUT, MMIO | Indirect via drivers | | Failure mode | Crash = system halt / BSOD | Crash = single process termination | **Forensic importance (1 mark):** User-mode malware leaves rich on-disk artifacts (registry, prefetch, $MFT) recoverable by disk forensics. Kernel-mode malware hides itself, hooks core structures, and often leaves only volatile RAM evidence — requiring memory forensics (Volatility) and live-response triage. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is a system call? Explain its role as the controlled gateway between User Mode and Kernel Mode.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A system call is the **only hardware-enforced mechanism** by which user-mode code requests privileged services from the kernel. On x86-64 it is invoked via the `SYSCALL` instruction, which causes the CPU to: 1. Save user context (RSP, RIP, RFLAGS, CS, SS) into MSRs. 2. Switch the stack pointer to the per-thread kernel stack. 3. Atomically flip CPL from 3 to 0. 4. Jump to the OS-registered entry point stored in the `IA32_LSTAR` MSR. The kernel validates every user pointer (guarding against TOCTOU bugs, buffer overflows, and SMEP/SMAP violations) before executing the requested service. **Forensic significance:** malware rootkits often hook either the user-mode ntdll wrapper *or* the kernel-side dispatch table to intercept and redirect these calls invisibly. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Past Pattern]** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the four privilege rings of the x86 architecture. Discuss how Windows and Linux use these rings and which rings are occupied by different categories of malware. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe in detail the System Service Descriptor Table (SSDT) hooking technique used by kernel-mode rootkits. Explain how PatchGuard counters this and list two modern rootkit techniques that bypass PatchGuard. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question A **(a) x86 Privilege Rings (7 marks)** The x86 architecture defines **four hierarchical privilege rings** numbered 0 (most privileged) to 3 (least privileged). The Current Privilege Level (CPL) is stored in the lower 2 bits of the CS register; transitions between rings are controlled by the IDT, GDT, and LDT descriptors. **Ring Assignment in Practice:** | Ring | Intended Use | Windows / Linux Use | |---|---|---| | **Ring 0** | Kernel | Kernel + drivers (combined) | | **Ring 1** | OS services | **Unused** | | **Ring 2** | Device drivers (historically) | **Unused** | | **Ring 3** | Applications | All user applications | **Malware Placement:** - **Ring 3 (most malware):** Trojans, ransomware, worms, droppers, RATs — anything running as a normal `.exe` or `.dll`. - **Ring 0 (advanced malware):** Rootkits, bootkits, some RAT drivers, anti-forensic tools. - **Ring 1/2:** Effectively **unused by malware too**, because mainstream OSes never run code there. **Why only Rings 0 and 3 are used:** Implementing Rings 1/2 would require OS developers to split kernel and drivers, adding complexity for marginal security gain. The microkernel vs. monolithic kernel debate is the academic equivalent of this trade-off. **Diagram (already drawn in Section 4.1) — refer to the four-ring schematic.** --- **(b) SSDT Hooking and PatchGuard Bypass (7 marks)** **SSDT Hooking — Step-by-Step:** 1. The SSDT (`KeServiceDescriptorTable`) is an array of function pointers exported by `ntoskrnl.exe`. Each entry maps a syscall number (e.g., `NtCreateFile = 0x00xx`) to its kernel implementation address. 2. A driver loaded in Ring 0 calls `MmGetSystemRoutineAddress` (or hardcodes the offset) to locate `ntoskrnl!KeServiceDescriptorTable`. 3. It walks the SSDT to find the entry for the target syscall (e.g., `NtQuerySystemInformation`). 4. It overwrites the 4-byte (or 8-byte on x64) pointer with the address of its malicious replacement function. 5. When user-mode code calls that API, the kernel dispatcher jumps to the malicious function, which performs filtering/stealth and optionally calls the original. **Example (conceptual, 32-bit):** $$ \text{Original SSDT entry} \rightarrow \text{Address of legitimate } \texttt{NtQuerySystemInformation} $$ $$ \text{After hook} \rightarrow \text{Address of } \texttt{MyStealthNtQuerySystemInformation} $$ **PatchGuard (Kernel Patch Protection) Counter-Measure:** PatchGuard is a kernel-mode integrity monitor that runs periodically (every few minutes) and validates critical structures: SSDT, IDT, GDT, MSRs (`LSTAR`, `STAR`), global function pointers, and the kernel image's own `.text` section. If any modification is detected, it triggers `KeBugCheckEx` with code `0x109` (CRITICAL_STRUCTURE_CORRUPTION), causing a BSOD. **Two Modern PatchGuard Bypass Techniques:** 1. **Direct Syscall from User-Mode (no SSDT hook needed):** The malware reads the syscall number from `ntdll.dll`, then executes the `SYSCALL` instruction directly. The kernel dispatcher is never bypassed, so PatchGuard sees nothing. EDR vendors counter this by inserting their own kernel driver that inspects every `SYSCALL` source RIP. 2. **Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD):** The malware drops a known-vulnerable but Microsoft-signed driver (e.g., `capcom.sys`, `dbutil_2_3.sys`) into the system and exploits it to gain arbitrary kernel read/write. It then patches PatchGuard out of memory *temporarily* and performs its SSDT hook, restoring PatchGuard after the patch to avoid detection. Microsoft's mitigations: Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity (HVCI) and the vulnerable driver blocklist. --- **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain the role of Model-Specific Registers (MSRs) in x86-64 system call handling. How do `IA32_LSTAR`, `IA32_STAR`, and `IA32_FMASK` coordinate a User→Kernel transition? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A forensic investigator suspects a server is infected with a kernel-mode rootkit. Design a step-by-step memory forensics investigation plan using Volatility. Include at least six indicators of compromise (IOCs) you would look for in kernel structures. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question B **(a) MSR Coordination in x86-64 Syscall (7 marks)** Three Model-Specific Registers orchestrate the fast `SYSCALL` instruction on x86-64: **1. `IA32_STAR` (MSR 0xC0000081):** - Stores two 32-bit CS selectors — bits [47:32] for the kernel CS used after SYSCALL, bits [31:0] for the user CS restored by SYSRET. - Typically kernel CS = 0x10, user CS = 0x23 (or 0x33 for 64-bit user mode). **2. `IA32_LSTAR` (MSR 0xC0000082):** - The **64-bit entry RIP** of the kernel's syscall handler (e.g., `KiSystemCall64`). - When `SYSCALL` executes, the CPU loads RIP from this MSR. - **Forensic hook point:** malware rootkits may overwrite LSTAR to redirect all syscalls to attacker-controlled code. EDR solutions periodically verify LSTAR's integrity. **3. `IA32_FMASK` (MSR 0xC0000084):** - A bitmask specifying which RFLAGS bits to **clear** during the transition (typically the `TF` trap flag and `AC` alignment-check flag, to avoid recursive traps). **Step-by-Step User→Kernel Transition:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & \text{User executes } \texttt{SYSCALL} \text{ with syscall number in RAX, args in RDI, RSI, RDX, R10, R8, R9.} \\ \text{Step 2: } & \text{CPU saves user RIP in RCX and user RFLAGS in R11.} \\ \text{Step 3: } & \text{CPU clears RFLAGS bits according to } \texttt{IA32\_FMASK}. \\ \text{Step 4: } & \text{CPU loads CS from } \texttt{IA32\_STAR[47:32]} \text{ and SS from CS+8.} \\ \text{Step 5: } & \text{CPU loads RIP from } \texttt{IA32\_LSTAR}. \\ \text{Step 6: } & \text{CPL now equals } 0 \text{; ring transition complete.} \end{aligned} $$ **Forensic Countermeasure:** `vol.py --profile=Win10x64 psxview` and the `malfind` plugin can detect user-mode processes performing direct syscalls (because their return RIP is inside `ntdll.dll` for benign, but raw for malicious direct-syscall shellcode). --- **(b) Volatility Investigation Plan for Kernel Rootkit (7 marks)** **Acquisition Phase (one-time, in order of volatility):** 1. Capture live network connections (`netstat -ano`, `Get-NetTCPConnection`). 2. Capture running processes via `tasklist /v` and `wmic`. 3. Capture loaded kernel modules via `driverquery /v /fo csv`. 4. Acquire full memory dump with WinPmem, FTK Imager, or `avml` (output: `memdump.raw`). **Analysis Phase — Six Volatility IOCs:** | # | Volatility Plugin | What It Reveals | IOC Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `pslist` | List of `_EPROCESS` structures | Baseline processes | | 2 | `psxview` | Cross-references 7 process enumeration methods | Process visible to TaskMgr but not `PsActiveProcessHead` = **DKOM hidden rootkit** | | 3 | `modules` | List of `_DRIVER_OBJECT` from `PsLoadedModuleList` | Baseline drivers | | 4 | `driverscan` | Pool-scans for `_DRIVER_OBJECT` tags | Driver in `driverscan` but NOT in `modules` = **unlinked rootkit driver** | | 5 | `ssdt` | Current SSDT entries | SSDT entry pointing outside `ntoskrnl.exe` range = **SSDT hook** | | 6 | `callbacks` | Enumerate kernel notification callbacks (PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, CmRegisterCallback) | Unknown callback addresses = **persistent rootkit hooks** | | 7 | `malfind` | Find RWX user-mode pages with no backing file | Injected shellcode (user-mode stage) | | 8 | `idte` / `gdt` / `ldrmodules` | Examine IDT entries and hidden drivers | Classic ring-0 hooks | **Triangulation Workflow:** $$ \text{Process Suspicion} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{psxview} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{pslist vs. thrdproc vs. pspcid} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Confirm DKOM unlink} $$ $$ \text{Driver Suspicion} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{modules vs. driverscan} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Confirm unlinked driver} $$ **Final Report Deliverables:** driver IRP capture, hash of malicious `.sys` file (extracted via `dumpfiles` plugin), timeline of infection, and recommended IOC signatures for the SIEM. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing CPL and DPL.** CPL describes the *running* code; DPL describes the *target* descriptor. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. > 2. **Writing that Windows uses Rings 0/1/2/3.** It uses **only 0 and 3**. Ring 1 and Ring 2 are *architecturally present* but *never populated*. > 3. **Ignoring PatchGuard / SMEP / SMAP** in rootkit answers. Modern examiners expect these as part of the discussion of *why classic SSDT hooks no longer work*. > 4. **Treating kernel-mode malware as "unremovable."** It is removable, but only via offline disk imaging + driver signing enforcement + HVCI enablement. > 5. **Missing the forensic dichotomy:** user-mode = disk forensics; kernel-mode = volatile memory forensics. This is a guaranteed 2-mark item on most KTU papers. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ x86 has **4 rings**; Windows & Linux use **only Ring 0 (Kernel)** and **Ring 3 (User)**. - ✅ Kernel mode = full hardware privilege; User mode = restricted + hardware-trapped. - ✅ Ring transition is **hardware-enforced** via `SYSCALL`/`SYSENTER`/`INT` gates — never bypassed in legitimate code. - ✅ User-mode malware (95 % of samples) leaves **on-disk forensic artifacts**. - ✅ Kernel-mode malware (rootkits/bootkits) leaves **only volatile RAM artifacts**. - ✅ SSDT, IDT, IRP, and DKOM are the **four classical hook locations**. - ✅ PatchGuard, SMEP, SMAP, KASLR, HVCI are **modern mitigations** that rootkits must bypass. - ✅ Direct syscalls and BYOVD are **2020s-era rootkit techniques**. - ✅ The privilege check formula is **DPL ≥ max(CPL, RPL)** — memorize it. - ✅ x86-64 canonical address hole (`0x0000800000000000` to `0xFFFF7FFFFFFFFFFF`) raises **#GP** if accessed. - ✅ Forensic tools: **Volatility** for memory, **FTK / EnCase / Autopsy** for disk, **Sysinternals** for live triage. - ✅ The single most important takeaway: **you cannot trust user-mode API output on a kernel-rootkit-infected machine** — only direct kernel structure examination is reliable. --- > **End of Module 2 Notes — Kernel vs. User-Mode** > *Aligned with PECCT741 — Malware Forensics, KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020).*

topics.title: Debugging - OllyDbg
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Debugging - OllyDbg

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**OllyDbg** is a 32-bit, *user-mode*, *ring-3* dynamic binary analysis debugger designed for the Microsoft Windows operating system. It combines a **disassembler**, **assembler**, **hexadecimal editor**, and a **debugging engine** into a single Graphical User Interface (GUI), allowing reverse engineers and malware analysts to inspect, control, and manipulate the runtime behaviour of executable files (.exe, .dll, .sys) without requiring source code access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> OllyDbg is the canonical tool listed under *static and dynamic analysis* in the PECCT741 syllabus. Students must understand its registers window, disassembly window, dump window, stack window, and breakpoint mechanism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Forensic Slow-Motion Camera

Imagine a **bank robbery in progress**. The criminal (malware) is moving through rooms (CPU instructions) very quickly, stealing valuables (files, credentials, registry entries). You, the detective (analyst), cannot see what's happening because everything is too fast.

- A **normal video camera** = running the program normally (you see only the final outcome — damage done).
- **OllyDbg** = a **slow-motion forensic camera** that lets you:
  - **Pause** the criminal at any room (breakpoints).
  - **Walk alongside** him step-by-step (single-stepping: F7 / F8).
  - **Look inside** his pockets (registers & memory dump).
  - **Watch his hands** as he picks a lock (API call interception).

OllyDbg gives the analyst **X-ray vision** into the running binary.

## 1.3 Categories of Debuggers — Where OllyDbg Fits

| Debugger Class | Privilege Ring | Examples | Use Case in Malware Forensics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User-Mode (Ring-3)** | Ring 3 | OllyDbg, x64dbg, IDA Pro Debugger | Analysing application-level malware payloads |
| **Kernel-Mode (Ring-0)** | Ring 0 | WinDbg, SoftICE, Syser | Analysing rootkits, drivers, kernel callbacks |
| **Hypervisor / Hardware** | Ring -1 | VMware, VirtualBox, Bochs | Whole-system isolation & behavioural tracing |

> [!NOTE]
> OllyDbg operates strictly in **Ring-3**. It cannot debug kernel-mode drivers directly. For rootkit analysis, analysts pair OllyDbg with a kernel debugger in a virtualised environment.

## 1.4 Why OllyDbg for Malware Forensics?

- **Free, lightweight, and portable** (a single ~5 MB executable — no installation required).
- **No source code dependency** — works on stripped binaries.
- **Plug-in architecture** (e.g., **OllyScript**, **Phantom**, **StrongOD**) enables anti-anti-debugging bypass.
- **Intuitive x86 assembly view** with conditional breakpoints and run-trace logging.

> [!TIP]
> The modern successor is **x64dbg**, but KTU examiners and most legacy malware samples still reference OllyDbg because of its mature plug-in ecosystem and educational familiarity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 OllyDbg Architecture — The Five Logical Layers

1. **Disassembler Engine** — Translates raw `.text` section machine bytes into Intel/AT&T x86 mnemonics using linear-sweep and recursive-traversal heuristics.
2. **Assembler Engine** — Assists in patching instructions directly into the loaded binary in memory (in-place patching).
3. **Debugging Engine (Windows Debug API)** — Wraps the Microsoft `kernel32!DebugActiveProcess`, `ContinueDebugEvent`, `WaitForDebugEvent`, and `GetThreadContext` API calls.
4. **GUI Front-End (TAB-based MDI)** — Hosts the four primary windows: **CPU**, **Dump**, **Stack**, and **Registers/Memory Map**.
5. **Plug-in Manager (Loaddll.exe)** — Loads `.dll` plug-ins that extend functionality (anti-anti-debug, string decryption, etc.).

## 2.2 The Four Core OllyDbg Windows

| Window | Hotkey | Function | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Window** | `F3` (open file) → main view | Disassembly, registers, hex dump side-by-side | Primary analysis pane |
| **Dump Window** | Hex view | Raw memory inspection, patching bytes | Patching IAT hooks, NOP-ing API calls |
| **Stack Window** | `Alt+K` | ESP-traced return addresses & locals | Detecting ROP chains, shellcode staging |
| **Memory Map** | `Alt+M` | Section-wise (`.text`, `.rdata`, `.rsrc`, heap) allocation table | Identifying unpacked PE image regions |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — OllyDbg Operational Reference

| Concept | Shortcut / Notation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Step Into (call) | `F7` | Enter into a `CALL` instruction's callee |
| Step Over (no call) | `F8` | Execute `CALL` as a black box |
| Execute till Return | `Ctrl+F9` | Run to the next `RET` |
| Execute till User Code | `Alt+F9` | Skip out of system DLLs back to malware |
| Set Breakpoint (BP) | `F2` | Toggle an INT3 (`0xCC`) software BP |
| Conditional BP | `Shift+F2` | Pause only if condition is true (e.g., `EAX == 0x41414141`) |
| Hardware BP (on access) | `Delete` in HW BP window | Trap memory reads/writes (anti-INT3 bypass) |
| Run | `F9` | Resume execution |
| Pause | `F12` | Halt the process |
| Comment | `;` | Annotate disassembly for forensic reports |
| Patch | `Ctrl+P` or right-click → Assemble | Modify instruction bytes live in memory |
| Trace Into | `Ctrl+F11` | Single-step logging (Run Trace) |
| Memory Breakpoint | Right-click → Breakpoint → Memory | One-shot access/write guard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metrics to memorise for KTU viva/lab:**
> - Default **INT3 opcode = `0xCC`** (single-byte software breakpoint).
> - x86 **Hardware Breakpoint registers**: DR0–DR3 (address), DR6 (status), DR7 (control). Max **4 hardware BPs** simultaneously.
> - OllyDbg supports **32-bit only**; for x64 binaries use **x64dbg**.

## 2.4 Anti-Debugging Detection via OllyDbg

Malware authors use several techniques to detect/evade OllyDbg. Reverse engineers use OllyDbg's plug-ins to defeat them:

| Anti-Debug Trick | Detection Mechanism | OllyDbg Counter |
|---|---|---|
| `IsDebuggerPresent` | Checks `PEB.BeingDebugged` flag | **Phantom** plug-in spoofs the flag |
| `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent` | `NtQueryInformationProcess` | **StrongOD** + **Phant0m** plug-ins |
| `NtQueryInformationProcess` (DebugPort) | Queries debug port handle | Plug-in hook returns `0` |
| `INT 2D` / `INT 3` tricks | Uses debug exception behaviour | Patch-out the check |
| Timing checks (`RDTSC`) | Measures execution delta | Use **Hide Debugger** plug-in or `rdtsc` filter |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab exams, you are commonly asked to **identify and bypass an `IsDebuggerPresent` call** using OllyDbg's Assemble feature (replace the conditional jump with `NOP`s).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response (IR):** Triaging suspected phishing attachments before detonation.
- **Threat Intelligence:** Extracting C2 (Command & Control) URLs and encryption keys from banking trojans like *Emotet*, *Dridex*, *TrickBot*.
- **CTF Competitions:** Solving reverse-engineering challenges in picoCTF and HTB.
- **Malware Research Labs:** Building YARA rules from unpacked binary signatures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Workflow & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Standard OllyDbg Analysis Pipeline

The following table presents the **seven-phase** procedure most examiners expect to see in a 14-mark answer.

| Phase | Action | OllyDbg Feature | Forensic Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Sample Ingestion | Load `sample.exe` | `File → Open` (`F3`) | Verify PE header |
| 2. Initial Pause | Stop at `EntryPoint` (usually `0x00401000`) | Default behaviour | Confirm EP `push ebp; mov ebp,esp` |
| 3. String Scan | Locate readable ASCII/Unicode | `Right-click → Search for → All Referenced Text Strings` | Find C2 URLs, mutex names |
| 4. API Reference Scan | Locate WinAPI calls | `Right-click → Search for → All Intermodular Calls` | Spot `InternetOpenA`, `CreateFileA` |
| 5. Breakpoint Strategy | Set BP on critical APIs | `F2` on `CreateFileW`, `RegSetValueEx`, `WSAStartup` | Capture parameters at runtime |
| 6. Live Tracing | Step through code | `F7` / `F8` / Run Trace | Capture register/memory mutations |
| 7. Patch & Dump | Fix/extract unpacked code | `Ctrl+P`, then `OllyDumpEx` plug-in | Recover original malware payload |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Resolving an `IsDebuggerPresent` Check

Consider the canonical x86 assembly snippet compiled by MSVC for the call:

```
call    IsDebuggerPresent
test    eax, eax
jne     <anti_debug_block>
```

**Goal:** Identify and neutralise this anti-debugging gate using OllyDbg.

### Step 1 — Locate the API reference

In the **CPU window**, right-click and choose **Search for → All Intermodular Calls**.

```
0x00401030   call    dword ptr [<&KERNEL32.IsDebuggerPresent>]    ; <<< target
0x00401036   test    eax, eax
0x00401038   jne     0x00401080                                  ; jump if debugged
0x0040103A   ...                                                  ; legitimate path
0x00401080   ...                                                  ; anti-debug path
```

### Step 2 — Inspect the API resolution

Double-click the `call` to follow it. OllyDbg reveals:

```
0x7C813093   mov     eax, dword ptr fs:[18h]   ; PEB pointer via TEB
0x7C813099   movzx   eax, byte ptr [eax+2]     ; PEB.BeingDebugged (offset 0x02)
0x7C81309D   ret
```

### Step 3 — Apply OllyDbg patch

Right-click the instruction at `0x00401036` (`test eax, eax`) and select **Assemble**:

```
old:  test    eax, eax
new:  xor     eax, eax        ; force EAX = 0, simulator's "not debugged"
```

Or equivalently, NOP the conditional jump at `0x00401038`:

```
old:  jne     0x00401080
new:  nop                     ; 2-byte NOP = 0x66 0x90 or single 0x90 if 1-byte jne
```

### Step 4 — Apply patch to file

Right-click the patched instruction → **Copy to Executable → All Modifications → Save to File**.

> [!TIP]
> Use the **`Phant0m`** plug-in instead of patching, because patching alters the sample's checksum and may disrupt YARA rule matching. Phant0m preserves original bytes while spoofing the `PEB.BeingDebugged` flag globally.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — Setting a Conditional Breakpoint on `WSAStartup` (Network Detection)

When analysing a trojan's network initialisation:

### Step 1 — Find the import

Search → All Intermodular Calls → locate `WSAStartup`.

### Step 2 — Set Conditional BP

Press `Shift+F2` on the `CALL` line and enter:

```
WS2_32.WSAStartup && [esp+8] != 0
```

OllyDbg halts only if the **lpWSAData** output structure is non-null — typically when Winsock is actually loaded, not just statically imported.

### Step 3 — Run (`F9`) and observe

When the BP fires, examine:

```
EAX = return value (0 = SUCCESS)
EBP = pointer to WSAData struct
Stack:  WORD wVersionRequested    ; desired Winsock version
        LPWSADATA lpWSAData        ; receiver buffer
```

> [!NOTE]
> This conditional breakpoint pattern is a **classic KTU Part-A question** — students must explain when a conditional BP is preferred over an unconditional one.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart Summary

$$
\text{Analysis\_Path} = \begin{cases}
\text{Dynamic (OllyDbg)}, & \text{if sample is packed/obfuscated} \\
\text{Static (strings, PE headers)}, & \text{if sample is plain/known family} \\
\text{Both in sequence}, & \text{for unknown Triage} \\
\text{Sandbox detonation}, & \text{for high-risk samples}
\end{cases}
$$

This conditional logic justifies why OllyDbg is **necessary, not optional**, in modern malware forensics workflows.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OllyDbg Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user[Analyst / Investigator] --> gui[GUI Front-End - Tabbed MDI]
    gui --> cpuWin[CPU Window - Disassembly]
    gui --> dumpWin[Dump Window - Hex View]
    gui --> stackWin[Stack Window - ESP Trace]
    gui --> memMap[Memory Map - Alt plus M]
    gui --> regWin[Registers Window - EAX ECX etc]
    gui --> bpMgr[Breakpoint Manager - F2 Shift F2]
    gui --> plugMgr[Plug-in Loader - Loaddll.exe]
    cpuWin --> disEng[Disassembler Engine]
    dumpWin --> hexEng[Hex Editor Engine]
    plugMgr --> phantomPlug[Phant0m Plug-in]
    plugMgr --> strongODPlug[StrongOD Plug-in]
    plugMgr --> ollyScript[OllyScript Engine]
    bpMgr --> dbgEng[Debugging Engine]
    disEng --> dbgEng
    hexEng --> dbgEng
    dbgEng --> winDebug[Windows Debug API Layer]
    winDebug --> kernel32[kernel32 DebugActiveProcess]
    winDebug --> ntdll[ntdll NtContinue]
    winDebug --> proc[Target Malware Process - Ring 3]
    proc -. EXCEPTION_DEBUG_EVENT .-> winDebug
    proc -. memory read/write .-> memMap
    proc -. register state .-> regWin
```

## 4.2 Malware Analysis Workflow Using OllyDbg

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acquire Sample - SHA256 hash] --> B[Stage in Isolated VM]
    B --> C[Verify PE Header - PE-bear]
    C --> D{Packed?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Set BP on EntryPoint]
    D -- No --> F[String Scan via OllyDbg]
