    E --> G[Step Through F8 to find OEP]
    G --> H[Dump Unpacked Code - OllyDumpEx]
    H --> F
    F --> I[API Reference Scan]
    I --> J[Set Strategic Breakpoints]
    J --> K[Execute F9 - Pause at BP]
    K --> L[Inspect Registers and Stack]
    L --> M{Anti-Debug Detected?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Apply Patch or Phant0m Plug-in]
    N --> J
    M -- No --> O[Document API Parameters]
    O --> P[Extract IOC - URLs, Mutex, Registry]
    P --> Q[Generate Forensic Report]
```

## 4.3 Software Breakpoint Mechanism — INT3 Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Analyst
    participant OllyDbg
    participant Process as Malware Process
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    Analyst->>OllyDbg: Press F2 at address 0x00401030
    OllyDbg->>Process: Read original byte (e.g., 0x55 = push ebp)
    OllyDbg->>Process: Write 0xCC at 0x00401030
    Analyst->>OllyDbg: Press F9 to Run
    OllyDbg->>CPU: ContinueDebugEvent
    CPU->>Process: Execute 0xCC
    Process->>CPU: EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT
    CPU->>OllyDbg: WaitForDebugEvent callback
    OllyDbg->>Process: Restore original byte (0x55)
    OllyDbg->>Analyst: Display paused state in CPU window
    Analyst->>OllyDbg: F7 or F8 to step
```

> [!NOTE]
> This `0xCC` mechanism is exactly why malware authors compute **CRC checksums** over their `.text` section — to detect any byte modifications like `0xCC` insertion by a debugger.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Explain the role of OllyDbg in malware forensics and list any four of its primary analysis windows.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

OllyDbg is a 32-bit **user-mode ring-3 debugger** used by malware forensic analysts to perform **dynamic analysis** of Windows executables without source code. It provides an integrated environment combining a disassembler, debugger engine, and hex editor to inspect the runtime behaviour of suspicious binaries.

The four primary windows are:

1. **CPU Window** – Shows disassembled x86 instructions, register values, and a hex dump side-by-side. (1 mark)
2. **Dump Window** – Displays raw memory contents for inspection and patching. (0.5 mark)
3. **Stack Window** – Traces the ESP stack pointer, showing return addresses and local variables. (0.5 mark)
4. **Memory Map** – Lists all allocated sections (`.text`, `.rdata`, heap, stack) with base addresses and protections. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is the difference between a Software Breakpoint (`F2`) and a Hardware Breakpoint in OllyDbg? Mention the opcode for the software breakpoint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Software BP | Hardware BP |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Writes `0xCC` to the target byte | Uses CPU debug registers DR0–DR3 |
| Limit | Unlimited | Max 4 simultaneously |
| Anti-Debug Resistance | Easily detected by CRC checksums | Not detected by checksum scans |
| Use Case | Quick, plentiful pausing | Circumventing self-checksumming malware |

The **opcode for the software breakpoint is `0xCC`** (the `INT3` instruction). (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe the architecture of OllyDbg. Explain any **three** plug-ins used to defeat anti-debugging techniques in malware. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat workflow diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure to **dynamically unpack a packed PE executable** using OllyDbg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — OllyDbg Architecture and Anti-Debug Plug-ins

**Architecture (4 marks):**

OllyDbg consists of the following five logical layers:

1. **GUI Front-End** — Tab-based MDI providing four core windows: CPU, Dump, Stack, and Memory Map.
2. **Disassembler Engine** — Converts raw machine bytes in the `.text` section into x86 mnemonics using linear-sweep and recursive-traversal heuristics.
3. **Assembler Engine** — Allows in-place patching of instructions directly in memory through the **Assemble** dialog (`Space` key).
4. **Debugging Engine** — Wraps the Windows Debug API (`DebugActiveProcess`, `ContinueDebugEvent`, `WaitForDebugEvent`, `GetThreadContext`, `SetThreadContext`).
5. **Plug-in Manager** — Loads `.dll` plug-ins via `loaddll.exe` to extend capabilities such as anti-anti-debugging, tracing, and unpacking.

**Three Anti-Debug Plug-ins (3 marks):**

| Plug-in | Defeats | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Phant0m** | `IsDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess` | Continuously clears the `PEB.BeingDebugged` flag and removes debug heap flags |
| **StrongOD** | `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent`, output-debug-string tricks | Hooks relevant API calls and spoofs return values |
| **HideOD** | Detection by window enumeration / process name scan | Hides OllyDbg window titles and renames process to a benign string |

#### Model Solution (b) — Step-by-Step Packed Executable Unpacking

**Step 1 — Acquire and Verify the Sample** (1 mark)
Open the sample in OllyDbg via `F3`. Confirm it has an unusual entry-point (often pointing into the `.text` section but with a small stub).

**Step 2 — Identify Packer Signatures** (1 mark)
Run **PEiD** or **Detect It Easy (DIE)** to confirm packer type (UPX, ASPack, Themida, etc.). OllyDbg will show very few readable strings, confirming packing.

**Step 3 — Set Strategic Breakpoints** (1 mark)
Set a BP on `GetProcAddress` and `LoadLibraryA`. Packers call these to rebuild the Import Address Table (IAT).

**Step 4 — Single-Step to OEP** (2 marks)
Press `F8` (step over) repeatedly. Watch for a long `jmp` (far jump) typically resembling `jmp eax` or a jump to a high address in the `.text` section — this is the **Original Entry Point (OEP)**. Set an F2 breakpoint here.

**Step 5 — Verify Unpacking** (1 mark)
Right-click → **Search for → All Intermodular Calls**. If real WinAPI names appear, the packer has finished resolving imports — you are now at OEP.

**Step 6 — Dump with OllyDumpEx** (1 mark)
Use the **OllyDumpEx** plug-in: `Plugins → OllyDumpEx → Dump Debugged Process`. Uncheck "Rebuild Import" if you wish to manually fix the IAT later. Save as `sample_unpacked.exe`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Students often lose 2–3 marks here because:**
> 1. They forget to set the BP on `GetProcAddress` and instead step blindly for thousands of instructions — leading to timeout.
> 2. They use OllyDbg's built-in dump instead of **OllyDumpEx**, which fails to handle large IATs and results in a non-functional unpacked binary.
> 3. They do not re-calculate the **EntryPoint RVA** in the dumped PE header — the dumped binary will crash on launch.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Run Trace** in OllyDbg. How does it help in detecting **self-modifying code**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Step Into (`F7`)** vs **Step Over (`F8`)**. Demonstrate, using assembly examples, how each behaves on a `CALL` instruction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Run Trace & Self-Modifying Code Detection

**Definition (2 marks):**
Run Trace (`Ctrl+F11` to trace into, `Ctrl+F12` to trace over) is a feature that **logs every executed instruction** along with the register/memory state at that point into a buffer. The trace log can be viewed as a table or saved to disk for offline analysis.

**How it detects self-modifying code (5 marks):**

Malware often decrypts its own code at runtime (a technique called **decryption stub execution**). Run Trace helps because:

1. **Initial dump** — The disassembler shows gibberish (encrypted bytes) at the start of tracing.
2. **Mid-trace divergence** — As the trace progresses, the actual disassembly may **change** in-place when the malware writes decrypted bytes via `MOV [EAX], BL` loops.
3. **Snapshot inspection** — By comparing the **CPU window state at trace step N** versus **trace step N+K**, the analyst observes that bytes at the same address have altered values.
4. **Repetition detection** — The trace log reveals an unusually long loop (high iteration count) — a hallmark of an XOR decryption routine.
5. **Forensic value** — Run Trace generates a deterministic record admissible as evidence, since each step can be replayed.

> [!TIP]
> Always combine Run Trace with the **Hardware Breakpoint on Write** to catch the *exact* moment a byte is overwritten by the malware's decryption stub.

#### Model Solution (b) — Step Into vs Step Over

**Conceptual Distinction (3 marks):**

- **Step Into (`F7`)** — Executes *one* instruction and enters the target of any `CALL`, `INT`, or `REP`-prefixed instruction.
- **Step Over (`F8`)** — Executes *one* instruction but treats `CALL` as a black box, never entering the callee.

**Assembly Demonstration (4 marks):**

```
0x00402000   push    ebp
0x00402001   mov     ebp, esp
0x00402003   call    0x00402100          ; function prologue
0x00402008   add     eax, 0x05
0x0040200B   call    kernel32!IsDebuggerPresent
0x00402010   test    eax, eax
0x00402012   ret
```

| Step | Keyboard | Action After `CALL 0x00402100` |
|---|---|---|
| `F7` | Step Into | EIP jumps to `0x00402100`; analyst sees the callee's instructions |
| `F8` | Step Over | Callee executes invisibly; EIP resumes at `0x00402008` |

For `call kernel32!IsDebuggerPresent`, you would almost always use `F8` because you do **not** want to trace into the Windows system DLL — you'd use `Alt+F9` to exit out of the system DLL instead.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistakes in 14-mark answers:**
> 1. **Confusing F7/F8** — Writing "`F7` skips calls" is a fatal error worth 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting `Alt+F9`** — Students step through thousands of instructions inside `ntdll.dll` and run out of time.
> 3. **Not mentioning Run Trace limitations** — Run Trace fills memory fast; on a long sample, the buffer can overflow and silently truncate.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for OllyDbg (PECCT741 — Module 2)**

- ☐ OllyDbg is a **32-bit user-mode ring-3 debugger** for Windows; for 64-bit, use **x64dbg**.
- ☐ The four core windows are **CPU, Dump, Stack, Memory Map** (`Alt+M`).
- ☐ **Software BP opcode = `0xCC`** (INT3); toggled via `F2`.
- ☐ **Hardware BPs** use CPU debug registers DR0–DR3 (max 4) and bypass CRC checksumming.
- ☐ **Step Into = `F7`** (enter callee), **Step Over = `F8`** (skip callee).
- ☐ **Run Trace = `Ctrl+F11`** — logs every instruction for self-modifying-code analysis.
- ☐ **Anti-debug bypasses:** `IsDebuggerPresent` checks `PEB.BeingDebugged` (offset `0x02`).
- ☐ **Plug-ins:** Phant0m, StrongOD, HideOD defeat anti-debugging; OllyDumpEx performs unpacking.
- ☐ Packed samples show **few strings** at entry; **OEP is found** by setting BP on `GetProcAddress`.
- ☐ Conditional BP syntax: `Shift+F2` → expression like `[esp+8] != 0` or symbolic API names.
- ☐ Always run OllyDbg inside a **virtual machine** (VMware/VirtualBox) with **snapshots** before analysis.
- ☐ For lab exams, be ready to: (i) locate the EP, (ii) find WinAPI calls, (iii) set F2 BPs, (iv) extract IOCs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breakpoints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breakpoints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛑 BREAKPOINTS — Module 2: Malware Forensics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **breakpoint** is a deliberate, controlled interruption point intentionally inserted (or hardware-registered) into the execution flow of a binary/process by a debugger, allowing a reverse engineer or malware analyst to **pause, inspect, and modify the runtime state** (registers, memory, flags, stack) of a target program at a precise instruction boundary.

In the context of **Malware Forensics**, breakpoints are the single most important operational tool in dynamic analysis. They allow an analyst to intercept the malware at critical moments such as:
- Before a **decryption routine** runs (to catch plaintext payloads).
- At **API call sites** (e.g., `CreateRemoteThread`, `WriteFile`, `InternetOpenUrlA`).
- At **anti-debug checks** (`IsDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess`).
- Inside **packing / unpacking stubs**.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Traffic Police Checkpost"

Imagine a long highway (the **CPU instruction stream**). A malware analyst is a **traffic police officer** who needs to inspect suspicious vehicles (the **malware's runtime state**).

- The officer cannot chase every car — it's too fast.
- So the officer sets up a **checkpost** (the **breakpoint**) at a known kilometer marker.
- Every car that crosses is **forced to stop** and present its documents (CPU **registers**, **memory contents**, **stack frame**).
- The officer can then either **let it go** (continue execution) or **detain** (step through / modify state).

Without checkposts (breakpoints), the officer is blind to what each vehicle carries.

### Key Terminology Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU 2024 Scheme (must be memorized verbatim):**
> - **INT 3 (0xCC):** The single-byte x86 software breakpoint instruction (opcode `\xCC`).
> - **Debug Exception (#BP):** Vector 3 interrupt generated by INT 3 / hardware breakpoints.
> - **Trap Flag (TF):** Bit 8 of the `EFLAGS` register, used for single-stepping.
> - **Debug Registers:** CPU registers `DR0`–`DR7` controlling hardware breakpoints.
> - **Page-Guard Breakpoint:** Memory breakpoint using the `PAGE_GUARD` protection flag (Windows `VirtualProtect`).
> - **Conditional Breakpoint:** Breakpoint that fires only when a user-defined Boolean expression evaluates to `true`.

### Standard Metrics in Breakpoint Engineering

The following numerical constants are **hardware-imposed** and must be committed to memory:

| Metric | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum hardware breakpoints (x86/x64) | **4** | Limited by debug registers `DR0`–`DR3` |
| INT 3 opcode size | **1 byte** | Overwrites the **first** byte of the target instruction |
| INT 3 opcode hex value | **0xCC** | Standard x86 software breakpoint opcode |
| Debug exception vector | **3** | Interrupt vector number for `#BP` |
| Trap Flag bit position in EFLAGS | **bit 8** | Used for single-stepping (vector 1, `#DB`) |
| x86 standard page size | **4096 bytes (4 KiB)** | Granularity of memory (page-guard) breakpoints |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Breakpoints

Breakpoints in malware analysis fall into **four principal classes**, each with distinct mechanisms, advantages, and forensic consequences.

### 2.1.1 Software (INT 3) Breakpoints

**Mechanism (The "Why"):**
- The debugger writes a single byte `0xCC` at the **first byte** of the target instruction in the **code section** of the process.
- When the CPU's instruction pointer (`EIP` / `RIP`) reaches that byte, the CPU decodes `0xCC` as the `INT 3` instruction and immediately raises a **debug exception (vector 3, #BP)**.
- The exception is delivered to the debugger, which gains control and can read/modify any register or memory location.
- The debugger must **restore the original byte** before resuming execution, then re-insert it on the next pause.

**Operational Steps (The "How"):**
1. Analyst clicks on a line in the disassembly view.
2. Debugger computes the **linear virtual address** of that instruction.
3. Debugger saves the original byte (e.g., `0x55` for `PUSH EBP`).
4. Debugger writes `0xCC` to that address using `WriteProcessMemory()`.
5. CPU fetches `0xCC` → raises `#BP` → control transfers to debugger.
6. Debugger restores original byte, single-steps **one instruction**, then re-inserts `0xCC`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Limitation:** Software breakpoints **modify the target's code section**. Sophisticated malware uses **integrity checksums (CRC32)** on its own code to detect this tampering. A defender must know this — otherwise the malware simply **stops running** or **erases itself** when the checksum fails.

### 2.1.2 Hardware Breakpoints

**Mechanism:**
- x86 CPUs provide **8 debug registers**: `DR0`–`DR7`.
- `DR0`–`DR3` store the **linear addresses** of up to 4 breakpoint locations.
- `DR7` (Debug Control Register) configures:
  - **L0–L3** local enable bits
  - **G0–G3** global enable bits
  - **RW0–RW3** condition bits (`00` = execute, `01` = write, `10` = I/O read/write, `11` = read/write)
  - **LEN0–LEN3** length bits (1, 2, or 4 bytes)

**Key Advantage:** Hardware breakpoints do **not modify code** — they are nearly invisible to integrity-checking malware. This makes them the **forensic analyst's primary weapon** against packed / self-checking malware.

### 2.1.3 Conditional Breakpoints

A conditional breakpoint is **not a new mechanism** — it is a **software or hardware breakpoint augmented with a Boolean predicate** evaluated by the debugger at the moment of exception delivery.

If the predicate returns `false`, the debugger silently **restores the original byte, single-steps one instruction, re-inserts the breakpoint, and continues** — completely transparent to the malware.

This causes a **massive slowdown** (typically 1000× to 100000×), but is irreplaceable for breaking inside loops that execute millions of times (e.g., a decryption loop running over a 10 MB buffer).

### 2.1.4 Memory (Page-Guard) Breakpoints

**Mechanism:**
- The Windows `VirtualProtect()` API can set a page's protection to `PAGE_GUARD` (`0x100`).
- Any code execution, read, or write touching that page generates a `STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION` exception (`0x80000001`).
- The debugger catches the exception, inspects the faulting address, then calls `VirtualProtect()` again to **remove** the guard bit before continuing.

**Critical Limit:** Page-guard breakpoints operate at **page granularity (4096 bytes)**, so they fire on **any access** within the 4 KiB page — useful for detecting when *any* code in a region is read, but imprecise.

## 2.2 The Single-Stepping Mechanism (Trap Flag)

To execute *exactly one* instruction after restoring a software breakpoint's original byte, the debugger sets the **Trap Flag (TF, bit 8)** in the `EFLAGS` register.

After the single instruction completes, the CPU raises a **debug exception (vector 1, #DB)**. The debugger catches it, clears TF, and re-inserts the `0xCC` byte. This two-step dance is the heart of every software breakpoint.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Debug Trick:** Some malware explicitly **pokes `EFLAGS`** or runs code inside a **Single-Step Interrupt Service Routine** to detect debuggers. Knowledge of the Trap Flag is therefore essential for the analyst.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Linear address of INT 3 | $L = \text{SegmentBase} + \text{Offset}$ | Address where `0xCC` is written |
| Memory modified by software BP | $\Delta M = 1 \text{ byte} = 0xCC$ | Code-section byte overwritten |
| Memory modified by hardware BP | $\Delta M = 0$ bytes | Code untouched — stealthy |
| Max hardware BPs | $N_{\max} = 4$ | Architectural limit (DR0–DR3) |
| Page size (granularity of memory BP) | $S_{\text{page}} = 4\,\text{KiB} = 4096$ bytes | `PAGE_GUARD` resolution |
| Condition bits (DR7) encoding | $RW \in \{00,01,10,11\}$ | 00=Ex, 01=Wr, 11=Rd/Wr |
| Length bits (DR7) encoding | $LEN \in \{00,01,10,11\}$ | 1, 2, 4, or 8 bytes |
| Conditional BP slowdown factor | $S \approx \dfrac{T_{\text{exception}}}{T_{\text{instruction}}}$ | Typically $10^3$ to $10^5$ |
| Debug exception vector (INT 3) | $V_{BP} = 3$ | `#BP` in IDT |
| Single-step exception vector | $V_{DB} = 1$ | `#DB` from TF in EFLAGS |
| EFLAGS TF bit position | $\text{bit}(TF) = 8$ | Bit-mask $0x100$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Breakpoints |
|---|---|
| **Anti-Virus Labs** | Trace ransomware's file-encryption loop, intercept encryption key in memory. |
| **Incident Response** | Set BP on `CreateFileW` to identify what files a backdoor reads at runtime. |
| **Exploit Development** | Hardware BP on return address to catch ROP gadget execution. |
| **Malware Unpacking** | BPs on `VirtualAlloc` / `VirtualFree` to find unpacked payload region. |
| **CTF / Forensic Challenges** | Conditional BP on a counter to solve self-modifying code challenges. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Breakpoint Type

A malware analyst must choose the correct breakpoint type based on the target's defensive posture. The decision matrix below is **examination-grade material** for KTU 2024.

| Scenario | Anti-Debug Present? | Code Integrity Checksum? | Recommended BP | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plain malware, no defenses | No | No | Software BP (`INT 3`) | Fastest, unlimited count. |
| Packed malware | No | Yes (on `.text` section) | **Hardware BP** | Avoids code modification. |
| Malware with anti-debug APIs | Yes | Mixed | **Hardware BP** + patch anti-debug | Anti-debug BPs themselves need hardware. |
| Decryption loop (10M iterations) | Any | Any | **Conditional BP** | Avoids manual stepping. |
| Monitor data-section access | Any | Any | **Memory (PAGE_GUARD) BP** | Page-level granularity, no code touched. |
| Tracing every instruction | Any | Any | **Trace (TF-based)** | Single-stepping for full trace logs. |

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Linear Address Calculation for INT 3

In 32-bit x86, the CPU computes the **linear address** of an instruction as:

$$
L = \text{SegmentBase}_{\text{CS}} + \text{EIP}
$$

In modern Windows (Flat Memory Model, 64-bit long mode):

$$
L = \text{EIP} \quad \text{(CS base = 0)}
$$

**Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical):**
Suppose `EIP = 0x00401F3A` and the original byte at that address is `0x55` (the opcode for `PUSH EBP`).

**Step 1:** Compute the linear address.
$$
L = 0x00000000 + 0x00401F3A = 0x00401F3A
$$

**Step 2:** The debugger writes `0xCC` to address `0x00401F3A`. The memory now contains:
$$
M[0x00401F3A] = 0xCC \quad \text{(was 0x55)}
$$

**Step 3:** CPU decodes `0xCC` as `INT 3` → raises `#BP` (vector 3).

**Step 4:** Debugger's exception handler runs. It must:
- Read the saved `EIP` from the trap frame: $EIP_{\text{saved}} = 0x00401F3A$
- Decrement `EIP` by 1 (since `INT 3` is a 1-byte instruction, and the CPU has already advanced the instruction pointer):
$$
EIP_{\text{rewound}} = EIP_{\text{saved}} - 1 = 0x00401F3A - 0x1 = 0x00401F39
$$

**Step 5:** Restore the original byte:
$$
M[0x00401F3A] = 0x55 \quad \text{(back to PUSH EBP)}
$$

**Step 6:** Set TF in `EFLAGS`, set `EIP = 0x00401F3A` (the rewound address), and resume. CPU executes the real `PUSH EBP` → raises `#DB` (vector 1) → debugger clears TF and re-inserts `0xCC` at `0x00401F3A`.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Memory Page-Guard Breakpoint Range

Memory breakpoints are **page-aligned** and **page-granular**. If the analyst wants to monitor a buffer at address `A` with length `L`, the number of pages spanned is:

$$
N_{\text{pages}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{(A \bmod S_{\text{page}}) + L}{S_{\text{page}}} \right\rceil
$$

where $S_{\text{page}} = 4096$.

**Worked Example:**
Suppose we want to guard a data section at $A = 0x00406000$ with $L = 32$ bytes.

**Step 1:** Compute the page offset of `A`:
$$
O_A = A \bmod 4096 = 0x00406000 \bmod 4096 = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Compute pages spanned:
$$
N_{\text{pages}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{0 + 32}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 0.00781 \right\rceil = 1
$$

Only **1 page** needs `PAGE_GUARD` set.

**Another Example:** $A = 0x00406FF0$, $L = 256$ bytes.

**Step 1:** Page offset:
$$
O_A = 0x00406FF0 \bmod 4096 = 0xFF0 = 4080
$$

**Step 2:** Pages spanned:
$$
N_{\text{pages}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{4080 + 256}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{4336}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.0586 \right\rceil = 2
$$

So **2 pages** (`0x00406000`–`0x00406FFF` and `0x00407000`–`0x00407FFF`) must be guarded.

## 3.4 Hardware Breakpoint Address Encoding

For a hardware breakpoint on a 4-byte write at address `A`:

$$
DR_i = A \quad (i \in \{0,1,2,3\})
$$

$$
DR7.RW_i = 01 \quad \text{(write access)}
$$

$$
DR7.LEN_i = 11 \quad \text{(4-byte length)}
$$

The CPU raises `#BP` whenever a memory write falls within:

$$
\text{Match} \iff A \le \text{phys\_addr} < A + 2^{LEN_i+1}
$$

For $LEN_i = 11$ (binary), the watched range is:

$$
A \le \text{phys\_addr} < A + 2^{3+1} = A + 16 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows why a single hardware breakpoint can monitor a contiguous 16-byte region for writes, but cannot cross a 16-byte alignment boundary on older CPUs.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table (Exhaustive)

| Breakpoint Type | Code Modified? | Max Count | Speed Penalty | Detectable by Malware? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Software (`INT 3`) | Yes (1 byte) | Unlimited | Negligible | **Yes** (CRC check) |
| Hardware (`DR0–DR3`) | No | 4 | Negligible | Hard (some PHE) |
| Conditional | Yes (1 byte) | Unlimited | High ($10^3$–$10^5\times$) | **Yes** (CRC) + slow |
| Memory (Page-Guard) | No | OS-limited by memory | Moderate | **Yes** (page-flag read) |
| Single-Step (TF) | No | Unlimited | High (full trace) | **Yes** (TF poke) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Software Breakpoint (INT 3) Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analyst clicks instruction in disassembly] --> B[Debugger computes linear address L]
    B --> C[Read original byte from M of L]
    C --> D[Write 0xCC to M of L]
    D --> E[CPU fetches byte at EIP]
    E --> F{Is byte equal to 0xCC?}
    F -- Yes --> G[CPU raises debug exception vector 3 #BP]
    G --> H[OS dispatches to debugger]
    H --> I[Debugger reads trap frame, rewinds EIP by 1]
    I --> J[Restore original byte to M of L]
    J --> K[Set TF bit in EFLAGS]
    K --> L[Resume execution of single instruction]
    L --> M[CPU raises debug exception vector 1 #DB]
    M --> N[Debugger clears TF in EFLAGS]
    N --> O[Re-insert 0xCC at M of L]
    O --> P[Continue normal execution]
    F -- No --> Q[Normal instruction execution]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Hardware Breakpoint Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Analyst selects hardware BP type] --> H2{Choose condition}
    H2 -- Execute --> H3[Set DR7 RW to 00]
    H2 -- Write --> H4[Set DR7 RW to 01]
    H2 -- Read or Write --> H5[Set DR7 RW to 11]
    H3 --> H6[Load target linear address into DR0]
    H4 --> H6
    H5 --> H6
    H6 --> H7[Set LEN bits in DR7 for range]
    H7 --> H8[Set L0 local enable bit in DR7]
    H8 --> H9[CPU monitors bus on every memory access]
    H9 --> H10{Does access match DR0 condition?}
    H10 -- Yes --> H11[CPU raises debug exception vector 1 #DB]
    H11 --> H12[Debugger inspects state at match]
    H10 -- No --> H13[Continue monitoring]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Block-Level Architecture of a Debugger with Breakpoint Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANALYST_UI[Analyst Interface Layer]
        UI1[Disassembly View]
        UI2[Register View]
        UI3[Memory Dump View]
        UI4[Breakpoint Manager]
    end
    subgraph DEBUG_ENGINE[Debug Engine Core]
        E1[Symbol Resolver]
        E2[Breakpoint Table]
        E3[INT 3 Installer]
        E4[Hardware BP Configurator]
        E5[Trap Flag Controller]
        E6[Exception Dispatcher]
    end
    subgraph TARGET[Target Malware Process]
        T1[Code Section]
        T2[Data Section]
        T3[CPU Registers and Flags]
        T4[Page Tables]
    end
    UI4 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> T1
    E4 --> T3
    E5 --> T3
    T1 -- exception vector 3 --> E6
    T3 -- exception vector 1 --> E6
    E6 --> UI1
    E6 --> UI2
    E6 --> UI3
    E6 --> E3
    E6 --> E4
    E6 --> E5
    T2 -. page-guard .-> E6
    T4 -. page-guard .-> E6
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Forensic Decision Flow for Selecting a Breakpoint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Target binary identified] --> D2{Code integrity checksum active?}
    D2 -- No --> D3{Anti-debug API calls present?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D4[Use hardware breakpoint DR0 to DR3]
    D3 -- No --> D5[Use software breakpoint INT 3]
    D3 -- Yes --> D4
    D4 --> D6{Loop iterations greater than 10000?}
    D6 -- Yes --> D7[Add conditional predicate to BP]
    D6 -- No --> D8[Proceed with hardware BP]
    D5 --> D9{Trace full execution required?}
    D9 -- Yes --> D10[Enable Trap Flag single-stepping]
    D9 -- No --> D11[Continue with software BP]
    D7 --> D12[Anomaly resolved]
    D8 --> D12
    D10 --> D12
    D11 --> D12
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a software breakpoint. Explain how the `INT 3` instruction is used to implement it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **software breakpoint** is a debugging mechanism implemented by overwriting the first byte of a target instruction in the code section of a process with the single-byte opcode `0xCC` (the `INT 3` instruction). When the CPU's instruction pointer reaches this byte, it decodes `0xCC` as `INT 3` and raises a **debug exception (vector 3, #BP)**. The debugger catches this exception, gains control of the process, inspects/modifies the runtime state, and before resuming execution, **restores the original byte** and sets the **Trap Flag (TF)** in `EFLAGS` to single-step one instruction, then re-inserts `0xCC` for the next pause. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between hardware and software breakpoints? Why are hardware breakpoints preferred against self-checking malware?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Software breakpoints** modify the target's code section by writing `0xCC` (1 mark).
- **Hardware breakpoints** use CPU debug registers `DR0`–`DR3` to monitor memory addresses and `DR7` to configure the condition, **without modifying code** (1 mark).
- Self-checking malware computes a checksum (e.g., CRC32) over its own `.text` section to detect tampering. Since hardware breakpoints leave the code unchanged, the checksum remains valid, allowing the analyst to debug the malware without triggering its self-defense. Software breakpoints would be detected and cause the malware to terminate. (1 mark)

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose marks by: (1) confusing the **debug exception vectors** (vector 1 for `#DB` from TF vs. vector 3 for `#BP` from `INT 3`); (2) forgetting that **EIP must be decremented by 1** after an `INT 3` exception; (3) not stating that the **maximum number of hardware breakpoints is 4** on x86; (4) omitting the role of the **Trap Flag** in the re-insertion step. Memorize these specifically.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **internal mechanism of an `INT 3` software breakpoint**, including the role of the debug exception, the Trap Flag, and the exception handler. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **hardware breakpoints**, their configuration via the debug registers `DR0`–`DR7`, and the meaning of the `RW` and `LEN` fields. Why are only **four** hardware breakpoints possible? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Selection and Patch (2 marks):** The analyst selects a target instruction in the disassembly view. The debugger computes the linear virtual address `L` of the instruction's first byte and reads the original byte `B_orig` from memory. It then writes `0xCC` to that address, transforming the original instruction into an `INT 3`.

2. **Exception Trigger (2 marks):** When the CPU's `EIP` reaches address `L`, the instruction decoder interprets `0xCC` as `INT 3` (a 1-byte instruction that raises interrupt vector 3). The CPU pushes the current `EIP`, `CS`, and `EFLAGS` onto the stack, then dispatches the `#BP` exception to the registered debug exception handler. The debugger receives control.

3. **Rewind and Restore (2 marks):** The debugger reads the saved `EIP` from the exception frame and **decrements it by 1** (since `INT 3` is only 1 byte long and the CPU has already advanced past it). It then writes `B_orig` back to address `L`, restoring the original instruction.

4. **Single-Step and Re-insert (1 mark):** To safely execute the restored original instruction without re-triggering the breakpoint, the debugger sets the **Trap Flag (bit 8 of EFLAGS)**. After one instruction executes, the CPU raises `#DB` (vector 1), the debugger clears TF, and re-inserts `0xCC` at `L`. Execution continues normally until the next pause.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Debug Register Layout (3 marks):** The x86 architecture provides 8 debug registers, `DR0`–`DR7`. `DR0`–`DR3` store the **linear addresses** of up to 4 breakpoint locations. `DR4` and `DR5` are reserved (aliased to `DR6`/`DR7` on some models). `DR6` is the **Debug Status Register**, indicating *which* breakpoint fired. `DR7` is the **Debug Control Register**, configuring the breakpoint behavior.

2. **Configuration Fields in DR7 (2 marks):** For each of the 4 breakpoint slots, `DR7` contains:
   - `L_i` (Local Enable) and `G_i` (Global Enable) bits — turn the breakpoint on for the current task or all tasks.
   - `RW_i` (2 bits) — condition: `00` = execute, `01` = write, `10` = I/O, `11` = read/write.
   - `LEN_i` (2 bits) — length of watched region: `00` = 1 byte, `01` = 2 bytes, `11` = 4 bytes.

3. **Why Exactly Four (2 marks):** The x86 ISA provides **only four address registers (`DR0`–`DR3`)** for breakpoint targets. Each address register can be configured independently, but there is no architectural slot for a fifth. Thus, **N_max = 4 hardware breakpoints** is a hard architectural limit. Analysts must fall back to software or memory breakpoints when more are needed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What are **conditional breakpoints**? Explain how they are implemented and why they cause a significant performance slowdown. Provide a numerical example of the slowdown. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **memory (page-guard) breakpoints** using the `VirtualProtect` API and the `PAGE_GUARD` flag. Calculate the number of guard pages required to monitor a buffer of **512 bytes** starting at the linear address **`0x00406A00`**. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** A **conditional breakpoint** is a software or hardware breakpoint augmented with a Boolean predicate that the debugger evaluates at the moment the breakpoint fires. The breakpoint pauses execution only if the predicate returns `true`; otherwise the debugger silently resumes execution.

2. **Implementation (2 marks):** At the time of the exception, the debugger's handler evaluates the user-supplied expression (e.g., `ECX == 0x1000` or `*(DWORD*)EBP == 0xDEADBEEF`). If `true`, the debugger stops and presents state to the analyst. If `false`, the debugger restores the original byte, single-steps one instruction, re-inserts `0xCC`, and resumes — all without UI interaction.

3. **Performance Slowdown (2 marks):** Every false evaluation still pays the cost of the full exception dispatch, trap-frame save/restore, predicate evaluation, single-step, and re-insertion. This cost is typically **1,000 to 100,000 times** the cost of a normal instruction. Numerical example: a loop of $10^7$ iterations with one BP fires $10^7$ exceptions. Each exception costs $\approx 50$ µs, so the loop takes $10^7 \times 50\,\mu s = 500$ seconds, compared to perhaps 0.05 s without the BP — a slowdown of $10{,}000\times$.

4. **Forensic Use (1 mark):** Conditional BPs are indispensable for breaking inside long loops (e.g., ransomware encryption loops) at the **specific iteration** where the cryptographic key is finally assembled.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Mechanism of Page-Guard Breakpoints (3 marks):** Windows allows setting page protection flags via `VirtualProtect(LPVOID lpAddress, SIZE_T dwSize, DWORD flNewProtect, PDWORD lpflOldProtect)`. By OR-ing in the flag `PAGE_GUARD` (value `0x100`), the OS marks the page(s) as guarded. The first access (read, write, or execute) to any byte in the guarded page raises a `STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION` exception (`0x80000001`), which the debugger catches. The debugger inspects the faulting address in the exception record, then calls `VirtualProtect` again to **clear the guard flag** before resuming.

2. **Granularity (1 mark):** The page size is $S_{\text{page}} = 4096$ bytes. The guard flag is applied to whole pages.

3. **Calculation (3 marks):** Given $A = 0x00406A00$, $L = 512$ bytes.

   **Step 1:** Compute the page offset:
$$
O_A = 0x00406A00 \bmod 4096
$$
Since $0x00406A00 = 0x4000 \times 4096 + 0xA00$ in terms of page offset, we have:
$$
O_A = 0xA00 = 2560
$$

   **Step 2:** Compute bytes remaining in the first page:
$$
R = S_{\text{page}} - O_A = 4096 - 2560 = 1536
$$

   **Step 3:** Compute pages spanned:
$$
N_{\text{pages}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{O_A + L}{S_{\text{page}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{2560 + 512}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{3072}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 0.75 \right\rceil = 1
$$

   **Result:** Only **1 page** (the page from `0x00406000` to `0x00406FFF`) needs to be guarded. The entire 512-byte buffer lies within this single 4 KiB page.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary for KTU 2024):**

- A **breakpoint** is a deliberate pause point in a process under a debugger.
- **Software breakpoints** use the `INT 3` opcode (`0xCC`), 1 byte, modify code, unlimited count.
- **Hardware breakpoints** use `DR0`–`DR3` (addresses) and `DR7` (control), max **4** slots, do **not** modify code.
- Debug exception for `INT 3` is **vector 3 (#BP)**; for Trap Flag single-step it is **vector 1 (#DB)**.
- After `INT 3` fires, the debugger must **decrement `EIP` by 1** before resuming.
- The **Trap Flag (TF)** is at **bit 8** of `EFLAGS` (mask `0x100`).
- **Conditional breakpoints** = breakpoint + Boolean predicate evaluated at fire time; cause $10^3$–$10^5\times$ slowdown.
- **Memory (page-guard) breakpoints** use `VirtualProtect` with `PAGE_GUARD` (`0x100`); granularity is **4096 bytes**.
- `DR7` fields: `L_i` (local enable), `G_i` (global enable), `RW_i` (condition), `LEN_i` (length 1/2/4 bytes).
- **Linear address** in flat model (32/64-bit Windows) = `EIP` directly (CS base = 0).
- **Anti-debug malware** checks code CRC → prefer **hardware breakpoints**.
- **Packed malware** → set BPs on `VirtualAlloc`/`VirtualFree` to catch unpacked region.
- **Decryption loops** → use **conditional BPs** keyed on loop counter or output buffer.
- Always set TF to safely re-execute the byte you just restored after an `INT 3` exception.
- Page-guard BPs fire on **any access** within the 4 KiB page — they are imprecise.
- $S_{\text{page}} = 4096$ bytes is the universal page size on x86/x64 Windows.
- Number of pages spanned by a buffer of length $L$ starting at address $A$ is:
$$
N_{\text{pages}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{(A \bmod 4096) + L}{4096} \right\rceil
$$
- The CPU's **interrupt descriptor table (IDT)** maps vectors 1 and 3 to their respective exception handlers.
- Hardware BPs can be set to fire on **execute (00)**, **write (01)**, **I/O (10)**, or **read/write (11)**.
- For self-modifying or self-checking malware, hardware BPs on **execute** at known function entries are the most reliable forensic tool.
- Always remember: **only 4 hardware breakpoints** are available — this is the single most-asked KTU fact on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tracing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Tracing in Malware Forensics

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Tracing** in Malware Forensics is the systematic process of **monitoring, recording, and reconstructing the execution path, behavioural artifacts, and communication channels of a malicious program** across a target system or network, in order to establish a verifiable, time-ordered evidentiary chain of what the malware did, how it did it, and what it touched.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education) framework, tracing is positioned as the **observational and reconstructive backbone** of dynamic malware analysis. It complements static analysis (which inspects the code without execution) by capturing *what actually happens at runtime* — system calls, file modifications, registry changes, network packets, process creation, and memory activity.

The three canonical sub-domains of tracing recognised by the KTU PECCT741 syllabus are:

| Sub-domain | What it observes | Primary Forensic Output |
|---|---|---|
| **System / API Call Tracing** | OS-level interactions (syscalls, DLL calls) | A linear log of `process ↔ kernel` events |
| **Network Traffic Tracing** | Packets leaving/entering the host | PCAP captures, flow records, C2 indicators |
| **Behavioural / Process Tracing** | Process trees, file & registry mutations | Timeline of artefacts, IoC extraction |

The **forensic rigor** of tracing is measured by the *Verifiability* (can a second analyst reproduce the trace?) and *Non-repudiation* (can the trace be tampered with without detection?) — typically guaranteed by cryptographic hashing such as **SHA-256** and write-once storage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The CCTV Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Malware is a Burglar, Tracing is the CCTV Network"**
>
> Imagine a thief (the malware) breaks into a building (the compromised system). **Static analysis** is like looking at the thief's tool kit *after* it has been recovered. **Tracing** is like installing CCTV cameras that *watch the burglar in real time* — recording every door opened, every drawer touched, every phone call made, and every escape route used.
>
> For the forensic investigator, the CCTV tape (the trace log) is the single most valuable piece of evidence, because:
> - It captures **intent and order** (the burglar went to the safe *before* the safe was opened).
> - It produces **non-repudiable evidence** admissible in court.
> - It reveals **lateral movement** (the thief moved from the server room to the HR office).

This is precisely what `strace`, `ltrace`, `Process Monitor`, `Wireshark`, and `Sysmon` do for malware — they are the **digital CCTV network** of the forensic lab.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| **System Call (syscall)** | A request from user-mode to kernel-mode | 300+ defined in Linux x86_64 |
| **DLL** (Dynamic-Link Library) | Shared code module loaded at runtime | Windows: ~1,500+ exported APIs |
| **PID** (Process Identifier) | Unique OS-assigned number for a process | 0 – 32767 (Windows), 0 – 4194304 (Linux) |
| **TID** (Thread Identifier) | Unique ID for an execution thread | Per-process thread pool |
| **PCAP** (Packet Capture) | Binary log of network frames | Default MTU: **1500 bytes** (Ethernet) |
| **IoC** (Indicator of Compromise) | Forensic artefact identifying intrusion | E.g., specific file hash, IP, mutex name |
| **C2 / C&C** | Command-and-Control server | Typically HTTP/HTTPS, DNS tunneling |
| **Volatility** | Time-bound nature of a data source | Order: nanoseconds (CPU cache) → weeks (logs) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** — A KTU-highlighted principle. The investigator must collect the *most volatile* evidence first:
>
> 1. CPU registers & cache
> 2. Routing table, ARP cache, kernel statistics
> 3. Process memory
> 4. Temporary file systems
> 5. Disk
> 6. Remote logs & monitoring data
> 7. Physical configuration, network topology
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Tracing Toolkit

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Tracing — Why & How

### 2.1.1 Pillar I — System Call Tracing (User ↔ Kernel Observation)

**Why it matters:** Modern malware is heavily *packed* and *obfuscated*, so static reverse engineering often fails. By the time malware reaches the kernel, however, its intent is decoded — it must call `NtCreateFile`, `NtWriteFile`, `InternetOpenUrl`, etc. These calls are **unforgeable** from a behavioural perspective.

**How it works (mechanism):**
- The tracer installs a **hooking layer** in user mode (LD_PRELOAD on Linux, inline patching on Windows) *or* a **kernel-mode driver** that intercepts syscalls via the System Service Dispatch Table (SSDT).
- Each intercepted call is logged with: timestamp (ns), PID/TID, syscall name, arguments, return value.

**Tool matrix (KTU high-yield):**

| Tool | Platform | Mode | Captures |
|---|---|---|---|
| `strace` | Linux | User (ptrace) | Syscalls, signals, faults |
| `ltrace` | Linux | User (LD_PRELOAD) | Library calls (libc) |
| `dtrace` | Solaris/macOS/BSD | Kernel | Any kernel/user probe |
| `Process Monitor (ProcMon)` | Windows | Kernel driver (legacy) | File, Registry, Process, Network |
| `Sysmon` (Sysinternals) | Windows | Kernel driver | Process create, network, file, registry |
| `API Monitor` | Windows | Inline hook | Win32 / COM / .NET APIs |

### 2.1.2 Pillar II — Network Traffic Tracing

**Why it matters:** Most modern malware (RATs, ransomware, banking trojans) requires communication with an external **C2** server. Capturing this traffic is often the *only* way to identify the operator's infrastructure.

**How it works:**
- The forensic host is placed on a *span port* / *mirror port* of the switch, OR runs a tap driver (`WinPcap`/`Npcap`).
- All frames are written to a **PCAP** file, which can be replayed and analysed.
- The investigator extracts: **5-tuple** (src-IP, dst-IP, src-port, dst-port, protocol), **payload**, **timing** (jitter, beacon interval), **TLS metadata** (SNI, JA3/JA3S fingerprints).

**Tool matrix:**

| Tool | Layer | Forensic Strength |
|---|---|---|
| `tcpdump` / `Wireshark` | 2–7 | Full packet payload, expert analysis |
| `NetworkMiner` | 2–7 | Reassembles transferred files (forensic extraction) |
| `tshark` (CLI) | 2–7 | Scriptable, headless, field extraction |
| `Zeek` (formerly Bro) | 3+ | Connection logs, behaviour-based detection |
| `Suricata` | 3+ | IDS with rule-based IoC matching |

### 2.1.3 Pillar III — Behavioural / Process Tree Tracing

**Why it matters:** A single malware sample often spawns multiple child processes, injects into legitimate ones, and uses *process hollowing* to disguise itself. Tracing the *parent → child* tree reveals this camouflage.

**How it works:**
- Event-driven monitoring: subscribe to process creation events (`PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx` on Windows, `inotify`/`fanotify` on Linux).
- Record PID, PPID, image path, command line, integrity level, hash of executable.
- Reconstruct the **execution graph** — a directed tree from the initial malicious `PID_0` to all descendants.

**KTU Examiner's preferred tool:** `Sysmon` with the `Process Create` event ID = 1, exporting to a SIEM (Splunk/ELK) for timeline reconstruction.

## 2.2 KTU Tracing Cheat-Sheet — Concept ↔ Tool ↔ Evidence

| Forensic Question | Tracing Technique | Tool of Choice | Evidence Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| Did the malware open a network socket? | Network tracing | `Wireshark`, `tcpdump` | `.pcap` |
| What files did the malware create/modify? | File-system tracing | `ProcMon`, `inotify` | `.csv` / `.xml` log |
| What registry keys were tampered with? | Registry tracing | `Regshot`, `ProcMon` | `.reg` diff |
| Did it inject into another process? | Process / memory tracing | `Volatility`, `API Monitor` | Memory dump `.dmp` |
| What is the C2 IP / domain? | DNS / HTTP tracing | `Wireshark`, `Zeek` | DNS query log, HTTP request |
| What was the first malicious process? | Process tree tracing | `Sysmon`, `pstree` | Event-log timeline |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Industry

- **Incident Response (IR) teams** use Sysmon + ELK to trace an entire APT campaign across thousands of endpoints in real time.
- **Law-enforcement (CBI, Interpol)** relies on PCAP and process-tree evidence admissible under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023).
- **Bug-bounty platforms (HackerOne)** use tracing to differentiate *intended vs. accidental* impact in submitted PoCs.
- **Malware sandboxes (Cuckoo, ANY.RUN, Joe Sandbox)** are essentially *automated tracing frameworks* with full API + network + behavioural instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Tracing by Malware — A Cat-and-Mouse Reality**
>
> Sophisticated malware actively *fights* the tracer:
> - **Anti-debug:** `IsDebuggerPresent()`, `NtQueryInformationProcess`
> - **Anti-VM:** checks for VMware/VirtualBox artefacts (registry, MAC OUI prefix `00:0C:29`, `00:1C:14`)
> - **Anti-strace:** detects `ptrace` parent, forks to confuse tracer
> - **Sleep obfuscation:** `Sleep(300000)` to outlast sandbox timeout
> - **API unhooking:** restores original SSDT entries
>
> KTU students must mention at least one anti-analysis countermeasure in answers to score full marks — this is a *favourite* of paper-setters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Tracing Methodology and Worked Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 The Canonical Five-Phase Tracing Methodology

Below is the *decomposition* of a complete tracing operation. Every KTU question on tracing in 2024 Scheme will map to one or more of these phases.

### Phase 1 — Baseline Establishment
- Capture the "clean" state of the system **before** suspected malware executes:
  - SHA-256 of all executables in `%SystemRoot%\System32` (Windows) or `/bin`, `/usr/bin` (Linux).
  - Snapshot of running processes.
  - Pre-execution PCAP (typically 60 seconds of idle traffic).
- *Reasoning:* Without a baseline, the investigator cannot prove that an observed change is **causally linked** to the malware.

### Phase 2 — Controlled Execution
- Run the sample in an **isolated sandbox** (air-gapped VM, with host-only network).
- Initiate the tracer: `strace -f -tt -T -o trace.log ./malware_sample` (Linux) or `ProcMon` with boot-log enabled (Windows).
- *The flag `-f` follows forks; `-tt` prints microsecond timestamps; `-T` prints syscall duration.*

### Phase 3 — Trace Capture
- Three concurrent log streams are produced:
  - **Syscall stream** (`trace.log`)
  - **Network stream** (`capture.pcap`)
  - **Process tree** (`sysmon.evtx` / `audit.log`)

### Phase 4 — Trace Correlation
- Merge the three streams on a unified **timeline** (events ordered by nanosecond timestamps).
- *Equation of forensic unification:*

$$
T_{\text{unified}} \;=\; \text{MergeSort}\bigl(\;E_{\text{syscall}} \;\cup\; E_{\text{network}} \;\cup\; E_{\text{process}}\;\bigr)
$$

where each event $E_i$ is a tuple:

$$
E_i \;=\; \langle\, t_i,\; s_i,\; a_i,\; p_i,\; r_i,\; h_i \,\rangle
$$

with the six components being:

$$
t_i = \text{timestamp (ns)},\quad s_i = \text{source (syscall/network/proc)},\quad a_i = \text{action / verb}
$$

$$
p_i = \text{principal (PID/port/IP)},\quad r_i = \text{resource (file/registry/IP)},\quad h_i = \text{SHA-256 hash of artefact}
$$

### Phase 5 — IoC Extraction & Report
- Apply **regular-expression rules** to the unified log.
- Generate the **IoC report**: a list of hashes, IPs, domains, mutexes, registry keys.

## 3.2 Worked Walk-Through #1 — `strace` of a Suspicious Linux Binary

Suppose we are given a binary `evil` and we trace it. The investigator executes:

$$
\text{\$ strace -f -tt -T -e trace=openat,connect,write ./evil}
$$

The (truncated) trace might read:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{[13:42:01.118344]}&\;\text{openat(AT_FDCWD, "/etc/shadow", O_RDONLY)} \;\rightarrow\; 3 \\
\text{[13:42:01.118512]}&\;\text{openat(AT_FDCWD, "/etc/shadow", O_RDONLY)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{-1 EACCES} \\
\text{[13:42:01.119001]}&\;\text{connect(3, \{sa\_family=AF\_INET, sin\_port=htons(4444),\} )} \;\rightarrow\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step-by-step logical interpretation:

- **Step 1** — At 13:42:01.118344 µs, the process called `openat` on `/etc/shadow` — a sensitive credential file. This is a strong forensic indicator: legitimate binaries rarely read `/etc/shadow`.
- **Step 2** — The call failed with `-1 EACCES` (Permission Denied) because the user-mode sandbox is non-root. The malware's *intent* is still recorded.
- **Step 3** — Immediately after, it opened a **TCP socket** to port **4444** (the canonical Metasploit `reverse_shell` default). The destination IP would be in the full trace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Inference Rule (FITB-style):**
> $$\text{Risk Score} = w_1 \cdot [\text{file touch}] + w_2 \cdot [\text{network call}] + w_3 \cdot [\text{privilege escalation attempt}]$$
> where the weights $w_i$ are empirically calibrated by the SOC. Any score exceeding threshold $T = 7.0$ triggers incident escalation.

## 3.3 Worked Walk-Through #2 — Reconstructing a Process Tree

Suppose `Sysmon` records the following events (abbreviated):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &: \text{ParentPID}= 1100,\; \text{Image}= \texttt{cmd.exe},\; \text{CommandLine}= \texttt{cmd /c powershell -enc ...} \\
E_2 &: \text{ParentPID}= 1200,\; \text{Image}= \texttt{powershell.exe},\; \text{CommandLine}= \texttt{powershell -enc SQBFAFgAIAAo...} \\
E_3 &: \text{ParentPID}= 1200,\; \text{Image}= \texttt{rundll32.exe},\; \text{CommandLine}= \texttt{rundll32.exe shell32.dll,...} \\
E_4 &: \text{ParentPID}= 1200,\; \text{Image}= \texttt{schtasks.exe},\; \text{CommandLine}= \texttt{schtasks /create /tn Updater /tr ...}
\end{aligned}
$$

The investigator draws the *parent → child* directed edges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{root}\,(1100) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{child}\,(1200) \\
\text{child}\,(1200) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{grandchild}_1\,(E_3) \\
\text{child}\,(1200) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{grandchild}_2\,(E_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical deduction:** This pattern is the *textbook TTP (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures) of a PowerShell-based dropper*:
- `-enc` indicates an **encoded Base64 command** (anti-static-analysis).
- `rundll32` is used as a **LOLBin** (Living-off-the-Land Binary) to load a malicious DLL.
- `schtasks /create` is **persistence** — schedule a task named "Updater" to auto-launch the malware at every user logon.

The full report is signed with the investigator's PGP key to satisfy non-repudiation:

$$
\text{SHA-256}(\text{report}) \;\oplus\; \text{PGP}_{\text{sig}}(\text{report}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{Evidence Package}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Walk-Through #3 — Network Trace Correlation

Given a `pcap` of 1,247 packets, the investigator applies a **Zeek** script to extract flows. A simplified correlation table:

| # | Time | Protocol | Source → Dest | Length | Forensic Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 13:42:00 | DNS | 10.0.0.5 → 8.8.8.8 | 78 B | Query: `evil-c2.xyz` |
| 2 | 13:42:01 | TCP SYN | 10.0.0.5 → 51.81.221.43:443 | 74 B | Initial C2 handshake |
| 3 | 13:42:02 | TLS ClientHello | 10.0.0.5 → 51.81.221.43:443 | 583 B | SNI: `cdn.legit.com` (cover) |
| 4 | 13:42:03 | HTTP POST | 10.0.0.5 → 51.81.221.43:80 | 1.2 KB | URI: `/beacon?id=ABC123` |

**Step-by-step deduction:**
- **Step 1:** DNS query for `evil-c2.xyz` *precedes* the connection → the malware resolves its C2 *before* any payload exchange. This is the **C2 Resolution Phase** of the MITRE ATT&CK framework (Tactic TA0011, Technique T1071.004).
- **Step 2:** Connection to port 443 (HTTPS) — encrypted, so payload is opaque.
- **Step 3:** **Tactical deception** — the SNI is `cdn.legit.com` (a known benign domain) while the actual destination IP is malicious. This is **Domain Fronting**.
- **Step 4:** A fallback to **HTTP/80** with `/beacon?id=ABC123` reveals a **C2 beacon pattern** — the unique ID `ABC123` is the infected host's campaign ID.

The reconstructed **C2 chain** is:

$$
\texttt{10.0.0.5} \;\xrightarrow{\text{DNS}}\; \texttt{evil-c2.xyz} \;\xrightarrow{\text{resolve}}\; \texttt{51.81.221.43} \;\xrightarrow{\text{HTTPS + Front}}\; \text{C2 Server}
$$

This single chain of evidence, when coupled with the process-tree trace and the syscall trace, satisfies the **three-leg stool** of forensic reliability: *what ran, what it did, where it talked to*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Tracing Architecture

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Malware Tracing System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Malware Sample] --> B[Isolated Sandbox VM]
    B --> C1[User-mode Tracer - strace ltrace API Monitor]
    B --> C2[Kernel-mode Tracer - Sysmon ProcMon eBPF]
    B --> C3[Network Tap - Wireshark tcpdump Zeek]
    C1 --> D[Unified Timeline Engine]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[IoC Extractor]
    D --> F[Behavioural Analyser]
    E --> G[Forensic Report - SHA-256 Signed]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Chain of Custody Repository]
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Tracing a Single Malicious Process

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant INV as Investigator
    participant SB as Sandbox
    participant TR as Tracer Layer
    participant KS as Kernel / OS
    participant NW as Network / C2

    INV->>SB: Launch malware sample
    SB->>TR: Attach tracer (strace/Sysmon)
    TR->>KS: openat /etc/shadow
    KS-->>TR: return EACCES
    TR->>INV: log syscall event t1
    TR->>KS: socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)
    KS-->>TR: return fd 3
    TR->>NW: connect(3, evil-c2.xyz:4444)
    NW-->>TR: SYN-ACK
    TR->>INV: log network event t2
    TR->>KS: execve(/bin/sh, ...)
    KS-->>TR: shell spawned
    TR->>INV: log process event t3
    INV->>TR: detach and export logs
```

## 4.3 Process-Tree Reconstruction Diagram (LOLBin Dropper Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[PID 1100 cmd.exe - entry vector] --> P1[PID 1200 powershell.exe -enc base64 payload]
    P1 --> P2[PID 1300 rundll32.exe LOLBin - loads evil.dll]
    P1 --> P3[PID 1310 schtasks.exe - persistence via scheduled task]
    P1 --> P4[PID 1320 bitsadmin.exe - downloads stage 2]
    P2 --> P5[PID 1350 svchost.exe - injection target]
    P3 --> P6[Task Scheduler - Updater runs at logon]
```

## 4.4 Network Tracing Stack — Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Application Layer - HTTP DNS TLS] --> L4[Transport Layer - TCP UDP ports]
    L4 --> L3[Network Layer - IPs and routes]
    L3 --> L2[Data Link Layer - MAC frames PCAP]
    L2 --> T1[Tap or Span Port Capture]
    T1 --> A1[WireShark / tcpdump - raw pcap]
    A1 --> A2[Zeek - flow logs]
    A2 --> A3[Suricata - signature alerts]
    A3 --> A4[ELK / Splunk - timeline and dashboards]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Tracer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the sample Windows or Linux?}
    Q1 -- Windows --> Q2{Need kernel events or user-mode APIs?}
    Q2 -- Kernel --> R1[Sysmon + ProcMon]
    Q2 -- User-mode --> R2[API Monitor]
    Q1 -- Linux --> Q3{Need full syscall stream?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[strace]
    Q3 -- Library only --> R4[ltrace]
    R1 --> R5[Export to ELK]
    R2 --> R5
    R3 --> R5
    R4 --> R5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**Define malware tracing. List any two tools used for system-call tracing in Linux.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Malware tracing is the *systematic capture and reconstruction of the runtime behaviour and execution path of a malicious program* — including system calls, network activity, and process interactions — in order to derive Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) and build a forensic timeline.
- **Two tools (1.5 marks):**
  1. **`strace`** — traces Linux syscalls via the `ptrace` interface.
  2. **`ltrace`** — traces library calls (e.g., `libc` functions) using `LD_PRELOAD` interception.
- *(Acceptable alternates: `dtrace`, `perf`, `bpftrace`, `SystemTap`.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Sample Paper]**
**What is an Indicator of Compromise (IoC)? Give two examples with reference to malware tracing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** An **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** is a forensic artefact — a hash, IP address, domain name, registry key, mutex, or file path — whose presence on a system provides *evidential* or *correlational* proof that an intrusion by malware has occurred or is in progress. IoCs are the *primary output* of a tracing operation.
- **Two examples (1.5 marks):**
  1. The SHA-256 hash of the malicious executable discovered via file-system tracing.
  2. The C2 IP address extracted from PCAP network tracing (e.g., `51.81.221.43`).
- *(Acceptable alternates: domain name, scheduled-task name, suspicious mutex, anomalous DNS query, JA3 fingerprint.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** *Module 2 carries 14 marks. Examiner provides internal choice between Question A and Question B, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Total = 14.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Explain the role of system-call tracing in dynamic malware analysis. With a suitable example, demonstrate how `strace` can be used to capture malicious activity of a Linux binary. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** System-call tracing intercepts the boundary between *user-mode* (malware) and *kernel-mode* (OS). Every file access, network packet, process creation, and memory allocation must cross this boundary, so the trace is **behaviourally authentic** even if the binary is packed or obfuscated.

2. **Mechanism (2 marks):** On Linux, `strace` uses the `ptrace()` system call. The tracer process attaches to the tracee, intercepts every syscall entry/exit, and writes a log line with timestamp, syscall name, arguments, and return value. The `-f` flag follows forks; `-tt` gives microsecond timestamps; `-e trace=<set>` filters to specific syscalls (e.g., `openat,connect,write`).

3. **Worked example (3 marks):**
   - **Command:** `$ strace -f -tt -T -e trace=openat,connect,write ./evil`
   - **Sample log (abridged):**
     - `[13:42:01.118344] openat(AT_FDCWD, "/etc/shadow", O_RDONLY) = 3`
     - `[13:42:01.119001] connect(3, {sa_family=AF_INET, sin_port=htons(4444)}, 16) = 0`
     - `[13:42:01.120500] write(3, "GET /beacon HTTP/1.1\r\n...", 142) = 142`
   - **Forensic inference:** The malware attempts to read `/etc/shadow` (credential theft) and connects to port 4444 (the canonical Metasploit reverse-shell port). The write of an HTTP `GET /beacon` confirms a **C2 beacon**. The tracer thus reveals the malware's *intent* even though the binary may be statically unreadable.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** System-call tracing provides a **forensically sound, time-ordered, reproducible** record of malicious behaviour, and is the most reliable technique for analysing unknown or obfuscated Linux malware.

#### **(b)** Describe the components of a Network Tracing infrastructure for malware analysis. Explain the role of PCAP, span ports, and JA3 fingerprints. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Network tracing — definition (1 mark):** Capturing and analysing packets generated by malware to identify C2 infrastructure, data exfiltration, and lateral movement.

2. **Components of the infrastructure (3 marks):**
   - **Span Port (Mirror Port):** A *passive* switch port configured to mirror all frames of one or more monitored ports. The forensic workstation attaches here, receiving a copy of every frame **without** injecting latency into production traffic.
   - **Network TAP (Test Access Point):** A *hardware* splitter that copies optical/electrical signals at the physical layer. More reliable than span ports (no missed frames under load) and a KTU-preferred approach for high-throughput analysis.
   - **Capture Library:** `libpcap` (Linux) or `Npcap`/`WinPcap` (Windows) — the kernel-mode driver that puts the NIC into *promiscuous mode* and writes frames to a `.pcap` file.
   - **Analyser:** `Wireshark` (GUI) or `tshark` (CLI) for human inspection; `Zeek` for automated connection logs.

3. **PCAP file (1.5 marks):** The **Packet Capture** file is the canonical evidence format. Each frame carries a timestamp, the 5-tuple `(src-IP, dst-IP, src-port, dst-port, protocol)`, and the raw payload. PCAP is *admissible* in court (subject to chain of custody) because it is bit-exact and tamper-evident when SHA-256 signed.

4. **JA3 fingerprint (1.5 marks):** A JA3 hash is a **TLS-client fingerprint** derived from the Client Hello — the cipher suites, extensions, elliptic-curve formats offered by the client. Two malware families using the same C2 framework produce the *same* JA3 even if the IP changes, enabling **campaign-level attribution** independent of network indicators.

5. **Conclusion (omit / implicit):** A well-designed network-tracing setup using span ports + TAPs + PCAP + JA3 forms the *network leg* of the forensic three-legged stool.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE***

#### **(a)** Discuss the process-tree tracing methodology for detecting process injection. Use a suitable case study to illustrate the LOLBin technique. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Process-tree tracing — definition (1 mark):** Reconstructing the *parent → child* ancestry of every process on the system, including their command lines, image paths, integrity levels, and hashes, in order to detect abnormal spawning patterns such as injection or LOLBin abuse.

2. **Methodology (2 marks):**
   - Subscribe to OS process-creation callbacks (e.g., `PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx` on Windows, `inotify`/`fanotify` on Linux).
   - For each new process, log: `PID`, `PPID`, `ImageFileName`, `CommandLine`, `CurrentDirectory`, `Hash(SHA-256)`, `IntegrityLevel`, `UserSID`.
   - Build a directed graph: an edge `(PID_parent → PID_child)` indicates the child was created by the parent.
   - Apply anomaly rules:
     - **R1:** Office application (Word, Excel) spawning `cmd.exe` or `powershell.exe` — high-risk.
     - **R2:** `svchost.exe` not started by `services.exe` — likely injection.
     - **R3:** A `rundll32.exe` with no `-sta` and a long, obfuscated command line — high-risk.

3. **LOLBin case study (3 marks):** *Living-off-the-Land Binaries* are legitimate Windows utilities (`rundll32.exe`, `mshta.exe`, `regsvr32.exe`, `bitsadmin.exe`, `certutil.exe`) abused by malware to execute malicious payloads *without dropping a new executable* on disk — defeating signature-based AV.
   - **Example trace event:**
     `Parent = powershell.exe (PID 1200); Child = rundll32.exe (PID 1300); CommandLine = "rundll32.exe shell32.dll,Control_RunDLL evil.dll"`.
   - **Inference:** `rundll32` is invoking `Control_RunDLL` on a *non-system* DLL (`evil.dll`) — a classic LOLBin pattern documented in MITRE ATT&CK T1218.011.

4. **Forensic remediation (1 mark):** Isolate the parent process tree at the firewall; revoke the user's tokens; image the disk for offline analysis; reset credentials for the affected account.

#### **(b)** Compare `strace` and `Sysmon` as malware-tracing tools. Mention at least three points of comparison and explain the role of `eBPF` in modern Linux tracing. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Comparison table (3 marks):**

   | Feature | `strace` (Linux) | `Sysmon` (Windows) |
   |---|---|---|
   | **OS** | Linux (and Android via `strace`) | Windows (7 → 11 / Server 2012 → 2022) |
   | **Mode** | User-mode (`ptrace`) | Kernel driver + user service |
   | **Output** | Plain-text `trace.log` | XML event-log (`.evtx`) — SIEM-friendly |
   | **Overhead** | High (10× – 100× slowdown) | Low (kernel-buffered, ring-buffered) |
   | **Coverage** | Syscalls, signals, faults | Process, Network, File, Registry, Driver-Load, DNS |
   | **Anti-analysis bypass** | Detected by malware `ptrace` check | Harder to detect (no user-mode `ptrace` hook) |

2. **Similarity (0.5 mark):** Both convert runtime behaviour into a *forensically analysable, time-ordered event log* and both are foundational tools in dynamic malware analysis.

3. **eBPF — modern Linux tracing (3 marks):**
   - **Definition:** **eBPF** (*Extended Berkeley Packet Filter*) is a sandboxed in-kernel virtual machine that lets user-supplied programs run *safely* inside the Linux kernel without modifying kernel source or loading kernel modules.
   - **Why it matters for tracing:** eBPF programs can attach to *kprobes*, *uprobes*, *tracepoints*, and *network hooks (XDP/TC)*, allowing syscall tracing with *near-zero* overhead — orders of magnitude faster than `strace`.
   - **Tools built on eBPF:** `bpftrace` (high-level scripting), `bcc` (toolkit — `execsnoop`, `tcpconnect`, `opensnoop`), `Pixie` (Kubernetes observability).
   - **Forensic significance:** eBPF traces are *kernel-mediated* and hence invisible to user-mode anti-debugging checks, making them a *next-generation* tracer that bypasses traditional malware anti-tracing tricks.

4. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** `strace` remains the teaching-grade workhorse, `Sysmon` is the SOC standard on Windows, and `eBPF` is the *future-proof* answer for high-fidelity, anti-analysis-resistant Linux tracing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tracing Answers**
>
> 1. **Confusing static with dynamic analysis.** Tracing belongs to *dynamic* (runtime) analysis. Writing about `IDA Pro` or `Ghidra` disassembly for a tracing question scores **zero** — it is a different module.
> 2. **Forgetting the Baseline.** A trace *without* a pre-execution baseline cannot prove causality. Always mention "**first capture clean state, then execute, then capture dirty state, then diff**."
> 3. **Skipping timestamps.** Examiners award marks for *time-ordered* reasoning. A timeline diagram earns 1–2 free marks.
> 4. **Ignoring Anti-Analysis.** For 14-mark questions, mentioning at least one anti-tracing countermeasure (e.g., `IsDebuggerPresent`, ptrace-detect, sleep-obfuscation) is **mandatory** to hit the top band.
> 5. **No chain-of-custody.** A trace that is not SHA-256 signed and logged in a chain-of-custody register may be **inadmissible** as evidence — examiners test this awareness.
> 6. **Mixing up PIDs and TIDs.** A process has one PID but many TIDs. Confusing them in a process-tree answer is a 0.5-mark deduction.
> 7. **Tool-only answers.** Listing tools without *explaining the captured artefact and its forensic meaning* loses at least 50% of the marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Tracing = dynamic observation** of a malware's runtime behaviour across syscalls, network, and process tree.
- [x] **Three pillars:** System-call tracing, Network tracing, Behavioural / process-tree tracing.
- [x] **Linux tools:** `strace` (syscall), `ltrace` (library), `tcpdump`/`Wireshark` (network), `bpftrace`/`bcc` (eBPF, modern).
- [x] **Windows tools:** `Sysmon` (kernel events), `ProcMon` (file/registry/proc), `API Monitor` (Win32 API), `Wireshark` (network).
- [x] **PCAP** = canonical network-evidence format; **SHA-256** = canonical integrity hash.
- [x] **JA3** = TLS-client fingerprint used for campaign attribution.
- [x] **IoC** = forensic artefact proving compromise (hash, IP, domain, mutex, registry key).
- [x] **Order of Volatility** (RFC 3227) governs evidence collection priority — collect the most volatile first.
- [x] **Process tree** = directed graph `parent → child`; anomalies (Office → cmd, svchost not by services.exe) indicate injection / LOLBin abuse.
- [x] **LOLBin** = legitimate OS utility abused by malware to avoid dropping new files (e.g., `rundll32.exe`, `mshta.exe`, `certutil.exe`).
- [x] **Anti-tracing** countermeasures: `IsDebuggerPresent`, ptrace detection, VM detection, sleep obfuscation, API unhooking.
- [x] **Chain of custody** + SHA-256 signing = non-repudiation → admissible evidence under Indian Evidence Act / Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023.
- [x] **Mermaid / block diagram** in answers earns 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.
- [x] **Order of arguments in `strace`**: process, then verb, then resource. **Order of arguments in tracing report**: who, what, when, where, how, why.
- [x] **Cyber-Law Connect:** Tracing outputs (PCAP + ProcMon/Sysmon logs) are the *evidentiary inputs* to an FIR under **Sec. 66F (Cyberterrorism)** and **Sec. 43/66 (Damage / Penalty)** of the IT Act 2000.
- [x] **Real-world framework:** MITRE ATT&CK — every traced technique should be mapped to a T-code (e.g., T1059 = Command & Scripting Interpreter, T1071 = Application-Layer Protocol).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exception Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Exception Handling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Exception Handling** in the context of *Malware Forensics* is a structured, hardware-and-software coordinated mechanism by which a computing system intercepts, classifies, dispatches, and resolves anomalous runtime events (exceptions) that disrupt the normal sequential flow of program execution. In Windows-based forensic analysis — the dominant platform for malware reverse engineering — Exception Handling operates at two synergistic tiers: **Hardware Exceptions** (traps, faults, and aborts raised by the CPU, e.g., `#PF`, `#BP`, `#UD`, `#GP`) and **Software Exceptions** (raised explicitly via `RAISE EXCEPTION`, `RtlRaiseException`, or high-level `throw` statements). For the KTU PECCT741 syllabus, the term is dual-scoped: 1. **Programmer’s Perspective** — How analyst-authored forensic tools (Python, C/C++) use `try/except`, `__try/__except`, and `try/finally` constructs to remain resilient when parsing corrupted, obfuscated, or hostile artifacts. 2. **Reverse Engineering / Anti-Analysis Perspective** — How malware authors weaponize Windows **Structured Exception Handling (SEH)** and **Vectored Exception Handling (VEH)** for control-flow obfuscation, anti-debugging traps, and stealthy shellcode delivery. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Memorize Verbatim)** > *“Exception Handling is the disciplined runtime mechanism — comprising SEH chains, VEH lists, and high-level language constructs — that detects, records, and dispatches anomalous events to a registered handler, enabling both graceful program recovery and, in adversarial code, deliberate control-flow hijacking.”* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine a **hospital emergency department**: | Hospital Element | Exception Handling Equivalent | |---|---| | Patient arriving in distress | Exception raised (hardware fault or `throw`) | | Triage nurse classifying severity | Exception dispatcher + exception code (e.g., `0xC0000005`) | | Specialist doctor assigned | Exception handler (`__except` block) | | Backup doctor if specialist unavailable | SEH chain traversal to next frame | | Patient referred to a super-specialist | Vectored Exception Handler (system-wide) | | Patient dies (no handler found) | Unhandled exception → `STATUS_NONCONTINUABLE_EXCEPTION` / process termination | > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition for a First-Time Reader** > Think of an exception as an *unscheduled interruption* — the CPU or code raises its hand mid-execution and says, “I cannot proceed normally, please send a supervisor.” The operating system then walks a pre-registered list of supervisors (handlers). If one accepts the case, control transfers there. In malware forensics, we study *who* raised the hand, *which* supervisor answered, and *what* code was smuggled in the dispatch — because adversaries hide payloads inside this very dispatch mechanism. ## 1.3 Physical & Standard Constants in Exception Handling The following constants are standardized by Microsoft and the Intel/AMD architectures and are **must-memorize** for KTU examinations: - **STATUS_BREAKPOINT** $= \mathbf{0x80000003}$ (INT 3 breakpoint) - **STATUS_SINGLE_STEP** $= \mathbf{0x80000004}$ (TF flag, single-step trap) - **STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION** $= \mathbf{0xC0000005}$ (read/write to invalid memory) - **STATUS_ILLEGAL_INSTRUCTION** $= \mathbf{0xC000001D}$ (encountered `#UD`) - **STATUS_PRIVILEGED_INSTRUCTION** $= \mathbf{0xC0000096}$ (CPL violation) - **STATUS_INTEGER_DIVIDE_BY_ZERO** $= \mathbf{0xC0000094}$ - **STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW** $= \mathbf{0xC00000FD}$ - **EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION** $= \mathbf{-1}$ - **EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH** $= \mathbf{0}$ - **EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER** $= \mathbf{1}$ > [!TIP] > The two most significant bits of an NTSTATUS code carry semantic weight: `0x8` prefix = *warning/information* (continuable), `0xC` prefix = *error* (typically fatal). Forensic analysts use this bit-pattern to triage exception storms in malware. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Exceptions A forensic analyst must classify every observed exception along three orthogonal axes: 1. **Source Axis** — Hardware vs. Software 2. **Scope Axis** — Per-Thread (SEH) vs. Process/Global (VEH) 3. **Intent Axis** — Legitimate error recovery vs. Adversarial control-flow hijack ### 2.1.1 Hardware Exceptions (CPU-Generated) The x86/x64 architecture reserves interrupt vectors `0`–`31` for synchronous exceptions. The most forensics-relevant are: | Vector | Mnemonic | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | 0 | `#DE` Divide Error | Used by malware packers to detect sandboxed execution | | 1 | `#DB` Debug Exception | Triggered by hardware breakpoints (DR0–DR3) and TF | | 3 | `#BP` Breakpoint | Triggered by `INT3` opcode `0xCC`; **the cornerstone of debugger breakpoints** | | 4 | `#OF` Overflow | Triggered by `INTO` opcode; rarely used legitimately | | 6 | `#UD` Invalid Opcode | Weaponized by shellcode-stagers against non-victim CPUs | | 8 | `#DF` Double Fault | Causes BSOD if no handler;
