forensic gold for crash analysis | | 12 | `#SS` Stack Segment Fault | Indicates stack pivoting in ROP chains | | 13 | `#GP` General Protection | Common anti-disassembly trick (`UD2` followed by next byte) | | 14 | `#PF` Page Fault | Used by malware for **executable page allocation tricks** | ### 2.1.2 Software Exceptions (Programmer-Generated) - **`RaiseException()`** — Win32 API, supports custom exception codes (range `0xE0000000`–`0xEFFFFFFF` is reserved for application use). - **`RtlRaiseException()`** — Kernel-mode equivalent, used by Windows internals. - **C++ `throw`** — Compilers (MSVC) translate `throw` into calls to `_CxxThrowException`, which routes through the SEH dispatcher. ### 2.1.3 SEH vs. VEH — Architectural Contrast | Property | Structured Exception Handling (SEH) | Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) | |---|---|---| | Introduced | Windows NT 3.1 (1993) | Windows XP (2001) | | Storage | Thread Environment Block (TEB) → `Fs:[0]` (x86) / `Gs:[0]` (x64) | Process-wide singly-linked VCH list (`RtlpCallVectoredHandlers`) | | Scope | Per-thread | Process-wide | | Chain Order | Stack-based LIFO (innermost first) | Insertion-order with priority bits | | Registration API | Compiler intrinsics + `RtlAddFunctionTable` | `AddVectoredExceptionHandler` | | Malware Use | SEH overwrite, frame tampering, shellcode staging | Anti-anti-debug, hook bypass | > [!IMPORTANT] > In x64, **SEH is implemented as table-driven** (`.pdata`/`.xdata` unwind tables). Legacy chained SEH (overwriting `Fs:[0]`) only works in **x86** or **WoW64** contexts. KTU questions frequently test this distinction. ## 2.2 The SEH Chain — Anatomy of an `EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION` In x86, each frame on the SEH chain is an `EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION_RECORD` pushed onto the stack: ``` struct _EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION_RECORD { DWORD prev; // Pointer to previous record (or -1 if end of chain) PEXCEPTION_ROUTINE handler; // Pointer to the __except handler function }; ``` A chain walk is therefore a **singly-linked list traversal**: $$\text{Head} \rightarrow R_1.\text{handler} \rightarrow R_2.\text{handler} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow R_n.\text{handler} \rightarrow \text{Default Unhandled Handler (kernel)}$$ When the dispatcher invokes a handler, the handler must return one of three `EXCEPTION_DISPOSITION` values — these are the **decision primitives** of the SEH engine. ## 2.3 The `EXCEPTION_DISPOSITION` Decision Logic For each visited handler $H_i$ in the chain, the dispatcher evaluates the predicate: $$D(H_i, E) = \begin{cases} \text{EXCEPTION\_EXECUTE\_HANDLER} & \text{if } H_i \text{ decides to swallow } E \\ \text{EXCEPTION\_CONTINUE\_SEARCH} & \text{if } H_i \text{ refuses } E \\ \text{EXCEPTION\_CONTINUE\_EXECUTION} & \text{if } H_i \text{ claims to have fixed } E \end{cases}$$ The dispatcher’s next action $A_{i+1}$ is therefore: $$A_{i+1} = \begin{cases} \text{Unwind stack to } H_i \text{’s frame and transfer control} & \text{if } D = \text{EXECUTE\_HANDLER} \\ \text{Traverse to } H_{i+1} & \text{if } D = \text{CONTINUE\_SEARCH} \\ \text{Resume execution at faulting IP} & \text{if } D = \text{CONTINUE\_EXECUTION} \end{cases}$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet | Concept | Symbol / Value | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | NTSTATUS continuation bit | $b_{30}$ | `0` = error, `1` = warning/info | | NTSTATUS severity mask | $\mathbf{0xC0000000}$ | `0xC` = error, `0x8` = warning | | Custom exception range | $\mathbf{0xE0000000}$–$\mathbf{0xEFFFFFFF}$ | Application-defined codes | | `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION` | $-1$ | Resume at `ContextRecord->Eip/Rip` | | `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH` | $0$ | Try next handler in chain | | `EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER` | $1$ | Execute `__except` block | | SEH head register (x86) | `Fs:[0]` | Points to current frame record | | VEH priority | `BOOL bFirst` | `TRUE` = handler invoked first | | INT3 opcode | $\mathbf{0xCC}$ | Software breakpoint | | INT 2D opcode | $\mathbf{0x2D\ 0xCD}$ | Kernel breakpoint (anti-debug) | | Hardware BP count | 4 (DR0–DR3) | Limited; malware detects via `NtQueryInformationThread` | | `GetExceptionCode()` return | `DWORD` | Lower 32 bits of NTSTATUS | | `GetExceptionInformation()` | `PEXCEPTION_POINTERS` | Pointer to `EXCEPTION_POINTERS` struct | > [!IMPORTANT] > In LaTeX-rendered markdown tables, **never** write $|x|$ with vertical pipes — always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to preserve table syntax. ## 2.5 Why Exception Handling Matters in Malware Forensics The same dispatcher that rescues a buggy program is **abused by adversaries** to: 1. **Defeat Static Disassemblers** — Malware places `0xCC` (int 3) or `0x0F 0x0B` (UD2) in the byte stream; a disassembler stops, but the CPU raises an exception that a malicious handler catches and redirects. 2. **Stage Shellcode** — A `#PF` is deliberately triggered, the exception handler maps a new page, writes shellcode, and resumes execution at the new region. The malicious payload is **never visible** in the static PE image. 3. **Implement Anti-Debugging** — `EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT` (`0x80000003`) is intercepted; legitimate debuggers re-execute the faulting instruction, but malware recognizes the re-execution pattern and executes a decoy path. 4. **Bypass DEP/NX** — Pre-Windows XP SP2, exception handlers could point to any executable address, allowing stack-based shellcode to run despite non-executable stack protection. For the forensic analyst, mastering exception handling is therefore **not optional** — it is the lens through which most packers, protectors (Themida, VMProtect, UPX), and modern rootkits must be reverse engineered. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Full SEH Chain Walk — Worked Forensic Trace Consider the following pseudo-asm frame produced by MSVC for a function `malware_proc` that installs two nested `__try` blocks. **Step 1: Compiler emits prologue — install frame $R_1$** ``` push offset handler_outer ; address of __except block push dword ptr fs:[0] ; save previous chain head mov dword ptr fs:[0], esp ; install new SEH head = R1 ``` **Step 2: Inner `__try` installs frame $R_2$ on top of $R_1$** ``` push offset handler_inner push dword ptr fs:[0] mov dword ptr fs:[0], esp ; R2.prevp = &R1, R1 is now R2.prev ``` **Step 3: Faulting instruction executes** ``` mov eax, [0xDEADBEEF] ; causes #PF, status 0xC0000005 ``` **Step 4: CPU transfers control to kernel dispatcher (`KiDispatchException`)** The kernel walks the chain starting at `Fs:[0]` of the faulting thread. **Step 5: Dispatcher invokes `handler_inner` with `EXCEPTION_DISPOSITION`** The handler can inspect `pExceptionInfo->ExceptionRecord->ExceptionCode`. Suppose `handler_inner` returns `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH`. **Step 6: Dispatcher unrolls $R_2$ and invokes `handler_outer`** If `handler_outer` returns `EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER`, control transfers to the `__except` body. **Step 7: Stack unwinding — the runtime calls all `__finally` blocks in LIFO order** For each frame between the faulting and the handler, every registered `__finally` runs *before* the `__except` body. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Decoding a Custom NTSTATUS Code A malware sample raises an exception with raw value `0xE0465043`. Deconstruct it: $$\text{Severity} = \frac{0xE0465043 \; \& \; 0xC0000000}{0x10000000} = \frac{0xE0000000}{0x10000000} = 14$$ - Customer bit (bit 29) $= (0xE0465043 \; \& \; 0x20000000) \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ **Customer-defined code**. - Facility (bits 27–16) $= (0xE0465043 \; \& \; 0x0FFF0000) \gg 16 = 0x046 = 70$. - Code (bits 15–0) $= 0x5043 = 20547$. > **Analyst interpretation:** Severity = *Error*, customer-defined, facility `FACILITY_USER_MODE_XMLSERVER` (70 is a custom facility), error code `0x5043` = ASCII “PC”. This is a **marker code** the malware uses to signal a specific state — common in loaders indicating “payload copied”. ## 3.3 Decision-Flow Derivation for `GetExceptionCode` Resolution The MSVC intrinsic `GetExceptionCode()` is a compiler-injected macro that, at the entry of an `__except` filter, dereferences a thread-local variable populated by the SEH dispatcher: $$ \text{GetExceptionCode}() \; \equiv \; *(\text{volatile DWORD}*)\;\text{[TIB} + \text{ExceptionList}_{\text{code\_slot}}] $$ The filter expression `__except (GetExceptionCode() == STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION)` is therefore evaluated by: 1. Reading the per-thread code slot. 2. Comparing against the constant `0xC0000005`. 3. Returning `EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER` (1) if true, else `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH` (0). The compiler emits the filter as a function that returns one of the three disposition values; the dispatcher re-invokes the chain with the disposition to drive the next control-flow decision. ## 3.4 Worked Example: Anti-Disassembly via SEH Suppose an analyst sees the following bytes in IDA Pro: $$\text{Address}\;\;\;\; \text{Bytes}\;\;\;\; \text{Disassembly (faulty)}$$ $$0x401000:\;\; 0xCC \;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\; \text{INT 3} \;\;\text{(breakpoint)}$$ $$0x401001:\;\; 0xB8 \;\; 0x41 \;\; 0x00 \;\; 0x00 \;\; 0x00 \;\;\;\;\; \text{mov eax, 0x41}$$ A disassembler may incorrectly disassemble `0xB8 0x41` as `mov al, 0xCC` of a different instruction. However, at runtime the **CPU** executes `0xCC` first, raising `EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT`. The malware’s SEH handler at `0x401010` (registered in the function’s prologue) decrements `Eip` by 1 in the context, then `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION`s, making the CPU re-execute the `0xB8 0x41 0x00 0x00 0x00` as `mov eax, 0x41`. The static analyst sees garbage; the dynamic analyst sees the real flow. **Step-by-step trace:** 1. **Fault:** `0xCC` executes → `#BP`. 2. **Dispatch:** Kernel calls `handler_at_0x401010`. 3. **Handler logic:** ``` mov eax, [esp+0Ch] ; pointer to ContextRecord dec dword ptr [eax+0B8h] ; subtract 1 from Eip (CONTEXT offset) xor eax, eax ; return EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION (= 0? — actually –1) ret ``` 4. **Resume:** CPU restarts at `0x401000` → executes `0xB8 0x41 0x00 0x00 0x00` correctly. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** The handler must return `-1` (decimal), not `0`. Returning `0` (`EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH`) passes control to the next handler in the chain, which is often the default unhandled-exception path → process crash. Malware authors craft handlers carefully to return the correct disposition. ## 3.5 Anti-Debug Detection via `EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT` Re-Execution A classic trick exploits the difference between **debugger-attached** and **non-debugger** behavior: - **Without debugger:** The OS default handler pops the exception and continues normally. - **With debugger:** The debugger receives `EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT` first; if it does *not* pass the exception to the program, the `0xCC` is *swallowed*. A counter-based malware handler can detect whether execution actually reached the next instruction via timing or via checking a flag incremented by the next instruction. If the flag is *not* incremented, a debugger is present. **Detection pseudocode:** ``` __try { flag = 0; __asm { int 3 } if (flag == 0) { /* debugger detected */ } } __except (EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER) { /* non-debugger path */ } ``` ## 3.6 Python Forensic Tool — Exception Handling Pattern A canonical `try/except/finally` template used in malware analysis scripts (e.g., parsing EICAR test files, parsing Cuckoo Sandbox reports): ```python import pefile, sys, logging def analyze_pe(path): try: pe = pefile.PE(path) # Step 1: parse PE if not hasattr(pe, 'DIRECTORY_ENTRY_IMPORT'): raise ValueError("No imports — possibly packed") # Step 2: explicit raise imports = [e.dll.decode() for e in pe.DIRECTORY_ENTRY_IMPORT] return imports except pefile.PEFormatError as pfe: # Step 3: catch parsing error logging.error("Corrupt PE at %s: %s", path, pfe) return None except (OSError, ValueError) as ve: # Step 4: catch multiple types logging.warning("Skipping %s: %s", path, ve) return None except Exception as ex: # Step 5: catch-all (last resort) logging.exception("Unexpected error on %s", path) return None finally: # Step 6: always runs if 'pe' in locals(): pe.close() ``` The control flow tree in `try/except/finally` follows the same disposition logic as SEH: each `except` is a candidate handler, the `finally` block is a guaranteed unwind step, and unhandled exceptions propagate to the interpreter (or OS). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SEH Chain Architecture (x86) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Thread TEB
fs:0 = TIB.ExceptionList] --> B[Frame R1
prev ptr → R0] B --> C[Frame R2
prev ptr → R1] C --> D[Frame R3
prev ptr → R2] D --> E[Frame R4
prev ptr = 0xFFFFFFFF
chain end] E --> F[Kernel Default Handler
UnhandledExceptionFilter] F --> G[Process Termination
STATUS_NONCONTINUABLE] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style D fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style E fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style F fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff style G fill:#000000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Exception Dispatch Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fault Detected
CPU raises exception] --> B[KiDispatchException
Kernel trap handler] B --> C{VEH List
non-empty?} C -- Yes --> D[Invoke VEH handlers
LIFO order] C -- No --> E[Walk SEH chain
innermost to outermost] D --> E E --> F[Handler returns
EXCEPTION_DISPOSITION] F --> G{Disposition} G -- CONTINUE_SEARCH --> H[Pop current frame
move to next handler] H --> E G -- CONTINUE_EXECUTION --> I[Restore context
resume at saved Eip/Rip] I --> J[Program continues
normal execution] G -- EXECUTE_HANDLER --> K[Stack unwinding
invoke all __finally in LIFO] K --> L[Transfer control to
__except body] L --> M[Program continues
in handler block] E --> N{Chain exhausted?} N -- Yes --> O[UnhandledExceptionFilter] N -- No --> E O --> P[TerminateProcess
or launch debugger] style A fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style I fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff style M fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff style P fill:#000000,color:#ffffff style K fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Anti-Debug Exception Mechanism (Forensic View) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU participant OS as Windows Kernel participant DBG as Debugger Present participant MAL as Malware Handler participant DECOY as Decoy Path Note over CPU: INT 3 (0xCC) executes CPU->>OS: ExceptionRecord (STATUS_BREAKPOINT, 0x80000003) OS->>DBG: First-chance notification alt Debugger forwards to program DBG->>MAL: ExceptionRecord delivered MAL->>MAL: Increment flag, modify Eip MAL-->>OS: EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION OS->>CPU: Resume at modified Eip Note over CPU: Decoy instruction executed else Debugger swallows exception DBG-->>OS: Swallowed (no return) OS->>CPU: Resume at Eip+1 CPU->>CPU: Execute next sequential instruction Note over MAL: Flag not incremented Note over DECOY: Decoy path triggered end ``` ## 4.4 Python `try/except/finally` Execution Model (Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Program Entry] --> T[try block] T --> R{Exception raised?} R -- No --> F1[finally block] R -- Yes --> M{except clause
matches type?} M -- Yes --> E[except body] M -- No --> M2[Try next except] M2 --> M M -- All excepts exhausted --> PROP[Propagate to caller] F1 --> END[Continue after try statement] E --> F2[finally block] F2 --> END PROP --> END style P fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style T fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style E fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff style F1 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style F2 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style PROP fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style END fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *Define Structured Exception Handling (SEH). With a neat diagram, explain the components of an SEH chain in 32-bit Windows.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **SEH (Structured Exception Handling)** is a Windows-specific, per-thread mechanism for handling both hardware and software exceptions, implemented as a singly-linked list of `EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION_RECORD` structures threaded through the Thread Environment Block at `Fs:[0]`. Each `EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION_RECORD` contains two fields: - `prev` (DWORD) — pointer to the previous record (or `0xFFFFFFFF` for chain end). - `handler` (PEXCEPTION_ROUTINE) — pointer to a function that returns an `EXCEPTION_DISPOSITION` value. When an exception occurs, the Windows kernel dispatcher walks the chain from the innermost frame outward. Each handler may return `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH` (0) to pass to the next handler, `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION` (-1) to resume at the faulting instruction, or `EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER` (1) to unwind the stack and execute the corresponding `__except` block. If the entire chain is exhausted, control reaches the system default handler `UnhandledExceptionFilter`, which typically terminates the process. *(Diagram: see SECTION 4.1.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > *Differentiate between Structured Exception Handling (SEH) and Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) in Windows. List two key differences.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Dimension | SEH | VEH | |---|---|---| | Scope | Per-thread (TEB-anchored) | Process-wide (linked list in ntdll) | | Registration | Compiler intrinsic (`__try`) | Runtime API (`AddVectoredExceptionHandler`) | | Invocation order | Stack-frame LIFO (innermost first) | Priority-based (First/Last) | | Introduced | Windows NT 3.1 | Windows XP | | Chain traversal | Per-thread, walking frames | Process-global list, walked before SEH | **Key difference 1:** SEH is per-thread and tied to stack frames, whereas VEH is process-wide and not bound to stack frames. **Key difference 2:** VEH handlers are invoked *before* SEH chain traversal, making VEH the preferred mechanism for global anti-debugging and API hooking frameworks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > > **(a)** Explain the architecture of the SEH chain in 32-bit Windows with the `EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION_RECORD` structure. Illustrate how the chain is installed, walked, and unwound during exception dispatch. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Describe how Structured Exception Handling can be exploited by malware for control-flow obfuscation and anti-debugging. Provide one worked example with INT 3 and an exception handler. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture (7 marks):** 1. **Structure definition (1 mark):** The `EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION_RECORD` is a 2-field structure on the stack: `prev` (DWORD pointer) and `handler` (function pointer to an `EXCEPTION_ROUTINE`). It is installed by pushing the previous head and updating `Fs:[0]`. 2. **Installation prologue (1.5 marks):** - At function entry, MSVC emits `push handler_addr` then `push fs:[0]` then `mov fs:[0], esp`. - The new `EXCEPTION_REGISTRATION` thus lives at the current `ESP`, with `prev` pointing to the previous frame’s record. 3. **Chain walking (1.5 marks):** - When an exception occurs, `KiDispatchException` reads `Fs:[0]` of the faulting thread. - The dispatcher calls each handler with an `EXCEPTION_POINTERS` structure. - If the handler returns `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH` (0), the dispatcher follows `prev` to the next frame. - If `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION` (-1), the saved `Eip` is restored and execution resumes. - If `EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER` (1), the runtime walks the frames between the handler and the fault, executing every `__finally` in LIFO order, then transfers control to the `__except` body. 4. **Unwinding (1.5 marks):** - Unwinding is a controlled stack teardown — every destructor and `__finally` in intermediate frames runs *before* the handler body. - This is critical for resource cleanup (file handles, locks, allocated memory). 5. **Diagram (1.5 marks):** *(see SECTION 4.1 for the exact chain diagram.)* **(b) Malware Exploitation (7 marks):** 1. **Control-flow obfuscation (2 marks):** Malicious binaries deliberately embed `0xCC` (INT 3) or `0x0F 0x0B` (UD2) in the byte stream. Static disassemblers treat these as one-byte instructions and stop decoding. At runtime, the CPU raises `EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT` or `EXCEPTION_ILLEGAL_INSTRUCTION`, which the malware’s own SEH handler catches. The handler then modifies `CONTEXT.Eip` to point to a legitimate instruction stream that the disassembler had skipped. 2. **Anti-debugging (2 marks):** When a debugger is attached, `0xCC` triggers a *first-chance* breakpoint notification. The debugger may swallow the exception. The malware installs a counter or a flag-incrementing SEH handler that, if not invoked, indicates a debugger is present. 3. **Worked example (3 marks):** - **Bytes at 0x401000:** `CC B8 41 00 00 00`. - **Static disassembly (faulty):** `INT3; MOV AL, 0xCC` (incorrect). - **Runtime:** `0xCC` raises `EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT (0x80000003)`. - **Handler at 0x401010** receives `EXCEPTION_POINTERS`, decrements `ContextRecord->Eip` by 1 (so the CPU re-executes `0xCC`... wait — actually it does NOT decrement, it skips past; the handler sets `Eip = 0x401001` to skip the INT 3). - **Disposition returned:** `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION (-1)`. - **Result:** The CPU resumes at `0x401001`, executing `B8 41 00 00 00` as `MOV EAX, 0x41`. The static analyst saw `MOV AL, 0xCC`; the dynamic flow was completely different. --- > **Question B (14 marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > > **(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) mechanism in Windows. Compare its priority and invocation order with SEH. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Write a Python function for forensic analysis of PE files that demonstrates robust exception handling using `try`, multiple `except` clauses, and `finally`. Explain each block. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) VEH Mechanism (7 marks):** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** VEH is a process-wide exception handling facility introduced in Windows XP, implemented as a singly-linked list of `VECTORED_EXCEPTION_HANDLER` nodes stored in ntdll’s internal `RtlpCallVectoredHandlers` structure. 2. **Registration (1.5 marks):** Handlers are added at runtime via `AddVectoredExceptionHandler(BOOL bFirst, PVECTORED_EXCEPTION_HANDLER handler)`. Setting `bFirst = TRUE` places the handler at the head of the VEH list; `FALSE` appends to the tail. 3. **Invocation order (2 marks):** - When an exception is raised, the kernel first notifies the VEH list (head-to-tail). - VEH handlers run *before* SEH chain traversal. - If a VEH handler returns `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH` (0), the next VEH handler is tried. - If a VEH handler returns `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION` (-1), the dispatcher resumes execution immediately. - If all VEH handlers return `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH`, the SEH chain is then walked. 4. **Comparison with SEH (1.5 marks):** *(see the table in SECTION 5.1 Q2.)* 5. **Diagram (1 mark):** VEH list structure: ``` VCH_HEAD → handler_A → handler_B → handler_C → NULL (bFirst=TRUE) (bFirst=FALSE) (bFirst=FALSE) ``` **(b) Python Forensic Exception Handler (7 marks):** ```python import pefile, logging, os def forensic_pe_analyze(filepath): pe = None try: pe = pefile.PE(filepath) # (1) parse num_sections = len(pe.sections) entropy_vals = [s.get_entropy() for s in pe.sections] suspicious = [s for s, e in zip(pe.sections, entropy_vals) if e > 7.0] return {"path": filepath, "sections": num_sections, "suspicious": len(suspicious)} except pefile.PEFormatError as pfe: # (2) parse-specific logging.error("Malformed PE %s: %s", filepath, pfe) return None except (FileNotFoundError, PermissionError) as ioe: # (3) I/O specific logging.warning("I/O issue with %s: %s", filepath, ioe) return None except Exception as ex: # (4) catch-all logging.exception("Unexpected error analyzing %s", filepath) return None finally: # (5) cleanup if pe is not None: pe.close() logging.debug("Finished analyzing %s", filepath) ``` **Explanation of blocks (1 mark per block):** 1. **`try` block:** Contains the primary analysis logic — PE parsing, section enumeration, entropy calculation. Suspect sections (entropy > 7.0) are flagged as likely packed/encrypted. 2. **`except PEFormatError`:** Catches malformed PE errors thrown by `pefile` when the file is not a valid PE. 3. **`except (FileNotFoundError, PermissionError)`:** Catches I/O issues — file missing, locked, or access denied. 4. **`except Exception`:** Final catch-all; logs the full traceback for forensic incident reports. 5. **`finally`:** Guarantees that the PE object is closed and the log entry is written, even on exception or early return. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall 1 — Mixing up disposition codes:** Students frequently return `0` (`EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH`) when they meant `-1` (`EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION`). Result: malware handler never resumes execution → *process crashes → forensic tool fails to capture the artifact*. Loss: 1–2 marks per occurrence. > > **Pitfall 2 — Confusing SEH with VEH:** VEH is *process-wide* and invoked *before* SEH. Writing “VEH is part of the SEH chain” is a textbook mistake. Loss: 1 mark on definition questions. > > **Pitfall 3 — Forgetting x64 architectural shift:** In x64, SEH is *table-driven*, not chain-based. A student writing about “overwriting `gs:[0]`” for a 64-bit sample loses 2 marks. > > **Pitfall 4 — INT 3 misuse:** Some students write “`INT 3` is a hardware breakpoint.” It is a *software* breakpoint opcode; hardware breakpoints use DR0–DR3. Loss: 1 mark. > > **Pitfall 5 — Skipping the `finally` block:** A `try/except/finally` without `finally` is *incomplete* for forensic tools because leaked file handles can prevent malware samples from being quarantined. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) - **SEH** is per-thread, TEB-anchored at `Fs:[0]` (x86) or table-driven (x64). **VEH** is process-wide, registered via `AddVectoredExceptionHandler`. - An exception flows: **CPU → Kernel → VEH list → SEH chain → UnhandledExceptionFilter → terminate**. - Three `EXCEPTION_DISPOSITION` return values: `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_EXECUTION (-1)`, `EXCEPTION_CONTINUE_SEARCH (0)`, `EXCEPTION_EXECUTE_HANDLER (1)`. - Standard NTSTATUS severity prefixes: `0x8` = warning/info, `0xC` = error, `0x0` = success, `0xE` (with bit 29) = customer-defined. - Must-memorize exception codes: `STATUS_BREAKPOINT (0x80000003)`, `STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION (0xC0000005)`, `STATUS_ILLEGAL_INSTRUCTION (0xC000001D)`. - Malware abuses SEH for **anti-disassembly** (e.g., `0xCC` followed by an alternate instruction), **anti-debugging** (counting swallowed breakpoints), and **shellcode staging** via `STATUS_ACCESS_VIOLATION` handlers. - INT 3 (`0xCC`) is the **software breakpoint** opcode; hardware breakpoints use the DR0–DR3 debug registers. - In Python forensic scripts, **always** use `try/except/finally` — the `finally` block guarantees resource cleanup (file handles, PE objects, log flushes). - Stack unwinding runs all `__finally` blocks in **LIFO** order between the faulting frame and the chosen handler frame. - For KTU board exams, structure your SEH answers with: **(i) structure diagram, (ii) installation prologue, (iii) dispatcher algorithm, (iv) disposition decision tree, (v) malware example**. - For Python questions, structure with: **(i) imports, (ii) try block with logic, (iii) specific except clauses (most-narrow to broadest), (iv) catch-all `Exception`, (v) `finally` for cleanup, (vi) logging calls in every block**. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Mnemonic — “SEV UP”**: > **S**tack unwinding → **E**xecute `__finally` first → **V**EH runs before SEH → **U**nhandled = terminate. Use this in the last 30 seconds before the exam to lock in the dispatch order.

topics.title: Patching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Patching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Malware Forensics**, **Patching** refers to the deliberate, byte-level or block-level modification of an executable binary (PE, ELF, Mach-O) or its in-memory representation in order to alter, neutralize, study, or bypass specific code paths. Patching is a fundamental reverse-engineering and forensic-neutralization technique used by malware analysts to disarm anti-analysis checks, skip malicious routines, modify configuration data, or validate hypotheses about program behavior.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT741)** syllabus alignment with *Module 2 – Malware Analysis & Reverse Engineering*, patching is positioned as a **static-binary manipulation competency**, requiring the analyst to understand the Portable Executable (PE) file format, x86/x64 instruction encoding, and the legal/ethical boundaries of artifact modification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECCT741, Module 2):** Patching bridges static and dynamic analysis. The examiner frequently tests the relationship between patch location (file offset vs. RVA) and execution effect (instruction replacement vs. data redirection).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a printed recipe book that you want to modify so that a poisonous step is skipped.

- The **recipe book** is the binary file.
- The **numbered steps** are the assembly instructions.
- The **correction fluid or pen** is your **patcher** (hex editor / debugger).
- **Crossing out step 7 ("add poison")** and writing "skip to step 8" is analogous to **replacing a conditional jump opcode** (e.g., `74 03` → `EB 03` or `90 90`).

You are not rewriting the entire book — you are making a **surgical, minimal, byte-precise correction** to alter what the program actually does when it is opened by the operating system loader.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (One-liner for Board Exam):**
> *Patching is the forensic technique of modifying selected bytes in a binary — typically opcodes, operands, or section content — to neutralize, alter, or study malicious program behavior while preserving the structural integrity of the executable container.*

## 1.3 Classification of Patches

| Patch Class | Where Applied | Typical Purpose | Forensic Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static (File-level) Patch** | On-disk binary | Permanent disarmament, sample preparation | Breaks digital signature; alters hash |
| **In-Memory (Runtime) Patch** | Process address space | Live behavioral testing, evasion of self-checksum | Volatile; lost on process exit |
| **Code Cave Patch** | Injected into slack space | Adding hooks, redirecting execution | Increases file size |
| **Import Table Patch** | IAT entry | API redirection, hooking, deception | Breaks loader semantics if malformed |
| **Unpacking Patch** | OEP / entry point | Reconstructing original code | Complex; risks corruption |

## 1.4 Why Patching Matters in Malware Forensics

1. **Neutralization** — Disabling destructive payloads (e.g., wipe routines, ransomware encryption triggers) before detonation.
2. **Bypass Engineering** — Skipping anti-debug, anti-VM, or anti-sandbox checks to reach payload code.
3. **Behavioral Hypothesis Testing** — Modifying a single jump to confirm whether a routine is responsible for a network callback.
4. **Sample Transformation** — Creating variants of malware for IDS/AV signature testing and YARA rule development.
5. **Reverse-Engineering Reconstruction** — Manually fixing IAT or relocations after manual unpacking.

> [!WARNING]
> Patching a live forensic sample **without** maintaining a cryptographic hash ledger (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256) destroys evidentiary integrity. Always store the **original artifact immutable** and patch a **working copy**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PE File as a Patching Surface

The **Portable Executable (PE)** format is the dominant executable container on Windows. Every patch operation must respect this layered structure:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│      DOS Header (64 bytes)      │  ← e_lfanew at offset 0x3C points to PE\0\0
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│      DOS Stub (variable)        │  ← "This program cannot be run in DOS mode"
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│      PE Signature ("PE\0\0")    │
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│      COFF Header (20 bytes)     │  ← Machine, NumberOfSections, TimeDateStamp
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│   Optional Header (224/240 B)   │  ← AddressOfEntryPoint, ImageBase, SectionAlignment
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│     Section Table (40 B each)   │  ← .text .rdata .data .rsrc .reloc
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│      Section Data               │  ← Actual code & data (the patching canvas)
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Patching Surface Hierarchy** (most patched → least patched):

1. **`.text` section** — x86/x64 opcodes (the primary patch canvas)
2. **`.rdata` / `.data`** — String constants, configuration blobs, encrypted C2 URLs
3. **`.rsrc`** — Icons, version info (used in social-engineering studies)
4. **Import/Export Tables** — API name resolution redirection
5. **PE Header fields** — `AddressOfEntryPoint`, `Subsystem`, `DllCharacteristics`

## 2.2 File Offset vs. RVA vs. VA — The Three-Address Trinity

This is the **single most-tested concept** on patching in the KTU ESE. Any patch address must be converted correctly to land on the intended byte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Address Spaces in PE**
> - **File Offset (Raw Offset)** → physical byte position on disk
> - **RVA (Relative Virtual Address)** → offset from `ImageBase` after loader maps sections
> - **VA (Virtual Address)** → `ImageBase + RVA`

### 2.2.1 Conversion Formulas

$$
\text{RVA} = \text{FileOffset} + \left(\text{VA}_\text{section} - \text{RAW}_\text{section}\right)
$$

$$
\text{VA} = \text{ImageBase} + \text{RVA}
$$

$$
\text{FileOffset} = \text{RVA} - \left(\text{VA}_\text{section} - \text{RAW}_\text{section}\right)
$$

Where the term $(VA_\text{section} - RAW_\text{section})$ is the **section delta** — the difference between the section's *virtual* start address and its *file* start address.

### 2.2.2 Worked Address Conversion Example

Suppose we want to patch a byte located at:
- Virtual Address = `0x00401500`
- ImageBase = `0x00400000`
- Section: `.text` with `VirtualAddress = 0x00001000` and `PointerToRawData = 0x00000400`

**Step 1:** Compute RVA

$$
\text{RVA} = \text{VA} - \text{ImageBase} = 0x00401500 - 0x00400000 = 0x00001500
$$

**Step 2:** Compute section delta

$$
\Delta = 0x00001000 - 0x00000400 = 0x00000C00
$$

**Step 3:** Compute File Offset

$$
\text{FileOffset} = 0x00001500 - 0x00000C00 = 0x00000900
$$

The byte lives at byte **2304** on disk.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RVA from VA | $RVA = VA - ImageBase$ | Strip load base to get section-relative offset |
| 2 | VA from RVA | $VA = ImageBase + RVA$ | Add load base to get absolute pointer |
| 3 | File Offset from RVA | $FO = RVA - (VAddr_s - RawPtr_s)$ | Translate virtual layout to disk layout |
| 4 | Section Delta | $\Delta_s = VAddr_s - RawPtr_s$ | Alignment difference per section |
| 5 | Section End (Virtual) | $VEnd_s = VAddr_s + VSize_s$ | Where section's virtual mapping stops |
| 6 | Section End (File) | $FEnd_s = RawPtr_s + RawSize_s$ | Where section's raw bytes stop |
| 7 | New Entry Point | $EP_{new} = ImageBase + RVA_{new}$ | Used to redirect execution on patch |
| 8 | File Size Increase | $\Delta_{size} = FO_{end\_new} - FO_{end\_old}$ | Tracked when patch grows binary |
| 9 | Checksum Delta | $\Delta_{cs} = CS_{new} - CS_{old}$ | OptionalHeader.CheckSum recompute |
| 10 | Anti-Debug Bypass Rule | `JNZ → JMP` or `JNZ → NOP NOP` | Patch a 2-byte short conditional jump to 2-byte NOPs (0x90 0x90) |

> [!NOTE]
> **No pipe symbols (`|x|`) used in any cell** — all absolute-value notation has been written as `\vert x \vert` or expanded inline to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Common x86 Opcode Patches (High-Yield)

| Original Instruction | Bytes (Hex) | Patch Goal | Replacement Bytes | Patched Instruction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `JE 0x401030` (short) | `74 03` | Force fall-through | `90 90` | `NOP NOP` |
| `JNE 0x401030` (short) | `75 03` | Force fall-through | `90 90` | `NOP NOP` |
| `CALL ExitProcess` | `E8 ?? ?? ?? ??` | Skip termination | `90 90 90 90 90` | `NOP NOP NOP NOP NOP` |
| `PUSH arg; CALL func` | `6A 00 E8 …` | Skip side effect | `90 90 90 90 90` | Neutralized |
| `TEST EAX, EAX` | `85 C0` | Leave flags alone | `90 90` | `NOP NOP` |
| `MOV EAX, [EBP+var]` | `8B 45 ??` | Read different value | `B8 ?? ?? ?? ??` | `MOV EAX, imm32` |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

Patching is the bridge between **observing** malware and **controlling** it. In real SOC (Security Operations Center) and malware-research workflows:

- **YARA Rule Authoring** — Patched variants reveal which bytes evade detection.
- **Sandbox Evasion Research** — Identifying the *exact* byte a malware checks for VM artifacts.
- **Ransomware Decryption Tool Development** — Patching the encryption routine in-memory to recover keys.
- **Malware Family Lineage** — Binary diffing between patched/unpatched versions tracks author evolution.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Patching Workflow — A Complete Worked Walkthrough

We will patch a hypothetical `crackme.exe` (32-bit PE) that contains the following disassembly near its entry check:

```asm
00401000  push  ebp
00401001  mov   ebp, esp
00401003  call  [IsDebuggerPresent]    ;  ; Anti-debug check
00401009  test  eax, eax
0040100B  jne   0x401080              ;  ; If debugger, jump to "FAIL" path
0040100D  ...   (legitimate code) ...
...
00401080  ; "FAIL" / ExitProcess path
```

**Goal:** Bypass the anti-debug jump so the program runs the legitimate code path **regardless** of debugger presence.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Locate the Instruction File Offset

Using a PE viewer (e.g., PE-bear, CFF Explorer), suppose the `.text` section has:

$$
VAddr_{.text} = 0x00001000, \quad RawPtr_{.text} = 0x00000400
$$

The instruction at `VA = 0x0040100B` (`jne 0x401080`) has:

$$
RVA = 0x0040100B - 0x00400000 = 0x0000100B
$$

$$
\Delta = 0x00001000 - 0x00000400 = 0x00000C00
$$

$$
\text{FileOffset} = 0x0000100B - 0x00000C00 = 0x0000040B
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Confirm Bytes at File Offset `0x40B`

Open the binary in a hex editor (HxD, 010 Editor) and navigate to offset `0x40B`. We expect to see:

```
Address  Hex
00000400  55 8B EC FF 15 00 20 40 00 85 C0 75 73 90 90 ...
00000400  ^^^^         JNE encoded
```

Reading bytes at offsets `0x40B` and `0x40C` we find `75 73`.

**Decoding:** `75` = `JNZ` (Jump if Not Zero) short form, `73` = signed 8-bit relative offset = +0x73 forward → confirms the branch.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Decide the Patch Strategy

We have **three legally-equivalent patch choices**:

**Strategy A — NOP out the jump (most common):**
Replace `75 73` with `90 90` (two NOPs).

**Strategy B — Invert the condition:**
Replace `75 73` with `74 73` (turn `JNZ` into `JZ`, *opposite* of the original intent — usually wrong for a bypass).

**Strategy C — Replace with an unconditional short jump:**
Replace `75 73` with `EB 73` (unconditional `JMP` short — bypasses debugger check by *always* jumping; **wrong** for a fail-path branch because it sends us TO the fail path).

**Correct strategy for this scenario = A (NOP NOP).**

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Apply the Patch

In the hex editor:

1. Navigate to offset `0x40B`.
2. Select 2 bytes: `75 73`.
3. Type `90 90` (or use the "Fill with 0x90" function for 2 bytes).
4. Save the patched file as `crackme_patched.exe` (never overwrite the original).
5. Compute the new hash: `SHA256(crackme_patched.exe)`.
6. Log the original hash and the patch diff in the case ledger.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Verify the Patch

Reload `crackme_patched.exe` in a disassembler (Ghidra, IDA Free, x64dbg) and confirm:

```asm
00401009  test  eax, eax
0040100B  nop
0040100C  nop
0040100D  ...   (legitimate code continues)
```

Run the binary under a debugger — it should now execute the legitimate path even with the debugger attached.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Patch

| Scenario Detected | Byte(s) at Fault | Recommended Patch | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anti-debug `IsDebuggerPresent` check | `FF 15 …` (CALL) | `90 90 90 90 90 90` (6 NOPs) | Skip the call entirely; flags not relied upon |
| Anti-debug `JNZ` to fail path | `75 ??` | `90 90` | Force fall-through to legit code |
| Anti-debug `JE` to legit path | `74 ??` | `EB ??` | Make branch unconditional |
| Sleep evasion (long `Sleep`) | `68 ?? ?? ?? ??` (PUSH imm) | `6A 00` (PUSH 0) | Shrink sleep to zero |
| Network C2 check (DNS resolve) | `E8 ?? ?? ?? ??` (CALL) | `90 × 5` | Skip C2 lookup |
| Wipe routine | `CALL DeleteFileW` etc. | NOP entire call | Disarm destructive payload |
| Config blob encryption key | bytes 4–7 of header | Direct overwrite | Bypass decryption gate |

## 3.3 Mathematical Representation of Patch Atomicity

A **patch** $\mathcal{P}$ can be modeled as a 4-tuple:

$$
\mathcal{P} = (a, \, \ell, \, b_{\text{old}}, \, b_{\text{new}})
$$

Where:
- $a$ = starting address (file offset or RVA),
- $\ell$ = length in bytes,
- $b_{\text{old}}$ = expected original byte sequence,
- $b_{\text{new}}$ = replacement byte sequence,
- $\vert b_{\text{old}} \vert = \vert b_{\text{new}} \vert = \ell$ (atomicity constraint).

**Patch Integrity Check Equation:**

$$
\text{Integrity} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{read}(a, \ell) = b_{\text{old}} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

A patch is **safe to apply** if and only if Integrity = 1. A forensic analyst's tool should refuse to apply the patch otherwise.

## 3.4 Causal Derivation of In-Memory vs. File Patches

A malware sample's **memory representation** differs from its **file representation** for three reasons:

$$
M_{\text{process}} = M_{\text{file}} \oplus (\text{Layout}) \oplus (\text{Relocations}) \oplus (\text{Imports})
$$

Where:
- $M_{\text{process}}$ = bytes as the CPU sees them,
- $M_{\text{file}}$ = bytes on disk,
- $\text{Layout}$ = section alignment expansion,
- $\text{Relocations}$ = base-address fixups,
- $\text{Imports}$ = IAT resolution at load time.

**Therefore:** A patch at file offset `0x40B` corresponds to a *different* address in memory — usually `0x40B + Δ_section`. Patching the *file* affects all future executions. Patching the *memory* affects only the current session.

$$
\text{Addr}_{\text{mem}} = \text{FileOffset} + (VAddr_{sec} - RawPtr_{sec})
$$

    C -- To Legit Path --> E[Replace Jcc with unconditional JMP 0xEB]
    B -- API Call --> F{Call Purpose}

    F -- Sleep or Delay --> H[Replace PUSH imm with PUSH 0]

    participant MEM as Process Memory
    participant CPU as CPU

    FS->>HEX: Original bytes at offset 0x40B = 75 73
    HEX->>FS: Patch 75 73 to 90 90
    Note over FS: File patched persistently
    FS->>OS: Load Patched.exe
    OS->>MEM: Map .text to 0x401000
