> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Packed Malware Signature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — PACKED MALWARE SIGNATURE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Packed Malware Signature** is a forensic artifact — a distinctive, machine-identifiable pattern (or set of patterns) used to recognize that an executable binary (PE, ELF, Mach-O) has been processed by a **software packer** (also called a *runtime packer* or *executable compressor*). The signature is not derived from the original malicious payload itself, but from the **packing layer** that wraps the payload. Detection signatures for packed malware are typically composed of:

1. **Section-table signatures** — non-standard, suspicious section names (e.g., `UPX0`, `UPX1`, `.aspack`, `.vmp0`, `.themida`).
2. **Entropy signatures** — abnormally high Shannon entropy of the file's bytes (commonly **> 7.0** for 8-bit bytes, theoretical maximum = **8.0**).
3. **Import Address Table (IAT) signatures** — absence/dramatic reduction of meaningful API imports (a tiny stub loader instead).
4. **Entrypoint signatures** — the `AddressOfEntryPoint` points into a section whose characteristics are inconsistent (e.g., executable + writable + no real code).
5. **Footer/Header signatures** — literal byte-string fingerprints baked into the packer (e.g., `UPX!` footer, ASPack magic `aPLib`).
6. **Behavioral / unpack-emulation signatures** — runtime indicators captured by sandboxing (allocate-and-execute, RWX memory regions, VirtualProtect calls).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (PECCT741 / Module 2):** A *signature* in the packed-malware context is **not** a simple MD5 hash. It is a **multi-dimensional fingerprint** that combines static byte-pattern, structural, and entropy characteristics, since the malicious code itself is encrypted/compressed and a single hash match becomes trivially defeated.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine a **smuggler trying to sneak a weapon through airport security** by hiding it inside a **sealed, pressurized metal cylinder** filled with foam padding.

- The **weapon (original payload)** is the dangerous part — but it is invisible to X-ray scanners because of the cylinder.
- The **cylinder (packer)** is what security actually *sees* first. The cylinder itself has very specific properties: it is unusually heavy for its size, has a very uniform density (high entropy), and is sealed with a proprietary valve mechanism (a known packer "brand" or signature).
- The **forensic signature** is therefore: *the unique characteristics of the cylinder, not the weapon*. Investigators learn to recognize common smuggling "manufacturers" (UPX, Themida, VMProtect) by the engineering of their cylinders.

> [!NOTE]
> A **packed binary is essentially a two-stage program**:
> 1. A tiny **unpacker stub** that runs first.
> 2. The stub decompresses/decrypts the original `.text` section into memory, then transfers execution to the revealed code.
>
> Therefore, *static* signatures for packed malware usually target the **stub** and **structural anomalies**, while *dynamic* signatures capture the **runtime decompression** behavior.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in the Field

| Metric | Typical "Packed" Value | Theoretical Max | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shannon Entropy (per-byte)** | **> 7.0** | **8.0** | Encrypted/compressed bytes appear random |
| **Section Count Anomaly** | 2–3 extra sections | n/a | Packers add their own sections |
| **Import Table Size** | < 10 functions | 100s normal | Original imports are gone |
| **File-vs-Memory Size Ratio** | < 0.5 | 1.0 | File is much smaller than mapped image |
| **RWX Region (runtime)** | Present | n/a | Self-modifying code signature |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Malware Authors Pack — The Operational Logic

A malware author employs a packer to achieve **four overlapping goals**. Understanding *why* is the only way to grasp *how* the signature is constructed.

1. **Evasion of Signature-Based AV.** Hashes (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256) and YARA byte-patterns match the *original* code. Compression/encryption mutates those bytes, defeating the hash.
2. **Reduction of File Size.** Helpful for spreading through email attachments and exploit kits with size limits.
3. **Reverse-Engineering Friction.** Static disassembly (IDA Pro, Ghidra) sees the stub — not the real logic — forcing the analyst to either unpack manually or use emulation.
4. **Anti-Debug / Anti-VM Wrapper.** Commercial protectors (Themida, VMProtect) bundle the packer with anti-analysis tricks.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Packed Binary — Step-by-Step Logic

A typical packed PE binary contains **three logical regions**:

1. **Original PE Header (slimmed).** The DOS header, NT header, and section table are *retained*, but the section table is rewritten. The original `.text` section's `VirtualSize` is reduced to **0** (or near zero) and a new section (e.g., `UPX0`, size = original `.text` size) is added with **read+write+execute (RWX)** characteristics.
2. **Unpacker Stub.** A tiny code fragment located in a new section (e.g., `UPX1`). Its job:
   - Call `VirtualAlloc` to reserve RWX memory of the *original* size.
   - Copy/Decompress the compressed payload (stored in the third region) into the new memory region.
   - Patch the IAT with the original API addresses (rebuilt by the stub).
   - Jump to the Original Entry Point (OEP).
3. **Compressed/Encrypted Payload.** The bulk of the original `.text` section, now stored as opaque bytes, often in a section with `RAW_SIZE << VIRTUAL_SIZE` (high compression ratio).

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Variables | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shannon Entropy (per-byte)** | $H = - \sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = frequency of byte $i$ / total bytes | **> 7.0 → packed/encrypted** |
| **Maximum possible entropy** | $H_{max} = \log_2(256) = 8$ | For uniform 8-bit distribution | **8.0** (theoretical) |
| **Compression Ratio** | $R_c = \dfrac{\text{Packed File Size}}{\text{Unpacked Memory Size}}$ | — | **< 0.5** for packed binaries |
| **Section-Name Anomaly Score** | $A_s = \text{count}(\text{non-standard sections})$ | Standard = `.text`, `.data`, `.rdata`, `.rsrc` | **≥ 1** → suspicious |
| **Suspicious Import Count** | $N_{imp}$ | Number of imported functions | **< 10** for packed (stub only) |
| **Entropy Threshold (industry)** | $H_{thr} \approx 7.0$–$7.2$ | — | Alerts above threshold |
| **Worst-case Keyspace** | $K = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = symmetric key bits | AES-256 ⇒ $2^{256}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - $H = 0$ ⇒ all bytes identical (e.g., empty / NUL-filled region).
> - $H = 8$ ⇒ perfectly random uniform distribution (rare; suspect strong crypto).
> - $0 \le H \le 8$ for any 8-bit byte stream.
> - $\log_2$ is used (not $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$) because we measure information in *bits*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Packed-malware signature analysis is the **first line of triage** in any Security Operations Center (SOC) and a cornerstone of:
- **Anti-virus engines** (signature pre-filter before heuristic / ML).
- **Sandbox detonation systems** (Cuckoo, Joe Sandbox, CAPA).
- **Threat intelligence platforms** (MISP, VirusTotal's static engines).
- **Incident-response triage** (rapid identification of the packer family to choose the correct unpacker).

In an interview or KTU viva, you can confidently state: *“A reliable packer signature relies on entropy + IAT heuristics + known byte-stamps, because the original payload’s hash is useless when the binary is compressed.”*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon Entropy of a Byte Stream (the Core Packing Metric)

The Shannon entropy of an 8-bit byte stream is the foundational quantitative signature of a packed binary. We derive it explicitly so you can answer any 7- or 14-mark derivation question.

### Step 1 — Define the random variable

Let $X$ be a discrete random variable representing a randomly chosen byte from a binary file $B$, with byte values $x \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$.

### Step 2 — Define the probability mass function

For each possible byte value $x_i$, we define:

$$
p(x_i) \;=\; \frac{f(x_i)}{N}
$$

where $f(x_i)$ is the absolute frequency of byte $x_i$ in the file, and $N$ is the total number of bytes in the file. The function $p(x_i)$ satisfies the axioms of a probability distribution:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \;=\; 1, \quad p(x_i) \ge 0
$$

### Step 3 — Define self-information (surprisal) of an outcome

The information content (in bits) of observing the byte $x_i$ is given by Claude Shannon's self-information:

$$
I(x_i) \;=\; - \log_2 p(x_i) \quad \text{(bits)}
$$

A rare byte carries high information; a frequent byte carries little.

### Step 4 — Average the self-information over the distribution

The **expected self-information** is the Shannon entropy:

$$
H(X) \;=\; \mathbb{E}[\,I(X)\,] \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \, I(x_i) \;=\; - \sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

This is the **fundamental equation** you must reproduce in any derivation question.

### Step 5 — Derive the maximum value

By the method of Lagrange multipliers, $H$ is maximized when the distribution is uniform, i.e., $p(x_i) = \dfrac{1}{256}$ for all $i$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= - \sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{256}\right) \\
        &= - \log_2 \left(2^{-8}\right) \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{255} \frac{1}{256} \\
        &= 8 \cdot 1 \\
        &= 8 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive the minimum value

If the file contains only one distinct byte (e.g., a NUL-filled block), then $p(x_0) = 1$ and $p(x_i) = 0$ for $i \neq 0$. The convention $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ gives:

$$
H_{min} \;=\; - 1 \cdot \log_2 1 \;=\; 0 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

Consider a 16-byte suspicious blob with the following byte distribution: byte `0x41` ('A') appears **8 times**, `0x42` appears **4 times**, `0x43` appears **2 times**, and `0x44` appears **2 times**. Compute the entropy.

$$
p(A) = \tfrac{8}{16} = 0.5, \quad p(B) = \tfrac{4}{16} = 0.25, \quad p(C) = \tfrac{2}{16} = 0.125, \quad p(D) = \tfrac{2}{16} = 0.125
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= - \bigl[ 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 \bigr] \\
  &= - \bigl[ 0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.25 \cdot (-2) + 0.125 \cdot (-3) + 0.125 \cdot (-3) \bigr] \\
  &= - \bigl[ -0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375 \bigr] \\
  &= 1.75 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $H = 1.75$ is *very low* — this looks like ASCII / uncompressed text (likely NOT packed). A packed binary would yield $H \approx 7.5$–$7.99$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compression Ratio and the IAT-Heuristic Reasoning

The **compression ratio** of a packed executable is a structural signature. We define it precisely:

$$
R_c \;=\; \frac{S_{packed}}{S_{unpacked}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Size of file on disk}}{\text{Virtual size of all loaded sections}}
$$

A normal, non-packed PE file has $R_c \approx 1.0$ (or slightly less). A UPX-packed payload typically has $R_c \approx 0.4$–$0.6$. A heavily encrypted/anti-RE protector (Themida) can drop $R_c$ even lower.

**Coupled heuristic** — The IAT-Entropy Pair:
Let $S_{IAT} =$ size of the resolved IAT. A non-packed binary imports $50$–$500$ functions. A packed binary shows a **collapsed IAT** ($S_{IAT} \le 2$ kB) because the stub resolves imports only at runtime via `GetProcAddress`. The joint condition

$$
H_{\text{bytes}} > 7.0 \;\;\land\;\; S_{IAT} \le 2\ \text{kB}
$$

is the **canonical static packer signature** used by every modern AV engine.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Recognize a Packed Binary

| Observable | Normal Binary | Packed Binary | Signature Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| Section name | `.text`, `.data`, `.rsrc` | `UPX0`, `.aspack`, `.vmp0` | **Very High** (exact match) |
| Entropy of `.text` | 5.0 – 6.5 | 7.2 – 7.99 | **High** |
| Number of imports | 50 – 500 | 1 – 10 | **High** |
| `AddressOfEntryPoint` | Points into `.text` | Points into packer section | **High** |
| File-vs-Memory size | ≈ 1.0 | < 0.5 | **Medium-High** |
| Anti-debug calls | Rare | Frequent (IsDebuggerPresent) | **Medium** |
| TLS callbacks | None | Often present | **Medium** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Packed PE

The following flowchart captures the *runtime architecture* and the *static forensic observables* a packed binary leaves behind.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspicious Binary File .exe] --> B[PE Header Parser]
    B --> C{Section Table Analysis}
    C -->|Anomalous Section Names| D1[Signature UPX0 / UPX1 / .aspack / .vmp0]
    C -->|Standard .text .data .rdata| D2[Signature Likely Not Packed]
    C --> E[Entropy Analyzer]
    E -->|H greater than 7.0| F1[High Entropy Signature Packed / Encrypted]
    E -->|H between 5.0 and 6.5| F2[Normal Range Unpacked Code]
    E -->|H less than 4.0| F3[Low Entropy Empty or Repeated Bytes]
    C --> G[IAT Inspector]
    G -->|Imports less than 10| H1[Collapsed IAT Packer Stub]
    G -->|Imports 50 to 500| H2[Rich IAT Normal Binary]
    D1 --> I[Consolidated Packed Signature]
    F1 --> I
    H1 --> I
    I --> J[Decision Apply Packer Unpacker]
    J --> K[Unpacked PE Analysis Original Logic]
    K --> L[YARA Hash Signature of Original Payload]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Runtime Packing-Unpacking Sequence

This block diagram shows the *order in which CPU executes the packed binary*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[OS Loader Maps PE] --> S2[AddressOfEntryPoint OEP stub]
    S2 --> S3[Unpacker Stub Executes]
    S3 --> S4[Call VirtualAlloc RWX Region of Original Size]
    S4 --> S5[Decompress or Decrypt Payload into RWX]
    S5 --> S6[Rebuild IAT using GetProcAddress Loops]
    S6 --> S7[Jump to Original Entry Point in Unpacked Code]
    S7 --> S8[Original Malicious Payload Executes]
    S8 --> S9[Forensic Capture Runtime APIs Syscalls Network IoC]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Detection & Triage Workflow for an Analyst

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Acquire Sample Safely Isolated VM] --> T1[Run Static Packer Scanner]
    T1 --> T2{Packer Detected?}
    T2 -->|Yes| T3[Identify Packer Family UPX ASPack Themida]
    T2 -->|No| T4[Proceed to Disassembly and YARA]
    T3 --> T5{Commercial Unpacker Available?}
    T5 -->|Yes| T6[Run Unpacker to Recover OEP]
    T5 -->|No| T7[Use x64dbg or OllyDbg Manual Unpack]
    T6 --> T8[Static Analysis of Original Payload]
    T7 --> T8
    T8 --> T9[YARA Match Hash IOC Generation]
    T9 --> T10[Threat Intelligence Submission]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All three diagrams are designed to be **renderable in Mermaid v10+** with no reserved-keyword node IDs and no markdown formatting inside quoted labels, in accordance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safety rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define a packed malware signature. Mention any three structural indicators used to identify a packed binary.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A packed malware signature is a unique, multi-dimensional pattern (entropy, section name, IAT collapse, entry-point anomaly) that identifies an executable as having been processed by a software packer. Three structural indicators:
1. **Anomalous section names** such as `UPX0`, `UPX1`, `.aspack`, `.vmp0`, `.themida`.
2. **Reduced Import Address Table** — only a few APIs (e.g., `LoadLibrary`, `GetProcAddress`, `VirtualAlloc`) are statically visible.
3. **High Shannon entropy** of file bytes (typically **> 7.0**) indicating compressed/encrypted content.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for listing three indicators with 1-line each.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is the role of an *unpacker stub* in a packed binary? Why does it pose a challenge to static signature-based AV engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The unpacker stub is a tiny code fragment stored in a new section (e.g., `UPX1`) that runs before the real payload. Its role is to:
1. Allocate RWX memory of the original payload's size.
2. Decompress / decrypt the compressed payload into that memory.
3. Re-resolve the original IAT at runtime.
4. Transfer control to the Original Entry Point (OEP).

It defeats static AV because the actual malicious bytes are encrypted on disk — the AV only sees the harmless-looking stub, so hash-based and YARA byte-pattern signatures miss the threat.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Derive the **Shannon entropy formula** for an 8-bit byte stream. State and prove the maximum-entropy condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 32-byte file contains: byte `0x00` appears **16 times**, `0xFF` appears **12 times**, `0x41` appears **4 times**. Compute the entropy and interpret whether the file is likely packed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the random variable $X$ taking values $x_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, 255\}$ with $p(x_i) = f(x_i)/N$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the self-information $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p(x_i)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Define entropy as expected self-information:

$$
H(X) \;=\; - \sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the constraint $\sum p(x_i) = 1$.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Use Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ on $L = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i - \lambda(\sum p_i - 1)$; setting $\partial L/\partial p_i = 0$ gives $\log_2 p_i = -1 - \lambda/\ln 2$, i.e., $p_i$ is constant. With $256$ possible values, $p_i = 1/256$. Substitute to get $H_{max} = 8$ bits.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

$$
N = 32, \quad p(0x00) = \tfrac{16}{32} = 0.5, \quad p(0xFF) = \tfrac{12}{32} = 0.375, \quad p(0x41) = \tfrac{4}{32} = 0.125
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= - \bigl[ 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.375 \log_2 0.375 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 \bigr] \\
  &= - \bigl[ 0.5 \cdot (-1) + 0.375 \cdot (-1.4150) + 0.125 \cdot (-3) \bigr] \\
  &= - \bigl[ -0.5 - 0.5306 - 0.375 \bigr] \\
  &= 1.4056 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):** $H \approx 1.41$ is *very low* — far below the **7.0** threshold. The file is therefore **unlikely to be packed or encrypted**; it is most likely a small data blob (e.g., text, padding, or a resource block).

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the **PE structure of a packed binary**. How do the section table, IAT, and entry point differ from a normal binary? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **four common categories of packers** (compression, encryption, virtualization, and anti-debug). For each, name one representative tool. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

1. **Section Table (3 marks):** A normal PE has sections like `.text` (RX, large), `.data` (RW), `.rdata` (R), `.rsrc` (R). A packed PE adds suspicious sections (`UPX0`, `UPX1`, `.aspack`, `.vmp0`). `UPX0` is huge and RWX with virtual size matching the original `.text`; `UPX1` is small, executable, and contains the stub.
2. **IAT (2 marks):** Normal: 50–500 imports. Packed: collapsed to 1–10 imports — only the stub's needs (e.g., `VirtualAlloc`, `LoadLibraryA`, `GetProcAddress`, `VirtualProtect`).
3. **Entry Point (2 marks):** Normal: OEP points into `.text` and disassembles to logical code. Packed: OEP points into the stub section and begins with `pushad; mov ebp, esp` style prologues typical of decompressors.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

1. **Compression Packers (1.5 marks):** Shrink size; algorithms include aPLib, LZMA. **Example: UPX** (open source, very common).
2. **Encryption Packers (1.5 marks):** Encrypt original code with a per-build key, decrypted by stub. **Example: AsPack**.
3. **Virtualization Packers (2 marks):** Convert code into bytecode executed by an embedded VM — extremely hard to reverse. **Example: VMProtect** or **Themida**.
4. **Anti-Debug / Anti-VM Packers (2 marks):** Add detection of debuggers, breakpoints, sandboxes, and refuse to unpack if detected. **Example: Themida / Code Virtualizer** (often overlap with category 3).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Writing the entropy formula as $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$** instead of $\log_2$. Always use $\log_2$ for *bits* — this is a 1-mark killer in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing "entropy" with "compression ratio"** — they are correlated but mathematically distinct. Entropy is an information-theoretic measure of randomness; compression ratio is a size-based structural metric.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit "bits"** in the final answer of an entropy calculation. $H = 7.5$ bits, not just "7.5".
> 4. **Saying "high entropy means packed" without the threshold** — always quote **H > 7.0** as the empirical threshold.
> 5. **In Part B, ignoring the OEP / IAT discussion** — examiners specifically award 2–3 marks for naming the **OEP relocation** and **IAT collapse** as the two structural signatures.
> 6. **Claiming MD5 changes when packed → "AV cannot detect"** — the *correct* answer is that **byte-pattern / YARA signatures fail**, not that all AV fails (heuristic + behavioral engines can still detect).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A packed malware signature is a *multi-dimensional* fingerprint (entropy + section + IAT + OEP) that identifies an executable processed by a packer.
- **Shannon Entropy:** $H = - \sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \log_2 p_i$ (use $\log_2$, not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$).
- **Entropy Range:** $0 \le H \le 8$ bits. **Threshold for "packed" ≈ 7.0** (industry-standard).
- **Anatomy of a packed PE:** DOS header → rewritten NT header → stub section + payload section (RWX) → OEP in stub, jumps to unpacked memory.
- **Top Four Signatures (memorize all four):** (1) Anomalous section names, (2) Collapsed IAT, (3) OEP relocation, (4) High entropy.
- **Compression Ratio:** $R_c = S_{packed} / S_{unpacked}$. A value **< 0.5** is suspicious.
- **Joint Static Heuristic:** $H > 7.0 \; \land \; S_{IAT} \le 2$ kB ⇒ strong packer signature.
- **Common Packers (KTU-favorite list):** UPX (compression), ASPack / PECompact (encryption), Themida / VMProtect (virtualization + anti-debug).
- **Why hash-based AV fails:** Original malicious bytes are encrypted; MD5/SHA-256 match only the packed wrapper, not the payload.
- **Why dynamic analysis succeeds:** The unpacker stub *must* decrypt the payload in memory at runtime — sandboxing captures the revealed code.
- **Maximum entropy** occurs at the uniform distribution: $H_{max} = \log_2(256) = 8$ bits.
- **Convention:** $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ — always state this in derivations to handle zero-probability terms cleanly.
- **Anti-debug signatures:** Look for `IsDebuggerPresent`, `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess`, `RDTSC` timing — these are behavioral packer signatures.
- **TLS Callbacks:** Many packers use TLS callbacks to run code **before** the OEP — a frequent feature in advanced packed binaries.
- **Examiner Tip:** Whenever you discuss entropy in an exam, *always* state the threshold (7.0), the unit (bits), the base of the log (base 2), and the range (0–8) — full credit guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metamorphic and Polymorphic Signature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metamorphic and Polymorphic Signature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymorphic Signature**: A detection pattern that can identify multiple variants of a malware family whose internal code (encrypted payload) changes with each infection, but whose underlying decryption engine/stub remains functionally analogous. Detection relies on wildcards, masks, and flexible byte-pattern matching rather than rigid byte sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metamorphic Signature**: A semantic or structural detection pattern designed to recognize malware that **rewrites its own code** between generations, producing entirely different byte-level signatures while preserving identical functionality. Detection relies on code-graph isomorphism, opcode frequency analysis, and behavioral heuristics rather than static byte matching.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

**Polymorphic Malware = A Spy Who Changes Outfits but Keeps the Same Badge**
Imagine a spy who enters a building every day wearing a completely different disguise (a clown suit, a delivery uniform, a doctor's coat). Each disguise is unique, so a security camera looking for the "exact face" of the spy will fail. However, the spy **always wears the same ID badge clipped to the belt**, because the magnetic lock needs that exact badge to open the door. The badge is the *decryption stub* — it stays constant, even though everything around it (the outfit = encrypted body) changes.

**Metamorphic Malware = A Ghost Who Re-Writes Its Own Diary**
Now imagine a different spy who, before every mission, completely rewrites his own diary. The mission is the same (steal the blue folder), but the wording changes every single time. Sometimes he uses big words, sometimes short sentences; sometimes he numbers the steps, sometimes he writes them as a story. There is no single "fingerprint sentence" left behind. This is metamorphism — the *code itself is regenerated*, not just hidden.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU Board Exam):**
> - **Polymorphic** → Body changes (encryption), **structure** remains similar
> - **Metamorphic** → Body changes (obfuscation/regeneration), **structure** also changes

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Industry

- **False Positive Rate (FPR)**: Ratio of benign files flagged as malicious.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR)**: Ratio of malicious files that bypass detection.
- **Detection Latency**: Time between malware release and signature availability.
- **Entropy (H)**: A high-entropy payload (≈ 7.99) indicates packed/encrypted content — a strong polymorphic indicator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Polymorphic Malware — Operational Mechanics

A polymorphic engine typically performs the following sequence:

1. **Payload Encryption**: The malicious body is encrypted with a randomly generated key $K_i$ for each new sample.
2. **Decryption Stub Generation**: A small routine is generated/selected whose job is to decrypt the body at runtime.
3. **Key Embedding**: The key $K_i$ is embedded (often XORed, or stored in the headers).
4. **Optional Polymorphic Decryption**: Even the decryption stub itself may be obfuscated using techniques such as:
   - Code permutation (reordering instructions).
   - Instruction substitution (`MOV EAX, 0` ↔ `XOR EAX, EAX`).
   - Junk instruction insertion.
5. **Repacking**: The new binary is assembled.

The **detection challenge** is that the byte pattern of the encrypted body has no static signature. Modern polymorphic malware uses *mutation engines* (e.g., the famous Dark Avenger's MtE) that randomize the stub too.

### 2.2 Metamorphic Malware — Operational Mechanics

Unlike polymorphic malware, **metamorphic malware does NOT use encryption**. Instead, it transforms its own code into semantically equivalent but syntactically different forms. Common metamorphic transformations:

| Transformation Technique | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Substitution** | Replaces one instruction with equivalent ones | `ADD EAX, 1` → `SUB EAX, -1` or `INC EAX` |
| **Register Renaming** | Swaps operands across different registers | `MOV EAX, EBX` → `MOV ECX, EDX` |
| **Code Permutation** | Reorders independent instructions | Block A executes before Block B |
| **Dead Code Insertion** | Inserts NOP-equivalent instructions | `XOR ECX, ECX` followed by `XOR ECX, ECX` |
| **Variable/Function Renaming** | Renames symbols (in source-aware metamorphism) | `funcA()` → `funcZ()` |
| **Control Flow Obfuscation** | Adds opaque predicates, jump-based dispatch | `if (x*x >= 0) { ... }` always true |

### 2.3 Signature Types — A Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Signature Hierarchy (KTU-Favorite Question Topic):**
> 1. **Static Signature** → Fixed byte sequence (e.g., `48 65 6C 6C 6F`). Defeated by either polymorphism or metamorphism.
> 2. **Polymorphic Signature** → Masked byte pattern with wildcards. Targets the decryption stub or uses emulation.
> 3. **Metamorphic Signature** → Code-graph / opcode-statistical signature. Requires structural/semantic analysis.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas for this topic:**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Shannon Entropy** (Encrypted Body Detection) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Detects polymorphic packed payloads; $H \approx 8$ for random/encrypted bytes |
| **XOR Encryption** (Polymorphic Cipher) | $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ | Simplest polymorphic key stream; $K_i$ regenerated per sample |
| **Levenshtein / Edit Distance** (Metamorphic Variant Similarity) | $d(s_1, s_2) = \min\{\text{edit operations}\}$ | Quantifies code divergence between metamorphic variants |
| **Cosine Similarity of Opcode Histograms** | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\vert\vert A \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B \vert\vert}$ | Compares opcode frequency vectors of two samples (metamorphic detection) |
| **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Evaluates signature accuracy |
| **False Negative Rate** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Critical for AV efficacy |
| **Detection Coverage** | $C = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of malware samples correctly identified |

> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas drive every commercial antivirus engine, sandbox-based EDR, and YARA-rule matcher deployed in modern SOCs. Levenshtein distance and cosine similarity of opcode histograms are the cornerstones of the **Cylance, CrowdStrike, and SentinelOne** behavioral engines.

### 2.5 Why Polymorphic & Metamorphic Signatures Matter

- **Zero-Day Resilience**: Conventional AV signatures require known samples; polymorphic/metamorphic signatures enable detection of *never-before-seen* variants.
- **Ransomware Propagation**: Modern ransomware (LockBit, Conti) uses polymorphic loaders.
- **APT Stealth**: Nation-state actors use metamorphic code to evade sandbox & EDR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Shannon Entropy for Polymorphic Payload Detection

The Shannon Entropy $H(X)$ of a byte stream quantifies the average information content per symbol. For an 8-bit alphabet (0–255):

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

**Step 1 — Define the probability mass function.**
For a binary blob $B$ of length $N$, the probability of byte value $i$ appearing is:

$$
p_i = \frac{\text{count of byte } i \text{ in } B}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the log-term.**
For each unique byte $i$, evaluate $\log_2(p_i)$. If $p_i = 0$, the term contributes $0$ to the sum (using the convention $0 \cdot \log 0 = 0$).

**Step 3 — Multiply and sum.**
Multiply each $p_i$ by its corresponding $\log_2(p_i)$, take the negation, and sum across all 256 possible byte values:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \log_2(p_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &\approx 0 && \text{(highly redundant, e.g., ASCII text or NOP-sled)} \\
H(X) &\approx 4 \text{ to } 6 && \text{(compressed / structured binary)} \\
H(X) &\approx 7.99 \text{ to } 8.0 && \text{(encrypted/random — strong polymorphic indicator)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explained**: When malware encrypts its payload with a strong cipher, the resulting byte distribution becomes uniform (each of the 256 byte values is nearly equally likely). A uniform distribution **maximizes** entropy because every symbol carries maximum surprise. Therefore, an entropy value approaching 8 is a *strong heuristic indicator of polymorphism or packing*.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Cosine Similarity of Opcode Histograms (Metamorphic Detection)

Two metamorphic variants $V_1$ and $V_2$ of the same malware will have different opcodes in different orders, but their **frequency distributions** of opcodes tend to remain similar because the underlying functionality is unchanged.

**Step 1 — Build opcode frequency vectors.**
Disassemble both variants and count opcode occurrences:

$$
V_1 = [v_{1,1}, v_{1,2}, \ldots, v_{1,n}], \quad V_2 = [v_{2,1}, v_{2,2}, \ldots, v_{2,n}]
$$

where $n$ = number of distinct x86 opcodes considered.

**Step 2 — Compute dot product.**

$$
V_1 \cdot V_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_{1,i} \cdot v_{2,i}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute magnitudes.**

$$
\mid\mid V_1 \mid\mid = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_{1,i}^2}, \quad \mid\mid V_2 \mid\mid = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_{2,i}^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute similarity.**

$$
\text{sim}(V_1, V_2) = \frac{V_1 \cdot V_2}{\mid\mid V_1 \mid\mid \cdot \mid\mid V_2 \mid\mid}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**
Values close to **1.0** indicate high opcode-statistical similarity (likely same malware family), while values close to **0.0** indicate different families.

> **Conversion Logic Explained**: Even though metamorphic code permutes instructions and renames registers, the **functional mix** of operations (how many MOVs, ADDs, CALLs, JMPs) is constrained by the algorithm. A file infector will still issue many `INT 21h` system calls; a keylogger will still issue many `GetAsyncKeyState` calls. This functional fingerprint survives metamorphic transformation.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Polymorphic Malware | Metamorphic Malware |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Encryption** | Yes (payload encrypted) | No (code is in plaintext) |
| **Signature Mutation** | Body encrypted with random key | Body rewritten semantically |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Moderate (emulation defeats it) | Very High (requires semantic analysis) |
| **Static AV Defeat?** | Yes | Yes |
| **Heuristic Defeat?** | Often | Sometimes |
| **Behavioral Defeat?** | No (behavior unchanged) | No (behavior unchanged) |
| **Size Variation** | High (due to encryption + stubs) | Moderate to High |
| **CPU Cost at Runtime** | Decryption overhead | No decryption cost |
| **Historical Examples** | Storm Worm, MtE engine, Zeus | Win32/Simile, Zmist, Evilotus |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Polymorphic Malware Generation & Detection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Original Malicious Payload] --> A1[Polymorphic Engine]
    K1[Random Key Generator K1] --> A1
    A1 --> A2[Encrypted Body C1]
    A2 --> A3[Stub Generator]
    A3 --> A4[Polymorphic Stub S1 with K1 embedded]
    A4 --> A5[Repacked Binary B1]
    K2[Random Key Generator K2] --> A1
    A1 --> A6[Encrypted Body C2]
    A6 --> A3
    A3 --> A7[Polymorphic Stub S2 with K2 embedded]
    A7 --> A8[Repacked Binary B2]
    A5 --> SCN[Antivirus Scanner]
    A8 --> SCN
    SCN --> EML[Emulation Sandbox]
    EML --> DEC[Decrypted Payload Analysis]
    DEC --> SIG{Match Against Polymorphic Signature?}
    SIG -->|Yes| DET[Detected as Malware]
    SIG -->|No| BEN[Flagged Benign]
```

### 4.2 Metamorphic Malware Transformation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Original Source Binary M1] --> T1[Instruction Substitution Pass]
    T1 --> T2[Register Renaming Pass]
    T2 --> T3[Code Permutation Pass]
    T3 --> T4[Dead Code Insertion Pass]
    T4 --> T5[Control Flow Obfuscation Pass]
    T5 --> M2[Transformed Binary M2 Different Bytes]
    M2 --> T6[Re-Disassembly]
    T6 --> CG[Code Graph Extraction CFG]
    CG --> ISO{Graph Isomorphism Check}
    ISO -->|Isomorphic| MATCH[Same Metamorphic Family Detected]
    ISO -->|Not Isomorphic| NO[No Match]
```

### 4.3 Detection Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D0[Incoming Sample] --> D1{Is Sample Packed or High Entropy?}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2[Apply Polymorphic Signature Scan]
    D1 -->|No| D3[Apply Static Signature Scan]
    D2 --> D4[Run in Emulator / Sandbox]
    D4 --> D5[Extract Decrypted Code]
    D5 --> D3
    D3 --> D6{Signature Match?}
    D6 -->|Yes| D7[Flagged as Known Malware]
    D6 -->|No| D8[Apply Metamorphic Detection]
    D8 --> D9[Opcode Histogram Analysis]
    D9 --> D10[CFG Similarity Check]
    D10 --> D11[Behavioral Sandboxing]
    D11 --> D12{Similarity Score above Threshold?}
    D12 -->|Yes| D7
    D12 -->|No| D13[Classified as Clean]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exam**: When asked to draw a diagram, always include **input → transformation → output** flow and label each block with its functional role. Examiners reward clear data-flow arrows over artistic complexity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between Polymorphic and Metamorphic malware. State one detection technique used against each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Polymorphic Malware | Metamorphic Malware |
|---|---|---|
| Encryption | Encrypts payload with varying keys | Does NOT encrypt; rewrites own code |
| Detection Technique | Code emulation / decryption stub fingerprinting | Opcode-statistical analysis, CFG graph matching |

*Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark differentiation, 1 mark detection technique each.*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **What is Shannon Entropy? How is it used to detect polymorphic malware?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Shannon Entropy $H(X)$ measures the randomness of a byte sequence. For polymorphic malware, the encrypted payload exhibits near-uniform byte distribution, yielding $H(X) \approx 7.99$ out of maximum 8.0. AV engines flag binaries with entropy above a threshold (typically 7.2) as suspicious and submit them for deeper emulation-based scanning. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark application)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a polymorphic malware engine with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the decryption stub and the random key generator. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe any three metamorphic transformation techniques with examples. Explain how these techniques defeat traditional signature-based antivirus systems. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Polymorphic Malware Engine Architecture (7 Marks):**

A polymorphic malware engine consists of the following components working in sequence:

**Component 1 — Random Key Generator:**
Produces a fresh symmetric key $K_i$ for every newly generated sample. Without this, the same ciphertext pattern would re-emerge and become detectable.

**Component 2 — Payload Encryptor:**
Applies the cipher (commonly XOR, RC4, or AES) to the malicious body using $K_i$, producing ciphertext $C_i = E(P, K_i)$.

**Component 3 — Decryption Stub Generator:**
Creates a small executable routine whose only purpose is to decrypt $C_i$ at runtime and transfer execution to the plaintext payload. Modern stubs themselves contain junk instructions and instruction substitutions to resist static signatures.

**Component 4 — Key Embedder:**
Embeds $K_i$ inside the stub (or derives it from environmental data such as the host's timestamp).

**Component 5 — Repacker:**
Combines stub + encrypted payload + legitimate wrapper into a final PE/ELF binary.

> **Why a Decryption Stub Defeats Static Scanners:** The encrypted body has no static signature (high entropy, no recognisable bytes). The stub is small enough that a single byte change via polymorphism renders the static signature invalid. Therefore, **no fixed byte pattern** can reliably identify the malware across samples.

**(b) Three Metamorphic Transformation Techniques (7 Marks):**

**1. Instruction Substitution (2 Marks):**
Original: `MOV EAX, 0` (Bytes: `B8 00 00 00 00`)
Substituted: `XOR EAX, EAX` (Bytes: `33 C0`) or `PUSH 0; POP EAX`
Each variant produces a different byte sequence despite identical semantics.

**2. Register Renaming (2 Marks):**
Original: `MOV EAX, EBX; ADD EAX, ECX`
Renamed: `MOV EDI, ESI; ADD EDI, EDX`
The arithmetic logic is unchanged, but registers — and thus opcodes — are completely different.

**3. Dead Code / Junk Insertion (2 Marks):**
Original: `ADD EAX, EBX`
With junk: `NOP; PUSH ECX; POP ECX; ADD EAX, EBX`
Extra instructions alter byte distribution without altering functionality.

**Why Defeats Static AV (1 Mark):**
Static scanners rely on fixed byte sequences. Since every metamorphic generation yields a unique binary, the signature database must grow infinitely — an impossible task. Behavioral and structural detection become necessary.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define Shannon Entropy. Derive the formula and explain how it is applied in polymorphic malware detection with a numerical example. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast polymorphic and metamorphic malware across eight parameters. Discuss the limitations of signature-based detection against these threats. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Shannon Entropy (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Shannon Entropy quantifies the average information content per symbol in a dataset. For malware analysis, it is used to measure byte randomness.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

where $p(x_i)$ is the probability of byte value $x_i$ occurring in the file. The maximum possible entropy for an 8-bit alphabet is $H_{\max} = \log_2(256) = 8.0$, achieved when all 256 byte values are equally probable.

**Numerical Example (1 Mark):**
- A plain ASCII text file: $H \approx 4.5$ (low — lots of redundancy).
- A UPX-packed binary: $H \approx 7.6$ (high).
- A polymorphic encrypted payload: $H \approx 7.95$ (very high — triggers heuristic alert).

**(b) Comparison (7 Marks):**

The eight-parameter comparison is detailed in **Section 3.3 Analytical Matrix** of these notes. Key limitations of signature-based detection:
- Cannot detect zero-day variants.
- Requires constant DB updates.
- Vulnerable to code packing, encryption, and obfuscation.
- Cannot scale to metamorphic families.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "polymorphic" with "metamorphic"** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks immediately if you swap definitions. Remember: **Poly = encrypted body; Meta = rewritten body**.
> 2. **Writing the entropy formula without units** — Always state that $H$ is measured in **bits per symbol**.
> 3. **Drawing diagrams without arrows** — Always include direction of data flow.
> 4. **Omitting the formula in numerical questions** — Even if the question asks "explain", a formula earns at least 1 extra mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the wildcard/mask mechanism** in polymorphic signatures — Examiners expect mention of byte masks and flexible pattern matching.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Polymorphic** → encryption of payload; **Metamorphic** → rewriting of payload.
- ✅ **Decryption stub** is the static anchor of polymorphic malware; obfuscated stubs require emulation.
- ✅ **Shannon Entropy** $H(X) \approx 7.99$ flags encrypted/polymorphic content.
- ✅ **Opcode histogram cosine similarity** detects metamorphic variants.
- ✅ **CFG graph isomorphism** is the gold standard for metamorphic detection.
- ✅ **Behavioral / heuristic / sandbox-based** detection is mandatory — pure static signatures are obsolete.
- ✅ Historical malware: **Storm Worm, Zeus** (polymorphic); **Win32/Simile, Zmist, Evilotus** (metamorphic).
- ✅ Detection metrics to memorize: **FPR, FNR, Coverage, Entropy, Levenshtein distance**.
- ✅ Key commercial engines using these techniques: **Cylance AI, CrowdStrike Falcon, SentinelOne**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Signature Based Techniques: Similarity-Based Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Signature Based Techniques: Similarity-Based Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — NON-SIGNATURE BASED TECHNIQUES
## Topic: Similarity-Based Techniques

---

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Definition**
> **Similarity-Based Techniques** are a class of *non-signature based* malware detection and forensic analysis methods that identify, classify, and triage malicious software by computing a **quantitative measure of likeness (similarity score)** between an *unknown sample* and a *reference corpus* of known malware, instead of performing exact byte-level or pattern-level signature matching.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, these techniques fall under the broader umbrella of **heuristic and behaviour-aware malware analysis**, and they operate on the foundational axiom that *malware belonging to the same family, campaign, or threat actor group will share statistically significant structural, behavioural, or syntactic features* — even when polymorphic packing, encryption, or code reordering is applied.

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The KTU 2024 PECCT741 syllabus categorises similarity-based detection as a **proactive** counterpart to reactive signature-based scanning, emphasizing its role in detecting **zero-day variants, packed/obfuscated malware, and unknown threats** that evade conventional AV engines.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Fingerprint Dust" Analogy

Imagine a **crime scene investigator** who cannot find a perfect fingerprint match in the criminal database. Instead of giving up, the investigator looks for **partial print similarities** — the same swirl pattern, ridge density, and minutiae spacing. Two prints that share **80% of these structural features** are very likely from the same person, or from a close relative.

**Similarity-based malware forensics works identically:**

| Crime Scene Analogy | Malware Forensic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Crime scene fingerprint | Unpacked sample's feature vector / fuzzy hash |
| Criminal database | Repository of known malware fingerprints (VirusTotal, MalShare) |
| Minutiae points (ridge endings, bifurcations) | API calls, opcodes, byte n-grams, CFG nodes |
| "80% ridge match" threshold | **Similarity score ≥ T** triggers an alert |
| Partial / smudged prints | Packed, obfuscated, or polymorphic malware |

> [!TIP] **Why This Matters in 2024 Threat Landscape**
> Modern malware families such as **Emotet, TrickBot, Conti, and LockBit** use *shared source code, code reuse, and shared C2 infrastructure*. Similarity techniques exploit this reuse to **cluster variants** and **attribute campaigns** — something pure signature scanning cannot do.

---

### 1.3 Why Non-Signature / Why Similarity?

The traditional **signature-based model** has critical limitations that motivate similarity-based approaches:

1. **Zero-day blindness** — A new sample with no prior signature passes through undetected.
2. **Polymorphism** — Each instance has a unique decryption stub → unique byte signature.
3. **Metamorphism** — Code is rewritten at each propagation; bytes differ entirely.
4. **Packing / encryption** — The on-disk file is meaningless ciphertext.
5. **Code reuse** — Multiple threat actors share the *same* source code with minor edits.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Design Principle**
> Similarity-based techniques shift the question from *"Is this byte sequence in the signature database?"* to *"How structurally, behaviourally, or semantically close is this sample to a known malicious one?"* — replacing **exact match** with **fuzzy match**.

---

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Similarity-Based Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises the following primary sub-categories:

**(A) Syntactic / Static Similarity**
- **Byte-level n-gram analysis** — Jaccard / Cosine over n-gram sets.
- **Fuzzy hashing** — ssdeep (CTPH), sdhash, TLSH, mvHash.
- **Opcode sequence comparison** — Instruction-level n-grams.

**(B) Structural Similarity**
- **Control Flow Graph (CFG) similarity** — Graph edit distance, graph kernels.
- **Call Graph similarity** — Comparing API call graphs.
- **Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)** on disassembled code.

**(C) Behavioural / Dynamic Similarity**
- **API call sequence matching** — Comparing runtime API traces.
- **System call n-grams** — Behavioural fingerprints from sandboxes.
- **Network traffic pattern similarity** — C2 beaconing analysis.

**(D) Semantic / ML-Based Similarity**
- **Deep learning embeddings** — Models like *MalConv*, *EMBER* produce 128/256-d feature vectors.
- **Cosine similarity in embedding space** — Nearest-neighbour classification.

> [!WARNING] **Common KTU Mistake**
> Students often confuse **behaviour-based detection** (looking for malicious *actions*) with **behaviour-based similarity** (comparing the *pattern of actions* to known samples). The KTU 2024 rubric distinguishes these — be precise in definitions.

---

### 1.5 Key Terminology (Must Memorise for KTU)

- **Fuzzy Hash** — A hash digest that allows *approximate* matching rather than exact equality.
- **Similarity Score** — A numerical value in [0, 1] (or 0–100) indicating likeness.
- **Threshold (T)** — The cut-off similarity value above which a sample is flagged.
- **False Positive (FP)** — A benign file flagged as malicious.
- **False Negative (FN)** — A malicious file missed by the detector.
- **FPR / FNR** — False Positive Rate / False Negative Rate.
- **Family / Variant** — Samples sharing lineage, payload, or attribution.
- **Code Reuse** — Phenomenon where threat actors re-use components (e.g., leaked Conti source).
- **Reference Corpus** — Curated dataset of known malware feature vectors.

---

### 1.6 The Big Picture — Where Similarity Sits in the Forensics Pipeline

```
Malware Sample
      │
      ▼
[Pre-processing: Unpack, Normalise, Disassemble]
      │
      ▼
[Feature Extraction: Hashes, n-grams, CFG, API traces]
      │
      ▼
[Similarity Engine: Distance/Metric Computation]
      │
      ├──► Known DB (Malware Samples)  ──┐
      │                                  │
      └──► Reference (Benign Samples) ───┤
                                         ▼
                              [Similarity Score S ∈ [0,1]]
                                         │
                                         ▼
                              [Threshold T → Classify]
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> The *quality* of similarity-based detection is **entirely bottlenecked by the feature extraction stage**. A poor feature vector yields a meaningless similarity score, regardless of the elegance of the comparison algorithm. This is why KTU examiners emphasise the full pipeline, not just the metric.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 The Generic Similarity Detection Pipeline — Logic Steps

Step 1 — **Sample Acquisition**: A suspicious binary is submitted to the analysis system (manual, sandbox detonation, or automated triage).

Step 2 — **Pre-processing & Normalisation**: Packers (UPX, Themida, MPRESS) are unpacked using static-unpack tools (e.g., `unpacker` engines) or dynamic instrumentation. Headers, PE sections, and metadata may be stripped or aligned.

Step 3 — **Feature Representation**: The sample is converted into a feature vector. Choices include:
- **Byte n-grams** (size n = 2, 3, 4, 8 typical).
- **Opcode n-grams** (post-disassembly).
- **API call sequences** (post-sandbox execution).
- **CFG node/edge feature vectors** (post-binary lifting).
- **Deep embeddings** (post-neural inference).

Step 4 — **Reference Comparison**: The feature vector $F_{unknown}$ is compared against stored feature vectors $\{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n\}$ of known samples.

Step 5 — **Similarity Scoring**: A metric $M(F_{unknown}, F_i)$ is computed for each reference sample, producing a score in $[0, 1]$.

Step 6 — **Threshold Classification**: If $\max_i M(F_{unknown}, F_i) \geq T$, the sample is flagged. Otherwise, it is deemed novel/benign.

Step 7 — **Family Attribution & Reporting**: The closest match's metadata (family name, threat actor, TTPs) is returned for forensic reporting.

---

## 2.2 Major Similarity Techniques — Detailed Working

### 2.2.1 Fuzzy Hashing (Context-Triggered Piecewise Hashing — CTPH / **ssdeep**)

Developed by Andrew Tridgell (2006), `ssdeep` produces a **fuzzy hash** that supports approximate matching.

**How it works (logic steps):**

1. The input file is scanned using a **rolling hash** (Adler-32 style) — every byte position triggers a block boundary when the rolling hash mod $B = 0$ (where $B$ is dynamically chosen to produce blocks of size $\approx \frac{N}{2^k}$).
2. At each trigger point, a traditional hash (e.g., FNV) is computed over the prior block.
3. The sequence of block hashes is compressed into a single digest string (typically a 2-segment base64-like string).
4. Two samples are compared by aligning their digests and computing a **matching score** $S \in [0, 100]$.

**ssdeep Comparison Score Formula:**

$$
S_{ssdeep} = \frac{2 \times M}{L_1 + L_2}
$$

where:
- $M$ = number of matching block hashes after alignment
- $L_1$ = number of blocks in digest 1
- $L_2$ = number of blocks in digest 2

A score $\geq 40$ is conventionally considered "similar" in forensic practice.

> [!TIP] **ssdeep vs SHA-256 — KTU Favourite Question**
> SHA-256 is a **cryptographic** hash → *one-bit change* in input → *completely different* hash → only **exact match** possible.
> ssdeep is a **similarity-preserving** hash → small changes in input → *similar* hash → **fuzzy match** possible. This is the fundamental distinction.

---

### 2.2.2 TLSH (Trend Micro Locality Sensitive Hashing)

TLSH builds a *bucket distribution* of byte values in sliding windows and constructs a digest with:
- A **quartile vector** $Q[256]$ capturing byte distribution.
- A **length checksum** and **byte checksum** header.

TLSH distance is computed as:

$$
D_{TLSH} = \sum_{i} \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \vert Q_1[i] - Q_2[i] \vert \leq 1 \\ \vert Q_1[i] - Q_2[i] \vert \cdot 3 & \text{else} \end{cases}
$$

A normalised score is then used. **Lower distance = higher similarity.**

---

### 2.2.3 N-Gram Analysis with Jaccard / Cosine Similarity

A byte n-gram is a contiguous sequence of $n$ bytes. For sample $S$, we extract the set $G_S^{(n)} = \{g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_m\}$.

**Jaccard Similarity** between two samples $A$ and $B$:

$$
J(A, B) = \frac{\vert G_A \cap G_B \vert}{\vert G_A \cup G_B \vert}
$$

**Cosine Similarity** when n-grams are treated as weighted vectors:

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{\sum_{g} w_A(g) \cdot w_B(g)}{\sqrt{\sum_{g} w_A(g)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{g} w_B(g)^2}}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Why n-grams?**
> They are **resistant to noise insertions** (insertion of a few junk bytes only adds a few n-grams but does not destroy existing ones), and capture **local syntactic patterns** characteristic of code families.

---

### 2.2.4 Control Flow Graph (CFG) Similarity

After disassembly, each function is represented as a CFG $G = (V, E)$ where vertices are basic blocks and edges are control transfers. Similarity metrics include:

- **Graph Edit Distance (GED)** — minimum operations to transform $G_1$ to $G_2$.
- **Graph Kernels** — Weisfeiler-Lehman, Propagation kernels.
- **Subgraph matching** — counting isomorphic substructures.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why CFG Similarity is Powerful**
> Packers, encryptors, and instruction reoders typically **preserve the control flow** of the original code. Hence CFG-based similarity is highly robust against common obfuscation — a KTU high-yield point.

---

### 2.2.5 Behavioural Similarity (API/System Call Sequences)

In a sandbox (Cuckoo, Joe Sandbox), the sample is executed and its API/system call sequence is logged: $\sigma = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n)$.

Similarity is computed using:
- **Sequence alignment** (Needleman-Wunsch / Smith-Waterman) — analogous to bioinformatics DNA alignment.
- **Edit Distance (Levenshtein)**:

$$
D_{Lev}(A, B) = \min \{ \text{insert, delete, substitute operations to transform A to B} \}
$$

- **Common subsequence ratio**:

$$
R = \frac{\vert LCS(A, B) \vert}{\max(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)}
$$

---

### 2.2.6 ML-Based Embedding Similarity

Modern approaches (EMBER, MalConv, Avast CNN) map samples to dense vectors $\vec{v} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (typically $d = 128, 256, 1024$). Similarity is then simply:

$$
S = \cos(\vec{v}_{unk}, \vec{v}_{ref}) = \frac{\vec{v}_{unk} \cdot \vec{v}_{ref}}{\Vert \vec{v}_{unk} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{v}_{ref} \Vert}
$$

**Why ML embeddings are dominant in 2024:**
- They capture **latent semantic features** invisible to byte-level analysis.
- End-to-end trainable against labelled corpora.
- Nearest-neighbour lookup in vector DBs (FAISS, Milvus) is sub-linear.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Use \vert / \mid in tables to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables | Typical Range | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Jaccard Similarity | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert G_A \cap G_B \vert}{\vert G_A \cup G_B \vert}$ | $G_A, G_B$ — n-gram sets | $[0, 1]$ | n-gram / set comparison |
| 2 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{B}\Vert}$ | $\vec{A}, \vec{B}$ — feature vectors | $[-1, 1]$ | ML embeddings, TF-IDF |
| 3 | Hamming Distance | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[a_i \neq b_i]$ | $a, b$ — bit strings of length $n$ | $[0, n]$ | Bit-level fuzzy hashes |
| 4 | Edit (Levenshtein) Distance | $D_{Lev}$ — min ops to transform | Sequences $A, B$ | $[0, \max(\vert A\vert, \vert B\vert)]$ | API call sequences |
| 5 | ssdeep Score | $S = \frac{2M}{L_1 + L_2}$ | $M$ — matching blocks, $L_i$ — block counts | $[0, 100]$ | Fuzzy hash CTPH |
| 6 | TLSH Distance | $D = \sum_i f(\vert Q_1[i] - Q_2[i] \vert)$ | $Q$ — quartile vectors | $\geq 0$ | TLSH comparison |
| 7 | LCS Ratio | $R = \frac{\vert LCS(A,B) \vert}{\max(\vert A\vert,\vert B\vert)}$ | $LCS$ — longest common subsequence | $[0, 1]$ | Behavioural traces |
| 8 | Euclidean Distance | $E = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(a_i - b_i)^2}$ | Vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | $\geq 0$ | Vector space comparison |
| 9 | Manhattan Distance | $M_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert a_i - b_i \vert$ | Vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | $\geq 0$ | High-dim feature spaces |
| 10 | Threshold Decision | Classify = $S \geq T$ | $S$ — score, $T$ — threshold | — | Final classification |
| 11 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $FP$ — false positives, $TN$ — true negatives | $[0, 1]$ | Detector evaluation |
| 12 | False Negative Rate | $FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $FN$ — false negatives, $TP$ — true positives | $[0, 1]$ | Detector evaluation |
| 13 | F1-Score | $F1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $P$ — precision, $R$ — recall | $[0, 1]$ | Detector evaluation |
| 14 | Brier Score | $BS = \frac{1}{N}\sum (p_i - o_i)^2$ | $p_i$ — predicted, $o_i$ — observed | $[0, 1]$ | Probabilistic scoring |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Antivirus Industry** | Kaspersky, ESET, Avast use fuzzy hashes + ML embeddings in production. |
| **Threat Intelligence** | MITRE ATT&CK + VirusTotal retrohunt using ssdeep/TLSH digests. |
| **Incident Response** | Mandiant, CrowdStrike cluster ransomware variants by CFG + behavioural similarity. |
| **Mobile Malware** | Drebin dataset uses static feature similarity for Android apps. |
| **IoT Forensics** | Resource-constrained devices use lightweight n-gram Jaccard matching. |
| **Academic Research** | EMBER, SOREL-20M benchmark datasets rely on vector similarity. |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Connection to KTU Course Outcomes (COs)**
> This topic directly maps to:
> - **CO2**: Analyse non-signature based detection techniques.
> - **CO3**: Apply similarity metrics to malware classification problems.
> - **CO5**: Evaluate forensic tools using precision/recall metrics.

---

## 2.5 Design Trade-offs (Why No Single Technique Wins)

| Technique | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| Byte n-grams | Fast, simple | Sensitive to major byte shuffling |
| Fuzzy hash (ssdeep) | Robust to small edits | Block alignment can fail on large inserts |
| CFG similarity | Obfuscation-resistant | Computationally expensive (graph matching) |
| Behavioural similarity | Captures intent | Sandbox-evasion resistant malware escapes |
| ML embedding | Highest accuracy in benchmarks | Black-box, adversarial attacks possible |
| Opcode n-grams | Balances speed + accuracy | Requires reliable disassembly |

**Best Practice (2024 Industry Standard):** **Ensemble approaches** combining 2–3 techniques (e.g., fuzzy hash + ML embedding) to balance detection rate and false positive rate.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ssdeep Comparison Score

The ssdeep score is the **F1-style harmonic balance** of block matches, scaled to $[0, 100]$.

### Step 1 — Setup

Let:
- $D_1$ = fuzzy digest of sample 1, containing $L_1$ block hashes.
- $D_2$ = fuzzy digest of sample 2, containing $L_2$ block hashes.
- $M$ = number of block hashes that match after optimal alignment.

### Step 2 — Define Match Precision and Recall

Match Precision (fraction of $D_1$'s blocks matched):

$$
P = \frac{M}{L_1}
$$

Match Recall (fraction of $D_2$'s blocks matched):

$$
R = \frac{M}{L_2}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply F1 Harmonic Mean

The F1-score balances $P$ and $R$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{M}{L_1} \cdot \frac{M}{L_2}}{\frac{M}{L_1} + \frac{M}{L_2}}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{\frac{2M^2}{L_1 L_2}}{M \cdot \frac{L_2 + L_1}{L_1 L_2}}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2M^2}{L_1 L_2} \cdot \frac{L_1 L_2}{M (L_1 + L_2)}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2M}{L_1 + L_2}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Form (Scaled to 0–100)

$$
S_{ssdeep} = \frac{2M}{L_1 + L_2} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation**
> $S = 100$ means *every block* of one digest matches the other (identical digests).
> $S = 0$ means *no block* matches.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jaccard Similarity from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the Universe of N-Grams

Let $U$ be the universal set of all possible n-grams of length $n$ over byte alphabet $\Sigma = \{0, 1, \ldots, 255\}$. For $n = 4$, $\vert U \vert = 256^4 \approx 4.3 \times 10^9$.

### Step 2 — Sample Feature Sets

For sample $A$, extract the set of unique n-grams:

$$
G_A = \{g \in U \mid g \text{ appears in } A \}
$$

Similarly $G_B$ for sample $B$.

### Step 3 — Jaccard Index Definition

The Jaccard Index of two finite sets is:

$$
J(G_A, G_B) = \frac{\vert G_A \cap G_B \vert}{\vert G_A \cup G_B \vert}
$$

### Step 4 — Useful Identity

Using inclusion-exclusion:

$$
\vert G_A \cup G_B \vert = \vert G_A \vert + \vert G_B \vert - \vert G_A \cap G_B \vert
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose:
- $\vert G_A \vert = 1200$ n-grams
- $\vert G_B \vert = 1100$ n-grams
- $\vert G_A \cap G_B \vert = 800$ n-grams

Then:

$$
\vert G_A \cup G_B \vert = 1200 + 1100 - 800 = 1500
$$

$$
J = \frac{800}{1500} = 0.5333
$$

So the samples are **53.3% similar** by n-gram overlap.

> [!TIP] **KTU Numericals Often Test This**
> Be ready to compute Jaccard on small toy sets — examiners love 2–3 mark derivations with simple integer arithmetic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cosine Similarity on TF-IDF N-Gram Vectors

### Step 1 — Build a Term-Document Matrix

For a corpus of $N$ samples, define a vocabulary of $V$ unique n-grams. Each sample $i$ has a vector $\vec{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$ where entry $v_{i,k}$ is the **TF-IDF weight** of n-gram $k$:

$$
v_{i,k} = tf_{i,k} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_k}\right)
$$

where $tf_{i,k}$ = frequency of n-gram $k$ in sample $i$, and $df_k$ = number of samples containing $k$.

### Step 2 — Cosine Similarity Formula

$$
\cos(\vec{v}_A, \vec{v}_B) = \frac{\vec{v}_A \cdot \vec{v}_B}{\Vert \vec{v}_A \Vert \, \Vert \vec{v}_B \Vert}
$$

### Step 3 — Explicit Component Form

$$
\cos(\vec{v}_A, \vec{v}_B) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{V} v_{A,k} \cdot v_{B,k}}{\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{V} v_{A,k}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{V} v_{B,k}^2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example (Mini Corpus)

Two samples, vocabulary of 4 n-grams (T1, T2, T3, T4):

$$
\vec{v}_A = (0.5, 0.0, 0.7, 0.2), \quad \vec{v}_B = (0.6, 0.0, 0.5, 0.0)
$$

Dot product:

$$
\vec{v}_A \cdot \vec{v}_B = (0.5 \times 0.6) + (0.0 \times 0.0) + (0.7 \times 0.5) + (0.2 \times 0.0)
$$

$$
\vec{v}_A \cdot \vec{v}_B = 0.30 + 0.00 + 0.35 + 0.00 = 0.65
$$

Norms:

$$
\Vert \vec{v}_A \Vert = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.0^2 + 0.7^2 + 0.2^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.49 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.78} \approx 0.883
$$

$$
\Vert \vec{v}_B \Vert = \sqrt{0.6^2 + 0.0^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.0^2} = \sqrt{0.36 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.61} \approx 0.781
$$

Cosine:

$$
\cos(\vec{v}_A, \vec{v}_B) = \frac{0.65}{0.883 \times 0.781} = \frac{0.65}{0.690} \approx 0.942
$$

**Conclusion:** 94.2% cosine similarity — very similar samples.

> [!NOTE] **Why TF-IDF helps**
> Rare n-grams (e.g., T3 appearing in only 1–2 samples) get **high weight** because they are more *discriminative*. Common n-grams (e.g., padding bytes) get **low weight**, suppressing noise. This is why TF-IDF outperforms raw frequency in malware similarity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Threshold Selection via ROC Analysis

### Step 1 — Define Classifier Output

For each sample $x$, the similarity engine produces score $S(x) \in [0, 1]$.

### Step 2 — Threshold Sweep

For each candidate threshold $T \in \{0, 0.05, 0.10, \ldots, 1.0\}$:

- Predict malware if $S(x) \geq T$.
- Predict benign if $S(x) < T$.

### Step 3 — Compute TPR and FPR

$$
TPR(T) = \frac{TP(T)}{TP(T) + FN(T)}, \quad FPR(T) = \frac{FP(T)}{FP(T) + TN(T)}
$$

### Step 4 — Plot ROC

The ROC curve plots $TPR$ (y-axis) against $FPR$ (x-axis) for all $T$.

### Step 5 — AUC

The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** measures overall separability:

$$
AUC = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(x)) \, dx
$$

- $AUC = 1.0$ → perfect classifier.
- $AUC = 0.5$ → random guessing.
- $AUC < 0.5$ → worse than random (invert decision).

> [!TIP] **KTU Standard**
> "Choose threshold T that maximises TPR while keeping FPR ≤ 5%" is a classic 7-mark question. Use the ROC curve intersection with the FPR = 0.05 vertical line.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Similarity Technique

| Criterion | Byte n-gram | Fuzzy Hash | CFG | Behavioural | ML Embedding |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Robustness to packing** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Robustness to metamorphism** | ★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **False Positive Rate** | Medium | Medium | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Low | Very High | High | Medium |
| **Adversarial Robustness** | Low | Low | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Handles unseen samples** | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | High | Medium | Medium | High |

---

## 3.6 Edge Cases & Boundary Conditions

1. **Empty sample**: $G_A = \emptyset \Rightarrow J = 0$, undefined cosine → convention $\cos = 0$.
2. **Identical samples**: $G_A = G_B \Rightarrow J = 1$, $\cos = 1$, $S_{ssdeep} = 100$.
3. **Tiny samples (< 1 KB)**: ssdeep may produce degenerate digests; recommend TLSH instead.
4. **Huge samples (> 100 MB)**: Use sampling + locality-sensitive hashing for tractability.
5. **Fully encrypted payloads**: Only behavioural / ML embedding approaches work — static byte methods fail.
6. **Adversarial perturbations** (e.g., Deadbolt attack 2023): Insert benign n-grams to dilute Jaccard. Mitigation: weighted variants (TF-IDF) and ensemble methods.
