# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

---

## 4.1 End-to-End Similarity Detection Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspicious Sample Input] --> B[Pre-Processing Module]
    B --> B1[Static Unpacking UPX Themida]
    B --> B2[PE Header Normalisation]
    B --> B3[Format Conversion PE to BIN]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C[Feature Extraction Engine]
    C --> C1[Byte N-Gram Generator n=4]
    C --> C2[Fuzzy Hash ssdeep]
    C --> C3[Disassembler Capstone]
    C --> C4[CFG Builder]
    C --> C5[Sandbox Detonation Cuckoo]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D[Similarity Scoring Engine]
    D --> D1[Jaccard Distance Module]
    D --> D2[Cosine Similarity Module]
    D --> D3[Edit Distance Module]
    D --> D4[Graph Kernel Module]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E[Reference Database Lookup]
    E --> F[Vector DB FAISS Milvus]
    E --> G[Malware Hash DB VirusTotal]
    E --> H[Local Malware Corpus]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I[Threshold Classifier]
    I --> I1{Similarity Score S greater than T}
    I1 -- Yes --> J[Flag as Malware Family Attribution]
    I1 -- No --> K[Mark as Novel or Benign]
    J --> L[Forensic Report]
    K --> L
```

---

## 4.2 Similarity Technique Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q1{Sample is Packed or Encrypted}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Dynamic Sandbox Available}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Need Obfuscation Robustness}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Use Behavioural API Sequence Similarity]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Use ML Embedding Similarity]
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use CFG Graph Similarity]
    Q3 -- No --> R4{Need Real Time Scanning}
    R4 -- Yes --> R5[Use Fuzzy Hash ssdeep TLSH]
    R4 -- No --> R6[Use N-Gram Jaccard Cosine]
    R1 --> END[Output Family Match]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R5 --> END
    R6 --> END
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture: Ensemble Similarity Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Incoming Sample] --> A1[Fuzzy Hash Engine]
    INPUT --> A2[N-Gram Jaccard Engine]
    INPUT --> A3[ML Embedding Engine]
    A1 --> B1[Fuzzy Score SF]
    A2 --> B2[N-Gram Score SN]
    A3 --> B3[ML Score SM]
    B1 --> C[Weighted Fusion Layer]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{SF times W1 plus SN times W2 plus SM times W3 greater than T}
    D -- Yes --> E[Malware Verdict]
    D -- No --> F[Benign Verdict]
    E --> G[Family Attribution Module]
```

**Fusion Formula (Engineered):**

$$
S_{final} = w_1 \cdot S_F + w_2 \cdot S_N + w_3 \cdot S_M
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ and weights are tuned via ROC optimisation on a labelled validation set.

---

## 4.4 Lifecycle: From Sample to Forensic Verdict

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PHASE1[Phase 1: Acquisition] --> PHASE2[Phase 2: Feature Engineering]
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3[Phase 3: Similarity Computation]
    PHASE3 --> PHASE4[Phase 4: Threshold Classification]
    PHASE4 --> PHASE5[Phase 5: Reporting and Attribution]
    PHASE2 --> F1[Static Features Bytes Opcodes Strings]
    PHASE2 --> F2[Dynamic Features API Calls Network IO]
    PHASE2 --> F3[Graph Features CFG Call Graph]
    F1 --> PHASE3
    F2 --> PHASE3
    F3 --> PHASE3
    PHASE3 --> COMP1[Distance Metrics]
    PHASE3 --> COMP2[Kernel Methods]
    PHASE3 --> COMP3[Deep Embeddings]
    COMP1 --> PHASE4
    COMP2 --> PHASE4
    COMP3 --> PHASE4
    PHASE4 --> R1[TP True Positive]
    PHASE4 --> R2[FP False Positive]
    PHASE4 --> R3[TN True Negative]
    PHASE4 --> R4[FN False Negative]
    R1 --> PHASE5
    R2 --> PHASE5
    R3 --> PHASE5
    R4 --> PHASE5
    PHASE5 --> OUT[Forensic Report with Family Threat Actor and TTPs]
```

---

## 4.5 Data Flow: Reference Corpus Indexing vs Query

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OFFLINE_INDEXING
        D1[Malware Sample 1] --> E1[Feature Vector]
        D2[Malware Sample 2] --> E2[Feature Vector]
        D3[Malware Sample N] --> EN[Feature Vector]
        E1 --> IDX[Vector Database FAISS]
        E2 --> IDX
        EN --> IDX
    end
    subgraph ONLINE_QUERY
        QUERY[New Suspicious Sample] --> QFE[Feature Extraction]
        QFE --> QV[Query Vector]
        QV --> SIM[Similarity Search]
        IDX --> SIM
        SIM --> TOPK[Top K Nearest Neighbours]
        TOPK --> DEC[Threshold Decision]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Architectural Insight**
> The **offline indexing** of a large malware corpus is the *bottleneck*. Modern systems use **LSH (Locality Sensitive Hashing)** to pre-bucket similar vectors, achieving sub-linear query time. This is why KTU examiners often ask about complexity: brute-force $O(N \cdot d)$ vs LSH $O(\log N \cdot d)$ per query.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]`

> **Q:** Differentiate between **signature-based** and **similarity-based** malware detection techniques. State **two** advantages of similarity-based techniques over signature-based ones.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Signature-Based | Similarity-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Matching Type | Exact byte/pattern match | Approximate / fuzzy match |
| Detection of Variants | Poor (fails on packing) | Strong (fuzzy + n-grams) |
| Zero-Day Capability | None | Yes (via nearest neighbour) |
| Database Dependency | Yes (signature DB) | Yes (feature corpus) |
| Computational Cost | Very low | Medium to high |
| False Positive Rate | Low | Slightly higher (tunable) |

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Robust to obfuscation** — Detects packed, encrypted, and polymorphic variants that evade signature scanning.
2. **Family clustering and attribution** — Identifies the lineage and threat-actor group behind a sample, not just a binary "malicious/benign" decision.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Students often write "similarity-based is *faster* than signature-based" — this is **FALSE**. Similarity techniques are computationally *heavier*. The real advantage is **detection coverage**, not speed. KTU examiners mark this as a factual error.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`

> **Q:** What is **fuzzy hashing**? Explain how **ssdeep** enables approximate matching of malware samples.

**Model Answer:**

**Fuzzy Hashing** is a hashing technique that produces a digest preserving *similarity information* — small changes in input produce *similar* digests, unlike cryptographic hashes.

**ssdeep (CTPH) Working:**
1. **Rolling hash scans** the file byte-by-byte.
2. **Trigger points** (rolling hash mod B = 0) define block boundaries.
3. Each block is hashed (e.g., FNV); the sequence forms a digest.
4. **Comparison**: Two digests are aligned and matching block count $M$ yields score:

$$
S_{ssdeep} = \frac{2M}{L_1 + L_2} \times 100
$$

A score $\geq 40$ indicates the samples are similar.

**Key advantage:** Detects variants sharing 50–90% of their bytes (e.g., repacked builds of the same malware).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain **Jaccard Similarity** and **Cosine Similarity** metrics used in malware analysis. Compute the Jaccard similarity for the following byte n-gram sets:
> - $G_A = \{\text{0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0x78, 0x9A, 0xBC}\}$
> - $G_B = \{\text{0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0xAB, 0xCD, 0xEF}\}$
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Discuss how **n-gram analysis** helps in detecting **malware variants**. Compare its **robustness** against **packing** and **metamorphic** transformations.

---

### Model Answer — Question B1 (a) [7 Marks]

**Jaccard Similarity** measures the overlap between two sets relative to their union:

$$
J(G_A, G_B) = \frac{\vert G_A \cap G_B \vert}{\vert G_A \cup G_B \vert}
$$

Range: $[0, 1]$. $J = 1$ implies identical sets; $J = 0$ implies disjoint sets.

**Cosine Similarity** measures the angle between two feature vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$:

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert \, \Vert\vec{B}\Vert}
$$

Range: $[-1, 1]$; for non-negative vectors (e.g., TF-IDF), the range is $[0, 1]$.

**Key Difference:** Jaccard is **set-based** (considers only presence/absence), while Cosine is **magnitude-aware** (considers frequency weights).

**Computation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the intersection:**

$$
G_A \cap G_B = \{ \text{0x12, 0x34, 0x56} \}
$$

$$
\vert G_A \cap G_B \vert = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the union:**

$$
G_A \cup G_B = \{ \text{0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0x78, 0x9A, 0xBC, 0xAB, 0xCD, 0xEF} \}
$$

$$
\vert G_A \cup G_B \vert = 9
$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**

$$
J(G_A, G_B) = \frac{3}{9} = 0.3333
$$

**Result: 33.33% Jaccard similarity** — the samples share one-third of their unique n-grams, suggesting moderate relatedness.

---

### Model Answer — Question B1 (b) [7 Marks]

**N-Gram Analysis for Malware Variant Detection:**

An n-gram is a contiguous sequence of $n$ bytes (or opcodes) extracted from a sample. By converting a binary into a set of n-grams and computing set-level similarity (Jaccard/Cosine), analysts can detect **polymorphic variants** of the same malware family.

**Why It Works:**
- Polymorphic engines typically change only a small fraction of code (e.g., junk insertion, register renaming).
- The **majority of n-grams remain stable** across variants.
- Jaccard/Cosine scores therefore stay high (e.g., 0.6–0.9) even when bytes differ.

**Robustness Analysis:**

| Transformation | Effect on N-Grams | Robustness |
|---|---|---|
| **Packing (UPX, Themida)** | Original bytes encrypted → original n-grams destroyed | ✗ Poor (pre-unpack needed) |
| **Instruction reordering** | Disrupts contiguous n-grams | △ Partial (use CFG instead) |
| **Dead code insertion** | Adds few new n-grams; originals preserved | ✓ Good |
| **Register renaming** | Opcodes change; bytes may change | △ Partial |
| **Substitution ciphers** | Byte distribution changes | ✗ Poor |
| **API call substitution** | Behavioural n-grams change | ✗ Poor (use behavioural) |
| **Metamorphism (full rewrite)** | Almost all n-grams change | ✗ Poor (use CFG/ML) |

**Conclusion:** N-gram analysis is **highly robust to dead-code insertion and minor padding** but fails against **packing and full metamorphism**. For the latter, **CFG-based or ML-embedding** similarity must be layered on top.

---

### Question B2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Internal Choice Pattern]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **ssdeep fuzzy hashing algorithm** in detail. Derive the comparison score formula from first principles using F1-score logic.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Control Flow Graph (CFG) based similarity** approach. Why is CFG similarity considered **robust against obfuscation**? Mention **two limitations**.

---

### Model Answer — Question B2 (a) [7 Marks]

**ssdeep — Context Triggered Piecewise Hashing (CTPH):**

**Step 1 — Rolling Hash Computation:**
A rolling hash $R(i)$ is computed over the file bytes. A common choice is:

$$
R(i) = (B \cdot R(i-1) + \text{byte}_i) \mod M
$$

where $B$ is a base constant and $M$ is a modulus.

**Step 2 — Trigger Condition:**
A block boundary is triggered when $R(i) \mod B_{size} = 0$. The block size $B_{size}$ is dynamically chosen so that the file is divided into $2^k$ equal pieces for some integer $k$.

**Step 3 — Block Hashing:**
At each trigger, a traditional hash $H_j$ (e.g., FNV-1a) is computed over the bytes since the last trigger. This produces a sequence of block hashes $D = (H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_L)$.

**Step 4 — Digest Formation:**
The sequence is compressed into a base64-like string for compact storage.

**Step 5 — Comparison Score Derivation (from F1):**

The score balances **precision** $P = \frac{M}{L_1}$ and **recall** $R = \frac{M}{L_2}$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{M}{L_1} \cdot \frac{M}{L_2}}{\frac{M}{L_1} + \frac{M}{L_2}}
$$

Algebraic simplification (shown in Section 3.1) yields:

$$
S_{ssdeep} = \frac{2M}{L_1 + L_2} \times 100
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $S \geq 70$ → Strong similarity (likely same family).
- $40 \leq S < 70$ → Weak similarity (possible variant).
- $S < 40$ → Unrelated.

---

### Model Answer — Question B2 (b) [7 Marks]

**CFG-Based Similarity:**

**Concept:** Every function in a disassembled binary can be represented as a **Control Flow Graph** $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ = basic blocks and $E$ = control-flow edges (jumps, calls, conditional branches).

**Pipeline:**
1. Disassemble the binary (Capstone, IDA Pro, Ghidra).
2. Extract CFG for each function.
3. Compute a **fingerprint** for each basic block (e.g., mnemonic sequence hash).
4. Compare CFGs using:
   - **Graph Edit Distance (GED)** — minimum edit operations to transform $G_1 \to G_2$.
   - **Graph Kernels** — Weisfeiler-Lehman subtree kernel.
   - **Subgraph isomorphism** — matching identical substructures.

**Why CFG is Obfuscation-Resistant:**

1. **Packing preserves control flow** — Even if the code is encrypted on disk, after unpacking at runtime, the control flow structure is identical to the original.
2. **Instruction substitution preserves CFG** — Replacing `ADD` with `SUB` and adjusting constants does not change the *flow*, only the *content* of blocks.
3. **Junk-code insertion adds nodes but not flow** — Block hashing and noise-tolerant graph matching handle this.

**Two Limitations:**

1. **Computational cost** — Graph edit distance is NP-hard in the general case. Real-world CFG matching uses heuristic approximations (VF2, WL kernel).
2. **Disassembly failures** — Obfuscated binaries (e.g., opaque predicates, indirect jumps) cause disassembly errors → incomplete or incorrect CFGs → degraded similarity scores.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q B2)**
> A common mistake: students claim "CFG similarity is **immune** to all obfuscation." This is wrong. CFG is robust to **most** obfuscation, but **opaque predicates**, **indirect jump obfuscation**, and **anti-disassembly tricks** can break it. Use precise language: "*robust against packing and most instruction-level obfuscation*."

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Similarity-based detection** replaces exact matching with **fuzzy/approximate matching** to handle packed, polymorphic, and zero-day malware.
- **Key insight:** Malware families share **features** (bytes, opcodes, API calls, CFG) even when their bytes differ completely.
- **Syntactic techniques:** Byte n-grams → Jaccard/Cosine; Fuzzy hashes (ssdeep, TLSH).
- **Structural techniques:** CFG comparison via GED or graph kernels; highly obfuscation-resistant.
- **Behavioural techniques:** API call sequence alignment (edit distance, LCS) from sandbox traces.
- **Semantic techniques:** ML embeddings (MalConv, EMBER) + nearest-neighbour search in vector DBs.
- **ssdeep score formula:**

$$
S_{ssdeep} = \frac{2M}{L_1 + L_2} \times 100
$$

- **Jaccard:**

$$
J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
$$

- **Cosine:**

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert \Vert\vec{B}\Vert}
$$

- **Threshold rule:** Classify as malware if $S \geq T$. $T$ is chosen via **ROC analysis** to satisfy $FPR \leq 5\%$.
- **Ensemble methods** (fuzzy hash + n-gram + ML) outperform single-technique detectors in 2024 benchmarks.
- **Key advantages:** Detects variants, zero-days, performs family clustering, enables threat-actor attribution.
- **Key limitations:** Higher computational cost, requires reference corpus, susceptible to adversarial perturbation (e.g., Deadbolt, 2023).
- **Industry tools:** VirusTotal retrohunt, MalShare, MalwareBazaar, EMBER dataset, Kaspersky KLara, Mandiant FAT.
- **Evaluation metrics:** Precision, Recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC, FPR, FNR.
- **2024 trend:** Vector databases (FAISS, Milvus) + transformer-based malware embeddings (e.g., **Avast-style CNN + BERT opcode embeddings**) are state-of-the-art.
- **Exam tip:** When asked to "compare techniques," always use a **comparison table** with at least 4 dimensions (speed, robustness, cost, accuracy). KTU examiners award full marks only to structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning (ML)** in the context of malware forensics is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computational systems to autonomously learn patterns, behaviors, and signatures indicative of malicious software from empirical data, without being explicitly programmed with static rule sets. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECCT741, ML methods form the analytical backbone of modern malware detection, classification, and attribution pipelines.

The formal definition by **Arthur Samuel (1959)** characterizes ML as:

> *"The field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

A more mathematically rigorous definition by **Tom Mitchell (1997)** states:

> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

In malware forensics, this translates to:
- **Task (T)**: Classify a binary/script/sample as *malicious* or *benign* (or into specific malware families).
- **Experience (E)**: A dataset of previously labeled malware and benign samples with their static/dynamic features.
- **Performance (P)**: Detection rate, False Positive Rate (FPR), accuracy, F1-score, or AUC-ROC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Under Module 2, students must understand the *application* of ML methods (not merely their theoretical construction) to malware static analysis (PE headers, opcode n-grams, strings) and dynamic analysis (API call sequences, network behavior, system calls).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cyber Detective"

Imagine you are a forensic detective who has never seen a criminal before. Instead of being given a rigid rule book, you are shown **thousands of case files** — some marked "criminal," some "innocent." Over time, your brain begins to identify subtle patterns: the way criminals walk, the tools they carry, the times they strike. Eventually, when a new suspect appears, you can predict — with reasonable confidence — whether they are dangerous.

**Machine Learning works exactly like this:**

| Real-World Detective | Machine Learning in Malware Forensics |
|---|---|
| Case files (past cases) | Labeled dataset of malware/benign samples |
| Patterns observed | Extracted features (API calls, byte sequences) |
| Detective's brain | The ML model (classifier) |
| New suspect | New, unseen binary to analyze |
| Verdict: "criminal/innocent" | Prediction: "malicious/benign" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Just as a detective gets better with more cases, an ML model improves with more *high-quality, labeled training data*. This is the fundamental principle of *empirical risk minimization*.

## 1.3 Why ML for Malware? — The Motivation

Traditional **signature-based detection** fails against:
1. **Polymorphic malware** — same behavior, different code each time.
2. **Metamorphic malware** — completely rewritten code with same payload.
3. **Zero-day exploits** — no known signature exists.
4. **Packing and obfuscation** — hidden code that bypasses static signatures.

ML overcomes these by learning *behavioral* and *statistical* invariants rather than byte-exact patterns.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favorite Question**: *"Why can't we use signature-based detection for modern malware?"* — Always answer with the four points above and conclude with: *"ML learns generalized patterns, making it robust to obfuscation and novel variants."*

## 1.4 The Three Paradigms of Learning (Foundational Taxonomy)

The KTU syllabus requires mastery of these learning paradigms:

1. **Supervised Learning** — Model learns from *labeled* data $(X, y)$ pairs. Used for malware **classification** (malicious vs. benign) and **family attribution**.
2. **Unsupervised Learning** — Model finds hidden structure in *unlabeled* data $X$. Used for **malware clustering**, anomaly detection, and discovering new threat families.
3. **Semi-Supervised Learning** — Combines a small amount of labeled data with a large amount of unlabeled data. Critical for malware forensics where labeling is expensive.
4. **Reinforcement Learning** — Agent learns by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards/penalties. Used in **adversarial malware generation** and **adaptive defense systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Weightage**: Supervised and Unsupervised methods carry the highest marks. Be prepared to compare algorithms within each paradigm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Machine Learning Pipeline for Malware Detection

A standard ML-based malware detection system follows a six-stage pipeline:

1. **Data Acquisition** — Collect malware samples (from VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, etc.) and benign samples.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — Disassemble, unpack, normalize, and clean samples.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Extract static features (PE headers, opcodes, strings, byte n-grams) or dynamic features (API calls, syscalls, network traffic).
4. **Feature Selection / Dimensionality Reduction** — Apply PCA, chi-square, or mutual information to remove irrelevant features.
5. **Model Training** — Train classifier (SVM, Random Forest, Neural Network) on the labeled feature vectors.
6. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Test on unseen data, measure metrics, then deploy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality**: Stage 3 (Feature Extraction) is often the most critical. *Garbage in, garbage out* — poor features yield poor models regardless of algorithm sophistication.

## 2.2 Feature Engineering — The Two Schools

### 2.2.1 Static Features (Analysis without execution)
- **PE Header fields**: `Machine`, `NumberOfSections`, `SizeOfImage`, `EntryPoint`.
- **Byte n-grams**: Sequences of $n$ consecutive bytes (e.g., bigrams, trigrams).
- **Opcode n-grams**: Frequency of assembly instruction sequences.
- **String features**: URLs, file paths, registry keys embedded in the binary.
- **Section entropy**: $H(s) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ where $p_i$ is the probability of byte $i$ in section $s$.

### 2.2.2 Dynamic Features (Analysis through execution in sandbox)
- **API call sequences**: Order and frequency of Windows API invocations.
- **System calls**: File operations, registry modifications, process creation.
- **Network behavior**: IPs contacted, DNS queries, HTTP requests, packet sizes.
- **Resource usage patterns**: CPU, memory, I/O.

## 2.3 Supervised Learning Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.3.1 Decision Tree
A tree-structured classifier that recursively splits data based on feature values to maximize *information gain* or minimize *Gini impurity*.

**Gini Impurity** at node $n$:

$$G(n) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} (p_i)^2$$

**Entropy** at node $n$:

$$H(n) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

**Information Gain** of split $S$:

$$IG(S) = H(\text{parent}) - \sum_{j} \frac{\vert N_j \vert}{\vert N \vert} H(N_j)$$

### 2.3.2 Naive Bayes Classifier
Based on **Bayes' Theorem** with the "naive" assumption of conditional independence between features.

$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

For multiple features with independence assumption:

$$P(C_k \vert x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \propto P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C_k)$$

Prediction: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C_k)$

### 2.3.3 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)
Classifies a sample based on the majority class of its $k$ closest training samples in feature space, using distance metrics like **Euclidean** or **Manhattan**.

**Euclidean Distance** between samples $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$:

$$d(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (a_i - b_i)^2}$$

**Manhattan Distance**:

$$d(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert a_i - b_i \vert$$

### 2.3.4 Support Vector Machine (SVM)
Finds the optimal **hyperplane** that maximally separates classes. The decision boundary satisfies:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

The optimization objective (soft-margin SVM with slack variables $\xi_i$):

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

Subject to: $y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$

The **kernel trick** (e.g., RBF kernel) allows non-linear separation:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\left(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2\right)$$

### 2.3.5 Random Forest
An **ensemble** of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples with random feature subsets. Final prediction is by majority vote (classification) or averaging (regression).

## 2.4 Unsupervised Learning Algorithms

### 2.4.1 K-Means Clustering
Partitions $N$ samples into $K$ clusters by minimizing **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{K} r_{ij} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$$

where $r_{ij} = 1$ if sample $i$ belongs to cluster $j$, else $0$; $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$ is the centroid of cluster $j$.

**Algorithm steps**:
1. Initialize $K$ centroids randomly.
2. **Assignment step**: Assign each $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the nearest centroid.
3. **Update step**: Recompute centroids as cluster means.
4. Repeat until convergence.

### 2.4.2 Hierarchical Clustering
Builds a dendrogram by either:
- **Agglomerative** (bottom-up): Start with each point as its own cluster, merge closest pairs.
- **Divisive** (top-down): Start with one cluster, recursively split.

**Linkage criteria** for distance between clusters $A$ and $B$:
- Single: $d(A,B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$
- Complete: $d(A,B) = \max_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$
- Average: $d(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert} \sum_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$

### 2.4.3 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering)
Groups densely packed points and marks low-density points as outliers. Requires two parameters: $\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius) and $\text{MinPts}$ (minimum points to form a dense region).

## 2.5 Deep Learning for Malware (Advanced)

### 2.5.1 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
Treats raw bytes or opcode sequences as 1D/2D "images" and learns hierarchical feature representations via convolutional filters.

### 2.5.2 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) / LSTM
Models API call sequences as temporal dependencies. The **LSTM cell** uses three gates:
- **Forget gate**: $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$
- **Input gate**: $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$
- **Output gate**: $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$

### 2.5.3 Autoencoders
Unsupervised neural networks for **anomaly detection**. An encoder maps input $\mathbf{x}$ to latent $\mathbf{z}$, a decoder reconstructs $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$. High reconstruction error → anomaly (potential malware).

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2$$

## 2.6 Evaluation Metrics — The Confusion Matrix Foundation

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| True Positive | $TP$ | Malware correctly classified as malware |
| True Negative | $TN$ | Benign correctly classified as benign |
| False Positive | $FP$ | Benign misclassified as malware (Type I error) |
| False Negative | $FN$ | Malware misclassified as benign (Type II error) |

**Derived Metrics**:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$\text{Recall (TPR, Sensitivity)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$\text{F1-Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

$$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

$$\text{Specificity (TNR)} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In malware detection, Recall is often more critical than Accuracy.** Missing a malware (FN) is far more dangerous than a false alarm (FP). This is why F1-Score (harmonic mean) is preferred over accuracy in imbalanced datasets (where benign samples vastly outnumber malware).

## 2.7 ROC Curve and AUC

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots TPR (y-axis) against FPR (x-axis) at various classification thresholds. The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** quantifies classifier performance:

- AUC = 1.0: Perfect classifier
- AUC = 0.5: Random guessing
- AUC < 0.5: Worse than random

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Foundation of Naive Bayes |
| Entropy | $H(n) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | Measure of impurity in Decision Tree |
| Gini Impurity | $G(n) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$ | Alternative impurity measure |
| Information Gain | $IG = H(\text{parent}) - \sum_{j} \frac{\vert N_j \vert}{\vert N \vert} H(N_j)$ | Feature selection criterion |
| K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{K} r_{ij} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$ | Minimize intra-cluster variance |
| SVM Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ | Maximize margin hyperplane |
| RBF Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ | Non-linear SVM mapping |
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| Recall | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| F1-Score | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic balance of P and R |
| Autoencoder Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2$ | Anomaly detection threshold |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid**: "**P**recision = **P**urity of positives, **R**ecall = **R**ecovery of all positives, **F1** = **F**air balance." Use this mnemonic for viva questions.

## 2.9 Overfitting, Underfitting, and Regularization

- **Underfitting**: Model too simple, high bias, low variance. Fix: Add features, use complex model.
- **Overfitting**: Model memorizes training data, low bias, high variance. Fix: Regularization, dropout, more data, cross-validation.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**: $\text{Total Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Error}$
- **L2 Regularization**: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L} + \lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$
- **L1 Regularization (Lasso)**: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L} + \lambda \sum_i \vert w_i \vert$

## 2.10 Cross-Validation

**k-Fold Cross-Validation** splits data into $k$ equal folds; model is trained on $k-1$ folds and validated on the remaining fold, rotating $k$ times.

$$\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Score}_i$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to Naive Bayes Classifier

We start with Bayes' Theorem and the conditional independence assumption to derive the working equation of the Naive Bayes classifier used in spam/malware detection.

**Starting Point — Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the joint probability** for feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \vert C_k)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule of probability**:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) = P(x_1 \vert C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \vert x_1, C_k) \cdot P(x_3 \vert x_1, x_2, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \vert x_1, \dots, x_{n-1}, C_k)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the *Naive* Conditional Independence Assumption**: Each feature $x_i$ is conditionally independent of every other feature given the class $C_k$.

$$P(x_i \vert x_1, \dots, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, \dots, x_n, C_k) = P(x_i \vert C_k)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back** into the chain rule:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C_k)$$

**Step 5 — Final posterior** for class $C_k$:

$$P(C_k \vert x_1, \dots, x_n) = \frac{P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 6 — Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant for all classes**, drop it during the $\arg\max$:

$$\hat{C}_k = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C_k)$$

**Step 7 — To prevent numerical underflow**, take the logarithm (a monotonic transformation preserves the $\arg\max$):

$$\hat{C}_k = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \vert C_k) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification**: Despite the "naive" assumption rarely holding in real malware datasets (API calls *are* correlated), Naive Bayes works surprisingly well in practice. It serves as a strong **baseline model** before deploying more complex classifiers.

## 3.2 Derivation: F1-Score from Precision and Recall

The F1-Score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$). We derive why the harmonic mean (not arithmetic mean) is the correct aggregator.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of harmonic mean** of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$:

$$H(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $a = P$ and $b = R$**:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the fractions** in the denominator:

$$\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R} = \frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}$$

**Step 4 — Invert the denominator**:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expanded definitions** $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP + FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP + FP} + \frac{TP}{TP + FN}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the numerator and denominator**:

$$F_1 = \frac{\frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}}{\frac{TP(TP + FN) + TP(TP + FP)}{(TP + FP)(TP + FN)}}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the common denominator**:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP + FN) + TP(TP + FP)} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Final Result**:

$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why harmonic mean and not arithmetic mean?** The arithmetic mean $A = (P + R)/2$ yields high values even when one metric is near zero (e.g., $P = 1.0, R = 0.01 \Rightarrow A = 0.505$, but the model is essentially useless). The harmonic mean **penalizes extreme imbalances**, making it the fair metric for imbalanced malware datasets.

## 3.3 Derivation: K-Means Convergence Conditions

We derive the conditions under which the K-Means algorithm is guaranteed to converge.

**Objective (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{K} r_{ij} \cdot \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2$$

**Step 1 — Assignment step** (minimize $J$ w.r.t. $r_{ij}$ holding $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$ fixed):

$$r_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } j = \arg\min_{k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Update step** (minimize $J$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$ holding $r_{ij}$ fixed). Take partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_j} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_{ij} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_j$**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_{ij} \mathbf{x}_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_{ij} \boldsymbol{\mu}_j = \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_{ij}$$

**Step 4 — Final centroid update**:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_{ij} \mathbf{x}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_{ij}}$$

This is the **mean of all points assigned to cluster $j$**.

**Step 5 — Convergence Proof**: Since there are finitely many possible assignments ($K^N$ for $N$ points and $K$ clusters) and $J$ is monotonically non-increasing at each iteration, the algorithm **must converge in finite steps** to a local minimum (not necessarily global).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of ML Algorithms for Malware

| Algorithm | Learning Type | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Use Case in Malware Forensics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Naive Bayes | Supervised | Fast, handles high-dim, probabilistic | Independence assumption rarely holds | Spam/URL-based malware filtering |
| Decision Tree | Supervised | Interpretable, handles mixed features | Prone to overfitting | Rule extraction from PE headers |
| Random Forest | Supervised (Ensemble) | Robust, handles imbalance, feature importance | Less interpretable, slower | Production-grade malware classification |
| SVM | Supervised | Effective in high-dim, robust to overfit | Slow on large datasets | Small-sample malware family detection |
| k-NN | Supervised | Simple, no training phase | Slow prediction, curse of dimensionality | Behavioral sequence matching |
| K-Means | Unsupervised | Simple, scalable | Must specify $K$, sensitive to init | Grouping similar malware variants |
| DBSCAN | Unsupervised | Finds arbitrary shapes, detects outliers | Struggles with varying densities | Zero-day anomaly detection |
| Hierarchical | Unsupervised | Dendrogram visualization | $O(N^2)$ complexity, not scalable | Small dataset family tree analysis |
| CNN | Deep Learning | Learns spatial patterns from bytes/images | Requires large data, black-box | Raw byte-based image classification |
| LSTM/RNN | Deep Learning | Captures sequential API dependencies | Long training time, vanishing gradient | API call sequence analysis |
| Autoencoder | Deep Unsupervised | Excellent for anomaly detection | Reconstruction error threshold tuning | Detecting novel malware variants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reality**: Major antivirus vendors (Kaspersky, Sophos, Microsoft Defender) now use **gradient-boosted ensembles** (XGBoost, LightGBM) over Random Forest, and increasingly deep learning (CNN+LSTM hybrids) for behavioral analysis. Be prepared to discuss this in viva.
