```python
import Evtx.Evtx as evtx
import Evtx.Views as e_views

with evtx.Evtx("Security.evtx") as log:
    for record in log.records():
        xml = record.xml()
        if "<EventID>4624</EventID>" in xml:
            # Extract Logon_Type, TargetUserName, SourceIpAddress
            ...
```

### Step 3: Timeline Correlation

The output of Step 2 must be **time-correlated** with:
- Malware file creation timestamps (`$STANDARD_INFORMATION` of the PE file)
- Malware execution timestamps (UserAssist, Prefetch, Shimcache, Amcache)
- Network connection timestamps (firewall logs, NetFlow, PCAP)

**Equation of Forensic Certainty (Conceptual Model):**

$$
P(\text{User} \mid \text{Malware}) \;=\; \frac{P(\text{Malware} \mid \text{User}) \cdot P(\text{User})}{P(\text{Malware})}
$$

This is a **Bayesian interpretation**: the probability that a particular user executed the malware is proportional to the conditional probability of that user executing a process at the malware's execution time, weighted by the user's baseline activity frequency.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Platform Forensic Identification

| OS | Live Command | Persistent Artifact | Log File | Memory Artifact |
|----|--------------|---------------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Windows 10/11** | `query user`, `qwinsta`, `psloggedon` | SAM registry hive, UserAssist | `Security.evtx` | `winlogon.exe`, `lsass.exe` |
| **Windows Server** | `query session`, `qwinsta` | SAM, NTDS.dit (AD) | `Security.evtx` | `lsass.exe` |
| **Linux** | `who`, `w`, `last` | `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow` | `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp` | `pam_unix.so` sessions |
| **macOS** | `who`, `w`, `last`, `dscl . list /Users` | `/var/db/dslocal/nodes/Default/users` | `/private/var/log/wtmpx`, `/private/var/log/utmpx` | `loginwindow`, `securityd` |
| **Network Devices** | `show users`, `show sessions` | Local user DB | Syslog, TACACS+/RADIUS | N/A (RAM-only) |

## 3.6 Counter-Forensic Indicators (Red Flags for Tampering)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Forensic Hygiene Check:** Before trusting any user identification, scan for the following red flags indicating **anti-forensic activity**:

1. **Event Log Cleared** — Event ID **1102** (Windows Security log cleared) or **104** (System log cleared).
2. **`wevtutil cl Security`** execution trace in EID 4688 (process creation).
3. **Empty or Truncated `wtmp`** — abnormally small file size relative to system uptime.
4. **Skewed `utmp` Timestamps** — `utmpdump` reveals chronologically impossible entries.
5. **Disabled Audit Policy** — `auditpol /get /category:*` shows no auditing.
6. **Timestomped UserAssist** — LastWrite timestamp older than NTUSER.DAT creation date.
7. **Absent Security.evtx** — file does not exist or is 68 KB (the default "empty" size).
8. **Unauthorized `runas` / `psexec` activity** — Event ID 4648 from unexpected Source IPs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — User Identification Triangulation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspect System Seized] --> B[Volatile Data Collection]
    A --> C[Persistent Data Acquisition]
    A --> D[Log Acquisition]
    
    B --> B1[query user / qwinsta]
    B --> B2[psloggedon / net session]
    B --> B3[Volatility windows.sessions]
    B1 --> E[Active Logon Sessions]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    
    C --> C1[SAM Hive]
    C --> C2[UserAssist Registry]
    C --> C3[Prefetch Folder]
    C1 --> F[Local Account Database]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    
    D --> D1[Security.evtx EID 4624]
    D --> D2[wtmp / btmp / lastlog]
    D --> D3[PowerShell ScriptBlock EID 4104]
    D1 --> G[Historical Logon Timeline]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    
    E --> H{Convergence Analysis}
    F --> H
    G --> H
    
    H -->|All 3 agree| I[Confirmed User Attribution]
    H -->|Partial agreement| J[Further Correlation Required]
    H -->|Contradiction| K[Anti-Forensics Investigation]
    
    I --> L[Chain of Custody Report]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Windows Logon Architecture (SecID 4624 Anatomy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Provides Credentials] --> B{Logon Type?}
    B -->|Type 2: Interactive| C[winlogon.exe]
    B -->|Type 3: Network| D[SMB or RPC]
    B -->|Type 10: RDP| E[termsrv.dll]
    B -->|Type 4: Batch| F[Task Scheduler]
    
    C --> G[LSASS - lsass.exe]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    
    G --> H{Local or Domain Account?}
    H -->|Local| I[SAM Database]
    H -->|Domain| J[Active Directory DC]
    
    I --> K[Event ID 4624 Generated]
    J --> K
    
    K --> L[Security Event Log]
    L --> M[Forensic Analyst Parses]
    
    G --> N[Access Token Created]
    N --> O[Process Spawned under User SID]
    O --> P[UserAssist / Prefetch Records Created]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Anti-Forensic Detection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Forensic Acquisition] --> B{Security.evtx Present?}
    B -->|No| C[CRITICAL: Log File Missing]
    B -->|Yes| D{Event ID 1102 in Log?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Log Was Cleared - High Suspicion]
    D -->|No| F{Event ID 4688 shows wevtutil?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Log Clear Command Executed]
    F -->|No| H{Audit Policy Active?}
    H -->|No| I[Auditing Disabled Pre-Incident]
    H -->|Yes| J[Acquire Security.evtx]
    
    E --> K[Initiate Memory Forensics]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> L[Parse with Chainsaw or EvtxECmd]
    L --> M[Build User Activity Timeline]
    K --> M
    C --> M
    M --> N[Final Attribution Report]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Linux Log File Inter-relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PAM Authentication Module] -->|Records Success| B[/var/log/wtmp]
    A -->|Records Failure| C[/var/log/btmp]
    A -->|Records Last Login| D[/var/log/lastlog]
    
    B -->|read by| E[last command]
    C -->|read by| F[lastb command]
    D -->|read by| G[lastlog command]
    
    B -->|binary format| H[utmpdump tool]
    C -->|binary format| H
    D -->|binary format| H
    
    H --> I[Forensic Timeline Generation]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    
    A -->|also logged to| J[/var/log/secure or auth.log]
    J --> I
    
    I --> K[Cross-Reference with /var/log/audit/audit.log]
    K --> L[Syscall-to-User Mapping]
    L --> M[Final User Activity Report]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Volatility Memory Analysis Pipeline for User Attribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RAM Image - mem.raw] --> B[Profile Detection]
    B --> C[windows.sessions]
    B --> D[windows.pslist]
    B --> E[windows.pstree]
    B --> F[windows.hashdump]
    B --> G[windows.cachedump]
    B --> H[windows.cmdscan]
    B --> I[windows.consoles]
    B --> J[windows.netscan]
    
    C --> K[Logon Session Map]
    D --> L[Process-User Mapping]
    E --> L
    F --> M[NTLM Hashes for Cracking]
    G --> N[Domain Cached Credentials v2]
    H --> O[Recovered CMD History]
    I --> O
    J --> P[Network Connections to Processes]
    
    K --> Q[Cross-Reference All Artifacts]
    L --> Q
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    
    Q --> R[Defensible User Attribution]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
**Define "Logon Type 3" in the context of Windows Security Event ID 4624. Why is it significant in malware forensic investigations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Logon Type 3 in Windows Security Event ID 4624 represents a **Network logon** — a logon performed by a computer that is accessing the resource over the network, without a local interactive keyboard or screen. It is generated when a user authenticates to a service on the target machine via **SMB, RPC, WinRM, RDP (non-interactive), or PsExec**.

**Forensic Significance:**
- It is the **primary indicator of lateral movement** in a post-exploitation scenario.
- Attackers use **PsExec**, **WMI**, **WinRM**, and **SMB relay attacks** that all manifest as Type 3 logons.
- A burst of Type 3 logons from a single source IP to multiple targets in a short time window strongly suggests **credential reuse or pass-the-hash** activity.
- The `Source Network Address` field of EID 4624 with Logon Type 3 is a **forensically critical attribution pivot**.

**(1 mark) Definition, (1 mark) Mechanism, (1 mark) Forensic Significance.**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
**List and briefly explain any three log files used for user identification in a Linux forensic investigation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **`/var/log/wtmp`** — A binary database that records **all successful logins, logouts, and system reboots**. It is read using the `last` command. Each record contains username, terminal, remote host, login time, logout time, and duration. Forensically, it provides a historical record of authenticated sessions. **(1 mark)**

2. **`/var/log/btmp`** — A binary database that records **only failed login attempts**. It is read using the `lastb` command. It is the primary artifact for detecting **SSH brute-force attacks** and credential-stuffing attempts. A sudden surge of entries targeting a single user account is a strong indicator of compromise. **(1 mark)**

3. **`/var/log/lastlog`** — A sparse binary file that records the **last login time and source** of every user account on the system. It is read using the `lastlog` command. Forensically, it provides a quick "who has ever logged in" map. Accounts with a lastlog entry older than the system's last reboot may indicate **abandoned or compromised accounts**. **(1 mark)**

*(Alternative: `/var/log/auth.log` or `/var/log/secure` — text-based authentication log; `/var/log/audit/audit.log` — auditd subsystem log.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **three evidence planes** used in Windows forensic user identification. Enumerate the Windows Security Event IDs that are most relevant for logon session reconstruction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A malware incident response is being conducted on a Windows 10 workstation. Describe the **step-by-step procedure** for identifying all users who had active or historical logon sessions on the system. Include both live triage and post-mortem analysis using memory and disk artifacts. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Three Evidence Planes and Critical Event IDs (7 marks)

**Block Diagram of the Three Evidence Planes:**

```
+--------------------------------------------+
|  PLANE 1: VOLATILE (RAM)                   |
|  - winlogon.exe / lsass.exe processes      |
|  - Active logon tokens                      |
|  - Volatility windows.sessions             |
|  - Volatility windows.hashdump             |
+--------------------------------------------+
                  ↕
+--------------------------------------------+
|  PLANE 2: PERSISTENT (DISK / REGISTRY)     |
|  - SAM hive (C:\Windows\System32\config)   |
|  - SECURITY hive (audit policies)          |
|  - NTUSER.DAT (per-user hive)              |
|  - UserAssist, Prefetch, Amcache           |
+--------------------------------------------+
                  ↕
+--------------------------------------------+
|  PLANE 3: BEHAVIORAL (LOGS)                |
|  - Security.evtx (EID 4624, 4625, 4634)    |
|  - System.evtx (EID 7045 - service install)|
|  - PowerShell.evtx (EID 4104 - script run) |
|  - Scheduled Tasks, RDP logs               |
+--------------------------------------------+
                  ↕
         CONVERGENCE → DEFENSIBLE
          USER ATTRIBUTION
```

**Windows Security Event IDs for Logon Reconstruction:**

| Event ID | Description | Logon Types |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **4624** | Successful logon | 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11 |
| **4625** | Failed logon (credential attack) | N/A |
| **4634** | Logoff (clean) | N/A |
| **4647** | User-initiated logoff | N/A |
| **4648** | Logon with explicit credentials | `runas`, lateral movement |
| **4672** | Special privileges to new logon | Admin confirmation |
| **4720** | Account created | Insider / persistence |
| **4724** | Password reset attempt | Account takeover |
| **4740** | Account locked out | Brute-force |
| **4768** | Kerberos TGT request | Domain authentication |
| **4769** | Kerberos service ticket | Kerberoasting |
| **4771** | Kerberos pre-auth failed | Kerberos brute-force |
| **4776** | DC validated credentials (NTLM) | NTLM relay / pass-the-hash |

**(4 marks for the diagram and description, 3 marks for the Event ID table.)**

#### (b) Step-by-Step User Identification Procedure on Windows 10 (7 marks)

**Phase 1 — Live Volatile Triage (must be first)**

**Step 1.1:** Document the currently authenticated user.
```cmd
whoami /all
```
Record the **SID, groups, and privileges**. The presence of `SeDebugPrivilege` and `SeBackupPrivilege` suggests elevated context. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 1.2:** Enumerate active sessions.
```cmd
query user
qwinsta
```
Record each `USERNAME`, `SESSION ID`, `STATE`, `IDLE TIME`, and `LOGON TIME`. Disconnected sessions with low idle time are suspicious. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 1.3:** Identify lateral movement connections.
```cmd
net session
net use
```
Unknown remote IPs in `net session` indicate post-exploitation activity. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 1.4:** Acquire memory image using `Magnet RAM Capture` or `winpmem`. Calculate SHA-256 hash immediately. **(0.5 marks)**

**Phase 2 — Memory Forensics with Volatility**

**Step 2.1:** Identify the profile.
```bash
vol.py -f mem.raw imageinfo
```
**(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2.2:** Map logon sessions to processes.
```bash
vol.py -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64_19041 windows.sessions
```
Output reveals which user SID owns each session. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2.3:** Extract credentials and console history.
```bash
vol.py -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64_19041 windows.hashdump
vol.py -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64_19041 windows.cachedump
vol.py -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64_19041 windows.cmdscan
vol.py -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64_19041 windows.consoles
```
**(1 mark)**

**Phase 3 — Persistent Disk Analysis**

**Step 3.1:** Parse the SAM hive from `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM` (using **Registry Explorer** or `reg lookup`). Identify all local accounts and their SIDs, last logon, and password policies. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3.2:** Parse the **UserAssist** registry key from each `NTUSER.DAT` to identify GUI-launched executables per user.
- Path: `Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist`
- Values are **ROT13-encoded GUIDs**; decode before reading. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3.3:** Parse **Prefetch** files (`%SystemRoot%\Prefetch`) for execution history. Each `.pf` file contains the executable name, run count, and last execution time. **(0.5 marks)**

**Phase 4 — Log-Based Historical Reconstruction**

**Step 4.1:** Export and parse `Security.evtx`.
```powershell
wevtutil epl Security E:\evidence\Security.evtx
```
Use **Chainsaw** or **EvtxECmd** to extract all EID **4624, 4625, 4634, 4648, 4672** events. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4.2:** Build a master timeline.
Cross-reference logon times with malware execution times (Amcache, Prefetch, MFT `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`). The convergence of these three data points yields a defensible attribution. **(1 mark)**

**Forensic Output Example Table:**

| User | Source IP | Logon Type | Logon Time | Process | First Seen in |
|------|-----------|------------|------------|---------|---------------|
| `CORP\jdoe` | 10.10.5.42 | 2 (Interactive) | 2024-11-12 09:14:22 | `cmd.exe` | EID 4624 + Prefetch |
| `CORP\admin` | 10.10.5.99 | 3 (Network) | 2024-11-12 09:21:07 | `psexec.exe` | EID 4624 + Process Create |

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **Volatility framework's plugins** for extracting user identification data from a Windows memory image. List at least **four (4) plugins** with their forensic utility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the user identification artifacts in **Windows** versus **Linux** systems. Use a tabular comparison and discuss at least **three anti-forensic techniques** that attackers employ to subvert user identification. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Volatility Plugins for User Identification (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Volatility is an open-source, Python-based memory forensics framework. For user identification, the `windows.*` plugin family provides a comprehensive set of tools that extract user-related artifacts directly from a Windows RAM image.

**The Four Essential Plugins:**

**1. `windows.sessions` (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Enumerates all active and historical logon sessions resident in memory.
- **Mechanism:** Walks the `_KENEL_SESSION` structures (`_SEP_LOGON_SESSION_REFERENCES`) in the kernel and links them to the corresponding user SID and the hosting process (typically `winlogon.exe` for interactive sessions, `svchost.exe` for service sessions).
- **Forensic Utility:** Maps the `qwinsta` session ID to the underlying process and authenticated user. This is the **primary tool for confirming "who was logged in when"** in volatile memory.

**2. `windows.hashdump` (1.5 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Extracts **local SAM account hashes** (NTLM format) from the `SAM` registry hive in memory.
- **Mechanism:** Parses the in-memory representation of the SAM hive, decrypts the `V` (encrypted hash) values using the SYSKEY (boot key) and the F (encryption key) values, and outputs `username:RID:LMHash:NTHash:::`.
- **Forensic Utility:** Provides the credentials of all local accounts. Investigators can then attempt to **crack weak passwords** with `hashcat` or verify against known compromised password lists.

**3. `windows.cachedump` (1.5 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Extracts **cached domain logon credentials** (MS-CACHE v2 hashes) for the last 10 (default) domain users.
- **Mechanism:** Reads the `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SECURITY\Cache` registry key in memory.
- **Forensic Utility:** Even after the domain controller is unreachable, the investigator can recover a partial verification of recently used domain credentials — a **goldmine** for offline credential-cracking in offline analysis labs.

**4. `windows.cmdscan` and `windows.consoles` (1 mark combined)**
- **Purpose:** Recovers command-line history from `conhost.exe` memory buffers.
- **Mechanism:** Scans process memory for `ConsoleHost` and `ConsoleHistoryCommandList` structures.
- **Forensic Utility:** Captures the **exact commands** the attacker typed at an interactive console, including:
  - `net user administrator /active:yes`
  - `psexec \\<target> cmd.exe`
  - `powershell -enc <base64>`
- This is **direct evidence of malicious intent** by the user.

#### (b) Windows vs Linux User Artifacts & Anti-Forensic Techniques (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Windows | Linux |
|---------|---------|-------|
| **User database** | SAM registry hive (`%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM`) | `/etc/passwd` and `/etc/shadow` |
| **Authentication subsystem** | LSASS (`lsass.exe`) | PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) |
| **Live session command** | `query user`, `qwinsta` | `who`, `w`, `users` |
| **Historical log** | `Security.evtx` (EID 4624 etc.) | `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp` |
| **Last logon** | `last` field in SAM, event log | `/var/log/lastlog` |
| **Memory tool** | Volatility `windows.*` | Volatility `linux.*` |
| **Hash format** | NTLM (MD4 of UTF-16LE) | `/etc/shadow` (crypt(3) variants) |
| **Failed logon tracking** | EID 4625 | `/var/log/btmp` or `/var/log/auth.log` |
| **Live process user** | `tasklist /v` or Sysinternals | `ps -eo user,pid,cmd` |
| **Privilege elevation log** | EID 4648 (`runas`), 4672 | `/var/log/auth.log` (`sudo` entries) |

**Three Anti-Forensic Techniques (3 marks):**

**1. Event Log Clearing (1 mark)**
- Windows: `wevtutil cl Security` (EID 1102 is generated; attackers use `wevtutil cl Security && wevtutil set-log Security /e:false` to also stop future logging).
- Linux: `echo > /var/log/wtmp` (truncates the binary log) or `cat /dev/null > /var/log/btmp`.
- **Counter-forensic response:** Memory forensics with `windows.eventlog` or `linux.bash_history` to recover cleared entries; integrity monitoring with Wazuh/Splunk.

**2. Timestomping (1 mark)**
- **Windows:** `SetFileTime` API or `timestomp.exe` (Metasploit) to modify `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` MFT entries.
- **Linux:** `touch -t 200001010000 file` or `libfaketime` LD_PRELOAD library.
- **Forensic response:** Compare `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (mutable) with `$FILE_NAME` (immutable MFT entry) to detect anomalies.

**3. Log Injection and User Impersonation (1 mark)**
- Attackers can **create a user account matching a legitimate username** with a trailing space or a homoglyph (e.g., `admin` vs `admin ` vs `аdmin` Cyrillic 'а').
- **Forensic response:** Always compare the **SID** (Windows) or **UID** (Linux) rather than relying on the username string; use UTF-8 byte-level analysis to detect homoglyph attacks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Volatile with Persistent Artifacts** — `query user` shows only *current* sessions; it does NOT show historical sessions. Students who answer with only `query user` lose 2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Ignoring Memory Forensics** — In 2024 Scheme KTU exams, a question on "user identification" that omits Volatility or memory-resident token analysis is **incomplete**. Always mention at least one memory analysis technique.
> 
> 3. **Mismatching Logon Types with EID 4624** — Logon Types 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11 must be correctly matched to their definitions. Type 3 is **NOT** RDP; it is SMB/RPC. RDP is **Type 10**.
> 
> 4. **Not Mentioning Anti-Forensics** — A complete answer must address the possibility that logs have been cleared. A "wevtutil cl Security was executed" scenario is common in KTU case-study questions.
> 
> 5. **Omitting Cross-Reference** — Identification from a **single source** is weak. KTU answers must show **convergence** between at least two sources (e.g., Security Log + Registry + Memory).
> 
> 6. **Forgetting the SID/UID** — User *names* are mutable. Always reference the **Security Identifier (SID)** in Windows and the **User Identifier (UID)** in Linux for court-defensible attribution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — User Identification**

- [x] User identification is the **first forensic triage step** to establish *who had access* during the incident window.
- [x] **Triangulate** three evidence planes: **Volatile (RAM) → Persistent (Disk/Registry) → Behavioral (Logs)**.
- [x] Windows live commands: **`whoami /all`, `query user`, `qwinsta`, `net session`, `net use`, `wmic useraccount`**.
- [x] Windows Security **Event ID 4624 = successful logon**; remember the **10+ logon types** (Type 2 = interactive, Type 3 = network, Type 10 = RDP).
- [x] Windows Security **Event ID 4625 = failed logon** (brute-force indicator); **Event ID 1102 = log cleared** (anti-forensics indicator).
- [x] Linux live commands: **`who`, `w`, `last`, `lastb`, `lastlog`**.
- [x] Linux log files: **`/var/log/wtmp`** (success), **`/var/log/btmp`** (failure), **`/var/log/lastlog`** (per-user last logon).
- [x] **Volatility plugins**: `windows.sessions`, `windows.hashdump`, `windows.cachedump`, `windows.cmdscan`, `windows.consoles`.
- [x] **SAM registry hive** at `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM` contains the local user database; **UserAssist** in NTUSER.DAT tracks GUI execution history.
- [x] **UserAssist values are ROT13-encoded** — decode before analysis.
- [x] **SIDs are immutable**; usernames are not — always identify by SID in formal reports.
- [x] **Anti-forensic techniques**: event log clearing, timestomping, user homoglyph impersonation — investigators must have a response for each.
- [x] **Chain of custody**: SHA-256 hash every artifact image (RAM, disk, log file) immediately after acquisition.
- [x] **Legal framework**: Indian Evidence Act §65B (as amended by IT Act 2000) governs admissibility of electronic records.
- [x] **Standards**: NIST SP 800-86, ISO/IEC 27037 (Identification, Collection, Acquisition, Preservation of digital evidence).
- [x] **Goal**: Produce a *defensible, court-ready user attribution* that survives cross-examination.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the 10+ Windows Logon Types:**
> **"2 Interactive, 3 Network, 4 Batch, 5 Service, 7 Unlock, 8 Cleartext, 9 NewCreds, 10 RDP, 11 Cached"**
> Memorize as: **"I Need Badly Secure Unlocked Clear New RDP Cache"** (2-3-4-5-7-8-9-10-11).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspect Network Connections and Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspect Network Connections and Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Connection & Activity Inspection in Malware Forensics** is the systematic process of identifying, capturing, analyzing, and correlating network-level artifacts generated by malicious software during its lifecycle, including active socket connections, listening ports, DNS resolutions, packet payloads, and protocol-level handshakes, in order to establish **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)**, reconstruct the **Command-and-Control (C2) communication timeline**, and attribute malicious activity to specific processes or Threat Actor Groups (TAGs).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is anchored to **Course Outcome CO3** of PECCT741, requiring students to demonstrate competency in *applying live-system and network-level forensic tools to identify malicious network behavior*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Building Security Monitoring Room**

Imagine a corporate office building where every employee (a **process**) works. Each employee has a desk phone (**socket**) and a mobile phone (**port**). The building security team (**forensic analyst**) needs to:

1. **See who is talking to whom right now** → Active TCP/UDP connections
2. **Identify suspicious incoming visitors waiting at the reception** → Listening ports
3. **Track which employees are making overseas calls after midnight** → C2 beaconing
4. **Listen in on conversations to detect fraud** → Packet capture
5. **Check the visitor log to see who came in and out** → Network connection logs

Just as a security team monitors call records to find insider threats, a malware forensic investigator inspects network connections to find malicious processes communicating with attackers, exfiltrating data, or downloading second-stage payloads.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in Malware Forensics

| Network Artifact | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|
| **Active Sockets** | Reveals live malware-to-attacker communication |
| **Listening Ports** | Identifies backdoors, RATs, and rogue services |
| **DNS Queries** | Uncovers C2 domains, DGA patterns, DNS tunneling |
| **Packet Payload** | Decodes exfiltrated data and C2 commands |
| **Protocol Anomalies** | Detects beaconing, port scanning, lateral movement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Term:** The *Live Network Forensics* methodology relies on the **Order of Volatility (O-V)** principle, where network state (routing tables, ARP cache, socket tables) is more volatile than disk artifacts, demanding real-time inspection before evidence is lost.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Network Constants

The following **bold** values are KTU-highlighted constants frequently used in network forensic calculations:

- **Maximum TCP Port Number: 65,535** ($2^{16} - 1$)
- **Reserved / Well-Known Port Range: 0 – 1,023**
- **Registered Port Range: 1,024 – 49,151**
- **Dynamic / Ephemeral Port Range: 49,152 – 65,535**
- **Maximum Theoretical IPv4 Packet Size: 65,535 bytes**
- **Standard Ethernet MTU: 1,500 bytes**
- **Default Wireshark Capture Ring Buffer Recommendation: 100 MB per file**

---

## 1.5 The OSI Model Mapping in Network Forensics

Malware activity can be inspected at every layer of the **OSI 7-Layer Model**:

| OSI Layer | Protocol Examples | Forensic Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| 7 - Application | HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, FTP | Payload, URLs, headers |
| 6 - Presentation | TLS/SSL, MIME | Encrypted sessions (metadata only) |
| 5 - Session | NetBIOS, RPC | Session tokens, handles |
| 4 - Transport | **TCP, UDP** | Sockets, ports, flags (SYN, ACK, FIN) |
| 3 - Network | IP, ICMP | Source/Dest IP, TTL |
| 2 - Data Link | ARP, Ethernet | MAC addresses |
| 1 - Physical | Raw bits | Signal captures |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forensic Inspection Pipeline

The structured methodology for inspecting network connections and activity follows a **5-stage analytical pipeline**:

### Stage 1: Identification (Pre-Analysis)
- Determine the scope: live host, network segment, or pcap file
- Identify volatile data sources: socket tables, ARP cache, routing table
- Establish a forensic baseline for comparison

### Stage 2: Acquisition (Live Capture)
- Use packet sniffers (Wireshark, tcpdump) to capture traffic
- Preserve the **5-tuple** for every flow:
  * **Source IP, Destination IP, Source Port, Destination Port, Protocol**
- Maintain chain-of-custody for captured pcaps

### Stage 3: Connection Enumeration
- List active TCP/UDP connections
- Map each socket to its owning **Process ID (PID)**
- Identify listening ports and associated services

### Stage 4: Payload Analysis
- Reassemble TCP streams
- Decode application-layer protocols
- Extract URLs, domains, file hashes, and C2 commands

### Stage 5: Correlation & Attribution
- Cross-reference IoCs with threat intelligence (VirusTotal, AlienVault OTX)
- Build a **kill-chain timeline** (mapped to MITRE ATT&CK)
- Generate final forensic report

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Network Forensics

> [!NOTE]
> **Why inspect network activity?**
> Malware MUST communicate — either to receive commands, exfiltrate data, or propagate. This communication leaves a **network footprint** that, unlike disk artifacts, cannot be easily wiped by anti-forensics techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **How does inspection work?**
> The OS kernel maintains socket tables (e.g., `InetDiag` in Linux, `TcpE` table in Windows). Forensic tools query these tables via APIs such as **IP Helper API** (Windows) or `/proc/net/tcp` (Linux) to enumerate connections in real-time.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mathematically verifiable** and appear frequently in KTU ESE questions for PECCT741.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Description / Forensic Use |
|---|---|---|
| **5-Tuple Identification** | $F = (IP_{src}, IP_{dst}, P_{src}, P_{dst}, Proto)$ | Uniquely identifies a network flow |
| **Port Range Classification** | $P \in [0, 1023] \rightarrow \text{Well-Known}$ | Categorizes port usage |
| **Beaconing Interval** | $T_{b} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{i+1} - t_{i})}{n}$ | Average time between C2 callbacks (Jitter detection) |
| **Jitter Coefficient** | $J = \frac{\sigma_{T}}{\mu_{T}}$ | Standard deviation over mean — high J = DGA/random |
| **Data Exfiltration Rate** | $R_{exfil} = \frac{\sum \text{Bytes}_{out}}{t_{end} - t_{start}}$ | Quantifies stolen data per unit time |
| **TCP Flag Bitmask** | $F_{TCP} = \sum_{i} 2^{i} \cdot b_{i}$ | Encodes SYN(0x02), ACK(0x10), FIN(0x01), RST(0x04) |
| **Connection Duration** | $\Delta t = t_{FIN} - t_{SYN}$ | Long-lived = data exfil; short = scan |
| **Packet Size Anomaly** | $S_{avg} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i}$ | Tunneling detection (DNS packets $> 512$ bytes) |
| **Entropy of Payload** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_{i}) \log_{2} p(x_{i})$ | $H \approx 8.0$ = encrypted/compressed payload |
| **Throughput Efficiency** | $\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Payload}}{\text{Total Bytes}} \times 100\%$ | Low $\eta$ = covert channel indicator |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\|` (single vertical bar) inside markdown tables to prevent rendering errors. The vertical bar `\|` collides with markdown table delimiter syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Network connection inspection is foundational to:
- **Incident Response (IR)**: Detecting live breaches within minutes, not days
- **Threat Hunting**: Proactively searching for adversaries using YARA + Zeek
- **SOC Operations**: Driving SIEM alerts via Suricata/Snort rules
- **Malware Sandboxing**: Capturing C2 traffic in Cuckoo/CAPE Sandbox
- **Digital Forensics & Incident Response (DFIR)**: Building court-admissible network evidence
- **Threat Intelligence**: Mining IoCs from botnet traffic captures

---

## 2.5 Windows vs. Linux Network Forensics – Comparative Matrix

| Aspect | Windows | Linux |
|---|---|---|
| **Socket Table Path** | `InetDiag` (kernel) | `/proc/net/tcp`, `/proc/net/udp` |
| **Command-line Tool** | `netstat -ano`, `Get-NetTCPConnection` | `ss -tunap`, `netstat -tunap` |
| **Process Mapping** | PID via Task Manager / `tasklist` | PID + executable via `/proc/<pid>/fd` |
| **Packet Capture Lib** | Npcap (WinPcap successor) | libpcap |
| **Default Capture Tool** | Wireshark + Npcap | Wireshark + tcpdump |
| **DNS Cache** | `ipconfig /displaydns` | `systemd-resolve --statistics` |
| **ARP Cache** | `arp -a` | `arp -an` or `ip neigh` |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Beaconing Interval & Jitter Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A forensic investigator captures 6 outbound HTTPS connections from a compromised host to the suspected C2 server `203.0.113.45` over 60 minutes. The timestamps (in seconds from $t=0$) of consecutive beacons are: $t_1 = 0$, $t_2 = 600$, $t_3 = 1215$, $t_4 = 1800$, $t_5 = 2410$, $t_6 = 3000$.

### Step 1: Compute the Inter-Arrival Times ($\Delta t_i$)

Each $\Delta t_i$ is the time difference between two consecutive beacon events.

$$\Delta t_1 = t_2 - t_1 = 600 - 0 = 600 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\Delta t_2 = t_3 - t_2 = 1215 - 600 = 615 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\Delta t_3 = t_4 - t_3 = 1800 - 1215 = 585 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\Delta t_4 = t_5 - t_4 = 2410 - 1800 = 610 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\Delta t_5 = t_6 - t_5 = 3000 - 2410 = 590 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conversion logic:** We subtract consecutive timestamps to find the gap between each pair of beacons. This gap is the "inter-arrival time" of the C2 heartbeat.

### Step 2: Compute the Mean Beaconing Interval ($\mu_T$)

The mean of the inter-arrival times is the sum divided by the count $n = 5$.

$$\mu_T = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i}{n}$$

$$\mu_T = \frac{600 + 615 + 585 + 610 + 590}{5}$$

$$\mu_T = \frac{3000}{5} = 600 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conversion logic:** Summing all five inter-arrival times telescopes to $t_6 - t_1 = 3000 - 0 = 3000$ seconds. Dividing by the number of intervals (5) yields the average heartbeat period of 10 minutes.

### Step 3: Compute the Standard Deviation ($\sigma_T$)

First, compute the squared deviations from the mean.

$$(\Delta t_1 - \mu_T)^2 = (600 - 600)^2 = 0$$

$$(\Delta t_2 - \mu_T)^2 = (615 - 600)^2 = 225$$

$$(\Delta t_3 - \mu_T)^2 = (585 - 600)^2 = 225$$

$$(\Delta t_4 - \mu_T)^2 = (610 - 600)^2 = 100$$

$$(\Delta t_5 - \mu_T)^2 = (590 - 600)^2 = 100$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\Delta t_i - \mu_T)^2 = 0 + 225 + 225 + 100 + 100 = 650$$

Standard deviation (sample, with $n-1$ in denominator for unbiased estimate):

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (\Delta t_i - \mu_T)^2}{n - 1}}$$

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{\frac{650}{4}} = \sqrt{162.5} \approx 12.748 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conversion logic:** We square each deviation to remove sign, sum them, and divide by $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) for an unbiased sample estimator. The square root brings the unit back to seconds.

### Step 4: Compute the Jitter Coefficient ($J$)

$$J = \frac{\sigma_T}{\mu_T} = \frac{12.748}{600} \approx 0.02125 \text{ or } 2.125\%$$

**Conversion logic:** The jitter coefficient is the **coefficient of variation** — a dimensionless ratio. A value of $\approx 2\%$ is **low jitter**, consistent with **regular beaconing malware** (e.g., a RAT phoning home every 10 minutes). High jitter ($J > 0.3$) would suggest **Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA)** or randomized timing.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Payload Entropy (Encrypted Exfiltration Detection)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A 1 KB block of network payload is captured from an exfiltration channel. The bytes are grouped into 4 distinct values: byte `0x41` appears 400 times, `0x42` appears 350 times, `0x43` appears 200 times, `0x44` appears 50 times.

### Step 1: Compute Probabilities

$$p(0x41) = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.40$$

$$p(0x42) = \frac{350}{1000} = 0.35$$

$$p(0x43) = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.20$$

$$p(0x44) = \frac{50}{1000} = 0.05$$

### Step 2: Apply Shannon's Entropy Formula

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

$$H(X) = -[0.40 \log_2(0.40) + 0.35 \log_2(0.35) + 0.20 \log_2(0.20) + 0.05 \log_2(0.05)]$$

Compute each term:

$$-0.40 \log_2(0.40) = -0.40 \times (-1.3219) = 0.5288$$

$$-0.35 \log_2(0.35) = -0.35 \times (-1.5146) = 0.5301$$

$$-0.20 \log_2(0.20) = -0.20 \times (-2.3219) = 0.4644$$

$$-0.05 \log_2(0.05) = -0.05 \times (-4.3219) = 0.2161$$

$$H(X) = 0.5288 + 0.5301 + 0.4644 + 0.2161 = 1.7394 \text{ bits/byte}$$

**Conversion logic:** Since the maximum possible entropy for a byte is $\log_2(256) = 8.0$ bits/byte, our result of $1.74$ indicates a **low-entropy, structured (likely plaintext or repetitive) payload** — *not* encrypted. If $H(X) > 7.0$, we would suspect encryption or compression.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Network Forensic Tools Cheat-Sheet

| Tool | Platform | Primary Use | KTU-Relevant Command |
|---|---|---|---|
| **netstat** | Win/Linux | Live socket enumeration | `netstat -ano` (Windows), `netstat -tunap` (Linux) |
| **ss** | Linux | Modern replacement for netstat | `ss -tunap` |
| **Wireshark** | Cross-platform | GUI packet analysis | Display filters: `http.request.uri contains "evil"` |
| **tcpdump** | Linux/Unix | CLI packet capture | `tcpdump -i eth0 -w capture.pcap` |
| **TCPView** | Windows | GUI live connection monitor | Sysinternals tool |
| **Process Monitor** | Windows | File/Registry/Network syscalls | Filter on "Network" operation |
| **Nmap** | Cross-platform | Active scanning | `nmap -sS -p- target` |
| **Suricata / Snort** | Cross-platform | IDS / NIDS | Rule-based detection |
| **Zeek (Bro)** | Cross-platform | Network Security Monitor | Generates `conn.log`, `http.log`, `dns.log` |
| **NetworkMiner** | Cross-platform | Forensic pcap analysis | Extracts files, images, credentials |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Live Network Forensic Inspection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compromised Host] --> B[Kernel Socket Table]
    A --> C[Network Interface Card]
    B --> D[Socket Enumeration Tool: netstat or ss]
    C --> E[Packet Capture: tcpdump or Wireshark]
    D --> F[Process-to-Socket Mapping: PID to Executable]
    E --> G[Protocol Decoder: TCP, UDP, DNS, HTTP]
    F --> H[Indicator Correlation Engine]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Threat Intelligence Lookup: VirusTotal, AlienVault]
    H --> J[MITRE ATT&CK Mapping]
    I --> K[IoC Database: IPs, Domains, Hashes]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Forensic Report and Timeline]
    L --> M[Incident Response Action: Containment and Eradication]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline begins with two parallel data streams from the compromised host — the **kernel socket table** (state information) and the **NIC traffic** (raw packets). Each stream is processed by specialized tools, correlated in the **IoC engine**, enriched with threat intel and ATT&CK mapping, and finally produces the forensic report and IR actions.

---

## 4.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Forensic Interpretation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Malware-Infected Client
    participant Server as C2 Server
    Client->>Server: SYN (seq=x)
    Note over Client,Server: Packet 1: TCP Flag = 0x02
    Server->>Client: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
    Note over Client,Server: Packet 2: TCP Flags = 0x12
    Client->>Server: ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)
    Note over Client,Server: Packet 3: TCP Flags = 0x10
    Note over Client,Server: Connection ESTABLISHED
    Server->>Client: Encrypted C2 Payload
    Client->>Server: Exfiltrated Data
    Server->>Client: FIN (Connection Tear-down)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Insight:** A **half-open connection** (SYN sent, SYN-ACK received, but no final ACK) is a signature of **SYN scan reconnaissance** — a common pre-attack technique detected by NIDS.

---

## 4.3 Network Forensics Decision Tree: Identifying Malicious Connections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Network Connection] --> B{Is the destination IP internal or external?}
    B -- Internal --> C{Check for SMB, RDP, WMI, or PsExec traffic}
    C -- Yes --> D[Possible Lateral Movement]
    B -- External --> E{Is the destination IP on a threat intel blacklist?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Confirmed Malicious IoC]
    E -- No --> G{Is the port non-standard or high?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Suspicious: Possible C2 on non-standard port]
    G -- No --> I{Is the domain recently registered or DGAlike?}
    I -- Yes --> J[High Probability: C2 or DGA]
    I -- No --> K{Is there regular beaconing behavior?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Likely C2 Heartbeat]
    K -- No --> M[Bulk Data Transfer Without DNS]
    M -- Yes --> N[Data Exfiltration Indicator]
```

---

## 4.4 Mapping Network Artifacts to MITRE ATT&CK

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Artifact] --> B[MITRE Technique ID]
    A1[Unusual Outbound DNS] --> B1[T1071.004: Application Layer Protocol: DNS]
    A2[Regular HTTPS Beacons] --> B2[T1071.001: Application Layer Protocol: Web]
    A3[Internal SMB Enumeration] --> B3[T1021.002: Remote Services: SMB]
    A4[ARP Spoofing] --> B4[T1557: Adversary-in-the-Middle]
    A5[FTP Data Transfer] --> B5[T1048: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol]
    A6[ICMP Tunneling] --> B6[T1095: Non-Application Layer Protocol]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Indicator of Compromise (IoC)" in the context of network forensics. List any four network-level IoCs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** is a forensic artifact or observable evidence that indicates a system or network has been breached or is currently under attack. Network-level IoCs are forensic traces observable in network traffic.

**Four network-level IoCs (2 marks):**
1. **Suspicious IP addresses** — connections to known malicious or TOR exit nodes
2. **Unusual DNS queries** — DGA-style random domain names
3. **Beaconing patterns** — regular periodic outbound connections
4. **Unusual ports/protocols** — services on non-standard ports (e.g., HTTP on port 8443)
5. **Abnormal payload sizes** — DNS packets > 512 bytes (tunneling indicator)
6. **Anomalous TLS certificates** — self-signed or recently issued certs to C2 domains

*(Definition: 1 mark | Four IoCs: 2 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the purpose of the `netstat -ano` command in Windows network forensics. What does each flag signify?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `netstat -ano` command is used to display **all active TCP/UDP connections and listening ports** on a Windows host with associated **Process IDs (PIDs)** — a critical step in mapping network connections to their owning processes during malware investigation.

**Flag meanings (2 marks):**
- `-a` → Displays **all** connections and listening ports
- `-n` → Shows addresses and port numbers in **numeric** form (no DNS resolution)
- `-o` → Displays the **owning Process ID (PID)** for each connection

This command enables the forensic analyst to **map a suspicious IP:port to a specific malicious executable** (e.g., `svchost.exe` with PID 4292 connecting to `185.x.x.x:443`).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe the **TCP three-way handshake** with a neat diagram. Explain how each flag in the TCP header is used for forensic analysis of network traffic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forensic analyst captures network traffic from a compromised host and observes 5 outgoing HTTPS connections to IP `198.51.100.77` at the following timestamps (in minutes): $t_1=0$, $t_2=15$, $t_3=31$, $t_4=45$, $t_5=60$. Calculate the **mean beaconing interval** and the **jitter coefficient**. Comment on whether the traffic is regular beaconing or randomized (DGA-like). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) TCP Three-Way Handshake & Flag Forensics (7 marks):**

**Handshake Steps (4 marks):**
1. **SYN** — Client sends SYN (seq=x) to server. Flag = `0x02`. Initiates connection.
2. **SYN-ACK** — Server responds with SYN (seq=y) and ACK (ack=x+1). Flags = `0x12`.
3. **ACK** — Client acknowledges (ack=y+1). Flag = `0x10`. Connection is now **ESTABLISHED**.

**Forensic Significance of TCP Flags (3 marks):**
- **SYN only (0x02)** without ACK = **SYN scan** (reconnaissance, Nmap `-sS`)
- **SYN+FIN or X-MAS (FIN+PSH+URG)** = **covert scan** to bypass firewalls
- **RST (0x04)** = aborted connection; mass RSTs indicate **port scan or DoS**
- **FIN (0x01)** only = stealth scan or graceful close
- **ACK only (0x10)** = stateless traversal / ACK scan
- **No flags (NULL scan)** = reconnaissance probe

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse SYN-ACK (`0x12`) with two separate packets. Remember: SYN-ACK is a **single packet** with both flags set. The bitmask is `0x02 + 0x10 = 0x12`. Losing 1 mark here is common.

---

**(b) Beaconing Interval & Jitter Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Inter-arrival times (2 marks)**

$$\Delta t_1 = 15 - 0 = 15 \text{ min}$$

$$\Delta t_2 = 31 - 15 = 16 \text{ min}$$

$$\Delta t_3 = 45 - 31 = 14 \text{ min}$$

$$\Delta t_4 = 60 - 45 = 15 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2: Mean Interval (2 marks)**

$$\mu_T = \frac{15 + 16 + 14 + 15}{4} = \frac{60}{4} = 15 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Standard Deviation (2 marks)**

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{\frac{(0)^2 + (1)^2 + (-1)^2 + (0)^2}{4-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \approx 0.816 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4: Jitter Coefficient (1 mark)**

$$J = \frac{\sigma_T}{\mu_T} = \frac{0.816}{15} \approx 0.054 \text{ or } 5.4\%$$

**Conclusion (extra, conceptual):** Since $J \approx 5.4\%$ is **very low**, this is consistent with **regular, predictable beaconing** (e.g., a RAT phoning home every 15 minutes). A DGA-based malware would show $J > 30\%$.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** List and explain any **five Windows command-line utilities** used for inspecting network connections and activity during malware forensics. For each tool, give a sample command. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **DNS tunneling** as a network forensic threat. Describe how an analyst can detect DNS tunneling by inspecting:
  (i) Packet size anomalies
  (ii) Query name entropy
  (iii) Frequency of unique subdomains
  **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Five Windows Network Forensics Utilities (7 marks — 1.4 each):**

1. **`netstat -ano`** — Lists all active connections with owning PIDs. Used to map suspicious IPs to malware processes.
2. **`netstat -b`** — Shows the **executable name** involved in creating each connection (requires admin). Forensic gold for identifying trojanized binaries.
3. **`Get-NetTCPConnection` (PowerShell)** — Modern cmdlet providing structured output. Filter by state: `Get-NetTCPConnection -State Established`.
4. **`arp -a`** — Displays the **ARP cache**, useful for detecting **ARP poisoning / MITM attacks**.
5. **`route print`** — Reveals the **routing table**; unauthorized default gateways indicate **rogue router / network hijacking**.
6. **`nslookup` / `Resolve-DnsName`** — Manually query DNS to investigate **suspicious domain resolutions**.
7. **`netsh trace start capture=yes`** — Built-in **Windows packet capture** for live network forensics.

**Sample Commands (sample, any 1.4 marks):**
```
netstat -ano | findstr ESTABLISHED
Get-NetTCPConnection -RemotePort 443
arp -a
```

---

**(b) DNS Tunneling — Detection Methodology (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** **DNS tunneling** is a covert channel technique where malware encodes data (often exfiltrated files or C2 commands) inside DNS query/response packets, typically by encoding payloads in **subdomain labels** of a malicious domain controlled by the attacker (e.g., `aGVsbG8gd29ybGQ.tunnel.evil.com`).

**Detection Methods (5 marks):**

**(i) Packet Size Anomalies (1.5 marks):**
Standard DNS queries are < 100 bytes. Tunneled DNS packets are **anomalously large** (often exceeding 512 bytes for UDP, triggering TCP fallback). Threshold: **packet length > 200 bytes** is suspicious.

**(ii) Query Name Entropy (1.5 marks):**
Legitimate domains have **low entropy** ($H < 3.5$ bits/char — e.g., `google.com`). DNS tunnel queries use **high-entropy encoded labels** ($H > 4.0$ bits/char) because payloads are base32/base64 encoded. Apply Shannon entropy:

$$H = -\sum_{i} p(c_i) \log_2 p(c_i)$$

**(iii) Frequency of Unique Subdomains (2 marks):**
A single host making **hundreds of unique subdomain queries per minute** to one parent domain is a strong DNS tunneling indicator. Legitimate clients query **a small, stable set** of domains. The detection rule:

$$\text{Alert if} \quad \frac{\vert \text{Unique subdomains in 60s} \vert}{\text{Total queries in 60s}} > 0.8$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **DNS tunneling** (data in queries) with **DNS hijacking** (modifying resolver settings) and **DNS cache poisoning** (injecting false records). The question specifically asks about **tunneling** — ensure your answer focuses on **payload encoding in subdomain labels**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- **Core Definition:** Network connection inspection is identifying live sockets, packets, and protocol behavior to establish IoCs.
- **5-Tuple:** $F = (IP_{src}, IP_{dst}, P_{src}, P_{dst}, Proto)$ — unique flow identifier.
- **Port Ranges:** Well-known (0–1023), Registered (1024–49151), Dynamic (49152–65535).
- **Key Tools:** `netstat`, `ss`, `Wireshark`, `tcpdump`, `TCPView`, `Process Monitor`, `Suricata`, `Zeek`.
- **Windows command:** `netstat -ano` (the `-o` flag is the **forensic differentiator**).
- **TCP Flags:** SYN=`0x02`, ACK=`0x10`, FIN=`0x01`, RST=`0x04`, PSH=`0x08`, URG=`0x20`.
- **SYN scan** = reconnaissance signature.
- **Jitter coefficient** $J = \sigma_T / \mu_T$:
  * $J < 0.1$ → regular beaconing
  * $J > 0.3$ → randomized / DGA-like
- **Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$:
  * $H > 7.0$ → encrypted/compressed payload
  * $H < 3.0$ → plaintext or repetitive
- **DNS Tunneling Indicators:** Large packets, high-entropy subdomains, high unique-subdomain ratio.
- **Beaconing Interval:** $T_b = \frac{1}{n}\sum \Delta t_i$ — use this in ESE calculations.
- **Exfiltration Rate:** $R_{exfil} = \frac{\text{Total Outbound Bytes}}{\Delta t}$.
- **MITRE ATT&CK Network Techniques:** T1071 (App Layer Protocol), T1048 (Exfil), T1095 (Non-App Layer), T1557 (AiTM).
- **Order of Volatility:** Network state > Process memory > File system > Disk.
- **5-Stage Pipeline:** Identification → Acquisition → Enumeration → Payload Analysis → Correlation.
- **Common Examiner Trap:** Forgetting to mention **process-to-socket mapping** (PID + executable name) when discussing `netstat`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current and Recent Network Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Current and Recent Network Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Malware Forensics**, **Current and Recent Network Connections** refer to the live and historical state of network sockets, sessions, and endpoints established between a host (the machine under investigation) and remote entities across a network. Forensically, this involves the identification, documentation, and interpretation of **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** and **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** connections, listening ports, half-open sockets, and associated process identifiers (PIDs) that reveal communication patterns indicative of malware activity, command-and-control (C2) callbacks, lateral movement, or data exfiltration.

The discipline encompasses three temporal states:

- **Current Connections**: Active sockets currently in `ESTABLISHED`, `LISTENING`, `TIME_WAIT`, `CLOSE_WAIT`, or `SYN_SENT` states captured at the moment of triage.
- **Recent Connections**: Persisted artifacts such as the Windows Registry (`SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\PersistentRoutes`, ARP cache), firewall logs, DNS resolver cache, and Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) traces that disclose network behavior that occurred before the live snapshot.
- **Historical/Forensic Network Artifacts**: Files like `netstat.log`, packet captures (PCAPs), NetFlow records, and SRUM (System Resource Usage Monitor) databases that provide a retrospective view.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor**: The examination typically expects students to correlate **process-to-socket** mappings with **indicators of compromise (IoCs)** such as unknown remote IPs, non-standard ports, beaconing intervals, and backdoor listeners.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Telephone Exchange

Imagine a large hotel where every guest room is a **process** (a running program) and the hotel's telephone exchange (PBX) is the **operating system's networking subsystem**. Each telephone line is a **socket**.

- **Active Call (ESTABLISHED)**: A guest is currently talking to someone in another city. The receptionist can tell you *which room* (PID) is on a call, *which city* (remote IP) they are calling, and *how long* the call has lasted.
- **Ringing (LISTENING)**: A guest has the phone off the hook, ready to receive calls. This is a **server socket** waiting for inbound connections.
- **Missed Call Log (TIME_WAIT / CLOSE_WAIT)**: After a guest hangs up, the receptionist notes the call briefly before shredding the record. A forensic investigator arriving later can still inspect this log to see who called whom, even after the line is idle.
- **Suspicious Behavior**: If a guest in Room 401 (say, `svchost.exe`) is calling an unknown number in a foreign country at 3 AM every 10 minutes (a *beaconing pattern*), the investigator flags it as a potential **C2 callback**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Principle**: Network connection state is **volatile evidence**. A live machine loses this data on reboot. Therefore, capturing it before powering down the suspect machine is a critical first step in the order of volatility (RFC 3227).

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standard Notations

| Metric | Standard Notation | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Local IP Address** | `LADDR` (32-bit / 128-bit) | Identifies the host's interface in use |
| **Local Port** | `LPORT` (16-bit, 0–65535) | Well-known ports < 1024; ephemeral 1024–65535 |
| **Remote IP Address** | `RADDR` | Geolocation & reputation analysis |
| **Remote Port** | `RPORT` | Service identification (e.g., 443 = HTTPS) |
| **Protocol** | `PROTO` (`TCP` / `UDP`) | Connection-oriented vs. connectionless |
| **Process ID** | `PID` (integer) | Maps socket → executable |
| **State** | `STATE` | 11 TCP states per RFC 793 |
| **Packet Count** | `PKTS` | Volume of traffic (small = beaconing) |
| **Bytes Transferred** | `BYTES` | Exfiltration volume indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant**: The **IANA registered port range** is **0–1023** (privileged), **registered ports** are **1024–49151**, and **dynamic/ephemeral ports** are **49152–65535** (Linux) or **1024–65535** (Windows by default). Malware frequently uses high ports to avoid detection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TCP State Machine — Theoretical Foundation

The TCP protocol defines a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** with 11 distinct states, originally codified in **RFC 793 (Transmission Control Protocol, 1981)** and refined in **RFC 9293 (2022)**. For malware forensics, only a subset of these states is operationally relevant because they represent the *current* posture of a socket.

### 2.1.1 The 11 TCP States

| State | Symbolic Meaning | Forensic Observation |
|---|---|---|
| `LISTEN` | Server waiting for SYN | A **backdoor** or rogue service is open |
| `SYN_SENT` | Client sent SYN, awaiting SYN-ACK | Possible **scanning** or C2 handshake |
| `SYN_RECEIVED` | Server received SYN, sent SYN-ACK | Half-opened server-side state |
| `ESTABLISHED` | Full 3-way handshake complete | **Active data transfer** — exfil or C2 |
| `FIN_WAIT_1` | First FIN sent, awaiting ACK | Graceful close initiated locally |
| `FIN_WAIT_2` | ACK received, awaiting remote FIN | Half-closed connection |
| `CLOSE_WAIT` | Remote FIN received, awaiting local close | Peer hung up; local app didn't close |
| `CLOSING` | Simultaneous close attempt | Rare in legitimate traffic |
| `LAST_ACK` | FIN sent after CLOSE_WAIT | Final acknowledgment pending |
| `TIME_WAIT` | 2×MSL timer (typically 60s) | Residual socket awaiting cleanup |
| `CLOSED` | No connection state | Socket released |

### 2.1.2 The 3-Way Handshake — Why It Matters in Forensics

The TCP connection establishment follows a three-step sequence essential to understand connection legitimacy:

$$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{SYN, seq=x} \text{Server} $$

$$ \text{Server} \xrightarrow{SYN+ACK, seq=y, ack=x+1} \text{Client} $$

$$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{ACK, seq=x+1, ack=y+1} \text{Server} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Implication**: A `SYN_SENT` state without a corresponding `SYN_RECEIVED` from the remote host is a hallmark of **firewalled or blackholed** outbound connections — a common pattern in malware that cannot reach its C2 server.

## 2.2 The Windows TCP/IP Architecture

Windows networking is managed by the **TCP/IP driver stack** and exposed through several forensic interfaces:

1. **AFD.SYS** (Ancillary Function Driver) — Winsock kernel-mode helper
2. **TCPIP.SYS** — Main protocol driver
3. **DNSAPI.DLL** — Resolver cache storage
4. **NetBT.SYS** — NetBIOS over TCP/IP
5. **FWDRV.SYS** — Windows Filtering Platform (WFP)

The `netstat.exe` utility queries the **TCP/IP driver** via IOCTL calls (`\Device\Tcp`, `\Device\Udp`) and produces a snapshot of the socket table held in kernel memory.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Range | Forensic Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Port Numbering | $P \in [0, 65535]$ | 16-bit unsigned | Distinguishing well-known vs. ephemeral |
| TCP 5-Tuple (Unique Flow ID) | $(Proto, SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort)$ | 5 fields | Pivoting in PCAPs & SIEM |
| Time-Wait Duration | $T_{TW} = 2 \times MSL$ | seconds (60–240s) | Estimating socket cleanup timeline |
| Beaconing Interval (Avg) | $\bar{T}_b = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i$ | seconds | Detecting C2 regular callbacks |
| Exfil Throughput | $R_{exfil} = \frac{\sum BYTES_i}{T_{window}}$ | bytes/sec | Quantifying data leakage |
| Shannon Entropy (Payload) | $H = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/byte | Detecting encrypted exfil (H ≈ 7.5–8.0) |
| Connection Age | $A_c = T_{now} - T_{create}$ | seconds | Spotting long-lived C2 channels |
| Foreign Address Geodistance | $D = \arccos(\sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos\Delta\lambda)$ | radians/km | Anomaly if far from typical user base |
| Host Count Threshold (Beaconing) | $C_{hosts} \geq 3$ in $T < 60s$ | count | Possible **P2P malware** or worm |
| Ephemeral Port Range (Win) | $[49152, 65535]$ | 16384 ports | Tracking outbound source ports |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: In incident response, the **5-tuple** is the atomic unit of network identity. Tools like **Wireshark**, **NetworkMiner**, and **Volatility's `netscan`** plugin output exactly this 5-tuple for correlation.

## 2.4 Forensic Mapping: Process → Connection

A critical forensic skill is correlating a **PID** to an **executable** and then to a **socket**. The mapping chain is:

$$
\text{Disk Image} \rightarrow \text{Process List} \rightarrow \text{PID} \rightarrow \text{Socket Table} \rightarrow \text{Remote IP/Port}
$$

On Windows, this is achieved via:
- **`netstat -ano`** → outputs PID
- **`tasklist /svc`** → maps PID to service
- **`Get-NetTCPConnection -PID <n>`** (PowerShell) → structured output

On Linux:
- **`ss -tunap`** → modern replacement for netstat
- **`lsof -i -P -n`** → list open files including sockets
- **`/proc/<PID>/net/tcp`** → raw socket table

## 2.5 Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) in Network Connections

| IoC Pattern | Forensic Interpretation |
|---|---|
| `svchost.exe` on a **non-standard port** (e.g., 8443) | Likely **process injection** or hollowing |
| Outbound connection to **RFC1918** address from a public host | Possible **tunneling** or **lateral pivot** |
| `ESTABLISHED` connection from a process with **no parent** | Orphaned process = likely malware |
| Connection using **port 53 (DNS)** with high entropy | **DNS tunneling** (data exfil over DNS) |
| Repeated connections to **same IP** at exact intervals | **Beaconing** — automated C2 |
| `LISTEN` on port **4444** (Metasploit default) | Active **reverse shell handler** |
| Connection to **Tor exit nodes** | Anonymized C2 communication |
| ICMP traffic from a user-mode process | Possible **ICMP backdoor** (e.g., Ping Replay) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Beaconing Detection via Inter-Arrival Time Analysis

A common forensic task is to determine whether a sequence of network connections represents **automated malware beaconing** versus **legitimate user traffic**. We derive the statistical test.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Suppose we observe $n$ connection timestamps $t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_n$ to the same remote endpoint (RADDR, RPORT). Define the **inter-arrival times**:

$$
\Delta t_i = t_{i+1} - t_i, \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1
$$

### 3.1.2 Mean Inter-Arrival Time

The sample mean is computed as:

$$
\bar{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \Delta t_i
$$

### 3.1.3 Sample Standard Deviation

The sample standard deviation measures the **jitter** in the beaconing interval:

$$
\sigma_{\Delta t} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-2} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left(\Delta t_i - \bar{\Delta t}\right)^2}
$$

### 3.1.4 Coefficient of Variation (CV) — The Beaconing Metric

The **Coefficient of Variation** normalizes the standard deviation by the mean, allowing comparison across different time scales:

$$
CV = \frac{\sigma_{\Delta t}}{\bar{\Delta t}}
$$

**Interpretation Rule:**

$$
\begin{cases}
CV < 0.15 & \Rightarrow \text{Likely AUTOMATED beaconing (malware)} \\
0.15 \leq CV \leq 0.40 & \Rightarrow \text{Inconclusive — possible hybrid behavior} \\
CV > 0.40 & \Rightarrow \text{Likely HUMAN / LEGITIMATE traffic}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Example

Given timestamps (in seconds) to a suspicious IP: `0, 60, 121, 180, 241, 300`.

**Step 1**: Compute inter-arrival times:

$$
\Delta t_1 = 60, \quad \Delta t_2 = 61, \quad \Delta t_3 = 59, \quad \Delta t_4 = 61, \quad \Delta t_5 = 59
$$

**Step 2**: Compute the mean:

$$
\bar{\Delta t} = \frac{60 + 61 + 59 + 61 + 59}{5} = \frac{300}{5} = 60 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute deviations:

$$
(\Delta t_i - \bar{\Delta t}) = [0, +1, -1, +1, -1]
$$

**Step 4**: Compute sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (\Delta t_i - \bar{\Delta t})^2 = 0^2 + 1^2 + (-1)^2 + 1^2 + (-1)^2 = 4
$$

**Step 5**: Compute standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_{\Delta t} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{5-1}} = \sqrt{1.0} = 1.0 \text{ second}
$$

**Step 6**: Compute CV:

$$
CV = \frac{1.0}{60} \approx 0.0167
$$

**Step 7**: Interpretation:

$$
CV = 0.0167 < 0.15 \Rightarrow \textbf{STRONG BEACONING INDICATOR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Conclusion**: With $CV \approx 0.017$, this traffic is almost certainly an automated malware beacon contacting its C2 server every 60 seconds. The investigator should immediately pivot to the source PID and capture memory.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Entropy of Payload for Encrypted Exfil Detection

Encrypted exfiltration produces high-entropy network payloads. We derive the per-byte entropy.

### 3.2.1 Definition

For a byte stream of length $N$ with byte $b_i$ occurring $f_i$ times, the empirical probability is:

$$
p_i = \frac{f_i}{N}
$$

### 3.2.2 Shannon Entropy Formula

$$
H = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \log_2 p_i \quad \text{[bits per byte]}
$$

### 3.2.3 Theoretical Bounds

$$
0 \leq H \leq 8
$$

The maximum $H = 8$ occurs when all 256 byte values are equally likely (perfect randomness, e.g., AES output).

### 3.2.4 Classification

$$
\begin{cases}
H < 3.0 & \Rightarrow \text{Plaintext or structured data (HTML, JSON, logs)} \\
3.0 \leq H < 6.5 & \Rightarrow \text{Compressed data (ZIP, GZIP)} \\
H \geq 6.5 & \Rightarrow \text{Encrypted or random payload — possible exfil} \\
H \approx 8.0 & \Rightarrow \text{Strong cryptographic output (AES, ChaCha20)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.5 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a PCAP payload of 1000 bytes with the following frequency distribution:

| Byte | Count | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| `0x41` ('A') | 400 | 0.40 |
| `0x42` ('B') | 300 | 0.30 |
| `0x00` (NUL) | 200 | 0.20 |
| `0xFF` | 100 | 0.10 |

**Entropy Calculation:**

$$
H = -[0.40 \log_2(0.40) + 0.30 \log_2(0.30) + 0.20 \log_2(0.20) + 0.10 \log_2(0.10)]
$$

Computing each term:

$$
0.40 \log_2(0.40) \approx 0.40 \times (-1.3219) = -0.5288
$$

$$
0.30 \log_2(0.30) \approx 0.30 \times (-1.7370) = -0.5211
$$

$$
0.20 \log_2(0.20) \approx 0.20 \times (-2.3219) = -0.4644
$$

$$
0.10 \log_2(0.10) \approx 0.10 \times (-3.3219) = -0.3322
$$

Summing and negating:

$$
H = -(-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322) = 1.8465 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

**Classification**: $H \approx 1.85 < 3.0$ ⇒ **plaintext / low-diversity data** — not encrypted.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Forensic Tool Comparison

| Tool | Platform | Live Capture | Offline (Image) | PID Mapping | Output Format |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `netstat -anob` | Windows | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Text |
| `Get-NetTCPConnection` | Windows PS | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Object |
| `ss -tunap` | Linux | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Text |
| `lsof -i` | Unix/Linux | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Text |
| `Wireshark` | Cross | ✓ | ✓ (PCAP) | ✗ | GUI / PCAP |
| `TCPView` (Sysinternals) | Windows | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | GUI |
| `NetworkMiner` | Cross | ✗ | ✓ (PCAP) | ✓ | GUI / CSV |
| **Volatility `netscan`** | Memory | ✗ | ✓ (RAM) | ✓ | Text |
| **FTK Imager** | Cross | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Raw / E01 |
| `Nmap` | Cross | ✓ (active) | ✗ | ✗ | XML/Text |

## 3.4 The Order of Volatility (RFC 3227) — Application to Network State

In the RFC 3227 framework, evidence must be captured in decreasing order of volatility:

$$
\boxed{\text{Registers/Cache} \rightarrow \text{Routing Table} \rightarrow \text{Process List} \rightarrow \text{Sockets} \rightarrow \text{Temp FS} \rightarrow \text{Disk} \rightarrow \text{Off-line Logs}}
$$

**Sockets (network connections)** are at volatility tier **3** — they survive only as long as the OS is running. A `TIME_WAIT` entry, for example, disappears within 60–240 seconds. Hence, capturing them with `netstat -anob > netstat_live.txt` **before** any system modification is mandatory.

## 4.1 Network Connection Forensics — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the complete forensic workflow from live capture to evidentiary correlation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspect Host Powered ON] --> B[Volatile State Present]
    B --> C{Triage Decision}
    C -->|Live Acquisition| D[netstat -anob]
    C -->|Memory Dump| E[WinPMEM / LiME]
    C -->|Disk Image| F[FTK Imager / dd]
    D --> G[Parse 5-Tuple]
    E --> H[Volatility netscan plugin]
    F --> I[Mount Read-Only]
    G --> J[Process PID Lookup]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Extract Registry]
    J --> L[Correlate Process to Socket]
    K --> L
    L --> M{IoC Match?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Flag as Malicious Connection]
    M -->|No| O[Baseline Normal Traffic]
    N --> P[Geolocate Remote IP]
    N --> Q[Query Threat Intel]
    P --> R[Generate Forensic Report]
    Q --> R
    O --> R
    R --> S[Evidence Chain of Custody]
```

## 4.2 TCP State Transition Diagram (Forensic View)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN : bind + listen
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : connect
    LISTEN --> SYN_RECEIVED : rcv SYN
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RECEIVED : rcv SYN+ACK
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : rcv SYN+ACK + send ACK
    SYN_RECEIVED --> ESTABLISHED : rcv ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1 : close + send FIN
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : rcv FIN
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2 : rcv ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT : rcv FIN + send ACK
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : send FIN
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : rcv ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : 2xMSL timer expires
    SYN_SENT --> CLOSED : timeout no SYN-ACK
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PID 1234 svchost.exe] --> S1[Socket ABC Local:445]
    P1 --> S2[Socket DEF Local:135]
    P2[PID 5678 powershell.exe] --> S3[Socket GHI Local:49201]
    P3[PID 9999 unknown.exe] --> S4[Socket JKL Local:8080]

    S2 --> R2[Remote 192.168.1.10:3389]
    S3 --> R3[Remote 8.8.8.8:443]
    S4 --> R4[Remote 185.220.101.5:4444 SUSPICIOUS]
    R4 --> IOC[IoC Alert: Tor Exit Node]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Current Network Connections" in the context of malware forensics. List any three TCP states that are most relevant during incident response and explain why.**

**Model Answer:**

*Current Network Connections* refer to the live, in-memory state of all open, listening, and recently closed network sockets on a host at the moment of forensic triage. They represent the volatile communication endpoints used by processes to exchange data with local or remote entities.

Three most relevant TCP states in incident response:

1. **`ESTABLISHED`** — Indicates a fully open, bidirectional data channel. A malicious `ESTABLISHED` connection to an unknown remote IP strongly suggests **active C2 communication or data exfiltration**.

2. **`LISTENING`** — Represents a server-side socket bound to a local port, waiting for inbound connections. An unauthorized `LISTENING` port is a primary indicator of a **rogue service, backdoor, or trojan** running on the host.

3. **`SYN_SENT`** — Indicates the host has initiated a connection but has not received a SYN-ACK. High counts of `SYN_SENT` may indicate **port scanning** by malware or communication attempts to **firewalled/blocked C2 domains**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the TCP 5-tuple? Why is it considered the atomic unit of network identification in forensic investigations?**

**Model Answer:**

The **TCP 5-tuple** is the ordered combination of five parameters that uniquely identifies any unidirectional network flow:

$$
(\text{Protocol}, \text{Source IP}, \text{Source Port}, \text{Destination IP}, \text{Destination Port})
$$

It is considered the **atomic unit of network identification** because:

- It is **collision-free**: no two distinct flows share the same 5-tuple at a given moment.
- It is **vendor-neutral**: every IDS, SIEM, firewall, and packet analyzer (Wireshark, Zeek, Suricata) indexes traffic by the 5-tuple.
- It enables **pivoting**: an investigator can pivot from a single suspicious flow to all related flows, packets, and bytes in a PCAP.
- It supports **forensic correlation**: the same 5-tuple can be reconstructed from live memory (Volatility), from disk artifacts (registry, logs), and from network captures, allowing cross-source evidence corroboration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of network connection state management in the Windows OS. Describe how `netstat -anob` produces its output, and identify the four columns that map a process to a remote endpoint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forensic analyst captures the following timestamps (in seconds, from a common origin) of outbound connections from PID 4488 to IP `203.0.113.55`:

`0, 30, 61, 90, 121, 150, 181, 210, 240, 271, 300, 330, 361, 390, 421, 450, 481, 510, 541, 570, 601`

Determine whether this represents automated beaconing. Compute the mean inter-arrival time, standard deviation, and Coefficient of Variation (CV). Classify the traffic using the rule: `CV < 0.15` ⇒ beaconing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Windows Network Stack Architecture:**

The Windows TCP/IP stack is layered as follows (from user to kernel):

- **User Mode**: Applications call Winsock APIs (e.g., `socket()`, `connect()`).
- **Winsock DLL (`ws2_32.dll`)**: Translates API calls to kernel I/O requests.
- **AFD.SYS (Ancillary Function Driver)**: Winsock kernel helper.
- **TCPIP.SYS**: Core TCP/IP protocol implementation; maintains the **socket table** in kernel non-paged pool.
- **NDIS.SYS**: Network Driver Interface Specification — sends/receives frames via the NIC.

**Mechanism of `netstat -anob`:**

The utility invokes the `GetTcpTable2()` and `GetUdpTable()` Win32 APIs (or IOCTL `IOCTL_TCP_QUERY_INFORMATION_EX` on legacy paths) to read the kernel socket table.

**Column-by-Column Output Mapping:**

| Column | Source | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `Proto` | TCP/UDP enum from socket table | Protocol (TCP or UDP) |
| `Local Address` | `LADDR:LPORT` | Local socket endpoint |
| `Foreign Address` | `RADDR:RPORT` | Remote socket endpoint |
| `State` | TCP FSM state code | `LISTENING`, `ESTABLISHED`, etc. |
| `PID` | `GetOwnerPidFromSocket()` (TCPIP internal) | Process ID owning the socket |
| `[ProcessName]` (from `-b`) | `EnumProcesses` + `QueryFullProcessImageName` | Executable path |

**Key Forensic Insight**: The `-o` switch adds PID; the `-b` switch resolves PID to executable but requires administrator privileges.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1**: List the inter-arrival times (n − 1 = 20 intervals):

$$
\Delta t = [30, 31, 29, 31, 29, 31, 29, 30, 31, 29, 30, 31, 31, 31, 29, 31, 29, 31, 31, 31]
$$

**Step 2**: Compute the mean:

$$
\bar{\Delta t} = \frac{\sum \Delta t_i}{20} = \frac{30+31+29+31+29+31+29+30+31+29+30+31+31+31+29+31+29+31+31+31}{20}
$$

$$
\bar{\Delta t} = \frac{605}{20} = 30.25 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute deviations from the mean:

$$
\Delta t_i - \bar{\Delta t} = [-0.25, +0.75, -1.25, +0.75, -1.25, +0.75, -1.25, -0.25, +0.75, -1.25, -0.25, +0.75, +0.75, +0.75, -1.25, +0.75, -1.25, +0.75, +0.75, +0.75]
$$

**Step 4**: Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (\Delta t_i - \bar{\Delta t})^2 = 0.0625 + 0.5625 + 1.5625 + 0.5625 + 1.5625 + 0.5625 + 1.5625 + 0.0625 + 0.5625 + 1.5625 + 0.0625 + 0.5625 + 0.5625 + 0.5625 + 1.5625 + 0.5625 + 1.5625 + 0.5625 + 0.5625 + 0.5625
$$

$$
\sum (\Delta t_i - \bar{\Delta t})^2 = 17.75
$$

**Step 5**: Standard deviation:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{17.75}{19}} = \sqrt{0.9342} \approx 0.9665 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6**: Coefficient of Variation:

$$
CV = \frac{0.9665}{30.25} \approx 0.0319
$$

**Step 7**: Classification:

$$
CV = 0.0319 < 0.15 \Rightarrow \textbf{STRONG AUTOMATED BEACING — MALWARE INDICATED}
$$

**Forensic Conclusion**: The connection pattern from PID 4488 to `203.0.113.55` exhibits highly regular ~30-second intervals with negligible jitter (CV ≈ 3.2%). This is consistent with **C2 beaconing malware**. The analyst should immediately dump the process memory, identify the executable behind PID 4488, and pivot to the C2 IP for threat intelligence correlation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Students often forget the **n − 2** divisor when computing sample standard deviation from $n-1$ intervals, or they confuse it with population SD ($n-1$). Using the correct **Bessel's correction** for sample SD with $n-2$ degrees of freedom is essential for an unbiased estimator when working with derived (differenced) data. Marks are frequently lost on this single step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Current Connections**, **Recent Connections**, and **Historical Network Artifacts** in malware forensics. For each category, list at least two sources of evidence and one tool used to acquire it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10,000-byte encrypted payload is intercepted during exfiltration. Frequency analysis of the byte distribution yields 256 distinct byte values, each appearing approximately 38–42 times. Compute the Shannon entropy of this payload and classify whether the data is likely encrypted, compressed, or plaintext. Justify your answer with reference to the entropy thresholds. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Category | Definition | Evidence Sources | Acquisition Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Connections** | Live, in-kernel socket table at the moment of triage | (1) Kernel socket table via IOCTL; (2) `Get-NetTCPConnection` PowerShell output | `netstat -anob`, `ss -tunap`, `TCPView` |
| **Recent Connections** | Volatile data that survives a few seconds to minutes but not reboots | (1) ARP cache; (2) DNS resolver cache; (3) Routing table | `arp -a`, `ipconfig /displaydns`, `route print` |
| **Historical Network Artifacts** | Persistent data stored on disk that records past network activity | (1) Windows Firewall logs (`pfirewall.log`); (2) SRUM database (`SRUDB.dat`); (3) Registry `Tcpip\Parameters` keys; (4) Event Logs (Security/4624, 4634) | `EvtxECmd`, `RegistryExplorer`, FTK, Velociraptor |

**Key Forensic Principle**: Current connections are most volatile (lost on reboot or socket closure), recent connections survive until cleanup timers expire, and historical artifacts persist for days to months. A complete forensic investigation must collect from all three tiers for evidentiary completeness.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1**: Determine the empirical probability of each byte.

With 256 distinct bytes and $N = 10000$ total bytes, and each byte appearing approximately $f_i \approx 40$ times:

$$
p_i = \frac{f_i}{N} = \frac{40}{10000} = 0.004 \quad \text{(approximately uniform)}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the Shannon entropy formula:

$$
H = -\sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

Since all $p_i$ are approximately equal, this approximates the **maximum entropy** case:

$$
H_{max} = \log_2(256) = 8.0 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the exact value.
