If each byte appears exactly 40 times, the calculation is:

$$
p_i = \frac{40}{10000} = 0.004
$$

$$
\log_2(0.004) = \log_2(4 \times 10^{-3}) = \log_2(4) + \log_2(10^{-3}) = 2 + (-9.9658) = -7.9658
$$

$$
H = -256 \times 0.004 \times (-7.9658) = -1.024 \times (-7.9658) = 8.1569 \approx 8.0
$$

**Step 4**: Classification:

$$
H \approx 8.0 \geq 6.5 \Rightarrow \textbf{ENCRYPTED PAYLOAD — EXFIL CONFIRMED}
$$

**Step 5**: Forensic Conclusion.

The near-maximum entropy indicates the payload exhibits **no statistical redundancy** — a signature of cryptographically strong encryption (e.g., AES-256 in CBC or CTR mode). Compressed data typically has $H \approx 5.0$–$6.5$, and plaintext falls below $3.0$. The conclusion is that this is **encrypted exfiltration** of approximately **10 KB of data** to the attacker's infrastructure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Students often incorrectly compute $\log_2(0.004)$ as a negative number but then forget the leading negative sign in the entropy formula, yielding a negative entropy. Always remember: the **outer negative sign** and the **negative logarithm** cancel, producing a **positive** entropy value. Another common error is misapplying the natural logarithm ($\ln$) instead of $\log_2$, which would yield incorrect units (nats instead of bits).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Current network connections** = live socket table (volatile, tier-3 evidence per RFC 3227).
- ✅ **The TCP 5-tuple** $(\text{Proto}, \text{SrcIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstIP}, \text{DstPort})$ is the atomic flow identifier.
- ✅ **Three tiers of network evidence**: Current (kernel sockets), Recent (ARP/DNS/route cache), Historical (firewall logs, SRUM, registry).
- ✅ **11 TCP states** exist; only ~6 are commonly seen: `LISTEN`, `SYN_SENT`, `ESTABLISHED`, `FIN_WAIT_2`, `CLOSE_WAIT`, `TIME_WAIT`.
- ✅ **Key tools**: `netstat -anob` (Windows), `ss -tunap` (Linux), `TCPView` (live), `Wireshark` / `NetworkMiner` (PCAP), Volatility `netscan` (memory).
- ✅ **Beaconing detection**: Compute CV of inter-arrival times; $CV < 0.15$ ⇒ automated.
- ✅ **Entropy classification**: $H < 3.0$ ⇒ plaintext; $3.0 \leq H < 6.5$ ⇒ compressed; $H \geq 6.5$ ⇒ encrypted.
- ✅ **Top IoCs in connections**: unknown remote IPs, non-standard ports, high-entropy payloads, regular intervals, rogue `LISTEN` ports (e.g., 4444, 8443).
- ✅ **Forensic ordering**: Capture volatile data (including socket table) **before** any system modification or reboot.
- ✅ **PID-to-executable mapping** is the bridge between network state and process artifacts — always correlate.
- ✅ **Port ranges**: 0–1023 (well-known), 1024–49151 (registered), 49152–65535 (ephemeral on Linux).
- ✅ **TIME_WAIT** duration is $T_{TW} = 2 \times MSL$ (typically 60–240 s) — relevant for post-shutdown socket recovery.
- ✅ **DNS tunneling**, **Tor exit nodes**, and **P2P botnets** are advanced IoC patterns examiners must recognize.
- ✅ **Examiner traps**: forgetting Bessel's correction ($n-2$) for sample SD of inter-arrival times; confusing $\ln$ with $\log_2$ in entropy; misidentifying ephemeral port ranges across Windows/Linux.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collecting Process Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Collecting Process Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Information** in the context of malware forensics refers to the systematic acquisition, preservation, and analysis of metadata and runtime state associated with all active, terminated, and dormant processes on a suspect system. This forms the **first and most critical live-response triage step** in any volatile evidence collection procedure governed by the **Order of Volatility (OOV)**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (PECCT741 – Malware Forensics), process information encompasses the following core data elements:

- **Process Identifier (PID)** – the unique integer assigned by the OS kernel to every executing process.
- **Parent Process Identifier (PPID)** – used to reconstruct the process tree and detect anomalies such as `explorer.exe` spawning `cmd.exe`.
- **Image File Path & Executable Name** – full path of the binary on disk.
- **Command-Line Arguments** – arguments passed during process invocation (key for identifying malicious launchers).
- **Loaded Modules / Dynamic-Link Libraries (DLLs)** – user-mode and kernel-mode modules mapped into the process address space.
- **Open Handles** – references to files, registry keys, mutexes, network sockets, and processes.
- **Process Memory Footprint** – Virtual Size, Working Set, Paged Pool, Non-Paged Pool.
- **Process User Context** – the security token, integrity level, and privilege set under which the process executes.
- **Creation / Exit Timestamps** – critical for timeline correlation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Process information is collected under the **Live System Forensics** umbrella. It is considered **Highly Volatile (Tier-1)** evidence and must be acquired **before** powering down the suspect machine, in strict adherence to RFC 3227 guidelines on the Order of Volatility.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **crime scene inside a busy shopping mall** at midnight. The investigators arrive and must quickly record every visible "actor" — who they are, what they are holding, who gave them their badge, and where they are standing.

In this analogy:
- The **shopping mall** = the live, running operating system.
- The **actors** = running processes (every program, service, and background task).
- The **badge / ID card** = PID and PPID.
- **What they are holding** = open file handles, registry keys, network sockets.
- **Who hired them (the manager)** = parent process in the tree.
- **Their clipboard and tools** = loaded DLLs and memory contents.
- **The investigators' notebook** = a forensic acquisition tool (Process Explorer, `tasklist`, Volatility).

A malware infection is akin to an **uninvited intruder** among the actors. The investigator's job is to photograph (snapshot) the entire crowd *before* the intruder notices and runs away or disguises themselves. Once the system is shut down, the intruder vanishes — which is exactly why **process information must be gathered while the system is alive**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A process in Windows is an instance of a running program. Each process has its own **virtual address space**, a **primary thread**, and a **handle table**. Malware, in nearly all cases, manifests as a process — or hides inside one. Therefore, **process-level visibility is the single highest-yield forensic vantage point** in live response.

### 1.3 Standard Forensic Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following standard metrics and identifiers are foundational to process forensics:

- **PID (Process ID):** 32-bit unsigned integer on Windows; up to **4,294,967,295** possible values.
- **EPROCESS structure size (Windows):** typically **~0x4A0 bytes** in Windows 10 x64.
- **Volatility `pslist` confidence:** High for active processes; **Medium** for recently terminated ones.
- **`tasklist` / `wmic` polling latency:** typically **< 500 ms** on an idle system.
- **Standard Process States (KTU taxonomy):** `Running`, `Ready`, `Waiting`, `Terminated`, `Suspended`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Process Information

In malware forensics, process information is decomposed into five logically independent pillars. Understanding each pillar is essential for KTU 14-mark questions.

**Pillar 1 — Process Identity and Provenance**
- PID, PPID, Image Name, Image Path, Hash (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256), Digital Signature status.
- **Why it matters:** Identifies *what* is running and *where* it came from. Unsigned binaries in `C:\Users\Public\` are high-risk indicators.

**Pillar 2 — Process Lineage (Tree Reconstruction)**
- Parent-child relationship reconstruction using PPID chains.
- **Why it matters:** Reveals process injection, fork-bomb behaviour, and persistence-launched children. The legitimate tree of `winlogon.exe → userinit.exe → explorer.exe` is the baseline; deviations are red flags.

**Pillar 3 — Module and Library Enumeration**
- List of all DLLs loaded by each process (via `VirtualQueryEx`, `EnumProcessModules`, or `ldrmodules` in Volatility).
- **Why it matters:** Detects **DLL hollowing, DLL injection, and reflective loading** — classic malware techniques.

**Pillar 4 — Handle and Resource Mapping**
- Open file handles, registry keys, named pipes, mutexes, network sockets, sections.
- **Why it matters:** A mutex named `Global\MyMalwareSingleton_v1.23` is a uniquely identifiable IOC (Indicator of Compromise).

**Pillar 5 — Memory Footprint and Execution Context**
- Working set, virtual size, page-fault count, I/O counters, CPU time, user context (token, integrity level).
- **Why it matters:** Memory-resident only malware (fileless malware) leaves footprints only in process memory.

### 2.2 Live vs. Post-Mortem Acquisition

| Aspect | Live Acquisition | Post-Mortem Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Running OS via APIs | Memory dump or disk image |
| **Tools** | `tasklist`, `wmic`, `Get-Process`, Process Explorer | Volatility (`pslist`, `psscan`, `pstree`) |
| **Time Sensitivity** | Seconds — anti-forensics can erase data | Hours — image is bit-exact and stable |
| **Anti-Forensic Risk** | **High** — rootkits can hide from APIs | **Lower** — kernel objects in memory still visible |
| **Output Format** | Text logs, CSV exports | Structured profiles (Volatility output) |
| **Forensic Validity** | Acceptable with hash + chain of custody | **Gold standard** when memory dump is intact |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 7-mark sub-question asks students to *compare* live and post-mortem process acquisition. Memorize the row "Anti-Forensic Risk" — examiners frequently test this.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{PID}$ | Process Identifier | Integer (uint32) |
| $\text{PPID}$ | Parent Process Identifier | Integer (uint32) |
| $H_{\text{SHA-256}}(B)$ | Cryptographic hash of process binary $B$ | 256-bit digest |
| $N_p$ | Total number of active processes | Count |
| $D_{proc}$ | Volatility confidence metric: $D_{proc} = \dfrac{\#\text{active}}{\#\text{active} + \#\text{hidden}}$ | Ratio $\in [0,1]$ |
| $T_{\text{vol}}$ | Time to acquire volatile data | Seconds (target: $\le 5$ min) |
| $M_{\text{dump}}$ | Memory dump size: $M_{\text{dump}} \approx 1.0 \times \text{RAM} + \epsilon$ | Bytes |
| $\Sigma_{\text{token}}$ | Process token: $\{SID, IL, Privileges\}$ | Set |
| $L_{\text{dll}}$ | Loaded DLL list: $L_{\text{dll}} = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n\}$ | Set of module names |
| $H_{\text{handle}}$ | Handle table: $H_{\text{handle}} = \{(obj, type, access)_i\}$ | Indexed set |
| $S_{\text{state}}$ | Process state $\in \{\text{Running, Ready, Waiting, Terminated, Suspended}\}$ | Categorical |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Collecting process information is foundational in:

- **Incident Response (IR):** Rapid triage to identify the patient zero process.
- **Threat Hunting:** Proactive search for IOCs in process trees.
- **Malware Reverse Engineering:** Identifies suspect binaries to feed into IDA/Ghidra.
- **Memory Forensics:** Provides the entry point for Volatility-based deep analysis.
- **Legal / E-Discovery:** Process evidence is admissible under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (analogous to U.S. FRE 902(13)/(14)) when properly hashed and chained.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Live Process Acquisition Procedure

The following is the **KTU-recommended procedure** for collecting process information from a live Windows host, derived directly from the NIST SP 800-86 and RFC 3227 methodologies. Every step is shown explicitly with its forensic justification.

**Step 1 — Authorization and Documentation**
- Verify the **Letter of Authorization (LoA)** and incident response scope.
- Document the investigator's name, date, time, system identifier, and tool versions in the **Chain of Custody (CoC)** form.
- Initialize a **forensic workstation** with verified binaries (hash-checked against a known-good baseline).

**Step 2 — Establish the Forensic Toolkit**
- Use **write-blockers** for any external media.
- Tools to be staged: `winpmem` (memory acquisition), `FTK Imager` (memory + disk), Sysinternals `Process Explorer`, `Handle.exe`, `ListDLLs.exe`, `Autoruns.exe`, PowerShell `Get-Process`, and a logging harness.

**Step 3 — Memory Dump Acquisition (FIRST, due to highest volatility)**
- Use `winpmem_mini_x64_rc2.exe E:\evidence\mem.raw`.
- Compute $\text{SHA-256}(M_{\text{dump}})$ immediately and record.
- *Justification:* Process memory is the only source that survives reboot and contains `psxview` viewable structures.

**Step 4 — Enumerate Active Processes via Native APIs**
- Run `tasklist /v /fo csv > E:\evidence\processes.csv`.
- For deeper enumeration: `wmic process get name,processid,parentprocessid,executablepath,commandline /format:csv`.
- Capture: PID, PPID, Image Name, User Context, CPU Time, Window Title.

**Step 5 — Reconstruct the Process Tree**
- Use `wmic process get ProcessId,ParentProcessId,Name` → feed into `pstree` (Volatility) for cross-validation.
- Visually verify the **baseline lineage**:
  $$\text{System} \rightarrow \text{smss.exe} \rightarrow \text{csrss.exe}, \text{wininit.exe}$$
  $$\text{wininit.exe} \rightarrow \text{services.exe} \rightarrow \text{svchost.exe}$$
  $$\text{userinit.exe} \rightarrow \text{explorer.exe}$$
- Any deviation = **anomaly requiring deeper inspection**.

**Step 6 — Dump Loaded DLLs per Process**
- Use `listdlls.exe -u > E:\evidence\dlls.txt` (Sysinternals).
- For suspicious PIDs: `listdlls.exe -u -p <PID>`.
- Cross-reference DLLs against the **KnownDLLs registry key** to detect **DLL hijacking / search-order hijacking**.

**Step 7 — Capture Open Handles**
- `handle.exe -a -u > E:\evidence\handles.txt`.
- Flag handles pointing to non-standard paths, suspicious mutexes (`Global\ZoneAllocator`, `Global\MsWinZonesCache`), or hidden registry keys under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`.

**Step 8 — Capture Command-Line and Network Information**
- `wmic process get commandline /format:csv`.
- Pair with `netstat -anob` for socket-to-process mapping.
- **Justification:** Command-line reveals dropper behaviour, e.g., `powershell.exe -enc <base64>` is a near-certain indicator of malicious activity.

**Step 9 — Hash Every Process Binary**
- For each unique Image Path: compute $\text{SHA-256}(B)$ and **VirusTotal lookup**.
- Rule: any binary with $\ge 10$ antivirus detections is a **Confirmed Indicator of Compromise (IoC)**.

**Step 10 — Acquire Volatile Network State**
- `netstat -anob`, `arp -a`, `ipconfig /displaydns`.
- Map outbound sockets to process PIDs — outbound connections from `svchost.exe` to non-Microsoft IPs is a high-fidelity IoC.

**Step 11 — Package and Verify**
- Compute hashes of all evidence files: $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(E_i)$.
- Sign with investigator's PGP key.
- Transfer to write-protected evidence drive; update CoC.

### 3.2 Derivation: Process Tree Anomaly Score

For the 14-mark analytical question, the following **anomaly score** is derived to quantify how suspicious a process tree is:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{tree}} &= w_1 \cdot \frac{N_{\text{unsigned}}}{N_p} + w_2 \cdot \frac{N_{\text{orphan}}}{N_p} + w_3 \cdot \frac{N_{\text{abnormal parent}}}{N_p} \\
&\quad + w_4 \cdot \frac{N_{\text{non-standard path}}}{N_p} + w_5 \cdot \frac{N_{\text{hidden from API}}}{N_p}
\end{aligned}$$

where:

- $N_{\text{unsigned}}$ = number of processes with no digital signature.
- $N_{\text{orphan}}$ = number of processes whose PPID does not exist (zombie / unlinked process).
- $N_{\text{abnormal parent}}$ = processes whose parent is not in the expected lineage (e.g., `cmd.exe` as child of `winword.exe`).
- $N_{\text{non-standard path}}$ = processes running from `C:\Users\Public\`, `C:\Temp\`, `%APPDATA%`.
- $N_{\text{hidden from API}}$ = processes visible in kernel structures (`psxview`) but not in `tasklist` — a **rootkit indicator**.

Typical weights (empirically tuned): $w_1 = 0.15$, $w_2 = 0.25$, $w_3 = 0.30$, $w_4 = 0.20$, $w_5 = 0.10$.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 1.0$$

**Classification threshold:**
$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{tree}} < 0.10 &\Rightarrow \text{Clean} \\
0.10 \le A_{\text{tree}} < 0.30 &\Rightarrow \text{Suspicious — deeper analysis required} \\
A_{\text{tree}} \ge 0.30 &\Rightarrow \text{High Confidence Compromise}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** During live triage, an investigator finds $N_p = 142$ active processes. The breakdown is:
- Unsigned binaries: $N_{\text{unsigned}} = 7$
- Orphan processes: $N_{\text{orphan}} = 2$
- Abnormal parents: $N_{\text{abnormal parent}} = 3$
- Non-standard path: $N_{\text{non-standard path}} = 5$
- Hidden from API: $N_{\text{hidden from API}} = 1$

**Step 1 — Compute each ratio:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_{\text{unsigned}}}{N_p} &= \frac{7}{142} \approx 0.0493 \\
\frac{N_{\text{orphan}}}{N_p} &= \frac{2}{142} \approx 0.0141 \\
\frac{N_{\text{abnormal parent}}}{N_p} &= \frac{3}{142} \approx 0.0211 \\
\frac{N_{\text{non-standard path}}}{N_p} &= \frac{5}{142} \approx 0.0352 \\
\frac{N_{\text{hidden from API}}}{N_p} &= \frac{1}{142} \approx 0.0070
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Weighted sum:**
$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{tree}} &= 0.15(0.0493) + 0.25(0.0141) + 0.30(0.0211) \\
&\quad + 0.20(0.0352) + 0.10(0.0070) \\
A_{\text{tree}} &= 0.00740 + 0.00353 + 0.00634 + 0.00704 + 0.00070 \\
A_{\text{tree}} &= 0.0250
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**
$$A_{\text{tree}} = 0.0250 < 0.10 \Rightarrow \textbf{Clean}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check on weights:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.0 \;\checkmark$$

**Verdict:** The tree is statistically clean under the empirical model. The single hidden-from-API process, however, warrants individual inspection — it may be a user-mode rootkit component that the weighted score under-penalizes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Even when the global $A_{\text{tree}}$ score is low, **any non-zero value of $N_{\text{hidden from API}}$** must be investigated manually, since it directly contradicts the API-only enumeration and is the strongest single signal of rootkit presence.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Volatility Plugin Mapping

| Investigation Goal | Recommended Volatility Plugin | Output | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| List active processes | `windows.pslist` | PID, PPID, name, create time | Baseline |
| List hidden/terminated processes | `windows.psscan` | Pool-scanning results | Detects DKOM rootkits |
| Cross-view comparison | `windows.psxview` | 6 columns: pslist, psscan, thrdproc, pspcid, csrss, session | Highlights discrepancies |
| Process tree | `windows.pstree` | ASCII tree | Visual lineage analysis |
| Loaded DLLs | `windows.dlllist` | Process $\rightarrow$ loaded DLLs | DLL injection detection |
| Command-line args | `windows.cmdline` | Full command-line per process | Encoded PowerShell, dropper args |
| Open handles | `windows.handles` | Object, type, access mask | IOC mutex / file detection |
| Process memory dump | `windows.memmap --dump --pid <PID>` | Raw memory of process | Reverse engineering |
| Network connections | `windows.netscan` | PID, local/remote IP, port | C2 beacon identification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Information Collection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Reported] --> B[Authorization and Chain of Custody]
    B --> C[Stage Forensic Toolkit]
    C --> D[Acquire Memory Dump winpmem]
    D --> E[Enumerate Processes tasklist and wmic]
    E --> F[Reconstruct Process Tree pstree]
    F --> G[Enumerate DLLs listdlls and dlllist]
    G --> H[Capture Handles handle and handles]
    H --> I[Capture Command Line cmdline]
    I --> J[Hash Binaries SHA-256]
    J --> K[Cross-Reference IOCs VirusTotal]
    K --> L{Anomaly Score}
    L -->|< 0.10| M[Mark as Clean Baseline]
    L -->|0.10 to 0.30| N[Deeper Memory Analysis]
    L -->|>= 0.30| O[High Confidence Compromise]
    N --> P[Memdump Suspect PIDs]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Package and Sign Evidence]
    M --> Q
    Q --> R[Handoff to Reporting]
```

### 4.2 Process Tree Baseline and Anomaly Markers

```mermaid
graph TD
    sysA[System] --> smssA[smss.exe]
    smssA --> csrssA[csrss.exe]
    smssA --> wininitA[wininit.exe]
    wininitA --> servicesA[services.exe]
    wininitA --> lsassA[lsass.exe]
    servicesA --> svchostA[svchost.exe]
    userinitA[userinit.exe] --> explorerA[explorer.exe]
    winlogonA[winlogon.exe] --> userinitA
    smssA --> winlogonA
    explorerA --> badA[cmd.exe ANOMALY]
    explorerA --> badB[powershell.exe ANOMALY]
    badB --> badC[suspicious.exe UNSIGNED]
    badC --> badD[Encoded Base64 Payload]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Process Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USERSPACE["User Space Acquisition"]
        A1[tasklist] --> M1[Process List]
        A2[wmic] --> M1
        A3[PowerShell Get-Process] --> M1
        A4[Process Explorer GUI] --> M1
    end
    subgraph KERNEL["Kernel Space Acquisition"]
        B1[EPROCESS List] --> M2[Pool Scan]
        B2[ETHREAD List] --> M2
        B3[Handle Table] --> M3[Handle Dump]
        B4[VAD Tree] --> M4[Memory Map]
    end
    subgraph TOOLS["Volatility Post-Processing"]
        M1 --> T1[pslist]
        M1 --> T2[psscan]
        M1 --> T3[psxview]
        M1 --> T4[pstree]
        M2 --> T2
        M3 --> T5[handles]
        M4 --> T6[memmap]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Forensic Output"]
        T1 --> O1[Process Tree Report]
        T2 --> O1
        T3 --> O1
        T4 --> O1
        T5 --> O2[Handle and IOC List]
        T6 --> O3[Memory Dump for RE]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "process information" in the context of malware forensics. List any FOUR core data elements that must be collected during a live system process enumeration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Process information is the metadata and runtime state of all active, terminated, and dormant processes on a suspect system, captured as **Tier-1 volatile evidence** under the Order of Volatility. Four core data elements are:
1. **PID and PPID** — process identity and lineage.
2. **Image Path and Executable Name** — source location of the binary.
3. **Command-Line Arguments** — invocation context (reveals droppers).
4. **Loaded DLLs** — modules mapped into the process address space (DLL injection detection).

*(Acceptable alternatives: handles, memory footprint, user token, creation timestamp, open network sockets.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is process information considered "highly volatile" evidence? State the recommended tool to enumerate processes on a live Windows system without installing third-party software.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Process information is highly volatile because it resides in **RAM only** — once the system is powered off, all process structures (`EPROCESS`, `ETHREAD`, handle tables) are lost permanently. Malware can also terminate itself or use rootkit techniques (DKOM) to hide from API-level enumeration within seconds. **Recommended built-in tool:** `tasklist` (or equivalently `wmic process` / PowerShell `Get-Process`). For richer data, Sysinternals **Process Explorer** is the gold standard.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete procedure for collecting process information** from a live Windows system during incident response. List the tools used at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Volatility plugins `pslist`, `psscan`, `psxview`, and `pstree`**. How does `psxview` help in detecting user-mode and kernel-mode rootkits? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure for Live Process Information Collection (7 marks):**

1. **Authorization and Setup (1 mark):** Verify Letter of Authorization, initiate Chain of Custody, hash-verify the forensic toolkit (Sysinternals Suite, `winpmem`, `FTK Imager`).

2. **Memory Acquisition (1 mark):** Use `winpmem_mini_x64_rc2.exe E:\evidence\mem.raw` to capture full RAM. Hash with SHA-256. This preserves the volatile `EPROCESS` linked list and pool tags.

3. **Process Enumeration via Native APIs (1 mark):**
   - `tasklist /v /fo csv > processes.csv` — quick baseline.
   - `wmic process get name,processid,parentprocessid,executablepath,commandline /format:csv` — rich detail.

4. **Process Tree Reconstruction (1 mark):** Cross-validate PPID chain using `pstree` (Volatility) against the live `wmic` output. Identify deviations from the legitimate lineage (`System → smss.exe → wininit.exe → services.exe → svchost.exe`).

5. **DLL Enumeration (1 mark):** `listdlls.exe -u > dlls.txt`. Detect unsigned DLLs in standard process address spaces — strong indicator of **DLL injection / hollowing**.

6. **Handle and Command-Line Capture (1 mark):** `handle.exe -a -u > handles.txt` and `wmic process get commandline`. Look for suspicious mutexes (`Global\ZoneAllocator`) and obfuscated PowerShell commands (`-enc` flag).

7. **Hashing, IOC Correlation, and Packaging (1 mark):** SHA-256 every binary, VirusTotal lookup, sign with PGP, update CoC.

**(b) Volatility Plugins — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **`windows.pslist` (2 marks):** Walks the doubly-linked list of `EPROCESS` structures in kernel memory. Lists *active* processes only. **Limitation:** Easily defeated by **DKOM (Direct Kernel Object Manipulation)** rootkits that unlink the `EPROCESS` from the list while keeping the object allocated.

2. **`windows.psscan` (2 marks):** Performs a **pool-tag scan** of kernel memory, looking for the `Proc` pool tag that marks every `EPROCESS` allocation — *active or terminated*. Detects hidden and recently terminated processes that `pslist` cannot see.

3. **`windows.psxview` (1 mark):** Cross-compares **six** different process enumeration techniques (`pslist`, `psscan`, `thrdproc`, `pspcid`, `csrss`, `session`). A process visible in `psscan` but missing in `pslist` is the **classic DKOM rootkit signature**.

4. **`windows.pstree` (1 mark):** Renders the PPID hierarchy as an ASCII tree for visual inspection of lineage anomalies (e.g., `winword.exe → cmd.exe`).

5. **Rootkit Detection Logic (1 mark):** `psxview` output contains columns: `pslist`, `psscan`, `thrdproc`, `pspcid`, `csrss`, `session`, `Description`. A `False` in `pslist` but `True` in `psscan` ⇒ **DKOM hiding**. A `False` in `csrss` ⇒ **user-mode rootkit** (process is hidden from `csrss.exe`'s handle table, indicating API hooking).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Differentiate between **live system process acquisition** and **post-mortem memory-based process acquisition**. Discuss the anti-forensic threats specific to each approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Windows system under investigation shows 180 active processes. The investigator observes: 9 unsigned, 3 orphan, 4 abnormal-parent, 6 non-standard-path, and 2 hidden-from-API. Using the weighted anomaly score formula with $w_1=0.15$, $w_2=0.25$, $w_3=0.30$, $w_4=0.20$, $w_5=0.10$, **compute the anomaly score and classify the system**. Justify your classification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Live vs Post-Mortem Acquisition (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Live Acquisition | Post-Mortem (Memory Dump) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | OS API queries (`NtQuerySystemInformation`) | Volatility plugins on `.raw` / `.dmp` file |
| **Visibility** | Subject to user-mode hooks | Operates on raw kernel structures |
| **Anti-Forensic Threat** | API hooking, DKOM, process hollowing, self-termination | Timestamp manipulation, memory wiping via secure erase, anti-VM tricks |
| **Latency** | Sub-second; data changes live | Static once image is captured |
| **Forensic Weight** | Lower — must be paired with hashes | **Higher** — bit-exact reproducible |

**Live-specific threats (1 mark):** Rootkits hooking `NtQuerySystemInformation` can return a sanitized list. Malware can detect forensic tools (via window titles like "Process Explorer") and self-terminate.

**Post-mortem-specific threats (1 mark):** Memory-only malware can clear pool tags. Compression / encryption by sophisticated malware reduces IOC visibility. Dump files may be incomplete if RAM was under heavy I/O.

**Why both are needed (1 mark):** Cross-validation — a process visible in `psscan` but absent in `pslist` is conclusive rootkit evidence.

**(b) Anomaly Score Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute ratios (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \frac{9}{180} = 0.0500 \\
r_2 &= \frac{3}{180} = 0.0167 \\
r_3 &= \frac{4}{180} = 0.0222 \\
r_4 &= \frac{6}{180} = 0.0333 \\
r_5 &= \frac{2}{180} = 0.0111
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Weighted sum (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{tree}} &= 0.15(0.0500) + 0.25(0.0167) + 0.30(0.0222) \\
&\quad + 0.20(0.0333) + 0.10(0.0111) \\
A_{\text{tree}} &= 0.00750 + 0.00418 + 0.00667 + 0.00667 + 0.00111 \\
A_{\text{tree}} &= 0.0261
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Classification (1 mark):**
$$A_{\text{tree}} = 0.0261 < 0.10 \Rightarrow \textbf{Clean}$$

**Step 4 — Justification (1 mark):**
Despite the global score being in the "Clean" band, the presence of **2 hidden-from-API processes** (i.e., $N_{\text{hidden from API}} = 2 \ne 0$) is a non-trivial rootkit signal. Per the manual-override rule, the investigator **must** memdump those PIDs and analyse them with `malfind`, `hollowfind`, or `yarascan`. The weighted model under-penalizes the most critical indicator; hence, statistical clean ≠ forensic clean.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify tool hashes** before running them on the suspect system — examiners deduct 1 mark under "Chain of Custody".
> 2. **Confusing `pslist` and `psscan`** — `pslist` walks the linked list; `psscan` does a pool-tag scan. This is a 2-mark differentiator in nearly every KTU paper.
> 3. **Ignoring the $N_{\text{hidden from API}}$ override** — many students compute a low $A_{\text{tree}}$ and conclude "no compromise". Examiners specifically test whether you can identify the **manual-override clause** and escalate.
> 4. **Not mentioning the Order of Volatility** before describing tools — this is the first thing the examiner looks for in a 14-mark answer.
> 5. **Mixing up `wmic` with `Get-CimInstance`** — `wmic` is deprecated; PowerShell 7+ uses `Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_Process`. Examiners reward modernization.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Process information = PID, PPID, image path, command-line, DLLs, handles, memory, token, and timestamps for every active/terminated process.
- **Volatility Tier:** **Tier-1 (Highest)** — must be acquired within seconds of incident response initiation.
- **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** Registers → Routing Table → Process List → Kernel Modules → Network Connections → Open Files → Memory Map → Disk → Remote Logs.
- **Five Pillars:** Identity, Lineage, Modules, Handles, Memory Context.
- **Key Tools:**
  - Live: `tasklist`, `wmic`, PowerShell `Get-Process`, Sysinternals `Process Explorer`, `ListDLLs`, `handle.exe`.
  - Post-mortem: Volatility (`pslist`, `psscan`, `psxview`, `pstree`, `dlllist`, `handles`, `cmdline`).
- **`pslist` vs `psscan`:** Linked-list walk vs pool-tag scan. **Always run both** to defeat DKOM.
- **`psxview`:** The rootkit-detection Swiss-army-knife — 6 cross-views in one report.
- **Anomaly Score Formula:** $A_{\text{tree}} = \sum w_i r_i$; thresholds: $< 0.10$ clean, $0.10$–$0.30$ suspicious, $\ge 0.30$ compromised.
- **Manual Override:** Any $N_{\text{hidden from API}} > 0$ requires direct investigation of those PIDs regardless of $A_{\text{tree}}$.
- **Hashing:** SHA-256 every binary; cross-reference with **VirusTotal** and **MalwareBazaar**.
- **Baseline Lineage (Memorize):** `System → smss.exe → wininit.exe → services.exe → svchost.exe` and `winlogon.exe → userinit.exe → explorer.exe`.
- **High-Fidelity IoCs:** `powershell.exe -enc`, `cmd.exe` as child of `winword.exe`, unsigned binaries in `%APPDATA%`, mutexes with malware-version strings, outbound sockets from `svchost.exe` to non-Microsoft IPs.
- **Chain of Custody:** Hash every evidence file, sign, log, and store on write-protected media.
- **Fileless Malware Note:** If $A_{\text{tree}}$ is low but the system shows symptoms, the malware is likely memory-resident — use `malfind`, `hollowfind`, and YARA scans.
- **Examiner Heuristic:** A 14-mark answer that *omits* the Order of Volatility and *omits* the `psxview` explanation will not cross **9/14**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlate Open Ports with Running Processes and Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Correlate Open Ports with Running Processes and Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Port-Process Correlation** is a foundational investigative technique in live-system malware forensics wherein a forensic examiner establishes a **bidirectional mapping** between an active **Transport Layer endpoint** (a TCP/UDP port in LISTENING, ESTABLISHED, or TIME_WAIT state) and the **user-space process ID (PID)** that owns or initiated that endpoint. This correlation allows the analyst to attribute otherwise anonymous network activity on the wire to a specific executable, parent process, user account, and loaded module.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Outcome-Based Education), this topic maps directly to **CO3: Analyse volatile artefacts and live network evidence to attribute malicious activity to specific processes and programs.**

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

- **Open Port (Listening Socket):** A port bound by a process that is actively accepting incoming connections (state `LISTENING` in netstat).
- **Active Connection (Established Socket):** A port currently participating in a TCP three-way handshake (state `ESTABLISHED`).
- **PID (Process Identifier):** The unique integer assigned by the operating system kernel to identify a running process.
- **Process Handle / Socket Descriptor:** A kernel-allocated file descriptor pointing to a socket structure (in Linux: `struct sock`; in Windows: `SOCKET` handle within the EPROCESS structure).
- **Image Path / Executable Name:** The full on-disk path of the binary that owns the process.
- **Foreign Address:** The remote IP:port of a connected peer — essential for spotting **outbound Command-and-Control (C2) callbacks**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Switchboard" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a large hotel (your computer). Each **room number** is a **port** (e.g., Room 80, Room 443, Room 3389). Each **room is owned by a guest** (a process/program). The **switchboard operator** (your operating system kernel) keeps a logbook: *"Room 80 is currently held by Mr. Apache (PID 1408), Room 3389 is held by a guest who checked in 3 minutes ago with a fake ID (suspicious process)."* When a guest makes an outgoing call, the operator also records the **number they dialed** (the Foreign Address).

Forensic analysts become "switchboard auditors": they don't just see calls happening — they open the logbook and ask, **"Who is occupying Room 4444 that nobody reserved? Whose phone line has been dialing an IP in Moscow every 30 seconds?"** That is the essence of port-process correlation.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Well-Known Ports:** **0 – 1023** (privileged, root/`SYSTEM`-only)
- **Registered Ports:** **1024 – 49151** (assigned by IANA for applications)
- **Dynamic/Private (Ephemeral) Ports:** **49152 – 65535** (assigned by OS to outbound clients)
- **Default Linux ephemeral range:** **32768 – 60999** (`/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range`)
- **Default Windows ephemeral range:** **49152 – 65535** (Windows 10/Server 2019+)
- **Standard forensic tools of the trade:** **netstat, lsof, ss, fuser, TCPView, CurrPorts, Process Explorer**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The act of correlating open ports to running processes is not a single command; it is a **layered investigative workflow**. Below is the structured logic an examiner follows.

### Step 1 — Enumerate All Active Endpoints
The examiner first commands the kernel to list every active socket. On the wire, this resolves to walking in-kernel hash tables (e.g., Linux's `tcp_hashinfo`, Windows' `TcpEThread` pool) and dumping entries.

> **Why?** You cannot correlate what you cannot enumerate. A port that is *closed* leaves no trace; a port that is *open* (even if stealthy to Nmap) still appears in the kernel socket table.

### Step 2 — Extract Process Ownership Metadata
For each socket, retrieve the owning PID. This is achieved by:
- **Windows:** Querying the *Eprocess* structure's `OwningProcess` field (visible via `Get-NetTCPConnection` cmdlet or `netstat -ano`).
- **Linux:** Reading the `inode` field of a TCP entry in `/proc/net/tcp` and cross-referencing it with `/proc/<PID>/fd/` symlinks (the mechanism `lsof` uses).

### Step 3 — Resolve PID to Executable Path
Convert the PID into a human-meaningful image. On Linux, read `/proc/<PID>/exe`. On Windows, walk the `Eprocess` → `Peb` → `ProcessParameters` → `ImagePathName` chain (this is how Process Explorer/TCPView work).

### Step 4 — Identify the Foreign Address
The peer IP:port is the **direction-of-travel indicator**. Outbound traffic to a non-corporate IP on a non-standard port is the single highest-yield red flag in live forensics.

### Step 5 — Establish Baseline vs. Anomaly
Compare current port-process pairs against an authorized baseline (e.g., a known-good image from a clean host). Any **unbound executable occupying a listening port** is a candidate for further static/dynamic analysis.

## 2.2 The Two-State Framework: Listening vs. Established

| State | Forensic Meaning | Typical Examiner Question |
|---|---|---|
| **LISTENING** | A service is **accepting** connections; the host is a *server* for this port. | *"Should this service even be running? Is it the legitimate binary or a trojanized copy?"* |
| **ESTABLISHED** | A bidirectional TCP session is **live**; data is being exchanged. | *"Where is it talking? How long? How much data?"* |
| **TIME_WAIT / FIN_WAIT** | Connection is **closing**; evidence of recent activity. | *"What just terminated? Who was the peer?"* |
| **CLOSE_WAIT** | Remote end closed; local has not yet closed — **often a sign of a stalled/leaking malicious client.** | *"Why is a process holding a half-open socket?"* |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Symbol Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated reference table for the symbolic notation, kernel offsets, and command syntax you may be asked to reproduce in the ESE.

| Symbol / Concept | Definition / Value | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{pid}$ | Process Identifier of owning process | Integer, $\geq 1$ (Linux/Windows) |
| $S_{port}$ | Local socket port number | Integer, $0$ – $65535$ |
| $S_{state}$ | TCP state code (L, E, T-W, F-W) | Enum |
| $F_{addr}$ | Foreign IP : port tuple | IPv4/IPv6 string |
| $T_{proto}$ | Transport protocol | $\{TCP, UDP\}$ |
| $\text{WellKnownRange}$ | Privileged port range | $0$ – $1023$ |
| $\text{EphemeralRange}_{\text{Win}}$ | Windows dynamic port pool | $49152$ – $65535$ |
| $\text{EphemeralRange}_{\text{Linux}}$ | Linux default dynamic pool | $32768$ – $60999$ |
| $\text{Inode}_{sock}$ | Kernel socket inode (Linux) | 64-bit unsigned int |
| $\text{Hash}(S_{port}, F_{addr})$ | In-kernel socket lookup function | Deterministic |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
Beyond malware forensics, this exact skill is used in:
- **Incident Response (IR):** Triage of a compromised server within the first 60 minutes ("the **Golden Hour**").
- **Threat Hunting:** Proactive sweeps using **Sysmon** (Event ID 3 — Network Connection) to log every port-process pair for later SIEM correlation.
- **Cloud Security:** Detecting **cryptominers** that open port 3333, 14444, or 14433.
- **OT/ICS Security:** Identifying rogue services on a SCADA historian that should never expose a listening port to the corporate VLAN.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Command-Syntax Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic frequently demand that you **write the exact command**, **explain each flag**, and **interpret the output**. Below are exhaustive, examiner-grade walkthroughs.

## 3.1 Derivation: Windows `netstat` with PID Correlation

The canonical exam question is: *"Write a Windows command to display all active TCP connections with their owning PID, in numerical form, and explain the output."*

### Derivation Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Recall the base command.** `netstat` is a built-in Windows utility located in `C:\Windows\System32\netstat.exe`.

**Step 2 — Apply the necessary switches by logical deduction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{netstat} \quad &\text{base command (no flags = show current connections)} \\
-\text{a} \quad &\text{Show ALL connections AND listening ports (a = "all")} \\
-\text{n} \quad &\text{Show addresses and port numbers NUMERICALLY (no DNS resolution)} \\
-\text{o} \quad &\text{Show the OWNING process ID associated with each connection}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compose the final command.**

```text
netstat -ano
```

**Step 4 — Compose the "with protocol filter" variant** (a frequent 7-mark follow-up):

```text
netstat -ano -p tcp
```

where `-p tcp` restricts the output to TCP only (omit `-p` for both TCP and UDP).

**Step 5 — Interpret a sample output row.**

```text
  Proto  Local Address          Foreign Address        State           PID
  TCP    0.0.0.0:445            0.0.0.0:0              LISTENING       4
  TCP    192.168.1.50:49152     185.220.101.45:443     ESTABLISHED     3712
  TCP    192.168.1.50:49210     10.0.0.5:3389          ESTABLISHED     6204
```

**Step 6 — Map PID to Executable.**

```text
tasklist /FI "PID eq 3712"
```

**Result interpretation logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1:} \quad & \text{Port 445 LISTENING, owned by PID 4.} \\
& \text{PID 4 is the System process → expected (SMB).} \\
\text{Row 2:} \quad & \text{Outbound to 185.220.101.45:443 (known TOR exit node).} \\
& \text{PID 3712 → likely TOR browser or malware beacon. \textbf{ANOMALY.}} \\
\text{Row 3:} \quad & \text{Outbound RDP to internal host 10.0.0.5.} \\
& \text{If user "guest" initiated → likely lateral movement.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Linux `lsof` (List Open Files) — the Most Powerful Single Tool

### Conceptual Foundation
On Linux, **everything is a file** — sockets included. Therefore, listing "open files" of a network type is equivalent to listing open sockets.

**The flagship command:**

```text
lsof -i -P -n
```

### Flag-by-Flag Logical Deduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{lsof} \quad &\text{List Open Files} \\
-\text{i} \quad &\text{Filter to internet (network) files only — sockets} \\
-\text{P} \quad &\text{Show port numbers NUMERICALLY (suppress /etc/services name lookups)} \\
-\text{n} \quad &\text{Show IP addresses NUMERICALLY (no DNS reverse lookup)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Examiner rationale (this is what you write in a 14-mark answer):**
*"The `-P -n` combination is critical in forensic triage because DNS resolution on a compromised host may be **poisoned**, **redirected**, or simply **slow** — it can mask the true destination IP and waste precious minutes of the golden hour."*

### Power-User Variant — Filter by Specific Port

```text
lsof -i :4444
```

This shows the process listening on or connected to port 4444 — the single most common reverse-shell port used by Metasploit's `meterpreter` and the **BlackCat/ALPHV** ransomware family.

### Process-Filter Variant — What Ports Does *This* Binary Have Open?

```text
lsof -c sshd -i
```

Here `-c sshd` filters to the command name `sshd`. This answers the question: *"Is the SSH daemon the only process opening port 22?"* — useful for detecting **SSH hijacking** or **port-rebinding attacks**.

## 3.3 Derivation: The Linux `ss` Command (Modern Replacement for netstat)

```text
ss -tulnp
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\text{t} \quad &\text{Show TCP sockets} \\
-\text{u} \quad &\text{Show UDP sockets} \\
-\text{l} \quad &\text{Show LISTENING sockets only} \\
-\text{n} \quad &\text{No DNS resolution (numerical)} \\
-\text{p} \quad &\text{Show process (PID/Program name) — requires root or CAP_NET_ADMIN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Examiner note:** `ss` reads directly from the kernel via `netlink` (the `NETLINK_INET_DIAG` family), making it **faster and more accurate** than the legacy `netstat`, which parses `/proc/net/tcp`. This is the KTU-preferred answer on a modern Linux host.

## 3.4 Derivation: The `fuser` Command — "Who is Holding This Port?"

A frequent KTU Part-A question is: *"How do you find out which process is using a specific port in Linux?"*

**Step-by-step derivation:**

```text
fuser -n tcp 80
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{fuser} \quad &\text{Display which process is using a file/socket} \\
-\text{n tcp} \quad &\text{Look in the network namespace, TCP protocol} \\
\text{80} \quad &\text{The target port number}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output interpretation:**
```text
80/tcp:             1234  5678
```
→ PIDs **1234** and **5678** are bound to TCP port 80.

**Verbose variant for forensic completeness:**

```text
fuser -v -n tcp 80
```

The `-v` flag adds the user, PID, and command columns.

## 3.5 Worked Example — A Complete 7-Mark "Trace" Question

> **Q:** *A user reports their machine is "slow." You are the IR analyst. Walk through correlating open ports to running processes on a suspected Windows 10 host.*

**Model answer (numbered, examiner-grade):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{netsh trace start capture=yes ipv4.address=yes} \quad \text{(start packet capture)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{netstat -ano -b} \quad \text{(shows PID + executable, requires admin)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{tasklist /v /fi "PID eq 3712"} \quad \text{(resolve PID 3712)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{wmic process where ProcessId=3712 get ParentProcessId,CommandLine} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \texttt{Get-CimInstance Win32\_Process -Filter "ProcessId=3712" \textbar} \\
                  & \quad \texttt{Invoke-CimMethod -MethodName GetOwner} \quad \text{(identify user)} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Cross-reference 185.220.101.45 against threat-intel feeds (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB)} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \texttt{autoruns.exe -a} \quad \text{(check persistence mechanism)} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \texttt{Get-FileHash C:\textbackslash Users\textbackslash\victim\textbackslash app.exe -Algorithm SHA256} \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{Memory dump with \texttt{winpmem\_min\_x64.rc4.exe}, then Volatility \texttt{malfind}} \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad & \text{Document IOC list and proceed to containment.}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Port-to-Process Correlation Architecture

> The following diagram illustrates the **logical flow** of an examiner's investigation: from raw kernel socket enumeration, through PID resolution, to the final forensic verdict.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Examiner Initiates Live Triage] --> B{Select OS Platform}
    B -- Windows --> C[Run netstat -ano or Get-NetTCPConnection]
    B -- Linux --> D[Run ss -tulnp or lsof -i -P -n]
    C --> E[Extract PID and State per Row]
    D --> F[Extract PID and Inode per Row]
    E --> G[Resolve PID to Executable via tasklist or Get-Process]
    F --> H[Resolve PID to Executable via readlink /proc PID exe]
    G --> I[Capture Foreign Address and Protocol]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Compare Against Baseline}
    J -- Match --> K[Mark as Known Good Service]
    J -- Mismatch --> L[Flag as Anomalous IOC]
    L --> M[Perform Static Analysis Hash Lookup]
    L --> N[Perform Dynamic Analysis Sandbox]
    L --> O[Capture Memory Dump for Volatility]
    M --> P[Generate Final Forensic Report]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    K --> P
    P --> Q[Containment and Remediation Phase]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — TCP State Machine Relevant to Forensic Correlation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN : passive open bind
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD : receive SYN
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : send SYN+ACK, receive ACK
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : active open connect
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : receive SYN+ACK, send ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1 : active close send FIN
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : passive close receive FIN
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2 : receive ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT : receive FIN, send ACK
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : send FIN
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : receive ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : 2xMSL timeout
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A port trapped in **`SYN_SENT`** without ever reaching `ESTABLISHED` indicates a **firewall-dropped outbound** — often a sign of a misconfigured C2 callback. A port in **`CLOSE_WAIT`** with no matching application = a **leaked socket descriptor**, a common indicator of malware that crashed mid-exfiltration.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Forensic Tool Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph LR
    Root[Port Process Correlation Tools] --> Win[Windows Native]
    Root --> Lnx[Linux Native]
    Root --> Third[Third Party Cross Platform]
    Win --> W1[netstat -ano]
    Win --> W2[Get-NetTCPConnection]
    Win --> W3[tasklist]
    Win --> W4[wmic process]
    Lnx --> L1[ss -tulnp]
    Lnx --> L2[lsof -i]
    Lnx --> L3[fuser]
    Lnx --> L4[/proc net tcp parsing]
    Third --> T1[TCPView Sysinternals]
    Third --> T2[CurrPorts NirSoft]
    Third --> T3[Process Explorer Sysinternals]
    Third --> T4[Sysmon Event ID 3 Logger]
    Third --> T5[OSQuery Pack osquery]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Sysmon Pipeline

> For complex real-world deployments, a single command is insufficient. Enterprises use **Microsoft Sysmon** (a Windows kernel-level service) to **continuously log** every port-process pair to the Windows Event Log.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KERNEL_LAYER[Windows Kernel NDIS Layer]
        K1[TCP IP Driver mup]
        K2[WinSock Kernel AFD]
        K3[Minifilter SysmonDrv]
    end
    subgraph USERSpace_LAYER[User Mode Sysmon Service]
        U1[Sysmon Service exe]
        U2[Event Log XML Formatter]
    end
    subgraph SIEM_LAYER[Downstream SIEM Stack]
        S1[Windows Event Collector WEC]
        S2[Splunk or Elastic Indexer]
        S3[Detection Rule Engine]
        S4[Analyst Dashboard]
    end
    K1 --> K3
    K2 --> K3
    K3 --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
```

> Each Sysmon **Event ID 3 (Network Connection)** record contains: `ProcessId, ProcessGuid, Image, User, Protocol, Initiated, SourceIsIpv6, SourceIp, SourcePort, DestinationIp, DestinationPort` — a complete port-process correlation tuple ready for SIEM correlation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECCT741]**

### Q1. Define an *open port* and explain how it differs from an *active connection* in the context of live forensic analysis.

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 per concept):**

An **open port** (technically a *listening socket*) is a Transport Layer endpoint bound by a process that is willing to **accept** incoming TCP connections or **receive** UDP datagrams; in `netstat` output, its state column reads `LISTENING`. An **active connection** is a fully established TCP session in the `ESTABLISHED` state, indicating an ongoing bidirectional data exchange between the local and foreign endpoints. Forensically, an open port signals "a service is reachable from the network," whereas an active connection signals "a service is currently communicating with a peer." Both must be correlated to their owning PID, but the **foreign address** is the high-yield indicator on active connections.

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain any three Windows utilities used to correlate open ports with running processes.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per utility):**

1. **`netstat -ano`** — Displays all connections and listening ports numerically with the owning Process ID; the `-b` switch additionally reveals the executable name (requires elevation).
2. **`Get-NetTCPConnection` (PowerShell cmdlet)** — A modern, pipeline-friendly equivalent to `netstat` that returns objects with `OwningProcess`, `LocalAddress`, `LocalPort`, and `State` properties, ideal for scripting automated triage.
3. **`tasklist /FI "PID eq <PID>"`** — Resolves a discovered PID into the human-readable image name and session ID; the natural follow-up command to `netstat -ano`.
4. *(Optional credit)* **`wmic process where ProcessId=<PID> get CommandLine,ParentProcessId`** — Exposes the full command-line invocation and parent process, critical for spotting masquerading malware.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination — December 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the methodology of correlating open ports with running processes on a **Linux system**. Include the relevant commands, their flags, and the rationale for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During an incident response engagement, a Linux web server is suspected of compromise. You observe an unknown process listening on **TCP port 4444** with PID **8821**. Outline the **complete triage sequence** you would execute to identify, classify, and contain this threat. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Detailed Linux Methodology (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Enumerate all listening and active sockets with `ss`:**

```text
sudo ss -tulnp
```

Flags: `-t` (TCP), `-u` (UDP), `-l` (LISTENING), `-n` (no DNS), `-p` (process info, requires root). This is faster than legacy `netstat` because it queries the kernel via `netlink`.

**Step 2 — Use `lsof` for forensic-grade detail:**

```text
sudo lsof -i -P -n
```

The `-P -n` combination **disables port-to-name and IP-to-DNS resolution**, which is critical because the compromised host's DNS may be hijacked — relying on reverse lookups would mislead the analyst.

**Step 3 — Targeted port query:**

```text
sudo lsof -i :4444
```

Isolates the suspect socket.

**Step 4 — Filter by command name:**

```text
sudo lsof -c <process_name> -i
```

Answers the question: *"Does this binary have any other network endpoints?"* — essential for spotting multi-port C2 frameworks.

**Step 5 — Identify the binary's location and integrity:**

```text
ls -la /proc/8821/exe
readlink /proc/8821/exe
```

The `exe` symlink resolves to the on-disk path of the binary; if it points to `/tmp/`, `/dev/shm/`, or a hidden dot-file directory, it is **almost certainly malware**.

**Step 6 — Inspect command-line arguments and environment:**

```text
cat /proc/8821/cmdline
cat /proc/8821/environ
```

**Step 7 — Check for deleted binary (a classic anti-forensic trick):**

```text
sudo lsof -p 8821 | grep deleted
```

If the binary has been unlinked from disk but the process is still running, it persists in memory — the `maps` and `exe` symlinks will show `(deleted)` and the file must be recovered from the `/proc/<PID>/mem` memory image.

**Step 8 — Build a complete socket table from kernel source-of-truth:**

```text
cat /proc/net/tcp
cat /proc/net/tcp6
```

These contain raw kernel structures including the `inode` field used to cross-reference open file descriptors in `/proc/<PID>/fd/`.

---

**(b) Triage Sequence for PID 8821 on Port 4444 (7 marks):**

| Step | Command | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `sudo lsof -i :4444` | Confirm PID 8821 owns the listening socket. |
| 2 | `sudo ls -la /proc/8821/exe` | Locate on-disk binary path. |
| 3 | `sudo cat /proc/8821/cmdline \| tr '\0' ' '` | Read full command-line arguments. |
| 4 | `sudo cat /proc/8821/status` | Inspect UID, PPID, state, threads. |
| 5 | `ps -o pid,ppid,user,etime,cmd -p 8821` | Establish process age and parent. |
| 6 | `sudo lsof -p 8821` | List all open files, libraries, sockets. |
| 7 | `sudo cat /proc/8821/maps` | Dump memory-mapped libraries; reveal packed or unsigned DLLs. |
| 8 | `sha256sum /path/to/binary` | Generate hash for threat-intel lookup. |
| 9 | Upload hash to **VirusTotal** and **MalwareBazaar** | Classify as known/unknown malware family. |
| 10 | `sudo ss -tnp dst :4444` | Identify the **foreign peer** initiating the connection. |
| 11 | `sudo iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 4444 -j DROP` | **Containment** — block further connections. |
| 12 | `sudo kill -STOP 8821` | **Suspension** — freeze the process for memory acquisition. |
| 13 | `sudo avml /tmp/8821.mem` or `sudo LiME` | **Memory dump** for later Volatility analysis. |
| 14 | `sudo kill -9 8821` | **Termination** — only after evidence preservation. |

**Examiner's verdict-tracking note:** A complete answer must show **evidence preservation BEFORE termination** and **containment BEFORE in-depth analysis** — the candidate who reverses this order loses a full mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast the Windows utilities `netstat`, `Get-NetTCPConnection`, and `TCPView` (Sysinternals) for port-process correlation. Discuss the advantages and forensic limitations of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the aid of a suitable diagram, describe the **Sysmon Event ID 3** logging pipeline and explain why it is considered the **gold standard** for continuous port-process correlation in enterprise environments. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Feature | `netstat -ano` | `Get-NetTCPConnection` | `TCPView` (Sysinternals) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Built-in to Windows** | Yes (all versions) | Yes (Win 8+ / Server 2012+) | No (third-party, ~600 KB) |
| **Shows PID** | Yes (`-o`) | Yes (`OwningProcess` property) | Yes (color-coded) |
| **Shows image name** | Only with `-b` (admin) | Via pipeline to `Get-Process` | Yes, real-time GUI |
| **DNS resolution cost** | Default: ON (slow). Off with `-n` | Default: OFF (fast) | Configurable |
| **GUI** | No (text only) | No (PowerShell objects) | Yes — color codes ESTABLISHED red, LISTEN green |
| **Remote capable** | No (local only) | Yes (`-CimSession`) | No (local only) |
| **Loggable** | Pipe to `clip` or file | Native object pipeline → CSV | GUI export to `.tsv` |
| **Persistent monitoring** | One-shot | One-shot (but scriptable in loop) | Continuous real-time |
| **Forensic limitation** | Cannot show parent process or hash | Cannot show full command line directly | Cannot run on a non-Windows host |
| **Best use case** | Quick ad-hoc check | Scripted automation, IR playbooks | Live analyst triage, demos |

**Synthesis (1 bonus mark in KTU marking):** In an enterprise IR playbook, the **best practice is to chain them** — use Sysmon for continuous logging, `Get-NetTCPConnection` for scripted real-time queries, and TCPView for visual analyst review.

---

**(b) Sysmon Event ID 3 Pipeline (7 marks):**

Sysmon (System Monitor), part of the **Sysinternals Suite** by Mark Russinovich, installs as a **kernel device driver** (`SysmonDrv.sys`) and a user-mode service (`Sysmon.exe`). It hooks into the **Network Driver Interface Specification (NDIS)** layer to intercept every TCP/UDP connect/bind operation before it reaches the wire.

**Pipeline stages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1:} \quad & \text{Application calls connect() or bind() in WinSock.} \\
\text{Stage 2:} \quad & \text{AFD.SYS passes the request to the TCP/IP driver (tcpip.sys).} \\
\text{Stage 3:} \quad & \text{Sysmon's minifilter driver inspects the IRP and enriches it with} \\
                       & \text{ProcessId, ImagePath, User SID, and ParentProcessId.} \\
\text{Stage 4:} \quad & \text{Enriched record is forwarded to the user-mode Sysmon service.} \\
\text{Stage 5:} \quad & \text{Sysmon formats the record as an XML event and writes to} \\
                       & \text{``Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational'' channel (Event ID 3).} \\
\text{Stage 6:} \quad & \text{Windows Event Collector (WEC) forwards to a central SIEM.} \\
\text{Stage 7:} \quad & \text{SIEM correlates ProcessId with file-write, registry, and DNS events} \\
                       & \text{to reconstruct the full kill-chain.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why gold standard:**
- **Kernel-level visibility** — cannot be bypassed by userland API hooks.
- **Tamper-resistant** — event log is protected; attackers must unload the driver to hide.
- **Enriched metadata** — every event has hash, parent PID, user, and command line.
- **Retentive** — can be configured with a 10 MB+ event log and shipped off-host for forensics.
- **Sigma-rule compatible** — community detection rules map directly to Sysmon fields.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `-n` in `netstat` / `lsof`.** Students who omit the no-DNS flag lose 1 mark for not recognizing the **forensic anti-poisoning** rationale.
> 2. **Reporting `LISTENING` PID 4 as malware.** PID 4 is the Windows `System` process — it legitimately owns many kernel sockets. Penalize for lack of process-tree awareness.
> 3. **Killing the malicious process before memory acquisition.** This destroys volatile evidence (encryption keys, C2 URLs, injected code). KTU explicitly tests evidence-handling order.
> 4. **Confusing `ss` and `netstat` flags on Linux.** `ss` does **not** have a `-p` PID column without root; writing the command without `sudo` is incomplete.
> 5. **Mixing up ephemeral port direction.** Outbound traffic uses **high ephemeral local ports** (49152+) — a high port in `ESTABLISHED` is the *client*, not the *server*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Definition:** Port-process correlation = mapping every listening/active socket to its owning PID and executable.
- **Default Windows command:** `netstat -ano` (add `-b` for binary name, add `-p tcp` for protocol filter).
- **Default PowerShell command:** `Get-NetTCPConnection | Select-Object LocalPort, OwningProcess, State`.
- **Default Linux command:** `ss -tulnp` (modern) or `netstat -tulnp` (legacy) or `lsof -i -P -n` (forensic).
- **PID-to-binary resolution:** Linux → `readlink /proc/<PID>/exe` ; Windows → `tasklist /FI "PID eq <PID>"` or `Get-Process -Id <PID>`.
- **Foreign Address = Direction:** Outbound to a non-corporate IP on a high port = C2 beacon. Inbound from internet on a listening port = exposed service.
- **The four forensic-critical TCP states:** `LISTENING` (service), `ESTABLISHED` (live session), `CLOSE_WAIT` (leaked socket), `TIME_WAIT` (recent termination).
- **Ephemeral port ranges to memorize:** Windows **49152–65535** ; Linux **32768–60999**.
- **Gold-standard tool for persistence:** Sysmon Event ID 3 (continuous kernel-level logging).
- **Standard forensic triage order:** **Enumerate → Correlate → Identify → Memory Dump → Contain → Terminate.** Reversing this order loses marks.
- **Memory-artifact companions:** After port-process correlation, always follow with **Volatility's `malfind`, `netscan`, and `pslist`** plugins to confirm in-memory presence.
- **The single most-common malware port:** **4444** (Metasploit default), followed by **1337, 8888, 8080, 9001** (Tor).
- **Three commands to know cold:** `netstat -ano`, `lsof -i -P -n`, `ss -tulnp`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identifying Services and Drivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identifying Services and Drivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Malware Forensics**, *Identifying Services and Drivers* refers to the systematic process of enumerating, analyzing, and correlating the **Windows Service Control Manager (SCM) database**, the **Registry hive** (`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services`), and the **loaded kernel modules** to discover anomalous, unauthorized, or malicious background processes. 

A **Windows Service** is a long-running executable that performs specific functions and is managed by the **Service Control Manager (SCM)** — a core component of the Windows Operating System that handles service lifecycle operations (start, stop, pause, continue). Services execute outside the scope of an interactive user session, making them a favored persistence vector for malware.

A **Kernel Driver** (often a `.sys` file) is a privileged, ring-0 software component loaded into the Windows kernel to facilitate low-level hardware or system interactions. Drivers can be **boot-start**, **system-start**, or **auto-start**, and are catalogued in the **DriverStore** and the `Services` registry hive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Module 3 syllabus explicitly requires students to identify services and drivers using **Volatility plugins** (`svcscan`, `driverscan`, `modscan`), the **`sc query`** command, the **Windows Registry**, and **live forensic tools** (Process Explorer, Autoruns).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Windows Operating System as a large **corporate office building**:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Forensic Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Service** | A background employee who works 24/7, even when no manager is present. They have an ID card (Service Name) and a job description (Display Name). | Malware hires itself as a "background employee" so it can run persistently without the user noticing. |
| **Kernel Driver** | A specialized technician with master-key access to electrical rooms and server racks (kernel ring-0). | Malware posing as a technician can intercept raw data, hide processes, and disable security software. |
| **Service Control Manager (SCM)** | The HR Department that hires, fires, and maintains records of all background employees. | The SCM database (`services.exe`) is the authoritative ledger forensic analysts must audit. |
| **Registry `Services` Key** | The company's personnel file cabinet. | Every service and driver must register a profile here — examining this cabinet reveals unauthorized hires. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** When a forensic analyst says "I am identifying services and drivers," they are essentially asking: *"Which background employees and technicians are actually doing legitimate work, and which ones snuck in disguised as workers?"*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Forensic Constants

The following standard Windows metrics are used throughout the analysis:

- **Kernel Ring Levels:** Ring 0 (Kernel Mode) and Ring 3 (User Mode).
- **Service Start Types:** `0` (Boot), `1` (System), `2` (Automatic), `3` (Manual), `4` (Disabled).
- **Service Type Flags:** `0x1` (Kernel Driver), `0x2` (File System Driver), `0x10` (Win32 Own Process), `0x20` (Win32 Share Process).
- **Standard Registry Path:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<ServiceName>`.
- **Driver Location Standard:** `%SystemRoot%\System32\drivers\*.sys`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The Service Identification Pipeline

The forensic identification of services follows a structured 5-stage logic chain. Each stage is a deliberate decision point in the analysis workflow:

- **Stage 1 — SCM Database Enumeration:** The analyst queries the live SCM using `sc query`, `sc queryex`, or in-memory structures via Volatility's `svcscan` plugin. This produces a flat list of registered services with their **PID, State, and Start Type**.

- **Stage 2 — Registry Cross-Verification:** The list from Stage 1 is cross-referenced with the `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services` hive. The forensic goal is to detect **registry tampering**, where a malware's service entry is hidden, or where the `ImagePath` is pointing to a non-standard directory (e.g., `%AppData%`).

- **Stage 3 — Binary Validation (Hashing):** The `ImagePath` registered in the registry is dereferenced to a physical file on disk. The analyst computes the **SHA-256 hash** of the binary and checks it against:
  * The Microsoft catalog of signed binaries.
  * Internal threat intelligence feeds.
  * VirusTotal / malware sandbox databases.

- **Stage 4 — Driver Identification:** Drivers are enumerated separately using `driverscan` (Volatility), `modscan`, or the live `fltmc` and `driverquery` commands. The analyst maps each loaded `.sys` file back to its publisher signature.

- **Stage 5 — Anomaly Triage:** Findings are scored using the *Malware Indicator Heuristic Formula* (see Section 2.2). Indicators such as missing signatures, unusual parent processes, and unsigned binaries elevate the suspicion score.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Heuristic Table

The following table summarizes the core forensic decision rules, start-type constants, and scoring formulas used during service and driver identification.

| Element | Definition / Formula | Forensic Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Service Start Type (DWORD)** | `0` = Boot, `1` = System, `2` = Auto, `3` = Manual, `4` = Disabled | Determines *when* a malicious service is launched. Auto-start (2) services are the most dangerous. |
| **Service Type (DWORD)** | `0x01` = KernelDriver, `0x02` = FileSystemDriver, `0x10` = Win32Own, `0x20` = Win32Share | Differentiates user-mode services from kernel-mode drivers. |
| **Malware Suspicion Score (MSS)** | $MSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot I_i$ | Weighted sum of behavioral indicators. $w_i$ is the indicator weight, $I_i$ is 0 or 1 (absent/present). |
| **Signature Trust Score (STS)** | $STS = 1$ if signed by valid Authenticode certificate, $0$ otherwise | Unsigned drivers (STS = 0) executing in kernel mode is a critical IOC. |
| **Path Deviation Score (PDS)** | $PDS = 1 - \frac{\text{Standard Path Matches}}{\text{Total Executables}}$ | Higher score means the binary lives outside `%SystemRoot%`, indicating potential tampering. |
| **Volatility Plugin** | `svcscan`, `driverscan`, `modscan` | In-memory forensic enumeration commands. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world SOC (Security Operations Center) environments, the **MSS heuristic** is the foundation of EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) tools like CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, and Microsoft Defender for Endpoint. The same logic you are studying in KTU Module 3 runs at machine speed on millions of endpoints globally.

## 2.3 Why This Analysis is Critical in Malware Forensics

- **Persistence Mechanism:** Over **60% of modern malware families** (e.g., Conti, LockBit, BlackCat) install themselves as Windows services to survive reboots.
- **Defense Evasion:** By registering as a **kernel driver**, rootkits (e.g., TDL4, Necurs) can intercept system calls and hide their processes from Task Manager.
- **Privilege Escalation:** A malicious driver loaded at boot time (Start Type 0) executes before any antivirus product, guaranteeing its survival.
- **Lateral Movement:** Stolen or forged driver-signing certificates (e.g., the 2020 *DigiCert* and *Symantec* breaches) allow malware to bypass Microsoft's Driver Signature Enforcement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Service & Driver Identification Workflow

## 3.1 Exhaustive Methodology — Live System Forensics

The following is the step-by-step forensic procedure expected in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) answers. Each step is given in executable detail.

### Step 1: Enumerate Live Services

The analyst opens an elevated `cmd.exe` and runs:

```
sc query type= service state= all
```

The output is parsed line-by-line. The forensic analyst extracts the following fields for **each service**:

- `SERVICE_NAME` (e.g., `TermService`, `AdobeARMservice`).
- `DISPLAY_NAME` (human-readable label).
- `TYPE` (e.g., `30  WIN32`).
- `STATE` (`4  RUNNING` is the most operationally relevant).
- `PID` (process ID for live process correlation).
- `START_TYPE` (manual start, auto start, etc.).

### Step 2: Cross-Check with the Registry

The forensic analyst exports the services registry hive:

```
reg export HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services C:\evidence\services.reg
```

For each suspicious service, the analyst examines the following registry values:

- `ImagePath` — e.g., `C:\Users\victim\AppData\Roaming\update.exe`. **Red flag:** the path is in user-space, not system-space.
- `ObjectName` — e.g., `LocalSystem`. Malware often abuses `LocalSystem` privileges.
- `Start` — DWORD value matching one of the constants in Section 2.2.
- `Type` — DWORD distinguishing user-mode from kernel-mode.
- `DisplayName` — may masquerade as a legitimate service (e.g., `"Windows Update Service"`).

### Step 3: Validate the Binary Path

The `ImagePath` is dereferenced to the actual file. The analyst performs:

- **Hash Calculation:** $H = \text{SHA-256}(\text{binary})$.
- **Digital Signature Verification:** Run `sigcheck64.exe -e -s -r C:\Path\To\Binary.exe` (Sysinternals).
- **Publisher Lookup:** Cross-reference with the Microsoft Trusted Signing Certificates list.

### Step 4: Identify Loaded Drivers

For **live driver enumeration**, the analyst uses:

```
driverquery /v /fo list
```

For **memory forensics** (post-mortem, from a memory dump), the analyst uses Volatility:

```
volatility.exe -f memory.dmp --profile=Win10x64 driverscan
volatility.exe -f memory.dmp --profile=Win10x64 modscan
```

The `driverscan` output reveals the **base address, size, service key, and name** of every driver mapped into kernel memory. The forensic analyst then maps each driver name back to its `Services` registry entry.

### Step 5: Triage and Indicator Scoring

Using the **Malware Suspicion Score (MSS)** formula from Section 2.2, each candidate service/driver is scored:

$$
MSS = w_1 \cdot I_{\text{unsigned}} + w_2 \cdot I_{\text{non-system path}} + w_3 \cdot I_{\text{hidden parent}} + w_4 \cdot I_{\text{auto-start}} + w_5 \cdot I_{\text{known IOC}}
$$

Forensic weight assignments (typical industry practice):

$$
w_1 = 0.30 \quad w_2 = 0.25 \quad w_3 = 0.15 \quad w_4 = 0.10 \quad w_5 = 0.20
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 1.00
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:** Any service/driver with $MSS \geq 0.60$ is treated as a **High-Confidence Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** and is escalated to malware sandboxing for dynamic analysis.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Volatility Plugin Output Interpretation

When a KTU question asks: *"Explain the output of `volatility svcscan`"*, the expected analytical breakdown is:

| Volatility Column | Forensic Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `Offset` | Virtual memory offset of the `_SERVICE_RECORD` structure. |
| `Name` | Symbolic name of the service (e.g., `svchost.exe` wrapper). |
| `Display Name` | Human-readable name shown in `services.msc`. |
| `Service Type` | Decimal value corresponding to the type flags. |
| `Start` | Decimal value of the start type (0–4). |
| `Binary Path` | Resolved path of the executable hosting the service. |

A **forensic anomaly** is declared if the `Binary Path` does not match the standard system location, or if the `Start` value is `0` (boot-start) for a non-Microsoft driver.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Windows Service & Driver Identification Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete forensic identification pipeline, from initial enumeration through final IOC scoring.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Acquisition Phase] --> B{Acquisition Type}
    B -->|Live System| C[sc query / driverquery]
    B -->|Memory Dump| D[Volatility svcscan / driverscan]
    C --> E[Enumerate Services and Drivers]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Export Registry HKLM Services Hive]
    F --> G[Cross-Reference Binary Paths]
    G --> H{Path in System32 or Program Files?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Hash Binary with SHA-256]
    H -->|No| J[Flag as Suspicious - PDS equals 1]
    I --> K{Authenticode Signature Valid?}
    J --> K
    K -->|Yes| L[Add to Baseline Legitimate List]
    K -->|No| M[Flag Unsigned - STS equals 0]
    M --> N[Compute MSS Score]
    L --> N
    J --> N
    N --> O{MSS greater than or equal to 0.60}
    O -->|Yes| P[ESCALATE: High Confidence IoC]
    O -->|No| Q[Log as Low Risk Anomaly]
    P --> R[Submit to Sandbox for Dynamic Analysis]
    Q --> S[End: Document Findings]
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Windows Service Architecture (User-Mode vs. Kernel-Mode)

The following block-level functional architecture diagram clarifies how the Service Control Manager (SCM), services, and drivers interact within the Windows OS architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USERMODE["USER MODE - Ring 3"]
        SC1[Service Control Manager - services.exe]
        SC2[Svchost.exe - Generic Host]
        SC3[Standalone Service Executable]
        SC1 --> SC2
        SC1 --> SC3
    end
    subgraph KERNELMODE["KERNEL MODE - Ring 0"]
        KM1[Windows Kernel - ntoskrnl.exe]
        KM2[Kernel Driver - .sys file]
        KM3[File System Driver - FltMgr]
        KM1 --> KM2
        KM1 --> KM3
    end
    USERMODE --> KERNELMODE
    SC1 -->|System Call Interface| KM1
    SC2 -->|IOCTL Request| KM2
```

## 4.3 Driver Loading Sequence (Forensic Timeline)

The following diagram illustrates the chronological loading of drivers during system boot — a critical timeline for forensic investigators.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Boot Loader] --> P2[Phase 2: Kernel Initialization]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: PnP Manager Enumeration]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: SCM Database Read]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Auto-Start Services Launched]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: User Logon]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: User-Triggered Manual Services]
    P1 -.Forensic Marker.-> M1[Boot-Start Driver 0]
    P2 -.Forensic Marker.-> M2[System-Start Driver 1]
    P5 -.Forensic Marker.-> M3[Auto-Start Driver 2]
    P7 -.Forensic Marker.-> M4[Manual-Start Driver 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The numbering `0`, `1`, `2`, `3` in the markers corresponds exactly to the registry `Start` DWORD values listed in the Section 2.2 cheat sheet.
