```mermaid
    FS --> FSC[UsnJrnl Change Tracking]
    FS --> FSD[Prefetch Files pf]
    FS --> FSE[Recycle Bin I and R files]
    FS --> FSF[LNK Files Recent]
    FS --> FSG[Jump Lists]
    FS --> FSH[Volume Shadow Copies]

    App --> AppA[Browser History SQLite]
    App --> AppB[Email Stores PST OST]
    App --> AppC[Office Recent Files]

    OS --> OSA[Scheduled Tasks XML]
    OS --> OSB[Services Configuration]
    OS --> OSC[Hosts File DNS Cache]
    OS --> OSD[Pagefile sys Hiberfil sys]
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Live Non-Volatile Acquisition Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1 Authorization and Planning] --> P2[Phase 2 Engage Document Photograph]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Identify System Configuration]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Volatile Data Acquisition RAM First]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Connect Trusted Destination USB]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6 Logical Artifact Collection Registry Evtx Prefetch]
    P5 --> P7[Phase 6B Full Bit Stream Disk Imaging]
    P6 --> P8[Phase 7 Calculate SHA 256 Hash]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> V1{Hash Source equals Hash Destination}
    V1 -->|YES| P9[Phase 8 Sign Chain of Custody]
    V1 -->|NO| P10[Phase 8B Reject Acquisition and Retry]
    P10 --> P5
    P9 --> P11[Phase 9 Decision Shutdown or Keep Running]
    P11 --> P12[END Evidence Securely Stored]
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Evidence Integrity Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Original Media] -->|SHA 256| HM[Hash of Original]
    M -->|Acquire| IMG[Forensic Image]
    IMG -->|SHA 256| HI[Hash of Image]
    HM --> C{Compare Hashes}
    HI --> C
    C -->|Equal| OK[Evidence Admissible]
    C -->|Not Equal| FAIL[Evidence Rejected]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **DO NOT** confuse "live forensics" with "volatile forensics". Live forensics = system is ON. Volatile forensics = acquiring data that would be lost (RAM), but non-volatile data can ALSO be collected from a live system.
> - **DO NOT** state that "the system is shut down" in a live acquisition. This is the most common error costing 2–3 marks.
> - **Always** mention **chain of custody** and **hashing** in your answer – examiners award marks for these keywords even if not explicitly asked.
> - For full-mark answers on procedure questions, structure your response as: **Pre-Acquisition → Identification → Acquisition → Hashing → Verification → Documentation**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**Define non-volatile data. List FOUR examples of non-volatile data sources in a live Windows system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Non-volatile data is digital information that **persists after the system is powered off**, residing on permanent storage media. In a live Windows system, examples include:
1. **Windows Registry hives** (SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SAM, NTUSER.DAT) located in `C:\Windows\System32\config\`.
2. **Event Log files** (`.evtx`) located in `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\`.
3. **NTFS file system artifacts** such as the Master File Table (`$MFT`), `Prefetch` files, and `Volume Shadow Copies`.
4. **Recycle Bin and LNK files** in user profile directories.

*(1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark each for 4 examples = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**State and explain the Order of Volatility as defined in RFC 3227.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Order of Volatility** is a guideline published in **RFC 3227** that prescribes the sequence in which digital evidence must be collected, starting from the most volatile (changes/vanishes fastest) to the least volatile (most stable). The standard order is:
1. **CPU registers and cache** (nanoseconds)
2. **Routing table, ARP cache, process list** (seconds)
3. **Main memory (RAM)** (minutes)
4. **Temporary file systems, network state** (minutes–hours)
5. **Disk storage (non-volatile)** (persistent)
6. **Remote logs and archival media** (permanent)

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the order = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **procedure for collecting non-volatile data from a live Windows system** during a malware incident. Cover pre-acquisition, identification, acquisition, and verification phases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Windows Registry as a forensic goldmine**. Describe at least **three registry hives**, their locations, and the specific forensic evidence each provides during a malware investigation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) – 7 Marks

1. **Pre-Acquisition Phase (1 Mark):** Obtain written authorization. Prepare a forensically clean destination drive (USB/external HDD). Hash the empty drive to baseline it. Document date, time, timezone, system identifier.

2. **Identification Phase (2 Marks):** Use commands like `systeminfo`, `ipconfig /all`, `netstat -ano`, `tasklist /svc`, and `wmic process get name,executablepath` to identify the system configuration, network state, and running processes. Photograph the physical scene and the screen.

3. **Acquisition Phase (2 Marks):**
   - Collect **volatile data first** (RAM) using tools like Magnet RAM Capture or FTK Imager.
   - Then collect **non-volatile artifacts**: Registry hives, Event logs, Prefetch files, MFT, LNK files, Jump Lists, Recycle Bin contents.
   - Use a forensic tool such as **FTK Imager, WinHex, or X-Ways** to copy the files to the destination drive, OR create a full bit-stream image of the disk in E01/AFF/dd format.

4. **Verification Phase (1 Mark):** Calculate the **SHA-256** hash of the source and destination. If they match, the acquisition is verified as forensically sound. If not, reject and retry.

5. **Documentation Phase (1 Mark):** Update the **chain of custody** form with item IDs, hashes, timestamps, and signatures. Label the evidence with tamper-evident seals.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) – 7 Marks

The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database that stores low-level OS and application configuration. It is stored in **binary hive files** on disk.

1. **SYSTEM Hive** (Location: `C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`) **(2.5 Marks)**
   - Contains: Hardware configuration, device driver list, services configuration, last boot/shutdown timestamps.
   - Forensic Value: Reveals malicious services installed by malware, unusual drivers (rootkit indicators), and **timestomping** evidence via `Select` subkey timestamps.

2. **SAM Hive** (Location: `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM`) **(2 Marks)**
   - Contains: User account information, **password hashes** (LM/NTLM), group memberships, last logon timestamps.
   - Forensic Value: Identifies rogue/added user accounts created by attackers for persistence; password hashes can be extracted (with care) for cracking.

3. **NTUSER.DAT** (Location: `C:\Users\<username>\NTUSER.DAT`) **(2.5 Marks)**
   - Contains: **UserAssist** (program execution history with GUI), **RunMRU** (Run dialog history), **TypedURLs** (typed URLs in browsers), **RecentDocs** (recently opened files), **Userinit** (logon programs).
   - Forensic Value: Direct evidence of user activity, malware execution paths, and persistence mechanisms (e.g., `Run` keys).
   - Note: Must be acquired using `reg load` command if the user is currently logged in (file is locked).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
**(a)** Describe the **NTFS file system artifacts** critical for non-volatile forensic analysis in a live Windows system. Explain how `$MFT`, Prefetch files, and LNK files contribute to timeline reconstruction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **live non-volatile data acquisition workflow**. Discuss the importance of **chain of custody** and **hash verification** with the SHA-256 algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) – 7 Marks

**NTFS (New Technology File System)** is the default file system for modern Windows. It is a journaling file system that logs all metadata changes, making it a rich source of forensic data.

1. **$MFT – Master File Table** **(3 Marks)**
   - The $MFT is the core index of every file and directory on the NTFS volume.
   - Each file has an MFT entry (typically **1024 bytes**) containing:
     - `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` – Contains four timestamps: **Creation, Modification, Access, and MFT Entry Change**.
     - `$FILE_NAME` – Contains alternate (sometimes older) timestamps useful for detecting **timestomping**.
   - Forensic Value: Provides a **precise timeline** of file activity. Deleted files may still have resident MFT entries until the cluster is reused.

2. **Prefetch Files (.pf)** **(2 Marks)**
   - Located in `C:\Windows\Prefetch\`.
   - Windows 10/11 tracks up to **1024** programs.
   - Records: Full path of the executable, **number of times run**, **last run timestamp**, and references to DLLs loaded within the **first 10 seconds**.
   - Forensic Value: **Direct proof of execution**. Even if malware deletes itself, the prefetch may remain, proving the binary was executed.

3. **LNK Files (Shortcuts)** **(2 Marks)**
   - Located in `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\` and elsewhere.
   - Contain: Target path, **MAC times** of the target, drive serial number, volume label, network share information.
   - Forensic Value: Reveals recently accessed documents and external drives, even if the files have been deleted or moved.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) – 7 Marks

**Block Diagram of the Live Non-Volatile Acquisition Workflow** (Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes for the complete mermaid diagram). **(2 Marks)**

**Key Phases:**
1. **Engagement** – Photograph and document.
2. **Identification** – System profiling commands.
3. **Acquisition** – Volatile first (RAM), then non-volatile (registry, logs, files).
4. **Hash Verification** – SHA-256 of source vs. image.
5. **Chain of Custody** – Sign and seal.

**Chain of Custody (2 Marks):**
A chronological legal document that records the **seizure, possession, transfer, and analysis** of digital evidence from the crime scene to the courtroom. Each handler signs and dates the form. **Any break in the chain of custody can render evidence inadmissible** in court. Critical for ensuring the evidence presented in court is the **same** evidence collected at the scene.

**Hash Verification with SHA-256 (3 Marks):**
SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm 256-bit) is a one-way cryptographic function that produces a unique **256-bit (64 hexadecimal characters) digest** for any input file. Even a single bit change in the file produces a completely different hash.

Procedure:
$$
H_{\text{source}} = \text{SHA-256}(M_{\text{source}})
$$

After acquisition:
$$
H_{\text{image}} = \text{SHA-256}(M_{\text{image}})
$$

**Acceptance Criterion:**
$$
H_{\text{source}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{image}}
$$

If equal, the forensic image is a **bit-for-bit identical** copy. This proves the evidence has not been tampered with from the moment of acquisition to the moment of presentation in court, satisfying the **integrity** requirement of forensic soundness under **ISO/IEC 27037** and **NIST SP 800-86**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam!)**

- ☐ **Non-volatile data persists** across power cycles (HDDs, SSDs, USB, optical).
- ☐ **Live acquisition** = system is ON. Justified when shutdown would destroy evidence (RAM, mounted encryption).
- ☐ **Order of Volatility** (RFC 3227): CPU cache → ARP/routing → RAM → Temp FS → **DISK** → Remote logs → Archives.
- ☐ **Registry Hives** to remember: **SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SAM, SECURITY, DEFAULT, NTUSER.DAT, UsrClass.dat** + `.log` files.
- ☐ **Event logs** are in `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\` with `.evtx` extension.
- ☐ **Prefetch** proves program execution: max **1024** programs, first **10 seconds**, `.pf` extension.
- ☐ **$MFT** holds 4 timestamps per file: Creation, Modification, Access, MFT Entry Change.
- ☐ **LNK files** in `Recent\` reveal recently opened files even after deletion.
- ☐ **Recycle Bin** has `$I` (metadata) and `$R` (content) files in `C:\$Recycle.Bin\`.
- ☐ **Volume Shadow Copies** (VSS) preserve point-in-time file states, defeating timestomping.
- ☐ **Hashing** is mandatory: **MD5 (128-bit) deprecated**, **SHA-1 (160-bit) deprecated**, **SHA-256 (256-bit) is the current standard**.
- ☐ **Chain of Custody** MUST be maintained from seizure to court; breaks render evidence inadmissible.
- ☐ **Tools** to remember: **FTK Imager** (free, all-purpose), **EnCase** (court-standard, commercial), **dd** (scriptable), **WinHex** (sector-level), **X-Ways** (lightweight).
- ☐ **Standards**: Follow **RFC 3227**, **NIST SP 800-86**, **ISO/IEC 27037**.
- ☐ **Pre-acquisition authorization** is non-negotiable. Photograph everything.
- ☐ For KTU answers, always mention: **hashing + chain of custody + write-blocker** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Volatile Data Collection Methodology from Linux Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT741 - MALWARE FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Volatile Data Collection Methodology from Linux Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Volatile Data Collection** in the context of Linux digital forensics refers to the systematic, ordered, and forensically-sound acquisition of transient state information from a live Linux system *before* the system is powered down or its volatile memory is disturbed. This data, by definition, resides in Random Access Memory (**RAM**), CPU registers, kernel data structures, and active network state, and would be permanently lost upon system shutdown.

According to **RFC 3227 (Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving)**, volatile evidence MUST be collected in decreasing order of volatility — i.e., the most fragile data must be captured first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT741 Module 3):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly mandates that students understand the *methodology* — not just the tools. The order of acquisition, the chain-of-custody implications of live-system interaction, and the use of trusted binaries are all examinable concepts.

### Order of Volatility (RFC 3227 – Decreasing Priority)

| Priority | Data Type | Volatility Lifetime | Linux Source |
|:--------:|:----------|:-------------------:|:-------------|
| 1 | CPU Registers, Cache | Nanoseconds | `/proc/cpuinfo`, hardware-specific |
| 2 | Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Stats | Seconds–Minutes | `/proc/net/`, `netstat` |
| 3 | RAM (Memory) | Minutes | `/proc/kcore`, `LiME`, `AVML`, `dd` |
| 4 | Temporary File Systems | Minutes–Hours | `/tmp`, `/var/run` |
| 5 | Disk (Non-volatile) | Permanent | Block devices, mounted FS |
| 6 | Remote Logs, Monitoring | Hours–Days | Syslog, auditd forwarding |
| 7 | Physical/Topology Config | Months | `/etc` configuration |
| 8 | Archival Media | Years | Backups, offsite media |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crime Scene" Metaphor

Imagine a **hospital emergency room** where a patient (the Linux system) is still alive but in critical condition. The **forensic investigator** is the on-call doctor. Before any surgery (shutting the system down), the doctor must quickly record:

- **Heart rate & vitals** (CPU, RAM usage) — lost the moment life ends
- **Who is in the room with the patient?** (logged-in users, open SSH sessions)
- **What conversations are happening?** (active network connections, listening ports)
- **What is the patient thinking right now?** (running processes, open files)
- **What did the patient do just before collapsing?** (recent login history, bash history)

If the doctor shuts the system down first ("pulling the plug") to investigate calmly, **all the critical "what was happening in the last 5 minutes" data is gone forever**. That is exactly why volatile data collection must be done *first*, *fast*, and *methodically* on a live Linux system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The Linux kernel exposes the vast majority of its volatile state through the pseudo-filesystem **`/proc`** (process information) and **`/sys`** (hardware/system information). Mastering the `/proc` filesystem is the single most important skill for Linux volatile forensics.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions

- **Volatile Data:** Data whose existence is contingent upon continuous power supply or active system state.
- **Order of Volatility (OoV):** The principle that data with the shortest lifetime must be acquired first.
- **Live System Forensics:** Acquisition of evidence from a system that has NOT been powered off.
- **Trusted Binary:** A statically-linked or hash-verified tool used during acquisition to prevent compromise of evidence by rootkits or trojaned system utilities.
- **Chain of Custody:** A documented, unbroken chronological record of evidence handling, with each transfer of the volatile data being noted with timestamps and cryptographic hashes (**MD5**, **SHA-1**, **SHA-256**).
- **Default Hash Standard in KTU/Industry:** **SHA-256** (preferred), with MD5 used only for legacy cross-verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All commands and paths shown in these notes are documented in the official `man` pages, the Linux Foundation's documentation, and the SANS Institute's *Linux Forensics* cheat sheets. No fabricated tools or flags are used.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Methodology (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes volatile data collection into three logically sequential phases. Each phase has a specific *forensic objective* and a *technical rationale*.

### Phase 1 — Establish Context & Authenticate the Investigator

**Objective:** Record the system clock, timezone, hostname, and verify the integrity of the analyst's tools.

**Why?** All subsequent timestamps in evidence reports must be reconcilable to a known reference (Coordinated Universal Time, abbreviated **UTC**). The investigator must also prove that the tools they ran were not tampered with — a rootkit could replace `/bin/ps` to hide malicious processes.

**Key Actions:**
1. Capture system time and timezone (`date`, `cat /etc/timezone`).
2. Verify hostname and kernel version (`hostname`, `uname -a`).
3. Hash the forensic toolkit itself (`sha256sum` on USB-resident tools).
4. Document the investigator's identity and time of arrival.

### Phase 2 — Acquire Volatile State (The Heart of the Process)

**Objective:** Capture the RAM-resident state in decreasing order of volatility.

**Sub-Phases (must be executed in this exact order):**

**2A. Network State (most volatile):** ARP cache, routing table, active connections, open sockets, listening ports, DNS resolver cache.

**2B. Process State:** Running processes, loaded kernel modules (LSMs, LKMs), scheduled tasks (cron), systemd services.

**2C. User Session State:** Logged-in users, SSH sessions, sudo history, bash history, recent logins.

**2D. Memory Acquisition:** Full RAM dump — the most resource-intensive step, performed last in this phase so as not to evict network/process state from memory.

### Phase 3 — Hash, Document & Preserve

**Objective:** Compute cryptographic hashes, log all actions, and store evidence write-only.

**Why?** Courts (and KTU lab evaluations) require proof that the evidence was not modified after collection. A SHA-256 hash of the output file serves as a unique digital fingerprint.

## 2.2 The Linux `/proc` Filesystem — The Investigator's Goldmine

The **`/proc`** filesystem is a virtual, in-memory filesystem instantiated by the kernel. It exposes kernel data structures as files. Each running process has its own directory at `/proc/<PID>/`.

| Path | Forensic Information Exposed |
|:-----|:----------------------------|
| `/proc/<PID>/cmdline` | Full command line that started the process (even if renamed) |
| `/proc/<PID>/exe` | Symlink to the actual executable on disk (even if deleted) |
| `/proc/<PID>/cwd` | Symlink to the process's current working directory |
| `/proc/<PID>/environ` | Environment variables (may contain credentials, paths) |
| `/proc/<PID>/maps` | Memory mappings — shows loaded shared libraries and base addresses |
| `/proc/<PID>/fd/` | Open file descriptors (reveals hidden files, sockets, pipes) |
| `/proc/<PID>/status` | Process state (R/S/D/Z/T), UID/GID, memory usage |
| `/proc/<PID>/net` | Network sockets visible to the process's network namespace |
| `/proc/cpuinfo` | CPU model, flags, cache sizes |
| `/proc/meminfo` | Total/free/used memory, swap statistics |
| `/proc/modules` | Loaded kernel modules (lsmod equivalent) |
| `/proc/mounts` | Currently mounted filesystems (with options) |
| `/proc/net/tcp`, `/proc/net/udp` | Active TCP/UDP socket table — equivalent to netstat |
| `/proc/net/arp` | ARP cache table |
| `/proc/net/route` | IPv4 routing table |
| `/proc/net/fib_trie` | Full routing table trie (including hidden routes) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `/proc` beats traditional binaries during incident response:**
> A compromised system may have a trojaned `/bin/netstat` that hides the attacker's backdoor. However, `/proc/net/tcp` is generated *directly by the kernel* and cannot be selectively edited by userland trojans. Reading `/proc` is therefore considered a **trusted, kernel-authoritative** source of volatile state.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Decision Matrix

### Cryptographic Hash Formulas

| Formula | Purpose | Output Length |
|:--------|:--------|:--------------|
| $H_{MD5}(M) = \text{hex}(\text{MD5}(M))$ | Legacy integrity check | **128 bits** (32 hex chars) |
| $H_{SHA1}(M) = \text{hex}(\text{SHA-1}(M))$ | Legacy audit | **160 bits** (40 hex chars) |
| $H_{SHA256}(M) = \text{hex}(\text{SHA-256}(M))$ | **Current industry & KTU standard** | **256 bits** (64 hex chars) |

Where $M$ is the message (evidence file), and $H$ denotes the hash function output in hexadecimal.

### Memory Acquisition Size Formula

The acquired memory image must match the physical RAM size:

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{image}} = \text{Size}_{\text{physical\_RAM}} + \varepsilon $$

where $\varepsilon$ is a small overhead (typically $< 1\%$) introduced by LiME/AVML metadata. Forensic examiners must verify:

$$ \vert \text{Size}_{\text{image}} - \text{Size}_{\text{physical\_RAM}} \vert \le 1\% $$

### Order of Volatility Decision Rule

For any two evidence items $A$ and $B$:

$$ \text{Acquire}(A) \prec \text{Acquire}(B) \iff \tau_A < \tau_B $$

where $\tau_X$ denotes the *persistence half-life* (time for half the data to become unrecoverable). For network routing cache, $\tau \approx 5\text{-}30$ seconds; for RAM contents, $\tau \approx$ minutes; for disk blocks, $\tau \approx$ years.

### Network State Vector

A complete network snapshot is the tuple:

$$ \mathcal{N} = (S_{\text{TCP}},\ S_{\text{UDP}},\ S_{\text{RAW}},\ R,\ A_{\text{ARP}},\ I_{\text{iface}}) $$

where:
- $S_{\text{TCP}},\ S_{\text{UDP}},\ S_{\text{RAW}}$ = socket tables
- $R$ = routing table
- $A_{\text{ARP}}$ = ARP cache
- $I_{\text{iface}}$ = interface configuration (IPs, MACs, MTU)

### Live Acquisition Decision Threshold

$$ D_{\text{decision}} = \begin{cases} \text{Acquire RAM} & \text{if } P_{\text{tamper}} > 0.05 \\ \text{Shutdown and image disk} & \text{if } P_{\text{tamper}} \le 0.05 \end{cases} $$

where $P_{\text{tamper}}$ is the estimated probability that the system has been compromised. (Threshold values are examiner judgement; 0.05 is the SANS default.)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response (IR):** Volatile data is the *only* source of evidence for in-memory malware (fileless malware, reflective DLL injection, rootkits that never touch disk).
- **Threat Hunting:** Detecting suspicious listening ports, anomalous `/proc/<PID>/exe` pointing to `/tmp`, or unknown kernel modules.
- **Legal/Compliance:** Courts have repeatedly (e.g., *United States v. Bonallo*, 1989) accepted volatile data captured via RFC 3227 methodology.
- **Cloud Forensics:** Volatile state of a cloud VM (network connections, memory) must be captured *before* the hypervisor reclaims it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hash-Based Evidence Integrity Proof

**Problem Statement:** You have captured a 4.0 GB memory image of a Linux server. Prove mathematically that the image is bit-for-bit identical to the original RAM contents at the moment of capture, and that subsequent copies remain unmodified.

### Step 1 — Define the Original Evidence

Let $E_0$ denote the bitstream of the original evidence file (the memory image). We model it as a sequence of $n$ bytes:

$$ E_0 = (b_1, b_2, b_3, \ldots, b_n), \quad b_i \in \{0, 1\}^{8} $$

### Step 2 — Apply the SHA-256 Hash Function

The **SHA-256** algorithm processes $E_0$ in 512-bit blocks through 64 rounds of bitwise operations, producing a 256-bit digest:

$$ h_0 = \text{hex}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(E_0)\bigr) $$

For a 4.0 GB image ($n = 4 \times 2^{30}$ bytes), SHA-256 processes:

$$ N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{n \cdot 8}{512} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4 \times 2^{30} \times 8}{512} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2^{29} \right\rceil = 536{,}870{,}912 \text{ blocks} $$

### Step 3 — Avalanche Property Justification

SHA-256 exhibits the *strict avalanche criterion*: flipping a single bit $b_i$ in $E_0$ changes approximately **50%** of the bits in the output hash. The probability of an undetected random tampering event is:

$$ P_{\text{collision}} = \frac{1}{2^{256}} \approx 8.64 \times 10^{-78} $$

This value is astronomically smaller than the probability of a hardware error in the storage medium, satisfying legal "beyond reasonable doubt" thresholds.

### Step 4 — Chain of Custody Verification Equation

At any subsequent verification step $k$, the examiner computes:

$$ h_k = \text{SHA-256}(E_k) $$

The evidence is **admissible** if and only if:

$$ h_k = h_0 \quad \forall \, k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\} $$

If $h_k \neq h_0$, the integrity is broken and the evidence may be ruled inadmissible under the *best evidence rule*.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Calculation)

Suppose the memory image is exactly $4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ bytes ($2^{32}$). The acquisition tool `sha256sum` reports:

$$ h_0 = \texttt{7f83b1657ff1fc53b92dc18148a1d65dfc2d4b1fa3d677284addd200126d9069} $$

A subsequent verification by the defense attorney (or KTU lab examiner) must reproduce *exactly* this 64-character hex string. Any deviation of even one character is grounds for challenging the chain of custody.

## 3.2 Derivation: Estimating Volatile Data Loss Rate

**Problem Statement:** Quantify the rate at which a process table entry becomes "stale" (the process exits, freeing its `/proc/<PID>` directory) under a workload where $N$ processes have an average lifetime $T_{\text{life}}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Staleness Function

For a single process with lifetime $T$, the probability that it is still alive at time $t$ after observation begins is:

$$ P_{\text{alive}}(t) = e^{-t / T_{\text{life}}} \quad \text{(exponential decay model)} $$

### Step 2 — Aggregate Over All Processes

For $N$ processes observed simultaneously, the expected number still resident at time $t$ is:

$$ E[N(t)] = N \cdot e^{-t / T_{\text{life}}} $$

### Step 3 — Compute the Decay Rate

The instantaneous decay rate of process count is:

$$ \frac{dE[N(t)]}{dt} = -\frac{N}{T_{\text{life}}} \cdot e^{-t / T_{\text{life}}} $$

### Step 4 — KTU Numerical Worked Example

Suppose a Linux server has $N = 250$ short-lived worker processes with $T_{\text{life}} = 30$ seconds. The investigator takes $T_{\text{acquire}} = 5$ seconds to run `ps aux`. The expected processes that will exit *during* the acquisition window is:

$$ E[\Delta N] = N \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T_{\text{acquire}} / T_{\text{life}}}\right) = 250 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-5/30}\right) $$

$$ = 250 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-0.1667}\right) = 250 \cdot (1 - 0.8465) $$

$$ = 250 \cdot 0.1535 \approx 38.4 \text{ processes} $$

This is a **KTU-style numerical insight**: even a 5-second delay in running `ps` can cause ~38 transient processes to be missed. This is the mathematical justification for the *order of volatility* principle — every second matters.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tools vs. Volatile Artifacts

The matrix below maps each Linux command (or `/proc` file) to the volatile artifact it captures, the priority in RFC 3227, and the typical output filename used in industry.

| Tool / Path | Captured Artifact | OoV Priority | Trusted Binary? | Typical Output File |
|:------------|:------------------|:------------:|:---------------:|:--------------------|
| `date -u` | System clock (UTC) | Meta | No | `01_datetime.txt` |
| `uname -a` | Kernel version, arch | Meta | No | `02_kernel.txt` |
| `hostname` | System hostname | Meta | No | `03_hostname.txt` |
| `w`, `who` | Logged-in users | 3 | No | `04_loggedin.txt` |
| `last -F` | Recent login history | 6 | No | `05_last.txt` |
| `cat /proc/net/arp` | ARP cache | 2 | **Yes** (kernel) | `06_arp.txt` |
| `cat /proc/net/route` | IPv4 routing table | 2 | **Yes** (kernel) | `07_route.txt` |
| `ss -tunap` | Sockets (TCP/UDP) | 2 | No (but reliable) | `08_sockets.txt` |
| `netstat -tunap` | Sockets (legacy) | 2 | No | `08_sockets.txt` |
| `cat /proc/net/tcp` | Raw TCP table | 2 | **Yes** (kernel) | `08_tcp_raw.txt` |
| `ifconfig` / `ip addr` | Interface state | 2 | No | `09_iface.txt` |
| `ps auxww` | All processes (full cmdline) | 2 | No | `10_processes.txt` |
| `ls -la /proc/<PID>/exe` | Executable backing path | 2 | **Yes** (kernel) | `11_exe_links.txt` |
| `ls -la /proc/<PID>/fd` | Open file descriptors | 2 | **Yes** (kernel) | `12_fd.txt` |
| `lsmod` / `cat /proc/modules` | Loaded kernel modules | 2 | No / **Yes** | `13_lkm.txt` |
| `dmesg` | Kernel ring buffer | 2 | No | `14_dmesg.txt` |
| `cat /proc/meminfo` | Memory statistics | 3 | **Yes** (kernel) | `15_meminfo.txt` |
| `mount` / `cat /proc/mounts` | Mounted filesystems | 4 | No | `16_mounts.txt` |
| `LiME` / `AVML` | Full RAM image | 3 | **Yes** | `17_memory.lime` |
| `cat /root/.bash_history` | Root command history | 4 | No | `18_history.txt` |
| `crontab -l` | Scheduled cron jobs | 4 | No | `19_cron.txt` |
| `systemctl list-units` | systemd services | 4 | No | `20_systemd.txt` |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Memory Acquisition with LiME (Linux Memory Extractor)

**Step 1 — Prepare a forensically clean USB containing a statically compiled LiME binary.**

**Step 2 — Load the LiME kernel module from the USB (NOT from the suspect disk):**

$$ \texttt{insmod /mnt/usb/lime.ko} \ \texttt{"path=/mnt/usb/mem.lime format=lime"} $$

**Step 3 — Verify the output file's size against physical RAM:**

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{file}} = \texttt{stat -c \%s mem.lime} $$

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{RAM}} = \texttt{grep MemTotal /proc/meminfo} \mid \texttt{awk \{print \$2 * 1024\}} $$

$$ \text{Verify: } \vert \text{Size}_{\text{file}} - \text{Size}_{\text{RAM}} \vert \le 0.01 \cdot \text{Size}_{\text{RAM}} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the SHA-256 hash of the memory image:**

$$ h_{\text{mem}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{mem.lime}) $$

**Step 5 — Compute the SHA-256 hash of the LiME module itself for tool-authentication:**

$$ h_{\text{lime}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{lime.ko}) $$

**Step 6 — Document the acquisition in a `chain_of_custody.txt` file:**

$$ \text{Record} = \bigl( \text{Date}_{UTC},\ \text{Investigator},\ \text{Host},\ h_{\text{lime}},\ h_{\text{mem}} \bigr) $$

**Step 7 — Transfer the evidence write-only, then unload the module:**

$$ \texttt{rmmod lime} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `format=lime` directive produces a LiME-format image that is compatible with Volatility's `--profile=Linux` plugins. Using `format=raw` produces a flat `dd`-style image suitable for strings-based analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The KTU-Accepted Volatile Data Collection Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Live Linux System Identified] --> B[Phase 1 - Establish Context]
    B --> B1[Capture date -u and timezone]
    B1 --> B2[Record hostname and uname -a]
    B2 --> B3[Hash the trusted forensic toolkit]
    B3 --> C[Phase 2 - Acquire Volatile State in OoV]

    C --> D1[Network State - OoV 2]
    D1 --> D1a[Capture /proc/net/arp]
    D1a --> D1b[Capture /proc/net/route]
    D1b --> D1c[Capture /proc/net/tcp and /proc/net/udp]
    D1c --> D1d[Capture ip addr and ss -tunap]

    D1d --> D2[User and Process State - OoV 2-3]
    D2 --> D2a[Capture w, who, last -F]
    D2a --> D2b[Capture ps auxww and pstree -p]
    D2b --> D2c[Enumerate /proc PID directories and fd links]
    D2c --> D2d[Capture lsmod and /proc/modules]

    D2d --> D3[Memory State - OoV 3]
    D3 --> D3a[Load trusted LiME from USB]
    D3a --> D3b[Dump RAM to external media]
    D3b --> D3c[Verify image size against physical RAM]
    D3c --> D3d[Unload LiME kernel module]

    D3d --> E[Phase 3 - Hash, Document, Preserve]
    E --> E1[Compute SHA-256 of all output files]
    E1 --> E2[Write chain_of_custody.txt]
    E2 --> E3[Move evidence to write-once media]
    E3 --> F[End: System ready for shutdown or disk imaging]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Linux Kernel Data Exposure via `/proc` and `/sys`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph kernel[Linux Kernel Space]
        K1[Process Scheduler]
        K2[Network Stack]
        K3[Memory Manager MM]
        K4[Module Loader]
        K5[VFS Layer]
        K6[ARP and Routing Tables]
    end

    subgraph procfs[/proc pseudo-filesystem]
        P1[/proc PID status]
        P2[/proc PID maps]
        P3[/proc PID fd]
        P4[/proc PID exe]
        P5[/proc net tcp]
        P6[/proc net arp]
        P7[/proc meminfo]
        P8[/proc modules]
        P9[/proc mounts]
    end

    subgraph sysfs[/sys pseudo-filesystem]
        S1[/sys class net]
        S2[/sys block]
        S3[/sys devices]
    end

    K1 --> P1
    K1 --> P2
    K1 --> P3
    K1 --> P4
    K2 --> P5
    K2 --> P6
    K2 --> S1
    K3 --> P7
    K4 --> P8
    K5 --> P9
    K6 --> P6
    K3 --> S2
    K1 --> S3

    procfs --> USER[Forensic Investigator reads text files]
    sysfs --> USER
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Memory Acquisition Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Inv as Forensic Investigator
    participant USB as Trusted USB Drive
    participant Sys as Live Linux System
    participant RAM as Physical RAM

    Inv->>Sys: Step 1 - date -u and hostname
    Sys-->>Inv: Timestamp and hostname string
    Inv->>USB: Step 2 - insmod lime.ko from USB
    USB->>Sys: Loads kernel module into Sys
    Inv->>Sys: Step 3 - echo "path=/mnt/usb/mem.lime format=lime" > /proc/lime
    Sys->>RAM: Direct DMA read of all physical pages
    RAM-->>Sys: Memory pages streamed
    Sys-->>USB: mem.lime written to USB
    Inv->>USB: Step 4 - sha256sum mem.lime
    USB-->>Inv: 64-character hex digest
    Inv->>Sys: Step 5 - rmmod lime
    Sys-->>Inv: Module unloaded
    Inv->>USB: Step 6 - write chain_of_custody.txt
```

```mermaid
  root((Volatile Data in Linux))
    Network State
      ARP cache
      Routing table
      TCP and UDP sockets
      Listening ports
      Interface configuration
      DNS cache
    Process State
      Running processes
      Open file descriptors
      Memory mappings
      Environment variables
      Loaded kernel modules
    User Session
      Logged in users
      SSH sessions
      Sudo history
      Bash history
      Login history

      Loaded services
```
